instruction: Could you identify which image most closely aligns with the text 'a bustling city street at night'?
description: The CLIP model was developed by researchers at OpenAI to study what contributes to robustness in computer vision tasks and to test the ability of models to generalize to arbitrary image classification tasks in a zero-shot manner. It was not developed for general model deployment, and researchers are advised to carefully study its capabilities in relation to the specific context of deployment. The model was developed in January 2021 and uses a ViT-B/16 Transformer architecture as an image encoder and a masked self-attention Transformer as a text encoder. These encoders are trained to maximize the similarity of (image, text) pairs via a contrastive loss. The original implementation had two variants: one using a ResNet image encoder and the other using a Vision Transformer. This repository contains the variant with the Vision Transformer. The model is intended for research purposes and is primarily aimed at AI researchers to better understand robustness, generalization, and other capabilities, biases, and constraints of computer vision models. Any deployed use case of the model, whether commercial or not, is currently out of scope. Non-deployed use cases such as image search in a constrained environment are also not recommended unless there is thorough in-domain testing of the model with a specific, fixed class taxonomy. This is because the model's performance varies significantly with different class taxonomies, and untested deployment could be harmful. Use cases related to surveillance and facial recognition are always out of scope, regardless of the model's performance, due to the lack of testing norms and checks to ensure fair use. The model has not been trained or evaluated on any languages other than English, so its use should be limited to English language use cases. The model was trained on publicly available image-caption data, including a combination of crawling a handful of websites and using commonly-used pre-existing image datasets. A large portion of the data comes from crawling the internet, which may skew towards more developed nations and younger, male users. The goal of the dataset was to test robustness and generalizability in computer vision tasks, and the data was gathered in a mostly non-interventionist manner. However, only websites with policies against excessively violent and adult images were crawled, and such content was filtered out. The dataset is not intended for use as the basis for any commercial or deployed model and will not be released. The model has been evaluated on a wide range of benchmarks across various computer vision datasets, including Food101, CIFAR10, CIFAR100, Birdsnap, SUN397, Stanford Cars, FGVC Aircraft, VOC2007, DTD, Oxford-IIIT Pet dataset, Caltech101, Flowers102, MNIST, SVHN, IIIT5K, Hateful Memes, SST-2, UCF101, Kinetics700, Country211, CLEVR Counting, KITTI Distance, STL-10, RareAct, Flickr30, MSCOCO, ImageNet, ImageNet-A, ImageNet-R, ImageNet Sketch, ObjectNet (ImageNet Overlap), Youtube-BB, and ImageNet-Vid. The model has several limitations, including struggles with fine-grained classification and counting objects. It also exhibits issues with fairness and bias, which are discussed in the paper. The performance of CLIP can depend significantly on class design and the choices made for categories to include and exclude. Testing revealed significant disparities with respect to race and gender when classifying images of people into crime-related and non-human animal categories, and these disparities could shift based on how the classes were constructed. Evaluations on gender, race, and age classification using the Fairface dataset showed accuracy greater than 96% across all races for gender classification, with 'Middle Eastern' having the highest accuracy (98.4%) and 'White' having the lowest (96.5%). CLIP averaged approximately 93% for racial classification and approximately 63% for age classification. These evaluations are used to assess the model's performance across different demographics and to surface potential risks, not to endorse or promote such tasks. The approach to testing CLIP also has a limitation: in many cases, linear probes were used to evaluate performance, and there is evidence suggesting that linear probes can underestimate model performance.

instruction: Can you find job roles that are similar to a 'Data Analyst' in the tech industry?
description: This is a Sentence Transformer model specifically trained for job title matching and similarity. It is fine-tuned on a large dataset of job titles and their associated skills or requirements. The model maps job titles and descriptions into a 1024-dimensional dense vector space and can be used for semantic job title matching, job similarity search, and related HR or recruitment tasks. The model is based on the MPNetModel architecture and includes a Transformer with a maximum sequence length of 64 tokens, a Pooling layer with mean token pooling, and an Asym layer with two dense layers that project 768-dimensional embeddings to 1024 dimensions using a Tanh activation function. The model uses cosine similarity as its similarity function and was trained on 5.5 million job title-skill pairs. It achieves a mean average precision (MAP) score of 0.6457 on the TalentCLEF benchmark. The model is intended for use in job title matching, job search, HR analytics, and talent management. Training was conducted with a batch size of 2048, a learning rate of 5e-05, and one epoch using FP16 training and the AdamW optimizer. The model was developed using Python 3.9.19, Sentence Transformers 3.1.0, Transformers 4.44.2, PyTorch 2.4.1+cu118, Accelerate 0.34.2, Datasets 3.0.0, and Tokenizers 0.19.1.

instruction: What kind of texture does the stone in the picture have?
description: NoctOWL (Not only coarse-text OWL) is an adaptation of OWL-ViT (referred to as NoctOWL) and OWLv2 (referred to as NoctOWLv2), designed for Fine-Grained Open-Vocabulary Detection (FG-OVD). Unlike standard open-vocabulary object detectors, which focus primarily on class-level recognition, NoctOWL enhances the ability to detect and distinguish fine-grained object attributes such as color, material, transparency, and pattern. It maintains a balanced trade-off between fine- and coarse-grained detection, making it particularly effective in scenarios requiring detailed object descriptions. The model includes the following variants: NoctOWL Base (lorebianchi98/NoctOWL-base-patch16), NoctOWLv2 Base (lorebianchi98/NoctOWLv2-base-patch16), NoctOWL Large (lorebianchi98/NoctOWL-large-patch14), and NoctOWLv2 Large (lorebianchi98/NoctOWLv2-large-patch14). Performance is reported in terms of mean Average Precision (mAP) on the Fine-Grained Open-Vocabulary Detection benchmarks across different difficulty levels, as well as performance on rare classes from the LVIS dataset. For OWL (B/16), the mAP scores are 20.6 on LVIS (Rare), 53.9 on Trivial, 38.4 on Easy, 39.8 on Medium, 26.2 on Hard, 45.3 for Color, 37.3 for Material, 26.6 for Pattern, and 34.1 for Transparency. For OWL (L/14), the scores are 31.2 on LVIS (Rare), 65.1 on Trivial, 44.0 on Easy, 39.3 on Medium, 26.5 on Hard, 43.8 for Color, 44.9 for Material, 36.0 for Pattern, and 29.2 for Transparency. For OWLv2 (B/16), the scores are 29.6 on LVIS (Rare), 52.9 on Trivial, 40.0 on Easy, 38.5 on Medium, 25.3 on Hard, 45.1 for Color, 33.5 for Material, 19.2 for Pattern, and 28.5 for Transparency. For OWLv2 (L/14), the scores are 34.9 on LVIS (Rare), 63.2 on Trivial, 42.8 on Easy, 41.2 on Medium, 25.4 on Hard, 53.3 for Color, 36.9 for Material, 23.3 for Pattern, and 12.2 for Transparency. For NoctOWL (B/16), the scores are 11.6 on LVIS (Rare), 46.6 on Trivial, 44.4 on Easy, 45.6 on Medium, 40.0 on Hard, 44.7 for Color, 46.0 for Material, 46.1 for Pattern, and 53.6 for Transparency. For NoctOWL (L/14), the scores are 26.0 on LVIS (Rare), 57.4 on Trivial, 54.2 on Easy, 54.8 on Medium, 48.6 on Hard, 53.1 for Color, 56.9 for Material, 49.8 for Pattern, and 57.2 for Transparency. For NoctOWLv2 (B/16), the scores are 17.5 on LVIS (Rare), 48.3 on Trivial, 49.1 on Easy, 47.1 on Medium, 42.1 on Hard, 46.8 for Color, 48.2 for Material, 42.2 for Pattern, and 50.2 for Transparency. For NoctOWLv2 (L/14), the scores are 27.2 on LVIS (Rare), 57.5 on Trivial, 55.5 on Easy, 57.2 on Medium, 50.2 on Hard, 55.6 for Color, 57.0 for Material, 49.2 for Pattern, and 55.9 for Transparency.

instruction: Produce a short clip of a cat and a dog playing together in a sunlit backyard.
description: LTX-Video is the first DiT-based video generation model capable of generating high-quality videos in real-time. It produces 30 FPS videos at a 1216×704 resolution faster than they can be watched. Trained on a large-scale dataset of diverse videos, the model generates high-resolution videos with realistic and varied content. The model supports both text-to-video and image+text-to-video use cases. It is a diffusion-based model developed by Lightricks and is designed to work with English prompts. The model works best on resolutions under 720 x 1280 and number of frames below 257. It is available in multiple versions, including 2B and 13B parameter sizes, with options for distilled, quantized, and LoRA variants. The model can be used locally with Python and PyTorch, and is compatible with the Diffusers library for both text-to-video and image-to-video generation. The model includes a spatial upscaler and a temporal upscaler for improved video quality. The model may fail to generate videos that perfectly match the prompts, and its performance is influenced by the prompting style. As a statistical model, it may amplify existing societal biases and is not intended to provide factual information.

instruction: I need to find all the clinical documents that mention a specific genetic mutation.
description: Clinical ModernBERT is a state-of-the-art encoder-based transformer designed for biomedical and clinical text, with a context length of up to 8192 tokens. It extends the innovations of ModernBERT by increasing the context window and incorporating domain-specific vocabulary refinements. The model is intended to produce semantically rich representations that capture the nuanced syntax of biomedical literature and the intricate semantics of clinical narratives. It utilizes architectural advancements such as rotary positional embeddings (RoPE), Flash Attention for near-linear memory usage with extended contexts, and GeGLU activation layers that enhance representational capacity through smooth gating mechanisms. Clinical ModernBERT is initialized from a ModernBERT-base checkpoint and pre-trained on a corpus of approximately 40 million PubMed abstracts and MIMIC-IV clinical notes, along with medical codes and descriptions. The pre-training process involved 150,000 masked language modeling (MLM) training steps with a batch size of 128. A custom collator dynamically adjusts the masking probability from 30% to 15% over training, emphasizing medically relevant tokens such as drug names and procedural codes. The MLM evaluation framework includes top-1, top-5, top-10, and top-25 accuracy metrics with values of 63.31%, 79.67%, 83.33%, and 88.10%, respectively. The model is optimized for tasks such as retrieval-augmented generation, fine-grained text classification, and domain-specific entity extraction. It is particularly suited for clinical information retrieval, narrative classification, and structured medical coding, and can be fine-tuned for specialized applications such as electronic health record analysis, clinical decision support systems, and evidence-based medical literature retrieval. The model is available for use through the Hugging Face platform with the identifier 'Simonlee711/Clinical_ModernBERT'.

instruction: I want to extract all the paragraphs that mention artificial intelligence in education. Can you help?
description: ColQwen2.5-7b-multilingual-v1.0 is a multilingual visual retriever based on Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct, utilizing the ColBERT strategy. It is designed to efficiently index documents using visual features and generates multi-vector representations of text and images. The model takes dynamic image resolutions as input without resizing, with a maximum resolution that allows up to 768 image patches. It was trained on 4xA100 80GB GPUs with a per_device_batch_size of 64 and gradient_accumulation_steps of 2 for 5 epochs. The training involved low-rank adapters with alpha=128 and r=128 on the transformer layers of the language model, as well as the final projection layer, using the paged_adamw_8bit optimizer. The training setup included a learning rate of 2e-4 with linear decay and 1% warmup steps, a batch size per device of 128, and gradient accumulation steps of 2 in bfloat16 format. The model was trained on data from the openbmb/VisRAG-Ret-Train-Synthetic-data, openbmb/VisRAG-Ret-Train-In-domain-data, Metric-AI/rag_docmatix_100k, vidore/colpali_train_set, and llamaindex/vdr-multilingual-train datasets. It is ranked #1 on the Vidore benchmark as of February 11, 2025. The model primarily focuses on PDF-type documents and high-resource languages, which may limit its generalization to other document types or less represented languages. It relies on multi-vector retrieving derived from the ColBERT late interaction mechanism, which may require engineering efforts to adapt to widely used vector retrieval frameworks that lack native multi-vector support. The vision language backbone model (Qwen2.5-VL) is licensed under the Qwen RESEARCH LICENSE AGREEMENT, while the adapters are under the MIT license.

instruction: What's the closest object in this image based on depth information?
description: Depth-Anything-V2 is a deep convolutional neural network model designed for depth estimation, which involves estimating depth at each point in an image. The model is optimized for mobile deployment and is intended to run on Qualcomm® devices. It is based on the DepthAnything_V2_Small checkpoint, with an input resolution of 518x518 and 24.7 million parameters, resulting in a model size of 94.3 MB in float precision. The model can be executed on various Qualcomm® devices, including QCS8275, QCS8450, QCS8550, QCS9075, SA7255P, SA8255, SA8295P, SA8650, SA8775P, and Snapdragon® devices such as the Samsung Galaxy S24 and S25, as well as the Snapdragon 8 Elite Gen 5 QRD and Snapdragon X Elite CRD. Performance metrics include inference times ranging from 27.597 ms to 172.982 ms and peak memory usage ranging from 0 MB to 883 MB, depending on the device and runtime (TFLITE, QNN_DLC, or ONNX). The model is primarily executed on the NPU (Neural Processing Unit) of these devices. The model can be compiled for on-device deployment using PyTorch's `jit.trace` and the Qualcomm® AI Hub API, and it supports performance profiling, accuracy verification, and deployment to Android using TensorFlow Lite or QNN shared libraries. The model is available for use via the Qualcomm® AI Hub Workbench, and it requires an API token for cloud-hosted device execution. The model's original implementation is licensed, and additional details on performance and deployment can be found on the Qualcomm® AI Hub.

instruction: Generate a fictional short story set in a futuristic city with advanced AI systems.
description: Qwen2.5 is the latest series of Qwen large language models. The Qwen2.5 series includes a range of base language models and instruction-tuned language models with parameter sizes ranging from 0.5 to 72 billion. Qwen2.5 introduces several improvements over Qwen2, including significantly more knowledge and enhanced capabilities in coding and mathematics, achieved through specialized expert models in these domains. It also shows significant improvements in instruction following, generating long texts (over 8K tokens), understanding structured data such as tables, and generating structured outputs, especially JSON. The model is more resilient to the diversity of system prompts, which enhances role-play implementation and condition-setting for chatbots. It supports long-context processing up to 128K tokens and can generate up to 8K tokens. The model supports over 29 languages, including Chinese, English, French, Spanish, Portuguese, German, Italian, Russian, Japanese, Korean, Vietnamese, Thai, and Arabic. This specific model is the instruction-tuned 32B version of Qwen2.5. It is a causal language model trained through pretraining and post-training. The model architecture is based on transformers with RoPE, SwiGLU, RMSNorm, and Attention QKV bias. It has 32.5B total parameters, with 31.0B non-embedding parameters. The model has 64 layers and uses 40 attention heads for queries and 8 for keys and values. It supports a full context length of 131,072 tokens and can generate up to 8192 tokens. The model requires the latest version of the Hugging Face `transformers` library for compatibility. Older versions of `transformers` (before 4.37.0) may result in a KeyError: 'qwen2'. To process long texts beyond 32,768 tokens, a technique called YaRN is used to enhance model length extrapolation. YaRN can be enabled by adding a specific configuration to the `config.json` file. For deployment, vLLM is recommended, although it currently only supports static YARN, which may impact performance on shorter texts. The model has been evaluated, and detailed results are available in a separate document. The model's performance is influenced by GPU memory and throughput, which are also documented separately. If used, the model should be cited using the provided references.

instruction: Can you provide a visual heatmap showing the detected body parts in this image?
description: MoveNet is a single pose estimation model designed for real-time processing and implemented in TensorFlow. The model is quantized in int8 format using the TensorFlow Lite converter. It is intended for pose estimation tasks and supports two types of output formats: heatmaps and direct keypoint coordinates. For heatmaps models, the input is a single NxM RGB image with UINT8 values between 0 and 255, and the output is a set of FLOAT values representing heatmaps of size WxH for K keypoints. For the other models, the input is the same, and the output is a set of FLOAT values representing the (x, y, confidence) coordinates of each keypoint. The model is optimized for deployment on STM32 platforms, with STM32MP2 and STM32N6 series being supported and recommended. Performance metrics include memory footprint, inference time, and accuracy (OKS) on the COCO Person dataset. For example, on the STM32N6 platform with a resolution of 192x192x3, the model uses 1674 KiB of internal RAM, 0 KiB of external RAM, and 3036.17 KiB of weights flash. Inference time is approximately 18.44 ms, resulting in about 54.23 inferences per second. On the STM32MP257F-DK2 board, using per-tensor quantization, inference time is approximately 7.82 ms with 84.47% utilization of the NPU and 14.53% utilization of the GPU. The model achieves an OKS score of up to 64.43% on the COCO Person dataset with a resolution of 256x256x3. The COCO Person dataset contains 80 classes and 118,287 images. It is recommended to use per-tensor quantization to maximize hardware acceleration on the STM32MP2 NPU. For integration and additional services, users are directed to the stm32ai-modelzoo-services GitHub repository.

instruction: Can you locate the section where the project objectives are outlined?
description: This model is a fine-tuned version of layoutlmv2-base-uncased on an unknown dataset. It achieves a loss of 2.9710 on the evaluation set. The model was trained using the following hyperparameters: learning rate of 5e-05, training batch size of 4, evaluation batch size of 8, seed of 42, Adam optimizer with betas of (0.9, 0.999) and epsilon of 1e-08, linear learning rate scheduler, and 3 training epochs. During training, the model's training loss decreased from 6.12 at step 5 to 2.2501 at step 530, and the validation loss decreased from 5.8130 at step 5 to 3.0351 at step 530. The model was trained using Transformers 4.44.2, PyTorch 2.4.0+cu121, Datasets 3.0.0, and Tokenizers 0.19.1.

instruction: Is this a known species of sea turtle based on the shell pattern?
description: BioCLIP 2 is a foundation model for biology organismal images. It is trained on a ViT-L/14 Transformer pre-trained on LAION-2B. BioCLIP 2 demonstrates state-of-the-art performance in recognizing various species and exhibits emergent properties beyond species classification after extensive hierarchical contrastive training. The model is trained on the TreeOfLife-200M dataset, the largest and most diverse available dataset of biology images. Through training at scale, BioCLIP 2 improves species classification by 18.1% over BioCLIP. It generalizes to diverse biological questions beyond species classification using only species-level supervision. Further analysis reveals that BioCLIP 2 acquires two emergent properties through scaling up hierarchical contrastive learning: inter-species ecological alignment and intra-species variation separation. The model uses a ViT-L/14 Transformer as an image encoder and a masked self-attention Transformer as a text encoder. It is licensed under the MIT license and was fine-tuned from a CLIP pre-trained on LAION-2B. BioCLIP 2 can be used for zero-shot classification provided the species names, for few-shot classification with some images serving as the support set, and as a visual encoder for other biological visual tasks. The model was trained on nearly 214 million images covering 952,000 taxa. A subset of LAION-2B consisting of 26 million samples was used for experience replay. The training used standard CLIP image preprocessing, bf16 mixed precision, an Adam optimizer with a maximum learning rate of 1e-4, 1,875 warm-up steps, and cosine decay. The batch size of biological images was 2,816 per GPU, and that of replay data was 320 per GPU. The model was trained on 32 GPUs for 30 epochs with a weight decay of 0.2. Each input image was resized to 224 x 224 resolution. BioCLIP 2 was evaluated on species classification and other biological visual tasks. For species classification, it was tested on 10 tasks, including NABirds, Plankton, Insects, Insects2, PlantNet, Fungi, PlantVillage, Medicinal Leaf, camera trap images, and a dataset of 400 species labeled Near Threatened through Extinct in the Wild. For tasks beyond species classification, it was tested on predicting feeding path and habitat labels, binary classification tasks, attribute classification, semi-supervised clustering for new species discovery, and plant disease classification. BioCLIP 2 surpasses BioCLIP by 18.0% on zero-shot species classification benchmarks and achieves the best performance on tasks beyond species classification. It yields a 10.2% performance gap over DINOv2 on these tasks. The model was trained on 32 NVIDIA H100-80GB GPUs distributed over 4 nodes on the Bridges-2 Cluster, and the training took 10 days to complete 30 epochs. BioCLIP 2 is trained on an imbalanced dataset, with the TreeOfLife-200M dataset exhibiting a long-tailed distribution across taxa. Therefore, the predictions of BioCLIP 2 might be biased toward well-represented species. While BioCLIP 2 and TreeOfLife-200M have the potential to improve conservation efforts by facilitating the recognition of threatened species, there is also potential for misuse by bad actors. Improved classification capability could be exploited by poachers to identify protected species, though these tools can also be used to monitor illicit trade. The primary risk to endangered species comes from the disclosure of precise location information rather than improved classification capability. The data used does not include geo-tagged information, minimizing vulnerabilities related to poaching.

instruction: What was the highest temperature recorded in this dataset, and on which date did it occur?
description: This model is a TAPAS base model fine-tuned on Sequential Question Answering (SQA). It has two versions: the default version corresponds to the `tapas_sqa_inter_masklm_base_reset` checkpoint, and the other version is `no_reset`, corresponding to `tapas_sqa_inter_masklm_base`. The model was pre-trained on masked language modeling (MLM) and an additional intermediate pre-training step, followed by fine-tuning on SQA. It uses relative position embeddings, resetting the position index at every cell of the table for the default version, while the non-default version uses absolute position embeddings. TAPAS is a BERT-like transformer model pre-trained on a large corpus of English data from Wikipedia in a self-supervised manner. It was pre-trained using two objectives: masked language modeling, where 15% of the words in a flattened table and associated context are randomly masked and the model must predict them, and intermediate pre-training, where the model is trained to classify whether a sentence is supported or refuted by the contents of a table using a balanced dataset of millions of syntactically created examples. This allows the model to learn a bidirectional representation of tables and associated texts, which can be used for downstream tasks such as answering questions about a table or determining if a sentence is entailed or refuted by the table. The model is fine-tuned by adding a cell selection head on top of the pre-trained model and jointly training this head with the base model on SQA. The model can be used for answering questions related to a table in a conversational setup. The model was fine-tuned on 32 Cloud TPU v3 cores for 200,000 steps with a maximum sequence length of 512 and a batch size of 128. The training took approximately 20 hours and used the Adam optimizer with a learning rate of 1.25e-5 and a warmup ratio of 0.2. An inductive bias was added to ensure the model only selects cells of the same column, as reflected by the `select_one_column` parameter of `TapasConfig`. The model's performance on the SQA development set shows a dev accuracy of 0.6874 for the base version with reset position embeddings and 0.6737 for the base version with no reset. The model card was written by the Hugging Face team and contributors, as the original TAPAS team did not provide one.
domain: Natural Language Processing Table Question Answering

instruction: Can you assess whether this video clip shows signs of being artificially generated?
description: This model is a fine-tuned version of the VideoMAE large model on a deepfake subset dataset. It is designed to perform tasks related to video analysis, specifically in the context of deepfake detection or classification. The model was trained using the Adam optimizer with betas=(0.9, 0.999) and epsilon=1e-08, a learning rate of 5e-05, a training batch size of 8, and an evaluation batch size of 8. The training process used a linear learning rate scheduler with a warmup ratio of 0.1 and a total of 4470 training steps. Native AMP was used for mixed precision training. The model was trained for a total of 9.1 epochs, with the training loss decreasing from 0.6169 to 0.0118 over the course of training. The validation loss fluctuated during training, starting at 0.6023 and reaching a minimum of 0.2647 at the end of training. The model achieves a loss of 0.2588 on the evaluation set. The training was conducted using Transformers 4.44.2, PyTorch 2.5.0+cu121, Datasets 3.1.0, and Tokenizers 0.19.1.

instruction: I’d like to hear this dialogue with a touch of enthusiasm—can you add some emotion?
description: Orpheus-TTS-Local is a lightweight client designed to run locally using the LM Studio API. It provides high-quality Text-to-Speech functionality using the Orpheus TTS model, eliminating the need for cloud API keys. The model supports multiple voice options, including tara, leah, jess, leo, dan, mia, zac, and zoe. Users can save generated audio as WAV files. The model allows customization through parameters such as temperature, top-p sampling, and repetition penalty. The default voice is tara, which is recommended for general use. Emotion can be added to the speech by using specific tags. The model is distributed under the Apache 2.0 license.

instruction: Locate the animals and plants in this forest scene for a biodiversity study.
description: The D-FINE model was proposed by Yansong Peng, Hebei Li, Peixi Wu, Yueyi Zhang, Xiaoyan Sun, and Feng Wu. This is the Hugging Face Transformers implementation for D-FINE. D-FINE is a real-time object detector that achieves outstanding localization precision by redefining the bounding box regression task in DETR models. The model comprises two key components: Fine-grained Distribution Refinement (FDR) and Global Optimal Localization Self-Distillation (GO-LSD). D-FINE is trained on COCO (Lin et al. [2014]) train2017 and validated on COCO val2017 dataset. The standard AP metrics are reported, averaged over uniformly sampled IoU thresholds ranging from 0.50 to 0.95 with a step size of 0.05, as well as APval5000, which is commonly used in real scenarios. D-FINE is ideal for real-time object detection in diverse applications such as autonomous driving, surveillance systems, robotics, and retail analytics. Its enhanced flexibility and deployment-friendly design make it suitable for both edge devices and large-scale systems, ensuring high accuracy and speed in dynamic, real-world environments. Variants of the model include 'coco' trained on COCO, 'obj365' trained on Object365, and 'obj2coco' trained on Object365 and then fine-tuned on COCO.

instruction: Which of these news headlines is most relevant to climate change?
description: The mxbai-rerank-base-v2 is the base model in the family of powerful reranker models from Mixedbread. It is designed to support a range of tasks, including retrieval, with state-of-the-art performance and strong efficiency. The model provides multilingual support for over 100 languages, with particularly strong performance in English and Chinese. It also supports code and long-context processing. The model is suitable for reranking tasks and can be used to rank documents based on a given query. It is trained using a three-step process that includes GRPO (Guided Reinforcement Prompt Optimization), contrastive learning, and preference learning. Performance benchmarks show that the mxbai-rerank-base-v2 achieves an average BEIR score of 55.57, with a multilingual score of 28.56, a Chinese score of 83.70, and a code search score of 31.73. The model has a latency of 0.67 seconds when measured on an A100 GPU. The technical report is coming soon. If you find the model useful, you are encouraged to give it a star and citation.

instruction: Which composition best represents the imagery of 'a bustling city at night'?
description: MuQ is a large music foundation model pre-trained using Self-Supervised Learning (SSL), achieving state-of-the-art performance in various Music Information Retrieval (MIR) tasks. MuQ-MuLan is a music-text joint embedding model trained via contrastive learning, supporting both English and Chinese texts. MuQ is designed to extract music audio features and supports tasks such as feature extraction with output of hidden states. MuQ-MuLan is designed to extract both music and text embeddings and calculate their similarity. MuQ has approximately 300 million parameters and was trained on the Million Song Dataset. MuQ-MuLan has approximately 700 million parameters and was trained on music-text pairs. It is important to note that the open-sourced MuQ model may not achieve the same level of performance as reported in the paper due to differences in dataset size.

instruction: Point out the part of the X-ray that corresponds to the finding 'peribronchial thickening'.
description: This is a checkpoint for Stable Diffusion fine-tuned with CXR-BERT on the MIMIC-CXR dataset, as presented in the paper. The model introduces a novel approach to phrase grounding in medical imaging, demonstrating that generative text-to-image diffusion models, specifically fine-tuned Stable Diffusion, can achieve superior zero-shot performance compared to traditional discriminative methods. Key innovations include leveraging cross-attention maps from generative diffusion models for phrase grounding, fine-tuning diffusion models with a frozen, domain-specific language model (CXR-BERT) to significantly improve performance in medical contexts, and introducing Bimodal Bias Merging (BBM), a novel post-processing technique that aligns text and image biases to refine cross-attention maps and enhance localization accuracy. The model aims to map natural language phrases from clinical reports to specific image regions, facilitating disease localization. Training was performed for 30,000 steps using eight A100 GPUs.
