The model was then fine-tuned on ImageNet 2012 (also referred to as ILSVRC2012), a dataset consisting of 1 million images and 1,000 classes, also at resolution 224x224. Images are presented to the model as a sequence of fixed-size patches (resolution 16x16), which are linearly embedded. A [CLS] token is added to the beginning of the sequence to enable classification tasks. Absolute position embeddings are also added before feeding the sequence to the layers of the Transformer encoder. By pre-training the model, it learns an inner representation of images that can be used to extract features useful for downstream tasks. For example, if a dataset of labeled images is available, a standard classifier can be trained by placing a linear layer on top of the pre-trained encoder. Typically, a linear layer is placed on top of the [CLS] token, as the last hidden state of this token can be seen as a representation of the entire image. The model is intended for use in image classification tasks. The ViT model was pre-trained on ImageNet-21k and fine-tuned on ImageNet 2012. During training, images were resized and normalized to a resolution of 224x224 with mean (0.5, 0.5, 0.5) and standard deviation (0.5, 0.5, 0.5) across the RGB channels. The model was trained on TPUv3 hardware (8 cores) with a batch size of 4096 and a learning rate warmup of 10,000 steps. Gradient clipping at a global norm of 1 was applied for ImageNet. Evaluation results on several image classification benchmarks are detailed in the original paper. The best results for fine-tuning are obtained with a higher resolution of 384x384, and increasing the model size generally results in better performance. The model was introduced in a paper by Dosovitskiy et al. and the weights were converted from JAX to PyTorch by Ross Wightman. The model card was written by the Hugging Face team, as the original team did not provide one.

instruction: Can you guide me through the reasoning needed for this data analysis task?
description: We introduce our first-generation reasoning models, DeepSeek-R1-Zero and DeepSeek-R1. DeepSeek-R1-Zero is a model trained via large-scale reinforcement learning (RL) without supervised fine-tuning (SFT) as a preliminary step. It demonstrated remarkable performance on reasoning tasks and naturally developed powerful and interesting reasoning behaviors. However, it encountered challenges such as endless repetition, poor readability, and language mixing. To address these issues and further enhance reasoning performance, we introduced DeepSeek-R1, which incorporates cold-start data before RL. DeepSeek-R1 achieves performance comparable to OpenAI-o1 across math, code, and reasoning tasks. We have open-sourced DeepSeek-R1-Zero, DeepSeek-R1, and six dense models distilled from DeepSeek-R1 based on Llama and Qwen. DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-32B outperforms OpenAI-o1-mini across various benchmarks, achieving new state-of-the-art results for dense models. The post-training process for DeepSeek-R1-Zero involves directly applying reinforcement learning to the base model without SFT, allowing the model to explore chain-of-thought (CoT) for solving complex problems. This approach resulted in the development of DeepSeek-R1-Zero, which demonstrates capabilities such as self-verification, reflection, and generating long CoTs. It is the first open research to validate that reasoning capabilities of LLMs can be incentivized purely through RL, without the need for SFT. The pipeline for developing DeepSeek-R1 includes two RL stages aimed at discovering improved reasoning patterns and aligning with human preferences, as well as two SFT stages that serve as the seed for the model's reasoning and non-reasoning capabilities. We demonstrate that the reasoning patterns of larger models can be distilled into smaller models, resulting in better performance compared to the reasoning patterns discovered through RL on small models. The open source DeepSeek-R1, as well as its API, will benefit the research community to distill better smaller models in the future. Using the reasoning data generated by DeepSeek-R1, we fine-tuned several dense models that are widely used in the research community. The evaluation results demonstrate that the distilled smaller dense models perform exceptionally well on benchmarks. We open-source distilled 1.5B, 7B, 8B, 14B, 32B, and 70B checkpoints based on Qwen2.5 and Llama3 series to the community. DeepSeek-R1-Zero and DeepSeek-R1 are trained based on DeepSeek-V3-Base. DeepSeek-R1-Distill models are fine-tuned based on open-source models, using samples generated by DeepSeek-R1. We slightly change their configurations and tokenizers. For all our models, the maximum generation length is set to 32,768 tokens. For benchmarks requiring sampling, we use a temperature of 0.6, a top-p value of 0.95, and generate 64 responses per query to estimate pass@1. DeepSeek-R1 achieves 90.8 on MMLU (Pass@1), 92.9 on MMLU-Redux (EM), 84.0 on MMLU-Pro (EM), 92.2 on DROP (3-shot F1), 83.3 on IF-Eval (Prompt Strict), 71.5 on GPQA-Diamond (Pass@1), 30.1 on SimpleQA (Correct), 82.5 on FRAMES (Acc.), 87.6 on AlpacaEval2.0 (LC-winrate), and 92.3 on ArenaHard (GPT-4-1106). In code tasks, DeepSeek-R1 achieves 65.9 on LiveCodeBench (Pass@1-COT), 96.3 on Codeforces (Percentile), 2029 on Codeforces (Rating), 49.2 on SWE Verified (Resolved), and 53.3 on Aider-Polyglot (Acc.). In math tasks, DeepSeek-R1 achieves 79.8 on AIME 2024 (Pass@1), 97.3 on MATH-500 (Pass@1), and 78.8 on CNMO 2024 (Pass@1). In Chinese tasks, DeepSeek-R1 achieves 92.8 on CLUEWSC (EM), 91.8 on C-Eval (EM), and 63.7 on C-SimpleQA (Correct). DeepSeek-R1-Distill models achieve 72.6 on AIME 2024 pass@1, 86.7 on AIME 2024 cons@64, 94.5 on MATH-500 pass@1, 65.2 on GPQA Diamond pass@1, 57.5 on LiveCodeBench pass@1, and 1633 on CodeForces rating. You can chat with DeepSeek-R1 on DeepSeek's official website and switch on the button 'DeepThink'. We also provide an OpenAI-Compatible API at DeepSeek Platform. To run DeepSeek-R1 models locally, please visit the repository for more information. Hugging Face's Transformers has not been directly supported yet. DeepSeek-R1-Distill models can be utilized in the same manner as Qwen or Llama models. For instance, you can easily start a service using vllm or sglang. We recommend adhering to the following configurations when utilizing the DeepSeek-R1 series models, including benchmarking, to achieve the expected performance: set the temperature within the range of 0.5-0.7 (0.6 is recommended) to prevent endless repetitions or incoherent outputs, avoid adding a system prompt; all instructions should be contained within the user prompt, for mathematical problems, include a directive in your prompt such as 'Please reason step by step, and put your final answer within \boxed{}', and when evaluating model performance, conduct multiple tests and average the results. Additionally, we have observed that the DeepSeek-R1 series models tend to bypass thinking pattern (i.e., outputting '\\n\\n\') when responding to certain queries, which can adversely affect the model's performance. To ensure that the model engages in thorough reasoning, we recommend enforcing the model to initiate its response with '\\n' at the beginning of every output. This code repository and the model weights are licensed under a permissive license. DeepSeek-R1 series support commercial use, allow for any modifications and derivative works, including, but not limited to, distillation for training other LLMs. Please note that DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-1.5B, DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-7B, DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-14B, and DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-32B are derived from models originally licensed under their respective licenses and are now fine-tuned with 800k samples curated with DeepSeek-R1. DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-8B is derived from Llama3.1-8B-Base and is originally licensed under its respective license. DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-70B is derived from Llama3.3-70B-Instruct and is originally licensed under its respective license. If you have any questions, please raise an issue or contact us.

instruction: Can you generate a map-like view of a car's position and nearby objects using camera input?
description: Simple-Bev is a machine learning model designed to generate a bird's eye view representation from sensors, such as cameras, mounted on a vehicle. It uses ResNet-101 as the backbone and segnet as a segmentation model for specific use cases. The model is optimized for mobile deployment and is intended for use on Qualcomm® devices. The model checkpoint is model-000025000.pth, with an input resolution of 448 x 800 and 49.7 million parameters. The model size in float format is 190 MB. Performance metrics across various devices include inference times ranging from 1685.256 ms to 3351.137 ms and peak memory usage between 1242 - 1729 MB, with CPU as the primary compute unit. The model can be installed via pip using the package qai-hub-models. It supports running on cloud-hosted Qualcomm® devices through the Qualcomm® AI Hub, which requires an API token for configuration. A demo is available to run the model on a sample input, including pre-processing, model inference, and post-processing. The model can be compiled for on-device deployment using PyTorch's jit.trace and the submit_compile_job API. Performance profiling and on-device accuracy verification can be conducted using the Qualcomm® AI Hub. The compiled model can be deployed on Android using TensorFlow Lite (.tflite) or QNN (.so) runtimes. More information on model performance across devices and deployment instructions is available through the Qualcomm® AI Hub. The license for the original implementation and compiled assets for on-device deployment is available separately. The model is part of a community for collaboration and support related to on-device AI.

instruction: 需要把一段音频内容转成文字，可以帮忙吗？
description: This is a fine-tuned XLSR-53 large model for speech recognition in Chinese. It was fine-tuned using the train and validation splits of unspecified datasets. When using this model, the speech input must be sampled at 16kHz. The model was fine-tuned with the support of GPU credits provided by an unspecified source. The model can be used directly for speech recognition without a language model. It is based on the Wav2Vec2 architecture and is designed to transcribe audio inputs into text. Evaluation on the Chinese (zh-CN) test data of Common Voice shows a Word Error Rate (WER) of 82.37% and a Character Error Rate (CER) of 19.03%. These results were obtained using a specific evaluation script. The model has been tested on a sample of 10 audio files from the Common Voice dataset, and example transcriptions show varying degrees of accuracy, with some predictions containing errors in character recognition. The model can be accessed and used via the Hugging Face library, and the training script is available at an unspecified location. Users are advised to ensure that their audio inputs are properly formatted and sampled at the required rate. The model is intended for speech recognition tasks in the Chinese language and is not suitable for other languages or tasks not explicitly mentioned.

instruction: Generate a scene of a city park at sunset, with each object like trees, benches, and people segmented in unique colors.
description: ControlNet v1.1 is a neural network structure designed to control diffusion models by adding extra conditions. It is the successor model to an earlier version and was developed by Lvmin Zhang and Maneesh Agrawala. This checkpoint is a conversion into diffusers format and is intended to be used in combination with Stable Diffusion. The model is a diffusion-based text-to-image generation model and is primarily associated with the English language. The license is adapted from the work conducted by others in the area of responsible AI licensing. ControlNet v1.1 is conditioned on segmentation images, supporting a broader range of colors under the COCO protocol compared to the previous Segmentation 1.0 version, which supported about 150 colors. Segmentation 1.1 supports an additional 182 colors from COCO. The model was trained to support various input conditions, including canny edge detection, pixel to pixel instruction, image inpainting, multi-level line segment detection, depth estimation, surface normal estimation, image segmentation, line art generation, anime line art generation, human pose estimation, scribble-based image generation, soft edge image generation, image shuffling, and image tiling. The model was trained in an end-to-end manner, and the learning process is robust even with a small training dataset. The model is described in the paper titled 'Adding Conditional Control to Text-to-Image Diffusion Models' by Lvmin Zhang and Maneesh Agrawala.

instruction: What details can you identify in the background of this photo?
description: VideoLLaMA 3 is a state-of-the-art series of multimodal foundation models designed to excel in both image and video understanding tasks. These models leverage advanced architectures to demonstrate exceptional capabilities in processing and interpreting visual content across various contexts. VideoLLaMA 3 is specifically designed to address complex multimodal challenges, such as integrating textual and visual information, extracting insights from sequential video data, and performing high-level reasoning over both dynamic and static visual scenes. The model series includes variants such as VideoLLaMA3-7B, VideoLLaMA3-2B, VideoLLaMA3-7B-Image, and VideoLLaMA3-2B-Image, each based on different base models including Qwen2.5-7B and Qwen2.5-1.5B. Additionally, a tuned vision encoder based on siglip-so400m-patch14-384 is available for VideoLLaMA3-7B for wider application. The models are intended for use in tasks involving image and video understanding, including but not limited to answering questions about visual content and reasoning over visual scenes.

instruction: What is the person in this video doing with the materials shown on the table?
description: Vamba is a hybrid Mamba-Transformer model designed for efficient hour-long video understanding. It leverages cross-attention layers and Mamba-2 blocks to process text and video tokens differently. The main computation overhead in transformer-based large multimodal models (LMMs) comes from the quadratic complexity of self-attention in video tokens. To address this, Vamba splits the self-attention operation over the entire video and text token sequence into two more efficient components. Since video tokens typically dominate the sequence while text tokens remain few, Vamba maintains the self-attention mechanism exclusively for the text tokens and eliminates it for the video tokens. Instead, it adds cross-attention layers that use text tokens as queries and video tokens as keys and values. In addition, Vamba employs Mamba blocks to effectively process the video tokens. The model is intended to support tasks involving video and image understanding, including describing the content of videos and images. The model is named Vamba-Qwen2-VL-7B.

instruction: I want to search through a large collection of documents—how can I encode their content efficiently?
description: The all-roberta-large-v1 model is a sentence and short paragraph encoder that maps input text to a 1024-dimensional dense vector space. It is designed to capture semantic information and can be used for tasks such as information retrieval, clustering, and sentence similarity. The model is based on a pretrained Roberta architecture and was fine-tuned using a contrastive learning objective on a dataset containing over 1 billion sentence pairs. During training, the model was optimized to predict which sentences were paired in the dataset by computing cosine similarity between sentence pairs and applying cross-entropy loss. The training was conducted over 400,000 steps with a batch size of 256, a learning rate of 2e-5, and a sequence length limit of 128 tokens. The model was developed using efficient hardware infrastructure, including 7 TPUs v3-8, and benefited from support from Google's Flax, JAX, and Cloud teams. Input text longer than 128 word pieces is truncated by default. The model can be used with the Sentence-Transformers library or directly with HuggingFace Transformers, where token embeddings are pooled and normalized to produce final sentence embeddings.

instruction: I need a detailed transcript of a sales pitch recorded in English—can you provide it with timestamps?
description: The parakeet-tdt-0.6b-v2 is a 600-million-parameter automatic speech recognition (ASR) model designed for high-quality English transcription. It supports punctuation, capitalization, and accurate timestamp prediction. The model is an XL variant of the FastConformer architecture, integrating the TDT decoder and trained with full attention, enabling efficient transcription of audio segments up to 24 minutes in a single pass. It achieves an RTFx of 3380 on the HF-Open-ASR leaderboard with a batch size of 128, though performance may vary depending on dataset audio duration and batch size. Key features include accurate word-level timestamp predictions, automatic punctuation and capitalization, and robust performance on spoken numbers and song lyrics transcription. The model is intended for developers, researchers, academics, and industries building applications that require speech-to-text capabilities, including conversational AI, voice assistants, transcription services, subtitle generation, and voice analytics platforms. It is trained on the Granary dataset, consisting of approximately 120,000 hours of English speech data, including 10,000 hours from human-transcribed NeMo ASR Set 3.0 and 110,000 hours of pseudo-labeled data. The model is evaluated using the Huggingface Open ASR Leaderboard datasets, with performance measured using Word Error Rate (WER). The base performance shows an average WER of 6.05, with specific WER values for datasets such as AMI (11.16), Earnings-22 (11.15), GigaSpeech (9.74), LS test-clean (1.69), LS test-other (3.19), SPGI Speech (2.17), TEDLIUM-v3 (3.38), and VoxPopuli (5.95). Performance across different Signal-to-Noise Ratios (SNR) shows an increase in WER as SNR decreases, with relative changes ranging from -14.75% at SNR 10 to -234.66% at SNR -5. Performance on telephony-style audio (μ-law encoding) shows a slight increase in WER compared to standard 16kHz audio. The model is compatible with NVIDIA GPU-accelerated systems and is optimized to run on NVIDIA Ampere, Blackwell, Hopper, and Volta microarchitectures. It is available for use in the NeMo toolkit and can be used as a pre-trained checkpoint for inference or fine-tuning on another dataset. The model is ready for commercial and non-commercial use and is governed by the specified license. Ethical considerations include ensuring the model meets requirements for the relevant industry and use case and addressing unforeseen product misuse. The model has been verified to meet prescribed NVIDIA quality standards and has technical limitations, including potential inaccuracies in transcripts and variations in accuracy based on language and input audio characteristics. The model is not recommended for word-for-word or incomplete sentences as accuracy varies based on the context of input text. The model is not designed to generate or reverse engineer personal data, and data labeling complies with privacy laws. However, data correction or removal is not possible with externally-sourced data.

instruction: Would it be possible to turn this daytime image into a nighttime version with stars visible?
description: FLUX.1 Kontext [dev] is a 12 billion parameter rectified flow transformer designed to edit images based on text instructions. It allows users to change existing images using an edit instruction, maintain character, style, and object reference without any finetuning, and refine images through multiple successive edits with minimal visual drift. The model is trained using guidance distillation, which makes it more efficient. It is available under a non-commercial license and supports personal, scientific, and commercial use within the terms of the license. FLUX.1 Kontext [dev] is provided with a reference implementation and sampling code, and is available via API through multiple platforms. The model includes an integrity checker to be used after image generation to ensure safety. Black Forest Labs has implemented several mitigations to address potential risks, including filtering pre-training data for NSFW content, partnering with the Internet Watch Foundation to filter known child sexual abuse material from post-training data, and undertaking multiple rounds of targeted fine-tuning to prevent the generation of synthetic CSAM or nonconsensual intimate imagery. The model underwent internal and external third-party evaluations, demonstrating high resilience against violative inputs. The FLUX API applies filters to intercept harmful content and includes cryptographically-signed metadata to indicate content provenance. Use of the model is governed by Developer Terms of Service, Usage Policy, and the FLUX.1 [dev] Non-Commercial License, which prohibit the generation of unlawful content or the use of generated content for unlawful, defamatory, or abusive purposes. The model is monitored for violative use, and developers who repeatedly violate policies may be banned. A dedicated email address is provided for community feedback, and the company maintains relationships with organizations focused on content safety and child protection.

instruction: Could you analyze the tone of the speaker in this clip and describe it?
description: Qwen2-Audio is a state-of-the-art small-scale multimodal model (AudioLM) that processes both audio and text inputs, enabling voice interactions without the need for ASR modules. It supports English, Chinese, and major European languages, and provides voice chat and audio analysis capabilities for local use cases. These include speaker identification and response, speech translation and transcription, mixed audio and noise detection, and music and sound analysis. The model is designed to run locally on edge devices using the Nexa-SDK, which is an open-sourced, local on-device inference framework supporting text generation, image generation, vision-language models, audio-language models, speech-to-text, and text-to-speech capabilities. Users can run Qwen2-Audio locally by installing Nexa-SDK and executing the command 'nexa run qwen2audio', which by default uses the q4_K_M quantization requiring 4.2GB of RAM. The model can be used via terminal by dragging and dropping an audio file or entering a file path, with optional text prompts to guide analysis. Alternatively, it can be used with a local UI via the command 'nexa run qwen2audio -st'. Qwen2-Audio is suitable for use cases such as voice chat, where it can answer daily questions, offer suggestions, perform speaker identification, provide speech translation, and detect background noise. For audio analysis, it supports information extraction, audio summary, speech transcription and expansion, mixed audio and noise detection, and music and sound analysis. Performance benchmarks show that Qwen2-Audio significantly outperforms previous state-of-the-art models and Qwen-Audio across all tasks.

instruction: Generate a picture of a serene beach at sunset with palm trees and calm waves.
description: Stable Diffusion 3.5 Medium is a Multimodal Diffusion Transformer with improvements (MMDiT-X) text-to-image model developed by Stability AI. It is designed to generate images based on text prompts and features three fixed, pretrained text encoders, QK-normalization to improve training stability, and dual attention blocks in the first 12 transformer layers. The model introduces self-attention modules in the first 13 layers of the transformer, enhancing multi-resolution generation and overall image coherence. It was trained using a wide variety of data, including synthetic and filtered publicly available data. The model supports progressive training stages with resolutions increasing from 256 to 1440, and includes extended positional embedding space and random crop augmentation to enhance robustness across mixed resolutions and aspect ratios. The model uses CLIPs and T5 text encoders with varying context lengths. It is optimized for improved performance in image quality, typography, complex prompt understanding, and resource-efficiency. However, it may produce artifacts when T5 tokens exceed 256, and the medium model may respond differently to prompts compared to the large model. It is recommended to use specific sampling methods for better structure and anatomy coherency. The model is available for non-commercial and commercial use under a community license for organizations or individuals with less than $1M in annual revenue, with enterprise licensing available for higher revenue entities. It is intended for use in artistic processes, educational tools, and research on generative models. It is not designed to generate factual or true representations of people or events. Safety measures are implemented to reduce the risk of certain harms, but developers are encouraged to conduct their own testing and apply additional mitigations based on specific use cases. The model is not guaranteed to prevent all harmful content, and developers should implement content safety guardrails. Misuse and privacy violations are out of scope, and users are required to adhere to acceptable use policies. Developers and deployers are encouraged to respect privacy regulations and data privacy techniques.

instruction: How would you describe the best way to cook a perfect omelette?
description: Qwen3 is the latest generation of large language models in the Qwen series, offering a comprehensive suite of dense and mixture-of-experts (MoE) models. Built upon extensive training, Qwen3 delivers groundbreaking advancements in reasoning, instruction-following, agent capabilities, and multilingual support. It uniquely supports seamless switching between thinking mode (for complex logical reasoning, math, and coding) and non-thinking mode (for efficient, general-purpose dialogue) within a single model, ensuring optimal performance across various scenarios. It significantly enhances its reasoning capabilities, surpassing previous QwQ (in thinking mode) and Qwen2.5 instruct models (in non-thinking mode) on mathematics, code generation, and commonsense logical reasoning. It excels in human preference alignment, delivering a more natural, engaging, and immersive conversational experience in creative writing, role-playing, multi-turn dialogues, and instruction following. It demonstrates expertise in agent capabilities, enabling precise integration with external tools in both thinking and non-thinking modes and achieving leading performance among open-source models in complex agent-based tasks. It supports 100+ languages and dialects with strong capabilities for multilingual instruction following and translation. The Qwen3-8B model is a causal language model with 8.2 billion parameters, 6.95 billion non-embedding parameters, 36 layers, and 32 attention heads for Q and 8 for KV. It natively supports a context length of 32,768 tokens and can be extended to 131,072 tokens using RoPE scaling techniques. For thinking mode, it is recommended to use Temperature=0.6, TopP=0.95, TopK=20, and MinP=0, while for non-thinking mode, Temperature=0.7, TopP=0.8, TopK=20, and MinP=0 are suggested. The model can be deployed using frameworks such as Hugging Face transformers, vLLM, and SGLang. It supports switching between thinking and non-thinking modes via the `enable_thinking` parameter or user input tags `/think` and `/no_think`. For agentic use, it is recommended to use Qwen-Agent to reduce coding complexity. For long text processing, YaRN scaling can be enabled through configuration modifications or command line arguments. The model's default max_position_embeddings is set to 40,960, with 32,768 tokens reserved for outputs and 8,192 for prompts. It is advised to enable YaRN only when processing long contexts is required. The model can be cited using the provided BibTeX entry.

instruction: Is it possible to make this person's hair look like it's blowing in the wind?
description: FLUX.1 Kontext [dev] is a 12 billion parameter rectified flow transformer designed to edit images based on text instructions. It allows users to change existing images using an edit instruction, maintain character, style, and object reference without any finetuning, and refine images through multiple successive edits with minimal visual drift. The model is trained using guidance distillation, which makes it more efficient. It is available under a non-commercial license and supports personal, scientific, and commercial use within the terms of the license. FLUX.1 Kontext [dev] is provided with a reference implementation and sampling code, and is available via API through multiple platforms. The model includes an integrity checker to be used after image generation to ensure safety. Black Forest Labs has implemented several mitigations to address potential risks, including filtering pre-training data for NSFW content, partnering with the Internet Watch Foundation to filter known child sexual abuse material from post-training data, and undertaking multiple rounds of targeted fine-tuning to prevent the generation of synthetic CSAM or nonconsensual intimate imagery. The model underwent internal and external third-party evaluations, demonstrating high resilience against violative inputs. The FLUX API applies filters to intercept harmful content and includes cryptographically-signed metadata to indicate content provenance. Use of the model is governed by Developer Terms of Service, Usage Policy, and the FLUX.1 [dev] Non-Commercial License, which prohibit the generation of unlawful content or the use of generated content for unlawful, defamatory, or abusive purposes. The model is monitored for violative use, and developers who repeatedly violate policies may be banned. A dedicated email address is provided for community feedback, and the company maintains relationships with organizations focused on content safety and child protection.

instruction: What is the most likely category for this vocal sound — a cry, a laugh, or a sigh?
description: voc2vec is a foundation model specifically designed for non-verbal human data. The voc2vec-hubert-ls-pt model is built upon the HuBERT framework and follows its pre-training setup. The pre-training datasets include AudioSet (vocalization), FreeSound (babies), HumanVoiceDataset, NNIME, NonSpeech7K, ReCANVo, SingingDatabase, TUT (babies), VocalSketch, and VocalSound. This model continues pre-training from a HuBERT-like model that was initially trained on the LibriSpeech dataset. The model is evaluated on six datasets: ASVP-ESD, ASPV-ESD (babies), CNVVE, NonVerbal Vocalization Dataset, Donate a Cry, and VIVAE. The model achieves an average performance of Unweighted Average Recall (UAR) of .696±.189 and F1 Macro of .678±.200 across these six datasets. This is currently the best released model of the voc2vec collection.

instruction: Would you classify this query as benign or potentially harmful?
description: Llama Prompt Guard 2 is a series of two classifier models, Llama Prompt Guard 2 86M and Llama Prompt Guard 2 22M, designed to detect prompt injection and jailbreaking attacks in LLM-powered applications. These attacks aim to subvert the intended behavior of models by manipulating input prompts. The models are trained on a large corpus of known vulnerabilities and are intended to help developers reduce the risk of prompt attacks with a customizable solution. The 22M model is a smaller, faster version based on DeBERTa-xsmall, offering a 75% reduction in latency and compute costs with minimal performance trade-offs. Both models use a refined tokenization strategy to resist adversarial attacks such as whitespace manipulations and fragmented tokens. They perform binary classification, labeling prompts as either 'benign' or 'malicious' based on whether they attempt to override prior instructions. The models support a 512-token context window and are evaluated for attack detection in English, French, German, Hindi, Italian, Portuguese, Spanish, and Thai. Llama Prompt Guard 2 86M uses a multilingual base model and is trained to detect both English and non-English attacks. The training dataset includes open-source benign and malicious data, as well as synthetic injections and red-teaming data. A custom energy-based loss function is used to improve precision on out-of-distribution data by penalizing large negative energy predictions on benign prompts. Performance metrics show that Llama Prompt Guard 2 86M achieves an AUC of 0.998 in English and 0.995 in multilingual settings, with a recall of 97.5% at 1% false positive rate. The 22M model achieves an AUC of 0.995 in English and 0.942 in multilingual settings, with a recall of 88.7% at 1% false positive rate. In real-world evaluations using AgentDojo, the 86M model achieves an APR of 81.2% at 3% utility reduction, outperforming other models such as ProtectAI, Deepset, and LLM Warden. The models offer benefits such as detecting common attack patterns, serving as an additional layer of defense, and enabling proactive monitoring of misuse patterns. Limitations include vulnerability to adaptive attacks, variability in performance based on application-specific prompts, and reduced multilingual performance for the 22M model due to the lack of multilingual pretraining in its base model. Fine-tuning on domain-specific datasets is recommended to improve accuracy and reduce false positives. The models can be used with the Transformers library via the pipeline API or with the AutoTokenizer and AutoModelForSequenceClassification APIs for more control.

instruction: How similar is the role of a 'UX Designer' to that of a 'Product Designer'?
description: This is a Sentence Transformer model specifically trained for job title matching and similarity. It is fine-tuned on a large dataset of job titles and their associated skills or requirements. The model maps job titles and descriptions into a 1024-dimensional dense vector space and can be used for semantic job title matching, job similarity search, and related HR or recruitment tasks. The model is based on the MPNetModel architecture and includes a Transformer with a maximum sequence length of 64 tokens, a Pooling layer with mean token pooling, and an Asym layer with two dense layers that project 768-dimensional embeddings to 1024 dimensions using a Tanh activation function. The model uses cosine similarity as its similarity function and was trained on 5.5 million job title-skill pairs. It achieves a mean average precision (MAP) score of 0.6457 on the TalentCLEF benchmark. The model is intended for use in job title matching, job search, HR analytics, and talent management. Training was conducted with a batch size of 2048, a learning rate of 5e-05, and one epoch using FP16 training and the AdamW optimizer. The model was developed using Python 3.9.19, Sentence Transformers 3.1.0, Transformers 4.44.2, PyTorch 2.4.1+cu118, Accelerate 0.34.2, Datasets 3.0.0, and Tokenizers 0.19.1.

instruction: Can you tell me the net income after taxes from this financial summary?
description: FinTabQA-A4 is a model designed for financial table question-answering. It is based on the LayoutLM architecture and is intended to extract answers from financial table images. The model supports tasks involving the interpretation of table data in response to natural language questions. It processes input that includes both the text content of the table and its spatial layout information, such as word positions. FinTabQA-A4 was trained on the SynFinTabs dataset, a large-scale, labelled dataset of synthetic financial tables. The model was evaluated on real-world financial tables and compared to a state-of-the-art generative model, with results discussed in the associated research. The model is available for use via the Hugging Face Transformers library and requires a compatible tokenizer for input processing. The model's performance is demonstrated through an example where it correctly predicts an answer from a table image. The model's development and training methodology are described in a paper presented at the ICDAR 2025 Workshops. The authors emphasize the importance of domain-specific datasets for training models in financial table extraction and highlight the potential for their synthetic data generation method to be applied to other domains.

instruction: Could you check if the speaker in this audio is expressing surprise or fear?
description: This model is a fine-tuned version for Speech Emotion Recognition (SER). It uses a Frozen Feature Extractor strategy to preserve the model's acoustic understanding while adapting to emotion detection. This approach ensures stable performance and prevents 'Catastrophic Forgetting,' achieving nearly 80% accuracy on the validation set. The 'Calm' and 'Neutral' classes have been merged to improve classification consistency, resulting in 7 distinct emotion classes. The model architecture is Wav2Vec2 with a frozen CNN feature extractor and a trainable sequence classification head. It is designed for English language and the task of SER. The model was fine-tuned from 'facebook/wav2vec2-base-960h.' The model classifies audio into one of the following emotions: Angry, Disgust, Fear, Happy, Neutral (includes Calm), Sad, and Surprise. The model was trained on a combined dataset of approximately 12,000 audio files. Performance metrics include an accuracy of 79.94%, an F1 score of 79.65%, and a validation loss of 0.644. Stable convergence was achieved using a Cosine Learning Rate Scheduler and Frozen Feature Extractor. The model was fine-tuned using a 'Robust' configuration on Google Colab T4 GPUs with the following parameters: Feature Extractor was frozen (Locked weights to preserve pre-trained acoustic features), 10 epochs, a learning rate of 3e-5, a Cosine Decay scheduler, a batch size of 4 (Physical) / 16 (Effective via Gradient Accumulation), the AdamW optimizer, and fp32 precision. The model requires audio input with a sampling rate of 16kHz; it will hallucinate if the input is not 16kHz, and resampling is required.

instruction: How can I determine how similar a description of a painting is to the painting itself?
description: ViT-B-16-SigLIP2-256 is a SigLIP 2 Vision-Language model trained on the WebLI dataset. It is designed for contrastive image-text tasks and zero-shot image classification. The model is based on a Vision Transformer (ViT) architecture with a 16-pixel patch size and is optimized using the Sigmoid loss for language image pre-training. It has been converted for use in OpenCLIP from the original JAX checkpoints. The model supports encoding both images and text into feature representations and computing similarity scores between them using a sigmoid function. It is intended for applications requiring multilingual vision-language understanding, improved semantic comprehension, and dense feature extraction. The model's performance is detailed in the research paper titled 'SigLIP 2: Multilingual Vision-Language Encoders with Improved Semantic Understanding, Localization, and Dense Features.'

instruction: I want to verify if the license plate in this image is from Iran.
description: YOLOv8 is a cutting-edge, state-of-the-art model that builds upon the success of previous YOLO versions and introduces new features and improvements to further boost performance and flexibility. YOLOv8 is designed to be fast, accurate, and easy to use, making it an excellent choice for a wide range of object detection and tracking, instance segmentation, image classification, and pose estimation tasks. This model has been fine-tuned for detecting Iranian vehicle license plates. The model can be used directly in the Command Line Interface (CLI) with a yolo command, and it can also be used in a Python environment. It supports training, validation, prediction, and deployment. The model accepts arguments such as image size and device selection. It can evaluate model performance on a validation set, perform object detection on an image, and export the model to ONNX format. The model can be used with code to detect objects, extract bounding boxes, and display or save the detected objects. The model outputs bounding box coordinates, and the detected object can be cropped from the input image for further processing or visualization.

instruction: I’m working on image editing and need a precise foreground extraction—can you help?
description: BiRefNet is a model designed for high-resolution dichotomous image segmentation. It was trained using images of size 512x512 to enable faster and more accurate inference at lower resolutions. The model's performance was evaluated in FP16 mode. On the DIS-VD dataset, BiRefNet achieved a maxFm score of 0.879, a wFmeasure of 0.840, an MAE of 0.040, an Smeasure of 0.888, a meanEm of 0.931, an HCE of 1526, a maxEm of 0.941, a meanFm of 0.864, an adpEm of 0.938, an adpFm of 0.857, an mBA of 0.732, a maxBIoU of 0.747, and a meanBIoU of 0.726. The model is based on a bilateral reference approach and is developed by researchers from Nankai University, Northwestern Polytechnical University, National University of Defense Technology, Aalto University, Shanghai AI Laboratory, and University of Trento. The model is part of the official implementation for the paper 'Bilateral Reference for High-Resolution Dichotomous Image Segmentation,' published in the CAAI Artificial Intelligence Research journal in 2024. The authors acknowledge the support of @freepik for providing GPU resources for training the model.

instruction: Can you help identify the likely gender associated with the name 'Skyler'?
description: This model, named Genderize, is a text classification model developed by Imran Ali. It is designed to classify gender based on input names. The model uses a pre-trained BERT model as its base and has been fine-tuned on a dataset of names and their associated genders, such as the Dannel gender-name dataset. The training procedure involved fine-tuning the BERT model with a classification head, using a batch size of 8, a learning rate of 2e-5, and a total of 20,005 training steps. The model has 109,483,778 trainable parameters. Evaluation was performed on a test dataset split from the training data, using metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 score. The model is intended for direct use in classifying the gender of a given name and for downstream applications that require gender identification based on names, such as personalized marketing and user profiling. It is not intended for use outside the scope of gender classification without proper validation. The model may reflect biases present in the training data, and its performance should be validated across diverse datasets. Risks include misclassification, particularly for unisex or less common names. The model's accuracy may vary depending on the cultural and linguistic context of the names. Users are advised to be aware of these potential biases and limitations and to perform further validation for specific use cases and datasets.

instruction: Please find and label all the objects that resemble a remote control in this photo.
description: The OmDet model was proposed by Tiancheng Zhao, Peng Liu, Xuan He, Lu Zhang, and Kyusong Lee from Om AI Lab. This model is intended for zero-shot (also called open-vocabulary) object detection. The OmDet model supports single image inference and batched multi-image inference, with support for different text prompts and classes in the same batch. The model can detect objects based on provided class labels and text prompts. For example, it can detect objects such as 'cat', 'remote', 'boat', 'statue', and 'trees' in images. The model outputs bounding boxes, class logits, and confidence scores for detected objects. Performance examples include detecting a remote with confidence 0.77, a cat with confidence 0.72, a boat with confidence 0.32, and a statue with confidence 0.73. The model uses a processor for input preparation and post-processing of outputs, including bounding box normalization and non-maximum suppression with a threshold of 0.3. The model is available through the Hugging Face model hub under the identifier 'omlab/omdet-turbo-swin-tiny-hf'.

instruction: How can I check if all the required components are correctly installed?
description: This is a direct GGUF conversion of the 13b-0.9.7-dev variant. The model files can be used with a custom node. The required model files should be placed in specific folders: the main model named ltxv-13b-0.9.7-dev should be placed in the ComfyUI/models/diffusion_models folder and can be downloaded as a GGUF file from this repository; the text encoder named T5-V1.1-XXL-Encoder should be placed in the ComfyUI/models/text_encoders folder; and the VAE named ltxv-13b-0.9.7-vae should be placed in the ComfyUI/models/vae folder. As this is a quantized model and not a finetune, all the original restrictions and license terms still apply. ComfyUI now supports GGUF files natively, so users should update ComfyUI to the latest version, and if issues persist, update all the nodes in the workflow. Other T5 clips may also work, and users can choose one they prefer, available as safetensors or GGUF files. The best tested option is the T5 v1.1 xxl version. Loras are compatible but require following the steps in the example workflow, and torchcompile should not be used with loras. Teacache works with ltx but is currently not very effective. In testing, the rel_l1_thresh only works at 0.01, and even then, it causes noticeable quality drops, so it is best to leave it disabled.

instruction: Find me similar articles about the impact of training data diversity on model performance.
description: ModernBERT is a modernized bidirectional encoder-only Transformer model (BERT-style) pre-trained on 2 trillion tokens of English and code data with a native context length of up to 8,192 tokens. It incorporates architectural improvements such as Rotary Positional Embeddings (RoPE) for long-context support, Local-Global Alternating Attention for efficiency on long inputs, and Unpadding and Flash Attention for efficient inference. ModernBERT is designed for tasks requiring processing of long documents, such as retrieval, classification, and semantic search within large corpora. It is suitable for a wide range of downstream tasks, including code retrieval and hybrid (text + code) semantic search. The model is available in two sizes: one with 22 layers and 149 million parameters, and another with 28 layers and 395 million parameters. ModernBERT was evaluated across a range of tasks, including natural language understanding (GLUE), general retrieval (BEIR), long-context retrieval (MLDR), and code retrieval (CodeSearchNet and StackQA). On GLUE, ModernBERT-base surpasses other similarly-sized encoder models, and ModernBERT-large is second only to Deberta-v3-large. For general retrieval tasks, ModernBERT performs well on BEIR in both single-vector (DPR-style) and multi-vector (ColBERT-style) settings. Thanks to the inclusion of code data in its training mixture, ModernBERT achieves new state-of-the-art code retrieval results on CodeSearchNet and StackQA. ModernBERT’s strong results, coupled with its efficient runtime on long-context inputs, demonstrate that encoder-only models can be significantly improved through modern architectural choices and extensive pretraining on diversified data sources. The model uses an encoder-only, Pre-Norm Transformer with GeGLU activations. It was pre-trained up to 1,024 tokens and then extended to 8,192 tokens. The training data consists of 2 trillion tokens of English text and code. The model was trained using the StableAdamW optimizer with trapezoidal LR scheduling and 1-sqrt decay, on 8x H100 GPUs. ModernBERT’s training data is primarily English and code, so performance may be lower for other languages. While it can handle long sequences efficiently, using the full 8,192 tokens window may be slower than short-context inference. Like any large language model, ModernBERT may produce representations that reflect biases present in its training data. Verify critical or sensitive outputs before relying on them. The model is released under the Apache 2.0 license.

instruction: What are the recommended steps for processing a histology tile set for classification?
description: H0-mini is a lightweight foundation model for histology developed by a team of researchers. The model is a Vision Transformer Base/14 that was distilled from H-optimus-0 (ViT-g/14) using the DINOv2 self-supervised distillation method on PanCancer40M, a dataset consisting of 43 million histology tiles extracted from 6,093 histology slides. H0-mini achieves comparable performance to current histology foundation models while significantly reducing inference cost. It also demonstrates strong robustness to variations in staining and scanning protocols. The model can be used with or without fine-tuning for various downstream applications, such as slide-level classification using multiple-instance learning algorithms like ABMIL. For feature extraction, the CLS token is recommended as input for downstream tasks, and concatenating the CLS token features with the average of patch token features may improve performance on some tasks. The model can be used to extract features from histology images for applications such as ROI classification, slide classification, and segmentation. The pre-training dataset includes tile coordinates provided in .txt files, with each file containing the paths to tiles for a given slide. These files include the cohort name, slide ID, and tile coordinates at a specific deepzoom level. The model requires software dependencies including torch version 2.0.0 or higher, torchvision version 0.15.0 or higher, and xformers version 0.0.18 or higher. If using this model, users are requested to cite the associated work titled 'Distilling foundation models for robust and efficient models in digital pathology' by Alexandre Filiot and colleagues. The work was supported by computing resources from the High-Performance Computing (HPC) resources of IDRIS under specific allocations. The model was built upon a repository licensed under the Apache License 2.0. The results are partly based on data generated by the TCGA Research Network.

instruction: Can you help me understand the meaning behind this visual?
description: Gemma is a family of lightweight, state-of-the-art open models from Google, built from the same research and technology used to create the Gemini models. Gemma 3 models are multimodal, handling text and image input and generating text output, with open weights for both pre-trained variants and instruction-tuned variants. Gemma 3 has a large, 128K context window, multilingual support in over 140 languages, and is available in more sizes than previous versions. Gemma 3 models are well-suited for a variety of text generation and image understanding tasks, including question answering, summarization, and reasoning. Their relatively small size makes it possible to deploy them in environments with limited resources such as laptops, desktops or your own cloud infrastructure, democratizing access to state of the art AI models and helping foster innovation for everyone. The input includes text strings such as questions, prompts, or documents to be summarized, and images normalized to 896 x 896 resolution and encoded to 256 tokens each. The total input context is 128K tokens for the 4B, 12B, and 27B sizes, and 32K tokens for the 1B size. The output is generated text in response to the input, such as an answer to a question, analysis of image content, or a summary of a document, with a total output context of 8192 tokens. The models were trained on a dataset of text data that includes a wide variety of sources. The 27B model was trained with 14 trillion tokens, the 12B model with 12 trillion tokens, the 4B model with 4 trillion tokens, and the 1B model with 2 trillion tokens. The training data includes web documents, code, mathematics, and images. The combination of these diverse data sources is crucial for training a powerful multimodal model that can handle a wide variety of different tasks and data formats. Rigorous data cleaning and filtering methods were applied to the training data, including CSAM filtering, sensitive data filtering, and additional methods based on content quality and safety. Gemma was trained using Tensor Processing Unit (TPU) hardware (TPUv4p, TPUv5p, and TPUv5e). TPUs offer advantages in performance, memory, scalability, and cost-effectiveness for training vision-language models. Training was done using JAX and ML Pathways. JAX allows researchers to take advantage of the latest generation of hardware, including TPUs, for faster and more efficient training of large models. ML Pathways is Google's effort to build artificially intelligent systems capable of generalizing across multiple tasks. Together, JAX and ML Pathways allow a single Python process to orchestrate the entire training run, simplifying the development workflow. The models were evaluated against a large collection of different datasets and metrics to cover different aspects of text generation, including reasoning and factuality, STEM and code, multilingual, and multimodal tasks. Evaluation results showed major improvements in the categories of child safety, content safety, and representational harms relative to previous Gemma models. The models have certain limitations, including the influence of training data quality and diversity, context and task complexity, language ambiguity and nuance, factual accuracy, and common sense. Ethical considerations include bias and fairness, misinformation and misuse, transparency and accountability, and privacy violations. The models are intended for a wide range of applications, including content creation and communication, research and education, and knowledge exploration. Risks identified include the perpetuation of biases, generation of harmful content, misuse for malicious purposes, and privacy violations. Mitigations include continuous monitoring, content safety safeguards, developer and end-user education, and adherence to privacy regulations.

instruction: Does this image suggest the person is a young adult or a middle-aged individual?
description: Age-Classification-SigLIP2 is an image classification vision-language encoder model fine-tuned from google/siglip2-base-patch16-224 for a single-label classification task. It is designed to predict the age group of a person from an image using the SiglipForImageClassification architecture. The model categorizes images into five age groups: Class 0: 'Child 0-12', Class 1: 'Teenager 13-20', Class 2: 'Adult 21-44', Class 3: 'Middle Age 45-64', and Class 4: 'Aged 65+'. The model achieves a precision of 0.9744 for the 'Child 0-12' class, 0.8675 for the 'Teenager 13-20' class, 0.9053 for the 'Adult 21-44' class, 0.9059 for the 'Middle Age 45-64' class, and 0.9144 for the 'Aged 65+' class. The corresponding recall values are 0.9562, 0.7032, 0.9769, 0.8317, and 0.8397, respectively. The F1-scores are 0.9652, 0.7768, 0.9397, 0.8672, and 0.8755, with support counts of 2193, 1779, 9999, 3785, and 1260. The overall accuracy of the model is 0.9109. The model is intended for use in demographic analysis, health and fitness applications, security and access control, retail and marketing, and forensics and surveillance. It is designed to assist in age-based health recommendations, age verification in digital systems, and age estimation for security purposes, as well as to help businesses and researchers analyze age distribution and enhance personalized customer experiences.

instruction: Find similar images to the text 'a cat sitting on a windowsill in the sun'?
description: Jina CLIP v2 is a general-purpose multilingual multimodal embedding model for text and images. Multimodal embeddings enable searching and understanding data across different modalities through a coherent representation, serving as the backbone of neural information retrieval and multimodal GenAI applications. Built upon recent advancements, Jina CLIP v2 features several significant improvements. It shows a 3% performance improvement over v1 in both text-image and text-text retrieval tasks. The text encoder can serve as an effective multilingual long-context dense retriever, performing on par with the frontier model `jina-embeddings-v3` on MTEB. It supports 89 languages for multilingual-image retrieval, showing up to 4% improvement compared to `nllb-clip-large-siglip` on multilingual image retrieval tasks. It supports 512x512 input image resolution, a significant increase from v1's 224x224, enabling better processing of detailed images, improved feature extraction, and more accurate recognition of fine-grained visual elements. It allows users to truncate the output dimensions of both text and image embeddings from 1024 down to 64, reducing storage and processing overhead while maintaining strong performance. Measuring 0.9B parameters, Jina CLIP v2 combines two powerful encoders: the text encoder `Jina-XLM-RoBERTa` and the vision encoder `EVA02-L14`. The text encoder supports 8,192 tokens as input and uses mean pooling, while the image encoder accepts 512x512 pixel inputs and uses CLS pooling. Both encoders have 24 layers. The text encoder uses FlashAttention2, and the image encoder uses xFormers for attention mechanisms. The minimum and maximum output dimensions for both encoders are 64 and 1,024, respectively. The text encoder supports 89 languages. The image encoder uses a patch size of 14x14. These encoders are jointly trained to create aligned representations of images and text. CLIP-like models have established themselves as the backbone for general-purpose multimodal applications. Jina CLIP v2 aims to break down language barriers to deliver more accurate cross-modal understanding and retrieval, making multimodal search and retrieval more powerful and accessible to developers worldwide.

instruction: Please extract the spoken text from this audio file recorded in Brazilian Portuguese.
description: This model is a fine-tuned version of distil-whisper-large-v3 for automatic speech recognition (ASR) in Brazilian Portuguese. It was trained using the Common Voice 16 dataset in conjunction with a private dataset transcribed using Whisper Large v3. The model aims to perform automatic speech transcription in Brazilian Portuguese with high accuracy. By combining data from Common Voice 16 with an automatically transcribed private dataset, the model achieved a Word Error Rate (WER) of 8.221% on the validation set of Common Voice 16. The model is a speech recognition model based on distil-whisper-large-v3. It supports the language Brazilian Portuguese (pt-BR) and is distributed under the MIT license. It was fine-tuned from the model distil-whisper/distil-large-v3.

instruction: I need a summary of this article for a presentation—can you help make it more concise?
description: BART is a large-sized model pre-trained on the English language and fine-tuned on the CNN Daily Mail dataset. It was introduced in the paper by Lewis et al. and first released in 2019. The model is designed for natural language generation, translation, and comprehension tasks. It supports summarization, as demonstrated by its ability to generate concise summaries of input text. The model's architecture is based on a denoising sequence-to-sequence pre-training approach. Performance results are not explicitly detailed in the description. The model includes explicit caveats related to its application context, such as the example of generating a summary of a news article about Liana Barrientos, who is facing legal charges related to multiple marriages and an immigration scam. The model is available through the Hugging Face Transformers library and can be used with a summarization pipeline.

instruction: What are the typical signs and symptoms of early-stage Alzheimer's disease?
description: Llama3.1-Aloe-Beta-8B is an open healthcare language model (LLM) that achieves state-of-the-art performance on several medical tasks. Aloe Beta is available in four model sizes, all trained using the same recipe on top of two different model families: Llama3.1 and Qwen2.5. Aloe is trained on 20 medical tasks, resulting in a robust and versatile healthcare model. Evaluations show Aloe models to be among the best in their class. When combined with a RAG system, the 7B and 8B versions get close to the performance of closed models like MedPalm-2 and GPT-4. With the same RAG system, Llama3.1-Aloe-Beta-70B and Qwen2.5-Aloe-Beta-72B outperform those private alternatives, producing state-of-the-art results. Aloe-8B-Beta is the latest iteration in the Aloe family, building and improving on the success of its predecessor. Beta more than triples the training data used by Alpha, for a total of 1.8B tokens, including a wider variety of medical tasks and instructions such as text summarization, explanation, diagnosis, text classification, and treatment recommendation. To mitigate catastrophic forgetting and enable the model to effectively learn new capabilities like function calling, a diverse set of high-quality general-purpose data, constituting 20% of the total training set, was incorporated. The curated data includes some of the highest-quality content available across a range of topics, including mathematics, programming, STEM, and very long instructions (> 8k tokens), to enrich the model's adaptability and comprehension across diverse domains. Beta also boosts the alignment and safety stages with respect to Alpha. This includes a red-teaming dataset (available soon). Complete training details, model merging configurations, and all training data (including synthetically generated data) can be found in the documentation. Aloe comes with a healthcare-specific risk assessment to facilitate the safe use and deployment of such systems. Aloe Beta is a causal decoder-only transformer language model. It is developed by the High Performance Artificial Intelligence Group at Barcelona Supercomputing Center (BSC). The model is based on Meta Llama 3.1 8B originally released under the Meta license. All Aloe modifications are available under a CC-BY-NC-4.0 license for non-commercial use, research purposes only. The model is primarily trained on English data, though it is capable of handling other languages, which have not been formally evaluated. Aloe Beta has been tested on the most popular healthcare QA datasets, with and without Medprompt inference technique. Results show competitive performance, achieving state-of-the-art results within models of the same size. The Beta model has been developed to excel in several different medical tasks. For this reason, the model was evaluated in many different medical tasks. We also compared the performance of the model in the general domain, using the OpenLLM Leaderboard benchmark. Aloe-Beta gets competitive results with the current state-of-the-art general models in the most used general benchmarks and outperforms the medical models. Aloe is intended for use in research as a stepping stone to build better foundational models for healthcare. In production, Aloe should always be used under the supervision of a human expert. These models are not to be used for clinical practice, medical diagnosis, or any other form of direct or indirect healthcare advice. Models are prone to error and can produce toxic content. The use of Aloe models for activities harmful to individuals, such as spam, fraud, or impersonation, is strictly prohibited. Minors should not be left alone to interact with Aloe without supervision. Aloe can produce toxic content under the appropriate prompts and includes multiple undesirable biases. While significant efforts were conducted to mitigate this, model safety cannot be fully guaranteed. We avoid the use of all personal data in our training. We identify at least three risk cases specific to healthcare LLMs: Healthcare professional impersonation, a fraudulent behaviour which currently generates billions of dollars in revenue. A model such as Aloe could be used to increase the efficacy of such deceiving activities, making them more widespread. The main preventive actions are public literacy on the unreliability of digitised information and the importance of medical registration, and legislation enforcing AI-generated content disclaimers. Medical decision-making without professional supervision. While this is already an issue in modern societies (e.g., self-medication), a model such as Aloe, capable of producing high-quality conversational data, can facilitate self-delusion, particularly in the presence of sycophancy. By producing tailored responses, it can also be used to generate actionable answers. Public literacy on the dangers of self-diagnosis is one of the main defenses, together with the introduction of disclaimers and warnings on the models' outputs. Access to information on dangerous substances or procedures. While the literature on sensitive content can already be found on different sources (e.g., libraries, the internet, dark web), LLMs can centralize such access, making it nearly impossible to control the flow of such information. Model alignment can help in that regard, but so far the effects remain insufficient, as jailbreaking methods still overcome it. The training set consists of around 1.8B tokens, having three different types of data: Medical domain datasets, which include data from 20 different medical tasks. Synthetic data, which was expanded by generating high-quality answers using Llama3.1-70B. General data, which includes maths, STEM, code, function calling, and instructions with a very long context. The training parameters include 3 epochs, a sequence length of 16384, the adamw_torch optimizer, a learning rate of 2e-5, a cosine learning rate scheduler, 100 warmup steps, no weight decay, gradient checkpointing, Zero 3, a total batch size of 128, a batch size per device of 1, and 4 gradient accumulation steps. The model was trained using Deepspeed's Zero-3 distributed training on the following hardware: 8B: 32x NVIDIA Hopper H100 64GB of the Marenostrum 5. 70B: 64x NVIDIA Hopper H100 64GB of the Marenostrum 5. The model trained was merged with the Llama-3.1-Instruct model using the DARE_TIES technique. The model is aligned using the Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) technique through a two-step process: General DPO Alignment, which uses a dataset combining medical, general preference, and safety data. We used our dataset, split into five parts, and the model was trained iteratively for one epoch on each chunk, with a learning rate of 2e-7. Red-Teaming Alignment, which further fine-tunes the model to resist a variety of potential attacks, enhancing its robustness and security. The dataset will be shared soon. In this stage, we set the learning rate to 1e-7. We used the library to align the model using 16x NVIDIA Hopper H100 64GB of the Marenostrum 5. Common hyperparameters include a sequence length of 4096, the Fused adam optimizer, a total batch size of 128, a batch size per device of 1, 8 gradient accumulation steps, and a beta of 0.1. The model was tested on a variety of datasets, including CareQA Open, and evaluated using metrics such as Accuracy for multiple-choice question-answering tasks and Rouge1 for the overlap of unigrams between the system and the gold standard. Benchmark results indicate the training conducted on Aloe has boosted its performance above Llama31-8B-Instruct. Llama31-Aloe-Beta-8B also outperforms other medical models like Llama3-OpenBioLLM and Llama3-Med42. All these results make Llama31-Aloe-8B-Beta the best healthcare LLM of its size. With the help of prompting techniques, the performance of Llama3-Aloe-8B-Beta is significantly improved. Medprompting in particular provides a 7% increase in reported accuracy, after which Llama31-Aloe-8B-Beta only lags behind much bigger models like Llama-3.1-70B-Instruct or MedPalm-2. This improvement is mostly consistent across the OpenLLM Leaderboard and the other medical tasks. The environmental impact of the model training includes the use of 32x H100 GPUs for 544 GPU hours for the 8B model and 4500 GPU hours for the 70B model. The hardware provider is the Barcelona Supercomputing Center (BSC), located in Spain. The carbon emitted during training is 34.1 kg of CO2. Aloe Beta has been developed by the research group from the Barcelona Supercomputing Center. Main authors are Dario Garcia-Gasulla, Jordi Bayarri-Planas, and Adrian Tormos. Red teaming efforts were led by Adrian Tormos. If you use this repository in a published work, please cite the corresponding paper as source.

instruction: Design a character with a unique hairstyle and glowing eyes, wearing a futuristic outfit and standing in a cyberpunk city.
description: Animagine XL 4.0, also stylized as Anim4gine, is an anime-themed fine-tuned SDXL model and the latest version in the series. It was retrained from scratch using a dataset of 8.4 million diverse anime-style images, with a knowledge cutoff of January 7th, 2025, and fine-tuned for approximately 2650 GPU hours. The model was trained using the tag ordering method for identity and style training. The optimized version, Animagine XL 4.0 Opt, was further refined with an additional dataset, improving stability, anatomy accuracy, noise reduction, color saturation, and overall color accuracy. These enhancements make Animagine XL 4.0 Opt more consistent and visually appealing while maintaining the signature quality of the series. The model is a diffusion-based text-to-image generative model designed to generate and modify anime-themed images based on text prompts. It is fine-tuned from an unspecified base model. The model supports a variety of tasks, including generating anime-style images with specific character details, styles, and ratings. It is intended for use with the diffusers library and can be integrated into applications using the StableDiffusionXLPipeline. The model was trained using state-of-the-art hardware, including 7 x H100 80GB SXM5 GPUs, with a batch size of 32 and gradient accumulation steps of 2. Training was conducted at a resolution of 1024x1024 using the Adafactor optimizer and mixed precision (fp16). The model supports special tags for quality, score, temporal, and rating control, which influence the artistic style, content safety, and overall image quality. The model has limitations, including a reliance on tag-based prompts, potential difficulties with complex anatomy, lack of text rendering support, and challenges with new or multiple characters. Higher resolutions may show degradation due to training on the original SDXL resolution. The model is licensed under the original SDXL license from Stability AI, which permits commercial use, modifications, and distributions, but prohibits illegal activities, harmful content generation, discrimination, and exploitation. The license requires including a copy of the license, stating any changes, and preserving notices. The model is provided 'AS IS' without warranties.

instruction: Show me educational content that discusses the role of AI in healthcare.
description: This is a model finetuned from BAAI/bge-base-en-v1.5. It maps sentences and paragraphs to a 768-dimensional dense vector space and can be used for semantic textual similarity, semantic search, paraphrase mining, text classification, clustering, and more. The model is a Sentence Transformer with a maximum sequence length of 512 tokens and an output dimensionality of 768. The similarity function used is cosine similarity. The model architecture consists of a Transformer with a BERT model, a Pooling layer with specific configuration parameters, and a Normalize layer. The model was trained on a dataset of 45 samples with anchor and positive columns, and evaluated on a dataset of 5 samples with similar structure. The training process used a loss function with parameters scale set to 20.0 and similarity_fct set to 'cos_sim'. Training hyperparameters included an evaluation strategy of steps, a per-device train and evaluation batch size of 16, a learning rate of 3e-06, a maximum of 24 steps, and a warmup ratio of 0.1. The model was trained using Python version 3.12.8, Sentence Transformers version 3.1.1, Transformers version 4.45.2, PyTorch version 2.2.2, Accelerate version 1.2.1, Datasets version 3.2.0, and Tokenizers version 0.20.3.

instruction: Find the most important phrases in this pathology report related to the diagnosis.
description: BioClinical ModernBERT is a domain-adapted encoder that builds on ModernBERT, incorporating long-context processing and substantial improvements in speed and performance for biomedical and clinical natural language processing (NLP). The model is available in two sizes: one with 150 million parameters and another with 396 million parameters. It is trained on the largest biomedical and clinical corpus to date, consisting of over 53.5 billion tokens. This includes 50.7 billion tokens of biomedical text from PubMed and PMC, and 2.8 billion tokens of clinical text from 20 datasets spanning diverse institutions, domains, and geographic regions. These datasets include clinical notes, radiology reports, pathology reports, and more, with examples from sources such as MIMIC-III, MIMIC-IV, CheXpert Plus, and others. BioClinical ModernBERT is trained in two phases: the first on a large biomedical and clinical corpus with a 30% masking probability, and the second on clinical data only with a 15% masking probability and a 1-sqrt learning rate decay. The model is designed for tasks such as masked language modeling, classification, retrieval, and question answering. It can be used with the Hugging Face Transformers library, and for optimal performance, it is recommended to use it with Flash Attention 2 if supported by the GPU. Unlike some earlier BERT models, BioClinical ModernBERT does not use token type IDs. Evaluation results show that BioClinical ModernBERT outperforms several existing models on tasks such as ChemProt, Phenotype, COS, Social History, and DEID, with performance metrics including 89.9 for ChemProt, 58.1 for Phenotype, 95.1 for COS, 58.5 for Social History, and 82.7 for DEID in the base version, and 90.8 for ChemProt, 60.8 for Phenotype, 95.1 for COS, 57.1 for Social History, and 83.8 for DEID in the large version. The model weights and training checkpoints are released under the MIT license. If used in research, it should be cited with the provided citation information. This project was a collaboration between members of the Dana-Farber Cancer Institute, LightOn, MIT, OpenEvidence, and Microsoft.

instruction: I need to know if there are any disruptions in the flow of speech in this clip.
description: This model implements a speech fluency classification system based on the Whisper Large v3 architecture, as described in the paper 'Vox-Profile: A Speech Foundation Model Benchmark for Characterizing Diverse Speaker and Speech Traits'. The model is designed to classify speech segments as either 'fluent' or 'disfluent' using a 3-second window with a 1-second step size. When disfluent speech is detected, the model further classifies the type of disfluency into one of the following categories: 'Block', 'Prolongation', 'Sound Repetition', 'Word Repetition', or 'Interjection'. The model is intended for use in characterizing speech traits and is not suitable for clinical or diagnostic applications, surveillance, privacy-invasive uses, or commercial purposes. The model is released under the Open RAIL license, and users are required to respect the privacy and consent of data subjects and comply with relevant laws and regulations in their jurisdictions.

instruction: I need to extract the main subject from this image for a presentation—can you help?
description: U-2-Netp is a lightweight version of the U2Net model designed for efficient and effective image segmentation tasks, especially for generating masks. It retains the core architectural design of U2Net while being optimized for faster inference times and reduced memory usage. The model is based on a simplified version of the original U2Net architecture, designed to be more lightweight while still achieving high performance in segmentation tasks. The model consists of several stages with down-sampling and up-sampling paths, using Residual U-blocks (RSU) for enhanced feature representation. The model is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 to match the original U-2-Net model.

instruction: Are there any animals visible in the video?
description: VideoChat-Flash-Qwen2_5-7B_InternVideo2-1B is a model constructed upon InternVideo2-1B and Qwen2.5-7B, using only 16 tokens per frame. It leverages Yarn to extend the context window to 128k, allowing input sequences of up to approximately 10,000 frames. The model is trained predominantly on an English corpus, and as a result, it exhibits basic Chinese comprehension. For optimal performance, it is recommended to use English for interaction. The model achieves a performance of 70.0 on MVBench, 58.3 on LongVideoBench, 57.0 on VideoMME (without subtitles), and supports a maximum input of 10,000 frames. Another version of the model achieves 73.2 on MVBench, 64.2 on LongVideoBench, 64.0 on VideoMME (without subtitles), and supports 10,000 frames. A third version achieves 73.4 on MVBench, 66.5 on LongVideoBench, 63.5 on VideoMME (without subtitles), and supports 50,000 frames. A fourth version achieves 74.3 on MVBench, 64.5 on LongVideoBench, 65.1 on VideoMME (without subtitles), and supports 10,000 frames. A final version achieves 74.0 on MVBench, 64.7 on LongVideoBench, 65.3 on VideoMME (without subtitles), and supports 10,000 frames. The model supports single-turn and multi-turn video-based conversations, where users can ask questions about the content of a video. It can describe a video in detail and answer specific questions about the number of people appearing in the video. The model uses a hierarchical compression approach for long-context video modeling. It is implemented using the Transformers library and requires several dependencies including av, imageio, decord, and opencv-python. Optional installation includes flash-attn. The model can be configured to use global compression or not, with specific settings for compression type, layers, and image token ratios. Evaluation settings include a maximum number of frames and a generation configuration with parameters such as do_sample, temperature, max_new_tokens, top_p, and num_beams. The model is described in the paper 'VideoChat-Flash: Hierarchical Compression for Long-Context Video Modeling' by Li et al., published as an arXiv preprint in 2024.

instruction: What's the best approach to find relevant resources in Swahili and Telugu?
description: This is a learned sparse retrieval model designed for efficient search relevance and retrieval. It encodes documents into 105879-dimensional sparse vectors. For queries, it uses a tokenizer and a weight look-up table to generate sparse vectors. In this representation, the non-zero dimension index corresponds to a token in the vocabulary, and the weight indicates the token's importance.
