The similarity score between a query and a document is calculated as the inner product of their sparse vectors. The model is intended to be used with OpenSearch's neural sparse feature, which supports learned sparse retrieval using a Lucene inverted index. The model is evaluated on the MIRACL benchmark, excluding Thai due to limitations with the uncased backbone. It achieves an average NDCG@10 score of 0.629 and an average FLOPS of 1.3. When using a prune_ratio of 0.1, the model maintains a high NDCG@10 score of 0.626 with reduced FLOPS of 0.8 and a smaller embedding size of 75. The model is multilingual and supports a range of languages including Bengali, Telugu, Spanish, French, Indonesian, Hindi, Russian, Arabic, Chinese, Persian, Japanese, Finnish, Swahili, Korean, and English. It is recommended to use the model with max_ratio pruning for optimal performance. The model is available on the HuggingFace Hub and can be used with Sentence Transformers or directly with the HuggingFace Transformers library. The model has 160 million parameters and is inference-free for retrieval. The model includes an IDF (inverse document frequency) file for weighting query tokens, which is used during encoding. Special tokens are excluded from the sparse vector during post-processing. The model is designed to be used with OpenSearch's high-level API for indexing and search, but it can also be used outside the cluster with HuggingFace's API. The model is open source and licensed under an unspecified license. Copyright is held by OpenSearch Contributors.

instruction: Based on the information here, which product had the highest customer satisfaction rating?
description: This model is a TAPAS base model fine-tuned on WikiSQL in a supervised fashion. It has two versions: the default version corresponds to the `tapas_wikisql_sqa_inter_masklm_base_reset` checkpoint, and the other version is `no_reset`, corresponding to `tapas_wikisql_sqa_inter_masklm_base`. The model was pre-trained on masked language modeling (MLM) and an additional step called intermediate pre-training, followed by fine-tuning on SQA and WikiSQL. It uses relative position embeddings, resetting the position index at every cell of the table. The non-default version uses absolute position embeddings. TAPAS is a BERT-like transformer model pre-trained on a large corpus of English data from Wikipedia in a self-supervised fashion. It was pre-trained on raw tables and associated texts with no human labeling, using an automatic process to generate inputs and labels. The pre-training objectives include masked language modeling, where 15% of the words in a flattened table and associated context are randomly masked, and the model must predict the masked words. The second objective is intermediate pre-training, where the model is trained on a balanced dataset of millions of syntactically created examples to predict whether a sentence is supported or refuted by a table. This allows the model to learn an inner representation of the English language used in tables and associated texts, which can be used for downstream tasks such as answering questions about a table or determining whether a sentence is entailed or refuted by the contents of a table. Fine-tuning involves adding a cell selection head and aggregation head on top of the pre-trained model and jointly training these heads with the base model on SQA and WikiSQL. The model is intended for answering questions related to a table. The texts are lowercased and tokenized using WordPiece with a vocabulary size of 30,000. The input format is [CLS] Question [SEP] Flattened table [SEP]. The WikiSQL dataset was first converted into the format of SQA using automatic conversion scripts. The model was fine-tuned on 32 Cloud TPU v3 cores for 50,000 steps with a maximum sequence length of 512 and a batch size of 512. Fine-tuning took approximately 10 hours. The optimizer used is Adam with a learning rate of 6.17164e-5 and a warmup ratio of 0.1424. The team releasing TAPAS did not write a model card for this model; this model card was written by the Hugging Face team and contributors.
domain: Natural Language Processing Table Question Answering

instruction: Based on past performance, how many units might be sold next quarter?
description: TabPFNMix regressor is a tabular foundation model pre-trained on purely synthetic datasets sampled from a mix of random regressors. It is based on a 12-layer encoder-decoder Transformer with 37 million parameters. The pre-training strategy incorporates in-context learning, similar to that used by TabPFN and TabForestPFN. The model is designed for regression tasks and supports fine-tuning and inference for tabular data. It is implemented within the AutoGluon framework, and users can install AutoGluon via pip. The model can be used by importing pandas and the TabularPredictor class from autogluon.tabular, loading training and test data, specifying hyperparameters, and fitting the predictor to the training data. The model's performance can be evaluated using a leaderboard on test data. The model is associated with the Apache-2.0 License. Users are encouraged to cite the associated papers if they find TabPFNMix useful for their research.

instruction: What kind of emotional response does this audio evoke according to your analysis?
description: This model is designed for speech emotion recognition, leveraging the Whisper Large V3 model to classify audio recordings into emotional categories, including Angry, Disgust, Fearful, Happy, Neutral, Sad, and Surprised. The model is fine-tuned for audio classification tasks and maps emotion labels to numeric IDs for training and evaluation. The training dataset includes multiple sources and contains labeled recordings with the following distribution: 752 samples for Sad, 752 for Happy, 752 for Angry, 716 for Neutral, 652 for Disgust, 652 for Fearful, 652 for Surprised, and 192 for Calm. The Calm emotion was excluded from training due to its underrepresentation. Audio preprocessing involves loading files with Librosa and extracting features using the Whisper Feature Extractor, which standardizes and normalizes the audio for model input. The model was trained with a learning rate of 5e-05, a train batch size of 2, an eval batch size of 2, a random seed of 42, and gradient accumulation steps of 5, resulting in an effective batch size of 10. Training used the Adam optimizer with betas (0.9, 0.999) and epsilon 1e-08, a linear learning rate scheduler with a warmup ratio of 0.1, and 25 epochs with native AMP for mixed precision. Training was monitored using Wandb. Evaluation metrics after training include a loss of 0.5008, accuracy of 0.9199, precision of 0.9230, recall of 0.9199, and an F1 score of 0.9198. These metrics indicate strong performance in identifying emotional states from speech data. The model is implemented using the Transformers library and requires PyTorch, with specific versions including Transformers 4.44.2, PyTorch 2.4.1+cu121, Datasets 3.0.0, and Tokenizers 0.19.1.

instruction: Find any objects in this image that fit the description 'a mountain with snow at the top'.
description: OWL-ViT, short for Vision Transformer for Open-World Localization, is a zero-shot text-conditioned object detection model that allows querying an image with one or multiple text queries. It was proposed by Matthias Minderer, Alexey Gritsenko, Austin Stone, Maxim Neumann, Dirk Weissenborn, Alexey Dosovitskiy, Aravindh Mahendran, Anurag Arnab, Mostafa Dehghani, Zhuoran Shen, Xiao Wang, Xiaohua Zhai, Thomas Kipf, and Neil Houlsby. OWL-ViT uses CLIP as its multi-modal backbone, combining a ViT-like Transformer for visual features and a causal language model for text features. To enable detection, the final token pooling layer of the vision model is removed, and a lightweight classification and box head is attached to each transformer output token. Open-vocabulary classification is achieved by replacing the fixed classification layer weights with class-name embeddings from the text model. The CLIP backbone is trained from scratch and fine-tuned end-to-end with the classification and box heads on standard detection datasets using a bipartite matching loss. The model uses a CLIP backbone with a ViT-B/32 Transformer architecture as an image encoder and a masked self-attention Transformer as a text encoder. These encoders are trained to maximize the similarity of (image, text) pairs via a contrastive loss. The CLIP backbone is trained from scratch and fine-tuned together with the box and class prediction heads with an object detection objective. The CLIP backbone of the model was trained on publicly available image-caption data, including data crawled from the internet and pre-existing image datasets. The prediction heads of OWL-ViT, along with the CLIP backbone, are fine-tuned on publicly available object detection datasets. The model is intended as a research output for the research community, enabling researchers to better understand and explore zero-shot, text-conditioned object detection. The primary intended users are AI researchers, who are expected to use the model to study robustness, generalization, and other capabilities, biases, and constraints of computer vision models.

instruction: What are the primary emotions conveyed in this social media post?
description: GLiNER-Multitask is a multi-task prompt-tunable model designed to extract various pieces of information from plain text based on a user-provided custom prompt. It leverages a bidirectional transformer encoder, similar to BERT, which ensures both high generalization and compute efficiency despite its compact size. The model supports a range of information extraction tasks, including named entity recognition, relation extraction, summarization, sentiment extraction, key-phrase extraction, question-answering, open information extraction, and text classification. The gliner-multitask-v1.0 variant achieves state-of-the-art performance on NER zero-shot benchmarks, demonstrating robustness and flexibility. On the CrossNER datasets, it achieves an average F1 score of 0.6325, compared to 0.6276 for the v0.5 variant. For relation extraction, it achieves an average F1 score of 0.749442 on the CrossRe dataset and 0.76598 on the DocRed dataset. For open information extraction, it achieves an F1 score of 0.4168 on the WiRe57_343-manual-oie dataset. For question-answering, it achieves an F1 score of 0.712745 on the SQuAD 2.0 dataset. For text classification, it achieves a micro F1 score of 0.7907 on the IMDb dataset and 0.7436 on the AG News dataset. The model also demonstrates performance on various NER benchmarks, achieving an F1 score of 0.6838 on CoNLL 2003, 0.7387 on bc5cdr, and 0.7845 on WikiNeural. The model is designed to be used with a custom prompt to extract specific information, and it can be fine-tuned for specific tasks. It is available for use through the GLiNER library, which can be installed via pip. The model is lightweight and efficient, making it suitable for a wide range of applications. However, it may have limitations in certain domains or with specific types of text, and its performance may vary depending on the task and dataset.

instruction: Please make a voice version of this poem for a school project.
description: Llasa is a text-to-speech (TTS) system that extends the text-based LLaMA (1B, 3B, and 8B) language model by incorporating speech tokens from the XCodec2 codebook, which contains 65,536 tokens. The model was trained on a dataset comprising 250,000 hours of Chinese-English speech data. Llasa is capable of generating speech either solely from input text or by utilizing a given speech prompt. The model supports two modes of operation: speech synthesis from text alone and speech synthesis that incorporates a provided speech prompt to guide the output. The model is based on the LLaMA architecture and is trained with a maximum sequence length of 2048 tokens. The model is available in different sizes, including Llasa-1B. The model uses the XCodec2 model to encode and decode speech waveforms. The model is licensed under the CC BY-NC 4.0 License, which prohibits free commercial use due to ethics and privacy concerns. The codebase is strictly prohibited from being used for any illegal purposes in any country or region.

instruction: Please detect if there are any overlapping voices in this conversation.
description: Qwen2-Audio is a state-of-the-art small-scale multimodal model (AudioLM) that processes both audio and text inputs, enabling voice interactions without the need for ASR modules. It supports English, Chinese, and major European languages, and provides voice chat and audio analysis capabilities for local use cases. These include speaker identification and response, speech translation and transcription, mixed audio and noise detection, and music and sound analysis. The model is designed to run locally on edge devices using the Nexa-SDK, which is an open-sourced, local on-device inference framework supporting text generation, image generation, vision-language models, audio-language models, speech-to-text, and text-to-speech capabilities. Users can run Qwen2-Audio locally by installing Nexa-SDK and executing the command 'nexa run qwen2audio', which by default uses the q4_K_M quantization requiring 4.2GB of RAM. The model can be used via terminal by dragging and dropping an audio file or entering a file path, with optional text prompts to guide analysis. Alternatively, it can be used with a local UI via the command 'nexa run qwen2audio -st'. Qwen2-Audio is suitable for use cases such as voice chat, where it can answer daily questions, offer suggestions, perform speaker identification, provide speech translation, and detect background noise. For audio analysis, it supports information extraction, audio summary, speech transcription and expansion, mixed audio and noise detection, and music and sound analysis. Performance benchmarks show that Qwen2-Audio significantly outperforms previous state-of-the-art models and Qwen-Audio across all tasks.

instruction: How would you summarize the visual information in this graphic?
description: Pix2Struct is an image encoder - text decoder model trained on image-text pairs for various tasks, including image captioning and visual question answering. It is designed for purely visual language understanding and can be fine-tuned on tasks involving visually-situated language. Pix2Struct is pretrained by learning to parse masked screenshots of web pages into simplified HTML. The web provides a large source of pretraining data well suited to the diversity of downstream tasks. This objective subsumes common pretraining signals such as OCR, language modeling, and image captioning. The model introduces a variable-resolution input representation and a more flexible integration of language and vision inputs, where language prompts such as questions are rendered directly on top of the input image. Pix2Struct has achieved state-of-the-art results in six out of nine tasks across four domains: documents, illustrations, user interfaces, and natural images. This model has been fine-tuned on visual question answering (VQA) tasks and supports question answering in a format that includes multiple choices. The model was originally contributed by Kenton Lee, Mandar Joshi et al. and added to the Hugging Face ecosystem. If you use this model, you should consider citing the original paper titled 'Pix2Struct: Screenshot Parsing as Pretraining for Visual Language Understanding' published on arXiv in 2022.

instruction: Can you explain how the model predicts masked words during training?
description: This model is a TAPAS mini model fine-tuned on Sequential Question Answering (SQA). It has two versions: the default version corresponds to the `tapas_sqa_inter_masklm_mini_reset` checkpoint, and the other version is `no_reset`, corresponding to `tapas_sqa_inter_masklm_mini`. The model was pre-trained on masked language modeling (MLM) and an additional step called intermediate pre-training, followed by fine-tuning. It uses relative position embeddings, resetting the position index at every cell of the table. The non-default version uses absolute position embeddings. TAPAS is a BERT-like transformer model pre-trained on a large corpus of English data from Wikipedia in a self-supervised manner. It was pre-trained with two objectives: masked language modeling, where 15% of the words in a flattened table and associated context are randomly masked and the model must predict them, and intermediate pre-training, where the model is trained to classify whether a sentence is supported or refuted by the contents of a table using a balanced dataset of millions of syntactically created examples. This allows the model to learn a bidirectional representation of tables and associated texts, which can be used for downstream tasks such as answering questions about a table or determining if a sentence is entailed or refuted by the table. Fine-tuning involves adding a cell selection head on top of the pre-trained model and jointly training this head with the base model on SQA. The model can be used for answering questions related to a table in a conversational setup. The texts are lowercased and tokenized using WordPiece with a vocabulary size of 30,000. The model inputs are of the form: [CLS] Question [SEP] Flattened table [SEP]. The model was fine-tuned on 32 Cloud TPU v3 cores for 200,000 steps with a maximum sequence length of 512 and a batch size of 128. Fine-tuning takes approximately 20 hours. The optimizer used is Adam with a learning rate of 1.25e-5 and a warmup ratio of 0.2. An inductive bias is added so that the model only selects cells of the same column, as reflected by the `select_one_column` parameter of `TapasConfig`. The model's performance on the SQA development set for the mini size is 0.4574 for the noreset version and 0.5148 for the reset version. The team releasing TAPAS did not write a model card for this model; this model card was written by the Hugging Face team and contributors.
domain: Natural Language Processing Table Question Answering

instruction: Please generate a caption that captures both the action and camera work in this scene.
description: SkyCaptioner-V1 is a structural video captioning model designed to generate high-quality, structural descriptions for video data. It integrates specialized sub-expert models and multimodal large language models (MLLMs) with human annotations to address the limitations of general captioners in capturing professional film-related details. The model combines general video descriptions from MLLMs with sub-expert captioners, such as shot types, shot angles, shot positions, and camera motions. It employs knowledge distillation to distill expertise from sub-expert captioners into a unified model. The model supports generating dense captions for text-to-video (T2V) and concise prompts for image-to-video (I2V) tasks. SkyCaptioner-V1 is trained on approximately 2 million high-quality, concept-balanced videos curated from 10 million raw samples. It is fine-tuned on Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct with a global batch size of 512 across 32 A800 GPUs. The training uses AdamW with a learning rate of 1e-5 for 2 epochs. The model features a dynamic caption fusion method that adapts output length based on application (T2V/I2V) and employs an LLM model to fuse structural fields into a natural and fluent caption for downstream tasks. SkyCaptioner-V1 demonstrates significant improvements over existing models in key film-specific captioning tasks, particularly in shot-language understanding and domain-specific precision. It outperforms Qwen2.5-VL-72B with +11.2% in shot type, +16.1% in shot angle, and +50.4% in shot position accuracy. It achieves +28.5% accuracy in camera motion compared to Tarsier2-recap-7B (88.8% vs. 41.5%). Its 6DoF motion analysis and active learning pipeline address ambiguities in composite motions. The model achieves 68.8% expression accuracy compared to 54.3% for Tarsier2-recap-7B, leveraging temporal-aware S2D frameworks to capture dynamic facial changes. Overall, SkyCaptioner-V1 achieves an average accuracy of 76.3%, outperforming Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Ins. (51.4%), Qwen2.5-VL-72B-Ins. (58.7%), and Tarsier2-recap-7B (49.4%). It achieves 93.7% accuracy in shot type, 89.8% in shot angle, 83.1% in shot position, 85.3% in camera motion, and 68.8% in expression. The model also performs well in other metrics, including 82.5% in TYPES_type, 75.4% in TYPES_sub_type, 59.3% in appearance, 68.8% in action, 57.5% in position, 80.9% in is_main_subject, 70.5% in environment, and 76.5% in lighting. SkyCaptioner-V1 uses Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct as its caption fusion model to intelligently combine structured caption fields, producing either dense or sparse final captions depending on application requirements. The model can be downloaded from Hugging Face using the huggingface-cli command. It is recommended to use Python 3.10 and CUDA version 12.2 for installation. The model can be run using the vllm_struct_caption.py script for generating structural captions and the vllm_fusion_caption.py script for T2V/I2V caption fusion. The model is based on the research described in the technical report titled 'Skyreels V2: Infinite-Length Film Generative Model' by Guibin Chen and others, available on arXiv.

instruction: I need to build a recommendation system based on the meaning of user queries. Can you help with the first step?
description: This is a model that maps sentences and paragraphs to a 768-dimensional dense vector space, suitable for tasks such as clustering or semantic search. The model is designed to be used as a sentence and short paragraph encoder, generating vectors that capture semantic information for applications like information retrieval, clustering, or sentence similarity. Input text longer than 128 word pieces is truncated by default. The model was developed using a self-supervised contrastive learning objective, where the model is trained to predict which sentence from a set of randomly sampled sentences was actually paired with a given sentence in the training data. The model was fine-tuned on a dataset containing over 1 billion sentence pairs, sourced from multiple datasets and weighted according to a configuration file. The training was conducted on a TPU v3-8 for 920,000 steps with a batch size of 512 (64 per TPU core), using a learning rate of 2e-5 and the AdamW optimizer. The sequence length was limited to 128 tokens, and a learning rate warm-up of 500 steps was applied. The model was developed as part of a project supported by efficient hardware infrastructure and guidance from Google's Flax, JAX, and Cloud teams. The full training script is available in the repository as `train_script.py`. The training data includes a wide range of sources such as citation pairs (abstracts and titles), duplicate question pairs, (question, answer) pairs, (title, body) pairs, (title, answer) pairs, triplets, image captions, and more, with a total of 1,124,818,467 training tuples. The model can be used with the Sentence-Transformers library or directly with HuggingFace Transformers, requiring tokenization, model inference, and pooling operations to generate normalized sentence embeddings.

instruction: Translate the Uzbek phrase 'Bugun juda qiyin kun bo'ldi' into English.
description: The LLaMA-3.1-8B-Instruct-Uz model has been instruction-tuned using a mix of publicly available and synthetically constructed Uzbek and English data to preserve its original knowledge while enhancing its capabilities. This model is designed to support various natural language processing tasks in Uzbek, such as machine translation, summarization, and dialogue systems, ensuring robust performance across these applications. The model can be used with transformers and with the original `llama` codebase. For evaluation, the model was tested on translation tasks using FLORES+ Uz-En / En-Uz datasets, where the dev and test sets were merged to create a larger evaluation dataset for each subset. A specific prompt was used for one-shot Uz-En evaluation, with the positions of the words 'English' and 'Uzbek' changed for En-Uz evaluation. For Uzbek sentiment analysis, the model was evaluated using the **risqaliyevds/uzbek-sentiment-analysis** dataset, with binary labels (0: Negative, 1: Positive) created using the GPT-4o API. A specific prompt was used to determine the sentiment as either 'Positive' or 'Negative'. For Uzbek News Classification, the model was evaluated using the **risqaliyevds/uzbek-zero-shot-classification** dataset, with a prompt asking the model to predict the category of the news article from a list of ten categories. On MMLU, the model was evaluated using a 5-shot template, with the first token generated by the model used to measure accuracy. The model is a quantized version created using llama.cpp.

instruction: How does hypertension typically progress in patients over 60 years of age?
description: Llama3.1-Aloe-Beta-8B is an open healthcare language model (LLM) that achieves state-of-the-art performance on several medical tasks. Aloe Beta is available in four model sizes, all trained using the same recipe on top of two different model families: Llama3.1 and Qwen2.5. Aloe is trained on 20 medical tasks, resulting in a robust and versatile healthcare model. Evaluations show Aloe models to be among the best in their class. When combined with a RAG system, the 7B and 8B versions get close to the performance of closed models like MedPalm-2 and GPT-4. With the same RAG system, Llama3.1-Aloe-Beta-70B and Qwen2.5-Aloe-Beta-72B outperform those private alternatives, producing state-of-the-art results. Aloe-8B-Beta is the latest iteration in the Aloe family, building and improving on the success of its predecessor. Beta more than triples the training data used by Alpha, for a total of 1.8B tokens, including a wider variety of medical tasks and instructions such as text summarization, explanation, diagnosis, text classification, and treatment recommendation. To mitigate catastrophic forgetting and enable the model to effectively learn new capabilities like function calling, a diverse set of high-quality general-purpose data, constituting 20% of the total training set, was incorporated. The curated data includes some of the highest-quality content available across a range of topics, including mathematics, programming, STEM, and very long instructions (> 8k tokens), to enrich the model's adaptability and comprehension across diverse domains. Beta also boosts the alignment and safety stages with respect to Alpha. This includes a red-teaming dataset (available soon). Complete training details, model merging configurations, and all training data (including synthetically generated data) can be found in the documentation. Aloe comes with a healthcare-specific risk assessment to facilitate the safe use and deployment of such systems. Aloe Beta is a causal decoder-only transformer language model. It is developed by the High Performance Artificial Intelligence Group at Barcelona Supercomputing Center (BSC). The model is based on Meta Llama 3.1 8B originally released under the Meta license. All Aloe modifications are available under a CC-BY-NC-4.0 license for non-commercial use, research purposes only. The model is primarily trained on English data, though it is capable of handling other languages, which have not been formally evaluated. Aloe Beta has been tested on the most popular healthcare QA datasets, with and without Medprompt inference technique. Results show competitive performance, achieving state-of-the-art results within models of the same size. The Beta model has been developed to excel in several different medical tasks. For this reason, the model was evaluated in many different medical tasks. We also compared the performance of the model in the general domain, using the OpenLLM Leaderboard benchmark. Aloe-Beta gets competitive results with the current state-of-the-art general models in the most used general benchmarks and outperforms the medical models. Aloe is intended for use in research as a stepping stone to build better foundational models for healthcare. In production, Aloe should always be used under the supervision of a human expert. These models are not to be used for clinical practice, medical diagnosis, or any other form of direct or indirect healthcare advice. Models are prone to error and can produce toxic content. The use of Aloe models for activities harmful to individuals, such as spam, fraud, or impersonation, is strictly prohibited. Minors should not be left alone to interact with Aloe without supervision. Aloe can produce toxic content under the appropriate prompts and includes multiple undesirable biases. While significant efforts were conducted to mitigate this, model safety cannot be fully guaranteed. We avoid the use of all personal data in our training. We identify at least three risk cases specific to healthcare LLMs: Healthcare professional impersonation, a fraudulent behaviour which currently generates billions of dollars in revenue. A model such as Aloe could be used to increase the efficacy of such deceiving activities, making them more widespread. The main preventive actions are public literacy on the unreliability of digitised information and the importance of medical registration, and legislation enforcing AI-generated content disclaimers. Medical decision-making without professional supervision. While this is already an issue in modern societies (e.g., self-medication), a model such as Aloe, capable of producing high-quality conversational data, can facilitate self-delusion, particularly in the presence of sycophancy. By producing tailored responses, it can also be used to generate actionable answers. Public literacy on the dangers of self-diagnosis is one of the main defenses, together with the introduction of disclaimers and warnings on the models' outputs. Access to information on dangerous substances or procedures. While the literature on sensitive content can already be found on different sources (e.g., libraries, the internet, dark web), LLMs can centralize such access, making it nearly impossible to control the flow of such information. Model alignment can help in that regard, but so far the effects remain insufficient, as jailbreaking methods still overcome it. The training set consists of around 1.8B tokens, having three different types of data: Medical domain datasets, which include data from 20 different medical tasks. Synthetic data, which was expanded by generating high-quality answers using Llama3.1-70B. General data, which includes maths, STEM, code, function calling, and instructions with a very long context. The training parameters include 3 epochs, a sequence length of 16384, the adamw_torch optimizer, a learning rate of 2e-5, a cosine learning rate scheduler, 100 warmup steps, no weight decay, gradient checkpointing, Zero 3, a total batch size of 128, a batch size per device of 1, and 4 gradient accumulation steps. The model was trained using Deepspeed's Zero-3 distributed training on the following hardware: 8B: 32x NVIDIA Hopper H100 64GB of the Marenostrum 5. 70B: 64x NVIDIA Hopper H100 64GB of the Marenostrum 5. The model trained was merged with the Llama-3.1-Instruct model using the DARE_TIES technique. The model is aligned using the Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) technique through a two-step process: General DPO Alignment, which uses a dataset combining medical, general preference, and safety data. We used our dataset, split into five parts, and the model was trained iteratively for one epoch on each chunk, with a learning rate of 2e-7. Red-Teaming Alignment, which further fine-tunes the model to resist a variety of potential attacks, enhancing its robustness and security. The dataset will be shared soon. In this stage, we set the learning rate to 1e-7. We used the library to align the model using 16x NVIDIA Hopper H100 64GB of the Marenostrum 5. Common hyperparameters include a sequence length of 4096, the Fused adam optimizer, a total batch size of 128, a batch size per device of 1, 8 gradient accumulation steps, and a beta of 0.1. The model was tested on a variety of datasets, including CareQA Open, and evaluated using metrics such as Accuracy for multiple-choice question-answering tasks and Rouge1 for the overlap of unigrams between the system and the gold standard. Benchmark results indicate the training conducted on Aloe has boosted its performance above Llama31-8B-Instruct. Llama31-Aloe-Beta-8B also outperforms other medical models like Llama3-OpenBioLLM and Llama3-Med42. All these results make Llama31-Aloe-8B-Beta the best healthcare LLM of its size. With the help of prompting techniques, the performance of Llama3-Aloe-8B-Beta is significantly improved. Medprompting in particular provides a 7% increase in reported accuracy, after which Llama31-Aloe-8B-Beta only lags behind much bigger models like Llama-3.1-70B-Instruct or MedPalm-2. This improvement is mostly consistent across the OpenLLM Leaderboard and the other medical tasks. The environmental impact of the model training includes the use of 32x H100 GPUs for 544 GPU hours for the 8B model and 4500 GPU hours for the 70B model. The hardware provider is the Barcelona Supercomputing Center (BSC), located in Spain. The carbon emitted during training is 34.1 kg of CO2. Aloe Beta has been developed by the research group from the Barcelona Supercomputing Center. Main authors are Dario Garcia-Gasulla, Jordi Bayarri-Planas, and Adrian Tormos. Red teaming efforts were led by Adrian Tormos. If you use this repository in a published work, please cite the corresponding paper as source.

instruction: What are the key differences between these two images, and how would you describe them?
description: Gemma is a family of lightweight, state-of-the-art open models from Google, built from the same research and technology used to create the Gemini models. Gemma 3 models are multimodal, handling text and image input and generating text output, with open weights for both pre-trained variants and instruction-tuned variants. Gemma 3 has a large, 128K context window, multilingual support in over 140 languages, and is available in more sizes than previous versions. Gemma 3 models are well-suited for a variety of text generation and image understanding tasks, including question answering, summarization, and reasoning. Their relatively small size makes it possible to deploy them in environments with limited resources such as laptops, desktops or your own cloud infrastructure, democratizing access to state of the art AI models and helping foster innovation for everyone. The input includes text strings such as questions, prompts, or documents to be summarized, and images normalized to 896 x 896 resolution and encoded to 256 tokens each. The total input context is 128K tokens for the 4B, 12B, and 27B sizes, and 32K tokens for the 1B size. The output is generated text in response to the input, such as an answer to a question, analysis of image content, or a summary of a document, with a total output context of 8192 tokens. These models were trained on a dataset of text data that includes a wide variety of sources. The 27B model was trained with 14 trillion tokens, the 12B model with 12 trillion tokens, the 4B model with 4 trillion tokens, and the 1B model with 2 trillion tokens. The training dataset includes web documents, code, mathematics, and images. Web documents ensure the model is exposed to a broad range of linguistic styles, topics, and vocabulary, including content in over 140 languages. Code exposure helps the model learn the syntax and patterns of programming languages, improving its ability to generate and understand code. Mathematics training helps the model learn logical reasoning, symbolic representation, and to address mathematical queries. A wide range of images enables the model to perform image analysis and visual data extraction tasks. Rigorous CSAM filtering was applied at multiple stages in the data preparation process to ensure the exclusion of harmful and illegal content. Automated techniques were used to filter out certain personal information and other sensitive data from training sets. Additional methods were applied for content quality and safety in line with safety policies. Gemma was trained using Tensor Processing Unit (TPU) hardware (TPUv4p, TPUv5p, and TPUv5e). TPUs are specifically designed to handle the massive computations involved in training vision-language models (VLMs), offering performance, memory, scalability, and cost-effectiveness advantages. Training was done using JAX and ML Pathways. JAX allows researchers to take advantage of the latest generation of hardware, including TPUs, for faster and more efficient training of large models. ML Pathways is Google's effort to build artificially intelligent systems capable of generalizing across multiple tasks, suitable for foundation models like these. These models were evaluated against a large collection of different datasets and metrics to cover different aspects of text generation. Evaluation results showed major improvements in the categories of child safety, content safety, and representational harms relative to previous Gemma models. All testing was conducted without safety filters to evaluate the model capabilities and behaviors. For both text-to-text and image-to-text, and across all model sizes, the model produced minimal policy violations and showed significant improvements over previous Gemma models' performance with respect to ungrounded inferences. A limitation of the evaluations was that they included only English language prompts. These models have certain limitations that users should be aware of. The quality and diversity of the training data significantly influence the model's capabilities. Biases or gaps in the training data can lead to limitations in the model's responses. The scope of the training dataset determines the subject areas the model can handle effectively. Models are better at tasks that can be framed with clear prompts and instructions. Open-ended or highly complex tasks might be challenging. A model's performance can be influenced by the amount of context provided (longer context generally leads to better outputs, up to a certain point). Natural language is inherently complex. Models might struggle to grasp subtle nuances, sarcasm, or figurative language. Models generate responses based on information they learned from their training datasets, but they are not knowledge bases. They may generate incorrect or outdated factual statements. Models rely on statistical patterns in language. They might lack the ability to apply common sense reasoning in certain situations. The development of vision-language models (VLMs) raises several ethical concerns. VLMs trained on large-scale, real-world text and image data can reflect socio-cultural biases embedded in the training material. These models underwent careful scrutiny, input data pre-processing, and posterior evaluations. VLMs can be misused to generate text that is false, misleading, or harmful. Guidelines are provided for responsible use with the model. Transparency and accountability are emphasized by summarizing details on the models' architecture, capabilities, limitations, and evaluation processes. A responsibly developed open model offers the opportunity to share innovation by making VLM technology accessible to developers and researchers across the AI ecosystem. Risks identified include the perpetuation of biases, generation of harmful content, misuse for malicious purposes, and privacy violations. Mitigations include continuous monitoring, content safety safeguards, developer and end-user education, and adherence to privacy regulations. At the time of release, this family of models provides high-performance open vision-language model implementations designed from the ground up for responsible AI development compared to similarly sized models. Using the benchmark evaluation metrics described in this document, these models have shown to provide superior performance to other, comparably-sized open model alternatives.

instruction: What is the role of the 'select_one_column' parameter in the model configuration?
description: This model is a TAPAS medium model fine-tuned on Sequential Question Answering (SQA). It has two versions: the default version corresponds to the `tapas_sqa_inter_masklm_medium_reset` checkpoint, and the other version is `no_reset`, corresponding to `tapas_sqa_inter_masklm_medium`. The model was pre-trained on masked language modeling (MLM) and an additional intermediate pre-training step, followed by fine-tuning on SQA. It uses relative position embeddings, resetting the position index at every cell of the table. The non-default version uses absolute position embeddings. TAPAS is a BERT-like transformer model pre-trained on a large corpus of English data from Wikipedia in a self-supervised manner. It was pre-trained with two objectives: masked language modeling, where 15% of the words in a flattened table and associated context are randomly masked and the model must predict them, and intermediate pre-training, where the model is trained to classify whether a sentence is supported or refuted by the contents of a table using a balanced dataset of millions of syntactically created examples. This allows the model to learn a bidirectional representation of tables and associated texts, which can be used for downstream tasks such as answering questions about a table or determining if a sentence is entailed or refuted by the table. Fine-tuning involves adding a cell selection head on top of the pre-trained model and jointly training this head with the base model on SQA. The model can be used for answering questions related to a table in a conversational setup. The texts are lowercased and tokenized using WordPiece with a vocabulary size of 30,000. The model inputs are of the form [CLS] Question [SEP] Flattened table [SEP]. The model was fine-tuned on 32 Cloud TPU v3 cores for 200,000 steps with a maximum sequence length of 512 and a batch size of 128. Fine-tuning takes approximately 20 hours and uses the Adam optimizer with a learning rate of 1.25e-5 and a warmup ratio of 0.2. An inductive bias is added so that the model only selects cells of the same column, as reflected by the `select_one_column` parameter of `TapasConfig`. The model achieves a dev accuracy of 0.6561 with the reset version and 0.6464 with the noreset version for the medium size. The team releasing TAPAS did not write a model card for this model, and this model card was written by the Hugging Face team and contributors.
domain: Natural Language Processing Table Question Answering

instruction: Can you analyze this video to determine if it might be a deepfake?
description: This model is a fine-tuned version of the VideoMAE large model on a deepfake subset dataset. It is designed to perform tasks related to video analysis, specifically in the context of deepfake detection or classification. The model was trained using the Adam optimizer with betas=(0.9, 0.999) and epsilon=1e-08, a learning rate of 5e-05, a training batch size of 8, and an evaluation batch size of 8. The training process used a linear learning rate scheduler with a warmup ratio of 0.1 and a total of 4470 training steps. Native AMP was used for mixed precision training. The model was trained for a total of 9.1 epochs, with the training loss decreasing from 0.6169 to 0.0118 over the course of training. The validation loss fluctuated during training, starting at 0.6023 and reaching a minimum of 0.2647 at the end of training. The model achieves a loss of 0.2588 on the evaluation set. The training was conducted using Transformers 4.44.2, PyTorch 2.5.0+cu121, Datasets 3.1.0, and Tokenizers 0.19.1.

instruction: I need accurate depth estimation for this image to support a computer vision application. Can you assist?
description: Prompt Depth Anything is a high-resolution and accurate metric depth estimation method. It leverages prompting to harness the power of depth foundation models, drawing inspiration from the success of prompting in vision-language and large language model foundation models. The method uses widely available iPhone LiDAR as a prompt to guide the model in producing up to 4K resolution accurate metric depth. A scalable data pipeline is introduced to train the method. Prompt Depth Anything is designed to benefit downstream applications, including 3D reconstruction and generalized robotic grasping. The model is compatible with Hugging Face Transformers and can be used with an image processor and depth estimation model from the transformers library. The model takes an input image and a prompt depth image, processes them, and outputs a predicted depth estimation. The predicted depth is post-processed to match the target image size. If you find this project useful, you are encouraged to cite the associated research paper titled 'Prompting Depth Anything for 4K Resolution Accurate Metric Depth Estimation' by Lin, Haotong and Peng, Sida and Chen, Jingxiao and Peng, Songyou and Sun, Jiaming and Liu, Minghuan and Bao, Hujun and Feng, Jiashi and Zhou, Xiaowei and Kang, Bingyi, published in arXiv in 2024.

instruction: 有没有办法把这段语音转成文本？
description: This is a fine-tuned XLSR-53 large model for speech recognition in Chinese. It was fine-tuned using the train and validation splits of unspecified datasets. When using this model, the speech input must be sampled at 16kHz. The model was fine-tuned with the support of GPU credits provided by an unspecified source. The model can be used directly for speech recognition without a language model. It is based on the Wav2Vec2 architecture and is designed to transcribe audio inputs into text. Evaluation on the Chinese (zh-CN) test data of Common Voice shows a Word Error Rate (WER) of 82.37% and a Character Error Rate (CER) of 19.03%. These results were obtained using a specific evaluation script. The model has been tested on a sample of 10 audio files from the Common Voice dataset, and example transcriptions show varying degrees of accuracy, with some predictions containing errors in character recognition. The model can be accessed and used via the Hugging Face library, and the training script is available at an unspecified location. Users are advised to ensure that their audio inputs are properly formatted and sampled at the required rate. The model is intended for speech recognition tasks in the Chinese language and is not suitable for other languages or tasks not explicitly mentioned.

instruction: What is the distance between the two buildings in this cityscape photo?
description: DPT-Hybrid, also known as MiDaS 3.0, is a Dense Prediction Transformer (DPT) model trained on 1.4 million images for monocular depth estimation. It was introduced in the paper by Ranftl et al. (2021) and first released in an unspecified year. DPT uses the Vision Transformer (ViT) as a backbone and adds a neck and head on top for monocular depth estimation. This repository hosts the 'hybrid' version of the model as stated in the paper. DPT-Hybrid diverges from DPT by using a different backbone and taking some activations from the backbone. The model card was written in combination by the Hugging Face team and Intel. The model is intended for zero-shot monocular depth estimation and is suitable for anyone working in this domain. However, it is generally expected that the model will need to be fine-tuned for specific tasks and should not be used to intentionally create hostile or alienating environments for people. The model was trained on a dataset called MIX 6, which contains around 1.4 million images, and was initialized with ImageNet-pretrained weights. During training, images were resized such that the longer side was 384 pixels, and random square crops of size 384 were used. Random horizontal flips were applied for data augmentation. Inference was completed on an Intel Xeon Platinum 8280 CPU @ 2.70GHz with 8 physical cores and an NVIDIA RTX 2080 GPU. Performance on monocular depth estimation benchmarks includes a DIW WHDR of 11.06 (-11.2%), an ETH3D AbsRel of 0.093 (-27.6%), a Sintel AbsRel of 0.274 (-16.2%), a KITTI δ>1.25 of 11.56 (-51.6%), a NYU δ>1.25 of 8.69 (-9.0%), and a TUM δ>1.25 of 10.89 (-23.2%). The model is not intended to inform decisions central to human life or flourishing and is an aggregated set of monocular depth image datasets. No additional risk mitigation strategies were considered during model development, and the extent of the risks involved by using the model remains unknown. Users should be made aware of the risks, biases, and limitations of the model.

instruction: Can you explain the sequence of events in the video step by step?
description: VideoChat-Flash-Qwen2_5-7B_InternVideo2-1B is a model constructed upon InternVideo2-1B and Qwen2.5-7B, using only 16 tokens per frame. It leverages Yarn to extend the context window to 128k, allowing input sequences of up to approximately 10,000 frames. The model is trained predominantly on an English corpus, and as a result, it exhibits basic Chinese comprehension. For optimal performance, it is recommended to use English for interaction. The model achieves a performance of 70.0 on MVBench, 58.3 on LongVideoBench, 57.0 on VideoMME (without subtitles), and supports a maximum input of 10,000 frames. Another version of the model achieves 73.2 on MVBench, 64.2 on LongVideoBench, 64.0 on VideoMME (without subtitles), and supports 10,000 frames. A third version achieves 73.4 on MVBench, 66.5 on LongVideoBench, 63.5 on VideoMME (without subtitles), and supports 50,000 frames. A fourth version achieves 74.3 on MVBench, 64.5 on LongVideoBench, 65.1 on VideoMME (without subtitles), and supports 10,000 frames. A final version achieves 74.0 on MVBench, 64.7 on LongVideoBench, 65.3 on VideoMME (without subtitles), and supports 10,000 frames. The model supports single-turn and multi-turn video-based conversations, where users can ask questions about the content of a video. It can describe a video in detail and answer specific questions about the number of people appearing in the video. The model uses a hierarchical compression approach for long-context video modeling. It is implemented using the Transformers library and requires several dependencies including av, imageio, decord, and opencv-python. Optional installation includes flash-attn. The model can be configured to use global compression or not, with specific settings for compression type, layers, and image token ratios. Evaluation settings include a maximum number of frames and a generation configuration with parameters such as do_sample, temperature, max_new_tokens, top_p, and num_beams. The model is described in the paper 'VideoChat-Flash: Hierarchical Compression for Long-Context Video Modeling' by Li et al., published as an arXiv preprint in 2024.

instruction: What do you think the purpose of this image is, based on its content?
description: Xinyuan-VL-2B is a high-performance multimodal large model for the end-side developed by the Cylingo Group. It is fine-tuned from Qwen/Qwen2-VL-2B-Instruct using more than 5 million multimodal data samples and a small amount of plain text data. The model performs well on several authoritative benchmarks. It is designed to support tasks involving both visual and textual information. Evaluation results show that Xinyuan-VL-2B outperforms models released by Alibaba Cloud and other influential open-source models of comparable parameter scale. For example, on the MMB-CN-V11-Test benchmark, it achieved 74.3, compared to 71.2 for Qwen2-VL-2B. On the MMB-EN-V11-Test benchmark, it achieved 76.5, compared to 73.2 for Qwen2-VL-2B. On the MMB-EN benchmark, it achieved 78.9, compared to 74.3 for Qwen2-VL-2B. On the MMB-CN benchmark, it achieved 76.12, compared to 73.8 for Qwen2-VL-2B. On the CCBench benchmark, it achieved 55.5, compared to 53.7 for Qwen2-VL-2B. On the MMT-Bench benchmark, it achieved 55.2, compared to 54.5 for Qwen2-VL-2B. On the RealWorld benchmark, it achieved 63.9, compared to 62.9 for Qwen2-VL-2B. On the SEEDBench_IMG benchmark, it achieved 73.4, compared to 72.86 for Qwen2-VL-2B. On the AI2D benchmark, it achieved 74.2, compared to 74.7 for Qwen2-VL-2B. On the MMMU benchmark, it achieved 40.9, compared to 41.1 for Qwen2-VL-2B. On the HallusionBench benchmark, it achieved 55.00, compared to 42.4 for Qwen2-VL-2B. On the POPE benchmark, it achieved 89.42, compared to 86.82 for Qwen2-VL-2B. On the MME benchmark, it achieved 1854.9, compared to 1872.0 for Qwen2-VL-2B. On the MMStar benchmark, it achieved 51.87, compared to 47.5 for Qwen2-VL-2B. On the SEEDBench2_Plus benchmark, it achieved 62.98, compared to 62.23 for Qwen2-VL-2B. On the BLINK benchmark, it achieved 42.98, compared to 43.92 for Qwen2-VL-2B. On the OCRBench benchmark, it achieved 782, compared to 794 for Qwen2-VL-2B. On the TextVQA benchmark, it achieved 77.6, compared to 79.7 for Qwen2-VL-2B.

instruction: Make a video that transitions from a winter scene to a spring garden, using the description I provide.
description: Wan2.1 is a comprehensive and open suite of video foundation models designed to push the boundaries of video generation. It offers state-of-the-art performance, consistently outperforming existing open-source models and state-of-the-art commercial solutions across multiple benchmarks. The T2V-1.3B model requires only 8.19 GB VRAM, making it compatible with most consumer-grade GPUs. It can generate a 5-second 480P video on an RTX 4090 in about 4 minutes, with performance comparable to some closed-source models. Wan2.1 supports multiple tasks, including Text-to-Video, Image-to-Video, Video Editing, Text-to-Image, and Video-to-Audio, advancing the field of video generation. It is the first video model capable of generating both Chinese and English text, enhancing its practical applications. The suite includes a powerful video VAE called Wan-VAE, which delivers exceptional efficiency and performance, encoding and decoding 1080P videos of any length while preserving temporal information. The T2V-14B model establishes a new state-of-the-art performance benchmark among both open-source and closed-source models, demonstrating exceptional capabilities in generating high-quality visuals with significant motion dynamics. It is the only video model capable of producing both Chinese and English text and supports video generation at both 480P and 720P resolutions. Wan2.1 is built on the mainstream diffusion transformer paradigm, with innovations such as a novel spatio-temporal variational autoencoder (VAE), scalable training strategies, large-scale data construction, and automated evaluation metrics. The model uses a 3D causal VAE architecture specifically designed for video generation, improving spatio-temporal compression, reducing memory usage, and ensuring temporal causality. The model's architecture employs the Flow Matching framework within the Diffusion Transformers paradigm, using a T5 Encoder to encode multilingual text input and cross-attention in each transformer block to embed the text into the model structure. An MLP with a Linear layer and a SiLU layer processes the input time embeddings and predicts six modulation parameters individually, shared across all transformer blocks. The model was trained on a curated and deduplicated dataset of image and video data, processed through a four-step data cleaning pipeline focusing on fundamental dimensions, visual quality, and motion quality. The model was evaluated using a set of 1,035 internal prompts across 14 major dimensions and 26 sub-dimensions, with total scores calculated using weights derived from human preferences. The model is licensed under the Apache 2.0 License, and users are responsible for ensuring their usage complies with the license provisions, including not generating or sharing content that violates applicable laws, causes harm, or spreads misinformation.

instruction: Generate a short video of a bustling street scene based on my written description and add some motion effects.
description: Wan2.1 is a comprehensive and open suite of video foundation models designed to push the boundaries of video generation. It offers state-of-the-art performance, consistently outperforming existing open-source models and state-of-the-art commercial solutions across multiple benchmarks. The T2V-1.3B model requires only 8.19 GB VRAM, making it compatible with almost all consumer-grade GPUs. It can generate a 5-second 480P video on an RTX 4890 in about 4 minutes (without optimization techniques like quantization), and its performance is comparable to some closed-source models. Wan2.1 supports multiple tasks, including Text-to-Video, Image-to-Video, Video Editing, Text-to-Image, and Video-to-Audio, advancing the field of video generation. It is the first video model capable of generating both Chinese and English text, featuring robust text generation that enhances its practical applications. Wan2.1 includes a powerful video VAE, Wan-VAE, which delivers exceptional efficiency and performance, encoding and decoding 1080P videos of any length while preserving temporal information, making it an ideal foundation for video and image generation. The model is built on the mainstream diffusion transformer paradigm, with innovations such as a novel spatio-temporal variational autoencoder, scalable training strategies, large-scale data construction, and automated evaluation metrics. Wan2.1's architecture uses the T5 Encoder to encode multilingual text input, with cross-attention in each transformer block embedding the text into the model structure. It employs an MLP with a Linear layer and a SiLU layer to process the input time embeddings and predict six modulation parameters individually. The model is trained on a large and diverse dataset of images and videos, curated through a four-step data cleaning process focusing on fundamental dimensions, visual quality, and motion quality. Wan2.1 has been evaluated against leading open-source and closed-source models using a carefully designed set of 1,035 internal prompts, tested across 14 major dimensions and 26 sub-dimensions. The results demonstrate superior performance compared to both open-source and closed-source models. The model is available under the Apache 2.0 License, and users are responsible for ensuring their usage complies with the provisions of this license. Users must not generate or share content that violates applicable laws, causes harm to individuals or groups, disseminates personal information intended for harm, spreads misinformation, or targets vulnerable populations.

instruction: I need to improve the order of these results—can you rank them for me?
description: This is an improved version of a reranker based on a model trained on a large dataset of text pairs. The model was trained in the same way and on the same data as a previous model, with the following improvements: predictions from a specific model were used for distillation instead of another model, and a custom implementation of the RoBERTa model with support for Flash Attention 2 was used. To use these features, the model should be loaded with the arguments `trust_remote_code=True` and `attn_implementation="flash_attention_2"`. The reranker achieves results close to a specific model on the PIRB benchmark, even outperforming it on some datasets. It is over 21 times smaller, with 435M parameters compared to 9.24B parameters. The model can be used with Huggingface Transformers by importing necessary modules, loading the tokenizer and model with specific arguments, and processing input texts. The model achieves an NDCG@10 of 65.30 in the Rerankers category of the Polish Information Retrieval Benchmark.

instruction: Could you help me locate a specific file buried in a large folder structure?
description: UI-TARS-1.5 is an open-source multimodal agent built upon a powerful vision-language model, designed to perform diverse tasks within virtual worlds, particularly excelling in playing games and performing GUI tasks. It integrates advanced reasoning enabled by reinforcement learning, allowing the model to reason through its thoughts before taking action, which enhances its performance and adaptability, especially in inference-time scaling. The model achieves state-of-the-art results across a variety of standard benchmarks, demonstrating strong reasoning capabilities and notable improvements over prior models. UI-TARS-1.5 outperforms previous models and competitors such as OpenAI CUA and Claude 3.7 in computer use, browser use, and phone use benchmarks. It also shows superior performance in grounding capability evaluations and in game-based scenarios such as Poki Game and Minecraft tasks. The model's performance is further enhanced when it includes thought processes during execution. The UI-TARS-1.5-7B variant focuses on general computer use and is not specifically optimized for game-based scenarios, where the full UI-TARS-1.5 model holds a significant advantage. The model is available for early research access to facilitate collaborative research.

instruction: What would be the expected return on investment for a new project using the available financial data?
description: The model is a RandomForestRegressor, designed for regression tasks. It is trained using a procedure that includes hyperparameters such as bootstrap set to True, ccp_alpha set to 0.0, criterion set to squared_error, max_features set to 1.0, min_samples_leaf set to 1, min_samples_split set to 2, n_estimators set to 100, oob_score set to False, and verbose set to 0. The model is evaluated using the test split method, with the root mean squared error (rmse) as the metric. The evaluation results show an rmse value of 0.231662. The model is unfitted, as indicated by its visual representation and associated styling. No specific intended uses or limitations are described. No information is provided on how to get started with the model, the authors of the model card, contact channels, or citation details.

instruction: What are the trade-offs between embedding size and retrieval accuracy?
description: Snowflake's Arctic-embed-l-v2.0 is an embedding model designed for retrieval performance and inference efficiency, particularly optimized for multilingual workloads. It introduces a new standard for multilingual embedding models, maintaining high-quality retrieval in both English and non-English languages. The model is released under the Apache 2.0 license, making it suitable for enterprise-grade multilingual search and retrieval at scale. Key features include multilingual performance without compromising English retrieval, inference efficiency with 303 million non-embedding parameters, compression-friendly embeddings as small as 128 bytes per vector using Matryoshka Representation Learning (MRL) and quantization-aware training, and support for drop-in replacement with existing libraries and inference engines. It also supports a context window of up to 8192 tokens via RoPE. Arctic-embed-l-v2.0 excels on benchmarks such as MTEB Retrieval, CLEF, and MIRACL, with an average NDCG@10 score of 55.6 on BEIR (15), 55.8 on MIRACL (4), 52.9 on CLEF (Focused), and 54.3 on CLEF (Full). The model can be used with vector truncation to reduce vector size by 4x with less than 3% degradation in quality. When using 256 dimensions, the model achieves a BEIR (15) score of 54.3 (-0.18% degradation), a MIRACL (4) score of 54.3 (-2.70% degradation), a CLEF (Focused) score of 51.9 (-1.81% degradation), and a CLEF (Full) score of 53.4 (-1.53% degradation). The model can be used with libraries such as Sentence Transformers and Huggingface Transformers, and it supports both Python and JavaScript implementations for embedding generation and similarity computation.

instruction: Hãy tìm các tài liệu hướng dẫn về thủ tục ly hôn và quyền lợi của người vợ, người chồng.
description: Vietnamese_Reranker is a reranker model fine-tuned from the bge-reranker-v2-m3 model to enhance retrieval capabilities for Vietnamese. The model was trained on approximately 1,100,000 triplets of queries, positive documents, and negative documents for Vietnamese. The model was trained with a maximum sequence length of 2304 (256 for query and 2048 for passages). The model type is a Sentence Transformer. The base model is unspecified. The maximum sequence length is 2304 tokens (256 for query and 2048 for passages). The output dimensionality is 1024 dimensions. The similarity function used is dot product similarity. The model is designed for the Vietnamese language. The model is licensed under Apache 2.0. The model was evaluated on the entire training dataset of Legal Zalo 2021, a dataset on which it was not trained. On this dataset, the model achieved an Accuracy@1 of 0.7944, an Accuracy@3 of 0.9324, an Accuracy@5 of 0.9537, an Accuracy@10 of 0.9740, and an MRR@10 of 0.8672. In comparison, Vietnamese_Embedding_v2 achieved an Accuracy@1 of 0.7262, an Accuracy@3 of 0.8927, an Accuracy@5 of 0.9268, an Accuracy@10 of 0.9578, and an MRR@10 of 0.8149. Vietnamese_Embedding achieved an Accuracy@1 of 0.7274, an Accuracy@3 of 0.8992, an Accuracy@5 of 0.9305, an Accuracy@10 of 0.9568, and an MRR@10 of 0.8181. Vietnamese-bi-encoder (BKAI) achieved an Accuracy@1 of 0.7109, an Accuracy@3 of 0.8680, an Accuracy@5 of 0.9014, an Accuracy@10 of 0.9299, and an MRR@10 of 0.7951. BGE-M3 achieved an Accuracy@1 of 0.5682, an Accuracy@3 of 0.7728, an Accuracy@5 of 0.8382, an Accuracy@10 of 0.8921, and an MRR@10 of 0.6822. Vietnamese_Reranker and Vietnamese_Embedding_v2 were both trained on 1,100,000 triplets. Although the score on the legal domain drops a bit on Vietnamese_Embedding_v2 (Phase 2), since this phase data is much larger, it is good for other domains.

instruction: What objects are currently in motion in this video frame?
description: The D-FINE model was proposed by Yansong Peng, Hebei Li, Peixi Wu, Yueyi Zhang, Xiaoyan Sun, and Feng Wu. This is the Hugging Face Transformers implementation for D-FINE. D-FINE is a real-time object detector that achieves outstanding localization precision by redefining the bounding box regression task in DETR models. The model comprises two key components: Fine-grained Distribution Refinement (FDR) and Global Optimal Localization Self-Distillation (GO-LSD). D-FINE is trained on COCO (Lin et al. [2014]) train2017 and validated on COCO val2017 dataset. The standard AP metrics are reported, averaged over uniformly sampled IoU thresholds ranging from 0.50 to 0.95 with a step size of 0.05, as well as APval5000, which is commonly used in real scenarios. D-FINE is ideal for real-time object detection in diverse applications such as autonomous driving, surveillance systems, robotics, and retail analytics. Its enhanced flexibility and deployment-friendly design make it suitable for both edge devices and large-scale systems, ensuring high accuracy and speed in dynamic, real-world environments.

instruction: Design an image of a cyberpunk-themed park with neon lights and flying cars.
description: SDXL-Turbo is a fast generative text-to-image model that can synthesize photorealistic images from a text prompt in a single network evaluation. It is a distilled version of a larger model, trained for real-time synthesis. The model is based on a novel training method called Adversarial Diffusion Distillation (ADD), which allows sampling large-scale foundational image diffusion models in 1 to 4 steps at high image quality. This approach uses score distillation to leverage large-scale off-the-shelf image diffusion models as a teacher signal and combines this with an adversarial loss to ensure high image fidelity even in the low-step regime of one or two sampling steps. SDXL-Turbo is developed and funded by Stability AI. The model is intended for both non-commercial and commercial usage, including research on generative models, real-time applications of generative models, the impact of real-time generative models, safe deployment of models that can generate harmful content, probing and understanding the limitations and biases of generative models, generation of artworks and use in design and other artistic processes, and applications in educational or creative tools. The model is not intended for use in generating factual or true representations of people or events, and such use is considered out-of-scope. The model has several limitations, including the generation of images at a fixed resolution of 512x512 pixels, inability to render legible text, potential improper generation of faces and people, and a lossy autoencoding component. The model supports text-to-image and image-to-image generation tasks. For image-to-image generation, it is important that the product of num_inference_steps and strength is greater than or equal to 1. The model does not use guidance_scale or negative_prompt and is best used with guidance_scale set to 0.0. A single inference step is sufficient to generate high-quality images. The model is intended for use with the diffusers library and can be loaded using the AutoPipelineForText2Image or AutoPipelineForImage2Image classes. The model is not trained to be factual or true representations of people or events, and therefore using the model to generate such content is out-of-scope for the abilities of this model. The model should not be used in any way that violates Stability AI's policies.

instruction: Show a futuristic race track with hover cars speeding around a glowing circuit under a neon sky.
description: Cosmos-1.0-Diffusion is a suite of diffusion-based world foundation models designed for generating physics-aware videos and world states to support physical AI development. The models are part of the Cosmos World Foundation Models family and are built to generate dynamic, high-quality videos from text, image, or video inputs. They can be used as building blocks for various applications and research related to world generation. The models are developed by NVIDIA and are available for commercial use under the NVIDIA Open Model License agreement. The Cosmos Diffusion WFM family includes models that can generate a 121-frame video from a text description or predict 120 future frames from a text description and an initial image. The Cosmos-1.0-Diffusion-7B-Text2World model is a diffusion transformer designed for video denoising in the latent space. It uses interleaved self-attention, cross-attention, and feedforward layers. Cross-attention layers allow the model to condition on input text during the denoising process. Adaptive layer normalization is applied before each layer to embed time information. When images or videos are provided as input, their latent frames are concatenated with the generated frames along the temporal dimension. Augment noise is added to conditional latent frames to bridge the training and inference gap. The model accepts text as input, with input strings limited to fewer than 300 words and containing descriptive content for world generation, such as scene descriptions, key objects, characters, background, and specific actions or motions. The output is a video in mp4 format, typically a 5-second clip with a resolution of 1280x704 pixels and a frame rate of 24 fps. The video visualizes the input text as a short animated scene, capturing key elements within the time constraints. Aspect ratios and resolutions are configurable, including 1:1 (960x960), 4:3 (960x704), 3:4 (704x960), 16:9 (1280x704), and 9:16 (704x1280). Frame rates can be adjusted between 12 and 40 fps. The model is compatible with NVIDIA Blackwell, Hopper, and Ampere hardware microarchitectures and is tested with BF16 precision. It is supported on Linux operating systems. The model is intended for use by physical AI developers and is designed to generate videos based on video inputs. It is verified to meet prescribed NVIDIA quality standards and includes quantitative and qualitative evaluation metrics. The model may not follow the video input accurately and can generate all forms of videos, including potentially toxic, offensive, or indecent content. The model is not known to generate or reverse engineer personal information, nor is it known to use protected class data. No consent was obtained for any personal data used, and no personal data was collected directly by NVIDIA. The model is tagged for known restrictions and potential safety implications. The model is subject to a safety guardrail, and bypassing or disabling this guardrail will terminate the user's rights under the license. The model is available for use with the Diffusers library and can be accessed via the Hugging Face model catalog. Inference time and GPU memory usage vary depending on the offloading strategy and hardware. For example, on a single H100 GPU, the 7B Text2World model with prompt upsampler offloading takes approximately 380 seconds for end-to-end inference and uses up to 74.0 GB of GPU memory. The 14B Text2World model with prompt upsampler and guardrails offloading takes approximately 590 seconds and uses up to 70.5 GB of GPU memory. Users are advised to select an appropriate offloading strategy based on their GPU capabilities. The model is reviewed before release and is tagged for known restrictions and potential safety implications. Developers are encouraged to ensure the model meets requirements for their specific industry and use case and to address any potential misuse.

instruction: What makes one mathematical explanation better than another?
description: Qwen2.5-Math-RM-72B is a reward model specifically designed to guide the Qwen2.5-Math model throughout the training process by offering more granular feedback on the quality of reasoning and intermediate steps, ultimately facilitating more robust model improvements. It provides preference signals across two languages (Chinese and English) and in dual modes (Chain-of-Thought and Tool-integrated Reasoning), enhancing versatility. The model employs a data selection process via reward model scoring combined with Rejection Sampling to incrementally enhance the quality of responses. It integrates seamlessly into the reinforcement learning training and provides effective reward signals, further improving model performance. By leveraging a combination of response sampling and Best-of-N strategies, the model selects the response with the top score judged by the reward model, yielding better results with increased inference time. For example, Qwen2.5-Math-1.5B-Instruct obtains 83.9 on MATH in RM@8 setting and even surpasses the performance of Qwen2.5-Math-7B-Instruct 83.6 with greedy decoding. RM@N scores are substantially better than Maj@N scores across almost all benchmarks and models. The model is typically used for offering feedback on the quality of reasoning and intermediate steps, serving in Rejection Sampling, reinforcement learning training, and RM@N. It requires the `transformers` library version 4.40.0 or higher, as the latest version is recommended. This is a must because `transformers` integrated Qwen2.5 codes since version 4.37.0. For requirements on GPU memory and the respective throughput, see similar results of Qwen2.

instruction: Is there any indication in this prompt that it might be unsafe or violate content policies?
description: Llama Guard 4 is a natively multimodal safety classifier with 12 billion parameters trained jointly on text and multiple images. It is a dense architecture pruned from the Llama 4 Scout pre-trained model and fine-tuned for content safety classification. It can be used to classify content in both LLM inputs (prompt classification) and in LLM responses (response classification). It generates text in its output that indicates whether a given prompt or response is safe or unsafe, and if unsafe, it also lists the content categories violated. Llama Guard 4 was aligned to safeguard against the standardized MLCommons and designed to support multimodal Llama 4 capabilities within a single safety classifier. It combines the capabilities of the previous Llama Guard 3-8B and Llama Guard 3-11B-vision models by supporting English and multilingual text prompts as well as mixed text-and-image prompts for image understanding. Unlike Llama Guard 3-11B-vision, Llama Guard 4 now supports safety classification when multiple images are given in the prompt as input. It is also integrated into the Llama Moderations API for text and images. The model is trained to predict safety labels on the following categories: S1: Violent Crimes, S2: Non-Violent Crimes, S3: Sex-Related Crimes, S4: Child Sexual Exploitation, S5: Defamation, S6: Specialized Advice, S7: Privacy, S8: Intellectual Property, S9: Indiscriminate Weapons, S10: Hate, S11: Suicide & Self-Harm, S12: Sexual Content, S13: Elections, and S14: Code Interpreter Abuse (text only). Llama Guard 4 is a natively multimodal safeguard model with 12 billion parameters in total and uses an early fusion transformer architecture with dense layers to keep the overall size small. It can be run on a single GPU and shares the same tokenizer and vision encoder as Llama 4 Scout and Maverick. Llama Guard 4 employs a dense feedforward early-fusion architecture, differing from Llama 4 Scout, which uses Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) layers. It is developed by pruning the pre-trained Llama 4 Scout checkpoint, which consists of one shared dense expert and sixteen routed experts in each MoE layer. All the routed experts and router layers are pruned, retaining only the shared expert. After pruning, the MoE is reduced to a dense feedforward layer initiated from the shared expert weights. The model is post-trained with a blend of data from the Llama 4 Scout and Maverick models, with additional multi-image training data and multilingual data. The training data is blended with a ratio of roughly 3:1 text-only data to multimodal data containing one or more images. Llama Guard 4 is designed to be used in an integrated system with a generative language model, reducing the overall rate of safety violations exposed to the user. It can be used for input filtering, output filtering, or both. Input filtering classifies user prompts into an LLM as safe or unsafe, while output filtering classifies an LLM’s generated output as safe or unsafe. Using both filtering types provides additional security. In some internal tests, input filtering reduces safety violation rate and raises overall refusal rate more than output filtering does, but results may vary. Llama Guard 4 matches or exceeds the overall performance of the Llama Guard 3 models on both input and output filtering, for English and multilingual text and for mixed text and images. Classifier performance shows that Llama Guard 4 matches or exceeds the overall performance of Llama Guard 3-8B (LG3) on English and multilingual text, as well as Llama Guard 3-11B-vision (LG3v) on prompts with single or multiple images. For English, recall is 69%, false positive rate is 11%, and F1 is 61%, with a 4% increase in recall, -3% in false positive rate, and 8% in F1 compared to LG3. For multilingual, recall is 43%, false positive rate is 3%, and F1 is 51%, with a -2% increase in recall, -1% in false positive rate, and 0% in F1 compared to LG3. For single-image, recall is 41%, false positive rate is 9%, and F1 is 38%, with a 10% increase in recall, 0% in false positive rate, and 8% in F1 compared to LG3v. For multi-image, recall is 61%, false positive rate is 9%, and F1 is 52%, with a 20% increase in recall, -1% in false positive rate, and 17% in F1 compared to LG3v.
