The model can be used with Sentence Transformers, Huggingface Transformers, or Huggingface Transformers.js, with specific instructions for encoding queries and documents and computing similarity scores. Users are encouraged to open issues or pull requests for questions or suggestions, and can contact Daniel Campos at daniel.campos@snowflake.com for further assistance.

instruction: Can you help me find the section of this paper that describes the experimental setup?
description: ColQwen2 is a model based on a novel model architecture and training strategy using Vision Language Models (VLMs) to efficiently index documents from their visual features. It generates multi-vector representations of text and images. This version is trained with a batch size of 256 for 5 epochs and includes an updated pad token. The model takes dynamic image resolutions as input without resizing, which may change the aspect ratio, similar to ColPali. The maximum resolution is set to create at most 768 image patches, with experiments showing improvements with more patches at the cost of increased memory requirements. This version is trained with colpali-engine version 0.3.1 and uses the same data as the ColPali dataset described in the paper. The training dataset consists of 127,460 query-page pairs, including 63% from openly available academic datasets and 37% from a synthetic dataset made of web-crawled PDF pages augmented with VLM-generated pseudo-questions. The training set is entirely in English, allowing for the study of zero-shot generalization to non-English languages. No multi-page PDF document is used in both the training and evaluation sets to prevent contamination. A validation set of 2% of the samples is used for hyperparameter tuning. Multilingual data may be present in the pretraining corpus of the language model and likely in the multimodal training. All models are trained for 1 epoch on the train set in bfloat16 format, using low-rank adapters with alpha=32 and r=32 on the transformer layers from the language model and the final randomly initialized projection layer. The paged_adamw_8bit optimizer is used, with training on an 8 GPU setup using data parallelism, a learning rate of 5e-5 with linear decay and 2.5% warmup steps, and a batch size of 32. The model relies on multi-vector retrieving derived from the ColBERT late interaction mechanism, which may require engineering efforts to adapt to widely used vector retrieval frameworks that lack native multi-vector support. The model primarily focuses on PDF-type documents and high-resource languages, potentially limiting its generalization to other document types or less represented languages. The vision language backbone model (Qwen2-VL) is licensed under Apache 2.0, while the adapters are under the MIT license.

instruction: How can I ensure that this image doesn’t contain content that could be considered explicit?
description: Marqo/nsfw-image-detection-384 is a lightweight image classification model designed to identify NSFW (Not Safe For Work) images. The model is approximately 18–20x smaller than other open-source models and achieves a superior accuracy of 98.56% on its dataset. It uses 384x384 pixel images for input with 16x16 pixel patches. The model was trained on a proprietary dataset of 220,000 images, including 100,000 NSFW examples and 100,000 SFW examples, with a test set containing 10,000 NSFW examples and 10,000 SFW examples. The dataset includes a diverse range of content such as real photos, drawings, Rule 34 material, memes, and AI-generated images. The definition of NSFW can be subjective and contextual, and the dataset was constructed to include challenging examples, though it may not be 100% aligned with every use case. The model outperforms existing NSFW detectors on its dataset. Adjusting the threshold for the NSFW probability can allow for trade-offs between precision, recall, and accuracy, which may be useful in different applications where varying degrees of confidence are required. This model is a fine-tune of another model. Training was conducted with the following parameters: batch size of 256, color jitter of 0.2 with a 0.05 probability, cutmix of 0.1, drop rate of 0.1, drop path rate of 0.05, 20 epochs, Gaussian blur probability of 0.005, horizontal flip probability of 0.5, learning rate of 5.0e-05, mixup of 0.1 with a batch mode and 1.0 probability, momentum of 0.9, 2 classes, AdamW optimizer, pixel remode, 0.5 reprob, cosine schedule, label smoothing of 0.1, 2 warmup epochs, warmup learning rate of 1.0e-05, and warmup prefix disabled. Like all models, this one can make mistakes. NSFW content can be subjective and contextual, and this model is intended to help identify such content. It is recommended to experiment and try different thresholds to determine if the model is suitable for specific needs.

instruction: Show me the part of the research article that includes the results from the field study.
description: Nomic Embed Multimodal 7B is a dense state-of-the-art multimodal embedding model designed for visual document retrieval tasks. It achieves 58.8 NDCG@5 on the Vidore-v2 benchmark, outperforming all other dense multimodal embedding models. The model directly encodes interleaved text and images without requiring complex preprocessing. It is a 7B parameter multimodal embedding model with a vision-language architecture that supports unified text and image input processing. The model was fine-tuned from Qwen2.5-VL 7B Instruct and incorporates key innovations such as same-source sampling to create harder in-batch negatives and hard negative mining with positive-aware techniques. It is fully open-source, with model weights, training data, and code available. The model integrates seamlessly with Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) workflows by enabling direct document embedding, faster processing, and capturing both textual and visual cues in a single embedding. It is particularly effective for real-world document retrieval scenarios, including research papers, technical documentation, product catalogs, financial reports, visually rich content, and multilingual documents. Performance may vary when processing documents with unconventional layouts, unusual visual elements, handwriting, or heavily stylized fonts. While it supports multiple languages, performance is strongest on English content. Processing very large or complex documents may require dividing them into smaller chunks.

instruction: Please separate the foreground and background elements in the image.
description: EoMT (Encoder-only Mask Transformer) is a Vision Transformer (ViT) architecture designed for high-quality and efficient image segmentation. It was introduced in the CVPR 2025 highlight paper titled 'Your ViT is Secretly an Image Segmentation Model' by Tommie Kerssies, Niccolò Cavagnero, Alexander Hermans, Narges Norouzi, Giuseppe Averta, Bastian Leibe, Gijs Dubbelman, and Daan de Geus. The key insight is that, given sufficient scale and pretraining, a plain ViT along with additional few parameters can perform segmentation without the need for task-specific decoders or pixel fusion modules. The same model backbone supports semantic, instance, and panoptic segmentation with different post-processing. The original implementation can be found in the associated repository, and the HuggingFace model page is available at the provided link.

instruction: Could you create a 3D model from this 512x512 pixel image of a statue?
description: Stable Point Aware 3D (SPAR3D) is a large reconstruction model that introduces the ability to make real-time edits and generate a textured UV-unwrapped 3D mesh asset from a single image in less than a second. SPAR3D combines the benefits of a fast point cloud diffusion model with regressive mesh predictions to enable unparalleled control over 3D object generation. The model is trained to create a 3D model from a single image in under one second. The asset is UV-unwrapped and textured. A delighting step is performed, enabling easier asset usage in downstream applications such as game engines or rendering work. The model also predicts per-object material parameters (roughness, metallic), enhancing reflective behaviors during rendering. The model expects an input size of 512x512 pixels. Improved backside modeling is achieved using a fast point diffusion model, which acts as a conditioning. We use renders from the dataset, available under the Open Data Commons Attribution License. An enhanced rendering method is utilized, which more closely replicates the distribution of images found in the real world, significantly improving the model's ability to generalize. Objects are filtered based on the review of licenses and a subset is curated suitable for training needs. Intended uses include the generation of artworks and use in design and other artistic processes, applications in educational or creative tools, and research on reconstruction models, including understanding the limitations of these models. All uses of the model should be in accordance with the provided guidelines. The model was not trained to be factual or true representations of people or events. As such, using the model to generate such content is out-of-scope of the abilities of this model. As part of a safety-by-design and responsible AI deployment approach, safety measures are implemented throughout the development of the model, from pre-training to ongoing development, fine-tuning, and deployment. A number of safety mitigations are intended to reduce the risk of severe harms. However, it is the responsibility of developers to conduct their own testing and apply additional mitigations based on their specific use cases. For individuals or organizations generating annual revenue of US $1,000,000 (or local currency equivalent) or more, regardless of the source of that revenue, an enterprise commercial license must be obtained directly from Stability AI before commercially using SPAR3D, any derivative work of SPAR3D (such as a fine tune model), or their outputs. A request for an Enterprise License may be submitted at the provided link. Please refer to Stability AI's Community License for more information.

instruction: Xác định các đoạn văn pháp luật liên quan đến việc xử lý vi phạm về bảo vệ môi trường.
description: Vietnamese_Embedding is an embedding model fine-tuned from the BGE-M3 model to enhance retrieval capabilities for Vietnamese. The model was trained on approximately 300,000 triplets of queries, positive documents, and negative documents for Vietnamese. The model was trained with a maximum sequence length of 2048. The model type is a Sentence Transformer. The base model is unspecified. The maximum sequence length is 2048 tokens. The output dimensionality is 1024 dimensions. The similarity function used is dot product similarity. The model is designed for the Vietnamese language. The model is licensed under Apache 2.0. The model is intended for tasks involving sentence embeddings and document retrieval. Evaluation results on the Legal Zalo 2021 dataset, which was not part of the training data, show the following performance metrics: Accuracy@1 is 0.7274, Accuracy@3 is 0.8992, Accuracy@5 is 0.9305, Accuracy@10 is 0.9568, and MRR@10 is 0.8181. These results are compared to other models such as Vietnamese_Reranker with Accuracy@1 of 0.7944 and MRR@10 of 0.8672, Vietnamese_Embedding_v2 with Accuracy@1 of 0.7262 and MRR@10 of 0.8149, Vietnamese-bi-encoder (BKAI) with Accuracy@1 of 0.7109 and MRR@10 of 0.7951, and BGE-M3 with Accuracy@1 of 0.5682 and MRR@10 of 0.6822. Vietnamese_Reranker and Vietnamese_Embedding_v2 were trained on 1,100,000 triplets. Although the score on the legal domain drops slightly for Vietnamese_Embedding_v2, it performs well on other domains due to the larger dataset used in training. The model can be accessed via a link. Evaluation results can be reproduced by running the code python evaluation_model.py with data downloaded from Kaggle.

instruction: Which priority level had the highest number of incidents in the past two weeks?
description: This model is a custom fine-tuned version of the TAPAS architecture, designed for question answering over tabular data, specifically for ServiceNow ITSM tables such as incident, change, and problem. The model is intended to answer natural language queries about these tables, such as identifying the most common issues or counting records in specific categories. The training data was based on synthetic datasets generated from a subset of fields in ServiceNow ITSM tables, including problem ID, priority, status, root cause, and resolved at. The data was structured in a synthetic question-answering (SQA) format, with each entry containing an ID, question, table file, answer coordinates, the actual answer, and a float answer for numeric values. The training process used full fine-tuning with specific hyperparameters, including one training epoch, a batch size of 32 for training and 64 for evaluation, a learning rate of 0.00001, and a random seed of 42. The model was evaluated on three benchmarks: WikiTableQuestions (WTQ), TabFact, and SQA. On the synthetic test set, the model's accuracy improved from 0.2933 to 0.4667 after fine-tuning. On the WTQ validation set and TabFact, the model maintained pre-training accuracy of 0.3405 and 0.5005, respectively. On the SQA dataset, the model showed a slight improvement. The model requires both a natural language question and a structured table in dataframe format to function. It outputs an aggregation operation, the predicted answer, and the cells used to derive the answer. Limitations include suboptimal accuracy, potential issues with larger table sizes, and the need for preprocessing to convert input data into the required format. The model is not guaranteed to achieve 100% accuracy, and increasing the number of training samples or using a larger model may improve performance.
domain: Natural Language Processing Table Question Answering

instruction: Could you assess if this voice sample is from a recorded voicemail or a live speaker?
description: This model is a fine-tuned version of wav2vec2-xls-r-300m for voicemail detection. It is trained on a dataset of call recordings to distinguish between voicemail greetings and live human responses. The model is intended for automated voicemail detection in AI-powered call assistants and for filtering voicemail responses in customer service and sales call automation. It was trained on a proprietary dataset of call recordings labeled as live human responses and voicemail greetings. The dataset includes diverse voicemail recordings across multiple types to improve generalization. The model achieved 98% accuracy on voicemail detection. It is only trained on the English language and assumes the voicemail track is isolated and contains no audio from the caller. It is designed for the first two seconds of audio when calling a voicemail. The model was trained using the following hyperparameters: learning_rate of 0.0003, train_batch_size of 16, eval_batch_size of 8, seed of 42, gradient_accumulation_steps of 2, total_train_batch_size of 32, optimizer ADAMW_TORCH with betas of (0.9, 0.999) and epsilon of 1e-08, lr_scheduler_type linear, lr_scheduler_warmup_steps of 500, num_epochs of 10, and mixed_precision_training using Native AMP. The training was conducted using Transformers 4.48.2, Pytorch 2.5.1+cu124, Datasets 1.18.3, and Tokenizers 0.21.0.

instruction: I have a list of podcasts on entrepreneurship. Could you help me identify the ones most relevant to startup funding?
description: The mxbai-rerank-large-v2 is a large model in the family of powerful reranker models from Mixedbread. It is designed to support a range of tasks, including retrieval, with state-of-the-art performance and strong efficiency. The model provides multilingual support for over 100 languages, with particularly strong performance in English and Chinese. It also supports code and long-context processing. The model is trained using a three-step process that includes GRPO (Guided Reinforcement Prompt Optimization), contrastive learning, and preference learning. Performance benchmarks show an average BEIR score of 57.49, a multilingual score of 29.79, a Chinese score of 84.16, and a code search score of 32.05, with a latency of 0.89 seconds measured on an A100 GPU. The model is available for use through the mxbai-rerank package, which can be installed via pip. Users can rank documents based on a query and receive scores for the most relevant results. The technical report and additional details are expected to be released soon. If users find the model useful, they are encouraged to provide a star and citation as described in the provided arXiv and blog post references.

instruction: I need to extract the structure of a document to generate a table of contents automatically. Can you help?
description: The Docling model for layout detection is designed to facilitate easy importing and usage like any other Hugging Face model. It is part of a collection that provides document layout analysis tools. The model is based on RT-DETR, a robust transformer-based object detector, and is intended for layout detection in documents. It was trained on an internal dataset for document structure recognition. The model is available under the Apache 2.0 license. If used in academic or research work, it should be cited by referencing the main Docling repository. For questions or issues, users are encouraged to open a discussion on Hugging Face or contact pandahd75@gmail.com.

instruction: Could you analyze this image and show me the relative distances of the buildings?
description: DPT-Large, also known as MiDaS 3.0, is a Dense Prediction Transformer (DPT) model trained on 1.4 million images for monocular depth estimation. It was introduced in the paper by Ranftl et al. (2021) and first released in March 2022. DPT uses the Vision Transformer (ViT) as a backbone and adds a neck and head on top for monocular depth estimation. The model is designed for zero-shot monocular depth estimation and can be used by anyone performing this task. It is typically intended to be fine-tuned for specific applications and should not be used to intentionally create hostile or alienating environments for people. The model was trained on a dataset called MIX 6, which contains around 1.4 million images, and was initialized with ImageNet-pretrained weights. During training, images were resized such that the longer side was 384 pixels, and random square crops of size 384 were used, with random horizontal flips for data augmentation. The model was evaluated on several benchmarks, including DIW WHDR, ETH3D AbsRel, Sintel AbsRel, KITTI δ>1.25, NYU δ>1.25, and TUM δ>1.25, achieving results of 10.82, 0.089, 0.270, 8.46, 8.32, and 9.97, respectively. The model is available under the Apache 2.0 license. The training and inference were conducted on an Intel Xeon Platinum 8280 CPU with 8 physical cores and an NVIDIA RTX 2080 GPU. Users should be made aware of the risks, biases, and limitations of the model. The model is not intended to inform decisions central to human life or flourishing and is an aggregated set of monocular depth image datasets. No additional risk mitigation strategies were considered during model development, and the extent of the risks involved by using the model remains unknown.

instruction: Could you tell me the highest score in the mathematics exam from the given data?
description: The model is a conditional generation model based on the MBart architecture, designed for table question answering (TableQA) in low-resource Indic languages, specifically Hindi and Bengali. It is intended to answer questions over structured tabular data, returning either individual cells or tables as output. The model supports tasks involving structured information retrieval and processing in these languages, where annotated data and neural models are scarce. The model is trained on large-scale datasets generated through a fully automatic data generation process, which is scalable and applicable to any low-resource language with a web presence. Evaluation results show that models trained on these datasets outperform state-of-the-art large language models. The model is evaluated on aspects such as mathematical reasoning capabilities and zero-shot cross-lingual transfer. The model uses a linearized representation of tables, combining the question with the processed table content as input. The model is implemented using the Hugging Face Transformers library and is available for use with the specified preprocessing and inference pipeline. The model is part of a research effort published in the 2024 Conference on Empirical Methods in Natural Language Processing. The model is trained to handle both Hindi and Bengali, with the data generation method being language-agnostic and adaptable to other low-resource languages. The model's performance is benchmarked against existing models, and the research emphasizes the importance of scalable data generation for low-resource language tasks. The model is released alongside datasets and code for further research and application.
domain: Natural Language Processing Table Question Answering

instruction: How can I ensure the system performs consistently across different languages and domains?
description: Snowflake's Arctic-embed-l-v2.0 is an embedding model designed for retrieval performance and inference efficiency, particularly optimized for multilingual workloads. It introduces a new standard for multilingual embedding models, maintaining high-quality retrieval in both English and non-English languages. The model is released under the Apache 2.0 license, making it suitable for enterprise-grade multilingual search and retrieval at scale. Key features include multilingual performance without compromising English retrieval, inference efficiency with 303 million non-embedding parameters, compression-friendly embeddings as small as 128 bytes per vector using Matryoshka Representation Learning (MRL) and quantization-aware training, and support for drop-in replacement with existing libraries and inference engines. It also supports a context window of up to 8192 tokens via RoPE. Arctic-embed-l-v2.0 excels on benchmarks such as MTEB Retrieval, CLEF, and MIRACL, with an average NDCG@10 score of 55.6 on BEIR (15), 55.8 on MIRACL (4), 52.9 on CLEF (Focused), and 54.3 on CLEF (Full). The model can be used with vector truncation to reduce vector size by 4x with less than 3% degradation in quality. When using 256 dimensions, the model achieves a BEIR (15) score of 54.3 (-0.18% degradation), a MIRACL (4) score of 54.3 (-2.70% degradation), a CLEF (Focused) score of 51.9 (-1.81% degradation), and a CLEF (Full) score of 53.4 (-1.53% degradation). The model can be used with libraries such as Sentence Transformers and Huggingface Transformers, and it supports both Python and JavaScript implementations for embedding generation and similarity computation.

instruction: Could you create a video showing a person walking through a snowy forest?
description: Wan2.1 is a comprehensive and open suite of video foundation models designed to push the boundaries of video generation. It offers state-of-the-art performance, consistently outperforming existing open-source models and state-of-the-art commercial solutions across multiple benchmarks. The T2V-1.3B model requires only 8.19 GB VRAM, making it compatible with most consumer-grade GPUs. It can generate a 5-second 480P video on an RTX 4090 in about 4 minutes, with performance comparable to some closed-source models. Wan2.1 supports multiple tasks, including Text-to-Video, Image-to-Video, Video Editing, Text-to-Image, and Video-to-Audio, advancing the field of video generation. It is the first video model capable of generating both Chinese and English text, enhancing its practical applications. The suite includes a powerful video VAE called Wan-VAE, which delivers exceptional efficiency and performance, encoding and decoding 1080P videos of any length while preserving temporal information. The repository contains the I2V-14B model, capable of generating 720P high-definition videos, which has outperformed both closed-source and open-source alternatives in thousands of rounds of human evaluations, achieving state-of-the-art performance. Wan2.1 is built on the mainstream diffusion transformer paradigm, with innovations such as a novel spatio-temporal VAE, scalable training strategies, large-scale data construction, and automated evaluation metrics. The model uses a 3D causal VAE architecture specifically designed for video generation, improving spatio-temporal compression, reducing memory usage, and ensuring temporal causality. Wan2.1 is designed using the Flow Matching framework within the Diffusion Transformers paradigm, with a T5 Encoder to encode multilingual text input and cross-attention in each transformer block embedding the text into the model structure. An MLP with a Linear layer and a SiLU layer processes the input time embeddings and predicts six modulation parameters individually, shared across all transformer blocks. The model supports both 480P and 720P resolutions, with the 1.3B model being less stable at 720P due to limited training at that resolution. The model is available for download via Hugging Face and ModelScope, and can be run using Diffusers or Gradio. The models are licensed under the Apache 2.0 License, with usage restrictions to ensure compliance with applicable laws and ethical standards.

instruction: Can you help me find the latest research on self-driving car technologies?
description: Granite-Embedding-30m-English is a 30M parameter dense bi-encoder embedding model from the Granite Embeddings suite, designed to generate high-quality text embeddings. It produces embedding vectors of size 384 and is trained using a combination of open-source relevance-pair datasets with permissive, enterprise-friendly licenses and IBM-collected and generated datasets. The model maintains competitive scores on academic benchmarks such as BEIR and performs well on enterprise use cases. It is developed using retrieval-oriented pre-training, contrastive fine-tuning, knowledge distillation, and model merging for improved performance. The revision granite-embedding-30m-r1.1 is specifically designed to support multi-turn information retrieval and is trained on data tailored for multi-turn conversational information retrieval. It uses multi-teacher distillation over granite-embedding-30m-english. The model is intended to produce fixed-length vector representations for a given text, which can be used for text similarity, retrieval, and search applications. It is compatible with the SentenceTransformer library and can be used to encode pairs of text and find the similarity between their representations. The model is based on an encoder-only RoBERTa-like transformer architecture, trained internally at IBM Research. It has an embedding size of 384, 6 layers, 12 attention heads, an intermediate size of 1536, uses the GeLU activation function, has a vocabulary size of 50265, and a maximum sequence length of 512. The training data consists of four key sources: unsupervised title-body paired data scraped from the web, publicly available paired data with permissive, enterprise-friendly licenses, IBM-internal paired data targeting specific technical domains, and IBM-generated synthetic data. Notably, the popular MS-MARCO retrieval dataset is not used due to its non-commercial license. The model is trained using IBM's computing cluster, Cognitive Compute Cluster, which is outfitted with NVIDIA A100 80gb GPUs. The data used to train the base language model was filtered to remove text containing hate, abuse, and profanity. The model is trained only for English texts and has a context length of 512 tokens (longer texts will be truncated to this size). The model is twice as fast as other models with similar embedding dimensions while maintaining competitive performance. On the MTEB Retrieval (BEIR) benchmark, it achieves a score of 49.1, and on the CoIR benchmark, it achieves a score of 47.0. The granite-embedding-30m-r1.1 revision maintains the fast speed of granite-embedding-30m-english while demonstrating strong performance on multi-turn information retrieval benchmarks. On the MTEB Retrieval (BEIR) benchmark, it achieves a score of 48.9, on the MT-RAG benchmark, it achieves a score of 52.33, and on the Mdoc2dial benchmark, it achieves a score of 85.78.

instruction: What is the percentage increase in sales from 2021 to 2022 for the 'Electronics' category?
description: This model is a TAPAS small model fine-tuned on WikiTable Questions (WTQ). It has two versions: the default version corresponds to the `tapas_wtq_wikisql_sqa_inter_masklm_small_reset` checkpoint, and the non-default version corresponds to `tapas_wtq_wikisql_sqa_inter_masklm_small`. The model was pre-trained on masked language modeling (MLM) and an additional step of intermediate pre-training, followed by fine-tuning on SQA, WikiSQL, and finally WTQ. It uses relative position embeddings, resetting the position index at every cell of the table. The non-default version uses absolute position embeddings. TAPAS is a BERT-like transformer model pre-trained on a large corpus of English data from Wikipedia in a self-supervised fashion. It was pre-trained with two objectives: masked language modeling, where 15% of the words in a flattened table and associated context are randomly masked and the model must predict them, and intermediate pre-training, where the model is trained to classify whether a sentence is supported or refuted by the contents of a table using a balanced dataset of syntactically created examples. This allows the model to learn a bidirectional representation of tables and associated texts. The model can be used for tasks such as answering questions about a table or determining whether a sentence is entailed or refuted by the contents of a table. Fine-tuning involves adding a cell selection head and aggregation head on top of the pre-trained model and jointly training these with the base model. The model was fine-tuned on 32 Cloud TPU v3 cores for 50,000 steps with a maximum sequence length of 512 and a batch size of 512. The optimizer used is Adam with a learning rate of 1.93581e-5 and a warmup ratio of 0.128960. An inductive bias is added to ensure the model only selects cells of the same column. The model's performance on the development set for the small version is 0.3681 for the noreset version and 0.3762 for the reset version. The team releasing TAPAS did not write a model card for this model; this model card was written by the Hugging Face team and contributors.
domain: Natural Language Processing Table Question Answering

instruction: I need help verifying my solution to this math problem. Can you provide a second opinion?
description: SuperCorrect is a novel two-stage fine-tuning method designed to improve both reasoning accuracy and self-correction ability in large language models (LLMs). It addresses the limitations of smaller models, such as Llama-3-8B and DeepSeekMath-Base, which struggle with complex mathematical reasoning due to their inability to effectively identify and correct reasoning errors. SuperCorrect introduces a framework that uses a large teacher model to supervise and correct the reasoning and reflection processes of a smaller student model. In the first stage, hierarchical high-level and detailed thought templates are extracted from the teacher model to guide the student model in eliciting more fine-grained reasoning thoughts. In the second stage, cross-model collaborative direct preference optimization (DPO) is introduced to enhance the student model's self-correction abilities by following the teacher's correction traces during training. This approach enables the student model to effectively locate and resolve erroneous thoughts using error-driven insights from the teacher model, thereby breaking the bottleneck of its thoughts and acquiring new skills and knowledge to tackle challenging problems. The SuperCorrect-7B model has been shown to significantly outperform DeepSeekMath-7B by 7.8%/5.3% and Qwen2.5-Math-7B by 15.1%/6.3% on MATH/GSM8K benchmarks, achieving new state-of-the-art performance among all 7B models. SuperCorrect is based on several open-source models and incorporates ideas from prior works, including the concept of thought templates.

instruction: Where in the image is the phrase 'air space consolidation' referring to?
description: This is a checkpoint for Stable Diffusion fine-tuned with CXR-BERT on the MIMIC-CXR dataset, as presented in the paper. The model introduces a novel approach to phrase grounding in medical imaging, demonstrating that generative text-to-image diffusion models, specifically fine-tuned Stable Diffusion, can achieve superior zero-shot performance compared to traditional discriminative methods. Key innovations include leveraging cross-attention maps from generative diffusion models for phrase grounding, fine-tuning diffusion models with a frozen, domain-specific language model (CXR-BERT) to significantly improve performance in medical contexts, and introducing Bimodal Bias Merging (BBM), a novel post-processing technique that aligns text and image biases to refine cross-attention maps and enhance localization accuracy. The model aims to map natural language phrases from clinical reports to specific image regions, facilitating disease localization. Training was performed for 30,000 steps using eight A100 GPUs.

instruction: Could you detect and label the text regions in this academic paper?
description: This model is a fine-tuned version of an unspecified base model on an unknown dataset. It achieves a loss of 0.0000 and an accuracy of 1.0 on the evaluation set. The model was trained using the ADAMW_TORCH optimizer with betas of (0.9, 0.999), an epsilon of 1e-08, and no additional optimizer arguments. The learning rate was set to 5e-05 and was scheduled using the reduce_lr_on_plateau scheduler with a warmup ratio of 0.06. The training batch size was 4, the evaluation batch size was 8, and the training was conducted for 100 epochs with a random seed of 42. During training, the training loss decreased from 5.3733 to 0.0, and the validation loss decreased from 4.1888 to 0.0000. The accuracy on the validation set increased from 0.42 to 1.0. The model was trained for a total of 700 steps. The framework versions used include Transformers 4.48.1, Pytorch 2.3.1.post300, Datasets 3.2.0, and Tokenizers 0.21.0.

instruction: How does the inclusion of diverse training data affect the performance of language models across different languages?
description: The Llama 3.2 collection is a set of multilingual large language models (LLMs) available in 1B and 3B parameter sizes, designed for text-in and text-out tasks. These models are pretrained and instruction-tuned for multilingual dialogue use cases, including agentic retrieval and summarization tasks. They outperform many open source and closed chat models on common industry benchmarks. The models are developed by Meta and use an optimized transformer architecture. The instruction-tuned versions are aligned with human preferences for helpfulness and safety through supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and reinforcement learning with human feedback (RLHF). Llama 3.2 supports English, German, French, Italian, Portuguese, Hindi, Spanish, and Thai, with the potential for developers to fine-tune for additional languages, provided they comply with the Llama 3.2 Community License and Acceptable Use Policy. The models use Grouped-Query Attention (GQA) for improved inference scalability and have a context length of 128k tokens for the text-only versions and 8k tokens for the quantized versions. The training data consists of a new mix of publicly available online data, with a knowledge cutoff in December 2023. The models are available under a custom, commercial license agreement. Llama 3.2 is intended for commercial and research use in multiple languages, with instruction-tuned models suitable for assistant-like chat and agentic applications, and pretrained models adaptable for various natural language generation tasks. Quantized models are suitable for on-device use-cases with limited compute resources. The models were trained using custom training libraries and Meta's GPU cluster, with a total of 916k GPU hours of computation and an estimated 240 tons of location-based CO2eq emissions. The training data includes up to 9 trillion tokens, with knowledge distillation used after pruning to recover performance. The models were fine-tuned using SFT, Rejection Sampling (RS), and Direct Preference Optimization (DPO). Quantization schemes include 4-bit groupwise quantization for weights and 8-bit per-token dynamic quantization for activations, with additional quantization for the classification and embedding layers. Quantization-aware training (QAT) with low-rank adaptation (LoRA) was used, and SpinQuant and generative post-training quantization (GPTQ) were applied. The models were evaluated on standard benchmarks, showing varying performance across different tasks and quantization methods. Inference time metrics were measured on an Android OnePlus 12 device, showing improvements in decode speed, time-to-first-token, and memory usage for quantized models. The models are part of a responsible release approach, with safety mitigations including safety fine-tuning, system safeguards, and red teaming exercises. Developers are encouraged to deploy the models with additional safety guardrails and to conduct their own evaluations and safety testing. The models are not designed to be deployed in isolation but as part of an overall AI system. The models introduce new capabilities that require specific considerations, especially in constrained environments like mobile devices. The models are part of Meta's efforts to promote generative AI safety through industry partnerships, grants, and community contributions. Ethical considerations include openness, inclusivity, and helpfulness, with the models designed to serve a wide range of use cases while respecting user autonomy and free expression. Developers should be aware of the potential for inaccurate, biased, or objectionable responses and perform safety testing and tuning tailored to their specific applications.

instruction: Which department has the highest average salary?
description: This model is a TAPAS large model fine-tuned on Sequential Question Answering (SQA). It has two versions: the default version corresponds to the `tapas_sqa_inter_masklm_large_reset` checkpoint, and the other version is `no_reset`, corresponding to `tapas_sqa_inter_masklm_large`. The model was pre-trained on masked language modeling (MLM) and an additional intermediate pre-training step, and then fine-tuned on SQA. It uses relative position embeddings, resetting the position index at every cell of the table. The non-default version uses absolute position embeddings. TAPAS is a BERT-like transformer model pre-trained on a large corpus of English data from Wikipedia in a self-supervised fashion. It was pre-trained with two objectives: masked language modeling, where 15% of the words in a flattened table and associated context are randomly masked and the model must predict them, and intermediate pre-training, where the model is trained to classify whether a sentence is supported or refuted by the contents of a table using a balanced dataset of millions of syntactically created training examples. This allows the model to learn a bidirectional representation of tables and associated texts, which can be used for downstream tasks such as answering questions about a table or determining whether a sentence is entailed or refuted by the contents of a table. Fine-tuning involves adding a cell selection head on top of the pre-trained model and jointly training this classification head with the base model on SQA. The model can be used for answering questions related to a table in a conversational setup. The texts are lowercased and tokenized using WordPiece with a vocabulary size of 30,000. The model inputs are of the form [CLS] Question [SEP] Flattened table [SEP]. The model was fine-tuned on 32 Cloud TPU v3 cores for 200,000 steps with a maximum sequence length of 512 and a batch size of 128. Fine-tuning takes approximately 20 hours and uses the Adam optimizer with a learning rate of 1.25e-5 and a warmup ratio of 0.2. An inductive bias is added so that the model only selects cells of the same column, as reflected by the `select_one_column` parameter of `TapasConfig`. The team releasing TAPAS did not write a model card for this model, and this model card was written by the Hugging Face team and contributors. The model achieves a dev accuracy of 0.7289 for the LARGE reset version and 0.7223 for the LARGE noreset version. The disclaimer indicates that the model card was not written by the original TAPAS team.
domain: Natural Language Processing Table Question Answering

instruction: How can I get a representation of this picture for further processing in a machine learning project?
description: The Vision Transformer (ViT) is a transformer encoder model, similar in structure to BERT, designed for supervised image classification on ImageNet. It has been adapted for self-supervised image feature extraction, allowing the model to learn meaningful features (embeddings) from images without requiring labeled data. The DINOv2 version of the ViT introduces a modification to address artifacts in attention maps, which are caused by the use of image patches as 'registers'. The authors propose adding new tokens, called 'register' tokens, during pre-training, which are discarded afterward. This modification results in no artifacts, interpretable attention maps, and improved performance. The model does not include any fine-tuned heads and is intended for use as a pre-trained encoder to extract features for downstream tasks. A linear layer can be added on top of the [CLS] token to create a standard classifier for labeled image datasets. The model can be used for feature extraction, and users are encouraged to look for fine-tuned versions for specific tasks. The model was introduced in a paper by Darcet et al. and is available through the Hugging Face model repository. The model card was written by the Hugging Face team, as the original authors did not provide one.

instruction: Can you detect all the vehicles in this street scene image in real-time?
description: The RT-DETRv2 model was proposed by Wenyu Lv, Yian Zhao, Qinyao Chang, Kui Huang, Guanzhong Wang, and Yi Liu. RT-DETRv2 refines RT-DETR by introducing selective multi-scale feature extraction, a discrete sampling operator for broader deployment compatibility, and improved training strategies like dynamic data augmentation and scale-adaptive hyperparameters. These changes enhance flexibility and practicality while maintaining real-time performance. RT-DETRv2 consistently outperforms its predecessor across all model sizes while maintaining the same real-time speeds. RT-DETRv2 is trained on COCO (Lin et al. [2014]) train2017 and validated on COCO val2017 dataset. We report the standard AP metrics (averaged over uniformly sampled IoU thresholds ranging from 0.50 − 0.95 with a step size of 0.05), and APval50 commonly used in real scenarios. RT-DETRv2 is ideal for real-time object detection in diverse applications such as autonomous driving, surveillance systems, robotics, and retail analytics. Its enhanced flexibility and deployment-friendly design make it suitable for both edge devices and large-scale systems and ensures high accuracy and speed in dynamic, real-world environments.

instruction: Kalimat 'Aplikasi ini membuat hidup saya lebih mudah' terdengar positif atau negatif?
description: This model is fine-tuned from `indobenchmark/indobert-base-p1` for binary sentiment classification (Positive/Negative) on Indonesian text. It is developed by agufsamudra and is a text classification model designed for the Indonesian language. The model is intended for binary sentiment classification tasks in Indonesian language texts, predicting whether a given text expresses positive or negative sentiment. It is not designed to classify neutral sentiments or handle languages other than Indonesian. The model's performance is reliant on the quality and diversity of the training data, and biases in the dataset may influence predictions. The model is limited to binary sentiment analysis and may not perform well on ambiguous or mixed-sentiment texts. Users should validate predictions on a case-by-case basis for high-stakes applications. The model was trained on a dataset of Indonesian text reviews from Play Store applications, labeled for binary sentiment analysis (Positive and Negative), with an equal distribution of positive and negative examples to ensure balanced learning. The training procedure used the AdamW optimizer, a learning rate of 3e-6, 3 epochs, and a max sequence length of 128 tokens. The model was evaluated on a separate test dataset of 20,000 samples (10,000 Positive, 10,000 Negative) using standard metrics including accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score. The model achieved 95.28% accuracy on the training set and 95.56% accuracy on the testing set. Precision, recall, and F1-score were all 96% for both the training and testing sets. The model is based on IndoBERT, a pre-trained transformer model tailored for Indonesian text, and was fine-tuned for binary classification tasks. The model was developed using Google Collab GPU hardware and Python, PyTorch, and Transformers library software.

instruction: Show me the location of the cat and the dog in the picture.
description: OWL-ViT, short for Vision Transformer for Open-World Localization, is a zero-shot text-conditioned object detection model that allows users to query an image with one or multiple text queries. It was proposed by Matthias Minderer, Alexey Gritsenko, Austin Stone, Maxim Neumann, Dirk Weissenborn, Alexey Dosovitskiy, Aravindh Mahendran, Anurag Arnab, Mostafa Dehghani, Zhuoran Shen, Xiao Wang, Xiaohua Zhai, Thomas Kipf, and Neil Houlsby. OWL-ViT uses CLIP as its multi-modal backbone, combining a ViT-like Transformer for visual features and a causal language model for text features. To enable detection, the final token pooling layer of the vision model is removed, and a lightweight classification and box head is attached to each transformer output token. Open-vocabulary classification is achieved by replacing the fixed classification layer weights with class-name embeddings from the text model. The CLIP backbone is trained from scratch and fine-tuned end-to-end with the classification and box heads on standard detection datasets using a bipartite matching loss. The model uses a CLIP backbone with a ViT-L/14 Transformer architecture as an image encoder and a masked self-attention Transformer as a text encoder. These encoders are trained to maximize the similarity of (image, text) pairs via a contrastive loss. The CLIP backbone is trained from scratch and fine-tuned together with the box and class prediction heads with an object detection objective. The CLIP backbone was trained on publicly available image-caption data, including data crawled from the internet and pre-existing image datasets. The prediction heads of OWL-ViT, along with the CLIP backbone, are fine-tuned on publicly available object detection datasets. The model is intended as a research output for the research community, enabling researchers to better understand and explore zero-shot, text-conditioned object detection. The primary intended users are AI researchers, who are expected to use the model to study robustness, generalization, and other capabilities, biases, and constraints of computer vision models. The model was released in May 2022.

instruction: Can you suggest a way to match job seekers with job postings based on the similarity of their skills and requirements?
description: PLaMo-Embedding-1B is a Japanese text embedding model developed by Preferred Networks, Inc. It converts Japanese text input into numerical vectors and can be used for a wide range of applications, including information retrieval, text classification, and clustering. As of early April 2025, it achieved top-class scores on a benchmark for Japanese text embedding, demonstrating particularly outstanding performance in retrieval tasks. PLaMo-Embedding-1B is released under a license that allows for free use, including for commercial purposes. The model has a size of 1B parameters, a maximum context length of 4096 tokens, and produces embeddings with a dimensionality of 2048. It uses cosine similarity as its similarity function. The model supports two encoding methods: `encode_query` for query texts in information retrieval and `encode_document` for other texts or sentences. Texts exceeding the model's maximum context length are truncated, and for `encode_query`, a prefix is added internally, making the effective maximum context length slightly shorter. The model was evaluated using a benchmark for Japanese text embedding, achieving an average score of 76.10, with a retrieval score of 79.94, an STS score of 83.14, a classification score of 77.20, a reranking score of 93.57, a clustering score of 53.47, and a pair classification score of 62.37. These scores were measured with a context length of 1024, although the model supports a context length of up to 4096. Evaluation at 4096 does not significantly affect the average score. The model is available on the Hugging Face Hub and can be used with the `sentencepiece`, `torch`, and `transformers` libraries. For information retrieval, the similarity between vectors obtained by inputting sentences into the model is high for similar sentences and low for dissimilar sentences.

instruction: How would you evaluate a patient with persistent headaches and blurred vision?
description: MedReason is a large-scale, high-quality medical reasoning dataset designed to enable faithful and explainable medical problem-solving in large language models (LLMs). The dataset is generated by utilizing a structured medical knowledge graph (KG) to convert clinical question-answer (QA) pairs into logical chains of reasoning, or 'thinking paths.' The pipeline generates detailed reasoning for various medical questions from seven medical datasets, resulting in a dataset of 32,682 question-answer pairs, each with detailed, step-by-step explanations. By fine-tuning with this dataset, the best-performing model achieves state-of-the-art performance. The MedReason-8B model is based on a base model and is designed to improve medical reasoning capabilities through supervised fine-tuning (SFT) using the MedReason data. The model can be deployed using tools like Sglang, and example code is provided for direct inference and deployment. The model is evaluated on challenging and common medical QA benchmarks, demonstrating accurate reasoning with reliable knowledge. Evaluation is performed using prompts that allow the model to respond freely, with options for stricter prompts to extract more precise answers. The MedReason dataset and models are open-sourced, along with the code for data generation, model training, and evaluation.

instruction: What objects are present in this photo of a kitchen scene?
description: MM Grounding DINO (tiny variant) is a model proposed by Xiangyu Zhao, Yicheng Chen, Shilin Xu, Xiangtai Li, Xinjiang Wang, Yining Li, and Haian Huang. It improves upon previous models by enhancing the contrastive class head and removing parameter sharing in the decoder, which leads to improved zero-shot detection performance. Specifically, it achieves 50.6 AP (an increase of 2.2) on COCO and 31.9 val AP (an increase of 11.8) and 41.4 minival AP (an increase of 12.6) on LVIS. The model is designed for zero-shot object detection and can be used with raw inputs. It was trained on a combination of datasets including O365, GoldG, GRIT, and V3Det. Evaluation results show that the model performs well across different backbones and pre-training data combinations. The model can be accessed through the Transformers library using the identifier 'rziga/mm_grounding_dino_tiny_o365v1_goldg_grit_v3det'.

instruction: What does the energy profile of a reasoning chain tell us about its quality?
description: This study introduces a novel framework for analyzing and optimizing multi-hop reasoning in AI systems by applying principles from Hamiltonian mechanics and differential geometry. The framework maps reasoning chains in embedding spaces to Hamiltonian systems, enabling the use of analytical tools from classical physics. A Hamiltonian function is defined to balance the progression of reasoning (kinetic energy) against the relevance to the question (potential energy). The method is applied to a dataset of reasoning chains from a multi-hop question-answering task, revealing patterns that distinguish valid from invalid reasoning. The project uses the OpenBookQA (OBQA) dataset, which assesses the question answering and reasoning abilities of AI systems. Key findings include that valid reasoning chains exhibit lower and more stable Hamiltonian energy profiles compared to invalid chains, and that trajectory curvature and conservation of angular momentum-like quantities are effective discriminators of reasoning validity. The framework suggests potential fundamental principles governing effective cognitive processes in AI systems. The research is detailed in the paper 'Optimizing AI Reasoning: A Hamiltonian Dynamics Approach to Multi-Hop Question Answering' by Javier Marín, available as an arXiv preprint. The implementation and analysis are provided in a Jupyter notebook. The project is licensed under the MIT License.
domain: Natural Language Processing Table Question Answering

instruction: Find the most relevant information about 5G network deployment in urban areas.
description: Granite-Embedding-30m-English is a 30M parameter dense bi-encoder embedding model from the Granite Embeddings suite, designed to generate high-quality text embeddings. It produces embedding vectors of size 384 and is trained using a combination of open-source relevance-pair datasets with permissive, enterprise-friendly licenses and IBM-collected and generated datasets. The model maintains competitive scores on academic benchmarks such as BEIR and performs well on enterprise use cases. It is developed using retrieval-oriented pre-training, contrastive fine-tuning, knowledge distillation, and model merging for improved performance. The revision granite-embedding-30m-r1.1 is specifically designed to support multi-turn information retrieval and is trained on data tailored for multi-turn conversational information retrieval. It uses multi-teacher distillation over granite-embedding-30m-english. The model is intended to produce fixed-length vector representations for a given text, which can be used for text similarity, retrieval, and search applications. It is compatible with the SentenceTransformer library and can be used to encode pairs of text and find the similarity between their representations. The model is based on an encoder-only RoBERTa-like transformer architecture, trained internally at IBM Research. It has an embedding size of 384, 6 layers, 12 attention heads, an intermediate size of 1536, uses the GeLU activation function, has a vocabulary size of 50265, and a maximum sequence length of 512. The training data consists of four key sources: unsupervised title-body paired data scraped from the web, publicly available paired data with permissive, enterprise-friendly licenses, IBM-internal paired data targeting specific technical domains, and IBM-generated synthetic data. Notably, the popular MS-MARCO retrieval dataset is not used due to its non-commercial license. The model is trained using IBM's computing cluster, Cognitive Compute Cluster, which is outfitted with NVIDIA A100 80gb GPUs. The data used to train the base language model was filtered to remove text containing hate, abuse, and profanity. The model is trained only for English texts and has a context length of 512 tokens (longer texts will be truncated to this size). The model is twice as fast as other models with similar embedding dimensions while maintaining competitive performance. On the MTEB Retrieval (BEIR) benchmark, it achieves a score of 49.1, and on the CoIR benchmark, it achieves a score of 47.0. The granite-embedding-30m-r1.1 revision maintains the fast speed of granite-embedding-30m-english while demonstrating strong performance on multi-turn information retrieval benchmarks. On the MTEB Retrieval (BEIR) benchmark, it achieves a score of 48.9, on the MT-RAG benchmark, it achieves a score of 52.33, and on the Mdoc2dial benchmark, it achieves a score of 85.78.

instruction: Does this photo of a document show a signature? I need your help to check.
description: YOLOS is a Vision Transformer (ViT) trained using the DETR loss. A base-sized YOLOS model achieves 42 AP on COCO validation 2017, similar to DETR and more complex frameworks such as Faster R-CNN. This model is a finetuned version of YOLOS, specifically adapted for detecting handwritten signatures in document images. It is intended for use in scanned documents, contracts, or forms. The model was finetuned using a dataset built from two public datasets, unified and processed by an external contributor. The processed dataset contains 1,980 training images, 420 validation images, and 419 testing images, all in COCO JSON format with a resolution of 640x640 pixels. Performance metrics on the testing set include an mAP50 of 0.856 and an mAP50-95 of 0.395. Inference times are 0.262 seconds on CPU and 0.014 seconds on GPU, measured on a laptop with an Intel Core i7-9750H CPU and an NVIDIA GeForce GTX 1650 GPU. The model is licensed under the Apache 2.0 license, which allows for permissive use, including commercial applications, without the requirement to share modified code. The model must not be used for fraudulent purposes, such as forging signatures or any illegal activity. It is not suitable for detecting non-signature elements in documents and should not be solely relied on for high-stakes decisions without human validation. The model may exhibit bias if the training data lacks diversity in signature styles or cultural context. False positives and negatives can occur, which may impact document validation. Performance may degrade on poor-quality images or in challenging visual conditions such as noise or lighting. To improve performance, it is recommended to fine-tune the model with diverse and representative samples, include human oversight for critical use cases, use clean, high-resolution images, and apply preprocessing if needed. The model is designed for legitimate signature detection in documents and should be used in accordance with legal and ethical standards, ensuring privacy and responsible deployment.

instruction: I need confirmation that this image is not a deepfake—can you assist?
description: The Deep-Fake-Detector-v2-Model is a deep learning model designed to detect deepfake images. It is based on the Vision Transformer (ViT) architecture, specifically the `google/vit-base-patch16-224-in21k` model, which has been fine-tuned on a dataset of real and deepfake images. The model classifies images into two categories: 'Realism' or 'Deepfake'. It achieves a precision of 0.9683 for 'Realism' and 0.8826 for 'Deepfake', with a recall of 0.8708 for 'Realism' and 0.9715 for 'Deepfake'. The F1 scores are 0.9170 for 'Realism' and 0.9249 for 'Deepfake', with an overall accuracy of 0.9212 on a test set of 56,001 images. The model processes RGB images resized to 224x224 pixels and uses a binary classification head for output. The Vision Transformer architecture divides the input image into 16x16 pixel patches, which are embedded and processed by a transformer encoder using multi-head self-attention mechanisms. The model was trained using the AdamW optimizer with a learning rate of 1e-6, a batch size of 32 for training and 8 for evaluation, and for two epochs. Data augmentation techniques included random rotation (±90 degrees), random sharpness adjustment, and random resizing and cropping. The model was fine-tuned using Hugging Face's `Trainer` API and evaluated using accuracy, F1 score, and a confusion matrix. The training dataset consists of real images of human faces and deepfake images generated using advanced AI techniques. The model may not generalize well to other deepfake datasets or domains, and its performance may degrade on low-resolution or heavily compressed images. It is designed for image classification and does not detect deepfake videos directly. The model should not be used for malicious purposes, such as creating or spreading deepfakes. It may inherit biases from the training dataset, and users should be informed when deepfake detection tools are used to analyze their content. Future work includes extending the model to detect deepfake videos, improving generalization by training on larger and more diverse datasets, and incorporating explainability techniques to provide insights into model predictions.

instruction: Can you locate the pedestrians in this autonomous vehicle camera feed?
description: The RT-DETRv2 model was proposed by Wenyu Lv, Yian Zhao, Qinyao Chang, Kui Huang, Guanzhong Wang, and Yi Liu. RT-DETRv2 refines RT-DETR by introducing selective multi-scale feature extraction, a discrete sampling operator for broader deployment compatibility, and improved training strategies like dynamic data augmentation and scale-adaptive hyperparameters. These changes enhance flexibility and practicality while maintaining real-time performance. RT-DETRv2 consistently outperforms its predecessor across all model sizes while maintaining the same real-time speeds. The model is trained on COCO (Lin et al. [2014]) train2017 and validated on COCO val2017 dataset. We report the standard AP metrics (averaged over uniformly sampled IoU thresholds ranging from 0.50 − 0.95 with a step size of 0.05), and APval50 commonly used in real scenarios. RT-DETRv2 is ideal for real-time object detection in diverse applications such as autonomous driving, surveillance systems, robotics, and retail analytics. Its enhanced flexibility and deployment-friendly design make it suitable for both edge devices and large-scale systems and ensures high accuracy and speed in dynamic, real-world environments.

description: VideoLLaMA 3 is a state-of-the-art series of multimodal foundation models designed to excel in both image and video understanding tasks. These models leverage advanced architectures to process and interpret visual content across various contexts. They are specifically designed to address complex multimodal challenges, such as integrating textual and visual information, extracting insights from sequential video data, and performing high-level reasoning over both dynamic and static visual scenes. The model series includes variants such as VideoLLaMA3-7B, VideoLLaMA3-2B, VideoLLaMA3-7B-Image, and VideoLLaMA3-2B-Image, each based on different base models including Qwen2.5-7B and Qwen2.5-1.5B. A tuned vision encoder based on siglip-so400m-patch14-384 is also available for the VideoLLaMA3-7B model to support wider application. The models are intended for tasks involving video and image understanding, including video description and multimodal reasoning. The model is available for use with specific code and dependencies, including the use of AutoModelForCausalLM and AutoProcessor from the transformers library. The model supports video input with parameters such as video path, frames per second, and maximum number of frames. The model is designed to generate text-based responses to video content. The model has been developed by a team of researchers from DAMO-NLP-SG and is associated with academic publications available on arXiv. Users are encouraged to cite the relevant papers if they use the model for research or applications.

instruction: Please transcribe this English voice note and ensure it’s formatted as a clean paragraph.
description: Whisper is a pre-trained model for automatic speech recognition (ASR) and speech translation. Trained on 680,000 hours of labelled speech data using large-scale weak supervision, Whisper models demonstrate strong generalization capabilities across many datasets and domains without the need for fine-tuning. Whisper is a Transformer-based encoder-decoder model, also referred to as a sequence-to-sequence model. The models were trained on either English-only data or multilingual data. English-only models were trained for speech recognition, while multilingual models were trained for both speech recognition and speech translation. For speech recognition, the model predicts transcriptions in the same language as the audio. For speech translation, the model predicts transcriptions in a different language from the audio. Whisper checkpoints come in five configurations of varying model sizes. The smallest four are trained on either English-only or multilingual data, while the largest checkpoints are multilingual only. The model is used in conjunction with a WhisperProcessor to pre-process audio inputs into log-Mel spectrograms and post-process model outputs into text. The model is informed of the task to perform (transcription or translation) by passing context tokens to the decoder. These context tokens include a transcription token, a language token, a task token, and optionally a no-timestamp token. Context tokens can be forced or un-forced. Forced tokens allow control over the output language and task, while un-forced tokens let the model predict them automatically. Whisper supports English to English transcription, French to French transcription, and French to English translation. The model can be evaluated using datasets like LibriSpeech, and it has a word error rate of approximately 2.9% on the LibriSpeech test clean dataset. Whisper is designed to work on audio samples up to 30 seconds in duration but can transcribe longer audio using a chunking algorithm. The model can also predict sequence-level timestamps. While the pre-trained model generalizes well, its performance can be improved through fine-tuning, especially for certain languages and tasks. Whisper is primarily trained and evaluated on ASR and speech translation to English tasks and shows strong results in approximately 10 languages. It may also support additional capabilities like voice activity detection, speaker classification, or speaker diarization, but these have not been robustly evaluated. Users are cautioned against using Whisper to transcribe recordings without consent or for subjective classification. The model is not appropriate for high-risk domains where accuracy flaws could lead to significant negative outcomes. Whisper is trained on 680,000 hours of audio data from the internet, with 65% in English, 18% in non-English audio with English transcripts, and 17% in non-English audio with corresponding non-English transcripts. The non-English data represents 98 different languages. Performance in a given language is correlated with the amount of training data available for that language. Whisper exhibits improved robustness to accents, background noise, technical language, and zero-shot translation from multiple languages into English. However, the model may hallucinate text not present in the audio input due to its language prediction capabilities. Performance varies across languages, with lower accuracy observed in low-resource or low-discoverability languages and across different accents and dialects. The model may also generate repetitive text, which can be partially mitigated with beam search and temperature scheduling. Whisper's transcription capabilities may be used to improve accessibility tools and enable near-real-time speech recognition and translation. However, the release of Whisper also raises dual-use concerns, as it could facilitate the development of surveillance technologies. The model may also have some capability to recognize specific individuals, which could present safety concerns. The broader implications of Whisper include potential economic impacts from disparate model performance and the affordability of large-scale audio transcription and translation.

description: BiRefNet is a bilateral reference network designed for high-resolution dichotomous image segmentation. It is an arbitrary shape adaptable model that was trained on input resolutions ranging from 256x256 to 2304x2304, demonstrating robustness on inputs of any shape. The model is intended for general segmentation tasks and has achieved state-of-the-art performance on three tasks: DIS (Dichotomous Image Segmentation), HRSOD (High-Resolution Salient Object Detection), and COD (Complementary Object Detection). It was trained on the DIS-TR dataset and validated on DIS-TE and DIS-VD datasets. The model's performance exceeds that of BiRefNet-general and BiRefNet_HR-general on the reserved validation sets. The model supports inference on arbitrary input resolutions and can be used for object extraction through segmentation mask generation. The model is implemented using PyTorch and can be loaded from HuggingFace or used locally with weights from HuggingFace or a local space. The model is described in the paper 'Bilateral Reference for High-Resolution Dichotomous Image Segmentation,' published in CAAI Artificial Intelligence Research in 2024. The authors are Peng Zheng, Dehong Gao, Deng-Ping Fan, Li Liu, Jorma Laaksonen, Wanli Ouyang, and Nicu Sebe, affiliated with Nankai University, Northwestern Polytechnical University, National University of Defense Technology, Aalto University, Shanghai AI Laboratory, and University of Trento, respectively. The model is available through an official GitHub repository and HuggingFace, and online demos for inference are provided. The model weights are publicly available for use and evaluation.

instruction: What is the total number of employees across all departments?
description: This model is a TAPAS large model fine-tuned on Sequential Question Answering (SQA). It has two versions: the default version corresponds to the `tapas_sqa_inter_masklm_large_reset` checkpoint, and the other version is `no_reset`, corresponding to `tapas_sqa_inter_masklm_large`. The model was pre-trained on masked language modeling (MLM) and an additional intermediate pre-training step, and then fine-tuned on SQA. It uses relative position embeddings, resetting the position index at every cell of the table. The non-default version uses absolute position embeddings. TAPAS is a BERT-like transformer model pre-trained on a large corpus of English data from Wikipedia in a self-supervised fashion. It was pre-trained with two objectives: masked language modeling, where 15% of the words in a flattened table and associated context are randomly masked and the model must predict them, and intermediate pre-training, where the model is trained to classify whether a sentence is supported or refuted by the contents of a table using a balanced dataset of millions of syntactically created training examples. This allows the model to learn a bidirectional representation of tables and associated texts, which can be used for downstream tasks such as answering questions about a table or determining whether a sentence is entailed or refuted by the contents of a table. Fine-tuning involves adding a cell selection head on top of the pre-trained model and jointly training this classification head with the base model on SQA. The model can be used for answering questions related to a table in a conversational setup. The texts are lowercased and tokenized using WordPiece with a vocabulary size of 30,000. The model inputs are of the form [CLS] Question [SEP] Flattened table [SEP]. The model was fine-tuned on 32 Cloud TPU v3 cores for 200,000 steps with a maximum sequence length of 512 and a batch size of 128. Fine-tuning takes approximately 20 hours and uses the Adam optimizer with a learning rate of 1.25e-5 and a warmup ratio of 0.2. An inductive bias is added so that the model only selects cells of the same column, as reflected by the `select_one_column` parameter of `TapasConfig`. The team releasing TAPAS did not write a model card for this model, and this model card was written by the Hugging Face team and contributors. The model achieves a dev accuracy of 0.7289 for the LARGE reset version and 0.7223 for the LARGE noreset version. The disclaimer indicates that the model card was not written by the original TAPAS team.
domain: Natural Language Processing Table Question Answering

instruction: What major policy code would apply to this excerpt from a political debate?
description: This is an xlm-roberta-large model that has been fine-tuned on Norwegian training data containing texts from the 'party' domain, labeled according to the Comparative Agendas Project's master codebook. The model is designed for text classification tasks, specifically for identifying major policy topic codes within political texts. It supports the classification of text into 21 CAP major policy topic codes, with an additional label '999' for texts that contain no relevant policy content. The model's architecture is based on the XLM-RoBERTa large transformer model. It was evaluated on a test set of 21,915 examples, achieving an overall accuracy of 0.83. Performance metrics per label include precision, recall, and F1-score, with values ranging from 0.70 to 0.95 across different labels. The model requires a token for access due to gated access restrictions. It is used by an open-source and free natural language processing tool designed to support comparative research projects. Model performance can be improved by expanding training data, and contributions of CAP-coded corpora are welcomed. The model uses the sentencepiece tokenizer, and users may need to install it manually for versions of the Transformers library prior to 4.27. If a RuntimeError occurs when loading the model, adding the parameter 'ignore_mismatched_sizes=True' may resolve the issue.

instruction: Can the video be enhanced to feel more realistic and less mechanical?
description: Wan2.1-Fun-Reward-LoRAs is a set of pre-trained models designed to enhance video generation by aligning outputs with human preferences using the Reward Backpropagation technique. These models are implemented as LoRAs (Low-Rank Adaptations) and can be used as plug-ins to improve the performance of corresponding base models. The provided LoRAs include both HPS v2.1 and MPS reward models for different base models, such as Wan2.1-Fun-1.3B-InP and Wan2.1-Fun-14B-InP. Each LoRA is trained with specific parameters, including rank and network alpha values, and varying batch sizes and training steps. For example, the HPS v2.1 reward LoRA for Wan2.1-Fun-1.3B-InP is trained with a batch size of 8 for 5,000 steps, while the MPS reward LoRA for the same base model is trained with a batch size of 8 for 7,500 steps. The models are intended to be used in conjunction with the base models to refine video outputs based on reward signals. However, there are limitations: after a certain point in training, the reward may continue to increase without a corresponding improvement in video quality, as the model may learn to exploit shortcuts such as adding background artifacts. Additionally, suitable preference models for video generation are currently lacking, and using image-based preference models can lead to a reduction in the dynamism of generated videos, even when mitigated by using only the first frame for reward computation.

instruction: Is there a way to generate a 3D model of a tree from a natural scene photo?
description: Direct3D-S2 is a scalable 3D generation framework based on sparse volumes, designed to generate high-resolution 3D shapes using volumetric representations such as Signed Distance Functions (SDFs). The model introduces a novel Spatial Sparse Attention (SSA) mechanism that enhances the efficiency of Diffusion Transformer (DiT) computations on sparse volumetric data. SSA allows the model to process large token sets within sparse volumes, achieving a 3.9× speedup in the forward pass and a 9.6× speedup in the backward pass. The framework also includes a variational autoencoder (VAE) that maintains a consistent sparse volumetric format across input, latent, and output stages, improving training efficiency and stability. Direct3D-S2 is trained on public available datasets and demonstrates superior generation quality and efficiency compared to state-of-the-art methods. It enables training at 10243 resolution with only 8 GPUs, a task that typically requires at least 32 GPUs for volumetric representations at 2563 resolution. The model supports tasks such as generating 3D meshes from images and is accessible through an inference pipeline and a Gradio web demo. The model is available under the MIT License.

instruction: Please extract the mosquito from this complex background for closer inspection.
description: culico-net-segm-v1-nano is an instance segmentation model focused on segmenting mosquito specimens in images and videos. It is a result of the CulicidaeLab project and was developed by fine-tuning the SAM2 (Segment Anything Model 2) architecture. The model is based on SAM2's efficient Hiera-Tiny backbone and was specifically adapted for mosquito specimen segmentation using a dedicated dataset. It can handle both image segmentation and video segmentation tasks with prompt-based interaction. The model accepts images or video frames with optional prompts, such as points or boxes, and produces binary masks for mosquito specimens. The Hiera-Tiny backbone is an efficient transformer architecture. The model was trained on the iloncka/mosquito-species-segmentation-dataset, which is part of a suite of datasets that also includes iloncka/mosquito-species-detection-dataset and iloncka/mosquito-species-classification-dataset. These datasets contain images of various mosquito species with detailed segmentation annotations. The model was pretrained on ImageNet-1k and the SA-1B (Segment Anything 1B dataset). The model can be used for both image and video segmentation tasks. The CulicidaeLab project aims to provide a comprehensive suite of tools for mosquito monitoring and research. The culico-net-segm-v1-nano model has several practical applications, including precise morphological analysis, automated specimen measurement, training data generation, deployment on edge devices for real-time mosquito monitoring in field conditions, and integration into expert systems for detailed specimen analysis. The development of CulicidaeLab is supported by a grant.

instruction: Generate a video of a rainstorm with thunder and lightning over a dark ocean.
description: LTX-Video is the first DiT-based video generation model capable of generating high-quality videos in real-time. It produces 30 FPS videos at a 1216×704 resolution faster than they can be watched. Trained on a large-scale dataset of diverse videos, the model generates high-resolution videos with realistic and varied content. The model is a diffusion-based image-to-video generation model developed by Lightricks. It supports image-to-video generation and video-to-video generation. The model works best on resolutions under 720 x 1280 and number of frames below 257. It works on resolutions that are divisible by 32 and number of frames that are divisible by 8 + 1.
