description: The MonoQwen2-VL-v0.1 is a multimodal reranker finetuned with LoRA, optimized for asserting pointwise image-query relevance using the objective. Given a pair of an image and a query fed into the prompt of the VLM, the model is tasked to generate 'True' if the image is relevant to the query and 'False' otherwise. During inference, a relevancy score can be obtained by comparing the logits of the two tokens, and this score can effectively be used to rerank the candidates generated by a first-stage retriever (such as DSE or ColPali) or filter them using a threshold. The model was used to train this model with negatives mined using DSE. The model has been evaluated on a set of datasets by retrieving 10 elements with and reranking them. The ndcg@5 scores for the model include 89.0 for vidore/arxivqa_test_subsampled, 59.7 for vidore/docvqa_test_subsampled, 93.2 for vidore/infovqa_test_subsampled, 96.0 for vidore/tabfquad_test_subsampled, 93.0 for vidore/shiftproject_test, 100.0 for vidore/syntheticDocQA_artificial_intelligence_test, 97.7 for vidore/syntheticDocQA_energy_test, 98.0 for vidore/syntheticDocQA_government_reports_test, 99.3 for vidore/syntheticDocQA_healthcare_industry_test, and 79.0 for vidore/tatdqa_test, with a mean score of 90.5. The model is licensed under the Apache 2.0 license.

instruction: Can you determine the regional accent in this 10-second English audio clip?
description: This model implements broader accent classification as described in the Vox-Profile: A Speech Foundation Model Benchmark for Characterizing Diverse Speaker and Speech Traits. It is designed to classify English accents into three categories: 'British Isles', 'North America', and 'Other'. The model is based on the Whisper architecture and is intended for use with audio inputs that are no shorter than 3 seconds and no longer than 15 seconds, sampled at 16kHz and in mono channel format. The model outputs logits and embeddings, and users can compute probabilities to determine the most likely accent category. The model is available for loading via Huggingface and requires a Python environment with PyTorch. The model is not intended for clinical or diagnostic applications, surveillance, privacy-invasive applications, or commercial use. Users are required to respect the privacy and consent of data subjects and to comply with relevant laws and regulations in their jurisdictions. The model is released under the Open RAIL license.

instruction: Compose a short email introducing a new product to a client.
description: OPT (Open Pre-trained Transformer Language Models) is a suite of decoder-only pre-trained transformer models developed by Meta AI and first released on May 3rd, 2022. The models range in size from 125M to 175B parameters and are designed to match the performance and scale of GPT-3 models while incorporating the latest best practices in data collection and efficient training. OPT was predominantly pre-trained on English text, with a small amount of non-English data included from CommonCrawl. The model uses a causal language modeling (CLM) objective and belongs to the same family of decoder-only models. The training data includes a combination of five filtered datasets: BookCorpus, CC-Stories, The Pile (including subsets like Pile-CC, OpenWebText2, USPTO, Project Gutenberg, OpenSubtitles, Wikipedia, DM Mathematics, and HackerNews), the Pushshift.io Reddit dataset, and CCNewsV2. The final training corpus contains 180B tokens across 800GB of data. The validation split was 200MB of the pretraining data, sampled proportionally to each dataset's size. The dataset may contain offensive content due to the inclusion of unfiltered CommonCrawl and Reddit data. The texts were tokenized using the GPT2 byte-level version of Byte Pair Encoding (BPE) with a vocabulary size of 50272 and input sequences of 2048 tokens. The 175B model was trained on 992 80GB A100 GPUs for approximately 33 days. The model can be used for prompting to evaluate downstream tasks, text generation, and fine-tuning on specific tasks. However, due to the unfiltered nature of the training data, the model exhibits biases and safety limitations, including issues with generation diversity, hallucination, and the propagation of harmful content. These limitations also affect all fine-tuned versions of the model. The intended use of OPT is to enable reproducible and responsible research at scale, encouraging the broader research community to study the impact of large language models on areas such as robustness, bias, and toxicity.

instruction: What media code would you assign to a story about a community crossword competition?
description: This is an xlm-roberta-large model that has been fine-tuned on multilingual training data in English, German, Hungarian, Spanish, and Slovakian, labeled with media codes. The model was trained using 18 media codes, including State and Local Government Administration, Weather, Fires, emergencies and natural disasters, Crime and trials, Arts, culture, entertainment and history, Style and fashion, Food, Travel, Wellbeing and learning, Personal finance and real estate, Personal technology and popular science, Churches and Religion, Celebrities and human interest, Obituaries and death notices, Sports, Crosswords, puzzles, comics, Media production/internal, letters, and Advertisements. The model is designed for text classification tasks and is intended to categorize text into one of these media codes. It was evaluated on a test set of 74,322 English examples, achieving an accuracy of 0.79, a precision of 0.77, a recall of 0.77, and a weighted average F1-score of 0.79. Performance varies by class, with some categories achieving high precision and recall, such as Weather (0.98 precision and recall), Fires, emergencies and natural disasters (0.96 precision, 0.98 recall), and Churches and Religion (0.92 precision, 0.94 recall). However, some categories, such as Advertisements, have very low performance, with 0 precision, recall, and F1-score. The model requires a token for access due to gated access restrictions, and users must provide the token when loading the model. The model uses the sentencepiece tokenizer and may require manual installation for versions of the Transformers library prior to 4.27. If a RuntimeError occurs when loading the model using the from_pretrained() method, adding ignore_mismatched_sizes=True may resolve the issue. The model is used by an open-source and free natural language processing tool designed to simplify and speed up projects for comparative research. Model performance can be improved by extending the training sets, and contributions of CAP-coded corpora in any domain and language are welcome.

instruction: What is the average score for students in Group 2 based on the table?
description: This model is a TAPAS mini model fine-tuned on WikiTable Questions (WTQ). It has two versions: the default version corresponds to the `tapas_wtq_wikisql_sqa_inter_masklm_mini_reset` checkpoint, and the non-default version corresponds to `tapas_wtq_wikisql_sqa_inter_masklm_mini`. The model was pre-trained on masked language modeling (MLM) and an additional intermediate pre-training step, and then fine-tuned in a chain on SQA, WikiSQL, and finally WTQ. The default version uses relative position embeddings, resetting the position index at every cell of the table, while the non-default version uses absolute position embeddings. TAPAS is a BERT-like transformer model pre-trained on a large corpus of English data from Wikipedia in a self-supervised fashion. It was pre-trained with two objectives: masked language modeling, where 15% of the words in a flattened table and associated context are randomly masked and the model must predict them, and intermediate pre-training, where the model is trained to classify whether a sentence is supported or refuted by the contents of a table using a balanced dataset of syntactically created training examples. This allows the model to learn a bidirectional representation of tables and associated texts, which can be used for downstream tasks such as answering questions about a table or determining whether a sentence is entailed or refuted by the contents of a table. Fine-tuning involves adding a cell selection head and aggregation head on top of the pre-trained model and jointly training these with the base model. The model was fine-tuned on 32 Cloud TPU v3 cores for 50,000 steps with a maximum sequence length of 512 and a batch size of 512. The optimizer used is Adam with a learning rate of 1.93581e-5 and a warmup ratio of 0.128960. An inductive bias is added so that the model only selects cells of the same column, as reflected by the `select_one_column` parameter of `TapasConfig`. The model can be used for answering questions related to a table. The team releasing TAPAS did not write a model card for this model, so this model card was written by the Hugging Face team and contributors. The model's performance varies by size and position embedding type. For the mini size, the dev accuracy is 0.2783 for the noreset version and 0.2854 for the reset version.

instruction: Would you mind summarizing this document in Italian for my presentation?
description: The mT5-multilingual-XLSum is a model checkpoint based on the mT5 architecture, finetuned on a dataset covering 45 languages. It is designed for multilingual abstractive summarization, supporting the generation of summaries in multiple languages. The model is intended to be used with the Transformers library, where it can be loaded and applied to generate summaries from input text. The model's performance has been evaluated on the XL-Sum test sets, with ROUGE scores reported for 44 languages. For example, in Amharic, the model achieves ROUGE-1 of 20.0485, ROUGE-2 of 7.4111, and ROUGE-L of 18.0753. In Arabic, the scores are 34.9107, 14.7937, and 29.1623, respectively. The model's performance varies across languages, with Japanese achieving the highest ROUGE-1 score of 48.1544. The model is part of a study titled 'XL-Sum: Large-Scale Multilingual Abstractive Summarization for 44 Languages,' published in the Findings of the Association for Computational Linguistics: ACL-IJCNLP 2021.

instruction: Which athlete has the highest score in the competition results table?
description: Tessar is an advanced table reasoning model developed by SVECTOR, building upon the groundbreaking research and pushing the boundaries of neural table understanding. Tessar (Textual SQL Analysis and Reasoning) is a sophisticated neural model designed to excel in table-based question answering. Tessar implements an innovative neural SQL executor that can interpret and reason over complex tabular data with remarkable precision. The model is constructed using the BART architecture, featuring a bidirectional encoder and an autoregressive decoder. This design allows Tessar to capture intricate contextual relationships within tabular data and generate accurate, contextually relevant answers. Tessar is particularly powerful for solving complex table-based questions across various domains. The model is fine-tuned on the WikiTableQuestions dataset. Tessar demonstrates state-of-the-art performance on complex table question answering, with exceptional accuracy on complex table reasoning tasks and robust handling of multi-step and contextual queries. Tessar is capable of addressing nuanced and multi-step queries, including identifying specific titles, calculating numerical differences, identifying entities with significant impact, and comparing first and last entries in a specific column. Tessar is released under the MIT License. Users are advised to review the licensing terms before use.
domain: Natural Language Processing Table Question Answering

instruction: Could you summarize the content of this document in a few sentences?
description: Perception Encoder (PE) is a state-of-the-art encoder for image and video understanding trained via simple vision-language learning. It is a family of large-scale vision encoder models with state-of-the-art performance on a large variety of vision tasks. By using a robust contrastive pretraining recipe and finetuning on synthetically aligned videos, PE not only outperforms all existing models on classification and retrieval, but it also internally produces strong, general features that scale for downstream tasks. PE unlocks the ability for large-scale contrastive pretraining to transfer to downstream tasks with alignment tuning to capitalize on those general features. PE lang takes the strong language performance from the intermediate layers of PE core and further aligns for language modeling. It is specifically tuned to be versatile for any multimodal language modeling use case, including using different language model decoders (e.g., Llama / Qwen) and using different eval settings (e.g., native res / tiling). PE lang performs particularly well on OCR and document tasks. Two PE Lang checkpoints, L14-448 and G14-448, were released. In a benchmark setting with a frozen encoder using 2.6M SFT datamix and 448px only (with no tiling), and Llama 3.1 8B as the decoder, the L14-448 checkpoint achieved 81.9 on Doc VQA (val), 46.4 on InfoQA (val), 73.0 on TextVQA, 52.3 on MVBench, 54.7 on PerceptionTest (val), and 59.8 on EgoSchema (val). The G14-448 checkpoint achieved 84.4 on Doc VQA (val), 48.3 on InfoQA (val), 75.2 on TextVQA, 52.4 on MVBench, 56.0 on PerceptionTest (val), and 62.0 on EgoSchema (val). Using PE Core G aligned further with tiling using 36+1 image tiles / 32 video frames and Llama 3.1 8B as the decoder, the PLM-8B model achieved 94.6 on Doc VQA (test), 78.8 on InfoQA (test), 86.5 on TextVQA, 77.1 on MVBench, 82.7 on PerceptionTest (test), and 68.8 on EgoSchema (test). The PE-Core-G14-448 checkpoint was further trained using tiling. The tiling aligned checkpoint will be released soon. See the paper for full performance evaluations and fair comparisons to other models.

instruction: Is there a way to get a consistent visual summary of a video regardless of its length?
description: V-JEPA 2 is a video understanding model developed by FAIR at Meta. It extends the pretraining objectives of previous models, resulting in state-of-the-art video understanding capabilities, and leverages data and model sizes at scale. The model is intended to represent any video (and image) for tasks such as video classification, retrieval, or as a video encoder for vision-language models. V-JEPA 2 is designed to process videos by sampling a specific number of frames, with 64 frames used for this model. For images, the model repeats the image across the desired number of frames. The model can generate video or image embeddings using its vision features. V-JEPA 2 is available on the Hugging Face model repository as 'facebook/vjepa2-vitl-fpc64-256'. The model is described in a technical report titled 'V-JEPA 2: Self-Supervised Video Models Enable Understanding, Prediction and Planning', authored by Mahmoud Assran, Adrien Bardes, David Fan, Quentin Garrido, Russell Howes, Mojtaba Komeili, Matthew Muckley, Ammar Rizvi, Claire Roberts, Koustuv Sinha, Artem Zholus, Sergio Arnaud, Abha Gejji, Ada Martin, Francois Robert Hogan, Daniel Dugas, Piotr Bojanowski, Vasil Khalidov, Patrick Labatut, Francisco Massa, Marc Szafraniec, Kapil Krishnakumar, Yong Li, Xiaodong Ma, Sarath Chandar, Franziska Meier, and Yann LeCun, published in 2025 by FAIR at Meta.

instruction: Is there a way to separate the object from the background in this image?
description: BiRefNet is a bilateral reference network designed for high-resolution dichotomous image segmentation. It is an arbitrary shape adaptable model that was trained on input resolutions ranging from 256x256 to 2304x2304, demonstrating robustness on inputs of any shape. The model is intended for general segmentation tasks and has achieved state-of-the-art performance on three tasks: DIS (Dichotomous Image Segmentation), HRSOD (High-Resolution Salient Object Detection), and COD (Complementary Object Detection). It was trained on the DIS-TR dataset and validated on DIS-TE and DIS-VD datasets. The model's performance exceeds that of BiRefNet-general and BiRefNet_HR-general on the reserved validation sets. The model supports inference on arbitrary input resolutions and can be used for object extraction through segmentation mask generation. The model is implemented using PyTorch and can be loaded from HuggingFace or used locally with weights from HuggingFace or a local space. The model is described in the paper 'Bilateral Reference for High-Resolution Dichotomous Image Segmentation,' published in CAAI Artificial Intelligence Research in 2024. The authors are Peng Zheng, Dehong Gao, Deng-Ping Fan, Li Liu, Jorma Laaksonen, Wanli Ouyang, and Nicu Sebe, affiliated with Nankai University, Northwestern Polytechnical University, National University of Defense Technology, Aalto University, Shanghai AI Laboratory, and University of Trento, respectively. The model is available through an official GitHub repository and HuggingFace, and online demos for inference are provided. The model weights are publicly available for use and evaluation.

instruction: Could you analyze this surveillance camera image and mark all the people present?
description: The D-FINE model was proposed by Yansong Peng, Hebei Li, Peixi Wu, Yueyi Zhang, Xiaoyan Sun, and Feng Wu. This model was contributed with the help of others and is the HF transformers implementation for D-FINE. D-FINE is a powerful real-time object detector that achieves outstanding localization precision by redefining the bounding box regression task in DETR models. The model comprises two key components: Fine-grained Distribution Refinement (FDR) and Global Optimal Localization Self-Distillation (GO-LSD). D-FINE is trained on COCO (Lin et al. [2014]) train2017 and validated on COCO val2017 dataset. The standard AP metrics are reported, averaged over uniformly sampled IoU thresholds ranging from 0.50 to 0.95 with a step size of 0.05, and APval5000 is also reported, which is commonly used in real scenarios. D-FINE is ideal for real-time object detection in diverse applications such as autonomous driving, surveillance systems, robotics, and retail analytics. Its enhanced flexibility and deployment-friendly design make it suitable for both edge devices and large-scale systems, ensuring high accuracy and speed in dynamic, real-world environments. The model is available in different versions: 'coco' for models trained on COCO, 'obj365' for models trained on Object365, and 'obj2coco' for models trained on Object365 and then fine-tuned on COCO.

instruction: Can you provide a step-by-step explanation for solving this differential equation?
description: Open-Reasoner-Zero is an open source implementation of large-scale reasoning-oriented reinforcement learning (RL) training, designed with a focus on scalability, simplicity, and accessibility. It uses the same base model as DeepSeek-R1-Zero-Qwen-32B and achieves superior performance on AIME2024, MATH500, and the GPQA Diamond benchmark, requiring only a tenth of the training steps compared to the DeepSeek-R1-Zero pipeline. The model supports various sizes, including 0.5B, 1.5B, 7B, and 32B parameters, with consistent scalability across model sizes demonstrated by steady increases in train reward and response length. Notably, the 32B model exhibits fluctuations in response length without negatively impacting training stability, highlighting the robustness of the minimalist training approach. Open-Reasoner-Zero is designed to enable broader participation in research toward artificial general intelligence (AGI) by releasing source code, parameter settings, training data, and model weights. The model supports PPO training across different configurations, including single GPU setups for the 0.5B variant, and multi-node training for larger models. Training data includes 129k curated high-quality samples from sources such as AIME, MATH, Numina-Math, and Tulu3 MATH, with additional data mined from the first stage of ORZ-32B training. The model is based on base models including Qwen2.5-7B and others, and the training framework is built on multiple open-source libraries. The model is available for use in the same way as standard chat models, with policy and critic models supporting standard loading and inference methods. The implementation includes a single controller trainer design to maximize GPU utilization by colocating training and generation on the same GPUs. The model is supported by computing resources and feedback from Tsinghua University and other contributors, and it is based on open-sourced data from multiple sources.

instruction: What is the main action taking place in this scene?
description: VideoLLaMA 2 is a video language model designed to advance spatial-temporal modeling and audio understanding in video-LLMs. It supports tasks such as multi-choice video question answering, video captioning, and open-ended video question answering. The model is available in different configurations, including base and chat variants, with varying numbers of training frames, such as 8 or 16 frames. As of June 25, 2024, the ~7B-sized version of VideoLLaMA 2 is the top-performing model in its category on the specified benchmark. The model includes a visual encoder and a language decoder, and it has been trained and evaluated using specific codebases that are publicly available. The model is intended for use in video understanding tasks and is accompanied by a technical report that includes the latest results and references. Users are encouraged to provide feedback on related works that may have been omitted from the paper.

instruction: What is the main idea conveyed by this visual and the text below it?
description: Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct-FP8-Dynamic is a quantized version of the Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct model, optimized for efficient inference using FP8 data type for both weights and activations. It is a vision-text input to text output model designed for multimodal tasks such as visual question answering, document analysis, and image captioning. The model was developed by Neural Magic and released on 2/24/2025, version 1.0. It is intended for deployment with the vLLM backend, which supports OpenAI-compatible serving. The model was created using the llmcompressor framework with a quantization recipe targeting linear layers and excluding specific components such as the language model head and vision tower. Evaluation results show that the model maintains or slightly improves upon the accuracy of the original model across various vision and text-based benchmarks, including MMMU, VQAv2, DocVQA, ChartQA, Mathvista, MGSM, and MMLU. The model achieves up to 1.3x speedup in single-stream deployment and 1.37x in multi-stream deployment, depending on the hardware and use-case scenario. Performance benchmarks were conducted using vLLM version 0.7.2 on A6000, A100, and H100 GPUs, showing improvements in latency, queries per second, and cost efficiency for tasks such as document visual question answering, visual reasoning, and image captioning. The model is suitable for deployment in environments where cost and inference speed are critical factors.

instruction: What is the value of the beta parameters in the optimizer?
description: This model is a fine-tuned version of a base model on an unknown dataset. It is designed to perform document question-answering tasks. The model was trained using the following hyperparameters: learning rate of 5e-05, training batch size of 4, evaluation batch size of 8, random seed of 42, Adam optimizer with betas of (0.9, 0.999) and epsilon of 1e-08, linear learning rate scheduler, and 20 training epochs. During training, the model achieved a final validation loss of 4.9512. The training process included 4501 training steps, with training loss decreasing from 5.2951 at step 50 to 0.025 at step 4500. The validation loss also fluctuated throughout training, starting at 4.7074 at step 50 and ending at 4.9512 at step 4500. The model was developed using Transformers 4.45.2, PyTorch 2.5.0, Datasets 3.0.1, and Tokenizers 0.20.1.

instruction: Could you render this short message in Korean for a cultural exchange program?
description: NLLB-200 is a machine translation model primarily intended for research in machine translation, especially for low-resource languages. It allows for single sentence translation among 200 languages. The model is a distilled 600M variant. The exact training algorithm, data, and strategies to handle data imbalances for high and low resource languages are described in the paper titled 'No Language Left Behind: Scaling Human-Centered Machine Translation' by the NLLB Team, published on Arxiv in 2022. The model is licensed under CC-BY-NC. The primary intended users are researchers and the machine translation research community. NLLB-200 is a research model and is not released for production deployment. It is trained on general domain text data and is not intended to be used with domain-specific texts, such as medical or legal texts. The model is not intended for document translation. It was trained with input lengths not exceeding 512 tokens, and translating longer sequences may result in quality degradation. NLLB-200 translations cannot be used as certified translations. The model was evaluated using BLEU, spBLEU, and chrF++ metrics, and human evaluation was performed using the XSTS protocol. The Flores-200 dataset was used for evaluation, as it provides full coverage of the languages in NLLB-200. The dataset was preprocessed using SentencePiece, and the SentencePiece model is released along with NLLB-200. The model was trained using parallel multilingual data from various sources, with detailed reports on data selection and construction provided in the paper. Monolingual data from Common Crawl was also used. Ethical considerations were taken into account, including the potential for misuse of the model by bad actors, the vulnerability of low-digital-literacy groups to misinformation, and the possibility of residual personally identifiable information in the training data. Mistranslations could have adverse impacts, particularly in health and safety contexts. The model has been tested on the Wikimedia domain with limited investigation on other domains supported in NLLB-MD. Supported languages may have variations that the model does not capture, and users should make appropriate assessments. The carbon dioxide (CO2e) estimate for the model is reported in Section 8.8 of the paper.

instruction: Identify and label any objects in this photo that could be described as 'office supplies'.
description: LLMDet (large variant) was proposed by Shenghao Fu, Qize Yang, Qijie Mo, Junkai Yan, Xihan Wei, Jingke Meng, Xiaohua Xie, and Wei-Shi Zheng. LLMDet improves upon previous models by co-training with a large language model. The model is designed for zero-shot object detection and can be used for this task without prior training on specific object categories. The checkpoints for LLMDet are available for inference only and do not include the large language model used during training. The inference process is identical to that of other similar models. The model was trained on a combination of datasets including O365, GoldG, GRIT, V3Det, O365V2, and OpenImageV6, along with GroundingCap-1M. Evaluation results on LVIS show that the large variant of LLMDet achieves a MiniVal APr of 51.1, MiniVal APc of 45.1, MiniVal APf of 46.1, and MiniVal AP of 56.6. On Val1.0, it achieves an APr of 42.0, APc of 31.6, APf of 38.8, and AP of 50.2. The model is intended for use in open-vocabulary object detection tasks and supports inference with custom text labels provided as input. Users are advised that the checkpoints provided are for inference only and do not include the training components.

instruction: What is the visual style of this clip, and what elements define it?
description: SkyCaptioner-V1 is a structural video captioning model designed to generate high-quality, structural descriptions for video data. It integrates specialized sub-expert models and multimodal large language models (MLLMs) with human annotations to address the limitations of general captioners in capturing professional film-related details. The model combines general video descriptions from MLLMs with sub-expert captioners, such as shot types, shot angles, shot positions, and camera motions. It employs knowledge distillation to distill expertise from sub-expert captioners into a unified model. The model supports generating dense captions for text-to-video (T2V) and concise prompts for image-to-video (I2V) tasks. SkyCaptioner-V1 is trained on approximately 2 million high-quality, concept-balanced videos curated from 10 million raw samples. It is fine-tuned on Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct with a global batch size of 512 across 32 A800 GPUs. The training uses AdamW with a learning rate of 1e-5 for 2 epochs. The model features a dynamic caption fusion method that adapts output length based on application (T2V/I2V) and employs an LLM model to fuse structural fields into a natural and fluent caption for downstream tasks. SkyCaptioner-V1 demonstrates significant improvements over existing models in key film-specific captioning tasks, particularly in shot-language understanding and domain-specific precision. It outperforms Qwen2.5-VL-72B with +11.2% in shot type, +16.1% in shot angle, and +50.4% in shot position accuracy. It achieves +28.5% accuracy in camera motion compared to Tarsier2-recap-7B (88.8% vs. 41.5%). Its 6DoF motion analysis and active learning pipeline address ambiguities in composite motions. The model achieves 68.8% expression accuracy compared to 54.3% for Tarsier2-recap-7B, leveraging temporal-aware S2D frameworks to capture dynamic facial changes. Overall, SkyCaptioner-V1 achieves an average accuracy of 76.3%, outperforming Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Ins. (51.4%), Qwen2.5-VL-72B-Ins. (58.7%), and Tarsier2-recap-7B (49.4%). It achieves 93.7% accuracy in shot type, 89.8% in shot angle, 83.1% in shot position, 85.3% in camera motion, and 68.8% in expression. The model also performs well in other metrics, including 82.5% in TYPES_type, 75.4% in TYPES_sub_type, 59.3% in appearance, 68.8% in action, 57.5% in position, 80.9% in is_main_subject, 70.5% in environment, and 76.5% in lighting. SkyCaptioner-V1 uses Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct as its caption fusion model to intelligently combine structured caption fields, producing either dense or sparse final captions depending on application requirements. The model can be downloaded from Hugging Face using the huggingface-cli command. It is recommended to use Python 3.10 and CUDA version 12.2 for installation. The model can be run using the vllm_struct_caption.py script for generating structural captions and the vllm_fusion_caption.py script for T2V/I2V caption fusion. The model is based on the research described in the technical report titled 'Skyreels V2: Infinite-Length Film Generative Model' by Guibin Chen and others, available on arXiv.

instruction: Which version should be used if the goal is to minimize file size without sacrificing too much performance?
description: This model provides various quantized versions of a neural network, optimized for different use cases and performance requirements. The available quantizations include IQ1_S, IQ1_M, IQ2_XXS, IQ2_XS, IQ2_S, IQ2_M, Q2_K_S, Q2_K, IQ3_XXS, IQ3_XS, Q3_K_S, IQ3_S, IQ3_M, Q3_K_M, Q3_K_L, IQ4_XS, IQ4_NL, Q4_0, Q4_K_S, Q4_K_M, Q4_1, Q5_K_S, Q5_K_M, and Q6_K. Each quantization is described with its file size in gigabytes and notes on its quality and performance. For example, the i1-IQ1_S quant is 2.0 GB and is noted as being for 'the desperate,' while the i1-Q4_K_M quant is 4.8 GB and is described as 'fast, recommended.' Some quants are noted for their trade-offs between size, speed, and quality, such as i1-Q4_K_S, which is described as 'optimal size/speed/quality.' The model card includes a graph comparing lower-quality quant types and references additional commentary from Artefact2. The model is supported by infrastructure provided by the author's company and a private supercomputer, which enabled the creation of higher-quality imatrix quants.

instruction: Could you highlight the posture of the individual in this photo?
description: The model is designed for pose estimation and is compatible with Transformers.js through ONNX weights. It supports tasks involving image processing to predict heatmaps and extract keypoints for pose estimation. The model architecture is based on the ViTPose framework, specifically the base-simple variant. It can be used with the Transformers.js library, which allows for model loading, image processing, heatmap prediction, and post-processing to obtain pose estimation results. The model outputs include keypoints and scores, which can be visualized by drawing connections between keypoints and marking each keypoint on the image. The model is intended to be used with ONNX weights as a temporary solution until WebML becomes more widely adopted. Users are advised to convert their models to ONNX format and organize their repositories with ONNX weights in a subfolder named 'onnx' to make them web-ready.

instruction: Can you create a clean outline of a person in a photo to be used for a transparent overlay?
description: U-2-Net-Human-Seg is a specialised version of the U-2-Net model designed specifically for human segmentation tasks. This model excels in distinguishing human figures from the background in images, making it particularly useful for applications such as background removal, virtual try-ons, and human-centric image editing. By leveraging a deep learning approach, U-2-Net-Human-Seg can accurately segment human subjects in various poses and environments, providing high-quality segmentation masks that can be utilized in different imaging tasks. The U-2-Net-Human-Seg model is based on a simplified version of the original U-2-Net architecture, designed to be more lightweight while still achieving high performance in segmentation tasks. The model consists of several stages with down-sampling and up-sampling paths, using Residual U-blocks (RSU) for enhanced feature representation. This model is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 to match the original U-2-Net model.

instruction: I have a voice sample and want to know which language it’s in—can you help?
description: This is a spoken language recognition model trained using SpeechBrain. The model employs the ECAPA-TDNN architecture, which has been previously used for speaker recognition. It includes more fully connected hidden layers after the embedding layer and uses cross-entropy loss for training, which has been observed to improve the performance of extracted utterance embeddings for downstream tasks. The system is trained with recordings sampled at 16kHz (single channel), and the code will automatically normalize audio (i.e., resampling + mono channel selection) when calling *classify_file* if needed. The model can classify a speech utterance according to the language spoken and supports 107 different languages, including Abkhazian, Afrikaans, Amharic, Arabic, Assamese, Azerbaijani, Bashkir, Belarusian, Bulgarian, Bengali, Tibetan, Breton, Bosnian, Catalan, Cebuano, Czech, Welsh, Danish, German, Greek, English, Esperanto, Spanish, Estonian, Basque, Persian, Finnish, Faroese, French, Galician, Guarani, Gujarati, Manx, Hausa, Hawaiian, Hindi, Croatian, Haitian, Hungarian, Armenian, Interlingua, Indonesian, Icelandic, Italian, Hebrew, Japanese, Javanese, Georgian, Kazakh, Central Khmer, Kannada, Korean, Latin, Luxembourgish, Lingala, Lao, Lithuanian, Latvian, Malagasy, Maori, Macedonian, Malayalam, Mongolian, Marathi, Malay, Maltese, Burmese, Nepali, Dutch, Norwegian Nynorsk, Norwegian, Occitan, Panjabi, Polish, Pushto, Portuguese, Romanian, Russian, Sanskrit, Scots, Sindhi, Sinhala, Slovak, Slovenian, Shona, Somali, Albanian, Serbian, Sundanese, Swedish, Swahili, Tamil, Telugu, Tajik, Thai, Turkmen, Tagalog, Turkish, Tatar, Ukrainian, Urdu, Uzbek, Vietnamese, Waray, Yiddish, Yoruba, and Mandarin Chinese. The model has two intended uses: it can be used 'as is' for spoken language recognition or as an utterance-level feature (embedding) extractor for creating a dedicated language ID model on your own data. The model is trained on automatically collected YouTube data. The system is trained with recordings sampled at 16kHz (single channel), and the code will automatically normalize your audio (i.e., resampling + mono channel selection) when calling *classify_file* if needed. Make sure your input tensor is compliant with the expected sampling rate if you use *encode_batch* and *classify_batch*. A warning is provided that the dataset and the defaults of this model use an obsolete ISO language code for Hebrew (should be `he` instead of `iw`) and an incorrect ISO language code for Javanese (should be `jv` instead of `jw`). The model has several limitations and biases, including potentially limited accuracy on smaller languages, potentially worse performance on female speech than male speech (due to more male speech in the dataset), poor performance on speech with foreign accents, and likely poor performance on children's speech and speech from persons with speech disorders. The model is trained on the VoxLingua107 dataset, which consists of short speech segments automatically extracted from YouTube videos and labeled according to the language of the video title and description, with some post-processing steps to filter out false positives. VoxLingua107 contains data for 107 languages, with a total of 6628 hours of speech in the training set. The average amount of data per language is 62 hours, though the actual amount varies significantly. There is also a separate development set containing 1609 speech segments from 33 languages, validated by at least two volunteers to ensure the correct language labeling. The model achieved an error rate of 6.7% on the VoxLingua107 development dataset.

instruction: Can you summarize the key points of this legal document in simple language?
description: The Mistral 7B Text Summarizer is a model designed for text summarization tasks. It is based on the Mistral 7B architecture and uses Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) techniques to improve fine-tuning efficiency and performance. The model is built using the Hugging Face Transformers framework and is fine-tuned using Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) through the Hugging Face TRL library. It is a general-purpose model capable of summarizing content across diverse domains. The model implements Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) with LoRA adapters to enhance performance while reducing computational costs. It is optimized for fast and efficient inference using gradient checkpointing and optimized data management. The model supports 4-bit quantization, which significantly reduces memory usage and computation time while maintaining accuracy. It is fine-tuned on the SURESHBEEKHANI text-summarizer dataset. The model supports a maximum sequence length of 2048 tokens and is configurable to use either float16 or float32 precision for hardware optimization. It is optimized for a reduced memory footprint, allowing for larger batch sizes and effective handling of longer sequences. The model is suitable for tasks requiring concise summaries of lengthy texts, documents, or articles, and is ideal for use in content generation, report summarization, and information distillation. It is efficient for deployment in production systems requiring scalable and fast text summarization. However, it has limitations, including the need for additional memory and computational power for extremely long documents, and potential inconsistencies in performance for niche domains underrepresented in the training dataset. Steps have been taken to mitigate bias in the training data and ensure fairness in generated summaries. The model is designed to respect user privacy by adhering to best practices in handling input text data. Comprehensive documentation and model cards are provided to promote transparency and trust in AI-driven summarization. The model was fine-tuned by Suresh Beekhani and is based on the SURESHBEEKHANI text-summarizer dataset. It is open-source under the Hugging Face and unsloth licenses, allowing for free use and modification.

instruction: How do you approach a problem that involves both combinatorics and probability?
description: Open-Reasoner-Zero is an open source implementation of large-scale reasoning-oriented reinforcement learning (RL) training, designed with a focus on scalability, simplicity, and accessibility. It uses the same base model as DeepSeek-R1-Zero-Qwen-32B and achieves superior performance on AIME2024, MATH500, and the GPQA Diamond benchmark, requiring only a tenth of the training steps compared to the DeepSeek-R1-Zero pipeline. The model supports various sizes, including 0.5B, 1.5B, 7B, and 32B parameters, with consistent scalability across model sizes demonstrated by steady increases in train reward and response length. Notably, the 32B model exhibits fluctuations in response length without negatively impacting training stability, highlighting the robustness of the minimalist training approach. Open-Reasoner-Zero is designed to enable broader participation in research toward artificial general intelligence (AGI) by releasing source code, parameter settings, training data, and model weights. The model supports PPO training across different configurations, including single GPU setups for the 0.5B variant, and multi-node training for larger models. Training data includes 129k curated high-quality samples from sources such as AIME, MATH, Numina-Math, and Tulu3 MATH, with additional data mined from the first stage of ORZ-32B training. The model is based on base models including Qwen2.5-7B and others, and the training framework is built on multiple open-source libraries. The model is available for use in the same way as standard chat models, with policy and critic models supporting standard loading and inference methods. The implementation includes a single controller trainer design to maximize GPU utilization by colocating training and generation on the same GPUs. The model is supported by computing resources and feedback from Tsinghua University and other contributors, and it is based on open-sourced data from multiple sources.

instruction: How deep is the shadow in this image in terms of metric distance?
description: Depth Pro is a foundation model for zero-shot metric monocular depth estimation. It synthesizes high-resolution depth maps with sharpness and high-frequency details. The model produces metric depth predictions with absolute scale, without requiring metadata such as camera intrinsics. It is capable of generating a 2.25-megapixel depth map in 0.3 seconds on a standard GPU. This performance is enabled by an efficient multi-scale vision transformer for dense prediction, a training protocol that combines real and synthetic datasets to achieve high metric accuracy and fine boundary tracing, dedicated evaluation metrics for boundary accuracy in depth maps, and state-of-the-art focal length estimation from a single image. The model was introduced by Aleksei Bochkovskii, Amaël Delaunoy, Hugo Germain, Marcel Santos, Yichao Zhou, Stephan R. Richter, and Vladlen Koltun. The checkpoint in this repository is a reference implementation that has been re-trained. Its performance is close to the model reported in the paper but does not match it exactly.

instruction: Can you help me solve this logic puzzle and explain how you arrived at the solution?
description: Gemma is a family of lightweight, state-of-the-art open models from Google, built from the same research and technology used to create the Gemini models. Gemma 3 models are multimodal, handling text and image input and generating text output, with open weights for both pre-trained variants and instruction-tuned variants. Gemma 3 has a large, 128K context window, multilingual support in over 140 languages, and is available in more sizes than previous versions. Gemma 3 models are well-suited for a variety of text generation and image understanding tasks, including question answering, summarization, and reasoning. Their relatively small size makes it possible to deploy them in environments with limited resources such as laptops, desktops or your own cloud infrastructure, democratizing access to state of the art AI models and helping foster innovation for everyone. The input includes text strings such as questions, prompts, or documents to be summarized, and images normalized to 896 x 896 resolution and encoded to 256 tokens each. The total input context is 128K tokens for the 4B, 12B, and 27B sizes, and 32K tokens for the 1B size. The output is generated text in response to the input, such as an answer to a question, analysis of image content, or a summary of a document, with a total output context of 8192 tokens. These models were trained on a dataset of text data that includes a wide variety of sources. The 27B model was trained with 14 trillion tokens, the 12B model with 12 trillion tokens, the 4B model with 4 trillion tokens, and the 1B model with 2 trillion tokens. The training dataset includes web documents, code, mathematics, and images. Web documents ensure the model is exposed to a broad range of linguistic styles, topics, and vocabulary, including content in over 140 languages. Code exposure helps the model learn the syntax and patterns of programming languages, improving its ability to generate and understand code. Mathematics training helps the model learn logical reasoning, symbolic representation, and to address mathematical queries. A wide range of images enables the model to perform image analysis and visual data extraction tasks. Rigorous CSAM filtering was applied at multiple stages in the data preparation process to ensure the exclusion of harmful and illegal content. Automated techniques were used to filter out certain personal information and other sensitive data from training sets. Additional methods were applied for content quality and safety in line with safety policies. Gemma was trained using Tensor Processing Unit (TPU) hardware (TPUv4p, TPUv5p, and TPUv5e). TPUs are specifically designed to handle the massive computations involved in training vision-language models (VLMs), offering performance, memory, scalability, and cost-effectiveness advantages. Training was done using JAX and ML Pathways. JAX allows researchers to take advantage of the latest generation of hardware, including TPUs, for faster and more efficient training of large models. ML Pathways is Google's effort to build artificially intelligent systems capable of generalizing across multiple tasks, suitable for foundation models like these. These models were evaluated against a large collection of different datasets and metrics to cover different aspects of text generation. Evaluation results showed major improvements in the categories of child safety, content safety, and representational harms relative to previous Gemma models. All testing was conducted without safety filters to evaluate the model capabilities and behaviors. For both text-to-text and image-to-text, and across all model sizes, the model produced minimal policy violations and showed significant improvements over previous Gemma models' performance with respect to ungrounded inferences. A limitation of the evaluations was that they included only English language prompts. These models have certain limitations that users should be aware of. The quality and diversity of the training data significantly influence the model's capabilities. Biases or gaps in the training data can lead to limitations in the model's responses. The scope of the training dataset determines the subject areas the model can handle effectively. Models are better at tasks that can be framed with clear prompts and instructions. Open-ended or highly complex tasks might be challenging. A model's performance can be influenced by the amount of context provided (longer context generally leads to better outputs, up to a certain point). Natural language is inherently complex. Models might struggle to grasp subtle nuances, sarcasm, or figurative language. Models generate responses based on information they learned from their training datasets, but they are not knowledge bases. They may generate incorrect or outdated factual statements. Models rely on statistical patterns in language. They might lack the ability to apply common sense reasoning in certain situations. The development of vision-language models (VLMs) raises several ethical concerns. VLMs trained on large-scale, real-world text and image data can reflect socio-cultural biases embedded in the training material. These models underwent careful scrutiny, input data pre-processing, and posterior evaluations. VLMs can be misused to generate text that is false, misleading, or harmful. Guidelines are provided for responsible use with the model. Transparency and accountability are emphasized by summarizing details on the models' architecture, capabilities, limitations, and evaluation processes. A responsibly developed open model offers the opportunity to share innovation by making VLM technology accessible to developers and researchers across the AI ecosystem. Risks identified include the perpetuation of biases, generation of harmful content, misuse for malicious purposes, and privacy violations. Mitigations include continuous monitoring, content safety safeguards, developer and end-user education, and adherence to privacy regulations. At the time of release, this family of models provides high-performance open vision-language model implementations designed from the ground up for responsible AI development compared to similarly sized models. Using the benchmark evaluation metrics described in this document, these models have shown to provide superior performance to other, comparably-sized open model alternatives.

instruction: Could you determine the speaker’s sentiment in this audio from a quarterly results call?
description: FinVoc2Vec is a vocal tone classifier designed for real-world corporate disclosures. The model is trained in two stages: first, a self-supervised pre-training procedure that adapts the base model to the acoustic characteristics of disclosure environments using 500,000 unlabeled sentences of conference call speech. Second, a supervised fine-tuning procedure that enables the model to learn representations of human-labeled vocal tone. The training data consists of 5,000 audio recordings of linguistically neutral sentences from conference calls, each manually labeled with perceived vocal tone — positive, negative, or neutral. The labeled audio data cannot be made publicly available due to third-party property rights, but interested researchers with an active license may contact the authors directly to explore potential access options. The model is based on a derivative of Wav2Vec2 and is licensed under the Creative Commons Attribution Non Commercial 4.0 licence (CC-BY-NC-4.0).

instruction: Can you analyze the audio and tell me if the accent is from the British Isles or elsewhere?
description: This model implements broader accent classification as described in the Vox-Profile: A Speech Foundation Model Benchmark for Characterizing Diverse Speaker and Speech Traits. It is designed to classify English accents into three categories: 'British Isles', 'North America', and 'Other'. The model is based on the Whisper architecture and is intended for use with audio inputs that are no shorter than 3 seconds and no longer than 15 seconds, sampled at 16kHz and in mono channel format. The model outputs logits and embeddings, and users can compute probabilities to determine the most likely accent category. The model is available for loading via Huggingface and requires a Python environment with PyTorch. The model is not intended for clinical or diagnostic applications, surveillance, privacy-invasive applications, or commercial use. Users are required to respect the privacy and consent of data subjects and to comply with relevant laws and regulations in their jurisdictions. The model is released under the Open RAIL license.

instruction: I need an expert opinion on whether my reasoning process for this problem is error-free.
description: Qwen2.5-Math-PRM-7B is a Process Reward Model (PRM) designed for process supervision in mathematical reasoning of Large Language Models (LLMs). It aims to identify and mitigate intermediate errors in the reasoning processes. The model is used to provide feedback on the quality of reasoning and intermediate steps rather than for generation. It is part of a series that includes Qwen2.5-Math-PRM-72B and is released alongside the mathematical Outcome Reward Model (ORM) Qwen2.5-Math-RM-72B. The model is trained to exhibit strong performance in the Best-of-N (BoN) evaluation and in error identification. For use, it is recommended to separate individual steps within a solution using double line breaks and to insert a special token for reward computation after each step. The reward value is derived from the probability score of this token being classified as positive, resulting in a value between 0 and 1. The model requires the `transformers` library version 4.40.0 or higher, as the Qwen2.5 code was integrated starting from version 4.37.0. The model is typically used in conjunction with the Qwen2.5-Math-Instruct model. The model is evaluated and described in the paper titled 'The Lessons of Developing Process Reward Models in Mathematical Reasoning' by Zhenru Zhang, Chujie Zheng, Yangzhen Wu, Beichen Zhang, Runji Lin, Bowen Yu, Dayiheng Liu, Jingren Zhou, and Junyang Lin, available as an arXiv preprint arXiv:2501.07301.

instruction: What kind of action is the subject performing in this recording?
description: The VideoMAE Base model is fine-tuned on the UCF101 dataset. It is designed for video action recognition. The model is available under the MIT license and is developed for the English language. During training, clips were sampled from videos consisting of 64 frames, and a uniform sample of 16 frames was used as input. PyTorchVideo was used to apply mixup and cutmix during training. The model was trained and evaluated on one fold of the UCF101 annotations. Evaluation results include an accuracy of 0.758209764957428 and a top 5 accuracy of 0.8983050584793091. It is noted that the results may be slightly lower than those achieved in the VideoMAE paper, as inference was not performed over multiple crops or segments of validation videos. Users are advised to be aware of the model's risks, biases, and limitations, although more information is needed for further recommendations.

instruction: Could you help me design a class structure for a to-do list application in Python?
description: Qwen2.5-Coder is a code-specific large language model, part of the Qwen series, previously known as CodeQwen. The Qwen2.5-Coder series includes six model sizes with 0.5, 1.5, 3, 7, 14, and 32 billion parameters, catering to different developer needs. Qwen2.5-Coder introduces significant improvements in code generation, code reasoning, and code fixing. It is trained on 5.5 trillion tokens, including source code, text-code grounding, and synthetic data. The Qwen2.5-Coder-32B model is currently the state-of-the-art open-source code language model, with coding abilities comparable to GPT-4o. The model also supports real-world applications such as Code Agents, maintaining strong performance in mathematics and general competencies. The 0.5B Qwen2.5-Coder model is an instruction-tuned version with the following characteristics: it is a causal language model, trained through pretraining and post-training, and uses a transformer architecture with RoPE, SwiGLU, RMSNorm, attention QKV bias, and tied word embeddings. It has 0.49 billion parameters, 0.36 billion non-embedding parameters, 24 layers, and 14 attention heads for Q and 2 for KV. The model supports a context length of 32,768 tokens. The model requires the latest version of the Hugging Face `transformers` library to avoid errors. Evaluation results and performance details are available in the provided documentation. If the model is used, it should be cited using the provided academic references.

instruction: Could you extract the pose data for this person performing a martial arts move?
description: The SynthPose model was proposed by Yoni Gozlan, Antoine Falisse, Scott Uhlrich, Anthony Gatti, Michael Black, and Akshay Chaudhari. This model uses a VitPose Huge backbone and is a new approach that enables fine-tuning of pre-trained 2D human pose models to predict an arbitrarily denser set of keypoints for accurate kinematic analysis through the use of synthetic data. This particular variant was fine-tuned on a set of keypoints usually found on motion capture setups, and includes COCO keypoints as well. The model predicts 52 markers, where the first 17 are the COCO keypoints and the next 35 are anatomical markers. The model is designed to support tasks related to human pose estimation, including the detection of human figures in images and the prediction of detailed anatomical keypoints for each detected person. The model is intended for use in applications requiring precise kinematic analysis, such as biomechanics or motion capture. The model's architecture is based on the VitPose Huge variant, and it is fine-tuned to enhance its ability to predict a larger and more detailed set of keypoints than standard models. The model is evaluated for its ability to accurately predict these additional keypoints, which are typically not included in standard pose estimation models. The model includes explicit caveats regarding its intended use and the necessity of using appropriate detection models in conjunction with it for accurate results.

instruction: What does this image suggest about the culture or environment it shows?

The evaluation methods included structured evaluations and internal red-teaming testing of relevant content policies. Red-teaming was conducted by different teams with different goals and human evaluation metrics. The models were evaluated against categories such as child safety, content safety, and representational harms. Transparency and accountability are emphasized, with details on the models' architecture, capabilities, limitations, and evaluation processes. A responsibly developed open model offers the opportunity to share innovation by making VLM technology accessible to developers and researchers across the AI ecosystem. Risks identified include the perpetuation of biases, generation of harmful content, misuse for malicious purposes, and privacy violations. Mitigations include continuous monitoring, content safety safeguards, developer and end-user education, and adherence to privacy regulations. At the time of release, this family of models provides high-performance open vision-language model implementations designed for responsible AI development compared to similarly sized models. Using the benchmark evaluation metrics described, these models have shown to provide superior performance to other, comparably-sized open model alternatives.

instruction: Can you show me the same scene from a first-person perspective, as if walking through it?
description: Puffin is a unified camera-centric multimodal model that extends spatial awareness along the camera dimension. It integrates language regression and diffusion-based generation to interpret and create scenes from arbitrary viewpoints. Puffin is designed to bridge the modality gap between cameras and vision-language by treating camera as language, enabling thinking with camera. This approach aligns spatially grounded visual cues with photographic terminology while reasoning across geometric context. The model is trained on Puffin-4M, a large-scale dataset of 4 million vision-language-camera triplets, which includes both global camera parameters and pixel-wise camera maps. Puffin supports a range of tasks, including camera-centric understanding and generation from a single image or a pair of text and camera, world exploration through cross-view generation, spatial imagination based on an initial view and target camera configuration, and 3D virtual object insertion in AR/VR by calibrating camera parameters. Puffin demonstrates superior performance over specialized models for camera-centric generation and understanding. With instruction tuning, it generalizes to diverse cross-view tasks. The model is developed by Kang Liao, Size Wu, Zhonghua Wu, Linyi Jin, Chao Wang, Yikai Wang, Fei Wang, Wei Li, and Chen Change Loy, affiliated with S-Lab, Nanyang Technological University. It was first released as an arXiv pre-print in 2025. Puffin operates within a unified multimodal framework and supports the following modalities: Image → Text+Camera; Text+Camera → Image; Image+Camera → Image; Image+Camera → Text.

instruction: Make a 3D version of a cartoonish robot holding a wrench.
description: This project presents an experimental pipeline that connects natural language prompts to 3D model generation using a modified version of a pre-trained multi-view diffusion model. It is part of a final year project for the Comprehensive Creative Technologies Project at UWE Bristol, with the goal of exploring the potential of AI-assisted 3D content creation through natural language input. The model is based on a pre-trained model developed for the ML for 3D Course by researchers at Google Research. This project does not claim authorship of the model architecture or training process but serves as a custom wrapper for experimentation with text-to-3D workflows. The model allows input of a natural language description, internally maps the input to a representative image or multi-view description, and generates a 3D model using the LGM pipeline. It is a prototype intended for academic use only, with limited ability to handle complex or abstract text. The performance and quality of the model depend entirely on the base pre-trained model. The project acknowledges Hugging Face and the authors of LGM for making their models publicly available. The author is Gordon CHIN HO AU, a final year BSc Digital Media student at the University of the West of England, Bristol.

instruction: Can you find and highlight the visible animals in this picture?
description: YoloX is a machine learning model that predicts bounding boxes and classes of objects in an image. It is optimized for mobile and edge deployment and is designed for real-time object detection. The model is an implementation of Yolo-X Small, with an input resolution of 640x640. It has 8.98 million parameters and a model size of 34.3 MB in float precision, 9.53 MB in w8a16 precision, and 8.96 MB in w8a8 precision. The model is intended to run on Qualcomm® devices and supports various target runtimes, including TFLITE, QNN_DLC, and ONNX. Performance metrics include inference time and peak memory usage across different devices and chipsets. For example, on the Samsung Galaxy S25 with Snapdragon® 8 Elite For Galaxy Mobile, the model achieves an inference time of 1.099 ms in w8a8 precision and 2.313 ms in w8a16 precision. The model can be compiled for on-device deployment using the Qualcomm® AI Hub Workbench, and it supports performance profiling, accuracy verification, and deployment to Android using TensorFlow Lite or QNN. The model can be run on cloud-hosted Qualcomm® devices for evaluation and testing. The model is available for installation via pip and includes a demo for end-to-end execution. The original implementation of Yolo-X is licensed separately.

instruction: I need to create a script from this audio. Can you transcribe it for me?
description: Moonshine is a sequence-to-sequence automatic speech recognition (ASR) and speech translation model developed by Useful Sensors. The model is designed to transcribe English speech audio into English text and is intended for use in real-time speech transcription products based on low-cost hardware. Two model sizes are available: 'tiny' with 27 million parameters and 'base' with 61 million parameters. Both models are English-only and were trained on 200,000 hours of audio and corresponding transcripts collected from the internet and open datasets available on HuggingFace. The models are trained and evaluated primarily on English ASR tasks and may exhibit additional capabilities if fine-tuned for tasks like voice activity detection, speaker classification, or speaker diarization, though these have not been robustly evaluated. The models are intended for use by AI developers deploying English speech recognition systems in platforms with severe memory and computational constraints. However, the models may generate texts that are not actually spoken in the audio input (hallucinations), likely due to the model's general knowledge of language and its dual task of predicting the next word and transcribing the audio. The sequence-to-sequence architecture also makes the model prone to generating repetitive texts, which can be partially mitigated by beam search and temperature scheduling. These issues may be more pronounced for short audio segments or segments where parts of words are cut off. The models are not appropriate for use in high-risk domains or for subjective classification, particularly for inferring human attributes. The release of the models raises dual-use concerns, as the technology could be used to build surveillance systems or scale existing surveillance efforts. The models may also have some capabilities to recognize specific individuals, which presents safety concerns related to dual use and disparate performance. The models are available for use in Hugging Face Transformers and can be run with the Transformers library, Datasets, and Accelerate. The models were released in October 2024 and are described in a paper available on arXiv.

instruction: Make a digital artwork of a phoenix rising from ashes with a vibrant sunrise in the background.
description: Stable Diffusion 3.5 Large is a Multimodal Diffusion Transformer (MMDiT) text-to-image model developed by Stability AI. It is designed to generate images based on text prompts and features improved performance in image quality, typography, complex prompt understanding, and resource-efficiency. The model uses three fixed, pretrained text encoders and incorporates QK-normalization to improve training stability. It was trained on a wide variety of data, including synthetic data and filtered publicly available data. The model supports non-commercial and commercial use for organizations or individuals with less than $1M in total annual revenue under a community license, while those with higher revenue must obtain an Enterprise License. For local or self-hosted use, it can be accessed through ComfyUI, Huggingface Space, Diffusers, or GitHub. The model is intended for use in artistic processes, educational or creative tools, and research on generative models. It is not intended to generate factual or true representations of people or events. Safety measures have been implemented to reduce the risk of certain harms, but developers are encouraged to conduct their own testing and apply additional mitigations based on specific use cases. The model has undergone integrity evaluation methods, including structured evaluations and red-teaming testing, primarily in English. Risks include the potential for harmful content generation, misuse, and privacy violations, and developers are encouraged to implement content safety guardrails and adhere to privacy regulations. Users are required to follow the Stability AI Acceptable Use Policy. Contact information is available for reporting safety, security, privacy, licensing, or enterprise licensing issues.

instruction: Which district in Odisha had the most number of schools in 2021?
description: The model is a conditional generation model based on the MBart architecture, designed for table question answering (TableQA) in low-resource Indic languages, specifically Hindi and Bengali. It is intended to answer questions over structured tabular data, returning either individual cells or tables as output. The model supports tasks involving structured information retrieval and processing in these languages, where annotated data and neural models are scarce. The model is trained on large-scale datasets generated through a fully automatic data generation process, which is scalable and applicable to any low-resource language with a web presence. Evaluation results show that models trained on these datasets outperform state-of-the-art large language models. The model is evaluated on aspects such as mathematical reasoning capabilities and zero-shot cross-lingual transfer. The model uses a linearized representation of tables, combining the question with the processed table content as input. The model is implemented using the Hugging Face Transformers library and is available for use with the specified preprocessing and inference pipeline. The model is part of a research effort published in the 2024 Conference on Empirical Methods in Natural Language Processing. The model is trained to handle both Hindi and Bengali, with the data generation method being language-agnostic and adaptable to other low-resource languages. The model's performance is benchmarked against existing models, and the research emphasizes the importance of scalable data generation for low-resource language tasks. The model is released alongside datasets and code for further research and application.
domain: Natural Language Processing Table Question Answering

instruction: How would you describe the mood or atmosphere of this image?
description: BLIP is a Vision-Language Pre-training (VLP) framework designed to transfer flexibly to both vision-language understanding and generation tasks. It is trained on visual question answering using a base architecture with a ViT base backbone. BLIP addresses the limitations of existing pre-trained models, which typically excel in either understanding-based or generation-based tasks but not both. The model improves performance by effectively utilizing noisy web data through a process of bootstrapping captions, where a captioner generates synthetic captions and a filter removes the noisy ones. BLIP achieves state-of-the-art results on a wide range of vision-language tasks, including image-text retrieval with a 2.7% increase in average recall@1, image captioning with a 2.8% increase in CIDEr score, and visual question answering (VQA) with a 1.6% increase in VQA score. It also demonstrates strong generalization ability when directly transferred to video-language tasks in a zero-shot manner. The model can be used for conditional and un-conditional image captioning. This release is for research purposes in support of an academic paper. The models, datasets, and code are not specifically designed or evaluated for all downstream purposes. Users are strongly encouraged to evaluate and address potential concerns related to accuracy, safety, and fairness before deployment. Particular attention should be given to high-risk scenarios where errors or misuse could significantly impact people’s lives, rights, or safety. Applicable laws and best practices should be followed when selecting use cases.

instruction: What is the most common cause of delays listed in this table?
description: TAPAS is a BERT-like transformer model pretrained on a large corpus of English data from Wikipedia in a self-supervised fashion. It was pretrained on raw tables and associated texts without human labeling, using an automatic process to generate inputs and labels. The model was pretrained with two objectives: masked language modeling (MLM), where 15% of the words in a flattened table and associated context are randomly masked and the model must predict them, and intermediate pre-training, where the model is trained to classify whether a sentence is supported or refuted by the contents of a table using a balanced dataset of millions of syntactically created training examples. This allows the model to learn a bidirectional representation of tables and associated text, useful for downstream tasks such as answering questions about a table or determining whether a sentence is entailed or refuted by the contents of a table. The model is available in two versions: the default version uses relative position embeddings (resetting the position index at every cell of the table) and corresponds to the `tapas_wtq_wikisql_sqa_inter_masklm_base_reset` checkpoint, while the non-default version uses absolute position embeddings and corresponds to the `tapas_wtq_wikisql_sqa_inter_masklm_base` checkpoint. The model was pre-trained on MLM and an additional intermediate pre-training step, and then fine-tuned in a chain on SQA, WikiSQL, and finally WTQ. Fine-tuning was performed on 32 Cloud TPU v3 cores for 50,000 steps with a maximum sequence length of 512 and a batch size of 512. The optimizer used was Adam with a learning rate of 1.93581e-5 and a warmup ratio of 0.128960. An inductive bias was added to ensure the model only selects cells of the same column, as reflected by the `select_one_column` parameter of `TapasConfig`. The model can be used for answering questions related to a table. The model's performance varies by size and position embedding type. For the base model, the dev accuracy is 0.4525 for the noreset version and 0.4638 for the reset version. The team releasing TAPAS did not write a model card for this model, so this model card was written by the Hugging Face team and contributors.
domain: Natural Language Processing Table Question Answering

instruction: Please locate and label all the sports equipment in this gym image for a facility assessment.
description: YOLOE is a highly efficient, unified, and open object detection and segmentation model designed to operate under different prompt mechanisms, including text, visual inputs, and a prompt-free paradigm. It integrates detection and segmentation capabilities within a single model, enabling real-time performance across open scenarios. For text prompts, YOLOE employs a Re-parameterizable Region-Text Alignment (RepRTA) strategy, which refines pretrained textual embeddings using a lightweight auxiliary network and enhances visual-textual alignment without inference or transfer overhead. For visual prompts, it uses a Semantic-Activated Visual Prompt Encoder (SAVPE), which improves visual embedding and accuracy with minimal complexity. In the prompt-free scenario, YOLOE introduces a Lazy Region-Prompt Contrast (LRPC) strategy that utilizes a built-in large vocabulary and specialized embedding to identify all objects without relying on a language model. Extensive experiments demonstrate YOLOE's strong zero-shot performance and transferability, with high inference efficiency and low training cost. On the LVIS dataset, YOLOE-v8-S achieves a 3.5 AP improvement over YOLO-Worldv2-S with 3× less training cost and 1.4× faster inference. When transferring to COCO, YOLOE-v8-L achieves 0.6 AP^b and 0.4 AP^m gains over closed-set YOLOv8-L with nearly 4× less training time. YOLOE can be re-parameterized into standard YOLO models with zero inference and transfer overhead, making it highly adaptable for deployment.

instruction: Generate a list of 10 healthy breakfast recipes for a busy morning.
description: Qwen2.5 is the latest series of Qwen large language models. The Qwen2.5 series includes a range of base language models and instruction-tuned language models with parameter sizes ranging from 0.5 to 72 billion. Qwen2.5 introduces several improvements over Qwen2, including significantly more knowledge and enhanced capabilities in coding and mathematics, achieved through specialized expert models in these domains. It also shows significant improvements in instruction following, generating long texts (over 8K tokens), understanding structured data such as tables, and generating structured outputs, especially JSON. The model is more resilient to the diversity of system prompts, which enhances role-play implementation and condition-setting for chatbots. It supports long-context processing up to 128K tokens and can generate up to 8K tokens. The model supports over 29 languages, including Chinese, English, French, Spanish, Portuguese, German, Italian, Russian, Japanese, Korean, Vietnamese, Thai, and Arabic. This repository contains the base 0.5B Qwen2.5 model, which is a causal language model trained in the pretraining stage. It uses a transformer architecture with RoPE, SwiGLU, RMSNorm, attention QKV bias, and tied word embeddings. The model has 0.49 billion parameters, with 0.36 billion parameters not including embeddings. It consists of 24 layers and uses 14 attention heads for queries and 2 for key-value pairs. The model supports a context length of 32,768 tokens. Base language models are not recommended for use in conversations. Instead, post-training methods such as SFT, RLHF, or continued pretraining are advised for this model. Detailed evaluation results are available in a separate document. Requirements for GPU memory and throughput are also provided in a separate resource. If you find this work helpful, you may cite it using the provided references.

instruction: Can you identify and highlight any buses in the image using the provided labels?
description: This repository implements a custom task for zero-shot object detection for Hugging Face Inference Endpoints. The model is designed to detect objects in images based on candidate class labels provided in the input, without prior training on those specific classes. It supports the task of identifying and localizing objects in images using a set of candidate labels specified by the user. The model outputs bounding boxes, confidence scores, and corresponding class labels for detected objects. The bounding boxes are represented as [x_min, y_min, x_max, y_max] coordinates. The model uses a custom handler file for inference, and the repository includes a requirements.txt file to install the timm library. The model is intended to be deployed as an Inference Endpoint by selecting the 'Custom' task option. The model's input requires a base64-encoded image and a list of candidate class labels. The output includes a list of bounding boxes, scores, and matched candidate labels. The model is adapted for Hugging Face Inference Endpoints and was inspired by prior work on zero-shot object detection.

instruction: Could you take out the background from this image so I can add a new one later?
description: RMBG v2.0 is a state-of-the-art background removal model that significantly improves upon RMBG v1.4. The model is designed to effectively separate foreground from background in a range of categories and image types. It has been trained on a carefully selected dataset that includes general stock images, e-commerce, gaming, and advertising content, making it suitable for commercial use cases and enterprise content creation at scale. The accuracy, efficiency, and versatility of the model currently rival leading source-available models. It is ideal for applications where content safety, legally licensed datasets, and bias mitigation are paramount. Developed by BRIA AI, RMBG v2.0 is available as a source-available model for non-commercial use. The model is a dichotomous image segmentation model trained exclusively on a professional-grade dataset.
