The training data consists of over 15,000 high-quality, high-resolution, manually labeled (pixel-wise accuracy), fully licensed images. The dataset includes balanced gender, balanced ethnicity, and people with different types of disabilities. The distribution of images is as follows: 45.11% objects only, 25.24% people with objects or animals, 17.35% people only, 8.52% people/objects/animals with text, 2.52% text only, and 1.89% animals only. The distribution of image types is 87.70% photorealistic and 12.30% non-photorealistic. The distribution of background types is 52.05% non-solid background and 47.95% solid background. The distribution of foreground content is 51.42% single main foreground object and 48.58% multiple objects in the foreground. RMBG-2.0 is developed on an enhanced architecture with proprietary dataset and training scheme, which significantly improves the model’s accuracy and effectiveness for the background-removal task. The model is released under a CC BY-NC 4.0 license for non-commercial use, and commercial use is subject to a commercial agreement with BRIA. If you use this model in your research, please cite the article titled 'Bilateral Reference for High-Resolution Dichotomous Image Segmentation' by Zheng, Peng and Gao, Dehong and Fan, Deng-Ping and Liu, Li and Laaksonen, Jorma and Ouyang, Wanli and Sebe, Nicu, published in CAAI Artificial Intelligence Research in 2024.

instruction: Write a Bash script that compresses all .txt files in a directory into a single .tar.gz archive.
description: Qwen2.5-Coder is a code-specific large language model, part of the Qwen series, previously known as CodeQwen. It is available in six different model sizes, ranging from 0.5 to 32 billion parameters, to accommodate the needs of various developers. Qwen2.5-Coder introduces significant improvements in code generation, code reasoning, and code fixing. It was trained on 5.5 trillion tokens, including source code, text-code grounding, and synthetic data. The Qwen2.5-Coder-32B model is currently the state-of-the-art open-source code language model, with coding abilities comparable to GPT-4o. The model supports real-world applications such as Code Agents, maintaining strong performance in mathematics and general competencies. It provides long-context support up to 128K tokens. The instruction-tuned 7B Qwen2.5-Coder model is a causal language model with 7.61B total parameters and 6.53B non-embedding parameters. It has 28 layers and uses a transformer architecture with RoPE, SwiGLU, RMSNorm, and Attention QKV bias. The model has 28 attention heads for queries and 4 for key-value pairs. It supports a full context length of 131,072 tokens. For long texts beyond 32,768 tokens, the model uses YaRN, a technique for enhancing model length extrapolation. YaRN can be enabled by adding a specific configuration to the model's config.json file. However, YaRN is currently only supported with static scaling in vLLM, which may affect performance on shorter texts. The model is compatible with the latest Hugging Face transformers library, and using versions below 4.37.0 may result in errors. The model can be loaded and used with the AutoModelForCausalLM and AutoTokenizer classes from the transformers library. Evaluation results and performance details are available in the provided references. The model is described in the Qwen2.5-Coder Technical Report and the Qwen2 Technical Report, both available on arXiv.

instruction: What objects are visible in this image, and can you highlight them with bounding boxes?
description: MM Grounding DINO (base variant) was proposed by Xiangyu Zhao, Yicheng Chen, Shilin Xu, Xiangtai Li, Xinjiang Wang, Yining Li, and Haian Huang. It improves upon previous models by enhancing the contrastive class head and removing parameter sharing in the decoder, which leads to improved zero-shot detection performance. Specifically, it achieves 50.6 AP (an increase of 2.2) on COCO and 31.9 val AP (an increase of 11.8) and 41.4 minival AP (an increase of 12.6) on LVIS. The model is designed for zero-shot object detection and can be used with raw inputs. It was trained on a combination of datasets including O365, GoldG, GRIT, and V3Det. Evaluation results show that the model achieves strong performance across different backbones and pre-training data combinations. For example, with a Swin-T backbone and pre-training data from O365, GoldG, and V3Det, the model achieves 50.6 COCO mAP. With a Swin-B backbone and pre-training data from O365, GoldG, and V3Det, the model achieves 52.5 COCO mAP. With a Swin-L backbone and pre-training data from O365V2, OpenImageV6, and GoldG, the model achieves 53.0 COCO mAP. On LVIS, the model achieves 41.4 minival AP and 31.9 val AP with pre-training data from O365, GoldG, GRIT, and V3Det. The model is available in the mm_grounding_dino_base_all collection, and users can find all original checkpoints there. The model can be used for zero-shot object detection by preparing a processor and model, loading an image, and running inference with text labels. Postprocessing is required to extract detection results, including boxes, scores, and labels. The model is described in the paper titled 'An Open and Comprehensive Pipeline for Unified Object Grounding and Detection,' which is available as an arXiv preprint.

instruction: How can I improve the visual flow of this text?
description: This model is a fine-tuned version of an unspecified base model on an unknown dataset. It achieves a loss of 0.0000 on the evaluation set. The model was trained using the following hyperparameters: a learning rate of 5e-05, a training batch size of 4, an evaluation batch size of 8, a random seed of 42, and the ADAMW_TORCH optimizer with betas of (0.9, 0.999) and an epsilon of 1e-08. The learning rate scheduler used is reduce_lr_on_plateau with a warmup ratio of 0.06. The model was trained for 100 epochs. During training, the training loss decreased from 3.3707 at epoch 0.7692 to 0.0 at epoch 100. The validation loss also decreased from 0.8298 at epoch 0.7692 to 0.0000 at epoch 100. The model was trained using Transformers 4.48.0, PyTorch 2.4.1.post100, Datasets 3.2.0, and Tokenizers 0.21.0.

instruction: I want to see an image of a romantic scene involving two people in a private setting. Could you generate that?
description: The FLUX Uncensored LoRA v2 is an enhanced version of the original FLUX Uncensored LoRA, designed for the base model black-forest-labs/FLUX.1-dev. This version addresses issues present in the previous release and offers improved performance and stability. The LoRA weights have been fine-tuned to remove the base model's content restrictions, allowing for the generation of NSFW (Not Safe For Work) and other uncensored content. This LoRA extension can be loaded into the FLUX.1-dev pipeline using the diffusers library. It is optimized for high-quality, explicit image generation based on user-provided prompts. The model is intended for research and personal use, and adheres to the non-commercial license terms. The model has been optimized for better performance and faster generation times. Issues present in the previous version have been addressed, ensuring a more stable and reliable experience. The LoRA weights have been streamlined to reduce the model size without compromising quality. The base model is black-forest-labs/FLUX.1-dev. The LoRA weights are provided by enhanceaiteam/Flux-uncensored-v2. The LoRA weight file is lora.safetensors. The recommended torch data type is torch.bfloat16. A CUDA-enabled GPU is recommended for optimal performance. The model follows the FLUX-1-dev Non-Commercial License. Users should exercise caution and adhere to legal and ethical guidelines. This model allows the generation of explicit content. The creators do not endorse illegal or unethical use of the model. Content generated using this model should comply with platform guidelines and local regulations regarding NSFW material. Use the following trigger words to guide the model toward generating NSFW content: nsfw, naked, pron, kissing, erotic, nude, sensual, adult content, explicit. These keywords, along with descriptive prompts, help the model generate explicit imagery.

instruction: Please find the object in the image that most closely matches the phrase 'a red sports car'.
description: SigLIP 2 So400m extends the pretraining objective with prior, independently developed techniques into a unified recipe, for improved semantic understanding, localization, and dense features. The model can be used for tasks like zero-shot image classification and image-text retrieval, or as a vision encoder for VLMs and other vision tasks. SigLIP 2 adds training objectives such as decoder loss, global-local and masked prediction loss, and aspect ratio and resolution adaptibility. The model is pre-trained on the WebLI dataset and was trained on up to 2048 TPU-v5e chips. Evaluation results are presented in the associated paper. The model is described in the paper titled 'SigLIP 2: Multilingual Vision-Language Encoders with Improved Semantic Understanding, Localization, and Dense Features' by Michael Tschannen, Alexey Gritsenko, Xiao Wang, Muhammad Ferjad Naeem, Ibrahim Alabdulmohsin, Nikhil Parthasarathy, Talfan Evans, Lucas Beyer, Ye Xia, Basil Mustafa, Olivier Hénaff, Jeremiah Harmsen, Andreas Steiner, and Xiaohua Zhai, available on arXiv with the identifier 2502.14786.

instruction: 특정 날짜 사이에 생성된 레코드를 검색하는 쿼리를 만들어줘.
description: Llama-3.1-8B-Spider-SQL-Ko는 한국어 질문을 SQL 쿼리로 변환하는 Text-to-SQL 모델입니다. 이 모델은 train 데이터셋을 한국어로 번역한 데이터셋을 활용하여 미세조정되었습니다. Spider 한국어 검증 데이터셋(1,034개) 평가 결과, 정확 일치율은 42.65% (441/1034), 실행 정확도는 65.47% (677/1034)입니다. 실행 정확도가 정확 일치율보다 높은 이유는 SQL 문법이 다르더라도 동일한 결과를 반환하는 경우가 많기 때문입니다. 이 모델은 Llama 3.1 8B Instruct (4bit 양자화)를 기반으로 하며, 1-epoch 학습 데이터를 사용하였습니다. 166개의 다양한 도메인 데이터베이스를 지원하며, LoRA (r=16, alpha=32)를 학습 방법으로 사용하였습니다. 영어 테이블/컬럼명을 사용하며, Spider 데이터셋 도메인에 최적화되어 있습니다. NoSQL, 그래프 DB는 미지원하며, 매우 복잡한 중첩 쿼리는 정확도가 하락할 수 있습니다. 학습은 NVIDIA Tesla T4 (16GB) GPU에서 약 4시간 동안 진행되었으며, 최대 7.6GB VRAM을 사용하였습니다. 학습 시 하이퍼파라미터로 per_device_train_batch_size 2, gradient_accumulation_steps 4, learning_rate 5e-4, num_train_epochs 1, optimizer adamw_8bit, lr_scheduler_type cosine, warmup_ratio 0.05를 사용하였습니다. LoRA 설정은 r=16, lora_alpha=32, lora_dropout=0, target_modules로 q_proj, k_proj, v_proj, o_proj, gate_proj, up_proj, down_proj를 포함합니다.
domain: Natural Language Processing Table Question Answering

instruction: Can you evaluate the safety of this prompt and its potential response?
description: Llama Guard 4 is a natively multimodal safety classifier with 12 billion parameters trained jointly on text and multiple images. It is a dense architecture pruned from the Llama 4 Scout pre-trained model and fine-tuned for content safety classification. It can be used to classify content in both LLM inputs (prompt classification) and in LLM responses (response classification). It generates text in its output that indicates whether a given prompt or response is safe or unsafe, and if unsafe, it also lists the content categories violated. Llama Guard 4 was aligned to safeguard against the standardized MLCommons and designed to support multimodal Llama 4 capabilities within a single safety classifier. It combines the capabilities of the previous Llama Guard 3-8B and Llama Guard 3-11B-vision models by supporting English and multilingual text prompts as well as mixed text-and-image prompts for image understanding. Unlike Llama Guard 3-11B-vision, Llama Guard 4 now supports safety classification when multiple images are given in the prompt as input. It is also integrated into the Llama Moderations API for text and images. The model is trained to predict safety labels on the following categories: S1: Violent Crimes, S2: Non-Violent Crimes, S3: Sex-Related Crimes, S4: Child Sexual Exploitation, S5: Defamation, S6: Specialized Advice, S7: Privacy, S8: Intellectual Property, S9: Indiscriminate Weapons, S10: Hate, S11: Suicide & Self-Harm, S12: Sexual Content, S13: Elections, and S14: Code Interpreter Abuse (text only). Llama Guard 4 is a natively multimodal safeguard model with 12 billion parameters in total and uses an early fusion transformer architecture with dense layers to keep the overall size small. It can be run on a single GPU and shares the same tokenizer and vision encoder as Llama 4 Scout and Maverick. Llama Guard 4 employs a dense feedforward early-fusion architecture, differing from Llama 4 Scout, which uses Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) layers. It is developed by pruning the pre-trained Llama 4 Scout checkpoint, which consists of one shared dense expert and sixteen routed experts in each MoE layer. All the routed experts and router layers are pruned, retaining only the shared expert. After pruning, the MoE is reduced to a dense feedforward layer initiated from the shared expert weights. The model is post-trained with a blend of data from the Llama 4 Scout and Maverick models, with additional multi-image training data and multilingual data. The training data is blended with a ratio of roughly 3:1 text-only data to multimodal data containing one or more images. Llama Guard 4 is designed to be used in an integrated system with a generative language model, reducing the overall rate of safety violations exposed to the user. It can be used for input filtering, output filtering, or both. Input filtering classifies user prompts into an LLM as safe or unsafe, while output filtering classifies an LLM’s generated output as safe or unsafe. Using both filtering types provides additional security. In some internal tests, input filtering reduces safety violation rate and raises overall refusal rate more than output filtering does, but results may vary. Llama Guard 4 matches or exceeds the overall performance of the Llama Guard 3 models on both input and output filtering, for English and multilingual text and for mixed text and images. Classifier performance shows that Llama Guard 4 matches or exceeds the overall performance of Llama Guard 3-8B (LG3) on English and multilingual text, as well as Llama Guard 3-11B-vision (LG3v) on prompts with single or multiple images. For English, recall is 69%, false positive rate is 11%, and F1 is 61%, with a 4% increase in recall, -3% in false positive rate, and 8% in F1 compared to LG3. For multilingual, recall is 43%, false positive rate is 3%, and F1 is 51%, with a -2% increase in recall, -1% in false positive rate, and 0% in F1 compared to LG3. For single-image, recall is 41%, false positive rate is 9%, and F1 is 38%, with a 10% increase in recall, 0% in false positive rate, and 8% in F1 compared to LG3v. For multi-image, recall is 61%, false positive rate is 9%, and F1 is 52%, with a 20% increase in recall, -1% in false positive rate, and 17% in F1 compared to LG3v. Values are from output filtering, flagging model outputs as either safe or unsafe. All values are an average over samples from safety categories S1 through S13, weighting each category equally, except for multilinguality, for which it is an average over the 7 shipped non-English languages of Llama Guard 3-8B: French, German, Hindi, Italian, Portuguese, Spanish, and Thai. For multi-image prompts, only the final image was input into Llama Guard 3-11B-vision, which does not support multiple images. Evaluations against competitor models are omitted, as they are typically not aligned with the specific safety policy that this classifier was trained on, prohibiting direct comparisons. There are some limitations associated with Llama Guard 4. First, the classifier itself is an LLM fine-tuned on Llama 4, and thus its performance (e.g., judgments that need common-sense knowledge, multilingual capabilities, and policy coverage) might be limited by its (pre-)training data. Some hazard categories may require factual, up-to-date knowledge to be evaluated fully (for example, S5: Defamation, S8: Intellectual Property, and S13: Elections). More complex systems should be deployed to accurately moderate these categories for use cases highly sensitive to these types of hazards, but Llama Guard 4 provides a good baseline for generic use cases. The performance of Llama Guard 4 was tested mostly with prompts containing a few images (three, most frequently), so performance may vary if using it to classify safety with a much larger number of images. As an LLM, Llama Guard 4 may be susceptible to adversarial attacks or prompt injection attacks that could bypass or alter its intended use. Users are encouraged to report vulnerabilities for potential improvements in future versions of Llama Guard.

instruction: Identify all the visible animals and their positions in this image.
description: MM Grounding DINO (base variant) was proposed by Xiangyu Zhao, Yicheng Chen, Shilin Xu, Xiangtai Li, Xinjiang Wang, Yining Li, and Haian Huang. It improves upon previous models by enhancing the contrastive class head and removing parameter sharing in the decoder, which leads to improved zero-shot detection performance. Specifically, it achieves 50.6 AP (an increase of 2.2) on COCO and 31.9 val AP (an increase of 11.8) and 41.4 minival AP (an increase of 12.6) on LVIS. The model is designed for zero-shot object detection and can be used with raw inputs. It was trained on a combination of datasets including O365, GoldG, GRIT, and V3Det. Evaluation results show that the model performs well across different backbones and pre-training data combinations. For example, with a Swin-T backbone and pre-training data of O365, GoldG, and V3Det, it achieves 50.6 COCO mAP. On LVIS, the model achieves 41.4 minival AP and 31.9 val AP when trained on O365, GoldG, GRIT, and V3Det. The model is available in the collection under the identifier rziga/mm_grounding_dino_base_o365v1_goldg_v3det.

instruction: What kind of food is shown in this image of a plate?
description: This is a CLIP ViT-B/32 model trained using the LAION-2B English subset of the LAION-5B dataset. The model was trained by Romain Beaumont. It is intended for research purposes and is designed to support zero-shot, arbitrary image classification, as well as interdisciplinary studies on the potential impact of such models. The model can be used for tasks such as zero-shot image classification, image and text retrieval, image classification and other image task fine-tuning, linear probe image classification, and image generation guiding and conditioning. However, any deployed use case of the model, whether commercial or not, is currently out of scope. Non-deployed use cases, such as image search in a constrained environment, are also not recommended unless there is thorough in-domain testing of the model with a specific, fixed class taxonomy. This is due to the high need for task-specific testing, especially given the variability of CLIP’s performance with different class taxonomies, which makes untested and unconstrained deployment potentially harmful. Use cases related to surveillance and facial recognition are always out of scope, regardless of the model's performance, due to the lack of testing norms and checks to ensure fair use. The model has not been trained or evaluated on any languages other than English, so its use should be limited to English language use cases. The model was trained on a 2 Billion sample English subset of LAION-5B, a dataset created to democratize research and experimentation around large-scale multi-modal model training and handling of uncurated, large-scale datasets crawled from publicly available internet. The dataset is uncurated, and the links may lead to strongly discomforting and disturbing content for human viewers. Users are advised to use the dataset with caution and at their own risk. A 'safe' subset can be extracted by filtering out samples based on safety tags using a customized trained NSFW classifier, but harmful content may still be present in safe mode. The dataset is provided openly to allow for transparent investigation of the benefits and pitfalls of training large-scale models, but it is not recommended for creating ready-to-go industrial products, as the research on the general properties and safety of such models is still in progress. The model was evaluated using a combination of VTAB (with additional robustness datasets) for classification and COCO and Flickr for retrieval. It achieves a 66.6 zero-shot top-1 accuracy on ImageNet-1k. An initial round of benchmarks has been performed on a wider range of datasets, but detailed metrics for this model are not yet available in table form. The model was trained using compute resources provided by the specified cluster.

instruction: Where in the paper is the training loss and performance metrics presented?
description: ColPali is a model based on a novel architecture and training strategy using Vision Language Models (VLMs) to efficiently index documents from their visual features. It generates multi-vector representations of text and images, extending the PaliGemma-3B model with a ColBERT strategy. ColPali was introduced in a research paper and first released as a HuggingFace `transformers` 🤗 implementation contributed by Tony Wu and Yoni Gozlan. The model is trained on a dataset of 127,460 query-page pairs, consisting of 63% from openly available academic datasets and 37% from a synthetic dataset made up of web-crawled PDF documents augmented with VLM-generated pseudo-questions. The training set is fully English, allowing for the study of zero-shot generalization to non-English languages. A validation set of 2% of the samples is used to tune hyperparameters. The model is trained for one epoch in `bfloat16` format, using low-rank adapters with `alpha=32` and `r=32` on the transformer layers from the language model and the final projection layer. Training is conducted on an 8 GPU setup with data parallelism, a learning rate of 5e-5 with linear decay and 2.5% warmup steps, and a batch size of 32. ColPali is designed for document retrieval tasks, particularly for PDF-type documents. It relies on multi-vector retrieval derived from the ColBERT late interaction mechanism, which may require engineering efforts to adapt to vector retrieval frameworks that lack native multi-vector support. The model's vision language backbone (PaliGemma) is licensed under the `gemma` license, and ColPali inherits this license. The model is primarily focused on high-resource languages and may have limited generalization to other document types or less represented languages.

instruction: I want to know where in this document the authors mention the sample size used in their research. Can you locate that?
description: ColSmolVLM-Instruct-256M is a visual retriever model based on SmolVLM-Instruct-250M, incorporating the ColBERT strategy for efficient document indexing using visual features. It generates multi-vector representations of text and images. This version was trained with a batch size of 32 for 3 epochs using the commit b983e40 of the Colpali repository. The training dataset consists of 127,460 query-page pairs, including 63% from openly available academic datasets and 37% from a synthetic dataset made up of web-crawled PDF documents augmented with VLM-generated pseudo-questions. The dataset is fully English by design, allowing for the study of zero-shot generalization to non-English languages. No multi-page PDF document is used in both the training and evaluation sets to prevent contamination. A validation set of 2% of the samples is used for hyperparameter tuning. Training is conducted in bfloat16 format, using low-rank adapters with alpha=32 and r=32 on the transformer layers of the language model and the final projection layer. The paged_adamw_8bit optimizer is used with a 4 GPU setup, data parallelism, a learning rate of 5e-4 with linear decay and 2.5% warmup steps, and a batch size of 8. The model is intended for document retrieval tasks, particularly for PDF-type documents. It relies on multi-vector retrieval derived from the ColBERT late interaction mechanism, which may require engineering efforts to adapt to vector retrieval frameworks that do not natively support multi-vectors. The model's performance is evaluated on its ability to generalize to non-English languages, though it may have limitations in this area. The vision language backbone model is licensed under Apache 2.0, while the adapters are under the MIT license.

instruction: Could you create a mask that isolates the main subject in this street scene?
description: BiRefNet is a model designed for high-resolution dichotomous image segmentation (DIS). It is trained on the DIS-TR dataset and validated on DIS-TE and DIS-VD datasets. The model has achieved state-of-the-art performance on three tasks: DIS, HRSOD, and COD. BiRefNet is based on a bilateral reference approach, which is intended to improve segmentation accuracy and resolution. The model supports inference on single images and can be used with adjustable resolutions. It is implemented in Python and can be loaded using the HuggingFace Transformers library or from local GitHub code. The model weights are available on HuggingFace, and the model can be used for inference with or without GPU acceleration. The model includes a preprocessing pipeline that resizes images, normalizes pixel values, and applies a sigmoid function to the final predictions. The model outputs a segmentation mask that can be visualized or used for further processing. The model is described in the paper 'Bilateral Reference for High-Resolution Dichotomous Image Segmentation,' published in CAAI Artificial Intelligence Research in 2024. The authors of the model are Peng Zheng, Dehong Gao, Deng-Ping Fan, Li Liu, Jorma Laaksonen, Wanli Ouyang, and Nicu Sebe, affiliated with Nankai University, Northwestern Polytechnical University, National University of Defense Technology, Aalto University, Shanghai AI Laboratory, and University of Trento, respectively. The model is supported by GPU resources provided by @fal and deployment assistance from @not-lain.

instruction: What is the main event taking place in this clip?
description: VideoLLaMA 2 is a video language model designed to advance spatial-temporal modeling and audio understanding in video-LLMs. It supports tasks such as multi-choice and open-ended video question answering, video captioning, multi-choice and open-ended audio question answering, and open-ended audio-visual question answering. The model includes both vision-only and audio-visual checkpoints, with different configurations for base and chat types, and varying numbers of training frames. VideoLLaMA 2 is available in a ~7B-sized version and has achieved top performance on relevant benchmarks. The model is capable of handling audio, video, and audio-visual modalities, with inference code provided for each. The model is based on a technical report that includes the latest results and references, and it is continuously updated with new checkpoints and features. Users are encouraged to cite the model in their research and applications.

instruction: Can you identify the brand of this smartphone shown in the image?
description: The BEiT model is a Vision Transformer (ViT), which is a transformer encoder model similar to BERT. It was pre-trained in a self-supervised manner on ImageNet-22k (also referred to as ImageNet-21k), a dataset containing 14 million images and 21,841 classes, at a resolution of 224x224 pixels. The pre-training objective involves predicting visual tokens from the encoder of OpenAI's DALL-E's VQ-VAE, based on masked patches. Following pre-training, the model was fine-tuned in a supervised fashion on the same dataset at the same resolution. Images are processed as a sequence of fixed-size 16x16 pixel patches, which are linearly embedded. Unlike original ViT models, BEiT uses relative position embeddings (similar to T5) instead of absolute position embeddings. For image classification, the model performs mean-pooling of the final hidden states of the patches, rather than relying on a [CLS] token. The pre-training process enables the model to learn an internal representation of images that can be used to extract features for downstream tasks. For example, a standard classifier can be trained by placing a linear layer on top of the pre-trained encoder, typically using the [CLS] token's final hidden state as a representation of the entire image. Alternatively, one can mean-pool the final hidden states of the patch embeddings and place a linear layer on top of that. The model is intended for use in image classification tasks. It was trained on a dataset consisting of 14 million images and 21k classes, and fine-tuned on the same dataset. During preprocessing, images are resized and normalized to a resolution of 224x224 with mean (0.5, 0.5, 0.5) and standard deviation (0.5, 0.5, 0.5) across the RGB channels. Evaluation results on image classification benchmarks are detailed in the original paper, with the best results achieved using a higher resolution. Increasing the model size is expected to improve performance. The model was introduced in the paper by Hangbo Bao, Li Dong, and Furu Wei and first released by the Hugging Face team, as the original team did not provide a model card. The model supports PyTorch for both the feature extractor and the model itself.

instruction: What emotional tone does this Indonesian news headline convey?
description: This model is a fine-tuned version designed for assessing text sentiment across multiple languages. It is based on the E5 small model architecture and supports sentiment assessment for text in various languages. The intended uses include assessing the sentiment of multilingual text, filtering multilingual content, and conducting comparative analysis of corpus text sentiment across different languages. The model was trained on parallel sentences in English and various other languages, including semantic similarity scores calculated using LaBSE, additional sentiment metrics, and data from sources such as JW300, Europarl, TED Talks, OPUS-100, Tatoeba, Global Voices, and News Commentary. Performance may vary for languages not well-represented in the training data, and the model should not be used as the sole criterion for sentiment assessment. The model was evaluated on a diverse set of multilingual text samples, including 10 English text samples of varying sentiment translated into Arabic, Chinese, French, Russian, and Spanish. The model demonstrated consistent sentiment assessment across different languages for the same text. Training was conducted using a learning rate of 5e-05, a train batch size of 128, an evaluation batch size of 8, a seed of 42, the adamw_torch optimizer with betas (0.9, 0.999) and epsilon 1e-08, a linear learning rate scheduler, and 3 training epochs. Training loss decreased from 0.1946 to 0.1121 over the three epochs, with corresponding validation losses of 0.1647, 0.1528, and 0.1455. The model was developed using Transformers 4.46.3, PyTorch 2.5.1+cu124, Datasets 3.1.0, and Tokenizers 0.20.3.

instruction: Can I apply this technique to classify regions in whole-slide images?
description: H0-mini is a lightweight foundation model for histology developed by a team of researchers. The model is a Vision Transformer Base/14 that was distilled from H-optimus-0 (ViT-g/14) using the DINOv2 self-supervised distillation method on PanCancer40M, a dataset consisting of 43 million histology tiles extracted from 6,093 histology slides. H0-mini achieves comparable performance to current histology foundation models while significantly reducing inference cost. It also demonstrates strong robustness to variations in staining and scanning protocols. The model can be used with or without fine-tuning for various downstream applications, such as slide-level classification using multiple-instance learning algorithms like ABMIL. For feature extraction, the CLS token is recommended as input for downstream tasks, and concatenating the CLS token features with the average of patch token features may improve performance on some tasks. The model can be used to extract features from histology images for applications such as ROI classification, slide classification, and segmentation. The pre-training dataset includes tile coordinates provided in .txt files, with each file containing the paths to tiles for a given slide. These files include the cohort name, slide ID, and tile coordinates at a specific deepzoom level. The model requires software dependencies including torch version 2.0.0 or higher, torchvision version 0.15.0 or higher, and xformers version 0.0.18 or higher. If using this model, users are requested to cite the associated work titled 'Distilling foundation models for robust and efficient models in digital pathology' by Alexandre Filiot and colleagues. The work was supported by computing resources from the High-Performance Computing (HPC) resources of IDRIS under specific allocations. The model was built upon a repository licensed under the Apache License 2.0. The results are partly based on data generated by the TCGA Research Network.

instruction: What do you think the video is trying to show?
description: VideoLLaMA 2 is a video language model designed to advance spatial-temporal modeling and audio understanding in video-LLMs. It supports tasks such as multi-choice video question answering, video captioning, and open-ended video question answering. The model is available in different configurations, including base and chat variants, with varying numbers of training frames, such as 8 or 16 frames. As of June 25, 2024, the ~7B-sized version of VideoLLaMA 2 is the top-performing model in its category on the specified benchmark. The model includes a visual encoder and a language decoder, and it has been trained and evaluated using specific codebases that are publicly available. The model is intended for use in video understanding tasks and is accompanied by a technical report that includes the latest results and references. Users are encouraged to provide feedback on related works that may have been omitted from the paper.

instruction: Could you process this image and mark the full set of body points needed for kinematic modeling?
description: The SynthPose model was proposed by Yoni Gozlan, Antoine Falisse, Scott Uhlrich, Anthony Gatti, Michael Black, and Akshay Chaudhari. This model uses a VitPose Base backbone and is a new approach that enables fine-tuning of pre-trained 2D human pose models to predict an arbitrarily denser set of keypoints for accurate kinematic analysis through the use of synthetic data. This particular variant was fine-tuned on a set of keypoints usually found on motion capture setups, and includes COCO keypoints as well. The model predicts 52 markers, where the first 17 are the COCO keypoints and the next 35 are anatomical markers. The model is designed to support tasks related to human pose estimation, including the detection of human figures in images and the prediction of detailed anatomical keypoints for each detected person. The model is intended for use in applications requiring precise kinematic analysis, such as biomechanics or motion capture. The model's architecture is based on the VitPose Base variant, and it is fine-tuned to enhance its ability to predict a larger and more detailed set of keypoints than standard models. The model is evaluated for its ability to accurately predict these additional keypoints, which are typically not included in standard pose estimation models. The model includes explicit caveats regarding its intended use and the necessity of using appropriate detection and processing pipelines to achieve accurate results. It is recommended to use a detector to identify human figures in images before applying the model for pose estimation. The model is not intended for use in scenarios where the input data does not conform to the expected format or where the model's assumptions about the input are not met.

instruction: Can you summarize the plot of this short story adaptation?
description: InternVideo2.5 is a video multimodal large language model (MLLM) built upon InternVL2.5, enhanced with long and rich context (LRC) modeling. It improves the ability to perceive fine-grained details and capture long-form temporal structures. This is achieved through dense vision task annotations using direct preference optimization (TPO) and compact spatiotemporal representations via adaptive hierarchical token compression (HiCo). On the VideoBenchmark, InternVideo2.5 achieves 75.7 on MVBench, 60.6 on LongVideoBench, and 65.1 on VideoMME (without subtitles). Inference speed is measured in tokens per second for generating 1024 new tokens and 5198 (8192-2998) tokens with a video context of 2998 tokens under BF16 precision. With BF16 quantization, the speed is 33.40 tokens/s for 3022 tokens, 31.91 tokens/s for 8192 tokens without the video encoder, and 21.33 tokens/s with the encoder. With INT4 quantization, the speed is 31.95 tokens/s for 8192 tokens without the encoder and 26.37 tokens/s with the encoder. These measurements are conducted on a single A800-SXM4-80G GPU with PyTorch 2.4.0 and CUDA 12.1. The model supports video analysis tasks, including detailed video description and multi-turn conversational interactions. It is designed to handle up to 512 frames and can be configured to process video segments based on duration. The model uses a generation configuration with no sampling, a temperature of 0.0, a maximum of 1024 new tokens, a top-p of 0.1, and a single beam. The model is available for use with specific installation requirements, including the installation of several Python packages and the use of a pre-trained model path. The model processes video input by loading and transforming video frames, applying dynamic preprocessing, and generating outputs through a chat interface that supports both single-turn and multi-turn conversations.

instruction: I’m trying to locate a document that visually represents the steps of a scientific process. Can you assist?
description: The embedding model trained by Jina AI. Jina Embeddings v4 is a universal embedding model for multimodal and multilingual retrieval. The model is specially designed for complex document retrieval, including visually rich documents with charts, tables, and illustrations. Built on the Qwen2.5-VL-3B-Instruct base model, Jina Embeddings v4 features unified embeddings for text, images, and visual documents, supporting both dense (single-vector) and late-interaction (multi-vector) retrieval. It provides multilingual support for 30+ languages and is compatible with a wide range of domains, including technical and visually complex documents. The model includes task-specific adapters for retrieval, text matching, and code-related tasks, which can be selected at inference time. It offers flexible embedding sizes, with dense embeddings defaulting to 2048 dimensions but capable of being truncated to as low as 128 with minimal performance loss. The model uses BFloat 16 as its data type, supports a maximum sequence length of 32768, and employs mean pooling as its pooling strategy. It also uses the FlashAttention2 attention mechanism. The model is available for use via the Jina AI Embeddings API, the Hugging Face Transformers library, the sentence-transformers interface, and vLLM. For vLLM, separate model versions are provided for each task, with specific adapters merged into the base model weights. The model is governed by the Qwen Research License. Alongside the model, Jina AI has released Jina-VDR, a multilingual, multi-domain benchmark for visual document retrieval. If used in research, the model should be cited with the provided citation information.

instruction: Where is the car located in this image?
description: OWL-ViT, short for Vision Transformer for Open-World Localization, is a zero-shot text-conditioned object detection model that allows users to query an image with one or multiple text queries. It was proposed by Matthias Minderer, Alexey Gritsenko, Austin Stone, Maxim Neumann, Dirk Weissenborn, Alexey Dosovitskiy, Aravindh Mahendran, Anurag Arnab, Mostafa Dehghani, Zhuoran Shen, Xiao Wang, Xiaohua Zhai, Thomas Kipf, and Neil Houlsby. OWL-ViT uses CLIP as its multi-modal backbone, combining a ViT-like Transformer for visual features and a causal language model for text features. To enable detection, the final token pooling layer of the vision model is removed, and a lightweight classification and box head is attached to each transformer output token. Open-vocabulary classification is achieved by replacing the fixed classification layer weights with class-name embeddings from the text model. The CLIP backbone is trained from scratch and fine-tuned end-to-end with the classification and box heads on standard detection datasets using a bipartite matching loss. The model uses a CLIP backbone with a ViT-L/14 Transformer architecture as an image encoder and a masked self-attention Transformer as a text encoder. These encoders are trained to maximize the similarity of (image, text) pairs via a contrastive loss. The CLIP backbone is trained from scratch and fine-tuned together with the box and class prediction heads with an object detection objective. The CLIP backbone was trained on publicly available image-caption data, including data crawled from the internet and pre-existing image datasets. The prediction heads of OWL-ViT, along with the CLIP backbone, are fine-tuned on publicly available object detection datasets. The model is intended as a research output for the research community, enabling researchers to better understand and explore zero-shot, text-conditioned object detection. The primary intended users are AI researchers, who are expected to use the model to study robustness, generalization, and other capabilities, biases, and constraints of computer vision models. The model was released in May 2022.

instruction: I need the content of this audio file in Italian written out for a transcript.
description: Whisper is a pre-trained model for automatic speech recognition (ASR) and speech translation. Trained on 680,000 hours of labelled speech data using large-scale weak supervision, Whisper models demonstrate strong generalization capabilities across many datasets and domains without the need for fine-tuning. Whisper is a Transformer-based encoder-decoder model, also referred to as a sequence-to-sequence model. The models were trained on either English-only data or multilingual data. English-only models were trained for speech recognition, while multilingual models were trained for both speech recognition and speech translation. For speech recognition, the model predicts transcriptions in the same language as the audio. For speech translation, the model predicts transcriptions in a different language from the audio. Whisper checkpoints come in five configurations of varying model sizes. The smallest four are trained on either English-only or multilingual data, while the largest checkpoints are multilingual only. The model is used in conjunction with a WhisperProcessor to pre-process audio inputs into log-Mel spectrograms and post-process model outputs into text. The model is informed of the task to perform (transcription or translation) by passing context tokens to the decoder. These context tokens include a transcription token, a language token, a task token, and optionally a no-timestamp token. The context tokens can be forced or un-forced. Forced tokens allow control over the output language and task, while un-forced tokens let the model predict them automatically. Whisper can transcribe audio samples of up to 30 seconds in duration, and with a chunking algorithm, it can transcribe audio of arbitrary length. Whisper can also predict sequence-level timestamps. The pre-trained model can be fine-tuned to improve performance on specific languages and tasks, particularly with as little as 5 hours of labelled data. Whisper is primarily trained and evaluated on ASR and speech translation to English tasks, showing strong results in approximately 10 languages. It may also support additional capabilities like voice activity detection, speaker classification, or speaker diarization, but these have not been robustly evaluated. Users are advised to perform thorough evaluations before deployment. Whisper should not be used to transcribe recordings without consent or for subjective classification. It is not recommended for high-risk domains where accuracy flaws could lead to significant negative outcomes. The model is intended for transcription and translation, and its use for classification is not appropriate, especially for inferring human attributes. Whisper is trained on 680,000 hours of audio and transcripts collected from the internet, with 65% in English, 18% in non-English audio with English transcripts, and 17% in non-English audio with corresponding non-English transcripts. The non-English data represents 98 different languages. Performance in a given language is correlated with the amount of training data available for that language. Whisper models show improved robustness to accents, background noise, technical language, and zero-shot translation from multiple languages into English. However, due to weakly supervised training on noisy data, the models may generate hallucinations—texts that are not actually spoken in the audio. Performance varies across languages, with lower accuracy observed in low-resource or low-discoverability languages and across different accents and dialects. The models may also exhibit higher word error rates for speakers of different genders, races, or ages. The sequence-to-sequence architecture can lead to repetitive text generation, which can be partially mitigated with beam search and temperature scheduling. Whisper's transcription capabilities may be used to improve accessibility tools and enable near-real-time speech recognition and translation applications. However, the release of Whisper also raises dual-use concerns, as it could facilitate the development of surveillance technologies. The models may also have some capability to recognize specific individuals, which could pose safety concerns. The performance disparities of Whisper may have economic implications, particularly in the accessibility and surveillance domains.

instruction: Is there a way to make a 3D version of a toy using just some snapshots from various angles?
description: LucidFusion is a flexible end-to-end feed-forward framework designed to generate high-resolution 3D Gaussians from unposed, sparse, and arbitrary numbers of multiview images. The model introduces the Relative Coordinate Map (RCM) to align geometric features coherently across different views, eliminating the need for pose information that traditional methods rely on to link images to 3D world coordinates. This makes LucidFusion highly adaptable for 3D generation from arbitrary, unposed images. The framework seamlessly integrates with the original single-image-to-3D pipeline, producing detailed 3D Gaussians at a resolution of 512 by 512. It is suitable for a wide range of applications and operates at approximately 13 frames per second on an A800 GPU. Recent large reconstruction models have struggled with controllability due to incomplete or inconsistent 3D reconstructions from single images, but LucidFusion addresses this limitation by leveraging multiple views to improve reconstruction quality.

instruction: Can you segment multiple objects at once in this image using bounding boxes?
description: SAM 2 is a foundation model designed for promptable visual segmentation in images and videos, developed by FAIR. It supports a wide range of segmentation tasks, including automatic mask generation, image segmentation using point clicks, bounding box inputs, and multi-object segmentation. The model can process both single images and batches of images, allowing for efficient inference across multiple inputs. It also supports video segmentation and tracking, enabling the tracking of objects across video frames with the ability to refine segmentation through additional prompts. SAM 2 can use masks from previous predictions as input to improve segmentation accuracy. The model is built to handle complex scenarios, such as batched images with multiple objects and multiple points per object, as well as batched bounding boxes. It supports streaming video inference for real-time applications and can track multiple objects simultaneously in video by adding them all at once. The model is available through the Hugging Face Transformers library and can be used with the `mask-generation` pipeline for automatic mask generation. SAM 2 is based on the Hiera architecture and is optimized for performance using mixed-precision inference with bfloat16. The model has been evaluated on a variety of tasks and is capable of generating multiple mask predictions per input, ranked by quality score. It is designed to be flexible, supporting both single and multi-object segmentation, as well as refinement through additional input points or masks. The model is intended for use in applications requiring accurate and efficient segmentation of objects in both static images and video sequences.

instruction: Could you determine a probable value for this scenario?
description: The model is a RandomForestRegressor, designed for regression tasks. It is trained using a procedure that includes hyperparameters such as bootstrap set to True, ccp_alpha set to 0.0, criterion set to squared_error, max_features set to 1.0, min_samples_leaf set to 1, min_samples_split set to 2, n_estimators set to 100, oob_score set to False, and verbose set to 0. The model is evaluated using the test split method, with the root mean squared error (rmse) as the performance metric. The evaluation results show an rmse value of 0.307471. The model is unfitted, as indicated by its visual representation and associated styling. No specific intended uses, limitations, or additional details about the training procedure are provided. No information is available about the model card authors, contact channels, or citation details.

instruction: Which of these paraphrased summaries best captures the main idea of the original research paper?
description: This is a model finetuned from BERT-tiny using the Sentence Transformers library. It computes scores for pairs of texts, which can be used for semantic textual similarity, semantic search, paraphrase mining, text classification, clustering, and more. The model is a Cross Encoder with a maximum sequence length of 512 tokens and produces one output label. It is designed for the English language and is licensed under the Apache 2.0 license. The model was trained on a dataset of 578,402 samples containing questions, answers, and labels. Training was conducted using a batch size of 2048, a learning rate of 0.0005, and one training epoch with a warmup ratio of 0.1. The model was trained on an NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3090 GPU and a 13th Gen Intel Core i7-13700K CPU with 31.78 GB of RAM. The training consumed 0.019 kWh of energy and emitted 0.007 kg of CO2. The model was evaluated on several datasets, including gooaq-dev, NanoMSMARCO, NanoNFCorpus, NanoNQ, and NanoBEIR_R100_mean, achieving an ndcg@10 score of 0.6157 on gooaq-dev, 0.4772 on NanoMSMARCO, 0.3308 on NanoNFCorpus, 0.4859 on NanoNQ, and 0.4313 on NanoBEIR_R100_mean. The model can be used with the Sentence Transformers library by loading it as a CrossEncoder and running inference on pairs of texts or ranking different texts based on similarity to a single text.

instruction: What is the main focus of this product demonstration video?
description: Vamba is a hybrid Mamba-Transformer model designed for efficient hour-long video understanding. It leverages cross-attention layers and Mamba-2 blocks to process text and video tokens differently. The main computation overhead in transformer-based large multimodal models (LMMs) comes from the quadratic complexity of self-attention in video tokens. To address this, Vamba splits the self-attention operation over the entire video and text token sequence into two more efficient components. Since video tokens typically dominate the sequence while text tokens remain few, Vamba maintains the self-attention mechanism exclusively for the text tokens and eliminates it for the video tokens. Instead, it adds cross-attention layers that use text tokens as queries and video tokens as keys and values. In addition, Vamba employs Mamba blocks to effectively process the video tokens. The model is intended to support tasks involving video and image understanding, including describing the content of videos and images. The model is named Vamba-Qwen2-VL-7B.

instruction: What kind of fruit is shown in this picture?
description: The Vision Transformer (ViT) is a transformer encoder model (BERT-like) pre-trained on a large collection of images in a supervised fashion, specifically on ImageNet-21k, a dataset comprising 14 million images and 21,843 classes, at a resolution of 224x224 pixels. The model was then fine-tuned on ImageNet 2012 (also referred to as ILSVRC2012), a dataset consisting of 1 million images and 1,000 classes, also at resolution 224x224. Images are presented to the model as a sequence of fixed-size patches (resolution 16x16), which are linearly embedded. A [CLS] token is added to the beginning of the sequence to enable classification tasks. Absolute position embeddings are also added before feeding the sequence to the layers of the Transformer encoder. By pre-training the model, it learns an inner representation of images that can be used to extract features useful for downstream tasks. For example, if a dataset of labeled images is available, a standard classifier can be trained by placing a linear layer on top of the pre-trained encoder. Typically, a linear layer is placed on top of the [CLS] token, as the last hidden state of this token can be seen as a representation of the entire image. The model is intended for use in image classification tasks. The ViT model was pre-trained on ImageNet-21k and fine-tuned on ImageNet 2012. During training, images were resized and normalized to a resolution of 224x224 with mean (0.5, 0.5, 0.5) and standard deviation (0.5, 0.5, 0.5) across the RGB channels. The model was trained on TPUv3 hardware (8 cores) with a batch size of 4096 and a learning rate warmup of 10,000 steps. Gradient clipping at a global norm of 1 was applied for ImageNet. Evaluation results on several image classification benchmarks are detailed in the original paper. The best results for fine-tuning are obtained with a higher resolution of 384x384, and increasing the model size generally results in better performance. The model was introduced in a paper by Dosovitskiy et al. and the weights were converted from JAX to PyTorch by Ross Wightman. The model card was written by the Hugging Face team, as the original team did not provide one.

instruction: I need to process a video for license plate tracking—can you detect the plate in this frame?
description: This is a fine-tuned version of YOLOv11 (n, s, m, l, x) specialized for License Plate Detection, trained using a public dataset from Roboflow Universe. The model is designed for use in Smart Parking Systems, Tollgate or Access Control Automation, Traffic Surveillance and Enforcement, and ALPR with OCR Integration. Training was conducted for 300 epochs with an input size of 640x640, using the SGD optimizer on an NVIDIA A100 device. The data format is YOLOv5-compatible, consisting of images and labels in text files. Evaluation metrics for the YOLOv11x variant include a precision of 0.9893, recall of 0.9508, mAP@50 of 0.9813, and mAP@50-95 of 0.7260. Model variants are available in PyTorch (.pt) for use with Ultralytics CLI and Python API, and in ONNX (.onnx) for cross-platform inference. The model is licensed under AGPLv3 due to license inheritance from YOLOv11. The dataset is licensed under CC BY 4.0 by Roboflow Universe. In accordance with the AGPLv3 license, if the model is used in a service or project, the code that uses it must be open-sourced. Proper attribution must be given to Roboflow, Ultralytics, and MorseTechLab when using or deploying the model.

instruction: Convert the audio from this meeting recording into a clean, written summary in English.
description: Whisper is a pre-trained model for automatic speech recognition (ASR) and speech translation. Trained on 680,000 hours of labelled speech data using large-scale weak supervision, Whisper models demonstrate strong generalization capabilities across many datasets and domains without the need for fine-tuning. Whisper is a Transformer-based encoder-decoder model, also referred to as a sequence-to-sequence model. The models were trained on either English-only data or multilingual data. English-only models were trained for speech recognition, while multilingual models were trained for both speech recognition and speech translation. For speech recognition, the model predicts transcriptions in the same language as the audio. For speech translation, the model predicts transcriptions in a different language from the audio. Whisper checkpoints come in five configurations of varying model sizes. The smallest four are trained on either English-only or multilingual data, while the largest checkpoints are multilingual only. The model is used in conjunction with a WhisperProcessor to pre-process audio inputs into log-Mel spectrograms and post-process model outputs into text. The model is informed of the task to perform (transcription or translation) by passing context tokens to the decoder. These context tokens include a transcription token, a language token, a task token, and optionally a no-timestamp token. Context tokens can be forced or un-forced. Forced tokens allow control over the output language and task, while un-forced tokens let the model predict them automatically. Whisper supports English to English transcription, French to French transcription, and French to English translation. The model can be evaluated using datasets like LibriSpeech, and it has a word error rate of approximately 2.9% on the LibriSpeech test clean dataset. Whisper is designed to work on audio samples up to 30 seconds in duration but can transcribe longer audio using a chunking algorithm. The model can also predict sequence-level timestamps. While the pre-trained model generalizes well, its performance can be improved through fine-tuning, especially for certain languages and tasks. Whisper is primarily trained and evaluated on ASR and speech translation to English tasks and shows strong results in approximately 10 languages. It may also support additional capabilities like voice activity detection, speaker classification, or speaker diarization, but these have not been robustly evaluated. Users are cautioned against using Whisper to transcribe recordings without consent or for subjective classification. The model is not appropriate for high-risk domains where accuracy flaws could lead to significant negative outcomes. Whisper is trained on 680,000 hours of audio data from the internet, with 65% in English, 18% in non-English audio with English transcripts, and 17% in non-English audio with corresponding non-English transcripts. The non-English data represents 98 different languages. Performance in a given language is correlated with the amount of training data available for that language. Whisper exhibits improved robustness to accents, background noise, technical language, and zero-shot translation from multiple languages into English. However, the model may hallucinate text not present in the audio input due to its language prediction capabilities. Performance varies across languages, with lower accuracy observed in low-resource or low-discoverability languages and across different accents and dialects. The model may also generate repetitive text, which can be partially mitigated with beam search and temperature scheduling. Whisper's transcription capabilities may be used to improve accessibility tools and enable near-real-time speech recognition and translation. However, the release of Whisper also raises dual-use concerns, as it could facilitate the development of surveillance technologies. The model may also have some capability to recognize specific individuals, which could present safety concerns. The broader implications of Whisper include potential economic impacts from disparate model performance and the affordability of large-scale audio transcription and translation.

instruction: Can the Llama 4 models be used for tasks like image captioning and visual reasoning?
description: The Llama 4 collection of models are natively multimodal AI models that enable text and multimodal experiences. These models leverage a mixture-of-experts architecture to offer industry-leading performance in text and image understanding. These Llama 4 models mark the beginning of a new era for the Llama ecosystem. We are launching two efficient models in the Llama 4 series, Llama 4 Scout, a 17 billion parameter model with 16 experts, and Llama 4 Maverick, a 17 billion parameter model with 128 experts. The Llama 4 models are auto-regressive language models that use a mixture-of-experts (MoE) architecture and incorporate early fusion for native multimodality. Llama 4 Scout was pretrained on ~40 trillion tokens and Llama 4 Maverick was pretrained on ~22 trillion tokens of multimodal data from a mix of publicly available, licensed data and information from Meta’s products and services. This includes publicly shared posts from Instagram and Facebook and people’s interactions with Meta AI. The pretraining data has a cutoff of August 2024. Llama 4 Scout has a context length of 10 million tokens and Llama 4 Maverick has a context length of 1 million tokens. Llama 4 Scout has a total of 109 billion parameters and Llama 4 Maverick has a total of 400 billion parameters. Llama 4 is intended for commercial and research use in multiple languages. Instruction tuned models are intended for assistant-like chat and visual reasoning tasks, whereas pretrained models can be adapted for natural language generation. For vision, Llama 4 models are also optimized for visual recognition, image reasoning, captioning, and answering general questions about an image. The Llama 4 model collection also supports the ability to leverage the outputs of its models to improve other models including synthetic data generation and distillation. Llama 4 has been trained on a broader collection of languages than the 12 supported languages (pre-training includes ). Developers may fine-tune Llama 4 models for languages beyond the 12 supported languages provided they comply with the Llama 4 Community License and the Acceptable Use Policy. Developers are responsible for ensuring that their use of Llama 4 in additional languages is done in a safe and responsible manner. Llama 4 has been tested for image understanding up to 5 input images. If leveraging additional image understanding capabilities beyond this, Developers are responsible for ensuring that their deployments are mitigated for risks and should perform additional testing and tuning tailored to their specific applications. Llama 4 was developed following the best practices outlined in our . The Llama 4 models are released under a custom commercial license, the Llama 4 Community License Agreement. The Llama 4 models are static models trained on an offline dataset. Future versions of the tuned models may be released as we improve model behavior with community feedback. Llama 4 Scout and Llama 4 Maverick were trained using custom training libraries, Meta's custom built GPU clusters, and production infrastructure. Model pre-training utilized a cumulative of 7.38 million GPU hours of computation on H100-80GB (TDP of 700W) type hardware. Training time is the total GPU time required for training each model and power consumption is the peak power capacity per GPU device used, adjusted for power usage efficiency. Estimated total location-based greenhouse gas emissions were 1,999 tons CO2eq for training. Since 2020, Meta has maintained net zero greenhouse gas emissions in its global operations and matched 100% of its electricity use with clean and renewable energy; therefore, the total market-based greenhouse gas emissions for training were 0 tons CO2eq. Llama 4 Scout has training time of 5 million GPU hours and training location-based greenhouse gas emissions of 1,354 tons CO2eq. Llama 4 Maverick has training time of 2.38 million GPU hours and training location-based greenhouse gas emissions of 645 tons CO2eq. The methodology used to determine training energy use and greenhouse gas emissions can be found . Since Meta is openly releasing these models, the training energy use and greenhouse gas emissions will not be incurred by others. Llama 4 Scout and Llama 4 Maverick are available for use with the transformers library version 4.51.0 or higher. The Llama 4 models are designed to be deployed as part of an overall AI system with additional guardrails as required. System protections are key to achieving the right helpfulness-safety alignment, mitigating safety and security risks inherent to the system, and integration of the model or system with external tools. We provide the community with system level tools like Llama Guard, Prompt Guard and Code Shield that developers should deploy with Llama models or other LLMs. All of our demos contain these safeguards by default so developers can benefit from system-level safety out-of-the-box. Llama 4 is a new technology, and like any new technology, there are risks associated with its use. Testing conducted to date has not covered, nor could it cover, all scenarios. For these reasons, as with all LLMs, Llama 4’s potential outputs cannot be predicted in advance, and the model may in some instances produce inaccurate or other objectionable responses to user prompts. Therefore, before deploying any applications of Llama 4 models, developers should perform safety testing and tuning tailored to their specific applications of the model. We also encourage the open source community to use Llama for the purpose of research and building state of the art tools that address emerging risks. Please refer to available resources including our Developer Use Guide: AI Protections, solutions, and other to learn more.

instruction: Is there a way to convert this video into a set of visual descriptors?
description: The VideoMAE-v2 (Large-sized model, Pretrained on UnlabeledHybrid-1M) is a model pre-trained for 800 epochs in a self-supervised manner on the UnlabeldHybrid-1M dataset. It was introduced in the paper 'VideoMAE V2: Scaling Video Masked Autoencoders with Dual Masking' by Wang et al. and first released in 2023. The model is intended for video feature extraction. It can be used by loading the model and processor from the 'transformers' library, processing video inputs into tensors, and extracting features through the model. The model is based on the VideoMAE architecture and incorporates dual masking for training. The model is available under the identifier 'OpenGVLab/VideoMAEv2-Large'.

instruction: How does the frequency of outpatient visits impact the likelihood of a diabetic patient returning to the hospital?
description: This model predicts 30-day hospital readmissions for diabetic patients using historical patient data and machine learning techniques. The model aims to identify high-risk individuals, enabling targeted interventions and improved healthcare resource allocation. It is a binary classification model implemented using the Autogluon framework in Python, and it is licensed under the MIT license. The model achieved a test accuracy of 0.8865 and a test ROC-AUC of 0.6467. Key features include patient demographics, hospital stay metrics, medication metrics, service utilization, diagnostic information, and engineered features such as interaction terms, resource utilization ratios, and diagnostic density metrics. The model is intended for use by healthcare professionals to assess the risk of 30-day readmission for diabetic patients, supporting resource allocation, intervention planning, and care management decision-making. It is not intended for use with non-diabetic patient populations, for predicting readmissions beyond 30 days, or as the sole determinant for patient care decisions. The model was trained on a dataset from 1999 to 2008 from the UCI ML Repository, which includes over 100,000 hospital admissions and 50+ features. The training process involved data preprocessing steps such as missing value imputation, outlier handling, feature scaling, categorical encoding, and log transformation. Feature engineering included the creation of interaction terms, resource utilization ratios, medication usage metrics, time-based interaction features, and diagnostic density metrics. The model training process used a 70% training, 15% validation, and 15% test data split, with cross-validation for model selection, hyperparameter optimization via grid search, and early stopping to prevent overfitting. The model's performance is based on ROC-AUC. Known limitations include dependence on data quality and completeness, limited generalizability beyond the training data timeframe and healthcare systems, and the need for standardized input data. Potential biases may include demographic biases present in the training data, performance variation across hospital systems, influence from regional healthcare practices, and temporal biases due to historical data. Recommendations for use include regular model monitoring and retraining, careful validation in new deployment contexts, performance assessment across demographic groups, and integration with existing clinical workflows. Monitoring requirements include tracking prediction accuracy across patient groups, monitoring input data distribution shifts, assessing feature importance stability, and evaluating performance metrics over time. Maintenance includes quarterly performance reviews, annual retraining with updated data, regular bias assessments, and ongoing validation against current practices.

instruction: What is the difference in price between the most expensive and cheapest item listed in the table?
description: This model is a TAPAS large model fine-tuned on WikiSQL in a supervised fashion. It has two versions: the default version corresponds to the `tapas_wikisql_sqa_inter_masklm_large_reset` checkpoint, and the other version is `no_reset`, corresponding to `tapas_wikisql_sqa_inter_masklm_large`. The model was pre-trained on masked language modeling (MLM) and an additional step called intermediate pre-training, followed by fine-tuning on SQA and WikiSQL. It uses relative position embeddings, resetting the position index at every cell of the table. The non-default version uses absolute position embeddings. TAPAS is a BERT-like transformer model pre-trained on a large corpus of English data from Wikipedia in a self-supervised fashion. It was pre-trained on raw tables and associated texts without human labeling, using an automatic process to generate inputs and labels. The pre-training objectives include masked language modeling, where 15% of the words in a flattened table and associated context are randomly masked, and the model must predict the masked words, and intermediate pre-training, where the model must classify whether a sentence is supported or refuted by the contents of a table. The model learns an inner representation of the English language used in tables and associated texts, which can be used for downstream tasks such as answering questions about a table or determining whether a sentence is entailed or refuted by the contents of a table. Fine-tuning involves adding a cell selection head and aggregation head on top of the pre-trained model and jointly training these heads with the base model on SQA and WikiSQL. The model is intended for answering questions related to a table. The texts are lowercased and tokenized using WordPiece with a vocabulary size of 30,000. The inputs are of the form [CLS] Question [SEP] Flattened table [SEP]. The WikiSQL dataset was first converted into the format of SQA using automatic conversion scripts. The model was fine-tuned on 32 Cloud TPU v3 cores for 50,000 steps with a maximum sequence length of 512 and a batch size of 512. Fine-tuning takes around 10 hours. The optimizer used is Adam with a learning rate of 6.17164e-5 and a warmup ratio of 0.1424. The team releasing TAPAS did not write a model card for this model; this model card was written by the Hugging Face team and contributors.
domain: Natural Language Processing Table Question Answering

instruction: Could you identify the section that provides the results of the recent survey?
description: This model is a fine-tuned version of sberbank-ai/ruBert-base designed for the task of document question answering. It has been adapted specifically for extracting answers from a provided document context and fine-tuned on a custom JSON dataset containing context, question, and answer triples. The model was fine-tuned using the Transformers library with a custom training pipeline. A loader reads a JSON file containing context, question, and answer triples. The script tokenizes the document and question with a sliding window approach to handle long texts. The training process leverages mixed precision training and the AdamW optimizer to improve optimization. The training script evaluates model performance on a validation set, saves checkpoints, and employs early stopping based on validation loss. The model is ideal for interactive document question answering tasks, making it a powerful tool for applications such as customer support, document search, and automated Q&A systems. The model was trained for 20 epochs with a batch size of 4 per device, using 0.1 of total steps for warmup. FP16 training was enabled if CUDA was available. Training was performed on an 1xRTX 3070. While primarily focused on Russian texts, the model also supports English language inputs. The model also supports English language, but its support was not tested.

instruction: What biomedical image is most similar to the text: 'A histological section of liver tissue showing cirrhosis'?
description: BiomedCLIP-PubMedBERT_256-vit_base_patch16_224 is a biomedical vision-language foundation model pretrained on a dataset of 15 million figure-caption pairs extracted from biomedical research articles in PubMed Central, using contrastive learning. It uses PubMedBERT as the text encoder and Vision Transformer as the image encoder, with domain-specific adaptations. The model can perform various vision-language processing tasks such as cross-modal retrieval, image classification, and visual question answering. BiomedCLIP establishes new state of the art in a wide range of standard datasets and substantially outperforms prior vision-language processing approaches. The model is based on the PMC-15M dataset, a large-scale parallel image-text dataset generated from the BiomedCLIP Data Pipeline, which automatically downloads and processes articles from the PubMed Central Open Access dataset. The dataset includes a diverse range of biomedical image types such as microscopy, radiography, and histology. The model is intended for future research on visual-language processing and for reproducibility of the experimental results reported in the reference paper. Its primary intended use is to support AI researchers building on this work, particularly in the radiology domain. Any deployed use case of the model, whether commercial or otherwise, is currently out of scope. The model was developed using English corpora and is considered English-only.

instruction: Can you provide a numerical description of this image suitable for clustering or comparison?
description: This model is a PyTorch port of the original JAX implementation of ResNet-10, which is a 4-stage ResNet with basic blocks. The conversion maintains the original model's architecture and weights while making it accessible to PyTorch users. The original model is from the HIL-SERL paper and is useful for robotics tasks due to its small size. The model has approximately 4.9 million parameters, with hidden sizes of [64, 128, 256, 512]. It accepts 3-channel RGB images with a resolution of 128x128 as input and produces an embedding size of 64. The conversion was performed using an automated JAX to PyTorch pipeline, ensuring weight preservation, architecture matching, and numerical stability.

instruction: Generate a motion sequence of a forest at dawn, capturing the sharp edges of the trees and the soft glow of the morning light.
description: This model is a dilated controlnet module designed for the Wan2.1 model. The dilated controlnet includes fewer basic blocks and incorporates a `stride` parameter. For the Wan14B model, the number of controlnet blocks is 6 and the stride is set to 4. The model is intended to support tasks involving video generation with control over visual features, particularly using the canny edge detection method. It is used in conjunction with the Wan2.1-T2V-14B-Diffusers base model. The model allows for inference through command-line interface or Gradio-based web interface, with options to specify parameters such as controlnet weight, guidance start and end, number of inference steps, guidance scale, video resolution, number of frames, negative prompts, seed, and output frame rate. The model is provided with example inference commands and is associated with a GitHub repository for code and issue tracking. The model is authored by Karachev Denis and is available on GitHub.

instruction: I have a voice memo that's hard to hear—can you enhance it so the words come through more clearly?
description: This model is a ResNet-like system designed for speech enhancement and robust automatic speech recognition (ASR) training within the SpeechBrain framework. The model is trained using a three-step mimic loss training system: first, a perceptual model is pre-trained on clean speech features; second, an enhancement model is trained with mimic loss using the pre-trained perceptual model; and third, a large ASR model pre-trained on LibriSpeech is fine-tuned using the enhancement front-end. The enhancement and ASR models can be used together or independently. The system is trained with recordings sampled at 16kHz (single channel) and includes functionality for automatic audio normalization, including resampling and mono channel selection, when using the enhance_file method. The model was trained with SpeechBrain version 150e1890 and is compatible with SpeechBrain v0.5.12. Performance metrics for the model include a Test PESQ of 3.05, Test COVL of 3.74, Valid WER of 2.89, and Test WER of 2.80. The SpeechBrain team does not provide any warranty on the performance of this model when used on datasets other than those it was trained on.

instruction: Generate a high-resolution image of a desert oasis with natural lighting and textures.
description: This model, referred to as MAR, presents a new method for autoregressive image generation that does not require vector quantization. Rather than using discrete tokens, the model functions within a continuous-valued space, employing a diffusion process to represent the per-token probability distribution. The model utilizes a Diffusion Loss function to enable efficient and high-quality image generation while leveraging the speed benefits of autoregressive sequence modeling. This method streamlines the generation process, making it suitable for a wider range of continuous-valued domains beyond image synthesis.
