The model can be loaded using the Hugging Face DiffusionPipeline, with options to customize parameters such as the model type, number of steps, and class labels. Three pre-trained MAR models are available in safetensors format: mar-base.safetensors, mar-large.safetensors, and mar-huge.safetensors. The model is based on the paper titled 'Autoregressive Image Generation without Vector Quantization' by Li, Tianhong and Tian, Yonglong and Li, He and Deng, Mingyang and He, Kaiming, published as an arXiv preprint arXiv:2406.11838 in 2024. The implementation is a Hugging Face Diffusers/GPU version of the paper, with the official PyTorch implementation also available. The developers acknowledge Congyue Deng and Xinlei Chen for helpful discussions, and Google TPU Research Cloud (TRC) for providing access to TPUs, and Google Cloud Platform for supporting GPU resources. A significant portion of the code in this repository is based on other sources.

instruction: I want to study the kinematics of this movement—can you provide the detailed pose data?
description: The SynthPose model was proposed by Yoni Gozlan, Antoine Falisse, Scott Uhlrich, Anthony Gatti, Michael Black, and Akshay Chaudhari. This model uses a VitPose Huge backbone and is a new approach that enables fine-tuning of pre-trained 2D human pose models to predict an arbitrarily denser set of keypoints for accurate kinematic analysis through the use of synthetic data. This particular variant was fine-tuned on a set of keypoints usually found on motion capture setups, and includes COCO keypoints as well. The model predicts 52 markers, where the first 17 are the COCO keypoints and the next 35 are anatomical markers. The model is designed to support tasks related to human pose estimation, including the detection of human figures in images and the prediction of detailed anatomical keypoints for each detected person. The model is intended for use in applications requiring precise kinematic analysis, such as biomechanics or motion capture. The model's architecture is based on the VitPose Huge variant, and it is fine-tuned to enhance its ability to predict a larger and more detailed set of keypoints than standard models. The model is evaluated for its ability to accurately predict these additional keypoints, which are typically not included in standard pose estimation models. The model includes explicit caveats regarding its intended use and the necessity of using appropriate detection models in conjunction with it for accurate results.

instruction: Can you show me a clip of a sunrise from a beach with waves crashing in the background?
description: CLIP4Clip/WebVid-150k is a video-text retrieval model trained on a subset of the WebVid dataset. The model and training method are described in the paper by Lou et al. and implemented in the accompanying materials. The training process utilized a dataset of short videos with corresponding textual descriptions sourced from the web, specifically the first 150,000 video-text pairs. This model is based on the CLIP architecture, with weights trained by Daphna Idelson. The model is intended for use in large-scale video-text retrieval applications. An interactive demonstration is available, showcasing the model's ability to retrieve videos based on text queries from a collection of approximately 1.5 million videos. The motivation behind this work is to leverage the CLIP image-language pre-training model and apply it to learning visual-temporal concepts from videos, thereby improving video-based searches. The WebVid dataset's large-scale and diverse nature enhanced the model's capabilities beyond those described in the original paper. To evaluate the model's performance, the last 10,000 video clips and their accompanying text from the Webvid dataset were used. Evaluation metrics include R1, R5, R10, MedianR, and MeanR. The CLIP4Clip model trained on 150k Webvid achieved R1 of 50.74, R5 of 77.30, R10 of 85.05, MedianR of 1.0, and MeanR of 14.9535. A binarized version of the model, further fine-tuned on 100 top searches for search acceleration and efficiency, achieved R1 of 50.56, R5 of 76.39, R10 of 83.51, MedianR of 1.0, and MeanR of 43.2964. Diana Mazenko is acknowledged for adapting and loading the model to Hugging Face and for creating a Hugging Face demo for a large-scale video-search application. Acknowledgments are also given to Lou et al. for their comprehensive work on CLIP4Clip and for making their code openly available.

instruction: Can you explain how to calculate the area of a triangle using the Pythagorean theorem?
description: Qwen2.5 is the latest series of Qwen large language models. The Qwen2.5 series includes a range of base language models and instruction-tuned language models with parameter sizes ranging from 0.5 to 72 billion. This model introduces several improvements over Qwen2, including significantly more knowledge and enhanced capabilities in coding and mathematics, achieved through specialized expert models in these domains. It also shows significant improvements in instruction following, generating long texts (over 8K tokens), understanding structured data such as tables, and generating structured outputs, especially JSON. The model is more resilient to the diversity of system prompts, which enhances role-play implementation and condition-setting for chatbots. It supports long-context processing up to 128K tokens and can generate up to 8K tokens. The model provides multilingual support for over 29 languages, including Chinese, English, French, Spanish, Portuguese, German, Italian, Russian, Japanese, Korean, Vietnamese, Thai, and Arabic. This specific model is the instruction-tuned 0.5B Qwen2.5 model. It is a causal language model that has undergone pretraining and post-training. The architecture includes transformers with RoPE, SwiGLU, RMSNorm, attention QKV bias, and tied word embeddings. It has 0.49B total parameters and 0.36B non-embedding parameters. The model consists of 24 layers and uses 14 attention heads for queries and 2 for keys and values. It supports a full context length of 32,768 tokens and can generate up to 8,192 tokens. The code for Qwen2.5 is available in the latest Hugging Face `transformers` library, and it is recommended to use the latest version to avoid errors. Earlier versions of `transformers` (before 4.37.0) may result in a KeyError: 'qwen2'. Detailed evaluation results are available in a separate document, and information on GPU memory requirements and throughput is also provided. If you find this work helpful, you may cite the associated publications.

instruction: I want to understand a French podcast in English as it plays. Can this be done with voice transfer?
description: Hibiki is a model for streaming speech translation, also known as simultaneous translation. It is designed to adapt its flow to accumulate just enough context to produce a correct translation in real-time, chunk by chunk. As the user speaks, Hibiki generates natural speech in the target language, optionally with voice transfer, along with a text translation. Hibiki currently only supports French-to-English translation. The model is referred to as Hibiki in the documentation and has 2.7B parameters. It is a hierarchical Transformer that produces speech and text tokens at a framerate of 12.5Hz, with audio being generated at a 2.2kbps bitrate. Hibiki is a decoder-only model for simultaneous speech translation. It leverages the multistream architecture to model source and target speech jointly, allowing it to continuously process the input stream while generating the target speech. Hibiki produces text and audio tokens at a constant framerate of 12.5Hz, enabling a continuous output audio stream along with timestamped text translation. It relies on simple temperature sampling, making it compatible with batching, unlike models that use complex inference policies. The fidelity of Hibiki's voice transfer can be controlled by changing the coefficient of the Classifier-Free Guidance. A larger coefficient increases voice similarity, but excessive coefficients can lead to worse translations. Hibiki is developed by Kyutai. It is a simultaneous speech-to-speech and speech-to-text translation model. It supports French-to-English translation. It is licensed under CC-BY. The model can be used for streaming translation from French to English in real-time settings or for batched simultaneous translation of many input sequences. It is robust to noisy conditions and is trained on sequences up to 120 seconds. Some components of the model can be used independently or repurposed relatively easily. For example, the Mimi codec is a state-of-the-art audio neural codec that combines semantic and acoustic information into audio tokens running at 12Hz and a bitrate of 1.1kbps, making it particularly adapted to train speech language models or text-to-speech systems. Supporting other pairs of languages would require finetuning. The model is not intended to be used to impersonate other people or for any malicious use. The underlying model is trained on a mix of data including Wikipedia, Stack Exchange, open-access scientific articles (from peS2o), and Common Crawl. The unsupervised audio dataset used for pre-training is a collection of 7M hours of readily available audio content in English and 450k hours in French, following the preprocessing and recipe of the referenced source. The synthetic translation dataset consists of around 40k hours of parallel French-English data synthesized with contextual alignment, with various levels of speaker similarity. Translation finetuning is based on a 900-hour mixture of a resynthesized version of the referenced dataset and synthetic long-form utterances. The training procedure and hyper-parameters are detailed in the paper. The final model was trained on 48 H100 Nvidia GPUs.

instruction: Fill in the blank space in this image with a realistic depiction of a forest path.
description: ControlNet v1.1 is a neural network structure designed to control diffusion models by adding extra conditions. This specific checkpoint is conditioned on inpaint images and is intended to be used in combination with Stable Diffusion models. The model was developed by Lvmin Zhang and Maneesh Agrawala and is based on a diffusion-based text-to-image generation approach. It supports English language inputs and is licensed under a framework adapted from responsible AI licensing practices. The model allows for task-specific conditions to be learned in an end-to-end manner, and it is robust even with small training datasets. The model can be used to generate images based on conditions such as inpainting, where a masked region of an image is filled in according to a given prompt. The authors have released 14 different checkpoints, each trained to support a different type of conditioning, including canny edge detection, pixel-to-pixel instruction, multi-level line segment detection, depth estimation, surface normal estimation, image segmentation, line art generation, anime line art generation, human pose estimation, scribble-based image generation, soft edge image generation, image shuffling, and image tiling. Each checkpoint is trained to interpret a specific type of control image, such as monochrome edge images, line segments, depth maps, or segmented regions, to guide the image generation process. The model is implemented using the diffusers format and can be integrated with the Stable Diffusion pipeline for image generation tasks. The model is described in the paper titled 'Adding Conditional Control to Text-to-Image Diffusion Models' by Lvmin Zhang and Maneesh Agrawala, available on arXiv with the identifier 2302.05543.

instruction: Where are the pedestrians walking in this sidewalk image?
description: The D-FINE model was proposed by Yansong Peng, Hebei Li, Peixi Wu, Yueyi Zhang, Xiaoyan Sun, and Feng Wu. This is the Hugging Face Transformers implementation for D-FINE. D-FINE is a real-time object detector that achieves outstanding localization precision by redefining the bounding box regression task in DETR models. The model comprises two key components: Fine-grained Distribution Refinement (FDR) and Global Optimal Localization Self-Distillation (GO-LSD). D-FINE is trained on COCO (Lin et al. [2014]) train2017 and validated on COCO val2017 dataset. The standard AP metrics are reported, averaged over uniformly sampled IoU thresholds ranging from 0.50 to 0.95 with a step size of 0.05, as well as APval5000, which is commonly used in real scenarios. D-FINE is ideal for real-time object detection in diverse applications such as autonomous driving, surveillance systems, robotics, and retail analytics. Its enhanced flexibility and deployment-friendly design make it suitable for both edge devices and large-scale systems, ensuring high accuracy and speed in dynamic, real-world environments.

instruction: Can you provide a segmentation result that clearly highlights the main object?
description: BiRefNet is a model designed for high-resolution dichotomous image segmentation. It was trained with images in 2048x2048 resolution to support higher resolution inference. The model is intended for tasks such as standard dichotomous image segmentation (DIS), high-resolution salient object detection (HRSOD), and contrastive object detection (COD). It has achieved state-of-the-art performance on these tasks. The model was trained on the DIS-TR dataset and validated on DIS-TEs and DIS-VD. Performance metrics include maxFm, wFmeasure, MAE, Smeasure, meanEm, HCE, maxEm, meanFm, adpEm, adpFm, mBA, maxBIoU, and meanBIoU. Evaluation results show that at 2048x2048 resolution, the model achieves a maxFm of 0.925, wFmeasure of 0.894, MAE of 0.026, Smeasure of 0.927, meanEm of 0.952, HCE of 811, maxEm of 0.960, meanFm of 0.909, adpEm of 0.944, adpFm of 0.888, mBA of 0.828, maxBIoU of 0.837, and meanBIoU of 0.817. At 1024x1024 resolution, the model achieves a maxFm of 0.876, wFmeasure of 0.840, MAE of 0.041, Smeasure of 0.893, meanEm of 0.913, HCE of 1348, maxEm of 0.926, meanFm of 0.860, adpEm of 0.930, adpFm of 0.857, mBA of 0.765, maxBIoU of 0.769, and meanBIoU of 0.742. The model is implemented using PyTorch and can be loaded using the HuggingFace Transformers library or from local GitHub repositories. It supports inference with different resolutions and includes functions for visualization and deployment. The model is described in the paper 'Bilateral Reference for High-Resolution Dichotomous Image Segmentation' by Peng Zheng, Dehong Gao, Deng-Ping Fan, Li Liu, Jorma Laaksonen, Wanli Ouyang, and Nicu Sebe, published in CAAI Artificial Intelligence Research in 2024. The model weights and codes are available on GitHub, and online demos for inference are provided. The model was trained using GPU resources generously supported by @freepik.

instruction: I need a quick estimate of energy consumption for different building types—can you analyze this small dataset?
description: TabPFN is a transformer-based foundation model for tabular data that leverages prior-data based learning to achieve strong performance on small tabular regression tasks without requiring task-specific training. It is developed by Prior Labs and is designed to provide accurate predictions on small datasets. The model is not intended for use with very large datasets or non-tabular data formats. It requires Python version 3.9 or higher, PyTorch version 2.1 or higher, and scikit-learn version 1.0 or higher. A minimum of 16GB of RAM is recommended, with CPU support and optional GPU support. The model was published in a paper titled 'Accurate predictions on small data with a tabular foundation model' in the journal Nature in January 2025. The research team includes Noah Hollmann, Samuel Müller, Lennart Purucker, Arjun Krishnakumar, Max Körfer, Shi Bin Hoo, Robin Tibor Schirrmeister, Frank Hutter, Eddie Bergman, and Léo Grinsztajn.

instruction: Can you translate a Portuguese speech into Mandarin text?
description: Granite-speech-3.3-8b is a compact and efficient speech-language model specifically designed for automatic speech recognition (ASR) and automatic speech translation (AST). It uses a two-pass design, where the first pass transcribes audio files into text, and the second pass processes the transcribed text using the underlying Granite language model. Each step must be explicitly initiated by the user. The model was trained on a collection of public corpora comprising diverse datasets for ASR and AST, as well as synthetic datasets tailored to support the speech translation task. It was trained by modality aligning granite-3.3-8b-instruct to speech on publicly available open source corpora containing audio inputs and text targets. Compared to revision 3.3.1, revision 3.3.2 supports multilingual speech inputs in English, French, German, Spanish, and Portuguese and provides additional accuracy improvements for English ASR. Compared to the initial release, revision 3.3.2 is also trained on additional data and uses a deeper acoustic encoder for improved transcription accuracy. The model was evaluated alongside other speech-language models in the less than 8b parameter range as well as dedicated ASR and AST systems on standard benchmarks. The evaluation spanned multiple public benchmarks, with particular emphasis on English ASR tasks while also including multilingual ASR and AST for X-En and En-X translations. The model is intended to be used in enterprise applications that involve processing of speech inputs. It is well-suited for English, French, German, Spanish, and Portuguese speech-to-text and speech translations to and from English for the same languages plus English-to-Japanese and English-to-Mandarin. The model can also be used for tasks that involve text-only input since it calls the underlying granite-3.3-8b-instruct when the user specifies a prompt that does not contain audio. The architecture of granite-speech-3.3-8b revision 3.3.2 consists of a speech encoder with 16 conformer blocks trained with CTC on character-level targets, a speech projector and temporal downsampler using a 2-layer window query transformer, a large language model (granite-3.3-8b-instruct with 128k context length), and LoRA adapters with rank=64 applied to the query and value projection matrices. The model was trained on a combination of publicly available datasets and synthetic data created from publicly available datasets specifically targeting the speech translation task. The training data includes datasets such as CommonVoice-17, MLS, Librispeech, VoxPopuli, AMI, YODAS, Earnings-22, Switchboard, CallHome, Fisher, and Voicemail, among others. The model was trained using IBM's super computing cluster, Blue Vela, equipped with NVIDIA H100 GPUs, and the training was completed in 13 days on 32 H100 GPUs. The use of large speech and language models can trigger certain risks and ethical considerations. Although alignment processes include safety considerations, the model may in some cases produce inaccurate, biased, offensive, or unwanted responses to user prompts. Smaller models may exhibit increased susceptibility to hallucination in generation scenarios due to their reduced sizes, which could limit their ability to generate coherent and contextually accurate responses. This aspect is an active area of research. IBM recommends using this model for ASR and translation tasks. The model's modular design improves safety by limiting how audio inputs can influence the system. If an unfamiliar or malformed prompt is received, the model simply echoes it with its transcription, minimizing the risk of adversarial inputs. More general speech tasks may pose higher inherent risks of triggering unwanted outputs. To enhance safety, IBM recommends using granite-speech-3.3-8b alongside Granite Guardian, a fine-tuned instruct model designed to detect and flag risks in prompts and responses across key dimensions outlined in the IBM AI Risk Atlas.

instruction: Can you generate a detailed 3D structure of a protein based on the provided molecular data?
description: Uni-3DAR is an autoregressive model designed to unify various 3D tasks. It is capable of handling multiple 3D data types, including microscopic structures such as molecules, proteins, and crystals, as well as macroscopic 3D structures. The model supports a wide range of tasks, particularly those involving both generation and understanding of 3D data. Uni-3DAR achieves high efficiency by using octree compression in combination with a proposed 2-level subtree compression, which allows the full 3D space to be represented using only hundreds of tokens, as opposed to tens of thousands in a full-size grid. This efficiency is reflected in its faster inference speed compared to diffusion-based models. Additionally, the model maintains structural details by tokenizing fine-grained 3D patches, which contributes to its high accuracy and substantially better generation quality than previous diffusion-based models. The core model and a QM9 training and inference pipeline have been released. To reproduce results on the QM9 dataset, users can download a pretrained checkpoint and dataset archive, and follow provided instructions for inference or training from scratch. The model is associated with a Docker image for convenience. Users are encouraged to cite the corresponding paper if they use the data, code, or model.

instruction: How can I quickly retrieve the paragraph where the research methodology is described?
description: ColSmolVLM-Instruct-256M is a visual retriever model based on SmolVLM-Instruct-250M, incorporating the ColBERT strategy for efficient document indexing using visual features. It generates multi-vector representations of text and images. This version was trained with a batch size of 32 for 3 epochs using the commit b983e40 of the Colpali repository. The training dataset consists of 127,460 query-page pairs, including 63% from openly available academic datasets and 37% from a synthetic dataset made up of web-crawled PDF documents augmented with VLM-generated pseudo-questions. The dataset is fully English by design, allowing for the study of zero-shot generalization to non-English languages. No multi-page PDF document is used in both the training and evaluation sets to prevent contamination. A validation set of 2% of the samples is used for hyperparameter tuning. Training is conducted in bfloat16 format, using low-rank adapters with alpha=32 and r=32 on the transformer layers of the language model and the final projection layer. The paged_adamw_8bit optimizer is used with a 4 GPU setup, data parallelism, a learning rate of 5e-4 with linear decay and 2.5% warmup steps, and a batch size of 8. The model is intended for document retrieval tasks, particularly for PDF-type documents. It relies on multi-vector retrieval derived from the ColBERT late interaction mechanism, which may require engineering efforts to adapt to vector retrieval frameworks that do not natively support multi-vectors. The model's performance is evaluated on its ability to generalize to non-English languages, though it may have limitations in this area. The vision language backbone model is licensed under Apache 2.0, while the adapters are under the MIT license.

instruction: Could you re-evaluate my solution and point out any errors in the reasoning process?
description: SuperCorrect is a novel two-stage fine-tuning method designed to improve both reasoning accuracy and self-correction ability in large language models (LLMs). It addresses the limitations of smaller models, such as Llama-3-8B and DeepSeekMath-Base, which struggle with complex mathematical reasoning due to their inability to effectively identify and correct reasoning errors. SuperCorrect introduces a framework that uses a large teacher model to supervise and correct the reasoning and reflection processes of a smaller student model. In the first stage, hierarchical high-level and detailed thought templates are extracted from the teacher model to guide the student model in eliciting more fine-grained reasoning thoughts. In the second stage, cross-model collaborative direct preference optimization (DPO) is introduced to enhance the student model's self-correction abilities by following the teacher's correction traces during training. This approach enables the student model to effectively locate and resolve erroneous thoughts using error-driven insights from the teacher model, thereby breaking the bottleneck of its thoughts and acquiring new skills and knowledge to tackle challenging problems. The SuperCorrect-7B model has been shown to significantly outperform DeepSeekMath-7B by 7.8%/5.3% and Qwen2.5-Math-7B by 15.1%/6.3% on MATH/GSM8K benchmarks, achieving new state-of-the-art performance among all 7B models. SuperCorrect is based on several open-source models and incorporates ideas from prior works, including the concept of thought templates.

instruction: What emotional state is the speaker in during this short audio?
description: This model implements categorical emotion classification as described in the paper 'Vox-Profile: A Speech Foundation Model Benchmark for Characterizing Diverse Speaker and Speech Traits'. It is based on the top-performing solution (SAILER) in the INTERSPEECH 2025 - Speech Emotion Challenge, with modifications to simplify the model by excluding certain augmentations and transcript usage, while maintaining effectiveness. The model is trained on the MSP-Podcast dataset and is capable of classifying speech into the following nine emotion categories: Anger, Contempt, Disgust, Fear, Happiness, Neutral, Sadness, Surprise, and Other. The model is built using the WavLM architecture and is designed to process audio inputs up to 15 seconds in length, sampled at 16kHz and in mono channel format. The model is released under the Open RAIL license, and users are required to respect the privacy and consent of data subjects and comply with relevant laws and regulations. The model is not intended for use in clinical or diagnostic applications, surveillance, privacy-invasive applications, or for commercial purposes.

instruction: Can you explain the setting and main elements in this photo of a beach?
description: BLIP is a Vision-Language Pre-training (VLP) framework designed to transfer flexibly to both vision-language understanding and generation tasks. It is trained on visual question answering using a base architecture with a ViT base backbone. BLIP addresses the limitations of existing pre-trained models, which typically excel in either understanding-based or generation-based tasks but not both. The model improves performance by effectively utilizing noisy web data through a process of bootstrapping captions, where a captioner generates synthetic captions and a filter removes the noisy ones. BLIP achieves state-of-the-art results on a wide range of vision-language tasks, including image-text retrieval with a 2.7% increase in average recall@1, image captioning with a 2.8% increase in CIDEr score, and visual question answering (VQA) with a 1.6% increase in VQA score. It also demonstrates strong generalization ability when directly transferred to video-language tasks in a zero-shot manner. The model can be used for conditional and un-conditional image captioning. This release is for research purposes in support of an academic paper. The models, datasets, and code are not specifically designed or evaluated for all downstream purposes. Users are strongly encouraged to evaluate and address potential concerns related to accuracy, safety, and fairness before deployment. Particular attention should be given to high-risk scenarios where errors or misuse could significantly impact people’s lives, rights, or safety. Applicable laws and best practices should be followed when selecting use cases.

instruction: What are some top brands offering high-quality shirts with long sleeves?
description: FashionCLIP is a CLIP-based model developed to produce general product representations for fashion concepts. Leveraging the pre-trained checkpoint (ViT-B/32) released by OpenAI, the model is trained on a large, high-quality fashion dataset to study whether domain-specific fine-tuning of CLIP-like models is sufficient to produce product representations that are zero-shot transferable to entirely new datasets and tasks. The model uses a ViT-B/32 Transformer architecture as an image encoder and a masked self-attention Transformer as a text encoder. These encoders are trained, starting from a pre-trained checkpoint, to maximize the similarity of (image, text) pairs via a contrastive loss on a fashion dataset containing 800K products. The model was trained on (image, text) pairs obtained from the Farfetch dataset, an English dataset comprising over 800K fashion products, with more than 3K brands across dozens of object types. The image used for encoding is the standard product image, which is a picture of the item over a white background, with no humans. The text used is a concatenation of the highlight (e.g., 'stripes', 'long sleeves', 'Armani') and short description ('80s styled t-shirt') available in the Farfetch dataset. The model was not developed for model deployment - to do so, researchers will first need to carefully study their capabilities in relation to the specific context they’re being deployed within. We acknowledge certain limitations of FashionCLIP and expect that it inherits certain limitations and biases present in the original CLIP model. We do not expect our fine-tuning to significantly augment these limitations: we acknowledge that the fashion data we use makes explicit assumptions about the notion of gender as in 'blue shoes for a woman' that inevitably associate aspects of clothing with specific people. Our investigations also suggest that the data used introduces certain limitations in FashionCLIP. From the textual modality, given that most captions derived from the Farfetch dataset are long, we observe that FashionCLIP may be more performant in longer queries than shorter ones. From the image modality, FashionCLIP is also biased towards standard product images (centered, white background). Model selection, i.e. selecting an appropriate stopping criteria during fine-tuning, remains an open challenge. We observed that using loss on an in-domain (i.e. same distribution as test) validation dataset is a poor selection criteria when out-of-domain generalization (i.e. across different datasets) is desired, even when the dataset used is relatively diverse and large. The model was updated on 10/03/23 to a newer version (FashionCLIP 2.0), which uses the `laion/CLIP-ViT-B-32-laion2B-s34B-b79K` checkpoint and shows improved performance. The performance gains are postulated to be due to the increased training data (5x OpenAI CLIP data). The architecture remains the same, but fine-tuning on the fashion dataset improved zero-shot performance across benchmarks. The weighted macro F1 scores across models are as follows: OpenAI CLIP (FMNIST: 0.66, KAGL: 0.63, DEEP: 0.45), FashionCLIP (FMNIST: 0.74, KAGL: 0.67, DEEP: 0.48), Laion CLIP (FMNIST: 0.78, KAGL: 0.71, DEEP: 0.58), and FashionCLIP 2.0 (FMNIST: 0.83, KAGL: 0.73, DEEP: 0.62).

instruction: Is there a way to generate a 3D model of a logo in a sculpted form?
description: CraftsMan is a high-fidelity mesh generation model designed for 3D native generation and interactive geometry refinement. It is intended to support tasks related to the creation and refinement of 3D mesh models. The model is part of a research effort led by contributors from HKUST, LightIllusions, HKUST(GZ), and Tencent AI Lab. The model is available for use through a pipeline interface, which can be loaded from local checkpoints. It is recommended to use the model on a CUDA-enabled device with appropriate PyTorch configurations. An installation script is provided for setting up the environment, including the creation of a Conda environment and installation of required packages. A Gradio demo is also available for interactive testing of the model. The model is open-source, with inference, checkpoints, and training components available, though the ComfyUI integration is not yet provided. Users are encouraged to cite the associated research paper when using the model or repository.

instruction: Generate a short story based on a character who discovers a hidden world beneath the city.
description: The Llama 3.2 collection is a set of multilingual large language models (LLMs) available in 1B and 3B parameter sizes, designed for text-in and text-out generation. These models are pretrained and instruction-tuned for multilingual dialogue use cases, including agentic retrieval and summarization tasks. They outperform many open source and closed chat models on common industry benchmarks. The models are developed by Meta and use an optimized transformer architecture with auto-regressive language modeling. Instruction-tuned versions are aligned with human preferences for helpfulness and safety using supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and reinforcement learning with human feedback (RLHF). The models support multilingual text and code as input and output, with a context length of 128k tokens for the standard version and 8k for the quantized version. The training data consists of a new mix of publicly available online data, with a knowledge cutoff in December 2023. The models are trained using Meta's custom GPU cluster and production infrastructure, with training energy use totaling 916k GPU hours and estimated greenhouse gas emissions of 240 tons CO2eq. The models are available under a custom commercial license and are intended for commercial and research use in multiple languages. Instruction-tuned models are suitable for assistant-like chat, agentic applications, knowledge retrieval, summarization, and mobile AI-powered writing assistants. Pretrained models can be adapted for additional natural language generation tasks, and quantized models are suitable for on-device use with limited compute resources. The models are not intended for use in any manner that violates applicable laws or regulations, or that is prohibited by the Acceptable Use Policy and Llama 3.2 Community License. The models are not designed to be deployed in isolation but as part of an overall AI system with additional safety guardrails. Developers are expected to deploy system safeguards when building agentic systems. The models have been evaluated on standard automatic benchmarks, showing varying performance across different tasks and quantization methods. The models are available in different quantization schemes, including SpinQuant and QLoRA, which aim to improve inference scalability and reduce model size and memory usage. The models are part of a responsible release approach that includes safety fine-tuning, adversarial evaluations, and red teaming exercises to identify and mitigate potential risks. The models are intended to be used in a variety of use cases, with developers expected to tailor safety for their specific applications. The models are part of an ongoing effort to promote generative AI safety through industry partnerships, open-source tools, and community contributions.

instruction: Can you detect the emotions in the phrase: 'This news made me feel anxious and hopeful'?
description: The model is a multi-label classification model trained on a dataset based on Reddit data with 28 labels. It is designed to classify input text into one or more of the 28 emotion-related categories. The model produces 28 probability outputs for each input text, with a typical threshold of 0.5 applied to determine the final prediction for each label. It was trained using the `AutoModelForSequenceClassification.from_pretrained` method with the problem type set to 'multi_label_classification', for 3 epochs with a learning rate of 2e-5 and a weight decay of 0.01. An ONNX version of the model, including an INT8 quantized variant, is available. These versions are faster for inference, especially for smaller batch sizes, reduce the size of dependencies required for inference, and are more multi-platform. The quantized version reduces the model file size by 75% while retaining almost all accuracy for inference purposes. Evaluation of the model on the test split of the dataset shows an overall accuracy of 0.474, precision of 0.575, recall of 0.396, and F1 score of 0.450. However, due to the multi-label nature of the task and the varying representation of labels in the dataset, per-label metrics are more meaningful. With a threshold of 0.5, the model achieves varying levels of performance across labels, with some labels like 'gratitude' achieving an F1 score of 0.919 and others like 'relief' achieving an F1 score of 0.000. Optimizing the threshold per label to maximize F1 score results in improved overall metrics, with a precision of 0.542, recall of 0.577, and F1 of 0.541. When weighted by the relative size of the support of each label, the model achieves a precision of 0.572, recall of 0.677, and F1 of 0.611. The dataset is noted to be challenging, with some labels having very few examples in the training and test splits, and potential issues with ambiguity and labeling errors in the training data. These factors are believed to constrain the model's performance, and data cleaning to address these issues could lead to a higher performing model.

instruction: Generate a simple and engaging caption for this image.
description: BLIP is a Vision-Language Pre-training (VLP) framework designed for both vision-language understanding and generation tasks. It is pretrained on the COCO dataset using a base architecture with a Vision Transformer (ViT) large backbone. BLIP is capable of performing a variety of vision-language tasks, including image-text retrieval, image captioning, and visual question answering (VQA). It also demonstrates strong generalization ability when applied to video-language tasks in a zero-shot manner. The model utilizes noisy web data by bootstrapping captions, where a captioner generates synthetic captions and a filter removes the noisy ones. BLIP achieves state-of-the-art results, with improvements of +2.7% in average recall@1 for image-text retrieval, +2.8% in CIDEr for image captioning, and +1.6% in VQA score. The model supports both conditional and unconditional image captioning. It is intended for research purposes in support of an academic paper and is not specifically designed or evaluated for all downstream applications. Users are advised to evaluate and address potential concerns related to accuracy, safety, and fairness before deployment, particularly in high-risk scenarios where errors or misuse could significantly impact people’s lives, rights, or safety.

instruction: I want to visualize how this sculpture looks from the back—can you generate that view from the front and side images?
description: NVComposer is a novel approach to generative multi-view novel view synthesis (NVS) that eliminates the need for explicit external alignment processes such as pose estimation or pre-reconstruction. The model is designed to implicitly infer spatial and geometric relationships between multiple conditional views. It achieves this through two key components: an image-pose dual-stream diffusion model that simultaneously generates target novel views and condition camera poses, and a geometry-aware feature alignment module that distills geometric priors from dense stereo models during training. NVComposer is intended to improve the flexibility and accessibility of generative NVS systems by removing reliance on external alignment. Extensive experiments show that it achieves state-of-the-art performance in generative multi-view NVS tasks, with substantial improvements in synthesis quality as the number of unposed input views increases. The model is implemented as a checkpoint file that can be downloaded using the Hugging Face Hub API. It is intended for use in tasks involving novel view generation from multi-view data, particularly in scenarios where alignment is unstable due to insufficient overlap or occlusions between views. The model is provided as a research contribution and is not guaranteed to be suitable for all applications without further adaptation or validation.

instruction: I need to process a video file and generate a feature vector for similarity comparisons—can you assist?
description: The VideoMAEv2-Base model is a base-sized model pre-trained for 800 epochs in a self-supervised manner on the UnlabeldHybrid-1M dataset. It was introduced in the paper 'VideoMAE V2: Scaling Video Masked Autoencoders with Dual Masking' by Wang et al. and first released in 2023. The model is intended for use in video feature extraction. It is designed to process video data and extract meaningful features from it. The model can be used with the Transformers library by loading the appropriate configuration, image processor, and model from the 'OpenGVLab/VideoMAEv2-Base' repository. The video input is expected to be in the form of a list of numpy arrays with dimensions (16, 3, 224, 24), representing 16 frames of 224x224 RGB images. The input is processed using the VideoMAEImageProcessor, and the model outputs are obtained by passing the processed inputs through the model in a no-grad context. The model is described in the following BibTeX entries: @InProceedings{wang2023videomaev2, author = {Wang, Limin and Huang, Bingkun and Zhao, Zhiyu and Tong, Zhan and He, Yinan and Wang, Yi and Wang, Yali and Qiao, Yu}, title = {VideoMAE V2: Scaling Video Masked Autoencoders With Dual Masking}, booktitle = {Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR)}, month = {June}, year = {2023}, pages = {14549-14560}} and @misc{videomaev2, title={VideoMAE V2: Scaling Video Masked Autoencoders with Dual Masking}, author={Limin Wang and Bingkun Huang and Zhiyu Zhao and Zhan Tong and Yinan He and Yi Wang and Yali Wang and Yu Qiao}, year={2023}, eprint={2303.16727}, archivePrefix={arXiv}, primaryClass={cs.CV}}.

instruction: What was the total rainfall recorded in July 2022 in Patna according to the data?
description: TableQA is the task of answering questions over tables of structured information, returning individual cells or tables as output. TableQA research has focused primarily on high-resource languages, leaving medium- and low-resource languages with little progress due to scarcity of annotated data and neural models. This work addresses this gap by introducing a fully automatic large-scale tableQA data generation process for low-resource languages with limited budget. The data generation method is applied to two Indic languages, Bengali and Hindi, which have no tableQA datasets or models. TableQA models trained on the large-scale datasets outperform state-of-the-art LLMs. The trained models are further studied on different aspects, including mathematical reasoning capabilities and zero-shot cross-lingual transfer. This work is the first on low-resource tableQA focusing on scalable data generation and evaluation procedures. The proposed data generation method can be applied to any low-resource language with a web presence. Datasets, models, and code are released.
domain: Natural Language Processing Table Question Answering

instruction: Is there a connection between the provided image and the query about machine learning techniques?
description: The MonoQwen2-VL-v0.1 is a multimodal reranker finetuned with LoRA, optimized for asserting pointwise image-query relevance using the objective. Given a pair of an image and a query fed into the prompt of the VLM, the model is tasked to generate 'True' if the image is relevant to the query and 'False' otherwise. During inference, a relevancy score can be obtained by comparing the logits of the two tokens, and this score can effectively be used to rerank the candidates generated by a first-stage retriever (such as DSE or ColPali) or filter them using a threshold. The model was used to train this model with negatives mined using DSE. The model has been evaluated on a set of datasets by retrieving 10 elements with and reranking them. The ndcg@5 scores for the model include 89.0 for vidore/arxivqa_test_subsampled, 59.7 for vidore/docvqa_test_subsampled, 93.2 for vidore/infovqa_test_subsampled, 96.0 for vidore/tabfquad_test_subsampled, 93.0 for vidore/shiftproject_test, 100.0 for vidore/syntheticDocQA_artificial_intelligence_test, 97.7 for vidore/syntheticDocQA_energy_test, 98.0 for vidore/syntheticDocQA_government_reports_test, 99.3 for vidore/syntheticDocQA_healthcare_industry_test, and 79.0 for vidore/tatdqa_test, with a mean score of 90.5. The model is licensed under the Apache 2.0 license.

instruction: Find me a document that shows the design process of a product, from sketches to final version.
description: ColNomic Embed Multimodal 7B is a state-of-the-art multimodal embedding model designed for visual document retrieval tasks. It is a 7B parameter vision-language model that directly encodes interleaved text and images without requiring complex preprocessing. The model achieves 62.7 NDCG@5 on the Vidore-v2 benchmark, outperforming all other models tested. It supports a range of document retrieval scenarios, including research papers, technical documentation, product catalogs, financial reports, and visually rich content. The model is fine-tuned from Qwen2.5-VL 7B Instruct and features a multi-vector output option for enhanced performance. Key innovations include same-source sampling to create harder in-batch negatives and a unified text-image input processing architecture. It integrates seamlessly with Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) workflows by enabling direct document embedding, faster processing, and capturing both textual and visual cues in a single embedding. The model is fully open-source, with model weights, training data, and code available. Performance may vary when processing documents with unconventional layouts, handwriting, or stylized fonts. While it supports multiple languages, performance is strongest on English content. Very large or complex documents may require chunking for optimal results.

instruction: Could you detect and label all the dogs in this photo using the candidate labels I provided?
description: This repository implements a custom task for zero-shot object detection for Hugging Face Inference Endpoints. The model is designed to detect objects in images based on candidate class labels provided in the input, without prior training on those specific classes. It supports the task of identifying and localizing objects in images using a set of candidate labels specified by the user. The model outputs bounding boxes, confidence scores, and corresponding class labels for detected objects. The bounding boxes are represented as [x_min, y_min, x_max, y_max] coordinates. The model uses a custom handler file for inference, and the repository includes a requirements.txt file to install the timm library. The model is intended to be deployed as an Inference Endpoint by selecting the 'Custom' task option. The model's input requires a base64-encoded image and a list of candidate class labels. The output includes a list of bounding boxes, scores, and matched candidate labels. The model is adapted for Hugging Face Inference Endpoints and was inspired by prior work on zero-shot object detection.

instruction: Can you determine the classification of this English post about elections?
description: xlm-roberta-large-twitter-cap-minor is an xlm-roberta-large model finetuned on multilingual (english, danish, hungarian) training data labelled with from the . The model is designed for text classification tasks, particularly for processing text in multiple languages. It supports the classification of text inputs, with a focus on english, danish, and hungarian. The model was evaluated on a test set of 15 349 english examples, which constituted 20% of the english data. On this test set, the model achieved an accuracy of 0.67 and a weighted average F1-score of 0.61. The model uses the sentencepiece tokenizer. Users must provide a token when loading the model due to gated access, using the token parameter in the Transformers package or the use_auth_token parameter in earlier versions. If a RuntimeError occurs when loading the model using the from_pretrained() method, adding ignore_mismatched_sizes=True should resolve the issue. The model's performance can be improved by expanding the training data, and the developers welcome submissions of CAP-coded corpora in any domain and language. Submissions can be sent to poltextlab{at}poltextlab{dot}com or through the specified method.

instruction: What is the quantity of items ordered in this document?
description: This model is a fine-tuned version of an unspecified base model on an unknown dataset. It is designed to perform document question-answering tasks. The model was trained using the following hyperparameters: learning rate of 5e-05, training batch size of 4, evaluation batch size of 8, random seed of 42, AdamW optimizer with betas of (0.9, 0.999) and epsilon of 1e-08, linear learning rate scheduler, and 20 training epochs. During training, the model achieved a final training loss of 0.0037 at epoch 19.9115 and step 4500, with a corresponding validation loss of 4.6448. The model was developed using Transformers 4.46.3, PyTorch 2.5.1+cu124, Datasets 3.1.0, and Tokenizers 0.20.3. More information is needed regarding the model's intended uses, limitations, and training and evaluation data.

instruction: I need help finding images for the phrase 'a dancer performing on a stage'.
description: SigLIP 2 Giant extends the pretraining objective with prior, independently developed techniques into a unified recipe, for improved semantic understanding, localization, and dense features. The model can be used for tasks like zero-shot image classification and image-text retrieval, or as a vision encoder for VLMs and other vision tasks. SigLIP 2 adds training objectives such as decoder loss, global-local and masked prediction loss, and aspect ratio and resolution adaptibility. The model is pre-trained on the WebLI dataset and was trained on up to 2048 TPU-v5e chips. Evaluation results are presented in the associated paper. The model is described in the research titled 'SigLIP 2: Multilingual Vision-Language Encoders with Improved Semantic Understanding, Localization, and Dense Features' by Michael Tschannen, Alexey Gritsenko, Xiao Wang, Muhammad Ferjad Naeem, Ibrahim Alabdulmohsin, Nikhil Parthasarathy, Talfan Evans, Lucas Beyer, Ye Xia, Basil Mustafa, Olivier Hénaff, Jeremiah Harmsen, Andreas Steiner, and Xiaohua Zhai, available on arXiv with the identifier 2502.14786.

instruction: Can you produce a video from this image, making it look like it's transitioning into nightfall?
description: Wan2.1 is a comprehensive and open suite of video foundation models designed to push the boundaries of video generation. It offers state-of-the-art performance, consistently outperforming existing open-source models and state-of-the-art commercial solutions across multiple benchmarks. The T2V-1.3B model requires only 8.19 GB VRAM, making it compatible with almost all consumer-grade GPUs. It can generate a 5-second 480P video on an RTX 4890 in about 4 minutes (without optimization techniques like quantization), and its performance is comparable to some closed-source models. Wan2.1 supports multiple tasks, including Text-to-Video, Image-to-Video, Video Editing, Text-to-Image, and Video-to-Audio, advancing the field of video generation. It is the first video model capable of generating both Chinese and English text, featuring robust text generation that enhances its practical applications. Wan2.1 includes a powerful video VAE, Wan-VAE, which delivers exceptional efficiency and performance, encoding and decoding 1080P videos of any length while preserving temporal information, making it an ideal foundation for video and image generation. The model is built on the mainstream diffusion transformer paradigm, with innovations such as a novel spatio-temporal variational autoencoder, scalable training strategies, large-scale data construction, and automated evaluation metrics. Wan2.1's architecture uses the T5 Encoder to encode multilingual text input, with cross-attention in each transformer block embedding the text into the model structure. It employs an MLP with a Linear layer and a SiLU layer to process the input time embeddings and predict six modulation parameters individually. The model is trained on a large and diverse dataset of images and videos, curated through a four-step data cleaning process focusing on fundamental dimensions, visual quality, and motion quality. Wan2.1 has been evaluated against leading open-source and closed-source models using a carefully designed set of 1,035 internal prompts, tested across 14 major dimensions and 26 sub-dimensions. The results demonstrate superior performance compared to both open-source and closed-source models. The model is available under the Apache 2.0 License, and users are responsible for ensuring their usage complies with the provisions of this license. Users must not generate or share content that violates applicable laws, causes harm to individuals or groups, disseminates personal information intended for harm, spreads misinformation, or targets vulnerable populations.

instruction: Could you visualize a futuristic cityscape with flying vehicles and neon signs?
description: Wan2.1 is a comprehensive and open suite of video foundation models designed to push the boundaries of video generation. It offers state-of-the-art performance, consistently outperforming existing open-source models and state-of-the-art commercial solutions across multiple benchmarks. The T2V-1.3B model requires only 8.19 GB VRAM, making it compatible with nearly all consumer-grade GPUs. It can generate a 5-second 480P video on an RTX 4090 in about 4 minutes, with performance comparable to some closed-source models. Wan2.1 supports multiple tasks, including Text-to-Video, Image-to-Video, Video Editing, Text-to-Image, and Video-to-Audio, advancing the field of video generation. It is the first video model capable of generating both Chinese and English text, enhancing its practical applications. The model features a powerful video VAE, Wan-VAE, which delivers exceptional efficiency and performance, encoding and decoding 1080P videos of any length while preserving temporal information. Wan2.1 is designed using the Flow Matching framework within the paradigm of mainstream Diffusion Transformers. It uses a T5 Encoder to encode multilingual text input, with cross-attention in each transformer block embedding the text into the model structure. An MLP with a Linear layer and a SiLU layer processes the input time embeddings and predicts six modulation parameters individually. This MLP is shared across all transformer blocks, with each block learning a distinct set of biases. The model's architecture includes a novel 3D causal VAE specifically designed for video generation, combining multiple strategies to improve spatio-temporal compression, reduce memory usage, and ensure temporal causality. Wan-VAE can encode and decode unlimited-length 1080P videos without losing historical temporal information, making it particularly well-suited for video generation tasks. The model is trained on a large-scale dataset curated through a four-step data cleaning process, focusing on fundamental dimensions, visual quality, and motion quality. The T2V-1.3B model has 1.3 billion parameters with a dimension of 1536, an input dimension of 16, an output dimension of 16, a feedforward dimension of 8960, a frequency dimension of 256, 12 heads, and 30 layers. The T2V-14B model has 14 billion parameters with a dimension of 5120, an input dimension of 16, an output dimension of 16, a feedforward dimension of 13824, a frequency dimension of 256, 40 heads, and 40 layers. The model is evaluated using the Wan-Bench framework, demonstrating that the 1.3B model surpasses the overall metrics of larger open-source models. The model is licensed under the Apache 2.0 License, and users are responsible for ensuring their usage complies with the provisions of this license. The model is intended for use in a variety of applications, including creative video creation and academic research, and is designed to be accessible to teams with limited computing resources.

instruction: Can you determine whether 'The student failed the test' is a consequence of 'He didn’t study'?
description: This model is based on a pre-trained model and fine-tuned on a combination of NLI data in 15 languages. It is designed for zero-shot text classification, particularly in languages other than English. The model is fine-tuned on XNLI, a multilingual NLI dataset, and can be used with any of the languages in the XNLI corpus, including English, French, Spanish, German, Greek, Bulgarian, Russian, Turkish, Arabic, Vietnamese, Thai, Chinese, Hindi, Swahili, and Urdu. Since the base model was pre-trained on 100 different languages, it has shown some effectiveness in languages beyond those listed. For English-only classification, it is recommended to use other models. The model can be loaded with the zero-shot-classification pipeline and used to classify text in any of the supported languages. It is possible to pass the labels in one language and the sequence to classify in another. The default hypothesis template is the English phrase 'This text is {}', which can be translated to the language being used. The model was pre-trained on a set of 100 languages and then fine-tuned on the task of NLI on the concatenated MNLI train set and the XNLI validation and test sets. It was further trained for one additional epoch on only XNLI data where the translations for the premise and hypothesis are shuffled such that the premise and hypothesis for each example come from the same original English example but are in different languages.

instruction: I want to see a bicycle being turned around in all directions—can you generate that?
description: This LoRA is trained on the Wan2.1 14B I2V 480p model and allows you to rotate any object in an image. The effect works on a wide variety of objects, from animals to vehicles to people. It transforms any image into a video of it being rotated and provides consistent results across different object types. The model is designed to be used with a simple prompt structure that is easy to adapt. The key trigger phrase is 'r0t4tion 360 degrees rotation'. The model is available in Safetensors format and is intended to be used with a modified version of Kijai's Wan Video Wrapper workflow, which includes a Wan LoRA node connected to the base model. The model was trained on 30 seconds of video comprised of 12 short clips (each clip captioned separately) of things being rotated, over the course of 20 epochs. The base model used for training is Wan2.1 14B I2V 480p. Training was conducted using Diffusion Pipe for Training. Special thanks are given to Kijai for the ComfyUI Wan Video Wrapper and to tdrussell for the training scripts. Recommended settings for use include a LoRA Strength of 1.0, an Embedded Guidance Scale of 6.0, and a Flow Shift of 5.0.

instruction: Design a surreal landscape with floating islands, waterfalls going upward, and glowing plants.
description: This model is an 8-step distilled LoRA trained based on the FLUX.1-dev model by the AlimamaCreative Team. It uses a multi-head discriminator to enhance the distillation quality. The model supports text-to-image generation, inpainting with controlnet, and other FLUX-related tasks. It is recommended to use a guidance_scale of 3.5 and a lora_scale of 1. The model can be used with the diffusers library and is compatible with the FluxPipeline. Training was conducted on 1 million open source and internal images with an aesthetic score of 6.3 or higher and a resolution greater than 800. Adversarial training was employed, with the original FLUX.1-dev transformer fixed as the discriminator backbone and multi-heads added to every transformer layer. The guidance scale was fixed at 3.5 during training, and a time shift of 3 was used. Training was performed using mixed precision (bf16), a learning rate of 2e-5, a batch size of 64, and an image size of 1024x1024.

instruction: Create a 3D version of a café based on the image I uploaded, including tables, chairs, and a barista counter.
description: Tencent Hunyuan3D-1.0 is a two-stage framework for text-to-3D and image-to-3D generation. It includes a lite version and a standard version, both supporting text- and image-conditioned generation. In the first stage, a multi-view diffusion model generates multi-view RGB images in approximately 4 seconds, capturing rich details of the 3D asset from different viewpoints. In the second stage, a feed-forward reconstruction model rapidly and faithfully reconstructs the 3D asset from the generated multi-view images in approximately 7 seconds. The reconstruction network is designed to handle noises and inconsistencies introduced by the multi-view diffusion and to leverage information from the condition image to recover the 3D structure. The framework incorporates the text-to-image model Hunyuan-DiT, enabling a unified approach for both text- and image-conditioned 3D generation. The standard version has three times more parameters than the lite version and other existing models. Hunyuan3D-1.0 achieves a balance between speed and quality, significantly reducing generation time while maintaining the quality and diversity of the produced assets. The lite model produces a 3D mesh from a single image in about 10 seconds on an NVIDIA A100 GPU, while the standard model takes about 25 seconds. Evaluations show that Hunyuan3D-1.0 received the highest user preference across five metrics. The model supports both text-to-3D and image-to-3D generation, with bilingual Chinese and English support for text prompts. The model is available in three versions: Hunyuan3D-1/lite for multi-view generation, Hunyuan3D-1/std for multi-view generation, and Hunyuan3D-1/svrm for sparse-view reconstruction. The model requires specific hardware and software configurations, including a compatible GPU and environment setup. Inference can be performed using provided scripts, with different VRAM requirements for the standard and lite pipelines. The model supports various configuration options, including texture mapping, rendering, and memory-saving settings. Camera parameters for output views include fixed azimuth angles relative to the input view: +0, +60, +120, +180, +240, and +300 degrees.

instruction: I’m curious if the voice in this clip is from a male or female—can you tell me?
description: This model combines the SpeechBrain ECAPA-TDNN speaker embedding model with an SVM classifier to predict speaker gender from audio input. The model was trained and evaluated on the VoxCeleb2, Mozilla Common Voice v10.0, and TIMIT datasets. The input is an audio file, which will be converted to 16kHz, mono, single channel. The output is a gender prediction, either 'male' or 'female'. The speaker embedding is a 192-dimensional ECAPA-TDNN embedding from SpeechBrain. The classifier is a Support Vector Machine optimized through Optuna with 200 trials. Performance on the VoxCeleb2 test set is 98.9% accuracy and 0.9885 F1-score. Performance on the Mozilla Common Voice v10.0 English validated test set is 92.3% accuracy. Performance on the TIMIT test set is 99.6% accuracy. The model was trained on the VoxCeleb2 dataset, with a training set of 1,691 speakers (845 females, 846 males), a validation set of 785 speakers (396 females, 389 males), and a test set of 1,647 speakers (828 females, 819 males). There is no speaker overlap between the sets. Audio preprocessing includes conversion to WAV format, single channel, 16kHz sampling rate, 256 kp/s bitrate, and application of SileroVAD for voice activity detection, taking the first voiced segment. The model was trained on celebrity voices from YouTube interviews, and performance may vary on different audio qualities or recording conditions. The model is designed for binary gender classification only.

instruction: Show a video of a mountain climber reaching the peak of a snow-covered summit.
description: LTX-Video is the first DiT-based video generation model capable of generating high-quality videos in real-time. It produces 30 FPS videos at a 1216×704 resolution faster than they can be watched. Trained on a large-scale dataset of diverse videos, the model generates high-resolution videos with realistic and varied content. The model is a diffusion-based image-to-video generation model developed by Lightricks. It supports image-to-video generation and video-to-video generation. The model works best on resolutions under 720 x 1280 and number of frames below 257. It works on resolutions that are divisible by 32 and number of frames that are divisible by 8 + 1. In case the resolution or number of frames are not divisible by 32 or 8 + 1, the input will be padded with -1 and then cropped to the desired resolution and number of frames. Prompts should be in English, with more elaborate prompts yielding better results. The model is available in multiple versions, including ltxv-13b-0.9.8-dev, ltxv-13b-0.9.8-distilled, ltxv-2b-0.9.8-distilled, and their quantized counterparts. The ltxv-13b-0.9.8-dev version provides the highest quality but requires more VRAM, while the ltxv-13b-0.9.8-distilled version offers a balanced speed-quality trade-off. The ltxv-2b-0.9.8-distilled version is faster, uses less VRAM, and is ideal for rapid iterations. The ltxv-2b-0.9.8-distilled-fp8 version is a smaller model with a slight quality reduction compared to the 13b distilled version, ideal for light VRAM usage. The model is compatible with the Diffusers library for image-to-video generation. It is not intended or able to provide factual information. As a statistical model, it might amplify existing societal biases. The model may fail to generate videos that match the prompts perfectly. Prompt following is heavily influenced by the prompting-style.

instruction: I want to know if there are any trees in this image and where they are located.
description: The OmDet model was proposed by Tiancheng Zhao, Peng Liu, Xuan He, Lu Zhang, and Kyusong Lee from Om AI Lab. This model is intended for zero-shot (also called open-vocabulary) object detection. The OmDet model supports single image inference and batched multi-image inference, with support for different text prompts and classes in the same batch. The model can detect objects based on provided class labels and text prompts. For example, it can detect objects such as 'cat', 'remote', 'boat', 'statue', and 'trees' in images. The model outputs bounding boxes, class logits, and confidence scores for detected objects. Performance examples include detecting a remote with confidence 0.77, a cat with confidence 0.72, a boat with confidence 0.32, and a statue with confidence 0.73. The model uses a processor for input preparation and post-processing of outputs, including bounding box normalization and non-maximum suppression with a threshold of 0.3. The model is available through the Hugging Face model hub under the identifier 'omlab/omdet-turbo-swin-tiny-hf'.

instruction: Identify the tools in this garage workbench image.
description: MM Grounding DINO (tiny variant) is a model proposed by Xiangyu Zhao, Yicheng Chen, Shilin Xu, Xiangtai Li, Xinjiang Wang, Yining Li, and Haian Huang. It improves upon previous models by enhancing the contrastive class head and removing parameter sharing in the decoder, which leads to improved zero-shot detection performance. Specifically, it achieves 50.6 AP (an increase of 2.2) on COCO and 31.9 val AP (an increase of 11.8) and 41.4 minival AP (an increase of 12.6) on LVIS. The model is intended for zero-shot object detection and can be used with the provided code example. It was trained on data including O365, GoldG, GRIT, and V3Det. Evaluation results show that the model with the Swin-T backbone and pre-training data of O365, GoldG, V3Det achieves 50.6(+2.2) COCO mAP. On LVIS, the model with pre-training data of O365, GoldG, GRIT, and V3Det achieves 41.4(+12.6) minival AP and 31.9(+11.8) val AP. The model is available in the specified collection, and users are provided with a BibTeX entry for citation.

instruction: What techniques can I use to enhance the accuracy of text retrieval in a search engine?
description: The gte-modernbert series of models is built upon the latest modernBERT pre-trained encoder-only foundation models. These models include both text embedding models and rerank models. The gte-modernbert models demonstrate competitive performance in several text embedding and text retrieval evaluation tasks when compared to similar-scale models from the current open-source community. This includes assessments such as MTEB, LoCO, and COIR evaluation. The gte-modernbert-base model is developed by Tongyi Lab, Alibaba Group. It is a text embedding model primarily designed for English. The model has a size of 149 million parameters, a maximum input length of 8192 tokens, and an output dimension of 768. The model is designed to support text embedding and reranking tasks. It is evaluated on multiple benchmarks, including MTEB, LoCO, COIR, and BEIR. On the MTEB benchmark, the model achieves an average score of 64.38. On the LoCO benchmark, it achieves an average score of 87.57. On the COIR benchmark, it achieves an average score of 79.31. On the BEIR benchmark, it achieves an average score of 55.33. The model is designed to be used with the transformers and sentence-transformers libraries. It supports GPU acceleration with Flash Attention 2 if installed. The model can be deployed using Docker for CPU or GPU environments. The model is based on the training scheme of previous models, with the pre-training language model base replaced. The model is described in the paper titled 'mGTE: Generalized Long-Context Text Representation and Reranking Models for Multilingual Text Retrieval' and another paper titled 'Towards general text embeddings with multi-stage contrastive learning'. The model is available for use and deployment, and the developers are seeking research interns and full-time researchers to join their team at Tongyi Lab in Beijing and Hangzhou.

instruction: Can you produce a segmentation map that distinguishes the object from its surroundings?
description: BiRefNet is a model designed for high-resolution dichotomous image segmentation (DIS). It is trained on the DIS-TR dataset and validated on the DIS-TE and DIS-VD datasets. The model has achieved state-of-the-art performance on three tasks: DIS, HRSOD, and COD. BiRefNet is based on a bilateral reference architecture, which is intended to improve segmentation accuracy and resolution. The model supports inference tasks for image segmentation, including object extraction and mask generation. It can be used with weights from HuggingFace or locally stored weights, and it supports inference on GPU. The model is implemented in Python using PyTorch and can be integrated with the HuggingFace Transformers library. The model weights and code are available through a GitHub repository and HuggingFace. The model has been evaluated on standard benchmarks and is described in a paper published in the CAAI Artificial Intelligence Research journal in 2024. The authors of the model are Peng Zheng, Dehong Gao, Deng-Ping Fan, Li Liu, Jorma Laaksonen, Wanli Ouyang, and Nicu Sebe, affiliated with institutions including Nankai University, Northwestern Polytechnical University, National University of Defense Technology, Aalto University, Shanghai AI Laboratory, and University of Trento. The model is supported by GPU resources and deployment assistance from external contributors.

instruction: What is the subject of the book on the table in this room shot?
description: The Aquila-VL-2B model is a vision-language model (VLM) trained using a specific framework. It is based on a selected large language model (LLM) and incorporates a vision tower for processing visual information. The model was trained on the Infinity-MM dataset, which contains approximately 40 million image-text pairs. This dataset is a combination of open-source data collected from the internet and synthetic instruction data generated using open-source VLM models. The Infinity-MM dataset and related resources have been open-sourced for public use. The model was evaluated using a benchmarking tool, with a preference for using the OpenAI API for test sets that support API-based evaluation. Evaluation results show that the model achieves high performance across multiple benchmarks, including MMBench-ENtest (78.8), MMBench-CNtest (76.4), MMBench_V1.1test (75.2), MMT-Benchtest (58.2), RealWorldQA (63.9), HallusionBench (43.0), SEEDBench2plus (63.0), LLaVABench (68.4), MMStar (54.9), POPE (83.6), MMVet (44.3), MMMUval (47.4), ScienceQAtest (95.2), AI2Dtest (75.0), MathVistatestmini (59.0), MathVersetestmini (26.2), MathVision (18.4), DocVQAtest (85.0), InfoVQAtest (58.3), ChartQAtest (76.5), TextVQAval (76.4), OCRVQAtestcore (64.0), VCRen easy (70.0), OCRBench (772), and an overall average score of 64.1. These scores are compared to other models such as MiniCPM-V-2, InternVL2-2B, XinYuan-VL-2B, and Qwen2-VL-2B-Instruct. Evaluations were conducted in a local environment, so the scores may differ slightly from those reported in papers or on the official VLMEvalKit leaderboard. The model can be used by loading it with a specified Python library, processing images, and generating text outputs based on visual inputs. The model supports tasks such as answering questions about images, and it can be used with a specific conversation template. The model is designed to be used in a local environment with a CUDA device. The developers plan to train models of various sizes and to incorporate multi-image and video data in future training. If the model is used, the associated work should be cited as described in the provided citation.

instruction: Can you transcribe this audio clip of a conversation in Singaporean English and summarize the key points?
description: MERaLiON-2 is a family of speech-text large language models designed for Singapore’s multilingual and multicultural context, as well as the wider Southeast Asian region. The flagship model, MERaLiON-2-10B, integrates a localized Whisper-large-v3 speech encoder and a Gemma-2-9b text decoder. It is finetuned on 120,000 hours of speech and audio data across six diverse tasks: Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR), Spoken Question Answering (SQA), Spoken Dialogue Summarization (SDS), Audio Captioning (AC), Audio-Scene Question Answering (ASQA), and Paralinguistic Question Answering (PQA). The model supports audio inputs up to 300 seconds (5 minutes) and is adapted to handle linguistic nuances, accents, and dialects common in Singapore and neighboring countries. MERaLiON-2-10B-ASR is an ASR-optimized variant that delivers a 5–30% performance improvement over OpenAI’s whisper-large-v3 on speech recognition tasks for Singapore’s four official languages—English, Mandarin, Malay, and Tamil—as well as Indonesian, Thai, and Vietnamese. The model also demonstrates robust handling of code-switching scenarios and local colloquialisms. MERaLiON-2-3B is a lightweight variant that balances performance with reduced computational requirements. The model supports a range of tasks, including transcription, translation, summarization, audio captioning, emotion recognition, gender recognition, and speech translation. It is trained to follow complex instructions and provides detailed explanations regarding speech content and speaker emotions. MERaLiON-2-10B exhibits competitive performance in benchmark evaluations, with results comparable to other state-of-the-art open-source AudioLLMs. The model is not intended for use in tool calling, math, and coding tasks. It is trained on a global batch size of 768 for around 200k steps using 16 nodes and 128 H100 GPUs. The model has not been specifically aligned for safety and may generate inappropriate, offensive, or harmful content. Developers and users are responsible for performing their own safety fine-tuning and implementing necessary security measures.

instruction: I'm working on a recommendation system. How can I embed both user preferences and item descriptions for better matching?
description: jina-embeddings-v3 is a multilingual multi-task text embedding model designed for a variety of natural language processing applications. Based on a foundational model, it supports rotary position embeddings to handle long input sequences up to 8192 tokens. The model includes five LoRA adapters to generate task-specific embeddings efficiently. It supports task-specific embeddings through the task argument with options for retrieval.query, retrieval.passage, separation, classification, and text-matching. The model also supports matryoshka embeddings with flexible embedding sizes of 32, 64, 128, 256, 512, 768, and 1024. While the foundation model supports 100 languages, the model has been tuned for 30 languages including Arabic, Bengali, Chinese, Danish, Dutch, English, Finnish, French, Georgian, German, Greek, Hindi, Indonesian, Italian, Japanese, Korean, Latvian, Norwegian, Polish, Portuguese, Romanian, Russian, Slovak, Spanish, Swedish, Thai, Turkish, Ukrainian, Urdu, and Vietnamese. A bug in the encode function related to matryoshka embedding truncation after normalization has been resolved in the latest code revision. Users who encoded data with the previous version should use the specific code revision to maintain consistency. Mean pooling is recommended when integrating the model, as it averages token embeddings at the sentence or paragraph level to produce high-quality sentence embeddings. The model can be used directly via the Transformers package or with ONNX for efficient inference. The model is licensed under CC BY-NC 4.0 for use beyond AWS and Azure platforms or on-premises within companies. Commercial usage inquiries should be directed to Jina AI.

instruction: I need the content of this audio converted into a written format. Can you do that?
description: Whisper is a pre-trained model for automatic speech recognition (ASR) and speech translation. Trained on 680,000 hours of labelled speech data using large-scale weak supervision, Whisper models demonstrate strong generalization across many datasets and domains without the need for fine-tuning. The model is based on a Transformer architecture and functions as a sequence-to-sequence model. It was developed by Alec Radford et al from OpenAI and is available in ten pre-trained checkpoints with five different configurations, varying in model size from 39 million to 1550 million parameters. The smallest four configurations are trained on either English-only or multilingual data, while the largest checkpoints are multilingual only. English-only models are trained for speech recognition, while multilingual models are trained for both speech recognition and speech translation. For speech recognition, the model predicts transcriptions in the same language as the audio, and for speech translation, it predicts transcriptions in a different language. Whisper requires a WhisperProcessor to pre-process audio inputs into log-Mel spectrograms and to post-process model outputs into text. The model is informed of the task (transcription or translation) through context tokens provided to the decoder.
