These tokens include a transcription token, a language token, a task token, and optionally a timestamp prediction token. Context tokens can be forced or un-forced, allowing control over the output language and task or letting the model predict them automatically. Whisper is capable of transcribing audio samples up to 30 seconds in duration, and with a chunking algorithm, it can handle audio of arbitrary length. The model has been evaluated on tasks like English speech recognition, showing a word error rate of 3.43% on the LibriSpeech test-clean dataset. Whisper models may exhibit hallucinations, where the model generates text not present in the audio, due to its tendency to predict the next word in a sequence. Performance varies across languages, with lower accuracy observed in low-resource or low-discoverability languages and across different accents and dialects. The model is also prone to generating repetitive text, which can be partially mitigated with beam search and temperature scheduling. Whisper is intended for use in ASR and speech translation, and while it may be useful for developers, especially for English speech recognition, it is not recommended for high-risk domains or subjective classification tasks. The model may be used to improve accessibility tools, but its release also raises concerns about potential dual use in surveillance technologies. The training data consists of 680,000 hours of audio and transcripts collected from the internet, with 65% in English, 18% in non-English audio with English transcripts, and 17% in non-English audio with non-English transcripts across 98 languages. Performance in a given language is correlated with the amount of training data available for that language. The model is not robustly evaluated for tasks like voice activity detection, speaker classification, or speaker diarization, and users are advised to perform their own evaluations before deployment. The model is not intended for use in decision-making contexts where accuracy flaws could lead to significant negative outcomes. The model may also have some capability to recognize specific individuals, which could present safety concerns.

instruction: Please provide a written version of this English podcast episode, highlighting the main ideas without any extra details.
description: Whisper is a state-of-the-art model for automatic speech recognition (ASR) and speech translation, proposed in a paper by Alec Radford et al. from OpenAI. It is trained on over 5 million hours of labeled data and demonstrates a strong ability to generalize to many datasets and domains in a zero-shot setting. Whisper large-v3-turbo is a finetuned version of a pruned model. It is the exact same model, except that the number of decoding layers has been reduced from 32 to 4. As a result, the model is significantly faster, at the expense of a minor quality degradation. Whisper is a Transformer-based encoder-decoder model, also referred to as a sequence-to-sequence model. There are two flavors of Whisper model: English-only and multilingual. The English-only models were trained on the task of English speech recognition. The multilingual models were trained simultaneously on multilingual speech recognition and speech translation. For speech recognition, the model predicts transcriptions in the same language as the audio. For speech translation, the model predicts transcriptions to a different language from the audio. Whisper checkpoints come in five configurations of varying model sizes. The smallest four are available as English-only and multilingual. The largest checkpoints are multilingual only. All ten of the pre-trained checkpoints are available on the Hugging Face Hub. The pre-trained Whisper model demonstrates a strong ability to generalize to different datasets and domains. However, its predictive capabilities can be improved further for certain languages and tasks through fine-tuning. Whisper is supported in Hugging Face Transformers. The model can be used with the pipeline class to transcribe audios of arbitrary length. It can transcribe local audio files by passing the path to the audio file when calling the pipeline. Multiple audio files can be transcribed in parallel by specifying them as a list and setting the batch_size parameter. Transformers is compatible with all Whisper decoding strategies, such as temperature fallback and condition on previous tokens. Whisper predicts the language of the source audio automatically. If the source audio language is known a-priori, it can be passed as an argument to the pipeline. By default, Whisper performs the task of speech transcription, where the source audio language is the same as the target text language. To perform speech translation, where the target text is in English, set the task to 'translate'. The model can be made to predict timestamps. For sentence-level timestamps, pass the return_timestamps argument. And for word-level timestamps, pass return_timestamps='word'. The above arguments can be used in isolation or in combination. For example, to perform the task of speech transcription where the source audio is in French, and we want to return sentence-level timestamps, the following can be used. For more control over the generation parameters, use the model + processor API directly. Whisper has a receptive field of 30 seconds. To transcribe audios longer than this, one of two long-form algorithms are required: sequential or chunked. The sequential long-form algorithm should be used in either of the following scenarios: when transcription accuracy is the most important factor, and speed is less of a consideration, or when transcribing batches of long audio files, in which case the latency of sequential is comparable to chunked, while being up to 0.5% WER more accurate. Conversely, the chunked algorithm should be used when transcription speed is the most important factor or when transcribing a single long audio file. By default, Transformers uses the sequential algorithm. To enable the chunked algorithm, pass the chunk_length_s parameter to the pipeline. The Whisper forward pass is compatible with torch.compile for 4.5x speed-ups. Note that torch.compile is currently not compatible with the chunked long-form algorithm or Flash Attention 2. We recommend using Flash Attention 2 if your GPU supports it and you are not using torch.compile. To do so, first install flash-attn. Then pass attn_implementation='flash_attention_2' to from_pretrained. If your GPU does not support Flash Attention, we recommend making use of PyTorch's scale-product-attention (SDPA). This attention implementation is activated by default for PyTorch versions 2.1.1 or greater. Once a valid PyTorch version is installed, SDPA is activated by default. It can also be set explicitly by specifying attn_implementation='sdpa'. The pre-trained Whisper model demonstrates a strong ability to generalize to different datasets and domains. However, its predictive capabilities can be improved further for certain languages and tasks through fine-tuning. The primary intended users of these models are AI researchers studying robustness, generalization, capabilities, biases, and constraints of the current model. However, Whisper is also potentially quite useful as an ASR solution for developers, especially for English speech recognition. We recognize that once models are released, it is impossible to restrict access to only 'intended' uses or to draw reasonable guidelines around what is or is not research. The models are primarily trained and evaluated on ASR and speech translation to English tasks. They show strong ASR results in approximately 10 languages. They may exhibit additional capabilities, particularly if fine-tuned on certain tasks like voice activity detection, speaker classification, or speaker diarization but have not been robustly evaluated in these areas. We strongly recommend that users perform robust evaluations of the models in a particular context and domain before deploying them. In particular, we caution against using Whisper models to transcribe recordings of individuals taken without their consent or purporting to use these models for any kind of subjective classification. We recommend against use in high-risk domains like decision-making contexts, where flaws in accuracy can lead to pronounced flaws in outcomes. The models are intended to transcribe and translate speech, use of the model for classification is not only not evaluated but also not appropriate, particularly to infer human attributes. Our studies show that, over many existing ASR systems, the models exhibit improved robustness to accents, background noise, technical language, as well as zero shot translation from multiple languages into English; and that accuracy on speech recognition and translation is near the state-of-the-art level. However, because the models are trained in a weakly supervised manner using large-scale noisy data, the predictions may include texts that are not actually spoken in the audio input (i.e. hallucination). We hypothesize that this happens because, given their general knowledge of language, the models combine trying to predict the next word in audio with trying to transcribe the audio itself. Our models perform unevenly across languages, and we observe lower accuracy on low-resource and/or low-discoverability languages or languages where we have less training data. The models also exhibit disparate performance on different accents and dialects of particular languages, which may include higher word error rate across speakers of different genders, races, ages, or other demographic criteria. The models also exhibit disparate performance on different accents and dialects of particular languages, which may include higher word error rate across speakers of different genders, races, ages, or other demographic criteria. The sequence-to-sequence architecture of the model makes it prone to generating repetitive texts, which can be mitigated to some degree by beam search and temperature scheduling but not perfectly. Further analysis on these limitations are provided in the paper. It is likely that this behavior and hallucinations may be worse on lower-resource and/or lower-discoverability languages. We anticipate that Whisper models’ transcription capabilities may be used for improving accessibility tools. While Whisper models cannot be used for real-time transcription out of the box – their speed and size suggest that others may be able to build applications on top of them that allow for near-real-time speech recognition and translation. The real value of beneficial applications built on top of Whisper models suggests that the disparate performance of these models may have real economic implications. There are also potential dual use concerns that come with releasing Whisper. While we hope the technology will be used primarily for beneficial purposes, making ASR technology more accessible could enable more actors to build capable surveillance technologies or scale up existing surveillance efforts, as the speed and accuracy allow for affordable automatic transcription and translation of large volumes of audio communication. Moreover, these models may have some capabilities to recognize specific individuals out of the box, which in turn presents safety concerns related both to dual use and disparate performance. In practice, we expect that the cost of transcription is not the limiting factor of scaling up surveillance projects.

instruction: What are the key elements of this product description?
description: Gemma is a family of lightweight, state-of-the-art open models from Google, built from the same research and technology used to create the Gemini models. Gemma 3 models are multimodal, handling text and image input and generating text output, with open weights for both pre-trained variants and instruction-tuned variants. Gemma 3 has a large, 128K context window, multilingual support in over 140 languages, and is available in more sizes than previous versions. Gemma 3 models are well-suited for a variety of text generation and image understanding tasks, including question answering, summarization, and reasoning. Their relatively small size makes it possible to deploy them in environments with limited resources such as laptops, desktops or your own cloud infrastructure, democratizing access to state of the art AI models and helping foster innovation for everyone. The input includes text strings, such as a question, a prompt, or a document to be summarized, and images normalized to 896 x 896 resolution and encoded to 256 tokens each. The total input context is 128K tokens for the 4B, 12B, and 27B sizes, and 32K tokens for the 1B size. The output is generated text in response to the input, such as an answer to a question, analysis of image content, or a summary of a document, with a total output context of 8192 tokens. These models were trained on a dataset of text data that includes a wide variety of sources. The 27B model was trained with 14 trillion tokens, the 12B model with 12 trillion tokens, the 4B model with 4 trillion tokens, and the 1B model with 2 trillion tokens. The training data includes web documents, code, mathematics, and images. The combination of these diverse data sources is crucial for training a powerful multimodal model that can handle a wide variety of different tasks and data formats. Rigorous CSAM filtering was applied at multiple stages in the data preparation process to ensure the exclusion of harmful and illegal content. Automated techniques were used to filter out certain personal information and other sensitive data from training sets. Additional methods were applied for content quality and safety in line with Google's policies. Gemma was trained using Tensor Processing Unit (TPU) hardware (TPUv4p, TPUv5p and TPUv5e). TPUs are specifically designed to handle the massive computations involved in training vision-language models (VLMs). They can speed up training considerably compared to CPUs. TPUs often come with large amounts of high-bandwidth memory, allowing for the handling of large models and batch sizes during training. TPU Pods provide a scalable solution for handling the growing complexity of large foundation models. You can distribute training across multiple TPU devices for faster and more efficient processing. In many scenarios, TPUs can provide a more cost-effective solution for training large models compared to CPU-based infrastructure, especially when considering the time and resources saved due to faster training. These advantages are aligned with Google's commitments to operate sustainably. Training was done using JAX and ML Pathways. JAX allows researchers to take advantage of the latest generation of hardware, including TPUs, for faster and more efficient training of large models. ML Pathways is Google's latest effort to build artificially intelligent systems capable of generalizing across multiple tasks. This is specially suitable for foundation models, including large language models like these ones. Together, JAX and ML Pathways are used as described in the paper about the Gemini family of models; the 'single controller' programming model of Jax and Pathways allows a single Python process to orchestrate the entire training run, dramatically simplifying the development workflow. These models were evaluated against a large collection of different datasets and metrics to cover different aspects of text generation. For reasoning and factuality, the models achieved the following results: on HellaSwag, 62.3 for 1B, 77.2 for 4B, 84.2 for 12B, and 85.6 for 27B; on BoolQ, 63.2 for 1B, 72.3 for 4B, 78.8 for 12B, and 82.4 for 27B; on PIQA, 73.8 for 1B, 79.6 for 4B, 81.8 for 12B, and 83.3 for 27B; on SocialIQA, 48.9 for 1B, 51.9 for 4B, 53.4 for 12B, and 54.9 for 27B; on TriviaQA, 39.8 for 1B, 65.8 for 4B, 78.2 for 12B, and 85.5 for 27B; on Natural Questions, 9.48 for 1B, 20.0 for 4B, 31.4 for 12B, and 36.1 for 27B; on ARC-c, 38.4 for 1B, 56.2 for 4B, 68.9 for 12B, and 70.6 for 27B; on ARC-e, 73.0 for 1B, 82.4 for 4B, 88.3 for 12B, and 89.0 for 27B; on WinoGrande, 58.2 for 1B, 64.7 for 4B, 74.3 for 12B, and 78.8 for 27B; on BIG-Bench Hard, 28.4 for 1B, 50.9 for 4B, 72.6 for 12B, and 77.7 for 27B; on DROP, 42.4 for 1B, 60.1 for 4B, 72.2 for 12B, and 77.2 for 27B. For STEM and code, the models achieved the following results: on MMLU, 59.6 for 4B, 74.5 for 12B, and 78.6 for 27B; on MMLU (Pro COT), 29.2 for 4B, 45.3 for 12B, and 52.2 for 27B; on AGIEval, 42.1 for 4B, 57.4 for 12B, and 66.2 for 27B; on MATH, 24.2 for 4B, 43.3 for 12B, and 50.0 for 27B; on GSM8K, 38.4 for 4B, 71.0 for 12B, and 82.6 for 27B; on GPQA, 15.0 for 4B, 25.4 for 12B, and 24.3 for 27B; on MBPP, 46.0 for 4B, 60.4 for 12B, and 65.6 for 27B; on HumanEval, 36.0 for 4B, 45.7 for 12B, and 48.8 for 27B. For multilingual tasks, the models achieved the following results: on MGSM, 2.04 for 1B, 34.7 for 4B, 64.3 for 12B, and 74.3 for 27B; on Global-MMLU-Lite, 24.9 for 1B, 57.0 for 4B, 69.4 for 12B, and 75.7 for 27B; on WMT24++ (ChrF), 36.7 for 1B, 48.4 for 4B, 53.9 for 12B, and 55.7 for 27B; on FloRes, 29.5 for 1B, 39.2 for 4B, 46.0 for 12B, and 48.8 for 27B; on XQuAD (all), 43.9 for 1B, 68.0 for 4B, 74.5 for 12B, and 76.8 for 27B; on ECLeKTic, 4.69 for 1B, 11.0 for 4B, 17.2 for 12B, and 24.4 for 27B; on IndicGenBench, 41.4 for 1B, 57.2 for 4B, 61.7 for 12B, and 63.4 for 27B. For multimodal tasks, the models achieved the following results: on COCOcap, 102 for 4B, 111 for 12B, and 116 for 27B; on DocVQA (val), 72.8 for 4B, 82.3 for 12B, and 85.6 for 27B; on InfoVQA (val), 44.1 for 4B, 54.8 for 12B, and 59.4 for 27B; on MMMU (pt), 39.2 for 4B, 50.3 for 12B, and 56.1 for 27B; on TextVQA (val), 58.9 for 4B, 66.5 for 12B, and 68.6 for 27B; on RealWorldQA, 45.5 for 4B, 52.2 for 12B, and 53.9 for 27B; on ReMI, 27.3 for 4B, 38.5 for 12B, and 44.8 for 27B; on AI2D, 63.2 for 4B, 75.2 for 12B, and 79.0 for 27B; on ChartQA, 63.6 for 4B, 74.7 for 12B, and 76.3 for 27B; on VQAv2, 63.9 for 4B, 71.2 for 12B, and 72.9 for 27B; on BLINK, 38.0 for 4B, 35.9 for 12B, and 39.6 for 27B; on OKVQA, 51.0 for 4B, 58.7 for 12B, and 60.2 for 27B; on TallyQA, 42.5 for 4B, 51.8 for 12B, and 54.3 for 27B; on SpatialSense VQA, 50.9 for 4B, 60.0 for 12B, and 59.4 for 27B; on CountBenchQA, 26.1 for 4B, 17.8 for 12B, and 68.0 for 27B. Our evaluation methods include structured evaluations and internal red-teaming testing of relevant content policies. Red-teaming was conducted by a number of different teams, each with different goals and human evaluation metrics. These models were evaluated against a number of different categories relevant to ethics and safety, including child safety, content safety, and representational harms. In addition to development level evaluations, we conduct 'assurance evaluations' which are our 'arms-length' internal evaluations for responsibility governance decision making. They are conducted separately from the model development team, to inform decision making about release. High level findings are fed back to the model team, but prompt sets are held-out to prevent overfitting and preserve the results' ability to inform decision making. Assurance evaluation results are reported to our Responsibility & Safety Council as part of release review. For all areas of safety testing, we saw major improvements in the categories of child safety, content safety, and representational harms relative to previous Gemma models. All testing was conducted without safety filters to evaluate the model capabilities and behaviors. For both text-to-text and image-to-text, and across all model sizes, the model produced minimal policy violations, and showed significant improvements over previous Gemma models' performance with respect to ungrounded inferences. A limitation of our evaluations was they included only English language prompts. These models have certain limitations that users should be aware of. The quality and diversity of the training data significantly influence the model's capabilities. Biases or gaps in the training data can lead to limitations in the model's responses. The scope of the training dataset determines the subject areas the model can handle effectively. Models are better at tasks that can be framed with clear prompts and instructions. Open-ended or highly complex tasks might be challenging. A model's performance can be influenced by the amount of context provided (longer context generally leads to better outputs, up to a certain point). Natural language is inherently complex. Models might struggle to grasp subtle nuances, sarcasm, or figurative language. Models generate responses based on information they learned from their training datasets, but they are not knowledge bases. They may generate incorrect or outdated factual statements. Models rely on statistical patterns in language. They might lack the ability to apply common sense reasoning in certain situations. The development of vision-language models (VLMs) raises several ethical concerns. In creating an open model, we have carefully considered the following: VLMs trained on large-scale, real-world text and image data can reflect socio-cultural biases embedded in the training material. These models underwent careful scrutiny, input data pre-processing described and posterior evaluations reported in this card. VLMs can be misused to generate text that is false, misleading, or harmful. Guidelines are provided for responsible use with the model, see the Responsible Generative AI Toolkit. This model card summarizes details on the models' architecture, capabilities, limitations, and evaluation processes. A responsibly developed open model offers the opportunity to share innovation by making VLM technology accessible to developers and researchers across the AI ecosystem. Risks identified and mitigations include: perpetuation of biases, generation of harmful content, misuse for malicious purposes, and privacy violations. It's encouraged to perform continuous monitoring (using evaluation metrics, human review) and the exploration of de-biasing techniques during model training, fine-tuning, and other use cases. Mechanisms and guidelines for content safety are essential. Developers are encouraged to exercise caution and implement appropriate content safety safeguards based on their specific product policies and application use cases. Technical limitations and developer and end-user education can help mitigate against malicious applications of VLMs. Educational resources and reporting mechanisms for users to flag misuse are provided. Prohibited uses of Gemma models are outlined in the Gemma Prohibited Use Policy. Models were trained on data filtered for removal of certain personal information and other sensitive data. Developers are encouraged to adhere to privacy regulations with privacy-preserving techniques. At the time of release, this family of models provides high-performance open vision-language model implementations designed from the ground up for responsible AI development compared to similarly sized models. Using the benchmark evaluation metrics described in this document, these models have shown to provide superior performance to other, comparably-sized open model alternatives.

instruction: What are the top 5 historical landmarks in Italy?
description: Qwen2.5 is the latest series of Qwen large language models. The Qwen2.5 series includes a range of base language models and instruction-tuned language models with parameter sizes ranging from 0.5 to 72 billion. Compared to Qwen2, Qwen2.5 offers significant improvements in knowledge, coding, and mathematics capabilities, supported by specialized expert models in these domains. It also shows enhanced instruction following, long text generation (over 8K tokens), structured data understanding (e.g., tables), and structured output generation, especially JSON. The model is more resilient to the diversity of system prompts, which improves role-play implementation and condition-setting for chatbots. It supports long-context processing up to 128K tokens and can generate up to 8K tokens. It provides multilingual support for over 29 languages, including Chinese, English, French, Spanish, Portuguese, German, Italian, Russian, Japanese, Korean, Vietnamese, Thai, and Arabic. The instruction-tuned 1.5B Qwen2.5 model is a causal language model that has undergone pretraining and post-training. It uses a transformer architecture with RoPE, SwiGLU, RMSNorm, attention QKV bias, and tied word embeddings. It has 1.54 billion parameters, 1.31 billion non-embedding parameters, 28 layers, and 12 attention heads for Q and 2 for KV. It supports a context length of 32,768 tokens and can generate up to 8192 tokens. The model is available on Hugging Face and requires the latest version of the `transformers` library to avoid errors. Detailed evaluation results and performance metrics are available in the provided documentation. If you find this work helpful, you may cite the associated publications.

instruction: Can you help me create a 3D mesh of a terrain based on a satellite image?
description: CraftsMan is a high-fidelity mesh generation model designed for 3D native generation and interactive geometry refinement. It is intended to support tasks related to the creation and refinement of 3D mesh models. The model is part of a research effort led by contributors from HKUST, LightIllusions, HKUST(GZ), and Tencent AI Lab. The model is available for use through a pipeline interface, which can be loaded from local checkpoints. It is recommended to use the model on a CUDA-enabled device with appropriate PyTorch configurations. An installation script is provided for setting up the environment, including the creation of a Conda environment and installation of required packages. A Gradio demo is also available for interactive testing of the model. The model is open-source, with inference, checkpoints, and training components available, though the ComfyUI integration is not yet provided. Users are encouraged to cite the associated research paper when using the model or repository.

instruction: Can you suggest a set of keywords that best describe this image?
description: SigLIP 2 Base extends the pretraining objective with prior, independently developed techniques into a unified recipe, for improved semantic understanding, localization, and dense features. The model can be used for tasks like zero-shot image classification and image-text retrieval, or as a vision encoder for VLMs and other vision tasks. SigLIP 2 adds training objectives such as decoder loss, global-local and masked prediction loss, and aspect ratio and resolution adaptibility. It is pre-trained on the WebLI dataset and was trained on up to 2048 TPU-v5e chips. Evaluation results are presented in the associated paper. The model is described in the research titled 'SigLIP 2: Multilingual Vision-Language Encoders with Improved Semantic Understanding, Localization, and Dense Features' by Michael Tschannen, Alexey Gritsenko, Xiao Wang, Muhammad Ferjad Naeem, Ibrahim Alabdulmohsin, Nikhil Parthasarathy, Talfan Evans, Lucas Beyer, Ye Xia, Basil Mustafa, Olivier Hénaff, Jeremiah Harmsen, Andreas Steiner, and Xiaohua Zhai, available on arXiv with the identifier 2502.14786.

instruction: Can you determine the orientation of the hand in this image?
description: Hand landmarks is a single pose estimation model designed for real-time processing and implemented in TensorFlow. The model is quantized in int8 format using the TensorFlow Lite converter. It is intended for detecting hand landmarks in images. The input to the model is a single RGB image with a resolution of NxM and UINT8 values between 0 and 255, with an input shape of (1, N, M, 3). The output consists of FLOAT values representing the (x, y, confidence) coordinates of K keypoints, with an output shape of (1, Kx3). Performance metrics indicate that the model has an internal RAM usage of 1739.5 KiB and weights flash usage of 3283.38 KiB when executed on the STM32N6 series with the COCO Person dataset. On the STM32N6570-DK board using the NPU/MCU execution engine, the model achieves an inference time of 20.75 ms, corresponding to 48.19 inferences per second. The model is supported on STM32MP1, STM32MP2, and STM32N6 platforms, with STM32MP2 and STM32N6 being recommended for use.

instruction: I want to extract the main figure from this high-resolution photo — can you help with that?
description: BiRefNet_lite is a model designed for high-resolution dichotomous image segmentation. It is trained to perform segmentation tasks on images with a resolution of 2K (2560, 1440) and is intended for general use in such settings. The model is trained on a variety of datasets, including DIS5K (excluding DIS-VD), HRS10K, UHRSD, P3M-10k (excluding TE-P3M-500-NP), TR-humans, AM-2k, AIM-500, Human-2k (synthesized with BG-20k), Distinctions-646 (synthesized with BG-20k), HIM2K, and PPM-100. High-resolution samples are selected based on the criteria that the image height and width must be at least 1440 and 2560, or greater than 80% of these dimensions. The model is validated on the DIS-VD and TE-P3M-500-NP datasets. On the DIS-VD dataset, the model achieves a maxFm of 0.867, wFmeasure of 0.831, MAE of 0.045, Smeasure of 0.879, meanEm of 0.919, HCE of 952, maxEm of 0.925, meanFm of 0.858, adpEm of 0.916, adpFm of 0.847, mBA of 0.796, maxBIoU of 0.750, and meanBIoU of 0.739. On the TE-P3M-500-NP dataset, the model achieves a maxFm of 0.993, wFmeasure of 0.986, MAE of 0.009, Smeasure of 0.975, meanEm of 0.986, HCE of 0.000, maxEm of 0.993, meanFm of 0.985, adpEm of 0.833, adpFm of 0.873, mBA of 0.825, maxBIoU of 0.921, and meanBIoU of 0.891. The model is part of the BiRefNet series and is developed by researchers from Nankai University, Northwestern Polytechnical University, National University of Defense Technology, Aalto University, Shanghai AI Laboratory, and University of Trento. The model weights are provided in this repository, and users are encouraged to refer to the main BiRefNet model repository for additional information and usage instructions. The model's development was supported by GPU resources provided by @freepik.

instruction: Could you detect the hand shape and mark the important joints?
description: Hand landmarks is a single pose estimation model designed for real-time processing and implemented in TensorFlow. The model is quantized in int8 format using the TensorFlow Lite converter. It is intended for detecting hand landmarks in images. The input to the model is a single RGB image with a resolution of NxM and UINT8 values between 0 and 255, with an input shape of (1, N, M, 3). The output consists of FLOAT values representing the (x, y, confidence) coordinates of K keypoints, with an output shape of (1, Kx3). Performance metrics indicate that the model has an internal RAM usage of 1739.5 KiB and weights flash usage of 3283.38 KiB when executed on the STM32N6 series with the COCO Person dataset. On the STM32N6570-DK board using the NPU/MCU execution engine, the model achieves an inference time of 20.75 ms, corresponding to 48.19 inferences per second. The model is supported on STM32MP1, STM32MP2, and STM32N6 platforms, with STM32MP2 and STM32N6 being recommended for use.

instruction: Could you provide a detailed evaluation of the process I used to solve this equation?
description: Qwen2.5-Math-PRM-7B is a Process Reward Model (PRM) designed for process supervision in mathematical reasoning of Large Language Models (LLMs). It aims to identify and mitigate intermediate errors in the reasoning processes. The model is used to provide feedback on the quality of reasoning and intermediate steps rather than for generation. It is part of a series that includes Qwen2.5-Math-PRM-72B and is released alongside the mathematical Outcome Reward Model (ORM) Qwen2.5-Math-RM-72B. The model is trained to exhibit strong performance in the Best-of-N (BoN) evaluation and in error identification. For use, it is recommended to separate individual steps within a solution using double line breaks and to insert a special token for reward computation after each step. The reward value is derived from the probability score of this token being classified as positive, resulting in a value between 0 and 1. The model requires the `transformers` library version 4.40.0 or higher, as the Qwen2.5 code was integrated starting from version 4.37.0. The model is typically used in conjunction with the Qwen2.5-Math-Instruct model. The model is evaluated and described in the paper titled 'The Lessons of Developing Process Reward Models in Mathematical Reasoning' by Zhenru Zhang, Chujie Zheng, Yangzhen Wu, Beichen Zhang, Runji Lin, Bowen Yu, Dayiheng Liu, Jingren Zhou, and Junyang Lin, available as an arXiv preprint arXiv:2501.07301.

instruction: Could you generate a fantasy creature image based on the outline I've drawn?
description: ControlNet v1.1 is a neural network structure designed to control diffusion models by adding extra conditions. This version is specifically conditioned on Canny edges, which are represented as a monochrome image with white edges on a black background. ControlNet was proposed by Lvmin Zhang and Maneesh Agrawala. The model is intended to be used in combination with Stable Diffusion and is a diffusion-based text-to-image generation model. It was developed to support additional input conditions in a task-specific manner, with the learning process being robust even when the training dataset is small. The Canny version of ControlNet v1.1 was trained to address several issues present in the previous version, including duplicated grayscale human images, low-quality or blurry images with JPEG artifacts, and incorrect paired prompts. The training dataset was improved, and the model was trained on a machine with 8 Nvidia A100 80G GPUs for 3 days with a batch size of 256, costing 2160 USD. The model was resumed from Canny 1.0 and includes data augmentations such as random left-right flipping. Although evaluating ControlNet is challenging, Canny 1.1 is reported to be slightly more robust and to produce slightly higher visual quality than Canny 1.0.

instruction: Can you identify if the person is feeling surprised or shocked in this short audio?
description: This model implements categorical emotion classification as described in the paper 'Vox-Profile: A Speech Foundation Model Benchmark for Characterizing Diverse Speaker and Speech Traits'. The training pipeline used is the top-performing solution (SAILER) in the INTERSPEECH 2025—Speech Emotion Challenge. However, compared to the official challenge submission system, this model does not use all the augmentations and does not use the transcript, instead creating a speech-only system to make the model simple but still effective. The model is trained on the MSP-Podcast data and is designed to classify speech into the following emotions: Anger, Contempt, Disgust, Fear, Happiness, Neutral, Sadness, Surprise, and Other. The model may be sensitive to content information when making emotion predictions, which could be a beneficial feature for classifying emotions from online content. The model is based on the Whisper-Large V3 architecture and is available through Huggingface under the identifier 'tiantiaf/whisper-large-v3-msp-podcast-emotion'. It is intended for use with audio files that are a maximum of 15 seconds in length, sampled at 16kHz and in mono channel format. The model is released under the Open RAIL license, and users are required to respect the privacy and consent of data subjects and adhere to relevant laws and regulations in their jurisdictions. The model is not intended for clinical or diagnostic applications, surveillance, privacy-invasive applications, or commercial use.

instruction: Can you provide a summary of this news story about recent political developments?
description: SummLlama3.2-3B is a text summarization model initialized from Llama3.2-3B-Instruct and further trained using Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) based on large-scale summarization feedback. The training data includes over 100K input documents spanning a wide range of lengths and formats, including both dialogue and non-dialogue texts, across seven distinct domains: four non-dialogue domains (News, Lifestyle, Report, Medical) and three dialogue domains (Daily Life, Interview, Meeting). The model is designed to generate human-preferred summaries that excel in three key aspects: faithfulness, completeness, and conciseness. Faithfulness ensures that the summary does not manipulate or add information not directly inferable from the input. Completeness ensures that all key information from the input is included in the summary. Conciseness ensures that the summary remains focused and avoids unnecessary details. Automated evaluation results show that SummLlama3.2-3B achieves a faithfulness score of 0.867, completeness of 0.598, and conciseness of 0.686, with an average score of 0.717, compared to 0.613 for Llama3.2-3B-Instruct. Human evaluation results also indicate that the model outperforms the original Llama3 series in summary quality. The model is recommended to be used with a specific prompt format that includes an instruction to summarize the input document. This model is part of the SummLlama3.2-Series, which includes variants such as SummLlama3.2-3B and SummLlama3.2-70B. The model is trained to produce summaries that are more aligned with human preferences by leveraging high-quality, multi-dimensional, and fine-grained feedback generated by large language models rather than relying on expensive human feedback.

instruction: How does the training batch size of 4 affect the learning process?
description: This model is a fine-tuned version of an unspecified base model on an unknown dataset. It achieves a loss of 5.1124 on the evaluation set. The model was trained using the following hyperparameters: learning rate of 5e-05, training batch size of 4, evaluation batch size of 8, random seed of 42, Adam optimizer with betas of (0.9, 0.999) and epsilon of 1e-08, linear learning rate scheduler, and 20 training epochs. During training, the model's training loss and validation loss were recorded at various steps and epochs. The training process was implemented using Transformers 4.44.2, PyTorch 2.5.0+cu121, Datasets 3.1.0, and Tokenizers 0.19.1. More information is needed regarding the model's intended uses, limitations, and the training and evaluation data.

instruction: Explain the difference between supervised and unsupervised learning in machine learning.
description: Qwen3 is the latest generation of large language models in the Qwen series, offering a comprehensive suite of dense and mixture-of-experts (MoE) models. Built upon extensive training, Qwen3 delivers groundbreaking advancements in reasoning, instruction-following, agent capabilities, and multilingual support. It uniquely supports seamless switching between thinking mode, for complex logical reasoning, math, and coding, and non-thinking mode, for efficient, general-purpose dialogue, within a single model. This ensures optimal performance across various scenarios. Qwen3 significantly enhances its reasoning capabilities, surpassing previous QwQ (in thinking mode) and Qwen2.5 instruct models (in non-thinking mode) on mathematics, code generation, and commonsense logical reasoning. It demonstrates superior human preference alignment, excelling in creative writing, role-playing, multi-turn dialogues, and instruction following, to deliver a more natural, engaging, and immersive conversational experience. The model exhibits expertise in agent capabilities, enabling precise integration with external tools in both thinking and unthinking modes and achieving leading performance among open-source models in complex agent-based tasks. It supports 100+ languages and dialects with strong capabilities for multilingual instruction following and translation. The Qwen3-1.7B variant is a causal language model with 1.7 billion parameters, of which 1.4 billion are non-embedding parameters. It has 28 layers and uses 16 attention heads for queries and 8 for key-value pairs. The model supports a context length of 32,768 tokens. For optimal performance, it is recommended to use specific sampling parameters depending on the mode: for thinking mode, use Temperature=0.6, TopP=0.95, TopK=20, and MinP=0, and avoid greedy decoding. For non-thinking mode, use Temperature=0.7, TopP=0.8, TopK=20, and MinP=0. Adjusting the presence_penalty parameter between 0 and 2 can help reduce endless repetitions, though higher values may occasionally result in language mixing and a slight decrease in model performance. Adequate output length is recommended, with 32,768 tokens for most queries and up to 38,912 tokens for highly complex problems. Standardizing output formats is advised, especially for math problems and multiple-choice questions. In multi-turn conversations, the historical model output should only include the final output part and not the thinking content. The model can be deployed using frameworks such as SGLang or vLLM, and is supported by applications like Ollama, LMStudio, MLX-LM, llama.cpp, and KTransformers. Users can switch between thinking and non-thinking modes via the enable_thinking parameter or by using /think and /no_think tags in user input. The model is designed to work with the Qwen-Agent framework for agentic use, which encapsulates tool-calling templates and parsers to reduce coding complexity. The model is available on Hugging Face and can be used with the latest version of the transformers library. For API compatibility, when enable_thinking is set to True, the model will always output a block wrapped in a specific format, though the content may be empty if thinking is disabled. When enable_thinking is set to False, the model will not generate any think content or include the block. The model is suitable for a wide range of tasks, including but not limited to, complex logical reasoning, mathematics, code generation, creative writing, role-playing, multi-turn dialogues, instruction following, and multilingual instruction following and translation. It is recommended to use the provided chat template in Jinja2 to ensure best practices are followed, especially for frameworks that do not directly use the Jinja2 chat template.

instruction: Explain the principles of quantum computing in a way that’s easy to understand.
description: Qwen2.5 is the latest series of Qwen large language models. The Qwen2.5 series includes a range of base language models and instruction-tuned language models with parameter sizes ranging from 0.5 to 72 billion. Qwen2.5 introduces several improvements over Qwen2, including significantly more knowledge and enhanced capabilities in coding and mathematics, achieved through specialized expert models in these domains. It also shows significant improvements in instruction following, generating long texts (over 8K tokens), understanding structured data such as tables, and generating structured outputs, especially JSON. The model is more resilient to the diversity of system prompts, which enhances role-play implementation and condition-setting for chatbots. It supports long-context processing up to 128K tokens and can generate up to 8K tokens. The model supports over 29 languages, including Chinese, English, French, Spanish, Portuguese, German, Italian, Russian, Japanese, Korean, Vietnamese, Thai, and Arabic. This specific model is the instruction-tuned 32B version of Qwen2.5. It is a causal language model trained through pretraining and post-training. The model architecture is based on transformers with RoPE, SwiGLU, RMSNorm, and Attention QKV bias. It has 32.5B total parameters, with 31.0B non-embedding parameters. The model has 64 layers and uses 40 attention heads for queries and 8 for keys and values. It supports a full context length of 131,072 tokens and can generate up to 8192 tokens. The model requires the latest version of the Hugging Face `transformers` library for compatibility. Older versions of `transformers` (before 4.37.0) may result in a KeyError: 'qwen2'. To process long texts beyond 32,768 tokens, a technique called YaRN is used to enhance model length extrapolation. YaRN can be enabled by adding a specific configuration to the `config.json` file. For deployment, vLLM is recommended, although it currently only supports static YARN, which may impact performance on shorter texts. The model has been evaluated, and detailed results are available in a separate document. The model's performance is influenced by GPU memory and throughput, which are also documented separately. If used, the model should be cited using the provided references.

instruction: I'm working on a project that requires comparing two sentences for similarity—what's a good way to do this?
description: Ettin is an open suite of paired encoder-only and decoder-only models trained with identical data, architecture, and training recipes. The models are designed to enable fair comparisons between encoder and decoder architectures across multiple scales, providing state-of-the-art performance for open-data models in their respective size categories. Ettin models use identical training data, matched architectures (differing only in attention patterns and training objectives), and a consistent training recipe across all models. The training data is publicly available and includes a high-quality mixture of diverse sources totaling 2T+ tokens, with phases including pre-training, mid-training, and decay. The model family includes encoder and decoder models with parameter sizes ranging from 17M to 1B, as well as cross-objective models that demonstrate the results of continuing training encoders as decoders and vice versa. Ettin supports a variety of tasks, including classification, embeddings, text generation, and retrieval. Performance highlights show that Ettin encoders outperform ModernBERT on GLUE and MTEB tasks, while Ettin decoders outperform SmolLM2 and Llama 3.2 on generative tasks. The models are based on a Transformer architecture with RoPE, GLU activations, and prenorm layers, and support context lengths up to 8K tokens. Training checkpoints are available for research and analysis, enabling detailed study of training dynamics and capability emergence. Ettin is intended for research applications such as architecture studies, training dynamics analysis, scaling law investigations, and replication studies. All training artifacts, including data, model weights, and hyperparameters, are publicly available. Caveats include the need to load cross-objective models using the architecture they were converted to, not their original architecture.

instruction: Could you summarize the key findings presented in the graph?
description: The H2OVL-Mississippi-800M is a compact yet powerful vision-language model developed by H2O.ai, featuring 0.8 billion parameters. Despite its relatively small size, it achieves state-of-the-art performance in text recognition, particularly excelling in the Text Recognition segment of OCRBench and outperforming larger models in this domain. The model is built upon the architecture of H2O-Danube language models, extending their capabilities by integrating vision and language tasks. It is trained on 19 million image-text pairs, with a focus on OCR, document comprehension, and the interpretation of charts, figures, and tables. The model is optimized for superior OCR performance and is suitable for OCR and document processing due to the balance it achieves between performance and efficiency. The model is designed to support a range of tasks, including text recognition, document comprehension, and the interpretation of visual data such as charts and tables. It can be used for both pure-text conversations and image-based queries. The model includes a disclaimer that acknowledges potential biases and limitations in the generated content, as well as ethical considerations for its use. Users are encouraged to use the model responsibly and to report any issues with the generated content.

instruction: I want to remove the background from a portrait. Can you assist with that?
description: BiRefNet is a model designed for high-resolution dichotomous image segmentation, specifically for general matting tasks. It is trained on a variety of datasets, including P3M-10k (excluding TE-P3M-500-NP), TR-humans, AM-2k, AIM-500, Human-2k (synthesized with BG-20k), Distinctions-646 (synthesized with BG-20k), HIM2K, and PPM-100. The model is validated on the TE-P3M-500-NP dataset. On this validation set, BiRefNet achieves an Smeasure of 0.979, a maxFm of 0.996, a meanEm of 0.988, an MSE of 0.003, a maxEm of 0.997, a meanFm of 0.986, a wFmeasure of 0.988, an adpEm of 0.864, an adpFm of 0.885, an HCE of 0.000, an mBA of 0.830, a maxBIoU of 0.940, and a meanBIoU of 0.888. The model is developed by researchers from Nankai University, Northwestern Polytechnical University, National University of Defense Technology, Aalto University, Shanghai AI Laboratory, and University of Trento. The authors acknowledge the support of @freepik for providing GPU resources for training the model. The model is described in the paper 'Bilateral Reference for High-Resolution Dichotomous Image Segmentation' published in CAAI Artificial Intelligence Research, volume 3, page 9150038, in 2024.

instruction: I need a voice message of a birthday greeting with a warm and personal tone—can you help?
description: Orpheus-TTS-Local is a lightweight client designed to run locally using the LM Studio API. It provides high-quality Text-to-Speech functionality using the Orpheus TTS model, eliminating the need for cloud API keys. The model supports multiple voice options, including tara, leah, jess, leo, dan, mia, zac, and zoe. Users can save generated audio as WAV files. The model allows customization through parameters such as temperature, top-p sampling, and repetition penalty. The default voice is tara, which is recommended for general use. Emotion can be added to the speech by using specific tags. The model is distributed under the Apache 2.0 license.

instruction: Create a dense vector for the sentence 'What are the effects of sleep deprivation?' for similarity-based analysis.
description: This is a model finetuned from Alibaba-NLP/gte-modernbert-base on a dataset. It maps sentences and paragraphs to a 768-dimensional dense vector space and can be used for semantic textual similarity for the purpose of semantic caching. The model is a Sentence Transformer with a maximum sequence length of 8192 tokens and uses cosine similarity as the similarity function. The model architecture includes a Transformer with ModernBertModel and a Pooling layer that uses the cls_token with a word embedding dimension of 768. The model was trained on the Quora dataset, which contains 323,491 training samples with columns question_1, question_2, and label. It was evaluated on the Quora dataset, which contains 53,486 evaluation samples with the same columns. The model achieves a cosine accuracy of 0.90, cosine f1 of 0.87, cosine precision of 0.84, cosine recall of 0.90, and cosine average precision of 0.92. The model is intended for use with the Sentence Transformers library and can be loaded from the Hugging Face Hub. The model is described in the paper titled 'Advancing Semantic Caching for LLMs with Domain-Specific Embeddings and Synthetic Data' by Gill, Cechmanek, Hutcherson, Rajamohan, Agarwal, Gulzar, Singh, and Dion, published in 2025.

instruction: Can you explain the meaning behind the colloquial phrases used in this Singaporean Tamil audio?
description: MERaLiON-2 is a family of speech-text large language models designed for Singapore’s multilingual and multicultural context, as well as the wider Southeast Asian region. The flagship model, MERaLiON-2-10B, integrates a localized Whisper-large-v3 speech encoder and a Gemma-2-9b text decoder. It is finetuned on 120,000 hours of speech and audio data across six diverse tasks: Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR), Spoken Question Answering (SQA), Spoken Dialogue Summarization (SDS), Audio Captioning (AC), Audio-Scene Question Answering (ASQA), and Paralinguistic Question Answering (PQA). The model supports audio inputs up to 300 seconds (5 minutes) and is adapted to handle linguistic nuances, accents, and dialects common in Singapore and neighboring countries. MERaLiON-2-10B-ASR is an ASR-optimized variant that delivers a 5–30% performance improvement over OpenAI’s whisper-large-v3 on speech recognition tasks for Singapore’s four official languages—English, Mandarin, Malay, and Tamil—as well as Indonesian, Thai, and Vietnamese. The model also demonstrates robust handling of code-switching scenarios and local colloquialisms. MERaLiON-2-3B is a lightweight variant that balances performance with reduced computational requirements. The model supports a range of tasks, including transcription, translation, summarization, audio captioning, emotion recognition, gender recognition, and speech translation. It is trained to follow complex instructions and provides detailed explanations regarding speech content and speaker emotions. MERaLiON-2-10B exhibits competitive performance in benchmark evaluations, with results comparable to other state-of-the-art open-source AudioLLMs. The model is not intended for use in tool calling, math, and coding tasks. It is trained on a global batch size of 768 for around 200k steps using 16 nodes and 128 H100 GPUs. The model has not been specifically aligned for safety and may generate inappropriate, offensive, or harmful content. Developers and users are responsible for performing their own safety fine-tuning and implementing necessary security measures.

instruction: Can you identify all the vehicles in this image?
description: MM Grounding DINO (large variant) was proposed by Xiangyu Zhao, Yicheng Chen, Shilin Xu, Xiangtai Li, Xinjiang Wang, Yining Li, and Haian Huang. It improves upon previous models by enhancing the contrastive class head and removing parameter sharing in the decoder, which leads to improved zero-shot detection performance. Specifically, it achieves 50.6 AP (an increase of 2.2) on COCO and 31.9 val AP (an increase of 11.8) and 41.4 minival AP (an increase of 12.6) on LVIS. The model is intended for zero-shot object detection and can be used with the provided code example. It was trained on a combination of O365V2, OpenImageV6, and GoldG datasets. Evaluation results show that the model achieves 53.0 COCO mAP with a Swin-L backbone and 41.4 minival AP with a Swin-T backbone when trained on O365, GoldG, GRIT, and V3Det. The model is available in the specified collection, and all original checkpoints can be found there.

instruction: Как будет на русском 'Сәуір айында Қазақстандағы барлық қалаларда жаңбыр жауды.'?
description: kazRush-kk-ru is a translation model for translating from Kazakh to Russian. The model was trained with randomly initialized weights based on the T5 configuration on available open-source parallel data. The model uses the sentencepiece library for tokenization. It supports translation tasks and can be used via direct model inference or through a pipeline wrapper. The model was trained on Russian-Kazakh language pairs totaling 718K, 2,150K, 5,063K, and 4,403K pairs. Data preprocessing included deduplication, removing trash symbols, special tags, and multiple whitespaces from texts, removing texts not in Russian or Kazakh, removing pairs with low alignment scores, and filtering using specific tools. The model was trained for 56 hours on 2 GPUs NVIDIA A100 80 Gb. Evaluation was performed by comparing the model to other open-source translation models, excluding nllb-moe-54b due to its size. Metrics such as BLEU, chrF, and COMET were calculated on the devtest part of a recent evaluation benchmark for multilingual machine translation. The model achieved a BLEU score of 18.8, a chrF score of 48.7, and a COMET score of 86.7. The model has a size of 197M parameters. Examples of usage include translating sentences such as 'Балық көбінесе сулардағы токсиндердің жоғары концентрацияларына байланысты өледі.' to 'Рыба часто умирает из-за высоких концентраций токсинов в воде.' and 'Өткен 3 айда 80-нен астам қамалушы ресми түрде айып тағылмастан изолятордан шығарылды.' to 'За прошедшие 3 месяца более 80 арестованных были официально извлечены из изолятора без обвинения.' and 'Бұл тастардың он бесі өткен шілде айындағы метеориттік жаңбырға жатқызылады.' to 'Пятнадцать этих камней относят к метеоритным дождям прошлого июля.' The model is available on Hugging Face and was developed by Anna Lebedeva and Andrey Sokolov.

instruction: Please identify the key elements in this document and describe their relationship.
description: InternVL3 is an advanced multimodal large language model (MLLM) series that demonstrates superior overall performance. Compared to InternVL 2.5, InternVL3 exhibits superior multimodal perception and reasoning capabilities, while further extending its multimodal capabilities to encompass tool usage, GUI agents, industrial image analysis, 3D vision perception, and more. InternVL3 integrates newly incrementally pre-trained InternViT with various pre-trained LLMs, including InternLM 3 and Qwen 2.5, using a randomly initialized MLP projector. The model retains the same architecture as its predecessors, following the 'ViT-MLP-LLM' paradigm. A pixel unshuffle operation is applied to reduce the number of visual tokens to one-quarter of the original, and a dynamic resolution strategy divides images into tiles of 448×448 pixels. InternVL3 introduces support for multi-image and video data, and utilizes V2PE, which employs smaller, more flexible position increments for visual tokens, enhancing long context understanding. The model is trained using a Native Multimodal Pre-Training approach that interleaves multimodal data with large-scale textual corpora, allowing the model to learn both linguistic and multimodal representations simultaneously. Supervised Fine-Tuning in InternVL3 uses higher-quality and more diverse training data, including samples for tool use, 3D scene understanding, GUI operations, long context tasks, video understanding, scientific diagrams, creative writing, and multimodal reasoning. Mixed Preference Optimization (MPO) is employed to align the model response distribution with the ground-truth distribution, improving reasoning performance. The training objective of MPO combines preference loss, quality loss, and generation loss. Test-Time Scaling is used to enhance reasoning abilities, employing a Best-of-N evaluation strategy with a critic model to select the best response. InternVL3 achieves better overall text performance than the Qwen2.5 series due to Native Multimodal Pre-Training. Evaluation results show that models fine-tuned with MPO demonstrate superior reasoning performance across seven multimodal reasoning benchmarks. The introduction of V2PE leads to significant performance gains across most evaluation metrics. InternVL3 supports a wide range of tasks, including multimodal reasoning and mathematics, OCR, chart and document understanding, multi-image and real-world comprehension, visual grounding, multilingual understanding, video understanding, GUI grounding, and spatial reasoning. The model can be loaded and used with the Transformers library, supporting various configurations including 16-bit and 8-bit quantization, and multi-GPU setups. It can handle pure-text conversations, single-image and multi-image conversations, batch inference, and video multi-round conversations. InternVL3 is compatible with LMDeploy for deployment, supporting multi-image inference, batch prompts, multi-turn conversations, and RESTful API services. The project is released under the MIT License and uses the pre-trained Qwen2.5, which is licensed under the Qwen License.

instruction: I need to know the product ID from this order confirmation. Can you extract it?
description: This model is a fine-tuned version of an unspecified base model on an unknown dataset. It is designed to perform document question-answering tasks. The model was trained using the following hyperparameters: learning rate of 5e-05, training batch size of 4, evaluation batch size of 8, random seed of 42, AdamW optimizer with betas of (0.9, 0.999) and epsilon of 1e-08, linear learning rate scheduler, and 20 training epochs. During training, the model achieved a final training loss of 0.0037 at epoch 19.9115 and step 4500, with a corresponding validation loss of 4.6448. The model was developed using Transformers 4.46.3, PyTorch 2.5.1+cu124, Datasets 3.1.0, and Tokenizers 0.20.3. More information is needed regarding the model's intended uses, limitations, and training and evaluation data.

instruction: Convert this story into audio with a natural-sounding American male voice.
description: Kokoro is a text-to-speech (TTS) model with 82 million parameters, designed to convert text input into audio output. It supports multiple voices with different nationalities and genders, including American and British accents for both male and female voices. The model is available in various quantized and precision formats, such as fp32, fp16, q8, q4, and mixed precision, with file sizes ranging from 86 MB to 326 MB. Kokoro can be used in JavaScript and Python environments, with libraries like kokoro-js providing methods to generate speech from text and save the output as audio files. The model requires tokenized input, which is processed through an inference session for audio generation. It is important to note that the model has a context length of 512 tokens, with padding required at the start and end of the input. The model is described as a frontier TTS model for its size.

instruction: Can you identify the project number mentioned in this report?
description: This model is a fine-tuned version of an unspecified base model, trained on an unknown dataset. It is designed to perform document question-answering tasks. The model was trained using the following hyperparameters: a learning rate of 5e-05, a training batch size of 4, an evaluation batch size of 8, a random seed of 42, the Adam optimizer with betas of (0.9, 0.999) and epsilon of 1e-08, a linear learning rate scheduler, and a total of 20 training epochs. During training, the model achieved a final training loss of 0.0232 at epoch 19.9115 and step 4500. On the evaluation set, the model achieved a final validation loss of 4.6669. The model was developed using the Transformers 4.44.2 library, PyTorch 2.4.1+cu121, Datasets 3.0.1, and Tokenizers 0.19.1. More information is needed regarding the model's intended uses, limitations, and the specific training and evaluation data used.

instruction: Make a high-quality image of a person with a mustache and a vintage-style outfit.
description: This model is a denoising diffusion probabilistic model (DDPM) trained on the CelebA HQ dataset at a resolution of 256x256. It is based on the original `google/ddpm-celebahq-256` implementation and has been updated to support the safetensors format for model storage. The model type is `UNet2DModel`, and it uses the DDPM diffusion process. The model is pretrained on the CelebA HQ dataset and is designed for high-quality image generation. The model weights are available in the safetensors format, which is a safer and more efficient format. The model can be loaded using the `diffusers` library from Hugging Face in safetensors format. The model supports discrete noise schedulers such as DDPM, DDIM, and PNDM for inference. The DDPM scheduler yields the highest quality but takes the longest to run, while the DDIM and PNDM schedulers offer a good trade-off between quality and inference speed. The model can be used to generate images by initializing the model and scheduler, creating an image with Gaussian noise, and performing a reverse diffusion process. The model weights are stored in the safetensors format, and the model configuration, scheduler configuration, and environment file are available for download. The model is provided under a license, and if used in research or projects, the original `google/ddpm-celebahq-256` repository should be cited.

instruction: Where in this paper is the hypothesis clearly stated?
description: ColPali is a model based on a novel architecture and training strategy using Vision Language Models (VLMs) to efficiently index documents from their visual features. It generates multi-vector representations of text and images. The model was introduced in a paper and first released in a version trained with a batch size of 256 for 3 epochs on the same data as the original ColPali model. This version is trained with right padding for queries to fix unwanted tokens in the query encoding and stems from the fixed `vidore/colpaligemma-3b-pt-448-base` to guarantee deterministic projection layer initialization. It was trained for 5 epochs, with in-batch negatives and hard mined negatives, and a warmup of 1000 steps to help reduce non-English language collapse. The data used is the same as the ColPali data described in the paper. The model is built iteratively starting from an off-the-shelf model. It is fine-tuned to create and fed the patch-embeddings output by SigLIP to an LLM to create. One benefit of inputting image patch embeddings through a language model is that they are natively mapped to a latent space similar to textual input (query). This enables leveraging the strategy to compute interactions between text tokens and image patches, which enables a step-change improvement in performance compared to BiPali. The training dataset consists of 127,460 query-page pairs, including 63% from openly available academic datasets and 37% from a synthetic dataset made up of pages from web-crawled PDF documents augmented with VLM-generated (Claude-3 Sonnet) pseudo-questions. The training set is fully English by design, enabling the study of zero-shot generalization to non-English languages. No multi-page PDF document is used both in the training and validation sets to prevent evaluation contamination. A validation set is created with 2% of the samples to tune hyperparameters. Multilingual data is present in the pretraining corpus of the language model (Gemma-2B) and may occur during PaliGemma-3B's multimodal training. All models are trained for 1 epoch on the train set in bfloat16 format, using low-rank adapters with alpha=32 and r=32 on the transformer layers from the language model, as well as the final randomly initialized projection layer, and the paged_adamw_8bit optimizer. Training is done on an 8 GPU setup with data parallelism, a learning rate of 5e-5 with linear decay and 2.5% warmup steps, and a batch size of 32. The model primarily focuses on PDF-type documents and high-resource languages, potentially limiting its generalization to other document types or less represented languages. It relies on multi-vector retrieving derived from the ColBERT late interaction mechanism, which may require engineering efforts to adapt to widely used vector retrieval frameworks that lack native multi-vector support. The vision language backbone model (PaliGemma) is under the Gemma license, while the adapters attached to the model are under the MIT license.

instruction: Can you assess whether this statement relates to defense or education policy?
description: This is an xlm-roberta-large model that has been fine-tuned on Norwegian training data containing texts from the 'party' domain, labeled according to the Comparative Agendas Project's master codebook. The model is designed for text classification tasks, specifically for identifying major policy topic codes within political texts. It supports the classification of text into 21 CAP major policy topic codes, with an additional label '999' for texts that contain no relevant policy content. The model's architecture is based on the XLM-RoBERTa large transformer model. It was evaluated on a test set of 21,915 examples, achieving an overall accuracy of 0.83. Performance metrics per label include precision, recall, and F1-score, with values ranging from 0.70 to 0.95 across different labels. The model requires a token for access due to gated access restrictions. It is used by an open-source and free natural language processing tool designed to support comparative research projects. Model performance can be improved by expanding training data, and contributions of CAP-coded corpora are welcomed. The model uses the sentencepiece tokenizer, and users may need to install it manually for versions of the Transformers library prior to 4.27. If a RuntimeError occurs when loading the model, adding the parameter 'ignore_mismatched_sizes=True' may resolve the issue.

instruction: What are the best practices for optimizing MySQL performance on Linux?
description: OZGURLUK-GPT-LinuxGeneral is a fine-tuned language model developed specifically to assist with Linux system administration, network management, and database troubleshooting. It was trained using a custom dataset focused on technical tasks, enabling it to interpret Linux commands and provide useful solutions. The model is suitable for tasks such as server troubleshooting, network configuration, and database management, offering step-by-step guidance. It is available in three formats: 4-bit, 8-bit, and 16-bit. The 8-bit version balances performance and memory usage, while the 16-bit version provides higher accuracy and precision for more demanding tasks. The 4-bit version is a lightweight alternative optimized for low-resource environments. The model also supports the HUGG format (GGUF), which reduces memory footprint and enables fast load times. The dataset used for fine-tuning was developed internally, gathering knowledge from various sources and internet databases. It contains 56,466 question-answer pairs covering a broad range of technical domains, including Kubernetes, Linux, PostgreSQL, Docker, and others. The dataset is organized under different 'ticker' headings, grouping similar questions and answers within each domain. This structure allows the model to better understand the nuances of technical problem-solving and provide relevant, tailored responses. The model is optimized for Linux, trained with a dataset of Linux system commands, troubleshooting solutions, and network configurations. It supports multiple formats, allowing users to choose between 4-bit, 8-bit, 16-bit, or HUGG based on their hardware. It was trained using Unsloth and TRL, achieving faster training and efficient inference. The base model used for fine-tuning is meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, and the fine-tuning method is LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation). The training was conducted on Google Colab with A100 GPUs.

instruction: I need a list of things present in this image — can you help?
description: LLMDet (tiny variant) was proposed by Shenghao Fu, Qize Yang, Qijie Mo, Junkai Yan, Xihan Wei, Jingke Meng, Xiaohua Xie, and Wei-Shi Zheng. LLMDet improves upon previous models by co-training with a large language model. The model can be used for zero-shot object detection. The checkpoints provided are for inference only and do not include the large language model used during training. Inference is performed in the same manner as other similar models. The model was trained on a combination of datasets including O365, GoldG, GRIT, V3Det, O365V2, OpenImageV6, and GroundingCap-1M. Evaluation results on LVIS show the following performance metrics: for one variant, MiniVal APr is 44.7, MiniVal APc is 37.3, MiniVal APf is 39.5, MiniVal AP is 50.7, Val1.0 APr is 34.9, Val1.0 APc is 26.0, Val1.0 APf is 30.1, and Val1.0 AP is 44.3. For another variant, MiniVal APr is 48.3, MiniVal APc is 40.8, MiniVal APf is 43.1, MiniVal AP is 54.3, Val1.0 APr is 38.5, Val1.0 APc is 28.2, Val1.0 APf is 34.3, and Val1.0 AP is 47.8. For a third variant, MiniVal APr is 51.1, MiniVal APc is 45.1, MiniVal APf is 46.1, MiniVal AP is 56.6, Val1.0 APr is 42.0, Val1.0 APc is 31.6, Val1.0 APf is 38.8, and Val1.0 AP is 50.2.

instruction: I need to search for documents in Spanish and Chinese—can you assist with that?
description: This is a learned sparse retrieval model designed for efficient search relevance and retrieval. It encodes documents into 105879-dimensional sparse vectors. For queries, it uses a tokenizer and a weight look-up table to generate sparse vectors. In this representation, the non-zero dimension index corresponds to a token in the vocabulary, and the weight indicates the token's importance. The similarity score between a query and a document is calculated as the inner product of their sparse vectors. The model is intended to be used with OpenSearch's neural sparse feature, which supports learned sparse retrieval using a Lucene inverted index. The model is evaluated on the MIRACL benchmark, excluding Thai due to limitations with the uncased backbone. It achieves an average NDCG@10 score of 0.629 and an average FLOPS of 1.3. When using a prune_ratio of 0.1, the model maintains a high NDCG@10 score of 0.626 with reduced FLOPS of 0.8 and a smaller embedding size of 75. The model is multilingual and supports a range of languages including Bengali, Telugu, Spanish, French, Indonesian, Hindi, Russian, Arabic, Chinese, Persian, Japanese, Finnish, Swahili, Korean, and English. It is recommended to use the model with max_ratio pruning for optimal performance. The model is available on the HuggingFace Hub and can be used with Sentence Transformers or directly with the HuggingFace Transformers library. The model has 160 million parameters and is inference-free for retrieval. The model includes an IDF (inverse document frequency) file for weighting query tokens, which is used during encoding. Special tokens are excluded from the sparse vector during post-processing. The model is designed to be used with OpenSearch's high-level API for indexing and search, but it can also be used outside the cluster with HuggingFace's API. The model is open source and licensed under an unspecified license. Copyright is held by OpenSearch Contributors.

instruction: Could you identify the date and the provider's name from this medical quote?
description: EraX-VL-2B-V1.5 is a multimodal model designed for Optical Character Recognition (OCR) and Visual Question Answering (VQA) across multiple languages, with a particular emphasis on Vietnamese. It is capable of accurately recognizing and extracting information from a wide range of documents, including medical forms, invoices, bills of sale, quotes, and medical records. This functionality is intended to support applications in hospitals, clinics, and insurance companies. The model is based on a high-quality, Vietnamese-fluent foundation and has been fine-tuned to improve performance. EraX-VL-2B-V1.5 is a small model with over 2 billion parameters and is capable of multi-turn Q&A with reasonable reasoning. It is not an OCR-only tool like Tesseract but a Multimodal LLM-based model, requiring careful prompt engineering for effective use. It has not been fine-tuned on medical X-ray or car accident datasets, and future versions are planned. EraX-VL-2B-V1.5 is part of the EraX's LànhGPT collection of LLM models. It is developed by Nguyễn Anh Nguyên, Nguyễn Hồ Nam, Phạm Huỳnh Nhật, and Phạm Đình Thục, and funded by EraX. The model is licensed under Apache 2.0 and is primarily focused on Vietnamese with multilingual capabilities. In benchmark tests, EraX-VL-2B-V1.5 achieved a score of 38.2 on the VI-MTVQA leaderboard, outperforming several other open-source and commercial models. The model is intended for use in a variety of document processing and information extraction tasks, and API access can be requested by contacting nguyen@erax.ai.

instruction: How can I create a real-time overhead view of a car's surroundings using onboard cameras?
description: Simple-Bev is a machine learning model designed to generate a bird's eye view representation from sensors, such as cameras, mounted on a vehicle. It uses ResNet-101 as the backbone and segnet as a segmentation model for specific use cases. The model is optimized for mobile deployment and is intended for use on Qualcomm® devices. The model checkpoint is model-000025000.pth, with an input resolution of 448 x 800 and 49.7 million parameters. The model size in float format is 190 MB. Performance metrics across various devices include inference times ranging from 1685.256 ms to 3351.137 ms and peak memory usage between 1242 - 1729 MB, with CPU as the primary compute unit. The model can be installed via pip using the package qai-hub-models. It supports running on cloud-hosted Qualcomm® devices through the Qualcomm® AI Hub, which requires an API token for configuration. A demo is available to run the model on a sample input, including pre-processing, model inference, and post-processing. The model can be compiled for on-device deployment using PyTorch's jit.trace and the submit_compile_job API. Performance profiling and on-device accuracy verification can be conducted using the Qualcomm® AI Hub. The compiled model can be deployed on Android using TensorFlow Lite (.tflite) or QNN (.so) runtimes. More information on model performance across devices and deployment instructions is available through the Qualcomm® AI Hub. The license for the original implementation and compiled assets for on-device deployment is available separately. The model is part of a community for collaboration and support related to on-device AI.

instruction: Which of the given documents is more relevant for the question about Shinkansen train routes?
description: Ruri-v3 Reranker is a general-purpose Japanese reranker model. It offers state-of-the-art performance with good robustness across various domains. The model supports sequence lengths up to 8192 tokens and features an expanded vocabulary of 100K tokens, an increase from 32K in previous versions. It integrates FlashAttention, following the ModernBERT architecture, and uses a tokenizer based solely on SentencePiece. The model is designed for tasks involving reranking and relevance scoring of Japanese text pairs or documents. It is compatible with the transformers library version 4.48.0 or higher and can be used with the Sentence Transformers framework. For optimal performance on compatible GPUs, it is recommended to use Flash Attention 2. The model is a Sentence Transformer, supports the Japanese language, and is licensed under Apache 2.0. It has demonstrated strong performance on benchmarks such as JQaRAnDCG@10, JaCWIRMAP@10, and MIRACLRecall@30, achieving scores of 86.9, 95.4, and 97.3 respectively. The model was developed by Hayato Tsukagoshi and Ryohei Sasano and is described in a paper available on arXiv with the identifier 2409.07737.

instruction: Identify the geographical places in this travel blog post.
description: The bert-large-NER is a fine-tuned BERT model designed for Named Entity Recognition (NER) and achieves state-of-the-art performance for the NER task. It is trained to recognize four types of entities: location (LOC), organizations (ORG), person (PER), and Miscellaneous (MISC). This model is based on the bert-large-cased architecture and was fine-tuned on the English version of the standard dataset. A smaller BERT model fine-tuned on the same dataset is also available. The model can be used with the Transformers pipeline for NER. However, it is limited by its training dataset of entity-annotated news articles from a specific time period, which may not generalize well to all use cases in different domains.
