Additionally, the model occasionally tags subword tokens as entities, and post-processing of results may be necessary to handle these cases. The model was fine-tuned on the English version of the standard dataset, which distinguishes between the beginning and continuation of an entity to allow for the identification of back-to-back entities of the same type. Each token is classified as one of the following classes: O (Outside of a named entity), B-MIS (Beginning of a miscellaneous entity right after another miscellaneous entity), I-MIS (Miscellaneous entity), B-PER (Beginning of a person’s name right after another person’s name), I-PER (Person’s name), B-ORG (Beginning of an organization right after another organization), I-ORG (Organization), B-LOC (Beginning of a location right after another location), and I-LOC (Location). The training dataset was derived from the Reuters corpus and consists of news stories. The dataset includes 7140 training examples for LOC, 3438 for MISC, 6321 for ORG, and 6600 for PER. The development set includes 1837 LOC, 922 MISC, 1341 ORG, and 1842 PER examples. The test set includes 1668 LOC, 702 MISC, 1661 ORG, and 1617 PER examples. The training dataset contains 946 articles, 14,987 sentences, and 203,621 tokens. The development dataset contains 216 articles, 3,466 sentences, and 51,362 tokens. The test dataset contains 231 articles, 3,684 sentences, and 46,435 tokens. The model was trained on a single NVIDIA V100 GPU using recommended hyperparameters from the training and evaluation on the CoNLL-2003 NER task. The model achieved an f1 score of 95.7 on the development set and 91.7 on the test set. The precision was 95.3 on the development set and 91.2 on the test set. The recall was 96.1 on the development set and 92.3 on the test set. The test metrics are slightly lower than the official Google BERT results, which included document context and experimented with CRF.

instruction: Write a short paragraph about the importance of recycling.
description: OPT (Open Pre-trained Transformer Language Models) is a suite of decoder-only pre-trained transformer models developed by Meta AI and first released on May 3rd, 2022. The models range in size from 125M to 175B parameters and are designed to match the performance and scale of GPT-3 models while incorporating the latest best practices in data collection and efficient training. OPT was predominantly pre-trained on English text, with a small amount of non-English data included from CommonCrawl. The model uses a causal language modeling (CLM) objective and belongs to the same family of decoder-only models. The training data includes a combination of five filtered datasets: BookCorpus, CC-Stories, The Pile (including subsets like Pile-CC, OpenWebText2, USPTO, Project Gutenberg, OpenSubtitles, Wikipedia, DM Mathematics, and HackerNews), the Pushshift.io Reddit dataset, and CCNewsV2. The final training corpus contains 180B tokens across 800GB of data. The validation split was 200MB of the pretraining data, sampled proportionally to each dataset's size. The dataset may contain offensive content due to the inclusion of unfiltered CommonCrawl and Reddit data. The texts were tokenized using the GPT2 byte-level version of Byte Pair Encoding (BPE) with a vocabulary size of 50272 and input sequences of 2048 tokens. The 175B model was trained on 992 80GB A100 GPUs for approximately 33 days. The model can be used for prompting to evaluate downstream tasks, text generation, and fine-tuning on specific tasks. However, due to the unfiltered nature of the training data, the model exhibits biases and safety limitations, including issues with generation diversity, hallucination, and the propagation of harmful content. These limitations also affect all fine-tuned versions of the model. The intended use of OPT is to enable reproducible and responsible research at scale, encouraging the broader research community to study the impact of large language models on areas such as robustness, bias, and toxicity.

instruction: I have a picture with a person in front of a busy background. Can I extract just the person?
description: BiRefNet is a model designed for high-resolution dichotomous image segmentation, specifically for general matting tasks. It is trained on a variety of datasets, including P3M-10k (excluding TE-P3M-500-NP), TR-humans, AM-2k, AIM-500, Human-2k (synthesized with BG-20k), Distinctions-646 (synthesized with BG-20k), HIM2K, and PPM-100. The model is validated on the TE-P3M-500-NP dataset. On this validation set, BiRefNet achieves an Smeasure of 0.979, a maxFm of 0.996, a meanEm of 0.988, an MSE of 0.003, a maxEm of 0.997, a meanFm of 0.986, a wFmeasure of 0.988, an adpEm of 0.864, an adpFm of 0.885, an HCE of 0.000, an mBA of 0.830, a maxBIoU of 0.940, and a meanBIoU of 0.888. The model is developed by researchers from Nankai University, Northwestern Polytechnical University, National University of Defense Technology, Aalto University, Shanghai AI Laboratory, and University of Trento. The authors acknowledge the support of @freepik for providing GPU resources for training the model. The model is described in the paper 'Bilateral Reference for High-Resolution Dichotomous Image Segmentation' published in CAAI Artificial Intelligence Research, volume 3, page 9150038, in 2024.

instruction: Can you clean up a speech audio file that was recorded in a very noisy environment?
description: TIGER is a lightweight model for speech separation that effectively extracts key acoustic features through frequency band-split, multi-scale, and full-frequency-frame modeling. The model, called Time-Frequency Interleaved Gain Extraction and Reconstruction Network (TIGER), leverages prior knowledge to divide frequency bands and applies compression on frequency information. It employs a multi-scale selective attention (MSA) module to extract contextual features and introduces a full-frequency-frame attention (F^3A) module to capture both temporal and frequency contextual information. TIGER significantly outperformed the state-of-the-art (SOTA) model TF-GridNet on the EchoSet dataset in both inference speed and separation quality, while reducing the number of parameters by 94.3% and the MACs by 95.3%. These results indicate that by utilizing frequency band-split and interleaved modeling structures, TIGER achieves a substantial reduction in parameters and computational costs while maintaining high performance. Notably, TIGER is the first speech separation model with fewer than 1 million parameters that achieves performance close to the SOTA model. To more realistically evaluate the performance of speech separation models in complex acoustic environments, a novel dataset called EchoSet was introduced. This dataset includes noise and more realistic reverberation, such as considering object occlusions and material properties, with speech from two speakers overlapping at random proportions.

instruction: Can you create a soft-edged image from a rough sketch I provide?
description: ControlNet v1.1 is a neural network structure designed to control diffusion models by adding extra conditions. It is the successor model to the original ControlNet and was developed by Lvmin Zhang and Maneesh Agrawala. This checkpoint is a conversion into `diffusers` format and is intended to be used in combination with Stable Diffusion. The model is a diffusion-based text-to-image generation model that supports additional input conditions. It is primarily used with English language inputs. The model is conditioned on soft edges, which are typically used to create a more painterly or artistic effect. The model was trained to support a variety of conditions, including canny edge detection, pixel to pixel instruction, image inpainting, multi-level line segment detection, depth estimation, surface normal estimation, image segmentation, line art generation, anime line art generation, human pose estimation, scribble-based image generation, soft edge image generation, image shuffling, and image tiling. The Soft Edge 1.1 version was previously called HED 1.0 in earlier versions of ControlNet. The training dataset for the previous version had several issues, including duplicated grayscale human images, low-quality or blurry images with JPEG artifacts, and incorrect paired prompts due to data processing errors. These issues have been resolved in the new model. The Soft Edge 1.1 version is significantly better than HED 1.0, with improvements attributed to the removal of hidden corrupted grayscale images in the control maps during training. This makes the model more robust and as usable as the depth model, with potential for more frequent use.

instruction: What insights can be gained from analyzing this image and its accompanying text together?
description: Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct-FP8-Dynamic is a quantized version of the Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct model, optimized for efficient inference using FP8 data type for both weights and activations. It is a vision-text input to text output model designed for multimodal tasks such as visual question answering, document analysis, and image captioning. The model was developed by Neural Magic and released on 2/24/2025, version 1.0. It is intended for deployment with the vLLM backend, which supports OpenAI-compatible serving. The model was created using the llmcompressor framework with a quantization recipe targeting linear layers and excluding specific components such as the language model head and vision tower. Evaluation results show that the model maintains or slightly improves upon the accuracy of the original model across various vision and text-based benchmarks, including MMMU, VQAv2, DocVQA, ChartQA, Mathvista, MGSM, and MMLU. The model achieves up to 1.3x speedup in single-stream deployment and 1.37x in multi-stream deployment, depending on the hardware and use-case scenario. Performance benchmarks were conducted using vLLM version 0.7.2 on A6000, A100, and H100 GPUs, showing improvements in latency, queries per second, and cost efficiency for tasks such as document visual question answering, visual reasoning, and image captioning. The model is suitable for deployment in environments where cost and inference speed are critical factors.

instruction: Design a sci-fi spaceship landing on a planet with unusual rock formations and alien trees.
description: Stable Diffusion 3.5 Medium is a Multimodal Diffusion Transformer with improvements (MMDiT-X) text-to-image model developed by Stability AI. It is designed to generate images based on text prompts and features three fixed, pretrained text encoders, QK-normalization to improve training stability, and dual attention blocks in the first 12 transformer layers. The model introduces self-attention modules in the first 13 layers of the transformer, enhancing multi-resolution generation and overall image coherence. It was trained using a wide variety of data, including synthetic and filtered publicly available data. The model supports progressive training stages with resolutions increasing from 256 to 1440, and includes extended positional embedding space and random crop augmentation to enhance robustness across mixed resolutions and aspect ratios. The model uses CLIPs and T5 text encoders with varying context lengths. It is optimized for improved performance in image quality, typography, complex prompt understanding, and resource-efficiency. However, it may produce artifacts when T5 tokens exceed 256, and the medium model may respond differently to prompts compared to the large model. It is recommended to use specific sampling methods for better structure and anatomy coherency. The model is available for non-commercial and commercial use under a community license for organizations or individuals with less than $1M in annual revenue, with enterprise licensing available for higher revenue entities. It is intended for use in artistic processes, educational tools, and research on generative models. It is not designed to generate factual or true representations of people or events. Safety measures are implemented to reduce the risk of certain harms, but developers are encouraged to conduct their own testing and apply additional mitigations based on specific use cases. The model is not guaranteed to prevent all harmful content, and developers should implement content safety guardrails. Misuse and privacy violations are out of scope, and users are required to adhere to acceptable use policies. Developers and deployers are encouraged to respect privacy regulations and data privacy techniques.

instruction: How can I improve a robot's ability to generalize its tasks across different room layouts?
description: RoboEngine is a plug-and-play visual robot data augmentation toolkit designed to enable users to generate physics- and task-aware robot scenes with minimal effort, using just a few lines of code. It enhances the visual robustness of imitation learning by overcoming limitations of existing methods, which often require camera calibration or controlled environments. The toolkit includes a novel robot scene segmentation dataset, a generalizable high-quality robot segmentation model, and a fine-tuned background generation model. These components work together to form an out-of-the-box solution for data augmentation. Using RoboEngine, robot manipulation tasks can be generalized across six entirely new scenes based solely on demonstrations collected from a single scene, resulting in a more than 200% performance improvement compared to the no-augmentation baseline. The datasets, model weights, and toolkit are publicly available.

instruction: Make a video of a street artist painting a large mural on a wall in a vibrant city setting.
description: LTX-Video is the first DiT-based video generation model capable of generating high-quality videos in real-time. It produces 30 FPS videos at a 1216×704 resolution faster than they can be watched. Trained on a large-scale dataset of diverse videos, the model generates high-resolution videos with realistic and varied content. The model supports both text-to-video and image+text-to-video use cases. It is a diffusion-based model developed by Lightricks and is designed to work with English prompts. The model works best on resolutions under 720 x 1280 and number of frames below 257. It is available in multiple versions, including 2B and 13B parameter sizes, with options for distilled, quantized, and LoRA variants. The model can be used locally with Python and PyTorch, and is compatible with the Diffusers library for both text-to-video and image-to-video generation. The model includes a spatial upscaler and a temporal upscaler for improved video quality. The model may fail to generate videos that perfectly match the prompts, and its performance is influenced by the prompting style. As a statistical model, it may amplify existing societal biases and is not intended to provide factual information.

instruction: How do I turn this sketch of a futuristic bike into a 3D model for a design project?
description: TripoSG-scribble is a model that converts a scribble image and a text prompt into a 3D shape. It is a variant of TripoSG, a state-of-the-art image-to-3D generation foundation model that uses large-scale rectified flow transformers to produce high-fidelity 3D shapes from single images. TripoSG-scribble employs a novel architecture that includes a rectified flow (RF) based Transformer for stable, linear trajectory modeling, an advanced VAE with SDF-based representation and hybrid geometric supervision, and a cross-attention mechanism for image feature conditioning. The model operates on 1.5B parameters and 512 latent tokens. For inference efficiency, TripoSG-scribble is a CFG-distilled model and should be used with CFG=0. The model is intended for converting scribble images and text prompts into high-quality 3D meshes, supporting creative and design applications, gaming and VFX asset creation, and prototyping and visualization. It requires a CUDA-capable GPU with more than 8GB VRAM. TripoSG-scribble is developed by Tripo, pushing the boundaries of 3D generative AI.

instruction: What are the most important news events affecting the market right now?
description: Orbital X ($ORBX) is a next-generation crypto token that integrates AI-driven insights to optimize and enhance the cryptocurrency experience. Built on a foundation of powerful, specially trained Large Language Models (LLMs), $ORBX aims to give investors and enthusiasts an edge in the fast-paced world of digital assets. Orbital X bridges the gap between complex data analysis and accessible insights, offering users interactive, personalized guidance, predictive analytics, and investment tools informed by real-time market data. At the heart of $ORBX is a highly trained LLM that processes large volumes of data, including market trends, historical price movements, and social sentiment, generating forecasts for asset volatility and identifying patterns and price indicators. The LLM continuously adapts to incoming information, offering data-driven predictions that factor in emerging patterns, sentiment shifts, and market dynamics. $ORBX’s LLM provides users with an interactive interface where they can ask investment-related questions in plain language. Whether users are looking to refine a trading strategy, understand a token’s performance, or gain insights on recent market trends, $ORBX’s AI responds with personalized, actionable advice. The AI model learns from user inputs and tailors responses to individual preferences, helping them make informed decisions with accurate, user-centered insights. $ORBX’s neural network scans vast amounts of social and market data, detecting shifts in sentiment and interpreting the impact on market behavior. This information enables the LLM to generate real-time analytics on market sentiment, helping investors stay ahead of emerging trends. By correlating news events, social sentiment, and market fluctuations, the LLM identifies major influences on $ORBX’s price and trading volume, delivering insights into the broader context of market changes. The $ORBX platform includes a 3D neural network visualization, allowing users to explore how AI interprets data, patterns, and predictions. The interactive display of neural connections simulates the token’s AI-driven decision-making, providing a clear view into the backend processing. Charts and graphs based on predictive models provide an easy-to-understand visualization of current and forecasted market conditions. The $ORBX AI is powered by a unique Large Language Model trained specifically for the financial and blockchain industries. $ORBX’s LLM is trained on financial data, blockchain transaction histories, social sentiment from platforms like Twitter, and relevant news sources, ensuring a holistic view of market influences. The LLM analyzes market data in real time, identifying trends and forecasting price shifts. It uses sophisticated neural network models to correlate social and market data with predictive power. Through its natural language interface, the LLM delivers custom insights, allowing users to ask questions and receive answers contextualized by recent data trends and personal investment history. The development of the core LLM and neural network for baseline prediction capabilities and sentiment analysis is part of the project’s roadmap, followed by the launch of an interactive dashboard, 3D neural network visualization, and AI-driven advisor. The project also plans to expand AI capabilities with enhanced NLP responses, improved predictive accuracy, and user-tailored insights. $ORBX - Orbital X is licensed under the MIT License.

instruction: Can you generate a visual overlay of the segmented heart and lungs on this radiograph?
description: This model performs both segmentation and classification on chest radiographs (X-rays). The model uses a tf_efficientnetv2_s backbone with a U-Net decoder for segmentation and a linear layer for classification. For frontal radiographs, the model segments the right lung, left lung, and heart. The model also predicts the chest X-ray view (AP, PA, lateral), patient age, and patient sex. The (small version) and datasets were used to train the model. Segmentation masks were obtained from the CheXmask. The final dataset comprised 335,516 images from 96,385 patients and was split into 80% training and 20% validation. A holdout test set was not used since minimal tuning was performed. The view classifier was trained only on CheXpert images (NIH images excluded from loss function), given that lateral radiographs are only present in CheXpert. This is to avoid unwanted bias in the model, which can occur if one class originates only from a single dataset. Validation performance is as follows: Segmentation (Dice similarity coefficient): Right Lung: 0.957, Left Lung: 0.948, Heart: 0.943. Age Prediction: Mean Absolute Error: 5.25 years. Classification: View (AP, PA, lateral): 99.42% accuracy, Female: 0.999 AUC. The model is for demonstration and research purposes only and has not been approved by any regulatory agency for clinical use. The user assumes any and all responsibility regarding their own use of this model and its outputs.

instruction: I need assistance in classifying a patient's medical report into the correct diagnostic category.
description: Clinical ModernBERT is a state-of-the-art encoder-based transformer designed for biomedical and clinical text, with a context length of up to 8192 tokens. It extends the innovations of ModernBERT by increasing the context window and incorporating domain-specific vocabulary refinements. The model is intended to produce semantically rich representations that capture the nuanced syntax of biomedical literature and the intricate semantics of clinical narratives. It utilizes architectural advancements such as rotary positional embeddings (RoPE), Flash Attention for near-linear memory usage with extended contexts, and GeGLU activation layers that enhance representational capacity through smooth gating mechanisms. Clinical ModernBERT is initialized from a ModernBERT-base checkpoint and pre-trained on a corpus of approximately 40 million PubMed abstracts and MIMIC-IV clinical notes, along with medical codes and descriptions. The pre-training process involved 150,000 masked language modeling (MLM) training steps with a batch size of 128. A custom collator dynamically adjusts the masking probability from 30% to 15% over training, emphasizing medically relevant tokens such as drug names and procedural codes. The MLM evaluation framework includes top-1, top-5, top-10, and top-25 accuracy metrics with values of 63.31%, 79.67%, 83.33%, and 88.10%, respectively. The model is optimized for tasks such as retrieval-augmented generation, fine-grained text classification, and domain-specific entity extraction. It is particularly suited for clinical information retrieval, narrative classification, and structured medical coding, and can be fine-tuned for specialized applications such as electronic health record analysis, clinical decision support systems, and evidence-based medical literature retrieval. The model is available for use through the Hugging Face platform with the identifier 'Simonlee711/Clinical_ModernBERT'.

instruction: Can you generate a 3D model with material details like roughness and metallic from this picture?
description: Stable Point Aware 3D (SPAR3D) is a large reconstruction model that introduces the ability to make real-time edits and generate a textured UV-unwrapped 3D mesh asset from a single image in less than a second. SPAR3D combines the benefits of a fast point cloud diffusion model with regressive mesh predictions to enable unparalleled control over 3D object generation. The model is trained to create a 3D model from a single image in under one second. The asset is UV-unwrapped and textured. A delighting step is performed, enabling easier asset usage in downstream applications such as game engines or rendering work. The model also predicts per-object material parameters (roughness, metallic), enhancing reflective behaviors during rendering. The model expects an input size of 512x512 pixels. Improved backside modeling is achieved using a fast point diffusion model, which acts as a conditioning. We use renders from the dataset, available under the Open Data Commons Attribution License. An enhanced rendering method is utilized, which more closely replicates the distribution of images found in the real world, significantly improving the model's ability to generalize. Objects are filtered based on the review of licenses and a subset is curated suitable for training needs. Intended uses include the generation of artworks and use in design and other artistic processes, applications in educational or creative tools, and research on reconstruction models, including understanding the limitations of these models. All uses of the model should be in accordance with the provided guidelines. The model was not trained to be factual or true representations of people or events. As such, using the model to generate such content is out-of-scope of the abilities of this model. As part of a safety-by-design and responsible AI deployment approach, safety measures are implemented throughout the development of the model, from pre-training to ongoing development, fine-tuning, and deployment. A number of safety mitigations are intended to reduce the risk of severe harms. However, it is the responsibility of developers to conduct their own testing and apply additional mitigations based on their specific use cases. For individuals or organizations generating annual revenue of US $1,000,000 (or local currency equivalent) or more, regardless of the source of that revenue, an enterprise commercial license must be obtained directly from Stability AI before commercially using SPAR3D, any derivative work of SPAR3D (such as a fine tune model), or their outputs. A request for an Enterprise License may be submitted at the provided link. Please refer to Stability AI's Community License for more information.

instruction: Can you recognize the different parts of a technical report, such as the methodology and results sections, based on their layout?
description: The Docling model for layout detection is designed to facilitate easy importing and usage like any other Hugging Face model. It is part of a collection that provides document layout analysis tools. The model is based on RT-DETR, a robust transformer-based object detector, and is intended for layout detection in documents. It was trained on an internal dataset for document structure recognition. The model is available under the Apache 2.0 license. If used in academic or research work, it should be cited by referencing the main Docling repository. For questions or issues, users are encouraged to open a discussion on Hugging Face or contact pandahd75@gmail.com.

instruction: I want to create subtitles for a video—can you transcribe the audio for me?
description: Whisper is a pre-trained model for automatic speech recognition (ASR) and speech translation. Trained on 680,000 hours of labelled speech data using large-scale weak supervision, Whisper models demonstrate strong generalization capabilities across many datasets and domains without the need for fine-tuning. Whisper is a Transformer-based encoder-decoder model, also referred to as a sequence-to-sequence model. The models were trained on either English-only data or multilingual data. English-only models were trained for speech recognition, while multilingual models were trained for both speech recognition and speech translation. For speech recognition, the model predicts transcriptions in the same language as the audio. For speech translation, the model predicts transcriptions in a different language from the audio. Whisper checkpoints come in five configurations of varying model sizes. The smallest four are trained on either English-only or multilingual data, while the largest checkpoints are multilingual only. The model is used in conjunction with a WhisperProcessor to pre-process audio inputs into log-Mel spectrograms and post-process model outputs into text. The model is informed of the task to perform (transcription or translation) by passing context tokens to the decoder. These context tokens include a transcription token, a language token, a task token, and optionally a no-timestamp token. The context tokens can be forced or un-forced. Forced tokens allow control over the output language and task, while un-forced tokens let the model predict them automatically. Whisper can transcribe audio samples of up to 30 seconds in duration, and with a chunking algorithm, it can transcribe audio of arbitrary length. Whisper can also predict sequence-level timestamps. The pre-trained model can be fine-tuned to improve performance on specific languages and tasks, particularly with as little as 5 hours of labelled data. Whisper is primarily trained and evaluated on ASR and speech translation to English tasks, showing strong results in approximately 10 languages. It may also support additional capabilities like voice activity detection, speaker classification, or speaker diarization, but these have not been robustly evaluated. Users are advised to perform thorough evaluations before deployment. Whisper should not be used to transcribe recordings without consent or for subjective classification. It is not recommended for high-risk domains where accuracy flaws could lead to significant negative outcomes. The model is intended for transcription and translation, and its use for classification is not appropriate, especially for inferring human attributes. Whisper is trained on 680,000 hours of audio and transcripts collected from the internet, with 65% in English, 18% in non-English audio with English transcripts, and 17% in non-English audio with corresponding non-English transcripts. The non-English data represents 98 different languages. Performance in a given language is correlated with the amount of training data available for that language. Whisper models show improved robustness to accents, background noise, technical language, and zero-shot translation from multiple languages into English. However, due to weakly supervised training on noisy data, the models may generate hallucinations—texts that are not actually spoken in the audio. Performance varies across languages, with lower accuracy observed in low-resource or low-discoverability languages and across different accents and dialects. The models may also exhibit higher word error rates for speakers of different genders, races, or ages. The sequence-to-sequence architecture can lead to repetitive text generation, which can be partially mitigated with beam search and temperature scheduling. Whisper's transcription capabilities may be used to improve accessibility tools and enable near-real-time speech recognition and translation applications. However, the release of Whisper also raises dual-use concerns, as it could facilitate the development of surveillance technologies. The models may also have some capability to recognize specific individuals, which could pose safety concerns. The performance disparities of Whisper may have economic implications, particularly in the accessibility and surveillance domains.

instruction: Detect any food items in this image and provide their names along with their positions.
description: LLMDet (base variant) was proposed by Shenghao Fu, Qize Yang, Qijie Mo, Junkai Yan, Xihan Wei, Jingke Meng, Xiaohua Xie, and Wei-Shi Zheng. LLMDet improves upon previous models by co-training the model with a large language model. The model can be used for zero-shot object detection. The raw model supports inference tasks, and checkpoints are available for use, though they do not include the large language model used during training. Inference with LLMDet is identical to that of other similar models. The model was trained on a combination of datasets, including O365, GoldG, GRIT, V3Det, O365V2, OpenImageV6, and GroundingCap-1M. Evaluation results on LVIS show that the model achieves a MiniVal APr of 44.7, MiniVal APc of 37.3, MiniVal APf of 39.5, and MiniVal AP of 50.7 when trained on (O365, GoldG, GRIT, V3Det) + GroundingCap-1M. When trained on (O365, GoldG, V3Det) + GroundingCap-1M, the model achieves a MiniVal APr of 48.3, MiniVal APc of 40.8, MiniVal APf of 43.1, and MiniVal AP of 54.3. When trained on (O365V2, OpenImageV6, GoldG) + GroundingCap-1M, the model achieves a MiniVal APr of 51.1, MiniVal APc of 45.1, MiniVal APf of 46.1, and MiniVal AP of 56.6. On Val1.0, the model achieves APr of 34.9, APc of 26.0, APf of 30.1, and AP of 44.3 when trained on (O365, GoldG, GRIT, V3Det) + GroundingCap-1M. When trained on (O365, GoldG, V3Det) + GroundingCap-1M, the model achieves Val1.0 APr of 38.5, APc of 28.2, APf of 34.3, and AP of 47.8. When trained on (O365V2, OpenImageV6, GoldG) + GroundingCap-1M, the model achieves Val1.0 APr of 42.0, APc of 31.6, APf of 38.8, and AP of 50.2. The model is designed to detect objects in images using textual descriptions as input, and it supports postprocessing of outputs to extract bounding boxes, confidence scores, and detected labels. The model is intended for use in scenarios where object detection is required without prior training on specific object categories.

instruction: Can you determine the relationship between 'The cat is sleeping on the mat' and 'The cat is awake'?
description: ModernBERT is a multi-task fine-tuned model based on the BERT architecture, trained on a collection of NLI (Natural Language Inference) tasks from Tasksource, including MNLI, ANLI, SICK, WANLI, doc-nli, LingNLI, FOLIO, FOL-NLI, LogicNLI, Label-NLI, and other datasets. This model is equivalent to an 'instruct' version and was trained for 200k steps on an Nvidia A30 GPU. It is particularly effective for reasoning tasks, long context reasoning, sentiment analysis, and zero-shot classification with new labels. The model demonstrates strong performance on a wide range of NLI and classification tasks, with test accuracy scores across multiple datasets. These scores reflect the performance of a single transformer with different classification heads on top. Further improvements can be achieved through single-task fine-tuning, but this checkpoint is optimized for zero-shot classification and natural language inference tasks involving contradiction, entailment, and neutral classification. The model supports zero-shot classification pipelines and natural language inference pipelines, and can also serve as a backbone for further fine-tuning. It has stronger reasoning and fine-grained classification abilities compared to the base version.

instruction: Which team won the most matches in the 2023 season?
description: This model is a TAPAS small model fine-tuned on WikiSQL in a supervised fashion. It has two versions: the default version corresponds to the `tapas_wikisql_sqa_inter_masklm_small_reset` checkpoint, and the other version is `no_reset`, corresponding to `tapas_wikisql_sqa_inter_masklm_small`. The model was pre-trained on masked language modeling (MLM) and an additional step called intermediate pre-training, followed by fine-tuning on SQA and WikiSQL. It uses relative position embeddings, resetting the position index at every cell of the table. The non-default version uses absolute position embeddings. TAPAS is a BERT-like transformer model pre-trained on a large corpus of English data from Wikipedia in a self-supervised fashion. It was pre-trained on raw tables and associated texts without human labeling, using an automatic process to generate inputs and labels. The pre-training objectives include masked language modeling, where 15% of the words in a flattened table and associated context are randomly masked, and the model must predict the masked words, and intermediate pre-training, where the model must classify whether a sentence is supported or refuted by the contents of a table. The model learns an inner representation of the English language used in tables and associated texts, which can be used for downstream tasks such as answering questions about a table or determining whether a sentence is entailed or refuted by the contents of a table. Fine-tuning involves adding a cell selection head and aggregation head on top of the pre-trained model and jointly training these heads with the base model on SQA and WikiSQL. The model is intended for answering questions related to a table. The texts are lowercased and tokenized using WordPiece with a vocabulary size of 30,000. The inputs are of the form [CLS] Question [SEP] Flattened table [SEP]. The WikiSQL dataset was first converted into the format of SQA using automatic conversion scripts. The model was fine-tuned on 32 Cloud TPU v3 cores for 50,000 steps with a maximum sequence length of 512 and a batch size of 512. Fine-tuning takes around 10 hours. The optimizer used is Adam with a learning rate of 6.17164e-5 and a warmup ratio of 0.1424. The team releasing TAPAS did not write a model card for this model; this model card was written by the Hugging Face team and contributors.
domain: Natural Language Processing Table Question Answering

instruction: From the voice in this clip, can you guess if it's a man or a woman?
description: This model combines the SpeechBrain ECAPA-TDNN speaker embedding model with an SVM classifier to predict speaker gender from audio input. The model was trained and evaluated on the VoxCeleb2, Mozilla Common Voice v10.0, and TIMIT datasets. The input is an audio file, which will be converted to 16kHz, mono, single channel. The output is a gender prediction, either 'male' or 'female'. The speaker embedding is a 192-dimensional ECAPA-TDNN embedding from SpeechBrain. The classifier is a Support Vector Machine optimized through Optuna with 200 trials. Performance on the VoxCeleb2 test set is 98.9% accuracy and 0.9885 F1-score. Performance on the Mozilla Common Voice v10.0 English validated test set is 92.3% accuracy. Performance on the TIMIT test set is 99.6% accuracy. The model was trained on the VoxCeleb2 dataset, with a training set of 1,691 speakers (845 females, 846 males), a validation set of 785 speakers (396 females, 389 males), and a test set of 1,647 speakers (828 females, 819 males). There is no speaker overlap between the sets. Audio preprocessing includes conversion to WAV format, single channel, 16kHz sampling rate, 256 kp/s bitrate, and application of SileroVAD for voice activity detection, taking the first voiced segment. The model was trained on celebrity voices from YouTube interviews, and performance may vary on different audio qualities or recording conditions. The model is designed for binary gender classification only.

instruction: Generate an image of a construction site where each piece of machinery and structure is segmented in unique colors.
description: ControlNet v1.1 is a neural network structure designed to control diffusion models by adding extra conditions. It is the successor model to an earlier version and was developed by Lvmin Zhang and Maneesh Agrawala. This checkpoint is a conversion into diffusers format and is intended to be used in combination with Stable Diffusion. The model is a diffusion-based text-to-image generation model and is primarily associated with the English language. The license is adapted from the work conducted by others in the area of responsible AI licensing. ControlNet v1.1 is conditioned on segmentation images, supporting a broader range of colors under the COCO protocol compared to the previous Segmentation 1.0 version, which supported about 150 colors. Segmentation 1.1 supports an additional 182 colors from COCO. The model was trained to support various input conditions, including canny edge detection, pixel to pixel instruction, image inpainting, multi-level line segment detection, depth estimation, surface normal estimation, image segmentation, line art generation, anime line art generation, human pose estimation, scribble-based image generation, soft edge image generation, image shuffling, and image tiling. The model was trained in an end-to-end manner, and the learning process is robust even with a small training dataset. The model is described in the paper titled 'Adding Conditional Control to Text-to-Image Diffusion Models' by Lvmin Zhang and Maneesh Agrawala.

instruction: Can you help me identify the top 10 most similar documents to a given query in Japanese?
description: Snowflake's Arctic-embed-l-v2.0 is an embedding model designed for retrieval performance and inference efficiency, with a focus on multilingual capabilities. It is part of a suite of embedding models optimized for enterprise-grade multilingual search and retrieval at scale. The model is released under the Apache 2.0 license, allowing for commercial use without charge. Arctic-embed-l-v2.0 excels in both English and non-English retrieval, outperforming leading open-source and proprietary models on benchmarks such as MTEB Retrieval, CLEF, and MIRACL. It features 303 million non-embedding parameters and supports inference at any scale. The model is compression-friendly, achieving high-quality retrieval with embeddings as small as 128 bytes per vector using Matryoshka Representation Learning (MRL) and 4-bit quantization. The MRL for this model is 256 dimensions, and high-quality 128-byte compression is achieved via 4-bit quantization. Arctic-embed-l-v2.0 is a drop-in replacement for previous models, allowing direct inference with new libraries, kernels, or inference engines. It supports a context window of up to 8192 tokens via RoPE. On quality benchmarks, Arctic-embed-l-v2.0 achieves an average NDCG@10 of 55.6 on BEIR (15), 55.8 on MIRACL (4), 52.9 on CLEF (Focused), and 54.3 on CLEF (Full). When using 256 dimensions instead of 1024, the model experiences less than 3% degradation in quality. The model can be used with Sentence Transformers, Huggingface Transformers, or Huggingface Transformers.js, with specific instructions for encoding queries and documents and computing similarity scores. Users are encouraged to open issues or pull requests for questions or suggestions, and can contact Daniel Campos at daniel.campos@snowflake.com for further assistance.

instruction: How can I improve the flow and rhythm of a generated video?
description: Wan2.1-Fun-Reward-LoRAs is a set of pre-trained models designed to enhance video generation by aligning outputs with human preferences using the Reward Backpropagation technique. These models are implemented as LoRAs (Low-Rank Adaptations) and can be used as plug-ins to improve the performance of corresponding base models. The provided LoRAs include both HPS v2.1 and MPS reward models for different base models, such as Wan2.1-Fun-1.3B-InP and Wan2.1-Fun-14B-InP. Each LoRA is trained with specific parameters, including rank and network alpha values, and varying batch sizes and training steps. For example, the HPS v2.1 reward LoRA for Wan2.1-Fun-1.3B-InP is trained with a batch size of 8 for 5,000 steps, while the MPS reward LoRA for the same base model is trained with a batch size of 8 for 7,500 steps. The models are intended to be used in conjunction with the base models to refine video outputs based on reward signals. However, there are limitations: after a certain point in training, the reward may continue to increase without a corresponding improvement in video quality, as the model may learn to exploit shortcuts such as adding background artifacts. Additionally, suitable preference models for video generation are currently lacking, and using image-based preference models can lead to a reduction in the dynamism of generated videos, even when mitigated by using only the first frame for reward computation.

instruction: What is the most likely setting of the image based on its visual features?
description: SigLIP 2 Large extends the pretraining objective with prior, independently developed techniques into a unified recipe, for improved semantic understanding, localization, and dense features. The model can be used for tasks like zero-shot image classification and image-text retrieval, or as a vision encoder for VLMs and other vision tasks. SigLIP 2 adds training objectives such as decoder loss, global-local and masked prediction loss, and aspect ratio and resolution adaptibility. The model is pre-trained on the WebLI dataset and was trained on up to 2048 TPU-v5e chips. Evaluation results are presented in the associated paper. The model is described in the research titled 'SigLIP 2: Multilingual Vision-Language Encoders with Improved Semantic Understanding, Localization, and Dense Features' by Michael Tschannen, Alexey Gritsenko, Xiao Wang, Muhammad Ferjad Naeem, Ibrahim Alabdulmohsin, Nikhil Parthasarathy, Talfan Evans, Lucas Beyer, Ye Xia, Basil Mustafa, Olivier Hénaff, Jeremiah Harmsen, Andreas Steiner, and Xiaohua Zhai, available on arXiv with the identifier 2502.14786.

instruction: I want to enhance my photo with depth effects—can you estimate the depth of each pixel?
description: Prompt Depth Anything is a high-resolution and accurate metric depth estimation method. It uses prompting to leverage the power of depth foundation models, drawing inspiration from the success of prompting in vision-language and large language model foundation models. The method utilizes widely available iPhone LiDAR as a prompt to guide the model in producing up to 4K resolution accurate metric depth. A scalable data pipeline is introduced to train the method. Prompt Depth Anything is designed to benefit downstream applications, including 3D reconstruction and generalized robotic grasping.

instruction: Can you determine if the statement 'The meeting was postponed' logically follows from the sentence 'We had to delay the meeting due to unexpected circumstances'?
description: This multilingual model can perform natural language inference (NLI) on 100 languages and is therefore also suitable for multilingual zero-shot classification. The underlying model was pre-trained by Microsoft. It was then fine-tuned on a dataset containing hypothesis-premise pairs from 15 languages, as well as the English MNLI dataset. As of December 2021, mDeBERTa-base is the best performing multilingual base-sized transformer model, introduced by Microsoft. The model was trained on the XNLI development dataset and the MNLI train dataset. The XNLI development set consists of 2490 professionally translated texts from English to 14 other languages (37350 texts in total). The XNLI contains a training set of 15 machine translated versions of the MNLI dataset for 15 languages, but due to quality issues with these machine translations, this model was only trained on the professional translations from the XNLI development set and the original English MNLI training set (392 702 texts). Not using machine translated texts can avoid overfitting the model to the 15 languages; avoids catastrophic forgetting of the other 85 languages mDeBERTa was pre-trained on; and significantly reduces training costs. mDeBERTa-v3-base-mnli-xnli was trained using the Hugging Face trainer with the following hyperparameters: num_train_epochs=2, learning_rate=2e-05, per_device_train_batch_size=16, per_device_eval_batch_size=16, warmup_ratio=0.1, weight_decay=0.06. The model was evaluated on the XNLI test set on 15 languages (5010 texts per language, 75150 in total). Multilingual NLI models are capable of classifying NLI texts without receiving NLI training data in the specific language (cross-lingual transfer). This means that the model is also able of doing NLI on the other 85 languages mDeBERTa was training on, but performance is most likely lower than for those languages available in XNLI. If other multilingual models on the model hub claim performance of around 90% on languages other than English, the authors have most likely made a mistake during testing since none of the latest papers shows a multilingual average performance of more than a few points above 80% on XNLI. The model achieved an average performance of 0.808 across 15 languages, with individual language scores as follows: ar 0.802, bg 0.829, de 0.825, el 0.826, en 0.883, es 0.845, fr 0.834, hi 0.771, ru 0.813, sw 0.748, th 0.793, tr 0.807, ur 0.740, vi 0.795, zh 0.8116. Please consult the original DeBERTa-V3 paper and literature on different NLI datasets for potential biases.

instruction: How can I assess the relevance of these documents and arrange them from most to least relevant?
description: The gte-reranker-modernbert-base is a text reranker model developed by Tongyi Lab, Alibaba Group. It is part of the gte-modernbert series, which is built upon the latest modernBERT pre-trained encoder-only foundation models. The model is designed for text reranking tasks and is primarily used in English. It has a model size of 149 million parameters and supports a maximum input length of 8192 tokens. The gte-modernbert series models demonstrate competitive performance in several text embedding and text retrieval evaluation tasks when compared to similar-scale models from the current open-source community. This includes assessments such as MTEB, LoCO, and COIR evaluations. The gte-reranker-modernbert-base model achieves an MTEB score of 64.38, a BEIR score of 55.33, a LoCo score of 90.68, and a COIR score of 79.31. The model is designed to be used with libraries such as transformers, sentence-transformers, and transformers.js. It can also be deployed using Docker for CPU or GPU environments. The model follows the training scheme of the previous series, with the only difference being that the pre-training language model base has been updated. The model is suitable for reranking tasks where the goal is to reorder a set of candidate documents based on their relevance to a given query. The model outputs scores that can be used to determine the relevance of each document to the query. The model is evaluated on a variety of benchmarks, including MTEB, LoCO, COIR, and BEIR, and its performance is compared to other models of similar size. The model is available for use and deployment, and it is recommended to use Flash Attention 2 if the GPU supports it. The model is part of a series of models that are designed to provide high-quality text representations and reranking capabilities for multilingual text retrieval tasks.

instruction: Show me images that correspond to the concept of 'a peaceful beach at sunset'?
description: Jina CLIP v2 is a general-purpose multilingual multimodal embedding model for text and images. Multimodal embeddings enable searching and understanding data across different modalities through a coherent representation, serving as the backbone of neural information retrieval and multimodal GenAI applications. Built upon recent advancements, Jina CLIP v2 features several significant improvements. It shows a 3% performance improvement over v1 in both text-image and text-text retrieval tasks. The text encoder can serve as an effective multilingual long-context dense retriever, performing on par with the frontier model `jina-embeddings-v3` on MTEB. It supports 89 languages for multilingual-image retrieval, showing up to 4% improvement compared to `nllb-clip-large-siglip` on multilingual image retrieval tasks. It supports 512x512 input image resolution, a significant increase from v1's 224x224, enabling better processing of detailed images, improved feature extraction, and more accurate recognition of fine-grained visual elements. It allows users to truncate the output dimensions of both text and image embeddings from 1024 down to 64, reducing storage and processing overhead while maintaining strong performance. Measuring 0.9B parameters, Jina CLIP v2 combines two powerful encoders: the text encoder `Jina-XLM-RoBERTa` and the vision encoder `EVA02-L14`. The text encoder supports 8,192 tokens as input and uses mean pooling, while the image encoder accepts 512x512 pixel inputs and uses CLS pooling. Both encoders have 24 layers. The text encoder uses FlashAttention2, and the image encoder uses xFormers for attention mechanisms. The minimum and maximum output dimensions for both encoders are 64 and 1,024, respectively. The text encoder supports 89 languages. The image encoder uses a patch size of 14x14. These encoders are jointly trained to create aligned representations of images and text. CLIP-like models have established themselves as the backbone for general-purpose multimodal applications. Jina CLIP v2 aims to break down language barriers to deliver more accurate cross-modal understanding and retrieval, making multimodal search and retrieval more powerful and accessible to developers worldwide.

instruction: Could you summarize this English article about the latest developments in global trade policy in Russian?
description: This model is a fine-tuned version of `facebook/mbart-large-50-many-to-many-mmt` for multilingual abstractive summarization of foreign trade news in Russian and English. It is a multilingual summarization model trained on economic and foreign trade news in Russian and English. It is based on the `facebook/mbart-large-50-many-to-many-mmt` architecture and fine-tuned specifically for the task of generating concise, informative summaries for news articles in the domain of international trade. The model type is a sequence-to-sequence transformer (mBART), and it supports the languages Russian and English. It is licensed under the MIT license. The model is intended for generating abstractive summaries of foreign trade-related news in Russian and English, and for assisting analysts and journalists working with economic content. It could be integrated into news apps or research tools that require multilingual summarization and is useful for training domain-specific summarization pipelines. It is not suitable for general-purpose summarization outside the economic or trade domain, not intended for languages other than Russian and English, and should not be used where factual precision is critical without human review, due to the risk of hallucination. As with most large language models, this model may hallucinate or omit important factual details, be sensitive to domain shift — performing best on economic/trade texts, and reflect biases present in the news sources used in the dataset. Users should apply human verification when using summaries in professional or sensitive settings and avoid use for non-economic domains without retraining. The model was evaluated on the test split of the dataset, separately for Russian and English subsets. For Russian, the evaluation metrics include ROUGE-1 of 0.5165, ROUGE-2 of 0.3646, ROUGE-L of 0.5102, ROUGE-Lsum of 0.5093, METEOR of 0.7335, BERTScore-F1 of 0.9533, and NER-F1 of 0.707. For English, the evaluation metrics include ROUGE-1 of 0.6053, ROUGE-2 of 0.4640, ROUGE-L of 0.5424, ROUGE-Lsum of 0.5427, METEOR of 0.5712, BERTScore-F1 of 0.9344, and NER-F1 of 0.643. ROUGE measures n-gram overlap between the generated summary and the reference. METEOR takes into account synonyms and stemming. BERTScore measures semantic similarity using contextual embeddings. NER-F1 measures preservation of named entities in summaries.

instruction: Describe the life cycle of a dragonfly in simple terms.
description: Qwen3 is the latest generation of large language models in the Qwen series, offering a comprehensive suite of dense and mixture-of-experts (MoE) models. Built upon extensive training, Qwen3 delivers groundbreaking advancements in reasoning, instruction-following, agent capabilities, and multilingual support. It uniquely supports seamless switching between thinking mode (for complex logical reasoning, math, and coding) and non-thinking mode (for efficient, general-purpose dialogue) within a single model, ensuring optimal performance across various scenarios. It significantly enhances its reasoning capabilities, surpassing previous QwQ (in thinking mode) and Qwen2.5 instruct models (in non-thinking mode) on mathematics, code generation, and commonsense logical reasoning. It demonstrates superior human preference alignment, excelling in creative writing, role-playing, multi-turn dialogues, and instruction following, to deliver a more natural, engaging, and immersive conversational experience. It has expertise in agent capabilities, enabling precise integration with external tools in both thinking and unthinking modes and achieving leading performance among open-source models in complex agent-based tasks. It supports 100+ languages and dialects with strong capabilities for multilingual instruction following and translation. The Qwen3-4B model is a causal language model with 4.0B parameters, 3.6B non-embedding parameters, 36 layers, and 32 attention heads for Q and 8 for KV. It has a context length of 32,768 tokens natively. The model is designed to support both thinking and non-thinking modes, with the ability to switch between them via user input or API parameters. In thinking mode, the model generates reasoning content wrapped in a block, followed by the final response. In non-thinking mode, the model does not generate any thinking content. The model is compatible with various frameworks and tools, including Hugging Face transformers, vLLM, SGLang, Ollama, LMStudio, MLX-LM, and llama.cpp. It supports agentic use through the Qwen-Agent framework, which encapsulates tool-calling templates and parsers. The model natively supports context lengths of up to 32,768 tokens and can be extended to 131,072 tokens using RoPE scaling techniques. For optimal performance, it is recommended to use specific sampling parameters for thinking and non-thinking modes, adequate output length, and standardized output formats for benchmarking. The model's historical output in multi-turn conversations should only include the final response part, not the thinking content. The model is available for deployment via APIs and supports dynamic YaRN by default through the endpoint provided by Alibaba Model Studio.

instruction: What is the average temperature recorded in July across all cities listed?
description: This model is a TAPAS small model fine-tuned on WikiTable Questions (WTQ). It has two versions: the default version corresponds to the `tapas_wtq_wikisql_sqa_inter_masklm_small_reset` checkpoint, and the non-default version corresponds to `tapas_wtq_wikisql_sqa_inter_masklm_small`. The model was pre-trained on masked language modeling (MLM) and an additional step of intermediate pre-training, followed by fine-tuning on SQA, WikiSQL, and finally WTQ. It uses relative position embeddings, resetting the position index at every cell of the table. The non-default version uses absolute position embeddings. TAPAS is a BERT-like transformer model pre-trained on a large corpus of English data from Wikipedia in a self-supervised fashion. It was pre-trained with two objectives: masked language modeling, where 15% of the words in a flattened table and associated context are randomly masked and the model must predict them, and intermediate pre-training, where the model is trained to classify whether a sentence is supported or refuted by the contents of a table using a balanced dataset of syntactically created examples. This allows the model to learn a bidirectional representation of tables and associated texts. The model can be used for tasks such as answering questions about a table or determining whether a sentence is entailed or refuted by the contents of a table. Fine-tuning involves adding a cell selection head and aggregation head on top of the pre-trained model and jointly training these with the base model. The model was fine-tuned on 32 Cloud TPU v3 cores for 50,000 steps with a maximum sequence length of 512 and a batch size of 512. The optimizer used is Adam with a learning rate of 1.93581e-5 and a warmup ratio of 0.128960. An inductive bias is added to ensure the model only selects cells of the same column. The model's performance on the development set for the small version is 0.3681 for the noreset version and 0.3762 for the reset version. The team releasing TAPAS did not write a model card for this model; this model card was written by the Hugging Face team and contributors.
domain: Natural Language Processing Table Question Answering

instruction: Please identify the key elements in this design layout and explain their arrangement.
description: Qwen2.5-VL-32B-Instruct is a vision-language model designed to understand and process visual and textual information. It has been enhanced through reinforcement learning to improve mathematical and problem-solving abilities, as well as subjective user experience, with response styles adjusted to better align with human preferences. The model is capable of recognizing common objects and analyzing texts, charts, icons, graphics, and layouts within images. It functions as a visual agent that can reason and dynamically direct tools, enabling computer and phone use. It can understand long videos of over one hour and capture events by pinpointing relevant video segments. It supports visual localization in different formats by generating bounding boxes or points and provides stable JSON outputs for coordinates and attributes. It can generate structured outputs for data like scans of invoices, forms, and tables, which is beneficial for applications in finance and commerce. The model architecture includes dynamic resolution and frame rate training for video understanding, which extends dynamic resolution to the temporal dimension by adopting dynamic FPS sampling. This allows the model to comprehend videos at various sampling rates and learn temporal sequence and speed, enabling the ability to pinpoint specific moments. The vision encoder is streamlined and efficient, with training and inference speeds enhanced by implementing window attention into the ViT. The ViT architecture is further optimized with SwiGLU and RMSNorm, aligning it with the structure of the Qwen2.5 LLM. The model has 32 billion parameters and is instruction-tuned. Evaluation results show strong performance across various vision and text benchmarks, including MMMU, MathVista, OCRBenchV2, DocVQA, and MMLU. The model supports input formats such as local files, base64, and URLs for images, and local files for videos. It can handle batch inference and long texts with a context length up to 32,768 tokens, with an option to use YaRN for enhancing model length extrapolation. However, this method is not recommended for tasks involving temporal and spatial localization. For long video inputs, the max_position_embeddings can be modified to a larger value, such as 64k.

instruction: Create a clip showing a peaceful beach at sunset with waves gently crashing and seagulls flying overhead.
description: Cosmos-1.0-Diffusion is a suite of diffusion-based world foundation models designed for generating physics-aware videos and world states to support physical AI development. The models are part of the Cosmos World Foundation Models family and are built to generate dynamic, high-quality videos from text, image, or video inputs. They can be used as building blocks for various applications and research related to world generation. The models are developed by NVIDIA and are available for commercial use under the NVIDIA Open Model License agreement. The Cosmos Diffusion WFM family includes models that can generate a 121-frame video from a text description or predict 120 future frames from a text description and an initial image. The Cosmos-1.0-Diffusion-7B-Text2World model is a diffusion transformer designed for video denoising in the latent space. It uses interleaved self-attention, cross-attention, and feedforward layers. Cross-attention layers allow the model to condition on input text during the denoising process. Adaptive layer normalization is applied before each layer to embed time information. When images or videos are provided as input, their latent frames are concatenated with the generated frames along the temporal dimension. Augment noise is added to conditional latent frames to bridge the training and inference gap. The model accepts text as input, with input strings limited to fewer than 300 words and containing descriptive content for world generation, such as scene descriptions, key objects, characters, background, and specific actions or motions. The output is a video in mp4 format, typically a 5-second clip with a resolution of 1280x704 pixels and a frame rate of 24 fps. The video visualizes the input text as a short animated scene, capturing key elements within the time constraints. Aspect ratios and resolutions are configurable, including 1:1 (960x960), 4:3 (960x704), 3:4 (704x960), 16:9 (1280x704), and 9:16 (704x1280). Frame rates can be adjusted between 12 and 40 fps. The model is compatible with NVIDIA Blackwell, Hopper, and Ampere hardware microarchitectures and is tested with BF16 precision. It is supported on Linux operating systems. The model is intended for use by physical AI developers and is designed to generate videos based on video inputs. It is verified to meet prescribed NVIDIA quality standards and includes quantitative and qualitative evaluation metrics. The model may not follow the video input accurately and can generate all forms of videos, including potentially toxic, offensive, or indecent content. The model is not known to generate or reverse engineer personal information, nor is it known to use protected class data. No consent was obtained for any personal data used, and no personal data was collected directly by NVIDIA. The model is tagged for known restrictions and potential safety implications. The model is subject to a safety guardrail, and bypassing or disabling this guardrail will terminate the user's rights under the license. The model is available for use with the Diffusers library and can be accessed via the Hugging Face model catalog. Inference time and GPU memory usage vary depending on the offloading strategy and hardware. For example, on a single H100 GPU, the 7B Text2World model with prompt upsampler offloading takes approximately 380 seconds for end-to-end inference and uses up to 74.0 GB of GPU memory. The 14B Text2World model with prompt upsampler and guardrails offloading takes approximately 590 seconds and uses up to 70.5 GB of GPU memory. Users are advised to select an appropriate offloading strategy based on their GPU capabilities. The model is reviewed before release and is tagged for known restrictions and potential safety implications. Developers are encouraged to ensure the model meets requirements for their specific industry and use case and to address any potential misuse.

instruction: Is this image considered sensual or enticing based on its content?
description: nsfw-image-detection is a vision-language encoder model fine-tuned from siglip2-base-patch16-256 for multi-class image classification. Built on the SiglipForImageClassification architecture, the model is trained to identify and categorize content types in images, especially for explicit, suggestive, or safe media filtering. The model classifies each image into one of the following content categories: Anime Picture, Hentai, Normal, Pornography, or Enticing or Sensual. Evaluation results show a weighted average accuracy of 0.9137 across 26483 samples. For Anime Picture, the model achieves a precision of 0.8940, recall of 0.8718, and f1-score of 0.8827. For Hentai, the precision is 0.8961, recall is 0.8935, and f1-score is 0.8948. For Normal, the precision is 0.9100, recall is 0.8895, and f1-score is 0.8997. For Pornography, the precision is 0.9496, recall is 0.9654, and f1-score is 0.9574. For Enticing or Sensual, the precision is 0.9132, recall is 0.9429, and f1-score is 0.9278.

instruction: Can you help me train a system to assess the risk of insurance claims using textual descriptions and claim details?
description: TabSTAR is a foundation tabular model with semantically target-aware representations, designed to enable transfer learning on tabular data that includes textual features. The model is free of dataset-specific parameters and is intended to improve performance on tabular learning tasks, which have historically been dominated by gradient boosting decision trees. TabSTAR incorporates a pretrained text encoder that is unfrozen during training, allowing the model to learn task-specific embeddings. It takes target tokens as input, providing the necessary context for learning. TabSTAR achieves state-of-the-art performance on classification tasks with text features across both medium- and large-sized datasets. Its pretraining phase demonstrates scaling laws in the number of datasets, suggesting potential for further performance improvements. The model supports both classification and regression tasks, as demonstrated in example usage. TabSTAR is released under the MIT license.

instruction: How would you explain the significance of this historical event?
description: Gemma is a family of lightweight, state-of-the-art open models from Google, built from the same research and technology used to create the Gemini models. Gemma 3 models are multimodal, handling text and image input and generating text output, with open weights for both pre-trained variants and instruction-tuned variants. Gemma 3 has a large, 128K context window, multilingual support in over 140 languages, and is available in more sizes than previous versions. Gemma 3 models are well-suited for a variety of text generation and image understanding tasks, including question answering, summarization, and reasoning. Their relatively small size makes it possible to deploy them in environments with limited resources such as laptops, desktops or your own cloud infrastructure, democratizing access to state of the art AI models and helping foster innovation for everyone. The input includes text strings such as questions, prompts, or documents to be summarized, and images normalized to 896 x 896 resolution and encoded to 256 tokens each. The total input context is 128K tokens for the 4B, 12B, and 27B sizes, and 32K tokens for the 1B size. The output is generated text in response to the input, such as an answer to a question, analysis of image content, or a summary of a document, with a total output context of 8192 tokens. The models were trained on a dataset of text data that includes a wide variety of sources. The 27B model was trained with 14 trillion tokens, the 12B model with 12 trillion tokens, the 4B model with 4 trillion tokens, and the 1B model with 2 trillion tokens. The training data includes web documents, code, mathematics, and images. Rigorous CSAM filtering was applied at multiple stages in the data preparation process to ensure the exclusion of harmful and illegal content. Automated techniques were used to filter out certain personal information and other sensitive data from training sets. Additional methods were applied for content quality and safety in line with safety policies. Gemma was trained using Tensor Processing Unit (TPU) hardware (TPUv4p, TPUv5p, and TPUv5e). TPUs are specifically designed to handle the massive computations involved in training vision-language models (VLMs), offering performance, memory, scalability, and cost-effectiveness advantages. Training was done using JAX and ML Pathways. JAX allows researchers to take advantage of the latest generation of hardware, including TPUs, for faster and more efficient training of large models. ML Pathways is Google's latest effort to build artificially intelligent systems capable of generalizing across multiple tasks, which is especially suitable for foundation models. The 'single controller' programming model of JAX and Pathways allows a single Python process to orchestrate the entire training run, dramatically simplifying the development workflow. The models were evaluated against a large collection of different datasets and metrics to cover different aspects of text generation. In reasoning and factuality, the models showed performance across various benchmarks such as HellaSwag, BoolQ, PIQA, SocialIQA, TriviaQA, Natural Questions, ARC-c, ARC-e, WinoGrande, BIG-Bench Hard, and DROP. In STEM and code, the models were evaluated on benchmarks such as MMLU, AGIEval, MATH, GSM8K, GPQA, MBPP, and HumanEval. In multilingual tasks, the models were evaluated on benchmarks such as MGSM, Global-MMLU-Lite, WMT24++, FloRes, XQuAD, ECLeKTic, and IndicGenBench. In multimodal tasks, the models were evaluated on benchmarks such as COCOcap, DocVQA, InfoVQA, MMMU, TextVQA, RealWorldQA, ReMI, AI2D, ChartQA, VQAv2, BLINK, OKVQA, TallyQA, SpatialSense VQA, and CountBenchQA. The evaluation methods included structured evaluations and internal red-teaming testing of relevant content policies. Red-teaming was conducted by different teams with different goals and human evaluation metrics. The models were evaluated against categories such as child safety, content safety, and representational harms. In addition to development level evaluations, assurance evaluations were conducted separately from the model development team to inform decision making about release. For all areas of safety testing, the models showed major improvements in the categories of child safety, content safety, and representational harms relative to previous Gemma models. All testing was conducted without safety filters to evaluate the model capabilities and behaviors. The models produced minimal policy violations and showed significant improvements over previous Gemma models' performance with respect to ungrounded inferences. A limitation of the evaluations was that they included only English language prompts. The models have certain limitations that users should be aware of. The quality and diversity of the training data significantly influence the model's capabilities. Biases or gaps in the training data can lead to limitations in the model's responses. The scope of the training dataset determines the subject areas the model can handle effectively. Models are better at tasks that can be framed with clear prompts and instructions. Open-ended or highly complex tasks might be challenging. A model's performance can be influenced by the amount of context provided (longer context generally leads to better outputs, up to a certain point). Natural language is inherently complex. Models might struggle to grasp subtle nuances, sarcasm, or figurative language. Models generate responses based on information they learned from their training datasets, but they are not knowledge bases. They may generate incorrect or outdated factual statements. Models rely on statistical patterns in language. They might lack the ability to apply common sense reasoning in certain situations. The development of vision-language models (VLMs) raises several ethical concerns. VLMs trained on large-scale, real-world text and image data can reflect socio-cultural biases embedded in the training material. These models underwent careful scrutiny, input data pre-processing, and posterior evaluations. VLMs can be misused to generate text that is false, misleading, or harmful. Guidelines are provided for responsible use with the model. Transparency and accountability are emphasized by summarizing details on the models' architecture, capabilities, limitations, and evaluation processes. A responsibly developed open model offers the opportunity to share innovation by making VLM technology accessible to developers and researchers across the AI ecosystem. Risks identified include the perpetuation of biases, generation of harmful content, misuse for malicious purposes, and privacy violations. It is encouraged to perform continuous monitoring and the exploration of de-biasing techniques during model training, fine-tuning, and other use cases. Mechanisms and guidelines for content safety are essential. Developers are encouraged to exercise caution and implement appropriate content safety safeguards based on their specific product policies and application use cases. Technical limitations and developer and end-user education can help mitigate against malicious applications of VLMs. Educational resources and reporting mechanisms for users to flag misuse are provided. Prohibited uses of Gemma models are outlined in the Gemma Prohibited Use Policy. Models were trained on data filtered for removal of certain personal information and other sensitive data. Developers are encouraged to adhere to privacy regulations with privacy-preserving techniques. At the time of release, this family of models provides high-performance open vision-language model implementations designed from the ground up for responsible AI development compared to similarly sized models. Using the benchmark evaluation metrics described in this document, these models have shown to provide superior performance to other, comparably-sized open model alternatives.

description: This is an xlm-roberta-large model that has been fine-tuned on multilingual training data in English, German, Hungarian, Spanish, and Slovakian, labeled with media codes. The model was trained using 18 media codes, including State and Local Government Administration, Weather, Fires, emergencies and natural disasters, Crime and trials, Arts, culture, entertainment and history, Style and fashion, Food, Travel, Wellbeing and learning, Personal finance and real estate, Personal technology and popular science, Churches and Religion, Celebrities and human interest, Obituaries and death notices, Sports, Crosswords, puzzles, comics, Media production/internal, letters, and Advertisements. The model is designed for text classification tasks and is intended to categorize text into one of these media codes. It was evaluated on a test set of 74,322 English examples, achieving an accuracy of 0.79, a precision of 0.77, a recall of 0.77, and a weighted average F1-score of 0.79. Performance varies by class, with some categories achieving high precision and recall, such as Weather (0.98 precision and recall), Fires, emergencies and natural disasters (0.96 precision, 0.98 recall), and Churches and Religion (0.92 precision, 0.94 recall). However, some categories, such as Advertisements, have very low performance, with 0 precision, recall, and F1-score.
