instruction: Can you help me write a compelling email to my professor about a missed deadline?
description: Qwen2.5 is the latest series of Qwen large language models. The Qwen2.5 series includes a range of base language models and instruction-tuned language models with parameter sizes ranging from 0.5 to 72 billion. Qwen2.5 introduces several improvements over Qwen2, including significantly more knowledge and enhanced capabilities in coding and mathematics, achieved through specialized expert models in these domains. It also shows significant improvements in instruction following, generating long texts (over 8K tokens), understanding structured data such as tables, and generating structured outputs, especially JSON. The model is more resilient to the diversity of system prompts, which enhances role-play implementation and condition-setting for chatbots. It supports long-context processing up to 128K tokens and can generate up to 8K tokens. The model provides multilingual support for over 29 languages, including Chinese, English, French, Spanish, Portuguese, German, Italian, Russian, Japanese, Korean, Vietnamese, Thai, and Arabic. This specific model is the instruction-tuned 3B Qwen2.5 model. It is a causal language model that has undergone pretraining and post-training. The architecture includes transformers with RoPE, SwiGLU, RMSNorm, attention QKV bias, and tied word embeddings. It has 3.09 billion parameters, with 2.77 billion non-embedding parameters. The model consists of 36 layers and uses 16 attention heads for Q and 2 for KV. It supports a context length of 32,768 tokens and can generate up to 8192 tokens. The code for Qwen2.5 is available in the latest Hugging Face `transformers` library, and it is recommended to use the latest version to avoid errors. Using versions of `transformers` earlier than 4.37.0 may result in a KeyError: 'qwen2'. Detailed evaluation results are available in a separate document, and information on GPU memory requirements and throughput is also provided. If you find this work helpful, you may cite the associated publications.

description: This model is based on a pre-trained model and fine-tuned on a combination of NLI data in 15 languages. It is designed for zero-shot text classification, particularly in languages other than English. The model is fine-tuned on XNLI, a multilingual NLI dataset, and can be used with any of the languages in the XNLI corpus, including English, French, Spanish, German, Greek, Bulgarian, Russian, Turkish, Arabic, Vietnamese, Thai, Chinese, Hindi, Swahili, and Urdu. Since the base model was pre-trained on 100 different languages, it has shown some effectiveness in languages beyond those listed. For English-only classification, it is recommended to use other models. The model can be loaded with the zero-shot-classification pipeline and used to classify text in any of the supported languages. It is possible to pass the labels in one language and the sequence to classify in another. The default hypothesis template is the English phrase 'This text is {}', which can be translated to the language being used. The model was pre-trained on a set of 100 languages and then fine-tuned on the task of NLI on the concatenated MNLI train set and the XNLI validation and test sets. It was further trained for one additional epoch on only XNLI data where the translations for the premise and hypothesis are shuffled such that the premise and hypothesis for each example come from the same original English example but are in different languages.

description: The RT-DETRv2 model was proposed by Wenyu Lv, Yian Zhao, Qinyao Chang, Kui Huang, Guanzhong Wang, and Yi Liu. RT-DETRv2 refines RT-DETR by introducing selective multi-scale feature extraction, a discrete sampling operator for broader deployment compatibility, and improved training strategies like dynamic data augmentation and scale-adaptive hyperparameters. These changes enhance flexibility and practicality while maintaining real-time performance. RT-DETRv2 consistently outperforms its predecessor across all model sizes while maintaining the same real-time speeds. RT-DETRv2 is trained on COCO (Lin et al. [2014]) train2017 and validated on COCO val2017 dataset. We report the standard AP metrics (averaged over uniformly sampled IoU thresholds ranging from 0.50 − 0.95 with a step size of 0.05), and APval50 commonly used in real scenarios. RT-DETRv2 is ideal for real-time object detection in diverse applications such as autonomous driving, surveillance systems, robotics, and retail analytics. Its enhanced flexibility and deployment-friendly design make it suitable for both edge devices and large-scale systems and ensures high accuracy and speed in dynamic, real-world environments.

description: The mxbai-rerank-base-v2 is the base model in the family of powerful reranker models from Mixedbread. It is designed to support a range of tasks, including retrieval, with state-of-the-art performance and strong efficiency. The model provides multilingual support for over 100 languages, with particularly strong performance in English and Chinese. It also supports code and long-context processing. The model is suitable for reranking tasks and can be used to rank documents based on a given query. It is trained using a three-step process that includes GRPO (Guided Reinforcement Prompt Optimization), contrastive learning, and preference learning. Performance benchmarks show that the mxbai-rerank-base-v2 achieves an average BEIR score of 55.57, with a multilingual score of 28.56, a Chinese score of 83.70, and a code search score of 31.73. The model has a latency of 0.67 seconds when measured on an A100 GPU. The technical report is coming soon. If you find the model useful, you are encouraged to give it a star and citation.

instruction: What actions are taking place in this visual?
description: VideoLLaMA 3 is a state-of-the-art series of multimodal foundation models designed to excel in both image and video understanding tasks. These models leverage advanced architectures to demonstrate exceptional capabilities in processing and interpreting visual content across various contexts. VideoLLaMA 3 is specifically designed to address complex multimodal challenges, such as integrating textual and visual information, extracting insights from sequential video data, and performing high-level reasoning over both dynamic and static visual scenes. The model series includes variants such as VideoLLaMA3-7B, VideoLLaMA3-2B, VideoLLaMA3-7B-Image, and VideoLLaMA3-2B-Image, each based on different base models including Qwen2.5-7B and Qwen2.5-1.5B. Additionally, a tuned vision encoder based on siglip-so400m-patch14-384 is available for VideoLLaMA3-7B for wider application. The models are intended for use in tasks involving image and video understanding, including but not limited to answering questions about visual content and reasoning over visual scenes.

instruction: Could you tell me which birds are most likely calling in this audio from a local park?
description: AudioProtoPNet is an interpretable deep learning model designed for multi-label bird sound classification. It is an adaptation of the Prototypical Part Network (ProtoPNet), which provides explanations for its decisions by comparing recordings to learned prototypes in an embedding space. The model uses a ConvNeXt backbone to extract embeddings from spectrograms of bird vocalizations and a prototype learning classifier trained on these embeddings to learn prototypical patterns for each bird species. AudioProtoPNet was trained on the BirdSet training dataset, which includes 9734 bird species and over 6800 hours of recordings. The model's performance was evaluated on seven BirdSet test datasets covering diverse geographical regions. AudioProtoPNet achieved an average AUROC of 0.90 and a cmAP of 0.42, representing relative improvements of 7.1% and 16.7% over the state-of-the-art model Perch, respectively. Evaluation results show that models with five, ten, and twenty prototypes per class performed similarly, while the model with only one prototype per class showed slightly lower performance. AudioProtoPNet-5 notably outperformed both Perch and ConvNeXt in terms of cmAP, AUROC, and top-1 accuracy scores. The model is designed to provide valuable insights for professionals in ornithology and machine learning by offering a powerful yet interpretable approach to the challenging task of multi-label bird sound classification.

instruction: Could you analyze this audio and give me a speaker activity report?
description: The `community-1` speaker diarization pipeline processes mono audio sampled at 16kHz and outputs speaker diarization. It automatically downmixes stereo or multi-channel audio to mono by averaging the channels and resamples audio files to 16kHz if they are at a different rate. The model supports speaker assignment and counting, simpler reconciliation with transcription timestamps using speaker diarization, and local processing without an internet connection. It also optionally supports processing on the pyannoteAI cloud. The pipeline is designed for use with the `pyannote.audio` library and can be run locally on a computer. It is reported to perform better than the `speaker-diarization-3.1` model, with performance metrics in terms of error rates (in percent) on a large collection of academic benchmarks, including 11.7% for the general case, 20.3% for channel 1, 17.0% for IHM, 19.9% for SDM, 44.6% for another benchmark, 26.7% for a specific benchmark, 20.2% for another, 46.8% for a development set, 22.8% for a different benchmark, 20.8% for another, 8.9% for phase2, and 11.2% for v0.3. The model can be processed on GPU by moving the pipeline to a CUDA device. Audio files can be pre-loaded in memory for faster processing. Progress monitoring is supported through hooks. The number of speakers can be controlled using the `num_speakers`, `min_speakers`, and `max_speakers` options. The pipeline also provides an exclusive speaker diarization, which helps in reconciling fine-grained speaker diarization timestamps with transcription timestamps. For offline use, the pipeline can be cloned to disk and loaded without an internet connection. The model is based on a speaker segmentation model, a speaker embedding model, and a speaker clustering method. Training and tuning were supported by resources on a supercomputer.

instruction: Can you help me optimize the search results by re-ranking them based on context?
description: The gte-modernbert series of models is built upon the latest modernBERT pre-trained encoder-only foundation models. These models include both text embedding models and rerank models. The gte-modernbert models demonstrate competitive performance in several text embedding and text retrieval evaluation tasks when compared to similar-scale models from the current open-source community. This includes assessments such as MTEB, LoCO, and COIR evaluation. The gte-modernbert-base model is developed by Tongyi Lab, Alibaba Group. It is a text embedding model primarily designed for English. The model has a size of 149 million parameters, a maximum input length of 8192 tokens, and an output dimension of 768. The model is designed to support text embedding and reranking tasks. It is evaluated on multiple benchmarks, including MTEB, LoCO, COIR, and BEIR. On the MTEB benchmark, the model achieves an average score of 64.38. On the LoCO benchmark, it achieves an average score of 87.57. On the COIR benchmark, it achieves an average score of 79.31. On the BEIR benchmark, it achieves an average score of 55.33. The model is designed to be used with the transformers and sentence-transformers libraries. It supports GPU acceleration with Flash Attention 2 if installed. The model can be deployed using Docker for CPU or GPU environments. The model is based on the training scheme of previous models, with the pre-training language model base replaced. The model is described in the paper titled 'mGTE: Generalized Long-Context Text Representation and Reranking Models for Multilingual Text Retrieval' and another paper titled 'Towards general text embeddings with multi-stage contrastive learning'. The model is available for use and deployment, and the developers are seeking research interns and full-time researchers to join their team at Tongyi Lab in Beijing and Hangzhou.

instruction: Can you walk me through a step-by-step example of how to get the most out of this system?
description: This model is a fine-tuned version of an unspecified base model on an unknown dataset. It is designed to perform a specific task, though the intended use is not explicitly described. The model achieves a loss of 5.7326 on the evaluation set. The training process used the following hyperparameters: learning rate of 5e-05, training batch size of 4, evaluation batch size of 8, random seed of 42, AdamW optimizer with betas of (0.9, 0.999) and epsilon of 1e-08, linear learning rate scheduler, and 20 training epochs. During training, the model's training loss and validation loss were recorded at various steps and epochs. The training loss decreased over time, reaching a minimum of 0.0006 at step 4150, epoch 18.3628, while the validation loss fluctuated and reached a final value of 5.7326 at step 4500, epoch 19.9115. The model was trained using the Transformers 4.46.2, PyTorch 2.5.1+cu121, Datasets 3.1.0, and Tokenizers 0.20.3 frameworks. More information is needed regarding the model's intended uses, limitations, and the training and evaluation data.

instruction: Can you extract the key points from this long article and present them in a few sentences?
description: BART is a large-sized model pre-trained on the English language and fine-tuned on the CNN Daily Mail dataset. It was introduced in the paper by Lewis et al. and first released in 2019. The model is designed for natural language generation, translation, and comprehension tasks. It supports summarization, as demonstrated by its ability to generate concise summaries of input text. The model's architecture is based on a denoising sequence-to-sequence pre-training approach. Performance results are not explicitly detailed in the description. The model includes explicit caveats related to its application context, such as the example of generating a summary of a news article about Liana Barrientos, who is facing legal charges related to multiple marriages and an immigration scam. The model is available through the Hugging Face Transformers library and can be used with a summarization pipeline.

instruction: How can I create a mask of a person in a photo for a virtual background application?
description: U-2-Net-Human-Seg is a specialised version of the U-2-Net model designed specifically for human segmentation tasks. This model excels in distinguishing human figures from the background in images, making it particularly useful for applications such as background removal, virtual try-ons, and human-centric image editing. By leveraging a deep learning approach, U-2-Net-Human-Seg can accurately segment human subjects in various poses and environments, providing high-quality segmentation masks that can be utilized in different imaging tasks. The U-2-Net-Human-Seg model is based on a simplified version of the original U-2-Net architecture, designed to be more lightweight while still achieving high performance in segmentation tasks. The model consists of several stages with down-sampling and up-sampling paths, using Residual U-blocks (RSU) for enhanced feature representation. This model is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 to match the original U-2-Net model.

instruction: Can you locate a visually structured guide that outlines the anatomy of the human heart with labeled diagrams?
description: ColNomic Embed Multimodal 3B is a state-of-the-art multimodal embedding model designed for visual document retrieval tasks. It is a 3B parameter vision-language model that directly encodes interleaved text and images without requiring complex preprocessing. The model is fine-tuned from Qwen2.5-VL 3B Instruct and features a unified text and image input processing architecture. It includes key innovations such as same-source sampling to create harder in-batch negatives and a multi-vector output option for enhanced performance. The model achieves 61.2 NDCG@5 on the Vidore-v2 benchmark, outperforming all other models except ColNomic Embed Multimodal 7B. It supports a variety of real-world document retrieval scenarios, including research papers, technical documentation, product catalogs, financial reports, visually rich content, and multilingual documents. The model integrates with Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) workflows by enabling direct document embedding, faster processing, and capturing both textual and visual cues in a single embedding. It is available for research use with open weights. Performance may vary when processing documents with unconventional layouts, unusual visual elements, handwriting, or heavily stylized fonts. Performance is strongest on English content, and very large or complex documents may require chunking for optimal results.

instruction: What is the estimated housing price for a house in a neighborhood with a median income of $60,000 and a house age of 40 years?
description: This is a RandomForestQuantileRegressor trained on the California Housing dataset. The model is not ready to be used in production. It was trained using default parameters on a 5-fold cross-validation pipeline. The hyperparameters include bootstrap set to True, ccp_alpha set to 0.0, criterion set to 'squared_error', default_quantiles set to 0.5, max_features set to 1.0, max_samples_leaf set to 1, min_impurity_decrease set to 0.0, min_samples_leaf set to 1, min_samples_split set to 2, min_weight_fraction_leaf set to 0.0, n_estimators set to 100, oob_score set to False, random_state set to RandomState(MT19937), verbose set to 0, and warm_start set to False. The model's evaluation results include a Mean Absolute Percentage Error of 0.164007, a Median Absolute Error of 0.171, a Mean Squared Error of 0.25832, and an R-Squared value of 0.806.

instruction: Can you take this audio of a business meeting and transcribe it into English?
description: Granite-speech-3.3-2b is a compact and efficient speech-language model designed for automatic speech recognition (ASR) and automatic speech translation (AST). It uses a two-pass design, where the first pass transcribes audio into text, and the second pass processes the transcribed text using the underlying Granite language model. Each step must be explicitly initiated by the user. The model was trained on a collection of public corpora and synthetic datasets tailored for ASR and AST tasks. Revision 3.3.2 supports multilingual speech inputs in English, French, German, Spanish, and Portuguese, and provides transcription accuracy improvements for English ASR by using a deeper acoustic encoder and additional training data. The model is intended for enterprise applications involving speech input processing, particularly for speech-to-text and speech translation to and from English for the same languages, as well as English-to-Japanese and English-to-Mandarin. It can also handle text-only inputs by calling the underlying granite-3.3-2b-instruct model when no audio is provided. The architecture includes a speech encoder with 16 conformer blocks trained with CTC on character-level targets, a speech projector and temporal downsampler using a 2-layer window query transformer, and a large language model with 128k context length. LoRA adapters with rank=64 are applied to the query and value projection matrices. The model was trained on a diverse set of datasets, including CommonVoice, MLS, Librispeech, VoxPopuli, AMI, YODAS, Earnings-22, Switchboard, CallHome, Fisher, and Voicemail datasets for ASR, and CommonVoice and Phi-4 translations for AST. Training was conducted on IBM's Blue Vela supercomputing cluster with NVIDIA H100 GPUs over 13 days using 32 GPUs. Ethical considerations include the potential for inaccurate, biased, or offensive outputs, as well as the risk of adversarial inputs. The model's modular design enhances safety by limiting how audio inputs can influence the system. IBM recommends using the model alongside Granite Guardian, a fine-tuned instruct model for detecting and flagging risks in prompts and responses.

instruction: What options do I have for creating diverse training scenarios for a home assistant robot?
description: RoboEngine is a plug-and-play visual robot data augmentation toolkit designed to enable users to generate physics- and task-aware robot scenes with minimal effort, using just a few lines of code. It enhances the visual robustness of imitation learning by overcoming limitations of existing methods, which often require camera calibration or controlled environments. The toolkit includes a novel robot scene segmentation dataset, a generalizable high-quality robot segmentation model, and a fine-tuned background generation model. These components work together to form an out-of-the-box solution for data augmentation. Using RoboEngine, robot manipulation tasks can be generalized across six entirely new scenes based solely on demonstrations collected from a single scene, resulting in a more than 200% performance improvement compared to the no-augmentation baseline. The datasets, model weights, and toolkit are publicly available.

instruction: Can you process this audio and give me a clean transcript with speaker identification?
description: Kotoba-Whisper-v2.2 is a Japanese automatic speech recognition (ASR) model that includes additional postprocessing stacks. It features speaker diarization and punctuation addition. The model was developed through collaboration between unspecified entities. It is supported in the Hugging Face Transformers library from version 4.39 onwards. The model can transcribe audio with speaker diarization, identifying different speakers and their corresponding speech segments with timestamps. It also supports adding punctuation to the transcribed text. Users can control the number of speakers by specifying parameters such as num_speakers, min_speakers, and max_speakers. Adding silence before and after the audio can improve transcription quality. The model can utilize Flash Attention 2 for improved performance on compatible GPUs. To use the model, users must install specific dependencies, including the Transformers library, accelerate, torchaudio, punctuators, and pyannote.audio. Additionally, users must accept the terms-of-use for certain models and log in with a Hugging Face authentication token to access pre-trained diarization models. The model outputs transcribed text with speaker identification, timestamps, and optional punctuation. The pipeline is implemented in Python using the Transformers library and supports batch processing and device-specific configurations.

instruction: What is the text on the shirt the person is wearing?
description: The Video-R1-7B model is a video understanding model designed to process and analyze video content. It supports a range of tasks, including answering questions about video content in various formats such as free-form, multiple choice, numerical, OCR, and regression. The model is built to handle multimodal inputs, including video and text, and is capable of generating detailed reasoning and final answers based on the input. It uses a processor and tokenizer for input preparation and relies on an LLM for inference, with configurable parameters such as temperature, top_p, and max_tokens. The model is trained to process video inputs with a maximum of 32 frames and 200704 pixels per frame. It is implemented using PyTorch and the vLLM library for efficient inference. The model is intended for use in scenarios where video content analysis and question answering are required, and it is provided with example code for inference on a single video example. However, the model's specific training details, evaluation results, and performance metrics are not described in the provided text.

instruction: How do I process this high-resolution image to extract the object with fine details?
description: BiRefNet is a model designed for high-resolution dichotomous image segmentation, specifically for image matting with transparency. It was trained using images of resolution 2048x2048 to support higher resolution processing. The model is intended for tasks such as standard dichotomous image segmentation (DIS), high-resolution salient object detection (HRSOD), and camouflage object detection (COD). It has achieved state-of-the-art performance on these tasks. The model's performance was evaluated on datasets including TE-AM-2k and TE-P3M-500-NP, with metrics such as maxFm, wFmeasure, MAE, Smeasure, meanEm, HCE, maxEm, meanFm, adpEm, adpFm, mBA, maxBIoU, and meanBIoU reported for different resolutions. The model was trained on the DIS-TR dataset and validated on DIS-TEs and DIS-VD. It is implemented using PyTorch and can be loaded via the HuggingFace Transformers library or from local GitHub repositories. The model supports inference on GPU in FP16 mode. Users are advised to consider the potential costs associated with deploying the model as an inference endpoint. The model is described in the paper 'Bilateral Reference for High-Resolution Dichotous Image Segmentation' by Peng Zheng, Dehong Gao, Deng-Ping Fan, Li Liu, Jorma Laaksonen, Wanli Ouyang, and Nicu Sebe, published in CAAI Artificial Intelligence Research in 2024.

instruction: Can you create a 3D sculpture based on my abstract sketch and the description 'minimalist and futuristic'?
description: TripoSG-scribble is a model that converts a scribble image and a text prompt into a 3D shape. It is a variant of TripoSG, a state-of-the-art image-to-3D generation foundation model that uses large-scale rectified flow transformers to produce high-fidelity 3D shapes from single images. TripoSG-scribble employs a novel architecture that includes a rectified flow (RF) based Transformer for stable, linear trajectory modeling, an advanced VAE with SDF-based representation and hybrid geometric supervision, and a cross-attention mechanism for image feature conditioning. The model operates on 1.5B parameters and 512 latent tokens. For inference efficiency, TripoSG-scribble is a CFG-distilled model and should be used with CFG=0. The model is intended for converting scribble images and text prompts into high-quality 3D meshes, supporting creative and design applications, gaming and VFX asset creation, and prototyping and visualization. It requires a CUDA-capable GPU with more than 8GB VRAM. TripoSG-scribble is developed by Tripo, pushing the boundaries of 3D generative AI.

instruction: Please identify the different types of vehicles in this image from a highway camera.
description: The D-FINE model was proposed by Yansong Peng, Hebei Li, Peixi Wu, Yueyi Zhang, Xiaoyan Sun, and Feng Wu. This is the Hugging Face Transformers implementation for D-FINE. D-FINE is a powerful real-time object detector that achieves outstanding localization precision by redefining the bounding box regression task in DETR models. The model comprises two key components: Fine-grained Distribution Refinement (FDR) and Global Optimal Localization Self-Distillation (GO-LSD). D-FINE is trained on COCO (Lin et al. [2014]) train2017 and validated on COCO val2017 dataset. The standard AP metrics are reported, averaged over uniformly sampled IoU thresholds ranging from 0.50 to 0.95 with a step size of 0.05, and APval5000 is also reported, which is commonly used in real scenarios. D-FINE is ideal for real-time object detection in diverse applications such as autonomous driving, surveillance systems, robotics, and retail analytics. Its enhanced flexibility and deployment-friendly design make it suitable for both edge devices and large-scale systems, ensuring high accuracy and speed in dynamic, real-world environments.

instruction: Comment interpréter le contexte de ce mot dans ce mémoire ampliatif ?
description: JuriBERT is a set of BERT models (tiny, mini, small, and base) pre-trained from scratch on French legal-domain specific corpora. The models are trained on 6.3GB of legal French raw text from two sources: one dataset is crawled from Légifrance, and the other consists of anonymized court decisions and pleadings from the Court of Cassation (mémoires ampliatifs), which contains more than 100,000 long documents from different court cases. JuriBERT is available on Hugging Face in four versions with varying numbers of parameters. The four models are dascim/juribert-tiny with 6 million parameters and a Tiny architecture (L=2, H=128, A=2), dascim/juribert-mini with 15 million parameters and a Mini architecture (L=4, H=256, A=4), dascim/juribert-small with 42 million parameters and a Small architecture (L=6, H=512, A=8), and dascim/juribert-base with 110 million parameters and a Base architecture (L=12, H=768, A=12). JuriBERT is designed to support tasks such as filling masks in legal French text and extracting contextual embedding features from its output. The model can be loaded along with its sub-word tokenizer using the Transformers library. JuriBERT was trained and evaluated at École Polytechnique in collaboration with HEC Paris by Stella Douka, Hadi Abdine, Mihalis Vazirgiannis, Rajaa El Hamdani, and David Restrepo Amariles.

instruction: Could you help me understand if this image is the result of human creativity or algorithmic processing?
description: This is a fine-tuned model trained on 60,000 AI-generated and 60,000 human images. The model is designed to classify images as either AI-generated or human-created. It supports image classification tasks and is based on the Siglip architecture. During training, the model was trained for 5 epochs with a total of 51,652,280,821 GFLOPs. The training loss was 0.0799, and the training process took 2 hours, 39 minutes, and 49.46 seconds, achieving a training speed of 69.053 samples per second and 4.316 steps per second. On the test set, the model achieved an evaluation accuracy of 0.9923 with an evaluation loss of 0.0551. The evaluation process took 2 minutes and 35.78 seconds, with a speed of 212.533 samples per second and 6.644 steps per second. The final test accuracy is 0.9923, with a macro F1 score of 0.9923 and a weighted F1 score of 0.9923. Some users have reported overfitting issues. The model can be used for inference by loading the model and processor, preprocessing the input image, and running inference to obtain predicted labels and confidence scores.

instruction: How well does this image capture the details of the subject?
description: VisualQuality-R1-7B is a non-reference image quality assessment (NR-IQA) model enhanced by reinforcement learning to rank (RL2R). It is designed to perform both quality description and rating through reasoning. The model is trained on a diverse combination of synthetic and realistic datasets. It is capable of assessing the quality of images by generating a rating as a float between 1 and 5, with 1 representing very poor quality and 5 representing excellent quality. The model supports both single and batch image quality rating tasks, with options for non-thinking mode to reduce inference time or thinking mode to include a reasoning process. The model is based on the Qwen2.5-VL architecture and uses the flash_attention_2 implementation for efficient computation. The model's training involves preparing IQA images in a single folder, generating a JSON file from an original MOS file, and executing training scripts either within a single node or across multiple nodes. The model's development was supported by the VLM-R1 codebase and acknowledges contributions from the field of AR generation. The model's paper has been accepted as a spotlight at NeurIPS 2025.

instruction: What is the value listed under 'Shareholder Equity' in this table?
description: FinTabQA-A4 is a model designed for financial table question-answering. It is based on the LayoutLM architecture and is intended to extract answers from financial table images. The model supports tasks involving the interpretation of table data in response to natural language questions. It processes input that includes both the text content of the table and its spatial layout information, such as word positions. FinTabQA-A4 was trained on the SynFinTabs dataset, a large-scale, labelled dataset of synthetic financial tables. The model was evaluated on real-world financial tables and compared to a state-of-the-art generative model, with results discussed in the associated research. The model is available for use via the Hugging Face Transformers library and requires a compatible tokenizer for input processing. The model's performance is demonstrated through an example where it correctly predicts an answer from a table image. The model's development and training methodology are described in a paper presented at the ICDAR 2025 Workshops. The authors emphasize the importance of domain-specific datasets for training models in financial table extraction and highlight the potential for their synthetic data generation method to be applied to other domains.

instruction: Identify the number of people in this retail store video and their positions.
description: The RT-DETRv2 model was proposed by Wenyu Lv, Yian Zhao, Qinyao Chang, Kui Huang, Guanzhong Wang, and Yi Liu. RT-DETRv2 refines RT-DETR by introducing selective multi-scale feature extraction, a discrete sampling operator for broader deployment compatibility, and improved training strategies like dynamic data augmentation and scale-adaptive hyperparameters. These changes enhance flexibility and practicality while maintaining real-time performance. RT-DETRv2 consistently outperforms its predecessor across all model sizes while maintaining the same real-time speeds. The model is trained on COCO (Lin et al. [2014]) train2017 and validated on COCO val2017 dataset. We report the standard AP metrics (averaged over uniformly sampled IoU thresholds ranging from 0.50 − 0.95 with a step size of 0.05), and APval50 commonly used in real scenarios. RT-DETRv2 is ideal for real-time object detection in diverse applications such as autonomous driving, surveillance systems, robotics, and retail analytics. Its enhanced flexibility and deployment-friendly design make it suitable for both edge devices and large-scale systems and ensures high accuracy and speed in dynamic, real-world environments.

instruction: Could you help me identify the top three responses that best match the query?
description: This is an improved version of a reranker based on a model trained on a large dataset of text pairs. The model was trained in the same way and on the same data as a previous model, with the following improvements: predictions from a specific model were used for distillation instead of another model, and a custom implementation of the RoBERTa model with support for Flash Attention 2 was used. To use these features, the model should be loaded with the arguments `trust_remote_code=True` and `attn_implementation="flash_attention_2"`. The reranker achieves results close to a specific model on the PIRB benchmark, even outperforming it on some datasets. It is over 21 times smaller, with 435M parameters compared to 9.24B parameters. The model can be used with Huggingface Transformers by importing necessary modules, loading the tokenizer and model with specific arguments, and processing input texts. The model achieves an NDCG@10 of 65.30 in the Rerankers category of the Polish Information Retrieval Benchmark.

instruction: How can I tell if the message I received is genuine or fake?
description: This model is based on DistilBERT and has been fine-tuned for multilabel classification of emails and URLs as safe or potentially phishing. The base architecture is DistilBERT, and the model was fine-tuned using the Hugging Face Trainer API over a training duration of 3 epochs. The model achieves an accuracy of 99.58, an F1-score of 99.579, a precision of 99.583, and a recall of 99.58. It was trained on a custom dataset of emails and URLs labeled as legitimate or phishing, which is available on the Hugging Face Hub. The model supports the classification of input text into four categories: legitimate_email, phishing_url, legitimate_url, and phishing_url_alt. Predictions are generated by tokenizing the input text, passing it through the model, and applying a softmax function to the output logits to obtain class probabilities. The most likely classification is determined by selecting the label with the highest probability. The model provides a prediction, confidence score, and probabilities for all classes as output.

instruction: Can you create a 3D model of a futuristic car based on my description?
description: LLaMA-Mesh is a pre-trained model designed to unify 3D mesh generation with language models. It enables the generation of 3D meshes from text prompts, the production of interleaved text and 3D mesh outputs, and the understanding and interpretation of 3D meshes. The model represents vertex coordinates and face definitions of 3D meshes as plain text, allowing direct integration with large language models (LLMs) without expanding the vocabulary. LLaMA-Mesh is built on the Llama 3.1 architecture, a Transformer-based model, and is fine-tuned using a supervised dataset derived from the Objaverse. The dataset includes 30,000 mesh data samples, each with fewer than 500 faces, converted into text format. The model is trained using 32 GPUs and supports input and output in text format with a maximum token length of 8k. It is licensed under the NSCLv1 license for non-commercial use and incorporates components of Llama 3.1 technology under the Llama 3.1 Community License Agreement. The model is attributed as 'Built with Llama' in accordance with the license requirements. Ethical considerations emphasize the shared responsibility of Trustworthy AI, and users are advised to ensure the model meets industry and use case requirements. The model is compatible with NVIDIA Ada microarchitecture and Linux operating systems, and inference is performed using PyTorch on A100 test hardware. The dataset used is licensed under the ODC-By v1.0 license.

instruction: Can you generate a short description of the image that captures its main elements and mood?
description: SigLIP 2 Large extends the pretraining objective with prior, independently developed techniques into a unified recipe, for improved semantic understanding, localization, and dense features. The model can be used for tasks like zero-shot image classification and image-text retrieval, or as a vision encoder for VLMs and other vision tasks. SigLIP 2 adds training objectives such as decoder loss, global-local and masked prediction loss, and aspect ratio and resolution adaptibility. The model is pre-trained on the WebLI dataset and was trained on up to 2048 TPU-v5e chips. Evaluation results are presented in the associated paper. The model is described in the research titled 'SigLIP 2: Multilingual Vision-Language Encoders with Improved Semantic Understanding, Localization, and Dense Features' by Michael Tschannen, Alexey Gritsenko, Xiao Wang, Muhammad Ferjad Naeem, Ibrahim Alabdulmohsin, Nikhil Parthasarathy, Talfan Evans, Lucas Beyer, Ye Xia, Basil Mustafa, Olivier Hénaff, Jeremiah Harmsen, Andreas Steiner, and Xiaohua Zhai, available on arXiv with the identifier 2502.14786.

instruction: Which bird calls in this audio are the most distinct and can be confidently identified?
description: AudioProtoPNet is an interpretable deep learning model designed for multi-label bird sound classification. It is an adaptation of the Prototypical Part Network (ProtoPNet), which provides explanations for its decisions by comparing recordings to learned prototypes in an embedding space. The model uses a ConvNeXt backbone to extract embeddings from spectrograms of bird vocalizations and a prototype learning classifier trained on these embeddings to learn prototypical patterns for each bird species. AudioProtoPNet was trained on the BirdSet training dataset, which includes 9734 bird species and over 6800 hours of recordings. The model's performance was evaluated on seven BirdSet test datasets covering diverse geographical regions. AudioProtoPNet achieved an average AUROC of 0.90 and a cmAP of 0.42, representing relative improvements of 7.1% and 16.7% over the state-of-the-art model Perch, respectively. Evaluation results show that models with five, ten, and twenty prototypes per class performed similarly, while the model with only one prototype per class showed slightly lower performance. AudioProtoPNet-5 notably outperformed both Perch and ConvNeXt in terms of cmAP, AUROC, and top-1 accuracy scores. The model is designed to provide valuable insights for professionals in ornithology and machine learning by offering a powerful yet interpretable approach to the challenging task of multi-label bird sound classification.

instruction: What is the person in the picture trying to accomplish?
description: BLIP-2 is a multimodal model consisting of three components: a CLIP-like image encoder, a Querying Transformer (Q-Former), and a large language model. The image encoder and large language model are initialized from pre-trained checkpoints and remain frozen during training, while the Q-Former, a BERT-like Transformer encoder, is trained to map a set of 'query tokens' to query embeddings that bridge the embedding spaces of the image encoder and the language model. The model is designed to predict the next text token given the query embeddings and the previous text. It supports tasks such as image captioning, visual question answering (VQA), and chat-like conversations by providing the image and previous conversation as input. BLIP-2 uses the Flan-T5-xl language model, which inherits the same risks and limitations, including potential misuse for harmful language generation. The model is fine-tuned on image-text datasets collected from the internet, which may result in the generation of inappropriate content or the replication of biases present in the training data. BLIP-2 has not been tested in real-world applications and should not be directly deployed without a careful assessment of safety and fairness concerns specific to the intended use. This release is for research purposes only and is not evaluated for all downstream applications. Users are strongly encouraged to evaluate and address potential concerns related to accuracy, safety, and fairness before deployment, particularly in high-risk scenarios where errors or misuse could significantly impact people's lives, rights, or safety.

instruction: How can I estimate the body joint positions in a video recorded from a drone?
description: Yolov8n_pose is a lightweight and efficient model designed for multi-pose estimation tasks. It is part of the YOLO (You Only Look Once) family of models, known for their real-time object detection capabilities. The 'n' in Yolov8n_pose indicates that it is a nano version, optimized for speed and resource efficiency, making it suitable for deployment on devices with limited computational power, such as mobile devices and embedded systems. Yolov8n_pose is implemented in PyTorch by Ultralytics and is quantized in int8 format using the TensorFlow Lite converter. The model processes an input of a single NxM RGB image with UINT8 values between 0 and 255 and produces output in the form of FLOAT values across three concatenated feature maps, with the number of output features dependent on the input resolution. The model is recommended for deployment on STM32MP2 and STM32N6 platforms. Performance metrics include internal and external RAM usage, weights flash size, and inference time, with values varying based on input resolution and platform. For example, on the STM32N6 platform with a resolution of 192x192x3, the model uses 477.56 KiB of internal RAM and 3247.89 KiB of weights flash, with an inference time of 24.46 ms. On the STM32MP257F-DK2 board with per-tensor quantization, the model achieves an inference time of 17.57 ms at a resolution of 256x256x3. The model's performance is evaluated using the COCO Person dataset, with AP0.5 scores ranging from 41.05% to 55.55% depending on the resolution. The model is stored in the Ultralytics repository and can be retrained using the provided resources. Users are advised to check the external GitHub folder for detailed information on the model's use and content, as it is subject to its own license terms.

instruction: Please give me a quick summary of this user guide.
description: Gemma is a family of lightweight, state-of-the-art open models from Google, built from the same research and technology used to create the Gemini models. Gemma 3 models are multimodal, handling text and image input and generating text output, with open weights for both pre-trained variants and instruction-tuned variants. Gemma 3 has a large, 128K context window, multilingual support in over 140 languages, and is available in more sizes than previous versions. Gemma 3 models are well-suited for a variety of text generation and image understanding tasks, including question answering, summarization, and reasoning. Their relatively small size makes it possible to deploy them in environments with limited resources such as laptops, desktops or your own cloud infrastructure, democratizing access to state of the art AI models and helping foster innovation for everyone. The input includes text strings, such as a question, a prompt, or a document to be summarized, and images normalized to 896 x 896 resolution and encoded to 256 tokens each. The total input context is 128K tokens for the 4B, 12B, and 27B sizes, and 32K tokens for the 1B size. The output is generated text in response to the input, such as an answer to a question, analysis of image content, or a summary of a document, with a total output context of 8192 tokens. These models were trained on a dataset of text data that includes a wide variety of sources. The 27B model was trained with 14 trillion tokens, the 12B model with 12 trillion tokens, the 4B model with 4 trillion tokens, and the 1B model with 2 trillion tokens. The training data includes web documents, code, mathematics, and images. The combination of these diverse data sources is crucial for training a powerful multimodal model that can handle a wide variety of different tasks and data formats. Rigorous CSAM filtering was applied at multiple stages in the data preparation process to ensure the exclusion of harmful and illegal content. Automated techniques were used to filter out certain personal information and other sensitive data from training sets. Additional methods were applied for content quality and safety in line with Google's policies. Gemma was trained using Tensor Processing Unit (TPU) hardware (TPUv4p, TPUv5p and TPUv5e). TPUs are specifically designed to handle the massive computations involved in training vision-language models (VLMs). They can speed up training considerably compared to CPUs. TPUs often come with large amounts of high-bandwidth memory, allowing for the handling of large models and batch sizes during training. TPU Pods provide a scalable solution for handling the growing complexity of large foundation models. You can distribute training across multiple TPU devices for faster and more efficient processing. In many scenarios, TPUs can provide a more cost-effective solution for training large models compared to CPU-based infrastructure, especially when considering the time and resources saved due to faster training. These advantages are aligned with Google's commitments to operate sustainably. Training was done using JAX and ML Pathways. JAX allows researchers to take advantage of the latest generation of hardware, including TPUs, for faster and more efficient training of large models. ML Pathways is Google's latest effort to build artificially intelligent systems capable of generalizing across multiple tasks. This is specially suitable for foundation models, including large language models like these ones. Together, JAX and ML Pathways are used as described in the paper about the Gemini family of models; the 'single controller' programming model of Jax and Pathways allows a single Python process to orchestrate the entire training run, dramatically simplifying the development workflow. These models were evaluated against a large collection of different datasets and metrics to cover different aspects of text generation. For reasoning and factuality, the models achieved the following results: on HellaSwag, 62.3 for 1B, 77.2 for 4B, 84.2 for 12B, and 85.6 for 27B; on BoolQ, 63.2 for 1B, 72.3 for 4B, 78.8 for 12B, and 82.4 for 27B; on PIQA, 73.8 for 1B, 79.6 for 4B, 81.8 for 12B, and 83.3 for 27B; on SocialIQA, 48.9 for 1B, 51.9 for 4B, 53.4 for 12B, and 54.9 for 27B; on TriviaQA, 39.8 for 1B, 65.8 for 4B, 78.2 for 12B, and 85.5 for 27B; on Natural Questions, 9.48 for 1B, 20.0 for 4B, 31.4 for 12B, and 36.1 for 27B; on ARC-c, 38.4 for 1B, 56.2 for 4B, 68.9 for 12B, and 70.6 for 27B; on ARC-e, 73.0 for 1B, 82.4 for 4B, 88.3 for 12B, and 89.0 for 27B; on WinoGrande, 58.2 for 1B, 64.7 for 4B, 74.3 for 12B, and 78.8 for 27B; on BIG-Bench Hard, 28.4 for 1B, 50.9 for 4B, 72.6 for 12B, and 77.7 for 27B; on DROP, 42.4 for 1B, 60.1 for 4B, 72.2 for 12B, and 77.2 for 27B. For STEM and code, the models achieved the following results: on MMLU, 59.6 for 4B, 74.5 for 12B, and 78.6 for 27B; on MMLU (Pro COT), 29.2 for 4B, 45.3 for 12B, and 52.2 for 27B; on AGIEval, 42.1 for 4B, 57.4 for 12B, and 66.2 for 27B; on MATH, 24.2 for 4B, 43.3 for 12B, and 50.0 for 27B; on GSM8K, 38.4 for 4B, 71.0 for 12B, and 82.6 for 27B; on GPQA, 15.0 for 4B, 25.4 for 12B, and 24.3 for 27B; on MBPP, 46.0 for 4B, 60.4 for 12B, and 65.6 for 27B; on HumanEval, 36.0 for 4B, 45.7 for 12B, and 48.8 for 27B. For multilingual tasks, the models achieved the following results: on MGSM, 2.04 for 1B, 34.7 for 4B, 64.3 for 12B, and 74.3 for 27B; on Global-MMLU-Lite, 24.9 for 1B, 57.0 for 4B, 69.4 for 12B, and 75.7 for 27B; on WMT24++ (ChrF), 36.7 for 1B, 48.4 for 4B, 53.9 for 12B, and 55.7 for 27B; on FloRes, 29.5 for 1B, 39.2 for 4B, 46.0 for 12B, and 48.8 for 27B; on XQuAD (all), 43.9 for 1B, 68.0 for 4B, 74.5 for 12B, and 76.8 for 27B; on ECLeKTic, 4.69 for 1B, 11.0 for 4B, 17.2 for 12B, and 24.4 for 27B; on IndicGenBench, 41.4 for 1B, 57.2 for 4B, 61.7 for 12B, and 63.4 for 27B. For multimodal tasks, the models achieved the following results: on COCOcap, 102 for 4B, 111 for 12B, and 116 for 27B; on DocVQA (val), 72.8 for 4B, 82.3 for 12B, and 85.6 for 27B; on InfoVQA (val), 44.1 for 4B, 54.8 for 12B, and 59.4 for 27B; on MMMU (pt), 39.2 for 4B, 50.3 for 12B, and 56.1 for 27B; on TextVQA (val), 58.9 for 4B, 66.5 for 12B, and 68.6 for 27B; on RealWorldQA, 45.5 for 4B, 52.2 for 12B, and 53.9 for 27B; on ReMI, 27.3 for 4B, 38.5 for 12B, and 44.8 for 27B; on AI2D, 63.2 for 4B, 75.2 for 12B, and 79.0 for 27B; on ChartQA, 63.6 for 4B, 74.7 for 12B, and 76.3 for 27B; on VQAv2, 63.9 for 4B, 71.2 for 12B, and 72.9 for 27B; on BLINK, 38.0 for 4B, 35.9 for 12B, and 39.6 for 27B; on OKVQA, 51.0 for 4B, 58.7 for 12B, and 60.2 for 27B; on TallyQA, 42.5 for 4B, 51.8 for 12B, and 54.3 for 27B; on SpatialSense VQA, 50.9 for 4B, 60.0 for 12B, and 59.4 for 27B; on CountBenchQA, 26.1 for 4B, 17.8 for 12B, and 68.0 for 27B. Our evaluation methods include structured evaluations and internal red-teaming testing of relevant content policies. Red-teaming was conducted by a number of different teams, each with different goals and human evaluation metrics. These models were evaluated against a number of different categories relevant to ethics and safety, including child safety, content safety, and representational harms. In addition to development level evaluations, we conduct 'assurance evaluations' which are our 'arms-length' internal evaluations for responsibility governance decision making. They are conducted separately from the model development team, to inform decision making about release. High level findings are fed back to the model team, but prompt sets are held-out to prevent overfitting and preserve the results' ability to inform decision making. Assurance evaluation results are reported to our Responsibility & Safety Council as part of release review. For all areas of safety testing, we saw major improvements in the categories of child safety, content safety, and representational harms relative to previous Gemma models. All testing was conducted without safety filters to evaluate the model capabilities and behaviors. For both text-to-text and image-to-text, and across all model sizes, the model produced minimal policy violations, and showed significant improvements over previous Gemma models' performance with respect to ungrounded inferences. A limitation of our evaluations was they included only English language prompts. These models have certain limitations that users should be aware of. The quality and diversity of the training data significantly influence the model's capabilities. Biases or gaps in the training data can lead to limitations in the model's responses. The scope of the training dataset determines the subject areas the model can handle effectively. Models are better at tasks that can be framed with clear prompts and instructions. Open-ended or highly complex tasks might be challenging. A model's performance can be influenced by the amount of context provided (longer context generally leads to better outputs, up to a certain point). Natural language is inherently complex. Models might struggle to grasp subtle nuances, sarcasm, or figurative language. Models generate responses based on information they learned from their training datasets, but they are not knowledge bases. They may generate incorrect or outdated factual statements. Models rely on statistical patterns in language. They might lack the ability to apply common sense reasoning in certain situations. The development of vision-language models (VLMs) raises several ethical concerns. In creating an open model, we have carefully considered the following: VLMs trained on large-scale, real-world text and image data can reflect socio-cultural biases embedded in the training material. These models underwent careful scrutiny, input data pre-processing described and posterior evaluations reported in this card. VLMs can be misused to generate text that is false, misleading, or harmful. Guidelines are provided for responsible use with the model, see the Responsible Generative AI Toolkit. This model card summarizes details on the models' architecture, capabilities, limitations, and evaluation processes. A responsibly developed open model offers the opportunity to share innovation by making VLM technology accessible to developers and researchers across the AI ecosystem. Risks identified and mitigations include: perpetuation of biases, generation of harmful content, misuse for malicious purposes, and privacy violations. It's encouraged to perform continuous monitoring (using evaluation metrics, human review) and the exploration of de-biasing techniques during model training, fine-tuning, and other use cases. Mechanisms and guidelines for content safety are essential. Developers are encouraged to exercise caution and implement appropriate content safety safeguards based on their specific product policies and application use cases. Technical limitations and developer and end-user education can help mitigate against malicious applications of VLMs. Educational resources and reporting mechanisms for users to flag misuse are provided. Prohibited uses of Gemma models are outlined in the Gemma Prohibited Use Policy. Models were trained on data filtered for removal of certain personal information and other sensitive data. Developers are encouraged to adhere to privacy regulations with privacy-preserving techniques. At the time of release, this family of models provides high-performance open vision-language model implementations designed from the ground up for responsible AI development compared to similarly sized models. Using the benchmark evaluation metrics described in this document, these models have shown to provide superior performance to other, comparably-sized open model alternatives.

instruction: I need the French version of this legal text reviewed for grammatical accuracy and fluency.
description: SalamandraTA-7b-instruct is a translation large language model (LLM) that has been instruction-tuned from SalamandraTA-7b-base. The base model is the result of continual pre-training on parallel data and is reserved for internal use. SalamandraTA-7b-instruct is proficient in 35 European languages (plus 3 varieties) and supports translation-related tasks, including sentence-level-translation, paragraph-level-translation, document-level-translation, automatic post-editing, grammar checking, machine translation evaluation, alternative translations, named-entity-recognition, and context-aware translation. It is not designed for chat capabilities and has not been trained with any chat instructions. The base model, SalamandraTA-7b-base, was pre-trained using 424 billion tokens of parallel data, including all official European languages plus Catalan, Basque, Galician, Asturian, Aragonese, and Aranese, amounting to 6,574,251,526 parallel sentence pairs. The model has 7,768,117,248 total parameters, with an embedding size of 1,048,576,000, 32 layers, a hidden size of 4,096, 32 attention heads, a context length of 8,192, a vocabulary size of 256,000, and uses bfloat16 precision. It employs RoPE embedding, SwiGLU activation function, RMS Norm for layer normalization, and supports Flash attention and Grouped Query Attention with 8 query groups. The model is intended for both research and commercial use in any of the languages included in the training data for general machine translation tasks. It is not intended for malicious activities, such as harming others or violating human rights, and any downstream application must comply with current laws and regulations. Irresponsible usage in production environments without proper risk assessment and mitigation is discouraged. The model was trained using NVIDIA’s framework, leveraging PyTorch Lightning for efficient model training in highly distributed settings. It was trained on a pre-exascale EuroHPC supercomputer hosted and operated by Barcelona Supercomputing Center, with nodes featuring 4x Nvidia Hopper GPUs, 2x Intel Sapphire Rapids CPUs, 4x NDR200 interconnects, 512 GB DDR5 memory, and 460 GB NVMe storage. The instruction-following model uses the ChatML template for interaction. It has been fine-tuned on approximately 135,000 instructions, primarily targeting machine translation performance for Catalan, English, and Spanish, with additional data for other European and Iberian languages. Evaluation results show strong performance across multiple languages and tasks, with metrics such as BLEU, TER, ChrF, COMET, and others. The model has been evaluated on English, Spanish, Catalan, and other European languages, with results compared to state-of-the-art models. It also performs well on low-resource languages of Spain, such as Asturian, Aranese, and Aragonese. The model has been tested for gender-aware translation, showing competitive accuracy in translating gendered content. Ethical considerations include the potential for biases and the need for further analysis to address translation accuracy across different languages, dialects, and domains. The model has only undergone preliminary instruction tuning, and developers are urged to consider potential limitations and conduct safety testing tailored to their specific applications. The model is the result of collaboration between the Language Technologies Unit at Barcelona Supercomputing Center and other partners. It is funded by the Government of Catalonia and the Ministerio para la Transformación Digital y de la Función Pública, with support from EU funding. The model is available for use under a license, and users are encouraged to cite the associated research paper.

instruction: Can you provide a short summary of this news report on global health initiatives?
description: SummLlama3.2-3B is a text summarization model initialized from Llama3.2-3B-Instruct and further trained using Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) based on large-scale summarization feedback. The training data includes over 100K input documents spanning a wide range of lengths and formats, including both dialogue and non-dialogue texts, across seven distinct domains: four non-dialogue domains (News, Lifestyle, Report, Medical) and three dialogue domains (Daily Life, Interview, Meeting). The model is designed to generate human-preferred summaries that excel in three key aspects: faithfulness, completeness, and conciseness. Faithfulness ensures that the summary does not manipulate or add information not directly inferable from the input. Completeness ensures that all key information from the input is included in the summary. Conciseness ensures that the summary remains focused and avoids unnecessary details. Automated evaluation results show that SummLlama3.2-3B achieves a faithfulness score of 0.867, completeness of 0.598, and conciseness of 0.686, with an average score of 0.717, compared to 0.613 for Llama3.2-3B-Instruct. Human evaluation results also indicate that the model outperforms the original Llama3 series in summary quality. The model is recommended to be used with a specific prompt format that includes an instruction to summarize the input document. This model is part of the SummLlama3.2-Series, which includes variants such as SummLlama3.2-3B and SummLlama3.2-70B. The model is trained to produce summaries that are more aligned with human preferences by leveraging high-quality, multi-dimensional, and fine-grained feedback generated by large language models rather than relying on expensive human feedback.

instruction: How can I find similar documents based on the content of this sentence?
description: This is a model that maps sentences and paragraphs to a 384-dimensional dense vector space and can be used for tasks like clustering or semantic search. The model is intended to be used as a sentence and short paragraph encoder. Given an input text, it outputs a vector which captures the semantic information. The sentence vector may be used for information retrieval, clustering, or sentence similarity tasks. Input text longer than 256 word pieces is truncated by default. The model was developed using a self-supervised contrastive learning objective, where the model is trained to predict which sentence from a set of randomly sampled sentences was actually paired with a given sentence in the dataset. The model was fine-tuned on a dataset containing over 1 billion sentence pairs. The training was conducted on a TPU v3-8 for 100,000 steps with a batch size of 1024 (128 per TPU core), a learning rate of 2e-5, and a sequence length limited to 128 tokens. The AdamW optimizer was used with a learning rate warm-up of 500 steps. The training data was a concatenation of multiple datasets, including citation pairs (abstracts and titles), duplicate question pairs, (question, answer) pairs, (title, body) pairs, (title+body, answer) pairs, (title, answer) pairs, triplets, image captions, and citation triplets. The total number of training tuples across all datasets is 1,170,060,424. The model was developed as part of a project during an event organized by Hugging Face, and the development was supported by efficient hardware infrastructure, including 7 TPUs v3-8, and assistance from members of Google's Flax, JAX, and Cloud teams.

instruction: Does the phrase 'The train is late' support the claim 'Passengers are waiting at the station'?
description: This multilingual model can perform natural language inference (NLI) on 100 languages and is therefore also suitable for multilingual zero-shot classification. The underlying model was pre-trained by Microsoft. It was then fine-tuned on a dataset containing hypothesis-premise pairs from 15 languages, as well as the English MNLI dataset. As of December 2021, mDeBERTa-base is the best performing multilingual base-sized transformer model, introduced by Microsoft. The model was trained on the XNLI development dataset and the MNLI train dataset. The XNLI development set consists of 2490 professionally translated texts from English to 14 other languages (37350 texts in total). The XNLI contains a training set of 15 machine translated versions of the MNLI dataset for 15 languages, but due to quality issues with these machine translations, this model was only trained on the professional translations from the XNLI development set and the original English MNLI training set (392 702 texts). Not using machine translated texts can avoid overfitting the model to the 15 languages; avoids catastrophic forgetting of the other 85 languages mDeBERTa was pre-trained on; and significantly reduces training costs. mDeBERTa-v3-base-mnli-xnli was trained using the Hugging Face trainer with the following hyperparameters: num_train_epochs=2, learning_rate=2e-05, per_device_train_batch_size=16, per_device_eval_batch_size=16, warmup_ratio=0.1, weight_decay=0.06. The model was evaluated on the XNLI test set on 15 languages (5010 texts per language, 75150 in total). Multilingual NLI models are capable of classifying NLI texts without receiving NLI training data in the specific language (cross-lingual transfer). This means that the model is also able of doing NLI on the other 85 languages mDeBERTa was training on, but performance is most likely lower than for those languages available in XNLI. If other multilingual models on the model hub claim performance of around 90% on languages other than English, the authors have most likely made a mistake during testing since none of the latest papers shows a multilingual average performance of more than a few points above 80% on XNLI. The model achieved an average performance of 0.808 across 15 languages, with individual language scores as follows: ar 0.802, bg 0.829, de 0.825, el 0.826, en 0.883, es 0.845, fr 0.834, hi 0.771, ru 0.813, sw 0.748, th 0.793, tr 0.807, ur 0.740, vi 0.795, zh 0.8116. Please consult the original DeBERTa-V3 paper and literature on different NLI datasets for potential biases.

instruction: Can you help me isolate the main object in this image using a few click points?
description: SAM 2 is a foundation model designed for promptable visual segmentation in images and videos, developed by FAIR. It supports a wide range of segmentation tasks, including automatic mask generation, image segmentation using point clicks, bounding box inputs, and multi-object segmentation. The model can process both single images and batches of images, allowing for efficient inference across multiple inputs. It also supports video segmentation and tracking, enabling the tracking of objects across video frames with the ability to refine segmentation through additional prompts. SAM 2 can use masks from previous predictions as input to improve segmentation accuracy. The model is built on a hierarchical architecture and is available through the Hugging Face Transformers library. It has been evaluated on various tasks, including generating multiple masks per image and tracking objects through video sequences. The model supports both single and multi-object tracking in video, with the ability to add refinement points on any frame. It is capable of processing video frames in a streaming fashion for real-time applications. The model is based on the SAM 2 architecture and is described in the paper titled 'SAM 2: Segment Anything in Images and Videos' by Ravi et al., published in 2024.

instruction: I need a system that can segment objects even if they haven’t been seen during training. Can you help?
description: Seg-Zero-7B is a model that introduces a novel framework for reasoning segmentation, addressing the limitations of traditional supervised fine-tuning methods, which often struggle with out-of-domain generalization and lack explicit reasoning processes. The framework features a decoupled architecture consisting of a reasoning model and a segmentation model. The reasoning model interprets user intentions, generates explicit reasoning chains, and produces positional prompts, which are subsequently used by the segmentation model to generate precise pixel-level masks. Seg-Zero is trained exclusively via reinforcement learning with GRPO, without explicit reasoning data, achieving robust zero-shot generalization and emergent test-time reasoning capabilities. A sophisticated reward mechanism integrating both format and accuracy rewards guides the optimization. Experiments show that Seg-Zero-7B achieves a zero-shot performance of 57.5 on the ReasonSeg benchmark, surpassing the prior LISA-7B by 18%. Seg-Zero demonstrates the following key features: it generates a reasoning chain before producing the final segmentation mask; it is trained exclusively using reinforcement learning, without any explicit supervised reasoning data; and it achieves superior performance on both in-domain and out-of-domain data compared to supervised fine-tuning methods. The model is based on EasyR1 and veRL, which supports model split during sampling and is more GPU memory friendly. It supports both Qwen2-VL and Qwen2.5-VL series models. It already implements commonly used rewards in Object Detection and Object Segmentation, including IoU reward and L1 reward. Seg-Zero employs a decoupled architecture, including a reasoning model and segmentation model. A sophisticated reward mechanism is manually designed to integrate both the format and accuracy rewards. The model generates several samples, calculates the rewards, and then optimizes towards samples that achieve higher rewards. The recommended training requirement for the 7B model is a 4x80G GPUs server or an 8x46G GPUs server. The GRPO algorithm is used for training, and hyper-parameters can be adjusted based on GPU memory availability. The model is evaluated using the ReasonSeg benchmark, and the best results on different benchmarks are evaluated using different checkpoints. For evaluation, all available checkpoints are assessed, and their values are recorded. The model is based on the paper titled 'Seg-Zero: Reasoning-Chain Guided Segmentation via Cognitive Reinforcement' and 'VisionReasoner: Unified Visual Perception and Reasoning via Reinforcement Learning'.

instruction: What visuals would match the sentence 'a peaceful lake surrounded by tall trees and a misty morning atmosphere'?
description: LLM2CLIP is a novel approach that leverages the power of large language models (LLMs) to enhance the capabilities of CLIP. By fine-tuning the LLM in the caption space with contrastive learning, the method extracts textual capabilities into the output embeddings, significantly improving the output layer's textual discriminability. The fine-tuned LLM then acts as a powerful teacher for CLIP's visual encoder in an efficient training process. This allows the incorporation of longer and more complex captions, overcoming the limitations of the vanilla CLIP text encoder's context window and ability constraints. Experiments show that this approach brings substantial improvements in cross-modal tasks. Specifically, it directly boosted the performance of the previously state-of-the-art EVA02 model by 16.5% on both long-text and short-text retrieval tasks, transforming a CLIP model trained solely on English data into a state-of-the-art cross-lingual model. When integrated into multimodal training with models like Llava 1.5, it consistently outperformed CLIP across nearly all benchmarks, demonstrating comprehensive performance improvements. LLM2CLIP is a vision foundation model and feature backbone, pretrained on datasets including CC3M, CC12M, YFCC15M, and a 30M subset of Recap-DataComp-1B. It is important to note that all results presented in the paper are evaluated using PyTorch weights, and there may be differences in performance when using Hugging Face (hf) models.

instruction: How can I verify that the latest version of the interface is installed correctly?
description: This is a direct GGUF conversion of the 13b-0.9.7-dev variant. The model files can be used with a custom node. The required model files should be placed in specific folders: the main model named ltxv-13b-0.9.7-dev should be placed in the ComfyUI/models/diffusion_models folder and can be downloaded as a GGUF file from this repository; the text encoder named T5-V1.1-XXL-Encoder should be placed in the ComfyUI/models/text_encoders folder; and the VAE named ltxv-13b-0.9.7-vae should be placed in the ComfyUI/models/vae folder. As this is a quantized model and not a finetune, all the original restrictions and license terms still apply. ComfyUI now supports GGUF files natively, so users should update ComfyUI to the latest version, and if issues persist, update all the nodes in the workflow. Other T5 clips may also work, and users can choose one they prefer, available as safetensors or GGUF files. The best tested option is the T5 v1.1 xxl version. Loras are compatible but require following the steps in the example workflow, and torchcompile should not be used with loras. Teacache works with ltx but is currently not very effective. In testing, the rel_l1_thresh only works at 0.01, and even then, it causes noticeable quality drops, so it is best to leave it disabled.

instruction: Make a video that shows a flower blooming from a bud to full bloom in a garden setting.
description: Wan2.1 is a comprehensive and open suite of video foundation models designed to push the boundaries of video generation. It offers state-of-the-art performance, consistently outperforming existing open-source models and state-of-the-art commercial solutions across multiple benchmarks. The T2V-1.3B model requires only 8.19 GB VRAM, making it compatible with most consumer-grade GPUs. It can generate a 5-second 480P video on an RTX 4090 in about 4 minutes, with performance comparable to some closed-source models. Wan2.1 supports multiple tasks, including Text-to-Video, Image-to-Video, Video Editing, Text-to-Image, and Video-to-Audio, advancing the field of video generation. It is the first video model capable of generating both Chinese and English text, enhancing its practical applications. The model features a powerful video VAE, Wan-VAE, which delivers exceptional efficiency and performance, encoding and decoding 1080P videos of any length while preserving temporal information. The T2V-14B model establishes a new state-of-the-art performance benchmark among both open-source and closed-source models, demonstrating exceptional capabilities in generating high-quality visuals with significant motion dynamics. It is the only video model capable of producing both Chinese and English text and supports video generation at both 480P and 720P resolutions. Wan2.1 is designed on the mainstream diffusion transformer paradigm, with innovations including a novel spatio-temporal variational autoencoder (VAE), scalable training strategies, large-scale data construction, and automated evaluation metrics. The model uses a Flow Matching framework within the Diffusion Transformers paradigm, with a T5 Encoder to encode multilingual text input and cross-attention in each transformer block embedding the text into the model structure. An MLP with a Linear layer and a SiLU layer processes the input time embeddings and predicts six modulation parameters individually, shared across all transformer blocks. The model was trained on a curated and deduplicated dataset of image and video data, processed through a four-step data cleaning pipeline focusing on fundamental dimensions, visual quality, and motion quality. Manual evaluation shows that the results generated after prompt extension are superior to those from both closed-source and open-source models. The model is licensed under the Apache 2.0 License, and users are responsible for ensuring their usage complies with the provisions of this license, including not generating content that violates applicable laws, causes harm, or spreads misinformation.

instruction: What is the difference in revenue between the first and second quarters from the table?
description: This model is a TAPAS mini model fine-tuned on WikiTable Questions (WTQ). It has two versions: the default version corresponds to the `tapas_wtq_wikisql_sqa_inter_masklm_mini_reset` checkpoint, and the non-default version corresponds to `tapas_wtq_wikisql_sqa_inter_masklm_mini`. The model was pre-trained on masked language modeling (MLM) and an additional intermediate pre-training step, and then fine-tuned in a chain on SQA, WikiSQL, and finally WTQ. The default version uses relative position embeddings, resetting the position index at every cell of the table, while the non-default version uses absolute position embeddings. TAPAS is a BERT-like transformer model pre-trained on a large corpus of English data from Wikipedia in a self-supervised fashion. It was pre-trained with two objectives: masked language modeling, where 15% of the words in a flattened table and associated context are randomly masked and the model must predict them, and intermediate pre-training, where the model is trained to classify whether a sentence is supported or refuted by the contents of a table using a balanced dataset of syntactically created training examples. This allows the model to learn a bidirectional representation of tables and associated texts, which can be used for downstream tasks such as answering questions about a table or determining whether a sentence is entailed or refuted by the contents of a table. Fine-tuning involves adding a cell selection head and aggregation head on top of the pre-trained model and jointly training these with the base model. The model was fine-tuned on 32 Cloud TPU v3 cores for 50,000 steps with a maximum sequence length of 512 and a batch size of 512. The optimizer used is Adam with a learning rate of 1.93581e-5 and a warmup ratio of 0.128960. An inductive bias is added so that the model only selects cells of the same column, as reflected by the `select_one_column` parameter of `TapasConfig`. The model can be used for answering questions related to a table. The team releasing TAPAS did not write a model card for this model, so this model card was written by the Hugging Face team and contributors. The model's performance varies by size and position embedding type. For the mini size, the dev accuracy is 0.2783 for the noreset version and 0.2854 for the reset version.
domain: Natural Language Processing Table Question Answering

instruction: Can you identify which object in this image matches the description 'a red apple on a wooden table'?
description: SigLIP 2 Base extends the pretraining objective with prior, independently developed techniques into a unified recipe, for improved semantic understanding, localization, and dense features. The model can be used for tasks like zero-shot image classification and image-text retrieval, or as a vision encoder for VLMs and other vision tasks. SigLIP 2 adds training objectives such as decoder loss, global-local and masked prediction loss, and aspect ratio and resolution adaptibility. The model is pre-trained on the WebLI dataset and was trained on up to 2048 TPU-v5e chips. Evaluation results are presented in the associated paper.
