The model is described in the research titled 'SigLIP 2: Multilingual Vision-Language Encoders with Improved Semantic Understanding, Localization, and Dense Features' by Michael Tschannen, Alexey Gritsenko, Xiao Wang, Muhammad Ferjad Naeem, Ibrahim Alabdulmohsin, Nikhil Parthasarathy, Talfan Evans, Lucas Beyer, Ye Xia, Basil Mustafa, Olivier Hénaff, Jeremiah Harmsen, Andreas Steiner, and Xiaohua Zhai, available on arXiv with the identifier 2502.14786.

instruction: What is the most likely scene or environment depicted in this photograph?
description: This is a CLIP ViT-B/16 model trained using the LAION-2B English subset of the LAION-5B dataset. The model was developed by Mehdi Cherti on a supercomputer. It is intended for research purposes, particularly for exploring zero-shot, arbitrary image classification and enabling interdisciplinary studies on the potential impact of such models. The model supports tasks such as zero-shot image classification, image and text retrieval, image classification and other image task fine-tuning, linear probe image classification, and image generation guiding and conditioning. However, any deployed use case—whether commercial or not—is currently out of scope. Non-deployed use cases, such as image search in a constrained environment, are also not recommended unless there is thorough in-domain testing with a specific, fixed class taxonomy. This is due to the high need for task-specific testing, especially given the variability of CLIP’s performance with different class taxonomies, which can make untested and unconstrained deployment potentially harmful. Use cases related to surveillance and facial recognition are always out of scope, regardless of model performance, due to the lack of testing norms and checks to ensure fair use. Additionally, since the model has not been trained or evaluated on any languages other than English, its use should be limited to English language use cases. The LAION-5B dataset used for training is uncurated and may contain discomforting or disturbing content. While it is possible to extract a safer subset using a customized NSFW classifier, harmful content may still be present. The dataset is provided to enable transparent research into the benefits and risks of large-scale models, and it is not recommended for creating ready-to-go industrial products. The model was evaluated using a combination of VTAB (with additional robustness datasets) for classification and COCO and Flickr for retrieval. It achieved a 70.2 zero-shot top-1 accuracy on ImageNet-1k. The training procedure is not detailed in the provided text. The model development was supported by the Gauss Centre for Supercomputing e.V. through the John von Neumann Institute for Computing on the GCS Supercomputer JUWELS Booster at Jülich Supercomputing Centre.

instruction: I need to compare the similarity between user queries and database entries in different languages. How can I handle multilingual embeddings?
description: jina-embeddings-v3 is a multilingual multi-task text embedding model designed for a variety of natural language processing applications. Based on a foundational model, it supports rotary position embeddings to handle long input sequences up to 8192 tokens. The model includes five LoRA adapters to generate task-specific embeddings efficiently. It supports task-specific embeddings through the task argument with options for retrieval.query, retrieval.passage, separation, classification, and text-matching. The model also supports matryoshka embeddings with flexible embedding sizes of 32, 64, 128, 256, 512, 768, and 1024. While the foundation model supports 100 languages, the model has been tuned for 30 languages including Arabic, Bengali, Chinese, Danish, Dutch, English, Finnish, French, Georgian, German, Greek, Hindi, Indonesian, Italian, Japanese, Korean, Latvian, Norwegian, Polish, Portuguese, Romanian, Russian, Slovak, Spanish, Swedish, Thai, Turkish, Ukrainian, Urdu, and Vietnamese. A bug in the encode function related to matryoshka embedding truncation after normalization has been resolved in the latest code revision. Users who encoded data with the previous version should use the specific code revision to maintain consistency. Mean pooling is recommended when integrating the model, as it averages token embeddings at the sentence or paragraph level to produce high-quality sentence embeddings. The model can be used directly via the Transformers package or with ONNX for efficient inference. The model is licensed under CC BY-NC 4.0 for use beyond AWS and Azure platforms or on-premises within companies. Commercial usage inquiries should be directed to Jina AI.

instruction: I need to find duplicate questions in a dataset that includes Czech and Italian—can you help?
description: Granite-Embedding-278M-Multilingual is a 278 million parameter model from the Granite Embeddings suite designed to generate high-quality text embeddings. It produces embedding vectors of size 768 and is trained using a combination of open source relevance-pair datasets with permissive, enterprise-friendly licenses and IBM-collected and generated datasets. The model is developed using contrastive fine-tuning, knowledge distillation, and model merging for improved performance. It supports 12 languages: English, German, Spanish, French, Japanese, Portuguese, Arabic, Czech, Italian, Korean, Dutch, and Chinese. Users may fine-tune the model for additional languages. The model is intended to produce fixed-length vector representations for a given text, which can be used for text similarity, retrieval, and search applications. It is compatible with the SentenceTransformer library and the Huggingface Transformers library. The model is based on an encoder-only XLM-RoBERTa-like transformer architecture, trained internally at IBM Research. It has 12 layers, 12 attention heads, an intermediate size of 3072, a vocabulary size of 250002, a maximum sequence length of 512, and uses the GeLU activation function. The training data includes unsupervised title-body paired data from the web, publicly available paired data with permissive licenses, IBM-internal paired data targeting specific technical domains, and IBM-generated synthetic data. The training data consists of 52,823,484 pairs from Multilingual MC4, 12,369,322 pairs from Multilingual Webhose, 20,745,403 pairs from English Wikipedia, 2,911,090 pairs from Multilingual Wikimedia, 10,120,398 pairs from Miracl Corpus, 304,525 pairs from Stack Exchange duplicate questions (titles), 250,519 pairs from Stack Exchange duplicate questions (bodies), 187,195 pairs from machine-translated Stack Exchange duplicate questions (titles), 4,067,139 pairs from Stack Exchange (Title, Answer) pairs, 23,978,013 pairs from Stack Exchange (Title, Body) pairs, 1,827,15 pairs from machine-translated Stack Exchange (Title+Body, Answer) pairs, 582,261 pairs from SearchQA, 41,769,185 pairs from S2ORC, 77,427,422 pairs from WikiAnswers duplicate question pairs, 614,664 pairs from CCNews, 226,711 pairs from XSum, 102,225 pairs from SimpleWiki, 28,376,115 pairs from machine-translated cross-lingual parallel corpora, 684,100 pairs from SPECTER citation triplets, 4,104,600 pairs from machine-translated SPECTER citation triplets, 100,231 pairs from Natural Questions, 87,599 pairs from SQuAD2.0, 85,000 pairs from HotpotQA, 109,810 pairs from Fever, 20,000,000 pairs from PubMed, 81,409 pairs from Multilingual Miracl Triples, 48,715 pairs from Multilingual MrTydi Triples, 4,037 pairs from Sadeeem Question Answering, 4,635,922 pairs from DBPedia Title-Body Pairs, 1,879,093 pairs from synthetic English Query-Wikipedia Passage, 9,888 pairs from synthetic English Fact Verification, 300,266 pairs from synthetic Multilingual Query-Wikipedia Passage, 37,489 pairs from synthetic Multilingual News Summaries, 40,290 pairs from IBM Internal Triples, and 1,524,586 pairs from IBM Internal Title-Body Pairs. The model is trained using IBM's Cognitive Compute Cluster, which is equipped with NVIDIA A100 80GB GPUs. The data used to train the base language model was filtered to remove text containing hate, abuse, and profanity. The model is fine-tuned on 12 languages and has a context length of 512 tokens, with longer texts being truncated to this size. The average performance of the model on Multilingual Miracl (across 18 languages), Mintaka Retrieval (across 8 languages), and MTEB Retrieval for English (across 15 tasks), German (across 4 tasks), Spanish (across 2 tasks), French (across 5 tasks), Japanese (across 2 tasks), Arabic (1 task), Korean (1 task), and Chinese (across 8 tasks) is reported as follows: 58.3 on Miracl, 23.2 on Mintaka Retrieval, 48.2 on MTEB English, 71.2 on MTEB German, 52.6 on MTEB Spanish, 54.1 on MTEB French, 61.7 on MTEB Japanese, 64.2 on MTEB Arabic, 71.8 on MTEB Korean, and 45.2 on MTEB Chinese. The model does not use the MS-MARCO retrieval dataset in its training corpus due to its non-commercial license, while other open-source models train on this dataset due to its high quality.

instruction: I need a way to compare two videos to see how similar their visual content is—how can I do that?
description: V-JEPA 2 is a video understanding model developed by FAIR at Meta. It extends the pretraining objectives of previous models, resulting in state-of-the-art video understanding capabilities, and leverages data and model sizes at scale. The model is intended to represent any video (and image) for tasks such as video classification, retrieval, or as a video encoder for vision-language models. V-JEPA 2 is designed to process videos by sampling a specific number of frames, with 64 frames used for this model. For images, the model repeats the image across the desired number of frames. The model can generate video or image embeddings using its vision features. V-JEPA 2 is available on the Hugging Face model repository as 'facebook/vjepa2-vitl-fpc64-256'. The model is described in a technical report titled 'V-JEPA 2: Self-Supervised Video Models Enable Understanding, Prediction and Planning', authored by Mahmoud Assran, Adrien Bardes, David Fan, Quentin Garrido, Russell Howes, Mojtaba Komeili, Matthew Muckley, Ammar Rizvi, Claire Roberts, Koustuv Sinha, Artem Zholus, Sergio Arnaud, Abha Gejji, Ada Martin, Francois Robert Hogan, Daniel Dugas, Piotr Bojanowski, Vasil Khalidov, Patrick Labatut, Francisco Massa, Marc Szafraniec, Kapil Krishnakumar, Yong Li, Xiaodong Ma, Sarath Chandar, Franziska Meier, and Yann LeCun, published in 2025 by FAIR at Meta.

instruction: Can you determine the speaker's accent type from this 14-second clip?
description: This model implements narrow accent classification based on the Whisper-Large v3 architecture, as described in the paper 'Vox-Profile: A Speech Foundation Model Benchmark for Characterizing Diverse Speaker and Speech Traits'. It is designed to classify English accents into 16 categories: 'East Asia', 'English', 'Germanic', 'Irish', 'North America', 'Northern Irish', 'Oceania', 'Other', 'Romance', 'Scottish', 'Semitic', 'Slavic', 'South African', 'Southeast Asia', 'South Asia', and 'Welsh'. The model is intended for use with audio inputs that are up to 15 seconds in length, sampled at 16kHz, and in mono channel format. Audio shorter than 3 seconds is considered unreliable for prediction, and audio longer than 15 seconds is subject to computational limitations. The model is implemented using the WhisperWrapper class and can be loaded from Huggingface. It is trained to process speech data and output probabilities for each accent category using a softmax function. One observed tendency is that text-to-speech (TTS) samples are more likely to be classified as Germanic. The model is released under the Open RAIL license, and users are required to respect the privacy and consent of data subjects and comply with relevant laws and regulations. The model is not intended for clinical or diagnostic applications, surveillance, privacy-invasive applications, or commercial use.

instruction: Generate a textured mesh for a dragon with wings and glowing eyes as described in the prompt.
description: TriplaneTurbo is a model designed for instant text-to-mesh generation without the need for 3D data. It adapts Stable Diffusion through a method called Progressive Rendering Distillation. The model is capable of generating textured meshes in approximately one second and demonstrates strong comprehension of complex text prompts to ensure accurate generation. It is trained without relying on any 3D datasets, making it more resource-efficient and adaptable. The model supports fast inference and is optimized for text comprehension. Evaluation metrics include CLIP Similarity Score and CLIP Recall@1. The model can be trained using text prompts from 3DTopia or DALLE+Midjourney datasets. Training options include single or multi-GPU configurations, with specific memory requirements depending on the configuration used. The model is based on several prior works, including codebases for 3D generation via Score Distillation, multi-view teachers for supervision, and methods for mesh rasterization and volume rendering. The model is accepted at CVPR 2025 and its paper is available on Arxiv.

instruction: What is the total revenue generated by all the sales agents listed?
description: This model is a TAPAS small model fine-tuned on Sequential Question Answering (SQA). It has two versions: the default version corresponds to the `tapas_sqa_inter_masklm_small_reset` checkpoint, and the other version is `no_reset`, corresponding to `tapas_sqa_inter_masklm_small`. The model was pre-trained on masked language modeling (MLM) and an additional step called intermediate pre-training, followed by fine-tuning. It uses relative position embeddings, resetting the position index at every cell of the table for the default version, while the non-default version uses absolute position embeddings. TAPAS is a BERT-like transformer model pre-trained on a large corpus of English data from Wikipedia in a self-supervised manner. It was pre-trained with two objectives: masked language modeling, where 15% of the words in a flattened table and associated context are randomly masked and the model must predict them, and intermediate pre-training, where the model is trained to classify whether a sentence is supported or refuted by a table using a balanced dataset of syntactically created examples. The model learns an inner representation of English language used in tables and associated texts, which can be used for downstream tasks such as answering questions about a table or determining if a sentence is entailed or refuted by a table. Fine-tuning involves adding a cell selection head on top of the pre-trained model and jointly training this classification head with the base model on SQA. The model can be used for answering questions related to a table in a conversational setup. The texts are lowercased and tokenized using WordPiece with a vocabulary size of 30,000. The model inputs are of the form [CLS] Question [SEP] Flattened table [SEP]. The model was fine-tuned on 32 Cloud TPU v3 cores for 200,000 steps with a maximum sequence length of 512 and a batch size of 128. Fine-tuning takes approximately 20 hours. The optimizer used is Adam with a learning rate of 1.25e-5 and a warmup ratio of 0.2. An inductive bias is added so that the model only selects cells of the same column, as reflected by the `select_one_column` parameter of `TapasConfig`. The model achieves a dev accuracy of 0.6155 for the reset version and 0.5876 for the noreset version in the small size category. The team releasing TAPAS did not write a model card for this model; this model card was written by the Hugging Face team and contributors.
domain: Natural Language Processing Table Question Answering

instruction: What output is produced when this image of a bookshelf is analyzed?
description: This is a MobileNet-V4 image classification model, trained on the ImageNet-1k dataset by Ross Wightman. The model was trained using `timm` scripts with hyper-parameters inspired by the MobileNet-V4 paper and enhanced by `timm` features. It is noted that these are the only known MobileNet-V4 (MNV4) weights available at the time, as official weights for Tensorflow models have not been released. The model is designed for image classification and can also serve as a feature backbone. It has 2.2 million parameters, 0.1 GMACs, and 1.2 million activations. The training image size is 224 x 224, and the test image size is 256 x 256. The model supports tasks such as image classification, feature map extraction, and image embeddings. For image classification, the model can be used to generate top-5 probabilities and class indices. For feature map extraction, the model can output feature maps with varying shapes, such as [1, 32, 112, 112] and [1, 480, 7, 7]. For image embeddings, the model can produce a tensor of image features by removing the classifier or using forward features and forward head methods. The model is based on the MobileNetV4 architecture described in the paper titled 'MobileNetV4 -- Universal Models for the Mobile Ecosystem' and is implemented using the PyTorch Image Models library. The model is evaluated on the ImageNet-1k dataset, and its performance is compared with other models in terms of top-1 accuracy, top-5 accuracy, parameter count, and image size. The model has a top-1 accuracy of 64.762% and a top-5 accuracy of 85.514% on 224 x 224 images. It is important to note that these are the only known MNV4 weights and that official weights for Tensorflow models are not yet available.

instruction: How can I generate a voice clip that includes both speech and a laugh in the middle?
description: Dia is a 1.6B parameter text to speech model created by Nari Labs. It directly generates highly realistic dialogue from a transcript. The output can be conditioned on audio, enabling control over emotion and tone. The model can also produce nonverbal communications such as laughter, coughing, and clearing the throat. To accelerate research, access to pretrained model checkpoints and inference code is provided. The model weights are hosted on a platform, and the model currently supports only English generation. A comparison of the model to other models is also provided. The model was not fine-tuned on a specific voice, so different voices will be generated with each run. Speaker consistency can be maintained by adding an audio prompt or by fixing the seed. Features include generating dialogue using [S1] and [S2] tags, generating non-verbal sounds like (laughs), (coughs), and others, and voice cloning by uploading an audio file and placing its transcript before the script. The model can be used as a Python library by importing and running the model from the Dia package. Hardware requirements include GPU support (pytorch 2.0+, CUDA 12.6), with CPU support to be added. On enterprise GPUs, audio can be generated in real-time, while older GPUs will have slower inference times. On an A4000 GPU, the model generates approximately 40 tokens per second, with 86 tokens equaling one second of audio. The full version of Dia requires around 10GB of VRAM, with a quantized version to be added in the future. The project is licensed under the Apache License 2.0. The model is intended for research and educational use, and the following uses are strictly forbidden: identity misuse, deceptive content, and illegal or malicious use. The creators are not responsible for any misuse and oppose unethical usage. Future work includes Docker support, optimizing inference speed, and adding quantization for memory efficiency. Contributions are welcome, and the team is small, consisting of one full-time and one part-time research-engineer. The project acknowledges computation resources provided by an unnamed entity, inspiration from other works, and support from HuggingFace for the ZeroGPU Grant. The name 'Nari' is a Korean word for lily, and Jason Y. is thanked for help with data filtering.

instruction: Could you highlight the area where the person signed this document?
description: This model is a YOLOv8s-based object detection model fine-tuned to detect handwritten signatures in document images. The model was selected for its balance between precision, recall, and inference time. It was trained on a dataset consisting of 2,819 document images, with 1,980 images used for training (70%), 420 for validation (15%), and 419 for testing (15%). The dataset was processed to a resolution of 640x640 pixels in COCO JSON format. During the training process, multiple object detection models were evaluated, including YOLO, DETR, RT-DETR, Conditional-DETR, and YOLOS, with the YOLOv8s model ultimately chosen for hyperparameter tuning. Hyperparameter optimization was performed using Optuna, with parameters including dropout, learning rate, box loss weight, class loss weight, and optimizer type. After tuning, the YOLOv8s model achieved a precision of 94.74%, recall of 89.72%, mAP@50 of 94.50%, and mAP@50-95 of 67.35% on the test set. Inference times were 171.56 ms using ONNX Runtime (CPU) and 7.657 ms using TensorRT (GPU - T4). The model can be used via Python code, CLI, ONNX Runtime, or TensorRT. It is available in PyTorch, ONNX, and TensorRT formats. The model weights are licensed under AGPL-3.0, while the code, training, deployment, and data are licensed under Apache 2.0. The model was developed and trained in a Jupyter Notebook environment on an Ubuntu 22.04 system with Python 3.10.12, PyTorch 2.5.1+cu121, Ultralytics 8.3.58, and other supporting libraries. Training was conducted on a Google Cloud Platform n1-standard-8 instance with 8 vCPUs and an NVIDIA Tesla T4 GPU. A live demo is available for real-time inference, and the model can be deployed using the NVIDIA Triton Inference Server for production environments.

instruction: Could you create a written transcript of a customer support call, including pauses and speaker changes?
description: The parakeet-tdt-0.6b-v2 is a 600-million-parameter automatic speech recognition (ASR) model designed for high-quality English transcription. It supports punctuation, capitalization, and accurate timestamp prediction. The model is an XL variant of the FastConformer architecture, integrating the TDT decoder and trained with full attention, enabling efficient transcription of audio segments up to 24 minutes in a single pass. It achieves an RTFx of 3380 on the HF-Open-ASR leaderboard with a batch size of 128, though performance may vary depending on dataset audio duration and batch size. Key features include accurate word-level timestamp predictions, automatic punctuation and capitalization, and robust performance on spoken numbers and song lyrics transcription. The model is intended for developers, researchers, academics, and industries building applications that require speech-to-text capabilities, including conversational AI, voice assistants, transcription services, subtitle generation, and voice analytics platforms. It is trained on the Granary dataset, consisting of approximately 120,000 hours of English speech data, including 10,000 hours from human-transcribed NeMo ASR Set 3.0 and 110,000 hours of pseudo-labeled data. The model is evaluated using the Huggingface Open ASR Leaderboard datasets, with performance measured using Word Error Rate (WER). The base performance shows an average WER of 6.05, with specific WER values for datasets such as AMI (11.16), Earnings-22 (11.15), GigaSpeech (9.74), LS test-clean (1.69), LS test-other (3.19), SPGI Speech (2.17), TEDLIUM-v3 (3.38), and VoxPopuli (5.95). Performance across different Signal-to-Noise Ratios (SNR) shows an increase in WER as SNR decreases, with relative changes ranging from -14.75% at SNR 10 to -234.66% at SNR -5. Performance on telephony-style audio (μ-law encoding) shows a slight increase in WER compared to standard 16kHz audio. The model is compatible with NVIDIA GPU-accelerated systems and is optimized to run on NVIDIA Ampere, Blackwell, Hopper, and Volta microarchitectures. It is available for use in the NeMo toolkit and can be used as a pre-trained checkpoint for inference or fine-tuning on another dataset. The model is ready for commercial and non-commercial use and is governed by the specified license. Ethical considerations include ensuring the model meets requirements for the relevant industry and use case and addressing unforeseen product misuse. The model has been verified to meet prescribed NVIDIA quality standards and has technical limitations, including potential inaccuracies in transcripts and variations in accuracy based on language and input audio characteristics. The model is not recommended for word-for-word or incomplete sentences as accuracy varies based on the context of input text. The model is not designed to generate or reverse engineer personal data, and data labeling complies with privacy laws. However, data correction or removal is not possible with externally-sourced data.

instruction: What does this diagram suggest about the relationship between the two variables?
description: INFRL-Qwen2.5-VL-72B-Preview improves visual reasoning upon a previous model. As of March 25th, 2025, INFRL-Qwen2.5-VL-72B-Preview is the best-performing open-sourced visual language (VL) model on various visual reasoning benchmarks. On the MathVision (test) benchmark, it achieves a score of 41.9, outperforming models such as GPT4o (30.6), Gemini-2.0-Flash (41.3), Claude 3.5 Sonnet (33.5), QvQ-72B (35.9), InternVL2.5-78B (34.9), and Qwen-VL-2.5-72B (38.1). On the MathVista (testmini) benchmark, it achieves a score of 77.8, outperforming GPT4o (60), Gemini-2.0-Flash (70.1), Claude 3.5 Sonnet (67.7), QvQ-72B (71.4), InternVL2.5-78B (72.3), and Qwen-VL-2.5-72B (74.8). On the MathVerse (testmini) benchmark, it achieves a score of 58.84, outperforming GPT4o (41.2), Gemini-2.0-Flash (50.6), Claude 3.5 Sonnet (47.8), QvQ-72B (48.6), InternVL2.5-78B (51.7), and Qwen-VL-2.5-72B (57.18). A code repository for VLM evaluation will be released, supporting RL training with simple rule-based rewards and aligning with LLM-Judge results.

instruction: Can you tell me if this picture is anime or something else?
description: nsfw-image-detection is a vision-language encoder model fine-tuned from siglip2-base-patch16-256 for multi-class image classification. Built on the SiglipForImageClassification architecture, the model is trained to identify and categorize content types in images, especially for explicit, suggestive, or safe media filtering. The model classifies each image into one of the following content categories: Anime Picture, Hentai, Normal, Pornography, or Enticing or Sensual. Evaluation results show a weighted average accuracy of 0.9137 across 26483 samples. For Anime Picture, the model achieves a precision of 0.8940, recall of 0.8718, and f1-score of 0.8827. For Hentai, the precision is 0.8961, recall is 0.8935, and f1-score is 0.8948. For Normal, the precision is 0.9100, recall is 0.8895, and f1-score is 0.8997. For Pornography, the precision is 0.9496, recall is 0.9654, and f1-score is 0.9574. For Enticing or Sensual, the precision is 0.9132, recall is 0.9429, and f1-score is 0.9278.

instruction: Is there a way to find the document that has the same chart but with more detailed data?
description: DSE-QWen2-2b-MRL-V1 is a bi-encoder model designed to encode document screenshots into dense vectors for document retrieval. The Document Screenshot Embedding approach captures documents in their original visual format, preserving all information such as text, images, and layout, thus avoiding tedious parsing and potential information loss. DSE aims to provide a generalizable embedding model for Text, PDF documents, Webpage, and Slides retrieval. For example, DSE-QWen2-2b-MRL-V1 achieves 85.8 nDCG@5 on a leaderboard. The QWen vision encoder may require high GPU memory if the input image is large. Users should adjust the 'resized_height' and 'resized_width' parameters to fit VRAM based on GPU resources. This checkpoint is trained to support flexible representation dimension and flexible input image size. The model can encode both document screenshots and document text inputs, with the latter being warmed up using the Tevatron/msmarco-passage-aug dataset. The model is intended to be used for encoding text queries and document screenshots into embeddings, after which similarity scores can be computed using cosine similarity. The model is based on the Qwen2VLForConditionalGeneration architecture and uses the AutoProcessor for input processing. The model supports adjusting the embedding dimensionality for an effectiveness-efficiency trade-off. The model is available for use with the Hugging Face Transformers library and requires specific parameters for loading and processing inputs. The model is evaluated on a retrieval task and achieves a performance score of 85.8 nDCG@5. Users are advised to consider GPU memory constraints when using large input images and to adjust the input size accordingly. The model can be cited in academic or research contexts by referencing QWen2, Docmatix, ViDoRe, and the DSE work.

instruction: What breed does this cat look like in the photo?
description: This model is a fine-tuned version of a pre-trained Vision Transformer (google/vit-base-patch16-224) for image classification on the Oxford-IIIT Pet Dataset. It uses transfer learning to adapt a generic vision model to identify 37 different cat and dog breeds. The model head is adjusted to output the number of classes in the dataset, and it is trained end-to-end using standard classification loss. The model achieves a loss of 0.1924 and an accuracy of 0.9445 on the evaluation set. It is intended for educational demos on transfer learning and fine-tuning vision models, pet breed classification in structured datasets similar to Oxford Pets, and comparative analysis with zero-shot models like CLIP. The model may not generalize well to breeds outside of the Oxford-IIIT dataset and is not suitable for real-world medical or safety-critical applications. Input images should be clear, centered, and close in style to the training data (cropped pet portraits). The model is trained and evaluated on the Oxford-IIIT Pet Dataset, which contains 7,349 images of cats and dogs spanning 37 different breeds. The dataset includes equal representation of pets and was split into training, validation, and test sets. Evaluation metrics used include accuracy, precision, and recall. The training hyperparameters used are a learning rate of 0.0003, a train batch size of 16, an eval batch size of 8, a seed of 42, the adamw_torch optimizer with betas of (0.9, 0.999) and epsilon of 1e-08, a linear learning rate scheduler, and 5 training epochs. During training, the training loss decreased from 0.3716 to 0.1235 over five epochs, with corresponding validation losses of 0.3013, 0.2342, 0.2124, 0.2050, and 0.2032, and validation accuracies of 0.9242, 0.9310, 0.9350, 0.9350, and 0.9350. The model was also evaluated using a zero-shot image classification model, with breed names provided as candidate labels. The zero-shot evaluation achieved an accuracy of 0.8800, a precision of 0.8768, and a recall of 0.8800. The model was developed using Transformers 4.50.0, Pytorch 2.6.0+cu124, Datasets 3.4.1, and Tokenizers 0.21.1.

instruction: What is the mood or tone of this video?
description: VideoLLaMA 2 is a video language model designed to advance spatial-temporal modeling and audio understanding in video-LLMs. It supports tasks such as multi-choice and open-ended video question answering, video captioning, multi-choice and open-ended audio question answering, and open-ended audio-visual question answering. The model includes both vision-only and audio-visual checkpoints, with different configurations for base and chat types, and varying numbers of training frames. VideoLLaMA 2 is available in a ~7B-sized version and has achieved top performance on relevant benchmarks. The model is capable of handling audio, video, and audio-visual modalities, with inference code provided for each. The model is based on a technical report that includes the latest results and references, and it is continuously updated with new checkpoints and features. Users are encouraged to cite the model in their research and applications.

instruction: Can you predict the function of this non-coding RNA sequence based on its structure?
description: RNAErnie is a pre-trained model on non-coding RNA (ncRNA) using a multi-stage masked language modeling (MLM) objective. It is designed to support various RNA-related tasks, including masked language modeling, sequence classification or regression, token classification or regression, and contact classification or regression. The model is intended to be used as a backbone for fine-tuning on downstream tasks. RNAErnie is a -style model pre-trained on a large corpus of non-coding RNA sequences in a self-supervised fashion, meaning it was trained on the raw nucleotides of RNA sequences only, with an automatic process to generate inputs and labels from those texts. The model has 12 layers, a hidden size of 768, 12 attention heads, an intermediate size of 3072, 86.06 million parameters, 22.37 gigaflops, 11.17 gigamacs, and a maximum of 512 tokens. It was trained using a 3-stage training pipeline with different masking strategies: base-level masking (each nucleotide in the sequence), subsequence-level masking (subsequences of 4-8bp in the sequence), and motif-level masking (motif datasets). The training data was a subset of RNAcentral containing 23 million sequences. During preprocessing, all tokens were preprocessed by replacing 'T's with 'S's, and the tokenizer may convert 'T's to 'U's, which can be disabled. The model was trained on 4 NVIDIA V100 GPUs with 32GiB memories, using a batch size of 50, 2,580,000 steps, the AdamW optimizer, a learning rate of 1e-4, a learning rate warm-up of 129,000 steps, a learning rate cool-down of 129,000 steps, a minimum learning rate of 5e-5, and a weight decay of 0.01. It is important to note that this is an unofficial implementation of RNAErnie, and the MultiMolecule team is unable to confirm that the provided model and checkpoints are producing the same intermediate representations as the original implementation. The model is licensed under the AGPL-3.0-or-later license.

instruction: Hãy tìm tài liệu pháp lý liên quan đến việc thành lập quỹ từ thiện.
description: DEk21_hcmute_embedding is a Vietnamese text embedding model focused on RAG and production efficiency. The model was trained on an in-house dataset consisting of approximately 100,000 examples of legal questions and their related contexts. It is trained with a Matryoshka loss, allowing embeddings to be truncated with minimal performance loss. This ensures that smaller embeddings are faster to compare, making the model efficient for real-world production use. The model type is a Sentence Transformer, with a maximum sequence length of 256 tokens and output dimensionality of 768 dimensions. It uses cosine similarity as the similarity function and is designed for the Vietnamese language. The model is licensed under the Apache-2.0 license. The model architecture consists of a Transformer with a RobertaModel and a Pooling layer that uses mean token pooling. The model is intended for tasks involving Vietnamese legal text retrieval and is evaluated on information retrieval metrics such as NDCG and MRR. Evaluation results show that the model achieves an NDCG@3 score of 0.752173, an NDCG@5 score of 0.769259, an NDCG@10 score of 0.785101, an MRR@3 score of 0.72474, an MRR@5 score of 0.734427, and an MRR@10 score of 0.741076. The model is compared to other Vietnamese embedding models and achieves competitive performance. The model is intended for use with the Sentence Transformers library and requires Vietnamese text to be tokenized using a Vietnamese tokenizer before encoding. The model is available on Hugging Face and can be loaded and used for inference with the Sentence Transformers library. The model is designed for use in production environments and is optimized for efficiency. The model is not intended for use with non-Vietnamese text or for tasks outside of information retrieval.

instruction: I need to sort these student test scores into pass and fail categories—can you assist?
description: TabPFNMix classifier is a tabular foundation model pre-trained on purely synthetic datasets sampled from a mix of random classifiers. It is based on a 12-layer encoder-decoder Transformer with 37 million parameters. The pre-training strategy incorporates in-context learning, similar to that used by TabPFN and TabForestPFN. The model is designed for tasks involving tabular data classification and supports fine-tuning and inference. It is implemented within the AutoGluon framework, and users are instructed to install AutoGluon to utilize the model. Performance details are not explicitly provided in the text. The model is intended for use with structured data and is evaluated in the context of solving small tabular classification problems. Users are advised to cite the associated papers if they find TabPFNMix useful for their research. The project is licensed under the Apache-2.0 License.

instruction: Could you help me estimate the outcome for this dataset?
description: The model is a RandomForestRegressor, designed for regression tasks. It is trained using a procedure that includes hyperparameters such as bootstrap set to True, ccp_alpha set to 0.0, criterion set to squared_error, max_features set to 1.0, min_samples_leaf set to 1, min_samples_split set to 2, n_estimators set to 100, oob_score set to False, and verbose set to 0. The model is evaluated using the test split method, with the root mean squared error (rmse) as the performance metric. The evaluation results show an rmse value of 0.307471. The model is unfitted, as indicated by its visual representation and associated styling. No specific intended uses, limitations, or additional details about the training procedure are provided. No information is available about the model card authors, contact channels, or citation details.

instruction: Find the best-matching answer from a knowledge base for a given user question in German.
description: This is a Sentence Transformer model trained on approximately 300k triple-samples. It maps sentences and paragraphs to a 1024-dimensional dense vector space and can be used for semantic textual similarity, semantic search, paraphrase mining, text classification, clustering, and more. The model was merged with a base model to maintain performance on other languages. The model has a maximum sequence length of 512 tokens and uses cosine similarity as the similarity function. The full model architecture includes a Transformer with a BERT model, a Pooling layer with a word embedding dimension of 1024 and pooling mode set to the CLS token, and a Normalize layer. The model was evaluated on several MTEB tasks, including classification, pair classification, retrieval, and semantic textual similarity. On the AmazonCounterfactualClassification task, the model achieved a score of 0.5401, which is a -2.48% decrease compared to the merged model. On the AmazonReviewsClassification task, the model achieved a score of 0.2782, which is a 0.44% increase compared to the merged model. On the FalseFriendsGermanEnglish task, the model achieved a score of 0.4703, which is a -1.05% decrease compared to the merged model. On the GermanQuAD-Retrieval task, the model achieved a score of 0.8628, which is an 8.18% increase compared to the merged model. On the GermanSTSBenchmark task, the model achieved a score of 0.6754, which is a 3.33% increase compared to the merged model. On the MassiveIntentClassification task, the model achieved a score of 0.4714, which is a -4.25% decrease compared to the merged model. On the MassiveScenarioClassification task, the model achieved a score of 0.6111, which is a 0.49% increase compared to the merged model. On the GermanDPR task, the model achieved a score of 0.7507, which is a 7.57% increase compared to the merged model. On the MTOPDomainClassification task, the model achieved a score of 0.7686, which is a 0.61% increase compared to the merged model. On the MTOPIntentClassification task, the model achieved a score of 0.4413, which is a -5.80% decrease compared to the merged model. On the PawsXPairClassification task, the model achieved a score of 0.5162, which is a -2.90% decrease compared to the merged model. The model was also evaluated on the German-RAG-Embedding-Benchmark, where it achieved an accuracy of 0.8866. The model was trained using Python 3.10.12, Sentence Transformers 3.2.1, Transformers 4.44.2, PyTorch 2.5.0+cu121, Accelerate 0.34.2, Datasets 2.19.0, and Tokenizers 0.19.1. The model is cited in the paper 'AnglE-optimized Text Embeddings' by Li, Xianming and Li, Jing, published in 2023 as an arXiv preprint.

instruction: I want to visualize depth in this photo—can you create a depth map for it?
description: Prompt Depth Anything is a high-resolution and accurate metric depth estimation method. It uses prompting to leverage the power of depth foundation models, drawing inspiration from the success of prompting in vision-language and large language model foundation models. The method utilizes widely available iPhone LiDAR as a prompt to guide the model in producing up to 4K resolution accurate metric depth. A scalable data pipeline is introduced to train the method. Prompt Depth Anything is designed to benefit downstream applications, including 3D reconstruction and generalized robotic grasping.

instruction: Is it possible to adjust how depth is applied in a video generated from a written description?
description: This model is a dilated controlnet module designed for the Wan2.1 model. The dilated controlnet includes fewer basic blocks and incorporates a `stride` parameter. For the Wan1.3B model, the number of controlnet blocks is 8 and the stride is set to 3. The model supports inference tasks for video generation, including command-line interface and Gradio-based web demonstrations. It is intended to be used with the Wan2.1-T2V-1.3B-Diffusers base model and the specified controlnet model path. The model allows for detailed inference with customizable parameters such as controlnet weight, guidance start and end, number of inference steps, guidance scale, video resolution, number of frames, negative prompts, seed, output frame rate, and output path. The model is based on original code and models, and users are encouraged to raise issues directly in the repository. Professional support can be requested via email.

instruction: Produce a visual of a dancer performing on a stage under colorful spotlights.
description: This is a GGUF quantized and fp8 scaled version of the LTX-Video model. It is designed to support video generation tasks, including text-to-video (t2v) and image-text-to-video (i2v) generation. The model can be run using the gguf-connector or through the gguf-node via ComfyUI. It supports various GGUF file options, including ltxv-2b-0.9.8-distilled-iq4_nl.gguf, ltxv-2b-0.9.8-distilled-q6_k.gguf, and ltxv-2b-0.9.8-distilled-q8_0.gguf. The model can be executed by running commands such as 'ggc x2' or 'ggc vg' for the t2v panel, and 'ggc v1' for the i2v panel. The model includes a distilled version that allows for fewer inference steps, such as 15 instead of 30, to reduce loading time. It supports the use of different quantized versions of the T5XXL text encoder and various VAE configurations, including enhanced fp8 to fp32 versions. A low RAM version of the GGUF VAE is also available. The model can be used with the diffusers library, including the LTXPipeline, T5EncoderModel, and LTXVideoTransformer3DModel. It is possible to mix and match different model components, such as using different VAEs with different model files, and to create or convert custom fp8_e4m3fn scaled safetensors to GGUF format. The q2_k GGUF file is noted to be very fast but not suitable for production use and is recommended for testing only. The 0.9_fp8_e4m3fn and 0.9-vae_fp8_e4m3fn versions are reported to work well. The model has been upgraded from version 0.9 to 0.9.6 distilled for improved results. The model can be run entirely offline after the first-time launch, which automatically pulls the model files to a local cache.

instruction: Can you describe the actions and scenes in this short video clip?
description: CogVLM2-Caption is a video captioning model designed to generate textual descriptions of video data, which are used as training data for the CogVideoX model. Most video data does not come with corresponding descriptive text, so it is necessary to convert the video data into textual descriptions to provide the essential training data for text-to-video models. The model is based on the CogVLM2 architecture and incorporates features from the Meta Llama 3. It is intended to support tasks involving video captioning and the generation of detailed descriptions of video content. The model uses a combination of video input processing and natural language generation to produce captions. The model is evaluated for its ability to accurately describe video content and generate suitable training data for downstream text-to-video models. The model is released under the CogVLM2 license, and for models built with Meta Llama 3, additional licensing requirements may apply. Users are encouraged to cite the associated research paper if they find the model useful.

instruction: What is the total cost of all the items listed in the inventory table?
description: This model is a TAPAS small model fine-tuned on WikiSQL in a supervised fashion. It has two versions: the default version corresponds to the `tapas_wikisql_sqa_inter_masklm_small_reset` checkpoint, and the other version is `no_reset`, corresponding to `tapas_wikisql_sqa_inter_masklm_small`. The model was pre-trained on masked language modeling (MLM) and an additional step called intermediate pre-training, followed by fine-tuning on SQA and WikiSQL. It uses relative position embeddings, resetting the position index at every cell of the table. The non-default version uses absolute position embeddings. TAPAS is a BERT-like transformer model pre-trained on a large corpus of English data from Wikipedia in a self-supervised fashion. It was pre-trained on raw tables and associated texts without human labeling, using an automatic process to generate inputs and labels. The pre-training objectives include masked language modeling, where 15% of the words in a flattened table and associated context are randomly masked, and the model must predict the masked words, and intermediate pre-training, where the model must classify whether a sentence is supported or refuted by the contents of a table. The model learns an inner representation of the English language used in tables and associated texts, which can be used for downstream tasks such as answering questions about a table or determining whether a sentence is entailed or refuted by the contents of a table. Fine-tuning involves adding a cell selection head and aggregation head on top of the pre-trained model and jointly training these heads with the base model on SQA and WikiSQL. The model is intended for answering questions related to a table. The texts are lowercased and tokenized using WordPiece with a vocabulary size of 30,000. The inputs are of the form [CLS] Question [SEP] Flattened table [SEP]. The WikiSQL dataset was first converted into the format of SQA using automatic conversion scripts. The model was fine-tuned on 32 Cloud TPU v3 cores for 50,000 steps with a maximum sequence length of 512 and a batch size of 512. Fine-tuning takes around 10 hours. The optimizer used is Adam with a learning rate of 6.17164e-5 and a warmup ratio of 0.1424. The team releasing TAPAS did not write a model card for this model; this model card was written by the Hugging Face team and contributors.
domain: Natural Language Processing Table Question Answering

instruction: Can you extract the text from this audio and mark each speaker's lines with their own ID?
description: Kotoba-Whisper-v2.2 is a Japanese automatic speech recognition (ASR) model that includes additional postprocessing stacks. It features speaker diarization and punctuation addition. The model was developed through collaboration between unspecified entities. It is supported in the Hugging Face Transformers library from version 4.39 onwards. The model can transcribe audio with speaker diarization, identifying different speakers and their corresponding speech segments with timestamps. It also supports adding punctuation to the transcribed text. Users can control the number of speakers by specifying parameters such as num_speakers, min_speakers, and max_speakers. Adding silence before and after the audio can improve transcription quality. The model can utilize Flash Attention 2 for improved performance on compatible GPUs. To use the model, users must install specific dependencies, including the Transformers library, accelerate, torchaudio, punctuators, and pyannote.audio. Additionally, users must accept the terms-of-use for certain models and log in with a Hugging Face authentication token to access pre-trained diarization models. The model outputs transcribed text with speaker identification, timestamps, and optional punctuation. The pipeline is implemented in Python using the Transformers library and supports batch processing and device-specific configurations.

instruction: How similar are the questions 'What is machine learning?' and 'How do computers learn from data?' semantically?
description: This is a model finetuned from Alibaba-NLP/gte-modernbert-base on a dataset. It maps sentences and paragraphs to a 768-dimensional dense vector space and can be used for semantic textual similarity for the purpose of semantic caching. The model is a Sentence Transformer with a maximum sequence length of 8192 tokens and uses cosine similarity as the similarity function. The model architecture includes a Transformer with ModernBertModel and a Pooling layer that uses the cls_token with a word embedding dimension of 768. The model was trained on the Quora dataset, which contains 323,491 training samples with columns question_1, question_2, and label. It was evaluated on the Quora dataset, which contains 53,486 evaluation samples with the same columns. The model achieves a cosine accuracy of 0.90, cosine f1 of 0.87, cosine precision of 0.84, cosine recall of 0.90, and cosine average precision of 0.92. The model is intended for use with the Sentence Transformers library and can be loaded from the Hugging Face Hub. The model is described in the paper titled 'Advancing Semantic Caching for LLMs with Domain-Specific Embeddings and Synthetic Data' by Gill, Cechmanek, Hutcherson, Rajamohan, Agarwal, Gulzar, Singh, and Dion, published in 2025.

instruction: Make a video of a knight riding a horse into a dark castle courtyard.
description: This model is a dilated controlnet module designed for the Wan2.1 model. The dilated controlnet includes fewer basic blocks and incorporates a `stride` parameter. For the Wan1.3B model, the controlnet consists of 8 blocks with a stride of 3. The model supports inference tasks for video generation based on text prompts and controlnet inputs. It is intended to be used with the Wan2.1-T2V-1.3B-Diffusers base model and the specified controlnet model path. The model can be run via command-line interface or through a Gradio-based web interface. Detailed inference allows for customization of parameters such as controlnet weight, guidance start and end, number of inference steps, guidance scale, video resolution, number of frames, negative prompts, seed, and output frame rate. The model is based on original code and models, and users are encouraged to raise issues directly in the repository. Professional support can be requested via email.

instruction: Which images align most closely with the description 'a cat sleeping on a windowsill'?
description: The CLIP model was developed by researchers at OpenAI to study what contributes to robustness in computer vision tasks and to test the ability of models to generalize to arbitrary image classification tasks in a zero-shot manner. It was not developed for general model deployment, and researchers are advised to carefully study its capabilities in relation to the specific context of deployment. The model was developed in January 2021 and uses a ViT-L/14 Transformer architecture as an image encoder and a masked self-attention Transformer as a text encoder. These encoders are trained to maximize the similarity of (image, text) pairs via a contrastive loss. The original implementation had two variants: one using a ResNet image encoder and the other using a Vision Transformer. This repository contains the variant with the Vision Transformer. The model is intended for research use by AI researchers to better understand robustness, generalization, and other capabilities, biases, and constraints of computer vision models. Any deployed use case, whether commercial or not, is currently out of scope. Non-deployed use cases such as image search in a constrained environment are also not recommended unless there is thorough in-domain testing of the model with a specific, fixed class taxonomy. This is because the model's performance varies significantly with different class taxonomies, and untested deployment may be harmful. Use cases involving surveillance and facial recognition are always out of scope due to the lack of testing norms and checks to ensure fair use. The model has not been trained or evaluated on languages other than English, so its use should be limited to English language use cases. The model was trained on publicly available image-caption data, including data crawled from the internet and pre-existing image datasets. The data is more representative of people and societies most connected to the internet, which tend to skew towards more developed nations and younger, male users. The goal of the dataset was to test robustness and generalizability in computer vision tasks, and the data was gathered in a mostly non-interventionist manner, with crawling limited to websites that had policies against excessively violent and adult images. The dataset is not intended for use as the basis for any commercial or deployed model and will not be released. The model has been evaluated on a wide range of benchmarks across various computer vision datasets, including Food101, CIFAR10, CIFAR100, Birdsnap, SUN397, Stanford Cars, FGVC Aircraft, VOC2007, DTD, Oxford-IIIT Pet dataset, Caltech101, Flowers102, MNIST, SVHN, IIIT5K, Hateful Memes, SST-2, UCF101, Kinetics700, Country211, CLEVR Counting, KITTI Distance, STL-10, RareAct, Flickr30, MSCOCO, ImageNet, ImageNet-A, ImageNet-R, ImageNet Sketch, ObjectNet (ImageNet Overlap), Youtube-BB, and ImageNet-Vid. The model has several limitations, including struggles with fine-grained classification and counting objects. It also exhibits issues with fairness and bias, which depend significantly on class design and the choices made for categories to include and exclude. Testing revealed significant disparities with respect to race and gender when classifying images of people into crime-related and non-human animal categories, and these disparities could shift based on how the classes were constructed. Evaluations on the Fairface dataset showed accuracy greater than 96% across all races for gender classification, with 'Middle Eastern' having the highest accuracy (98.4%) and 'White' having the lowest (96.5%). CLIP averaged approximately 93% for racial classification and approximately 63% for age classification. These evaluations are used to assess performance across different demographics and to surface potential risks, not to endorse or promote such tasks.

instruction: I want to retrieve a part of a document that explains the history of quantum computing.
description: ColQwen2 is a model based on a novel architecture and training strategy using Vision Language Models (VLMs) to efficiently index documents from their visual features. It generates multi-vector representations of text and images, extending the Qwen2-VL-2B-Instruct model with a ColBERT strategy. The model was introduced in a paper and first released as a HuggingFace `transformers` 🤗 implementation contributed by Tony Wu and Yoni Gozlan. It is designed for document retrieval tasks, particularly for PDF-type documents, and supports the generation of embeddings for both images and text queries. The model was trained on a dataset of 127,460 query-page pairs, consisting of 63% from openly available academic datasets and 37% from a synthetic dataset of web-crawled PDF pages augmented with VLM-generated pseudo-questions. The training set is fully English, allowing for the study of zero-shot generalization to non-English languages. A validation set of 2% of the samples was used to tune hyperparameters. The model relies on multi-vector retrieval derived from the ColBERT late interaction mechanism, which may require engineering efforts to adapt to vector retrieval frameworks that lack native multi-vector support. ColQwen2's vision language backbone model (Qwen2-VL) is licensed under the Apache-2.0 license, and ColQwen2 inherits this license. The model primarily focuses on PDF-type documents and high-resource languages, which may limit its generalization to other document types or less represented languages.

instruction: I want to turn a spoken idea into a creative writing piece. Can you assist?
description: OmniAudio is a 2.6B-parameter multimodal audio-language model designed for on-device deployment, enabling secure and responsive audio-text processing directly on edge devices. It integrates three components: Gemma-2-2b, Whisper turbo, and a custom projector module, unifying speech recognition and language model capabilities in a single architecture to minimize latency and resource overhead. The model supports a range of tasks, including offline voice question answering, voice-in conversations, creative content generation from voice prompts, recording summarization, and voice tone modification. On a 2024 Mac Mini M4 Pro, the FP16 GGUF version of OmniAudio-2.6B achieves 35.23 tokens/second, and the Q4_K_M quantized GGUF version reaches 66 tokens/second, delivering 5.5x to 10.3x faster performance than Qwen2-Audio-7B-Instruct on the same hardware. The model was developed using a three-stage training pipeline: pretraining on the MLS English 10k transcription dataset with a special token to distinguish between transcription and completion tasks, supervised fine-tuning using synthetic datasets to enhance conversation capabilities, and direct preference optimization using GPT-4o API and Gemma2 text responses to refine model quality. The q4_K_M version of OmniAudio-2.6B requires 1.30GB RAM and 1.60GB storage space for on-device operation. The model is currently in active development, with future goals including direct audio generation for two-way voice communication and function calling support.

instruction: I need a clean segmentation of the foreground object in this photo. Can you generate it?
description: BiRefNet is a model designed for high-resolution dichotomous image segmentation (DIS). It is trained on the DIS-TR dataset and validated on the DIS-TE and DIS-VD datasets. The model has achieved state-of-the-art performance on three tasks: DIS, HRSOD, and COD. BiRefNet is based on a bilateral reference architecture, which is intended to improve segmentation accuracy and resolution. The model supports inference tasks for image segmentation, including object extraction and mask generation. It can be used with weights from HuggingFace or locally stored weights, and it supports inference on GPU. The model is implemented in Python using PyTorch and can be integrated with the HuggingFace Transformers library. The model weights and code are available through a GitHub repository and HuggingFace. The model has been evaluated on standard benchmarks and is described in a paper published in the CAAI Artificial Intelligence Research journal in 2024. The authors of the model are Peng Zheng, Dehong Gao, Deng-Ping Fan, Li Liu, Jorma Laaksonen, Wanli Ouyang, and Nicu Sebe, affiliated with institutions including Nankai University, Northwestern Polytechnical University, National University of Defense Technology, Aalto University, Shanghai AI Laboratory, and University of Trento. The model is supported by GPU resources and deployment assistance from external contributors.

instruction: At what point in the video does the car start moving?
description: VideoChat-Flash-7B is built upon UMT-L (300M) and Qwen2-7B, using only 16 tokens per frame. The model leverages Yarn to extend the context window from Qwen2's native 32k to 128k, enabling input sequences of up to approximately 10,000 frames. Due to a predominantly English training corpus, the model exhibits basic Chinese comprehension, and for optimal performance, it is recommended to use English for interaction. The model achieves a performance of 70.0 on MVBench, 58.3 on LongVideoBench, 57.0 on VideoMME (without subtitles), and supports a maximum of 10,000 input frames. Another version of the model achieves 73.2 on MVBench, 64.2 on LongVideoBench, 64.0 on VideoMME (without subtitles), and supports 10,000 input frames. A further variant achieves 73.4 on MVBench, 66.5 on LongVideoBench, 63.5 on VideoMME (without subtitles), and supports 50,000 input frames. Another version achieves 74.3 on MVBench, 64.5 on LongVideoBench, 65.1 on VideoMME (without subtitles), and supports 10,000 input frames. A final version achieves 74.0 on MVBench, 64.7 on LongVideoBench, and 65.3 on VideoMME (without subtitles), supporting 10,000 input frames. The model supports single-turn and multi-turn video-based conversations, where users can ask questions about the content of a video. It can describe a video in detail and answer specific questions about the number of people appearing in the video. The model uses a global compression setting, which can be toggled on or off, with specific configurations for compression type, layers, and image token ratios. The model is evaluated with a maximum of 512 frames and a generation configuration that includes no sampling, a temperature of 0.0, a maximum of 1024 new tokens, a top-p value of 0.1, and a single beam for decoding.

instruction: What’s the easiest way to detect facial structure in a portrait for a face recognition project?
description: Facial-Landmark-Detection is a real-time 3D facial landmark detection model optimized for mobile and edge deployment. It detects facial landmarks such as the nose, mouth, and other key features. The model's architecture was developed by Qualcomm and was trained on a proprietary dataset of faces, though it can be used on any image. The model has an input resolution of 128x128, 5.42 million parameters, and a model size of 20.7 MB in float precision and 5.27 MB in w8a8 precision. It is designed to run on a variety of Qualcomm® devices, including QCS8275, QCS8450, QCS8550, QCS9075, SA7255P, SA8255, SA8295P, SA8650, SA8775P, and Snapdragon®-powered devices such as the Samsung Galaxy S24 and S25. Performance metrics include inference times ranging from 0.118 ms to 3.655 ms and peak memory usage ranging from 0 MB to 146 MB, depending on the device and precision. The model is primarily executed on the NPU, with some configurations using the GPU or CPU. The model can be compiled for on-device deployment using the Qualcomm® AI Hub, and it supports TensorFlow Lite, QNN, and ONNX runtimes. It can be profiled and tested for performance and accuracy on cloud-hosted Qualcomm® devices. The model can also be deployed on Android using TensorFlow Lite or QNN shared libraries. The model is available for use via the Qualcomm® AI Hub, and users can access additional resources, including installation instructions, demos, and deployment guides. The license for the original implementation of the model is available separately.

description: Stable Diffusion 3.5 Large is a Multimodal Diffusion Transformer (MMDiT) text-to-image model developed by Stability AI. It is designed to generate images based on text prompts and features improved performance in image quality, typography, complex prompt understanding, and resource-efficiency. The model uses three fixed, pretrained text encoders and incorporates QK-normalization to improve training stability. It was trained on a wide variety of data, including synthetic data and filtered publicly available data. The model supports non-commercial and commercial use for organizations or individuals with less than $1M in total annual revenue under a community license, while those with higher revenue must obtain an Enterprise License. For local or self-hosted use, it can be accessed through ComfyUI, Huggingface Space, Diffusers, or GitHub. The model is intended for use in artistic processes, educational or creative tools, and research on generative models. It is not intended to generate factual or true representations of people or events. Safety measures have been implemented to reduce the risk of certain harms, but developers are encouraged to conduct their own testing and apply additional mitigations based on specific use cases. The model has undergone integrity evaluation methods, including structured evaluations and red-teaming testing, primarily in English. Risks include the potential for harmful content generation, misuse, and privacy violations, and developers are encouraged to implement content safety guardrails and adhere to privacy regulations. Users are required to follow the Stability AI Acceptable Use Policy. Contact information is available for reporting safety, security, privacy, licensing, or enterprise licensing issues.

description: The Aquila-VL-2B model is a vision-language model (VLM) trained using a specific framework. It is based on a selected large language model (LLM) and incorporates a vision tower for processing visual information. The model was trained on the Infinity-MM dataset, which contains approximately 40 million image-text pairs. This dataset is a combination of open-source data collected from the internet and synthetic instruction data generated using open-source VLM models. The Infinity-MM dataset and related resources have been open-sourced for public use. The model was evaluated using a benchmarking tool, with a preference for using the OpenAI API for test sets that support API-based evaluation. Evaluation results show that the model achieves high performance across multiple benchmarks, including MMBench-ENtest (78.8), MMBench-CNtest (76.4), MMBench_V1.1test (75.2), MMT-Benchtest (58.2), RealWorldQA (63.9), HallusionBench (43.0), SEEDBench2plus (63.0), LLaVABench (68.4), MMStar (54.9), POPE (83.6), MMVet (44.3), MMMUval (47.4), ScienceQAtest (95.2), AI2Dtest (75.0), MathVistatestmini (59.0), MathVersetestmini (26.2), MathVision (18.4), DocVQAtest (85.0), InfoVQAtest (58.3), ChartQAtest (76.5), TextVQAval (76.4), OCRVQAtestcore (64.0), VCRen easy (70.0), OCRBench (772), and an overall average score of 64.1. These scores are compared to other models such as MiniCPM-V-2, InternVL2-2B, XinYuan-VL-2B, and Qwen2-VL-2B-Instruct. Evaluations were conducted in a local environment, so the scores may differ slightly from those reported in papers or on the official VLMEvalKit leaderboard. The model can be used by loading it with a specified Python library, processing images, and generating text outputs based on visual inputs. The model supports tasks such as answering questions about images, and it can be used with a specific conversation template. The model is designed to be used in a local environment with a CUDA device. The developers plan to train models of various sizes and to incorporate multi-image and video data in future training. If the model is used, the associated work should be cited as described in the provided citation.

instruction: Make a video of a train passing through a tunnel, with dramatic lighting and motion blur.
description: Wan2.1 is a comprehensive and open suite of video foundation models designed to push the boundaries of video generation. It offers state-of-the-art performance, consistently outperforming existing open-source models and state-of-the-art commercial solutions across multiple benchmarks. The T2V-1.3B model requires only 8.19 GB VRAM, making it compatible with nearly all consumer-grade GPUs. It can generate a 5-second 480P video on an RTX 4090 in about 4 minutes, with performance comparable to some closed-source models. Wan2.1 supports multiple tasks, including Text-to-Video, Image-to-Video, Video Editing, Text-to-Image, and Video-to-Audio, advancing the field of video generation. It is the first video model capable of generating both Chinese and English text, enhancing its practical applications. The model features a powerful video VAE, Wan-VAE, which delivers exceptional efficiency and performance, encoding and decoding 1080P videos of any length while preserving temporal information. Wan2.1 is designed using the Flow Matching framework within the paradigm of mainstream Diffusion Transformers. It uses a T5 Encoder to encode multilingual text input, with cross-attention in each transformer block embedding the text into the model structure. An MLP with a Linear layer and a SiLU layer processes the input time embeddings and predicts six modulation parameters individually. This MLP is shared across all transformer blocks, with each block learning a distinct set of biases. The model's architecture includes a novel 3D causal VAE specifically designed for video generation, combining multiple strategies to improve spatio-temporal compression, reduce memory usage, and ensure temporal causality. Wan-VAE can encode and decode unlimited-length 1080P videos without losing historical temporal information, making it particularly well-suited for video generation tasks. The model is trained on a large-scale dataset curated through a four-step data cleaning process, focusing on fundamental dimensions, visual quality, and motion quality. The T2V-1.3B model has 1.3 billion parameters with a dimension of 1536, an input dimension of 16, an output dimension of 16, a feedforward dimension of 8960, a frequency dimension of 256, 12 heads, and 30 layers. The T2V-14B model has 14 billion parameters with a dimension of 5120, an input dimension of 16, an output dimension of 16, a feedforward dimension of 13824, a frequency dimension of 256, 40 heads, and 40 layers. The model is evaluated using the Wan-Bench framework, demonstrating that the 1.3B model surpasses the overall metrics of larger open-source models. The model is licensed under the Apache 2.0 License, and users are responsible for ensuring their usage complies with the provisions of this license. The model is intended for use in a variety of applications, including creative video creation and academic research, and is designed to be accessible to teams with limited computing resources.

instruction: What are the different zones in this image, and how are they classified?
description: EoMT (Encoder-only Mask Transformer) is a Vision Transformer (ViT) architecture designed for high-quality and efficient image segmentation. It was introduced in the CVPR 2025 highlight paper titled 'Your ViT is Secretly an Image Segmentation Model' by Tommie Kerssies, Niccolò Cavagnero, Alexander Hermans, Narges Norouzi, Giuseppe Averta, Bastian Leibe, Gijs Dubbelman, and Daan de Geus. The key insight is that, given sufficient scale and pretraining, a plain ViT along with additional few parameters can perform segmentation without the need for task-specific decoders or pixel fusion modules. The same model backbone supports semantic, instance, and panoptic segmentation with different post-processing. The original implementation is available in a separate repository, and the HuggingFace model page is also available.

instruction: What does the law say about property ownership for foreigners in Turkmenistan?
description: This is a Turkmen language large language model (LLM) that has been fine-tuned using Turkmen law data. It was developed by Annamyrat Saparow and funded on 30.04.2025. The model was shared on 07.05.2025 and is categorized as an instruction model. It supports the Turkmen and English languages and is licensed under the MIT license. The model was fine-tuned from the llama 3.1 8b instruct model. The training data used is the maximilianshwarzmullers/hukukchy dataset, which is the developer's own dataset for law. The training procedure involved QLora. The model uses a preprocessing method where each example is formatted with an instruction, input, and response, followed by an end-of-sequence token. The dataset was loaded from the maximilianshwarzmullers/hukukchy repository and processed in batches. The training was conducted using the SFTTrainer from the trl library with specific hyperparameters including a per-device train batch size of 2, gradient accumulation steps of 4, warmup steps of 5, a maximum of 750 training steps, a learning rate of 3e-5, and support for either fp16 or bf16 precision depending on hardware support. Additional training settings include logging every step, the use of the adamw_8bit optimizer, a weight decay of 0.01, a linear learning rate scheduler, a seed of 3407, and an output directory named 'outputs'.
domain: Natural Language Processing Table Question Answering

instruction: What is the predicted outcome for a patient with a high diagnostic density score and frequent hospital stays?
description: This model predicts 30-day hospital readmissions for diabetic patients using historical patient data and machine learning techniques. The model aims to identify high-risk individuals, enabling targeted interventions and improved healthcare resource allocation. It is a binary classification model implemented using the Autogluon framework in Python, and it is licensed under the MIT license. The model achieved a test accuracy of 0.8865 and a test ROC-AUC of 0.6467. Key features include patient demographics, hospital stay metrics, medication metrics, service utilization, diagnostic information, and engineered features such as interaction terms, resource utilization ratios, and diagnostic density metrics. The model is intended for use by healthcare professionals to assess the risk of 30-day readmission for diabetic patients, supporting resource allocation, intervention planning, and care management decision-making. It is not intended for use with non-diabetic patient populations, for predicting readmissions beyond 30 days, or as the sole determinant for patient care decisions. The model was trained on a dataset from 1999 to 2008 from the UCI ML Repository, which includes over 100,000 hospital admissions and 50+ features. The training process involved data preprocessing steps such as missing value imputation, outlier handling, feature scaling, categorical encoding, and log transformation. Feature engineering included the creation of interaction terms, resource utilization ratios, medication usage metrics, time-based interaction features, and diagnostic density metrics. The model training process used a 70% training, 15% validation, and 15% test data split, with cross-validation for model selection, hyperparameter optimization via grid search, and early stopping to prevent overfitting. The model's performance is based on ROC-AUC. Known limitations include dependence on data quality and completeness, limited generalizability beyond the training data timeframe and healthcare systems, and the need for standardized input data. Potential biases may include demographic biases present in the training data, performance variation across hospital systems, influence from regional healthcare practices, and temporal biases due to historical data. Recommendations for use include regular model monitoring and retraining, careful validation in new deployment contexts, performance assessment across demographic groups, and integration with existing clinical workflows. Monitoring requirements include tracking prediction accuracy across patient groups, monitoring input data distribution shifts, assessing feature importance stability, and evaluating performance metrics over time. Maintenance includes quarterly performance reviews, annual retraining with updated data, regular bias assessments, and ongoing validation against current practices.

instruction: Create a scene of a dancer performing in a dimly lit theater with dramatic lighting.
description: Mochi 1 is a state-of-the-art video generation model with high-fidelity motion and strong prompt adherence in preliminary evaluation. It is an open-source model that significantly closes the gap between closed and open video generation systems. The model is released under a permissive Apache 2.0 license. Mochi 1 is based on a 10 billion parameter diffusion model built on the Asymmetric Diffusion Transformer (AsymmDiT) architecture. It is the largest video generative model ever openly released and features a simple, hackable design. The model is trained entirely from scratch and includes an efficient context parallel implementation for inference. Mochi 1 uses an AsymmVAE video compression model with an asymmetric encoder-decoder structure to compress videos to a 128x smaller size, using 8x8 spatial and 6x temporal compression to a 12-channel latent space. The AsymmDiT architecture processes user prompts and compressed video tokens by streamlining text processing and focusing neural network capacity on visual reasoning. It jointly attends to text and visual tokens with multi-modal self-attention and uses separate MLP layers for each modality. The visual stream has nearly four times as many parameters as the text stream, with non-square QKV and output projection layers to unify the modalities in self-attention. Mochi 1 encodes prompts using a single T5-XXL language model, rather than multiple pretrained language models. The model supports both multi-GPU and single-GPU operation, with single-GPU operation requiring approximately 60GB VRAM. ComfyUI can optimize the model to run on less than 20GB VRAM, but this implementation prioritizes flexibility over memory efficiency. It is recommended to use at least one H100 GPU when using the repository. Mochi 1 is a research preview and is a living and evolving checkpoint. It currently generates videos at 480p resolution and may exhibit minor warping and distortions in some edge cases with extreme motion. The model is optimized for photorealistic styles and does not perform well with animated content. It is expected that the community will fine-tune the model to suit various aesthetic preferences. Genmo video models are general text-to-video diffusion models that reflect the biases and preconceptions found in their training data. While steps have been taken to limit NSFW content, organizations should implement additional safety protocols and careful consideration before deploying these model weights in any commercial services or products.
