description: This is an xlm-roberta-large model that has been fine-tuned on multilingual (English, Danish) training data labeled with from the . Additionally, the model incorporates seven media codes, as follows: State and Local Government Administration (24), Weather and Natural Disaster (26), Fires (27), Sports and Recreation (29), Death Notices (30), Churches and Religion (31), and Other, Miscellaneous and Human Interest (99). The model was evaluated on a test set of 91,331 examples, achieving a weighted average F1-score of 0.68. The model requires a token for access due to gated access restrictions, and users must provide the token parameter when loading the model. Earlier versions of the Transformers package may require the use of the use_auth_token parameter instead. The model uses the sentencepiece tokenizer, and users running the model before transformers version 4.27 may need to install it manually. If a RuntimeError occurs when loading the model using the from_pretrained() method, adding ignore_mismatched_sizes=True should resolve the issue. instruction: Render a video of a futuristic city using a depth control to add dimension to the buildings.
description: This repository contains the code for a dilated controlnet module designed for the Wan2.1 model. The dilated controlnet features fewer basic blocks and includes a `stride` parameter. For the Wan14B model, the number of controlnet blocks is 6 and the stride is set to 4. The model supports inference tasks through command-line interface and Gradio web interface. It is intended for video generation based on text prompts and depth controlnet type. The model can be used with specified parameters such as controlnet weight, guidance start and end, number of inference steps, guidance scale, video resolution, number of frames, negative prompts, seed, output frame rate, and output path. The model is based on the Wan-AI/Wan2.1-T2V-14B-Diffusers base model and the TheDenk/wan2.1-t2v-14b-controlnet-depth-v1 controlnet model. The model is authored by Karachev Denis and is available on Github. For issues or professional support, contact welcomedenk@gmail.com.

instruction: Suggest some effective study techniques for memorizing historical facts.
description: BLOOMZ and mT0 are families of models designed to follow human instructions in dozens of languages in a zero-shot manner. These models are finetuned from BLOOM and mT5, which are pretrained multilingual language models, on a crosslingual task mixture called xP3. The resulting models demonstrate the ability to generalize across unseen tasks and languages. The BLOOMZ and mT0 models are available in various parameter sizes, including 300M, 580M, 1.2B, 3.7B, 13B, 560M, 1.1B, 1.7B, 3B, 7.1B, and 176B parameters. Some models are multitask finetuned on xP3mt for non-English prompting, while others are multitask finetuned on P3 for research purposes, though these are strictly inferior to the main models. The original pretrained checkpoints are not recommended for use. The models are intended for performing tasks expressed in natural language, such as translation, generating related search terms, writing stories, and explaining technical concepts in different languages. The performance of the models may depend on the clarity of the prompt, and it is recommended to clearly indicate when the input ends to avoid the model continuing the input. For BLOOMZ models, it is advised to provide as much context as possible, especially when specifying the language of the output. The models were finetuned for 1750 steps on 3.67 billion tokens using a layout of 1x pipeline parallel, 1x tensor parallel, and 1x data parallel, with training conducted in float16 precision. The training was carried out on hardware including AMD CPUs with 512GB memory per node and 64 A100 80GB GPUs with 8 GPUs per node, using NVLink 4 inter-gpu connections and 4 OmniPath links. Communication was handled via an NCCL-communications network with a fully dedicated subnet. Evaluation results are detailed in Table 7 of the associated paper, with zero-shot performance reported for the best prompt per dataset configuration.

instruction: Is it possible to get a depth estimation of a room from a single image?
description: Depth Anything is a deep convolutional neural network model designed for estimating depth at each point in an image. It is optimized for mobile deployment and is intended to be used on Qualcomm® devices. The model can be executed using different runtimes, including TFLITE, QNN_DLC, and ONNX. The model is part of the Qualcomm® AI Hub and can be deployed on Android using TensorFlow Lite or QNN. The model can be compiled for on-device deployment using PyTorch, and performance profiling and accuracy checks can be conducted on cloud-hosted devices. A demo is available to run the model on sample input data, and the model can also be executed on-device using the Qualcomm® AI Hub Workbench. description: test_resnet.r160_in1k is a very small test ResNet image classification model designed for testing and sanity checks. It is trained on the ImageNet-1k dataset by Ross Wightman. The model is intended for image classification tasks and can also be used as a feature backbone for tasks such as feature map extraction and image embeddings. The model has 0.5 million parameters, 0.1 GMACs, and 0.6 million activations. It processes images of size 160 x 160. The model is part of the PyTorch Image Models project. Performance evaluations indicate that the model achieves a top-1 accuracy of 41.578% and a top-5 accuracy of 67.956% on the ImageNet-1k dataset when using an image size of 160 x 160. instruction: Can you determine the type of music playing in this 16 kHz audio clip?
description: This model is the speech encoder component of the SeamlessM4Tv2-Large model, which performs strongly on cross- and multilingual sequence-level audio classification tasks. It is designed to extract features from audio inputs for classification purposes. The model supports audio classification tasks, particularly demonstrating performance on the SIB-Fleurs dataset. It is based on the architecture of the original SeamlessM4Tv2-Large model. The model can be used with the `AutoModel` or `AutoModelForAudioClassification` classes from the Transformers library. It is recommended to use the model with a GPU for optimal performance. The model expects a 16 kHz audio waveform as input and returns attention masks for batching. It has been evaluated on the SIB-Fleurs dataset, which contains 7 labels. Users are advised to cite the original SeamlessM4Tv2 paper when using this model.

instruction: Can you tell me what emotion is being expressed in this voice recording?
description: This model is a fine-tuned version for Speech Emotion Recognition (SER). It uses a Frozen Feature Extractor strategy to preserve the model's acoustic understanding while adapting to emotion detection. This approach ensures stable performance and prevents 'Catastrophic Forgetting,' achieving nearly 80% accuracy on the validation set. The 'Calm' and 'Neutral' classes have been merged to improve classification consistency, resulting in 7 distinct emotion classes. The model architecture is Wav2Vec2 with a frozen CNN feature extractor and a trainable sequence classification head. It is designed for English language and the task of SER. The model was fine-tuned from 'facebook/wav2vec2-base-960h.' The model classifies audio into one of the following emotions: Angry, Disgust, Fear, Happy, Neutral (includes Calm), Sad, and Surprise. The model was trained on a combined dataset of approximately 12,000 audio files. Performance metrics include an accuracy of 79.94%, an F1 score of 79.65%, and a validation loss of 0.644. Stable convergence was achieved using a Cosine Learning Rate Scheduler and Frozen Feature Extractor. The model was fine-tuned using a 'Robust' configuration on Google Colab T4 GPUs with the following parameters: Feature Extractor was frozen (Locked weights to preserve pre-trained acoustic features), 10 epochs, a learning rate of 3e-5, a Cosine Decay scheduler, a batch size of 4 (Physical) / 16 (Effective via Gradient Accumulation), the AdamW optimizer, and fp32 precision. The model requires audio input with a sampling rate of 16kHz; it will hallucinate if the input is not 16kHz, and resampling is required.

instruction: Can you create a short 720P video showing a peaceful sunrise over a mountain range?
description: Wan2.1 is a comprehensive and open suite of video foundation models designed to push the boundaries of video generation. It offers state-of-the-art performance, consistently outperforming existing open-source models and state-of-the-art commercial solutions across multiple benchmarks. The T2V-1.3B model requires only 8.19 GB VRAM, making it compatible with most consumer-grade GPUs. It can generate a 5-second 480P video on an RTX 4090 in about 4 minutes, with performance comparable to some closed-source models. Wan2.1 supports multiple tasks, including Text-to-Video, Image-to-Video, Video Editing, Text-to-Image, and Video-to-Audio, advancing the field of video generation. It is the first video model capable of generating both Chinese and English text, enhancing its practical applications. The suite includes a powerful video VAE called Wan-VAE, which delivers exceptional efficiency and performance, encoding and decoding 1080P videos of any length while preserving temporal information. The I2V-14B model is capable of generating 720P high-definition videos and has outperformed both closed-source and open-source alternatives in thousands of rounds of human evaluations, achieving state-of-the-art performance. Wan2.1 is built on the mainstream diffusion transformer paradigm, incorporating innovations such as a novel spatio-temporal VAE, scalable training strategies, large-scale data construction, and automated evaluation metrics. The model uses a Flow Matching framework within the diffusion transformer paradigm, with a T5 Encoder to encode multilingual text input and cross-attention in each transformer block to embed the text into the model structure. An MLP with a Linear layer and a SiLU layer processes the input time embeddings and predicts six modulation parameters individually, shared across all transformer blocks. The model was trained on a curated and deduplicated dataset of image and video data, processed through a four-step data cleaning pipeline focusing on fundamental dimensions, visual quality, and motion quality. The model was evaluated using a set of 1,035 internal prompts across 14 major dimensions and 26 sub-dimensions, with total scores calculated using weights derived from human preferences. The results demonstrate superior performance compared to both open-source and closed-source models. The model is licensed under the Apache 2.0 License, with no rights claimed over generated content. Users are responsible for ensuring their use of the models complies with the license and applicable laws, avoiding harmful or illegal content.

description: The H2OVL-Mississippi-2B is a high-performing, general-purpose vision-language model developed by H2O.ai to handle a wide range of multimodal tasks. This model, with 2 billion parameters, excels in tasks such as image captioning, visual question answering (VQA), and document understanding, while maintaining efficiency for real-world applications. The Mississippi-2B model builds on the strong foundations of H2O-Danube language models, now extended to integrate vision and language tasks. It competes with larger models across various benchmarks, offering a versatile and scalable solution for document AI, OCR, and multimodal reasoning. The model is optimized for vision-language tasks and achieves high performance across a wide range of applications, including document AI, OCR, and multimodal reasoning. It is trained on 17 million image-text pairs, ensuring broad coverage and strong task generalization. The model is capable of handling pure-text conversations, single-image analysis, and multi-image, multiround conversations. The model is designed to extract structured information from images, including simple details, nested information, lists and arrays, table data, and chart data, by using well-crafted prompts. instruction: I need to know if there's a handwritten signature on this image of a form. Can you check?
description: This model is a YOLOv8s-based object detection model fine-tuned to detect handwritten signatures in document images. The model was selected for its balance between precision, recall, and inference time. It was trained on a dataset consisting of 2,819 document images, with 1,980 images used for training (70%), 420 for validation (15%), and 419 for testing (15%). The dataset was processed to a resolution of 640x640 pixels in COCO JSON format. During the training process, multiple object detection models were evaluated, including YOLO, DETR, RT-DETR, Conditional-DETR, and YOLOS, with the YOLOv8s model ultimately chosen for hyperparameter tuning. Hyperparameter optimization was performed using Optuna, with parameters including dropout, learning rate, box loss weight, class loss weight, and optimizer type. After tuning, the YOLOv8s model achieved a precision of 94.74%, recall of 89.72%, mAP@50 of 94.50%, and mAP@50-95 of 67.35% on the test set. Inference times were 171.56 ms using ONNX Runtime (CPU) and 7.657 ms using TensorRT (GPU - T4). The model can be used via Python code, CLI, ONNX Runtime, or TensorRT. It is available in PyTorch, ONNX, and TensorRT formats. The model weights are licensed under AGPL-3.0, while the code, training, deployment, and data are licensed under Apache 2.0. The model was developed and trained in a Jupyter Notebook environment on an Ubuntu 22.04 system with Python 3.10.12, PyTorch 2.5.1+cu121, Ultralytics 8.3.58, and other supporting libraries. Training was conducted on a Google Cloud Platform n1-standard-8 instance with 8 vCPUs and an NVIDIA Tesla T4 GPU. A live demo is available for real-time inference, and the model can be deployed using the NVIDIA Triton Inference Server for production environments.

instruction: Can you find a radiology image that matches the description of a lung X-ray showing signs of pneumonia?
description: BiomedCLIP-PubMedBERT_256-vit_base_patch16_224 is a biomedical vision-language foundation model pretrained on a dataset of 15 million figure-caption pairs extracted from biomedical research articles in PubMed Central, using contrastive learning. It uses PubMedBERT as the text encoder and Vision Transformer as the image encoder, with domain-specific adaptations. The model can perform various vision-language processing tasks such as cross-modal retrieval, image classification, and visual question answering. BiomedCLIP establishes new state of the art in a wide range of standard datasets and substantially outperforms prior vision-language processing approaches. The model is based on the PMC-15M dataset, a large-scale parallel image-text dataset generated from the BiomedCLIP Data Pipeline, which automatically downloads and processes articles from the PubMed Central Open Access dataset. The dataset includes a diverse range of biomedical image types such as microscopy, radiography, and histology. The model is intended for future research on visual-language processing and for reproducibility of the experimental results reported in the reference paper. Its primary intended use is to support AI researchers building on this work, particularly in the radiology domain. Any deployed use case of the model, whether commercial or otherwise, is currently out of scope. The model was developed using English corpora and is considered English-only.

instruction: Please convert this sentence into a natural-sounding audio clip for a presentation.
description: Kokoro is a text-to-speech (TTS) model with 82 million parameters, designed to convert text input into audio output. It is intended for use in applications requiring high-quality speech synthesis and is noted for its resilience to quantization, allowing efficient performance at a reduced model size. The model supports multiple quantization formats, including fp32, fp16, q8, q4, and mixed precision, which vary in file size and computational efficiency. Kokoro provides a range of available voices, including both male and female voices from American and British nationalities, such as 'af_bella', 'am_liam', 'bf_lily', and 'bm_lewis'. The model can be used via JavaScript or Python, with specific libraries and code examples provided for implementation. The model is described as being capable of generating speech from text, as demonstrated with the example sentence 'Life is like a box of chocolates. You never know what you're gonna get.'

instruction: Can you describe what is happening in this image?
description: This is an ONNX version of the Phi-4 multimodal model that is quantized to int4 precision to accelerate inference with ONNX Runtime. The model is developed by Microsoft and is a conversion of the Phi4 multimodal model for ONNX Runtime inference. It is licensed under the MIT license. The base model, Phi-4-multimodal-instruct, is a lightweight open multimodal foundation model that leverages the language, vision, and speech research and datasets used for Phi-3.5 and 4.0 models. The model processes text, image, and audio inputs, generating text outputs, and comes with 128K token context length. The model underwent an enhancement process, incorporating both supervised fine-tuning and direct preference optimization to support precise instruction adherence and safety measures. The model is only an optimization of the base model, and any risk associated with the model is the responsibility of the user. There may be a slight difference in output from the base model with the optimizations applied.

instruction: What are the most notable visual features in this video?
description: CogVLM2-Caption is a video captioning model designed to generate textual descriptions of video data, which are used as training data for the CogVideoX model. Most video data does not come with corresponding descriptive text, so it is necessary to convert the video data into textual descriptions to provide the essential training data for text-to-video models. The model is based on the CogVLM2 architecture and incorporates features from the Meta Llama 3. It is intended to support tasks involving video captioning and the generation of detailed descriptions of video content. The model uses a combination of video input processing and natural language generation to produce captions. The model is evaluated for its ability to accurately describe video content and generate suitable training data for downstream text-to-video models. The model is released under the CogVLM2 license, and for models built with Meta Llama 3, additional licensing requirements may apply. Users are encouraged to cite the associated research paper if they find the model useful.

instruction: Tôi đang tìm hiểu về các quy định pháp luật liên quan đến đất đai, hãy cung cấp tài liệu phù hợp.
description: DEk21_hcmute_embedding is a Vietnamese text embedding model focused on RAG and production efficiency. The model was trained on an in-house dataset consisting of approximately 100,000 examples of legal questions and their related contexts. It is trained with a Matryoshka loss, allowing embeddings to be truncated with minimal performance loss. This ensures that smaller embeddings are faster to compare, making the model efficient for real-world production use. The model type is a Sentence Transformer, with a maximum sequence length of 256 tokens and output dimensionality of 768 dimensions. It uses cosine similarity as the similarity function and is designed for the Vietnamese language. The model is licensed under the Apache-2.0 license. The model architecture consists of a Transformer with a RobertaModel and a Pooling layer that uses mean token pooling. The model is intended for tasks involving Vietnamese legal text retrieval and is evaluated on information retrieval metrics such as NDCG and MRR. Evaluation results show that the model achieves an NDCG@3 score of 0.752173, an NDCG@5 score of 0.769259, an NDCG@10 score of 0.785101, an MRR@3 score of 0.72474, an MRR@5 score of 0.734427, and an MRR@10 score of 0.741076. The model is compared to other Vietnamese embedding models and achieves competitive performance. The model is intended for use with the Sentence Transformers library and requires Vietnamese text to be tokenized using a Vietnamese tokenizer before encoding. The model is available on Hugging Face and can be loaded and used for inference with the Sentence Transformers library. The model is designed for use in production environments and is optimized for efficiency. The model is not intended for use with non-Vietnamese text or for tasks outside of information retrieval.

instruction: Can you provide a depth map for an underwater photo to show the distance of objects from the camera?
description: DepthPro is a foundation model for zero-shot metric monocular depth estimation, designed to generate high-resolution depth maps with remarkable sharpness and fine-grained details. It employs a multi-scale Vision Transformer (ViT)-based architecture, where images are downsampled, divided into patches, and processed using a shared Dinov2 encoder. The extracted patch-level features are merged, upsampled, and refined using a DPT-like fusion stage, enabling precise depth estimation. The model synthesizes high-resolution depth maps with unparalleled sharpness and high-frequency details. The predictions are metric, with absolute scale, without relying on the availability of metadata such as camera intrinsics. The model is fast, producing a 2.25-megapixel depth map in 0.3 seconds on a standard GPU. These characteristics are enabled by an efficient multi-scale vision transformer for dense prediction, a training protocol that combines real and synthetic datasets to achieve high metric accuracy alongside fine boundary tracing, dedicated evaluation metrics for boundary accuracy in estimated depth maps, and state-of-the-art focal length estimation from a single image. The model was developed by Aleksei Bochkovskii, Amaël Delaunoy, Hugo Germain, Marcel Santos, Yichao Zhou, Stephan R. Richter, and Vladlen Koltun. It is licensed under the Apple-ASCL license. The model uses a DepthProEncoder for encoding the input image and a FeatureFusionStage for fusing the output features from the encoder. The DepthProEncoder further uses two encoders: a patch_encoder and an image_encoder. The input image is scaled with multiple ratios, split into smaller patches of size patch_size with overlapping areas, and processed by the patch_encoder. The input image is also rescaled to patch_size and processed by the image_encoder. Both encoders can be configured via patch_model_config and image_model_config, both of which are separate Dinov2Model by default. Outputs from both encoders (last_hidden_state) and selected intermediate states (hidden_states) from the patch_encoder are fused by a DPT-based FeatureFusionStage for depth estimation. The network is supplemented with a focal length estimation head. A small convolutional head ingests frozen features from the depth estimation network and task-specific features from a separate ViT image encoder to predict the horizontal angular field-of-view. Images are preprocessed by being rescaled by 1/225, normalized with mean=[0.5, 0.5, 0.5] and std=[0.5, 0.5, 0.5], and resized to 1536x1536 pixels.

instruction: Show me how to make a stable brick model of a sofa.
description: BrickGPT is the first approach for generating physically stable toy brick models from text prompts. It is an autoregressive model that operates in English and is licensed under the MIT license. The model was fine-tuned using LoRA applied to the `q_proj` and `v_proj` matrices, with AdamW optimization and a learning rate that followed a cosine decay with warmup. Training was conducted using bf16 mixed precision for 3 epochs with a global batch size of 64. The maximum learning rate was 0.002, with 100 warmup steps. The LoRA rank was 32, with a LoRA alpha of 16 and a LoRA dropout of 0.05. The model was trained on a dataset of 47k toy brick structures and was evaluated on 21 object categories: basket, bed, bench, birdhouse, bookshelf, bottle, bowl, bus, camera, car, chair, guitar, jar, mug, piano, pot, sofa, table, tower, train, and vessel. The model is limited to creating structures made of 1-unit-tall cuboid bricks on a 20x20x20 grid, and its performance may be limited for prompts outside these categories. The model was developed with support from the Packard Foundation, Cisco Research Grant, Amazon Faculty Award, the Manufacturing Futures Institute at Carnegie Mellon University through a grant from the Richard King Mellon Foundation, and the Microsoft Research PhD Fellowship. Training was conducted using 8x NVIDIA RTX A6000 (48 GB) GPUs for 0.5 hours. The model is associated with the PEFT 0.15.0 framework.

instruction: What are the distinct regions in this picture, and how are they categorized?
description: EoMT (Encoder-only Mask Transformer) is a Vision Transformer (ViT) architecture designed for high-quality and efficient image segmentation. It was introduced in the CVPR 2025 highlight paper titled 'Your ViT is Secretly an Image Segmentation Model' by Tommie Kerssies, Niccolò Cavagnero, Alexander Hermans, Narges Norouzi, Giuseppe Averta, Bastian Leibe, Gijs Dubbelman, and Daan de Geus. The key insight is that, given sufficient scale and pretraining, a plain ViT along with additional few parameters can perform segmentation without the need for task-specific decoders or pixel fusion modules. The same model backbone supports semantic, instance, and panoptic segmentation with different post-processing. The original implementation is available in a separate repository, and the HuggingFace model page is also available.

instruction: Can you help me turn this Korean message into English for a client?
description: GemmaX2-28-2B-v0.1 is an LLM-based translation model. It has been fine-tuned on GemmaX2-28-2B-Pretrain, which is a language model developed through continual pretraining of Gemma2-2B using a mix of 56 billion tokens from both monolingual and parallel data across 28 different languages. GemmaX2-28-2B-Pretrain is obtained by continually pretraining Gemma2-2B on a large amount of monolingual and parallel data. Subsequently, GemmaX2-28-2B-v0.1 is derived through supervised fine-tuning on a small set of high-quality translation instruction data. The model supports translation in the following languages: Arabic, Bengali, Czech, German, English, Spanish, Persian, French, Hebrew, Hindi, Indonesian, Italian, Japanese, Khmer, Korean, Lao, Malay, Burmese, Dutch, Polish, Portuguese, Russian, Thai, Tagalog, Turkish, Urdu, Vietnamese, and Chinese. GemmaX2-28-2B-v0.1 only supports the 28 languages listed above and does not guarantee strong translation performance for other languages. We will continue to enhance the translation performance of GemmaX2-28-2B, and future models will be released in due course.

instruction: Create a 720P video of a quiet countryside morning with birds flying and the sun rising.
description: Wan2.1 is a comprehensive and open suite of video foundation models designed to push the boundaries of video generation. It offers state-of-the-art performance, consistently outperforming existing open-source models and state-of-the-art commercial solutions across multiple benchmarks. The T2V-1.3B model requires only 8.19 GB VRAM, making it compatible with most consumer-grade GPUs. It can generate a 5-second 480P video on an RTX 4090 in about 4 minutes, with performance comparable to some closed-source models. Wan2.1 supports multiple tasks, including Text-to-Video, Image-to-Video, Video Editing, Text-to-Image, and Video-to-Audio, advancing the field of video generation. It is the first video model capable of generating both Chinese and English text, enhancing its practical applications. The suite includes a powerful video VAE called Wan-VAE, which delivers exceptional efficiency and performance, encoding and decoding 1080P videos of any length while preserving temporal information. The I2V-14B model is capable of generating 720P high-definition videos and has outperformed both closed-source and open-source alternatives in thousands of rounds of human evaluations, achieving state-of-the-art performance. Wan2.1 is built on the mainstream diffusion transformer paradigm, incorporating innovations such as a novel spatio-temporal VAE, scalable training strategies, large-scale data construction, and automated evaluation metrics. The model uses a Flow Matching framework within the diffusion transformer paradigm, with a T5 Encoder to encode multilingual text input and cross-attention in each transformer block to embed the text into the model structure. An MLP with a Linear layer and a SiLU layer processes the input time embeddings and predicts six modulation parameters individually, shared across all transformer blocks. The model was trained on a curated and deduplicated dataset of image and video data, processed through a four-step data cleaning pipeline focusing on fundamental dimensions, visual quality, and motion quality. The model was evaluated using a set of 1,035 internal prompts across 14 major dimensions and 26 sub-dimensions, with total scores calculated using weights derived from human preferences. The results demonstrate superior performance compared to both open-source and closed-source models. The model is licensed under the Apache 2.0 License, with no rights claimed over generated content. Users are responsible for ensuring their use of the models complies with the license and applicable laws, avoiding harmful or illegal content.

instruction: What activity is the individual doing in the footage captured on my phone?
description: ResNet Mixed Convolutions is a network that combines 2D and 3D convolutions for video understanding. It is designed for sports and human action recognition in videos and is optimized for mobile deployment. The model is based on the ResNet-Mixed-Convolution architecture and is trained on the Kinectics-400 dataset. It accepts an input resolution of 112x112 and has 11.7 million parameters. The model size is 44.6 MB in float precision and 11.5 MB in w8a16 precision. The model is intended to run on Qualcomm® devices and supports various target runtimes including TFLITE, QNN_DLC, and ONNX. Performance metrics include inference times and memory usage across multiple Qualcomm® chipsets and devices such as the QCS8275, QCS8450, QCS8550, QCS9075, SA7255P, SA8255, SA8295P, SA8650, SA8775P, and Snapdragon® 8 Gen 3 Mobile, 8 Elite Gen 5 Mobile, and X Elite. The model is primarily executed on the NPU, with some CPU-based execution for w8a16 precision on certain devices. The model can be installed via pip and includes a demo for end-to-end execution, as well as scripts for compiling, profiling, and verifying model accuracy on cloud-hosted Qualcomm® devices. Deployment options include TensorFlow Lite and QNN for Android applications. The model is available on the Qualcomm® AI Hub for further exploration and performance analysis. The original implementation of ResNet-Mixed-Convolution is licensed separately.

instruction: I’m trying to find the best match for a specific question among several options—can you assist with that?
description: The gte-reranker-modernbert-base is a text reranker model developed by Tongyi Lab, Alibaba Group. It is part of the gte-modernbert series, which is built upon the latest modernBERT pre-trained encoder-only foundation models. The model is designed for text reranking tasks and is primarily used in English. It has a model size of 149 million parameters and supports a maximum input length of 8192 tokens. The gte-modernbert series models demonstrate competitive performance in several text embedding and text retrieval evaluation tasks when compared to similar-scale models from the current open-source community. This includes assessments such as MTEB, LoCO, and COIR evaluations. The gte-reranker-modernbert-base model achieves an MTEB score of 64.38, a BEIR score of 55.33, a LoCo score of 90.68, and a COIR score of 79.31. The model is designed to be used with libraries such as transformers, sentence-transformers, and transformers.js. It can also be deployed using Docker for CPU or GPU environments. The model follows the training scheme of the previous series, with the only difference being that the pre-training language model base has been updated. The model is suitable for reranking tasks where the goal is to reorder a set of candidate documents based on their relevance to a given query. The model outputs scores that can be used to determine the relevance of each document to the query. The model is evaluated on a variety of benchmarks, including MTEB, LoCO, COIR, and BEIR, and its performance is compared to other models of similar size. The model is available for use and deployment, and it is recommended to use Flash Attention 2 if the GPU supports it. The model is part of a series of models that are designed to provide high-quality text representations and reranking capabilities for multilingual text retrieval tasks.

instruction: What are the environmental impacts associated with training the Llama 4 Scout model?
description: The Llama 4 collection of models are natively multimodal AI models that enable text and multimodal experiences. These models leverage a mixture-of-experts architecture to offer industry-leading performance in text and image understanding. These Llama 4 models mark the beginning of a new era for the Llama ecosystem. We are launching two efficient models in the Llama 4 series, Llama 4 Scout, a 17 billion parameter model with 16 experts, and Llama 4 Maverick, a 17 billion parameter model with 128 experts. The Llama 4 models are auto-regressive language models that use a mixture-of-experts (MoE) architecture and incorporate early fusion for native multimodality. Llama 4 Scout was pretrained on ~40 trillion tokens and Llama 4 Maverick was pretrained on ~22 trillion tokens of multimodal data from a mix of publicly available, licensed data and information from Meta’s products and services. This includes publicly shared posts from Instagram and Facebook and people’s interactions with Meta AI. The pretraining data has a cutoff of August 2024. Llama 4 Scout has a context length of 10 million tokens and Llama 4 Maverick has a context length of 1 million tokens. Llama 4 Scout has a total of 109 billion parameters and Llama 4 Maverick has a total of 400 billion parameters. Llama 4 is intended for commercial and research use in multiple languages. Instruction tuned models are intended for assistant-like chat and visual reasoning tasks, whereas pretrained models can be adapted for natural language generation. For vision, Llama 4 models are also optimized for visual recognition, image reasoning, captioning, and answering general questions about an image. The Llama 4 model collection also supports the ability to leverage the outputs of its models to improve other models including synthetic data generation and distillation. Llama 4 has been trained on a broader collection of languages than the 12 supported languages (pre-training includes ). Developers may fine-tune Llama 4 models for languages beyond the 12 supported languages provided they comply with the Llama 4 Community License and the Acceptable Use Policy. Developers are responsible for ensuring that their use of Llama 4 in additional languages is done in a safe and responsible manner. Llama 4 has been tested for image understanding up to 5 input images. If leveraging additional image understanding capabilities beyond this, Developers are responsible for ensuring that their deployments are mitigated for risks and should perform additional testing and tuning tailored to their specific applications. Llama 4 was developed following the best practices outlined in our . The Llama 4 models are released under a custom commercial license, the Llama 4 Community License Agreement. The Llama 4 models are static models trained on an offline dataset. Future versions of the tuned models may be released as we improve model behavior with community feedback. Llama 4 Scout and Llama 4 Maverick were trained using custom training libraries, Meta's custom built GPU clusters, and production infrastructure. Model pre-training utilized a cumulative of 7.38 million GPU hours of computation on H100-80GB (TDP of 700W) type hardware. Training time is the total GPU time required for training each model and power consumption is the peak power capacity per GPU device used, adjusted for power usage efficiency. Estimated total location-based greenhouse gas emissions were 1,999 tons CO2eq for training. Since 2020, Meta has maintained net zero greenhouse gas emissions in its global operations and matched 100% of its electricity use with clean and renewable energy; therefore, the total market-based greenhouse gas emissions for training were 0 tons CO2eq. Llama 4 Scout has training time of 5 million GPU hours and training location-based greenhouse gas emissions of 1,354 tons CO2eq. Llama 4 Maverick has training time of 2.38 million GPU hours and training location-based greenhouse gas emissions of 645 tons CO2eq. The methodology used to determine training energy use and greenhouse gas emissions can be found . Since Meta is openly releasing these models, the training energy use and greenhouse gas emissions will not be incurred by others. Llama 4 Scout and Llama 4 Maverick are available for use with the transformers library version 4.51.0 or higher. The Llama 4 models are designed to be deployed as part of an overall AI system with additional guardrails as required. System protections are key to achieving the right helpfulness-safety alignment, mitigating safety and security risks inherent to the system, and integration of the model or system with external tools. We provide the community with system level tools like Llama Guard, Prompt Guard and Code Shield that developers should deploy with Llama models or other LLMs. All of our demos contain these safeguards by default so developers can benefit from system-level safety out-of-the-box. Llama 4 is a new technology, and like any new technology, there are risks associated with its use. Testing conducted to date has not covered, nor could it cover, all scenarios. For these reasons, as with all LLMs, Llama 4’s potential outputs cannot be predicted in advance, and the model may in some instances produce inaccurate or other objectionable responses to user prompts. Therefore, before deploying any applications of Llama 4 models, developers should perform safety testing and tuning tailored to their specific applications of the model. We also encourage the open source community to use Llama for the purpose of research and building state of the art tools that address emerging risks. Please refer to available resources including our Developer Use Guide: AI Protections, solutions, and other to learn more.

instruction: Produce a textured mesh of a fantasy weapon based on the detailed text I provided.
description: TriplaneTurbo is a model designed for instant text-to-mesh generation without the need for 3D data. It adapts Stable Diffusion through a method called Progressive Rendering Distillation. The model is capable of generating textured meshes in approximately one second and demonstrates strong comprehension of complex text prompts to ensure accurate generation. It is trained without relying on any 3D datasets, making it more resource-efficient and adaptable. The model supports fast inference and is optimized for text comprehension. Evaluation metrics include CLIP Similarity Score and CLIP Recall@1. The model can be trained using text prompts from 3DTopia or DALLE+Midjourney datasets. Training options include single or multi-GPU configurations, with specific memory requirements depending on the configuration used. The model is based on several prior works, including codebases for 3D generation via Score Distillation, multi-view teachers for supervision, and methods for mesh rasterization and volume rendering. The model is accepted at CVPR 2025 and its paper is available on Arxiv.

instruction: I want to build a search system that works across 12 languages—how should I process the text first?
description: Granite-Embedding-107M-Multilingual is a 107 million parameter dense biencoder embedding model from the Granite Embeddings suite, designed to generate high-quality text embeddings. It produces embedding vectors of size 384 and is trained using a combination of open source relevance-pair datasets with permissive, enterprise-friendly licenses and IBM-collected and generated datasets. The model is developed using contrastive finetuning, knowledge distillation, and model merging for improved performance. It supports 12 languages: English, German, Spanish, French, Japanese, Portuguese, Arabic, Czech, Italian, Korean, Dutch, and Chinese, and users may finetune it for additional languages. The model is intended to produce fixed-length vector representations for a given text, which can be used for text similarity, retrieval, and search applications. It is compatible with the SentenceTransformer library and the Huggingface Transformers library, and uses CLS pooling for embedding generation. The model is based on an encoder-only XLM-RoBERTa-like transformer architecture, trained internally at IBM Research. It has an embedding size of 384, 6 layers, 12 attention heads, an intermediate size of 1536, a GeLU activation function, a vocabulary size of 250002, a maximum sequence length of 512, and 107 million parameters. The training data includes unsupervised title-body paired data from the web, publicly available paired data with permissive licenses, IBM-internal paired data targeting specific technical domains, and IBM-generated synthetic data. The training data consists of 52,823,484 pairs from Multilingual MC4, 12,369,322 pairs from Multilingual Webhose, 20,745,403 pairs from English Wikipedia, 2,911,090 pairs from Multilingual Wikimedia, 10,120,398 pairs from Miracl Corpus, 304,525 pairs from Stack Exchange duplicate questions (titles), 250,519 pairs from Stack Exchange duplicate questions (bodies), 187,195 pairs from machine translations of Stack Exchange duplicate questions (titles), 4,067,139 pairs from Stack Exchange (Title, Answer) pairs, 23,978,013 pairs from Stack Exchange (Title, Body) pairs, 1,827,15 pairs from machine translations of Stack Exchange (Title+Body, Answer) pairs, 582,261 pairs from SearchQA, 41,769,185 pairs from S2ORC, 77,427,422 pairs from WikiAnswers duplicate question pairs, 614,664 pairs from CCNews, 226,711 pairs from XSum, 102,225 pairs from SimpleWiki, 28,376,115 pairs from machine translated cross-lingual parallel corpora, 684,100 pairs from SPECTER citation triplets, 4,104,600 pairs from machine translations of SPECTER citation triplets, 100,231 pairs from Natural Questions, 87,599 pairs from SQuAD2.0, 85,000 pairs from HotpotQA, 109,810 pairs from Fever, 20,000,000 pairs from PubMed, 81,409 pairs from Multilingual Miracl Triples, 48,715 pairs from Multilingual MrTydi Triples, 4,037 pairs from Sadeeem Question Answering, 4,635,922 pairs from DBPedia Title-Body Pairs, 1,879,093 pairs from synthetic English Query-Wikipedia Passage, 9,888 pairs from synthetic English Fact Verification, 300,266 pairs from synthetic Multilingual Query-Wikipedia Passage, 37,489 pairs from synthetic Multilingual News Summaries, 40,290 pairs from IBM Internal Triples, and 1,524,586 pairs from IBM Internal Title-Body Pairs. The model is trained using IBM's Cognitive Compute Cluster, which is equipped with NVIDIA A100 80GB GPUs. The data used to train the base language model was filtered to remove text containing hate, abuse, and profanity. The model is finetuned on 12 languages and has a context length of 512 tokens, with longer texts being truncated to this size. The average performance of the model on Multilingual Miracl (across 18 languages), Mintaka Retrieval (across 8 languages), and MTEB Retrieval for English (across 15 tasks), German (across 4 tasks), Spanish (across 2 tasks), French (across 5 tasks), Japanese (across 2 tasks), Arabic (1 task), Korean (1 task), and Chinese (across 8 tasks) is reported as follows: 55.9 on Miracl, 22.6 on Mintaka Retrieval, 45.3 on MTEB English, 70.3 on MTEB German, 48.7 on MTEB Spanish, 51.1 on MTEB French, 59.0 on MTEB Japanese, 63.2 on MTEB Arabic, 70.5 on MTEB Korean, and 40.8 on MTEB Chinese. The model is twice as fast as other models with similar embedding dimensions. The model is not trained on the MS-MARCO retrieval dataset due to its non-commercial license.

instruction: Can you transform this text into a vector that preserves its semantic content for further processing?
description: This is a model that maps sentences and paragraphs to a 384-dimensional dense vector space and can be used for tasks like clustering or semantic search. The model is intended to be used as a sentence and short paragraph encoder. Given an input text, it outputs a vector which captures the semantic information. The sentence vector may be used for information retrieval, clustering, or sentence similarity tasks. Input text longer than 256 word pieces is truncated by default. The model was developed using a self-supervised contrastive learning objective, where the model is trained to predict which sentence from a set of randomly sampled sentences was actually paired with a given sentence in the dataset. The model was fine-tuned on a dataset containing over 1 billion sentence pairs. The training was conducted on a TPU v3-8 for 100,000 steps with a batch size of 1024 (128 per TPU core), a learning rate of 2e-5, and a sequence length limited to 128 tokens. The AdamW optimizer was used with a learning rate warm-up of 500 steps. The training data was a concatenation of multiple datasets, including citation pairs (abstracts and titles), duplicate question pairs, (question, answer) pairs, (title, body) pairs, (title+body, answer) pairs, (title, answer) pairs, triplets, image captions, and citation triplets. The total number of training tuples across all datasets is 1,170,060,424. The model was developed as part of a project during an event organized by Hugging Face, and the development was supported by efficient hardware infrastructure, including 7 TPUs v3-8, and assistance from members of Google's Flax, JAX, and Cloud teams.

instruction: Can I generate a video from scratch using only text descriptions?
description: VACE is an all-in-one model designed for video creation and editing. It supports various tasks, including reference-to-video generation (R2V), video-to-video editing (V2V), and masked video-to-video editing (MV2V), allowing users to compose these tasks freely. This functionality enables users to explore diverse possibilities and streamlines their workflows effectively, offering a range of capabilities, such as Move-Anything, Swap-Anything, Reference-Anything, Expand-Anything, Animate-Anything, and more. VACE-Wan2.1-1.3B-Preview and VACE-LTX-Video-0.9 are available models, with the former supporting a video size of approximately 81 x 480 x 832 and the latter supporting approximately 97 x 512 x 768. The input supports any resolution, but to achieve optimal results, the video size should fall within a specific range. All models inherit the license of the original model. Users can input text prompts and optional video, mask, and image for video generation or editing. The codebase was tested with Python 3.10.13, CUDA version 12.4, and PyTorch version 2.5.1 or higher. For model inference, users can run end-to-end pipelines or separate preprocessing and inference steps. Preprocessing tools are available for more flexible control over the input, and users can customize preprocessors by implementing and registering them. For model inference, users can use single GPU or multi GPU acceleration for Wan2.1, or run LTX inference. Gradio demos are also available for preprocessors and model inference. For LTX-Video and English language Wan2.1 users, prompt extension is required to unlock the full model performance. The model is described in the paper titled 'VACE: All-in-One Video Creation and Editing' by Jiang, Zeyinzi, Han, Zhen, Mao, Chaojie, Zhang, Jingfeng, Pan, Yulin, and Liu, Yu, published as an arXiv preprint in 2025.

instruction: Can you evaluate if the sentence 'The actor received an award' implies that 'The actor was recognized for their performance'?
description: DeBERTa-v3-base-mnli-fever-anli is a model trained on the MultiNLI, Fever-NLI, and Adversarial-NLI (ANLI) datasets, which together contain 763,913 NLI hypothesis-premise pairs. The base model outperforms almost all large models on the given tasks. The v3 variant of DeBERTa substantially outperforms previous versions by including a different pre-training objective, as detailed in annex 11 of the original paper. The model is designed for natural language inference (NLI) tasks and supports zero-shot classification. It was trained using the Hugging Face trainer with the following hyperparameters: 3 training epochs, a learning rate of 2e-05, a per-device train and evaluation batch size of 32, a warmup ratio of 0.1, a weight decay of 0.06, and mixed precision training (fp16). The model was evaluated using the test sets for MultiNLI and ANLI and the dev set for Fever-NLI, with accuracy as the metric. The model achieved an accuracy of 0.903 on mnli-m, 0.903 on mnli-mm, 0.777 on fever-nli, 0.579 on anli-all, and 0.495 on anli-r3. The model is intended for use in NLI and zero-shot classification tasks. It is recommended to use Transformers version 4.13 or higher to avoid issues with the tokenizer, and to install sentencepiece to prevent tokenizer errors. The model is ranked 2nd among all tested models for the microsoft/deberta-v3-base architecture as of 09/01/2023, achieving an average score of 79.69 compared to 79.04 by the original microsoft/deberta-v3-base. Potential biases should be considered, and users are advised to consult the original DeBERTa paper and literature on different NLI datasets for further information. If using this model, please cite the relevant preprint by Laurer et al. (2022).

instruction: Could you identify the mood of this translated marketing slogan in Japanese?
description: This model is a fine-tuned version designed for assessing text sentiment across multiple languages. It is based on the E5 small model architecture and supports sentiment assessment for text in various languages. The intended uses include assessing the sentiment of multilingual text, filtering multilingual content, and conducting comparative analysis of corpus text sentiment across different languages. The model was trained on parallel sentences in English and various other languages, including semantic similarity scores calculated using LaBSE, additional sentiment metrics, and data from sources such as JW300, Europarl, TED Talks, OPUS-100, Tatoeba, Global Voices, and News Commentary. Performance may vary for languages not well-represented in the training data, and the model should not be used as the sole criterion for sentiment assessment. The model was evaluated on a diverse set of multilingual text samples, including 10 English text samples of varying sentiment translated into Arabic, Chinese, French, Russian, and Spanish. The model demonstrated consistent sentiment assessment across different languages for the same text. Training was conducted using a learning rate of 5e-05, a train batch size of 128, an evaluation batch size of 8, a seed of 42, the adamw_torch optimizer with betas (0.9, 0.999) and epsilon 1e-08, a linear learning rate scheduler, and 3 training epochs. Training loss decreased from 0.1946 to 0.1121 over the three epochs, with corresponding validation losses of 0.1647, 0.1528, and 0.1455. The model was developed using Transformers 4.46.3, PyTorch 2.5.1+cu124, Datasets 3.1.0, and Tokenizers 0.20.3.

instruction: Show me images related to the concept of a quiet forest in the morning.
description: SigLIP 2 Giant extends the pretraining objective with prior, independently developed techniques into a unified recipe, for improved semantic understanding, localization, and dense features. The model can be used for tasks like zero-shot image classification and image-text retrieval, or as a vision encoder for VLMs and other vision tasks. SigLIP 2 adds training objectives such as decoder loss, global-local and masked prediction loss, and aspect ratio and resolution adaptibility. The model is pre-trained on the WebLI dataset and was trained on up to 2048 TPU-v5e chips. Evaluation results are presented in the associated paper. The model is described in the research titled 'SigLIP 2: Multilingual Vision-Language Encoders with Improved Semantic Understanding, Localization, and Dense Features' by Michael Tschannen, Alexey Gritsenko, Xiao Wang, Muhammad Ferjad Naeem, Ibrahim Alabdulmohsin, Nikhil Parthasarathy, Talfan Evans, Lucas Beyer, Ye Xia, Basil Mustafa, Olivier Hénaff, Jeremiah Harmsen, Andreas Steiner, and Xiaohua Zhai, available on arXiv with the identifier 2502.14786.

instruction: What is the main message of this educational video about climate change?
description: VideoLLaMA 3 is a state-of-the-art series of multimodal foundation models designed to excel in both image and video understanding tasks. These models leverage advanced architectures to process and interpret visual content across various contexts. They are specifically designed to address complex multimodal challenges, such as integrating textual and visual information, extracting insights from sequential video data, and performing high-level reasoning over both dynamic and static visual scenes. The model series includes variants such as VideoLLaMA3-7B, VideoLLaMA3-2B, VideoLLaMA3-7B-Image, and VideoLLaMA3-2B-Image, each based on different base models including Qwen2.5-7B and Qwen2.5-1.5B. A tuned vision encoder based on siglip-so400m-patch14-384 is also available for the VideoLLaMA3-7B model to support wider application. The models are intended for tasks involving video and image understanding, including video description and multimodal reasoning. The model is available for use with specific code and dependencies, including the use of AutoModelForCausalLM and AutoProcessor from the transformers library. The model supports video input with parameters such as video path, frames per second, and maximum number of frames. The model is designed to generate text-based responses to video content. The model has been developed by a team of researchers from DAMO-NLP-SG and is associated with academic publications available on arXiv. Users are encouraged to cite the relevant papers if they use the model for research or applications.

instruction: Can I segment a video and export it in a format that supports transparency for use in editing software?
description: BEN2 (Background Erase Network) introduces a novel approach to foreground segmentation through its innovative Confidence Guided Matting (CGM) pipeline. The architecture employs a refiner network that targets and processes pixels where the base model exhibits lower confidence levels, resulting in more precise and reliable matting results. This model is built on BEN. BEN2 was trained on the DIS5k and a 22K proprietary segmentation dataset. The enhanced model delivers superior performance in hair matting, 4K processing, object segmentation, and edge refinement. The Base model is open source. BEN2 supports tasks such as foreground segmentation, hair matting, 4K processing, object segmentation, and edge refinement. The model includes a postprocessing step called refine_foreground, which increases inference time but can improve matting edges. The default value for this step is False. For batch image processing, it is recommended that the batch size not exceed 3 for consumer GPUs as there are minimal inference gains due to the model's custom batch processing for the MVANet decoding steps. BEN2 also supports video segmentation, with options to control output format, frame rate, batch size, and background color when not using the webm format. RMBG 2.0 did not preserve the DIS 5k validation dataset.

instruction: Create a small tabular dataset that could be used to test machine learning algorithms on diabetes data.
description: Tabby is a post-training architecture modification for Transformer-based large language models, enabling their use for tabular dataset synthesis. This specific demo checkpoint is based on and fine-tuned on the UCI Diabetes dataset, using a novel Plain training method as an example of Tabby’s tabular synthesis capabilities. Tabby enhances transformer-based LLMs by incorporating Mixture of Experts (MoE) layers, allowing for better modeling of structured data. The model is developed by the University of Wisconsin-Madison and shared by Sonia Cromp et al. It is an MoE-enhanced GPT-2-based causal language model for tabular data, licensed under the MIT license. The model can be used for high-fidelity synthesis of diabetes patients based on the UCI Diabetes dataset, data augmentation for training machine learning models on the same dataset, and comparison with other tabular synthesis approaches. It can also be used for further fine-tuning on other structured datasets, such as financial records, medical records, or survey data, and for generating synthetic tabular data for privacy-preserving machine learning. This Tabby checkpoint inherits biases from the GPT-2 architecture and the UCI Diabetes dataset used for fine-tuning. Considerations include bias in synthetic data feature distributions, particularly those that may reflect real-world disparities in the dataset, and potential hallucinations that do not perfectly match real-world distributions.

instruction: What is the name of this cat breed shown in the photo?
description: This model is a fine-tuned version of a pre-trained Vision Transformer (google/vit-base-patch16-224) for image classification on the Oxford-IIIT Pet Dataset. It uses transfer learning to adapt a generic vision model to identify 37 different cat and dog breeds. The model head is adjusted to output the number of classes in the dataset, and it is trained end-to-end using standard classification loss. The model achieves a loss of 0.1924 and an accuracy of 0.9445 on the evaluation set. It is intended for educational demos on transfer learning and fine-tuning vision models, pet breed classification in structured datasets similar to Oxford Pets, and comparative analysis with zero-shot models like CLIP. The model may not generalize well to breeds outside of the Oxford-IIIT dataset and is not suitable for real-world medical or safety-critical applications. Input images should be clear, centered, and close in style to the training data (cropped pet portraits). The model is trained and evaluated on the Oxford-IIIT Pet Dataset, which contains 7,349 images of cats and dogs spanning 37 different breeds. The dataset includes equal representation of pets and was split into training, validation, and test sets. Evaluation metrics used include accuracy, precision, and recall. The training hyperparameters used are a learning rate of 0.0003, a train batch size of 16, an eval batch size of 8, a seed of 42, the adamw_torch optimizer with betas of (0.9, 0.999) and epsilon of 1e-08, a linear learning rate scheduler, and 5 training epochs. During training, the training loss decreased from 0.3716 to 0.1235 over five epochs, with corresponding validation losses of 0.3013, 0.2342, 0.2124, 0.2050, and 0.2032, and validation accuracies of 0.9242, 0.9310, 0.9350, 0.9350, and 0.9350. The model was also evaluated using a zero-shot image classification model, with breed names provided as candidate labels. The zero-shot evaluation achieved an accuracy of 0.8800, a precision of 0.8768, and a recall of 0.8800. The model was developed using Transformers 4.50.0, Pytorch 2.6.0+cu124, Datasets 3.4.1, and Tokenizers 0.21.1.

instruction: What's the best way to track several objects in a video while ensuring consistent segmentation?
description: SAM 2 is a foundation model designed for promptable visual segmentation in images and videos, developed by FAIR. It supports a wide range of segmentation tasks, including automatic mask generation, image segmentation using point clicks, bounding box inputs, and multi-object segmentation. The model can process both single images and batches of images, allowing for efficient inference across multiple inputs. It also supports video segmentation and tracking, enabling the tracking of objects across video frames, including multi-object tracking and refinement through additional prompts. SAM 2 can use masks from previous predictions as input to refine segmentation results. The model is built with support for batched processing, including batched images, batched objects per image, and batched bounding boxes. It also supports streaming video inference for real-time applications. The model is available through the Hugging Face Transformers library and can be used with the `mask-generation` pipeline for automatic mask generation. SAM 2 is based on the Hiera architecture and is available in different sizes, including the `sam2.1-hiera-small` variant. The model is evaluated on a range of segmentation tasks and is capable of generating multiple mask predictions per input, with the ability to select the best mask based on quality scores. It supports both single and multiple point inputs for refinement and can handle complex batch scenarios with multiple images, objects, and points per object. The model is trained to propagate prompts across video frames, enabling consistent object tracking and segmentation throughout the video. It also allows for refinement of video segmentation by adding additional clicks on any frame. The model is designed to be flexible and can be used in a variety of applications, including image and video analysis, object detection, and segmentation. It is implemented in PyTorch and supports mixed precision inference using bfloat16. The model is publicly available for research and development purposes.

instruction: Which country has the lowest unemployment rate in this dataset?
description: This model is a TAPAS base model fine-tuned on Sequential Question Answering (SQA). It has four versions, with the latest and default version corresponding to the `tapas_sqa_inter_masklm_base_reset` checkpoint. The model was pre-trained on masked language modeling (MLM) and an additional intermediate pre-training step, followed by fine-tuning on SQA. It uses relative position embeddings by default, resetting the position index at every cell of the table. Other versions include `tapas_sqa_inter_masklm_base` with absolute position embeddings, `tapas_sqa_masklm_base_reset` with relative position embeddings and no intermediate pre-training, and `tapas_sqa_masklm_base` with absolute position embeddings and no intermediate pre-training. TAPAS is a BERT-like transformer model pre-trained on a large corpus of English data from Wikipedia in a self-supervised manner. It was pre-trained using two objectives: masked language modeling, where 15% of the words in a flattened table and associated context are randomly masked and the model must predict them, and intermediate pre-training, where the model is trained on a balanced dataset of millions of syntactically created examples to predict whether a sentence is supported or refuted by a table. The model learns an inner representation of English language used in tables and associated texts, which can be used for downstream tasks such as answering questions about a table or determining whether a sentence is entailed or refuted by the contents of a table. Fine-tuning involves adding a cell selection head on top of the pre-trained model and jointly training this classification head with the base model on SQA. The model can be used for answering questions related to a table in a conversational setup. The texts are lowercased and tokenized using WordPiece with a vocabulary size of 30,000. The model inputs are of the form [CLS] Question [SEP] Flattened table [SEP]. The model was fine-tuned on 32 Cloud TPU v3 cores for 200,000 steps with a maximum sequence length of 512 and a batch size of 128. Fine-tuning takes approximately 20 hours. The optimizer used is Adam with a learning rate of 1.25e-5 and a warmup ratio of 0.2. An inductive bias is added so that the model only selects cells of the same column, as reflected by the `select_one_column` parameter of `TapasConfig`. The model card was written by the Hugging Face team and contributors, as the original TAPAS team did not provide one.
domain: Natural Language Processing Table Question Answering

instruction: Please create a summary of this research paper in German.
description: The mT5-multilingual-XLSum is a model checkpoint based on the mT5 architecture, finetuned on a dataset covering 45 languages. It is designed for multilingual abstractive summarization, supporting the generation of summaries in multiple languages. The model is intended to be used with the Transformers library, where it can be loaded and applied to generate summaries from input text. The model's performance has been evaluated on the XL-Sum test sets, with ROUGE scores reported for 44 languages. For example, in Amharic, the model achieves ROUGE-1 of 20.0485, ROUGE-2 of 7.4111, and ROUGE-L of 18.0753. In Arabic, the scores are 34.9107, 14.7937, and 29.1623, respectively. The model's performance varies across languages, with Japanese achieving the highest ROUGE-1 score of 48.1544. The model is part of a study titled 'XL-Sum: Large-Scale Multilingual Abstractive Summarization for 44 Languages,' published in the Findings of the Association for Computational Linguistics: ACL-IJCNLP 2021.

instruction: How do I find all the financial figures in this business report?
description: This repository hosts the ONNX version of NuNerZero, a zero-shot named entity recognition (NER) model optimized for fast inference using ONNX Runtime. This conversion aims to provide efficient and production-ready performance while maintaining the original capabilities of the NuNerZero model. The model is part of Zink, a zero-shot anonymizer that currently uses the onnx NuNERZero model for anonymization. The repository includes the primary ONNX model file used for inference, configuration settings for the model, additional tokens required by the tokenizer, mapping for special tokens, tokenizer vocabulary and configuration files, and the SentencePiece model file used by the tokenizer. The model requires Python 3.7 or higher and the GLiNER package, with the onnx version created on 'gliner==0.2.3'. The model file is approximately 1.85 GB in size, so sufficient bandwidth and disk space are required for downloading. Leveraging the ONNX format can significantly accelerate inference compared to the original PyTorch implementation. Contributions, suggestions, or bug reports are welcome through opening an issue or submitting a pull request for improvements. The model is available under the citation 10.57967/hf/4902.

instruction: Could you outline the process from image input to final segmentation output?
description: The VisionReasoner-7B model is part of the Seg-Zero framework, which is designed to demonstrate generalizability and derive explicit chain-of-thought reasoning through cognitive reinforcement for reasoning segmentation. The VisionReasoner-7B model uses a decoupled architecture that includes a reasoning model and a segmentation model. The reasoning model interprets user intentions, generates explicit reasoning chains, and produces positional prompts, which are then used by the segmentation model to generate precise pixel-level masks. The model is trained exclusively through reinforcement learning with GRPO and without the use of explicit reasoning data. This training approach enables Seg-Zero to achieve robust zero-shot generalization and to exhibit emergent test-time reasoning capabilities. Experimental results show that Seg-Zero-7B achieves a zero-shot performance of 57.5 on the ReasonSeg benchmark, which is an 18% improvement over the prior LISA-7B model. This improvement highlights the framework's ability to generalize across domains while presenting an explicit reasoning process.

instruction: Could you provide a visual representation of the 37 keypoint skeleton for this image?
description: SpinePose is a family of 2D human pose estimation models trained to estimate a 37-keypoint skeleton, extending standard human body models to include the spine, pelvis, and feet regions in detail. Four SpinePose variants (small, medium, large, and x-large) are available, with 0.72, 1.98, 4.22, and 17.37 GFLOPS respectively at inference time. The model is a top-down 2D keypoint estimator developed by the Technical University of Kaiserslautern and DFKI GmbH. It is compatible with PyTorch and ONNX Runtime, and accepts input resolutions of 256×192 or 384×288, depending on the variant. SpinePose is intended for human body and spine joint localization from RGB images or videos, real-time motion analysis for research, animation, or sports applications, and for augmenting general-purpose pose estimators for anatomically rich tasks. It can be integrated with clinical posture tracking systems, used for 3D pose lifting or musculoskeletal modeling via the SpineTrack synthetic subset, and fine-tuned on domain-specific datasets such as industrial, rehabilitation, or yoga. It is not intended for medical diagnosis or treatment without human oversight, full-body 3D reconstruction (which requires a separate lifting model), or unverified use in safety-critical systems. The model is trained primarily on controlled and synthetic datasets and may underperform in occluded or extreme poses. It has limited diversity in body types and cultural attire representation and inherits bias from the COCO/Body8 datasets used for pretraining the teachers. It is recommended to evaluate the model on specific domains and retrain or augment using domain-specific samples to mitigate dataset bias. The model is evaluated using Average Precision (AP) and Average Recall (AR) under varying Object Keypoint Similarity (OKS) thresholds, consistent with COCO conventions but extended to the 37-keypoint SpineTrack format. The SpineTrack dataset comprises both real and synthetic data, including SpineTrack-Real with annotated natural images and SpineTrack-Unreal with synthetic data rendered in Unreal Engine. The synthetic subset was primarily used in an active learning pipeline to bootstrap and refine annotations for real-world images. All released SpinePose models were trained exclusively on the real portion of the dataset. A small number of annotations in the synthetic subset are corrupted, and their use is not recommended until updated labels are released in the next dataset version.

instruction: Please highlight all the mosquitoes in this video frame from a surveillance camera.
description: culico-net-segm-v1-nano is an instance segmentation model focused on segmenting mosquito specimens in images and videos. It is a result of the CulicidaeLab project and was developed by fine-tuning the SAM2 (Segment Anything Model 2) architecture. The model is based on SAM2's efficient Hiera-Tiny backbone and was specifically adapted for mosquito specimen segmentation using a dedicated dataset. It can handle both image segmentation and video segmentation tasks with prompt-based interaction. The model accepts images or video frames with optional prompts, such as points or boxes, and produces binary masks for mosquito specimens. The Hiera-Tiny backbone is an efficient transformer architecture. The model was trained on the iloncka/mosquito-species-segmentation-dataset, which is part of a suite of datasets that also includes iloncka/mosquito-species-detection-dataset and iloncka/mosquito-species-classification-dataset. These datasets contain images of various mosquito species with detailed segmentation annotations. The model was pretrained on ImageNet-1k and the SA-1B (Segment Anything 1B dataset). The model can be used for both image and video segmentation tasks. The CulicidaeLab project aims to provide a comprehensive suite of tools for mosquito monitoring and research. The culico-net-segm-v1-nano model has several practical applications, including precise morphological analysis, automated specimen measurement, training data generation, deployment on edge devices for real-time mosquito monitoring in field conditions, and integration into expert systems for detailed specimen analysis. The development of CulicidaeLab is supported by a grant.

instruction: Can you transcribe this audio of a song in Tamil and explain the meaning of the lyrics?
description: MERaLiON-2 is a family of speech-text large language models designed for Singapore’s multilingual and multicultural context, as well as the wider Southeast Asian region. The flagship model, MERaLiON-2-10B, integrates a localized Whisper-large-v3 speech encoder and a Gemma-2-9b text decoder. It is fine-tuned on 120,000 hours of curated speech and audio data across six diverse tasks: Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR), Spoken Question Answering (SQA), Spoken Dialogue Summarization (SDS), Audio Captioning (AC), Audio-Scene Question Answering (ASQA), and Paralinguistic Question Answering (PQA). The model supports audio inputs up to 300 seconds (5 minutes) and is adapted to handle linguistic nuances, accents, and dialects common in Singapore and neighboring countries. MERaLiON-2-10B-ASR is an ASR-optimized variant that delivers a 5–30% performance improvement over OpenAI’s whisper-large-v3 on speech recognition tasks for Singapore’s four official languages—English, Mandarin, Malay, and Tamil—as well as Indonesian, Thai, and Vietnamese. The model also demonstrates robust handling of code-switching scenarios and local colloquialisms. MERaLiON-2-3B is a lightweight variant that balances performance with reduced computational requirements. The model supports a range of tasks, including transcription, translation, summarization, audio captioning, emotion recognition, gender recognition, and speech translation. It is trained to follow complex instructions and provides detailed explanations regarding speech content and speaker emotions. MERaLiON-2-10B exhibits competitive performance in benchmark evaluations, with results comparable to other state-of-the-art open-source AudioLLMs. The model is not intended for use in tool calling, math, and coding tasks. It is trained on a global batch size of 768 for around 200k steps using 16 nodes and 128 H100 GPUs. The model has not been specifically aligned for safety and may generate inappropriate, offensive, or harmful content. Developers and users are responsible for performing their own safety fine-tuning and implementing necessary security measures.

instruction: Please generate a caption that captures the main elements of this picture.
description: BLIP is a Vision-Language Pre-training (VLP) framework designed for both vision-language understanding and generation tasks. It is pretrained on the COCO dataset using a base architecture with a Vision Transformer (ViT) large backbone. BLIP is capable of performing a variety of vision-language tasks, including image-text retrieval, image captioning, and visual question answering (VQA). It also demonstrates strong generalization ability when applied to video-language tasks in a zero-shot manner. The model utilizes noisy web data by bootstrapping captions, where a captioner generates synthetic captions and a filter removes the noisy ones. BLIP achieves state-of-the-art results, with improvements of +2.7% in average recall@1 for image-text retrieval, +2.8% in CIDEr for image captioning, and +1.6% in VQA score. The model supports both conditional and unconditional image captioning. It is intended for research purposes in support of an academic paper and is not specifically designed or evaluated for all downstream applications. Users are advised to evaluate and address potential concerns related to accuracy, safety, and fairness before deployment, particularly in high-risk scenarios where errors or misuse could significantly impact people’s lives, rights, or safety.

instruction: How would you classify this photo of a kitchen appliance?
description: This is a CLIP ViT-B/32 model trained using the LAION-2B English subset of the LAION-5B dataset. The model was trained by Romain Beaumont. It is intended for research purposes and is designed to support zero-shot, arbitrary image classification, as well as interdisciplinary studies on the potential impact of such models. The model can be used for tasks such as zero-shot image classification, image and text retrieval, image classification and other image task fine-tuning, linear probe image classification, and image generation guiding and conditioning. However, any deployed use case of the model, whether commercial or not, is currently out of scope. Non-deployed use cases, such as image search in a constrained environment, are also not recommended unless there is thorough in-domain testing of the model with a specific, fixed class taxonomy. This is due to the high need for task-specific testing, especially given the variability of CLIP’s performance with different class taxonomies, which makes untested and unconstrained deployment potentially harmful. Use cases related to surveillance and facial recognition are always out of scope, regardless of the model's performance, due to the lack of testing norms and checks to ensure fair use. The model has not been trained or evaluated on any languages other than English, so its use should be limited to English language use cases. The model was trained on a 2 Billion sample English subset of LAION-5B, a dataset created to democratize research and experimentation around large-scale multi-modal model training and handling of uncurated, large-scale datasets crawled from publicly available internet. The dataset is uncurated, and the links may lead to strongly discomforting and disturbing content for human viewers. Users are advised to use the dataset with caution and at their own risk. A 'safe' subset can be extracted by filtering out samples based on safety tags using a customized trained NSFW classifier, but harmful content may still be present in safe mode. The dataset is provided openly to allow for transparent investigation of the benefits and pitfalls of training large-scale models, but it is not recommended for creating ready-to-go industrial products, as the research on the general properties and safety of such models is still in progress. The model was evaluated using a combination of VTAB (with additional robustness datasets) for classification and COCO and Flickr for retrieval. It achieves a 66.6 zero-shot top-1 accuracy on ImageNet-1k. An initial round of benchmarks has been performed on a wider range of datasets, but detailed metrics for this model are not yet available in table form. The model was trained using compute resources provided by the specified cluster.

instruction: What are the different types of objects visible in this surveillance footage?
description: The D-FINE model was proposed by Yansong Peng, Hebei Li, Peixi Wu, Yueyi Zhang, Xiaoyan Sun, and Feng Wu. This is the Hugging Face Transformers implementation for D-FINE.
