D-FINE is a real-time object detector that achieves outstanding localization precision by redefining the bounding box regression task in DETR models. The model comprises two key components: Fine-grained Distribution Refinement (FDR) and Global Optimal Localization Self-Distillation (GO-LSD). D-FINE is trained on COCO (Lin et al. [2014]) train2017 and validated on COCO val2017 dataset. The standard AP metrics are reported, averaged over uniformly sampled IoU thresholds ranging from 0.50 to 0.95 with a step size of 0.05, as well as APval5000, which is commonly used in real scenarios. D-FINE is ideal for real-time object detection in diverse applications such as autonomous driving, surveillance systems, robotics, and retail analytics. Its enhanced flexibility and deployment-friendly design make it suitable for both edge devices and large-scale systems, ensuring high accuracy and speed in dynamic, real-world environments. Variants of the model include 'coco' trained on COCO, 'obj365' trained on Object365, and 'obj2coco' trained on Object365 and then fine-tuned on COCO.

instruction: How would you summarize the visual content of a video showing a storm approaching a town?
description: The VideoMAEv2-Huge model is a video masked autoencoder pre-trained for 1200 epochs in a self-supervised manner on the UnlabeldHybrid-1M dataset. It was introduced in the paper 'VideoMAE V2: Scaling Video Masked Autoencoders with Dual Masking' by Wang et al. and first released in 2023. The model is designed for video feature extraction and can be used to extract features from video inputs. It supports tasks involving video processing and analysis. The model is based on an architecture that incorporates dual masking strategies to enhance learning from unlabeled video data. The model can be loaded using the Transformers library, with a video input processed through a VideoMAEImageProcessor and passed to the model for feature extraction. The model is available under the identifier 'OpenGVLab/VideoMAEv2-Huge'.

instruction: Can you find the total expenses listed in this invoice?
description: This model is a fine-tuned version of an unspecified base model, trained on an unknown dataset. It is designed to perform document question-answering tasks. The model was trained using the following hyperparameters: a learning rate of 5e-05, a training batch size of 4, an evaluation batch size of 8, a random seed of 42, the Adam optimizer with betas of (0.9, 0.999) and epsilon of 1e-08, a linear learning rate scheduler, and a total of 20 training epochs. During training, the model achieved a final training loss of 0.0232 at epoch 19.9115 and step 4500. On the evaluation set, the model achieved a final validation loss of 4.6669. The model was developed using the Transformers 4.44.2 library, PyTorch 2.4.1+cu121, Datasets 3.0.1, and Tokenizers 0.19.1. More information is needed regarding the model's intended uses, limitations, and the specific training and evaluation data used.

instruction: Please translate the German speech in this recording into Japanese text.
description: Granite-speech-3.3-2b is a compact and efficient speech-language model designed for automatic speech recognition (ASR) and automatic speech translation (AST). It uses a two-pass design, where the first pass transcribes audio into text, and the second pass processes the transcribed text using the underlying Granite language model. Each step must be explicitly initiated by the user. The model was trained on a collection of public corpora and synthetic datasets tailored for ASR and AST tasks. Revision 3.3.2 supports multilingual speech inputs in English, French, German, Spanish, and Portuguese, and provides transcription accuracy improvements for English ASR by using a deeper acoustic encoder and additional training data. The model is intended for enterprise applications involving speech input processing, particularly for speech-to-text and speech translation to and from English for the same languages, as well as English-to-Japanese and English-to-Mandarin. It can also handle text-only inputs by calling the underlying granite-3.3-2b-instruct model when no audio is provided. The architecture includes a speech encoder with 16 conformer blocks trained with CTC on character-level targets, a speech projector and temporal downsampler using a 2-layer window query transformer, and a large language model with 128k context length. LoRA adapters with rank=64 are applied to the query and value projection matrices. The model was trained on a diverse set of datasets, including CommonVoice, MLS, Librispeech, VoxPopuli, AMI, YODAS, Earnings-22, Switchboard, CallHome, Fisher, and Voicemail datasets for ASR, and CommonVoice and Phi-4 translations for AST. Training was conducted on IBM's Blue Vela supercomputing cluster with NVIDIA H100 GPUs over 13 days using 32 GPUs. Ethical considerations include the potential for inaccurate, biased, or offensive outputs, as well as the risk of adversarial inputs. The model's modular design enhances safety by limiting how audio inputs can influence the system. IBM recommends using the model alongside Granite Guardian, a fine-tuned instruct model for detecting and flagging risks in prompts and responses.

instruction: Based on the table, which player has the highest average score in the tournament?
description: This model is a TAPAS large model fine-tuned on WikiSQL in a supervised fashion. It has two versions: the default version corresponds to the `tapas_wikisql_sqa_inter_masklm_large_reset` checkpoint, and the other version is `no_reset`, corresponding to `tapas_wikisql_sqa_inter_masklm_large`. The model was pre-trained on masked language modeling (MLM) and an additional step called intermediate pre-training, followed by fine-tuning on SQA and WikiSQL. It uses relative position embeddings, resetting the position index at every cell of the table. The non-default version uses absolute position embeddings. TAPAS is a BERT-like transformer model pre-trained on a large corpus of English data from Wikipedia in a self-supervised fashion. It was pre-trained on raw tables and associated texts without human labeling, using an automatic process to generate inputs and labels. The pre-training objectives include masked language modeling, where 15% of the words in a flattened table and associated context are randomly masked, and the model must predict the masked words, and intermediate pre-training, where the model must classify whether a sentence is supported or refuted by the contents of a table. The model learns an inner representation of the English language used in tables and associated texts, which can be used for downstream tasks such as answering questions about a table or determining whether a sentence is entailed or refuted by the contents of a table. Fine-tuning involves adding a cell selection head and aggregation head on top of the pre-trained model and jointly training these heads with the base model on SQA and WikiSQL. The model is intended for answering questions related to a table. The texts are lowercased and tokenized using WordPiece with a vocabulary size of 30,000. The inputs are of the form [CLS] Question [SEP] Flattened table [SEP]. The WikiSQL dataset was first converted into the format of SQA using automatic conversion scripts. The model was fine-tuned on 32 Cloud TPU v3 cores for 50,000 steps with a maximum sequence length of 512 and a batch size of 512. Fine-tuning takes around 10 hours. The optimizer used is Adam with a learning rate of 6.17164e-5 and a warmup ratio of 0.1424. The team releasing TAPAS did not write a model card for this model; this model card was written by the Hugging Face team and contributors.
domain: Natural Language Processing Table Question Answering

instruction: What's the best way to extract body pose data from this image for a mobile app?
description: Movenet is a model designed for accurate human pose estimation on human images. It is optimized for mobile deployment and is intended to be used on Qualcomm® devices. The model has an input resolution of 192x192 and contains 2.33 million parameters, with a model size of 8.91 MB in float format. Movenet is implemented using PyTorch and can be compiled for on-device deployment using the Qualcomm® AI Hub Workbench. The model supports performance profiling, accuracy verification, and inference on cloud-hosted Qualcomm® devices. It can be deployed on Android using runtimes such as TensorFlow Lite (`.tflite`) or QNN (`.so`). The model uses the w8a16_mixed_int16 precision format and runs on the CPU. Performance metrics across various devices include inference times ranging from 9.21 ms to 57.706 ms and peak memory usage ranging from 12 MB to 41 MB. The model is part of the Qualcomm® AI Hub and can be accessed through the QAI Hub Models package. A demo is available to run the model on sample input data, with options to run locally or on a cloud-hosted device. The model can be compiled for on-device deployment by tracing the PyTorch model and submitting a compile job. Performance profiling and on-device inference require access to the Qualcomm® AI Hub Workbench. The model's original implementation is licensed separately, and more information about the license can be found in the provided link. Users can join the Qualcomm® community for collaboration and support.

instruction: I want to document this call. Can you generate a written transcript of the audio?
description: This model was merged using the TIES merge method. The TIES merge method was applied with a ties_density parameter of 0.9. The encoder weights were set to 0.8 and 0.2, and the decoder weights were set to 0.2 and 0.8. The model was created by merging two models: model_a located at '/mnt/cloud/llm/whisper/whisper-large-v3-russian' and model_b located at '/mnt/cloud/llm/whisper/whisper-large-v3-ru-podlodka'. The output directory for the merged model is '/mnt/cloud/llm/whisper/whisper-large-v3-russian-ties-podlodka'. The model is intended for use in automatic speech recognition (ASR) tasks, particularly for processing phone calls. It is recommended to preprocess audio recordings by normalizing volume and adjusting the sample rate before performing ASR. An example preprocessing command is provided using the sox tool. The model can be loaded using the Transformers library, with support for different device types such as CPU, CUDA, or MPS. The model is currently in a work in progress (WIP) state, with the goal of further fine-tuning it for speech recognition of phone calls. Contributions, especially in the form of relevant datasets, are welcomed and appreciated.

instruction: Could you assess the quality of the approach used to solve this trigonometry problem?
description: Qwen2.5-Math-PRM-72B is a Process Reward Model (PRM) designed for process supervision in mathematical reasoning of Large Language Models (LLMs). It aims to identify and mitigate intermediate errors in the reasoning processes. The model is part of a series that includes Qwen2.5-Math-PRM-7B and Qwen2.5-Math-RM-72B, with the latter being an Outcome Reward Model (ORM). The PRMs demonstrate strong performance in the Best-of-N (BoN) evaluation and in error identification. The model is intended to provide feedback on the quality of reasoning and intermediate steps rather than to generate responses. It requires the use of the `transformers` library version 4.40.0 or higher, as this version includes the necessary integration for Qwen2.5 models. For optimal use, it is recommended to separate individual steps in a solution using double line breaks and to insert a special token for reward computation after each step. The reward value is derived from the probability score of this token being classified as positive, resulting in a value between 0 and 1. The model is evaluated for its ability to assess the quality of reasoning processes and has been developed with contributions from Zhenru Zhang, Chujie Zheng, Yangzhen Wu, Beichen Zhang, Runji Lin, Bowen Yu, Dayiheng Liu, Jingren Zhou, and Junyang Lin, as detailed in the arXiv preprint arXiv:2501.07301.

instruction: Can you help me find the section in this research paper that discusses the experimental setup?
description: ColQwen2 is a model based on a novel architecture and training strategy derived from Vision Language Models (VLMs), designed to efficiently index documents using their visual features. It generates multi-vector representations of text and images, extending the capabilities of VLMs. This version is an untrained base model that ensures deterministic initialization of the projection layer. The model accepts dynamic image resolutions without resizing, preserving the aspect ratio, with a maximum resolution that allows up to 768 image patches. Larger numbers of image patches improve performance but increase memory requirements. It was trained using colpali-engine version 0.3.1 and uses the same data as the ColPali dataset described in the associated paper. The training dataset consists of 127,460 query-page pairs, including 63% from openly available academic datasets and 37% from a synthetic dataset of web-crawled PDF pages augmented with pseudo-questions generated by a VLM (Claude-3 Sonnet). The training set is entirely in English, allowing for the study of zero-shot generalization to non-English languages. No multi-page PDF document is used in both the training and evaluation sets to avoid contamination. A validation set of 2% of the samples is used for hyperparameter tuning. All models are trained for one epoch using bfloat16 format, low-rank adapters with alpha=32 and r=32 on the language model's transformer layers and the final projection layer, and the paged_adamw_8bit optimizer. Training is conducted on an 8 GPU setup with data parallelism, a learning rate of 5e-5 with linear decay and 2.5% warmup steps, and a batch size of 32. The model is intended for use with the colpali-engine version 0.3.1 or higher and requires transformers version greater than 4.45.0. It supports processing of images and queries, generating embeddings for multi-vector retrieval. Limitations include a primary focus on PDF-type documents and high-resource languages, which may limit generalization to other document types or less represented languages. The model relies on multi-vector retrieval based on the ColBERT late interaction mechanism, which may require additional engineering to adapt to standard vector retrieval frameworks. The vision language backbone model (Qwen2-VL) is licensed under Apache 2.0, while the attached adapters are under the MIT license.

instruction: I need to identify the position of every body part in this photo for animation purposes.
description: DWPose is a state-of-the-art whole-body pose estimation model designed to estimate body keypoints. It is intended for use in a pipeline and is provided in ONNX format for ease of integration. The model was developed by researchers Zhendong Yang, Ailing Zeng, Chun Yuan, and Yu Li. The methodology is described in the paper titled 'Effective Whole-body Pose Estimation with Two-stages Distillation,' published at ICCV 2023 (CV4Metaverse Workshop). DWPose is hosted by FASHN AI, which does not claim ownership of the model and provides the weights solely for integration with its virtual try-on pipeline. The model files include 'yolox_l.onnx,' a YOLOX-L detector for person detection, and 'dw-ll_ucoco_384.onnx,' the DWPose-l model for whole-body keypoint estimation. DWPose builds upon the work of other open-source projects, including an OpenMMLab pose estimation toolbox, a Real-Time Multi-Person Pose Estimation system, and a High-performance object detector. The model is released under a license that permits commercial use with proper attribution. Users are encouraged to visit the original repository for full documentation, training code, additional model variants, and discussions. If DWPose is used in research, the original authors should be cited as specified in the provided BibTeX citation.

instruction: I need to analyze inventor patterns over time—can you help me link records from different years?
description: This is a sentence transformer model fine-tuned on the model 'sentence-transformers/all-mpnet-base-v2' and pretrained for the English language. It is designed for patent-inventor linking tasks, specifically for clustering unique patent-inventor records based on attributes such as name, occupation, year, address, firm, and patent title. The model can be used with the SentenceTransformer class or the LinkTransformer package, which implements the SLINK clustering algorithm with cosine similarity as the metric. The model architecture consists of a Transformer with a maximum sequence length of 384 and an MPNetModel, followed by a Pooling layer that averages token embeddings and a Normalize layer. Training was conducted using a DataLoader with a batch size of 64 and a custom loss function called 'linktransformer.modified_sbert.losses.SupConLoss_wandb'. The training parameters included 100 epochs, a learning rate of 2e-05, a weight decay of 0.01, and a warmup of 3100 steps. Evaluation was performed using standard information retrieval metrics, though specific results are not provided in the text. The model is associated with the research paper '300 Years of British Patents' by Enrico Berkes, Matthew Lee Chen, and Matteo Tranchero, and the LinkTransformer package by Abhishek Arora and Melissa Dell.

instruction: Can you help me understand what's shown in this image?
description: This is an ONNX version of the Phi-4 multimodal model that is quantized to int4 precision to accelerate inference with ONNX Runtime. The model is developed by Microsoft and is a conversion of the Phi4 multimodal model for ONNX Runtime inference. It is licensed under the MIT license. The base model, Phi-4-multimodal-instruct, is a lightweight open multimodal foundation model that leverages the language, vision, and speech research and datasets used for Phi-3.5 and 4.0 models. The model processes text, image, and audio inputs, generating text outputs, and comes with 128K token context length. The model underwent an enhancement process, incorporating both supervised fine-tuning and direct preference optimization to support precise instruction adherence and safety measures. The model is only an optimization of the base model, and any risk associated with the model is the responsibility of the user. There may be a slight difference in output from the base model with the optimizations applied.

instruction: Can you evaluate how well this response aligns with common preferences in a safety-sensitive context?
description: The Skywork Reward Model Series includes two advanced reward models built on the Llama-3.1 and Gemma-2 architectures. These models were trained using the Skywork Reward Preference dataset, version v0.2, which contains 80K high-quality preference pairs sourced from publicly available data. The dataset was curated to include high-quality preference pairs and to target specific capability and knowledge domains, such as mathematics, coding, and safety. The models were developed to demonstrate that high-performance reward models can be achieved with a relatively small dataset and straightforward data curation techniques, without further algorithmic or architectural modifications. The resulting reward models excel at handling preferences in complex scenarios, including challenging preference pairs. During dataset curation, top samples from math, code, and other categories in the combined Magpie dataset were selected based on the average ArmoRM score. Adjustments were made to prioritize certain subsets, and a reward model was used to score and filter samples from the WildGuard dataset to improve performance in the Safety domain. The models were evaluated on the RewardBench leaderboard, where Skywork-Reward-Llama-3.1-8B-v0.2 ranks first among 8B models as of October 2024. The models are designed to provide reward scores for conversations, with the 27B model requiring the `flash_attention_2` or `eager` implementation for optimal performance. Users are advised to use the v0.2 version of both the dataset and models to avoid contamination issues. The models are available for both research and commercial use, subject to the terms and conditions outlined in the license agreement. The Skywork model should not be used for activities that pose a threat to national or societal security or engage in unlawful actions. Users are requested not to deploy the model for internet services without appropriate security reviews and records. The model developers disclaim any responsibility for issues arising from the use of the model, including data security issues, public opinion risks, or any risks from the model being misled, abused, or improperly utilized.

instruction: What is the main subject or focus of the video?
description: VideoChat-Flash-7B is built upon UMT-L (300M) and Qwen2-7B, using only 16 tokens per frame. The model leverages Yarn to extend the context window from Qwen2's native 32k to 128k, enabling input sequences of up to approximately 10,000 frames. Due to a predominantly English training corpus, the model exhibits basic Chinese comprehension, and for optimal performance, it is recommended to use English for interaction. The model achieves a performance of 70.0 on MVBench, 58.3 on LongVideoBench, 57.0 on VideoMME (without subtitles), and supports a maximum of 10,000 input frames. Another version of the model achieves 73.2 on MVBench, 64.2 on LongVideoBench, 64.0 on VideoMME (without subtitles), and supports 10,000 input frames. A further variant achieves 73.4 on MVBench, 66.5 on LongVideoBench, 63.5 on VideoMME (without subtitles), and supports 50,000 input frames. Another version achieves 74.3 on MVBench, 64.5 on LongVideoBench, 65.1 on VideoMME (without subtitles), and supports 10,000 input frames. A final version achieves 74.0 on MVBench, 64.7 on LongVideoBench, and 65.3 on VideoMME (without subtitles), supporting 10,000 input frames. The model supports single-turn and multi-turn video-based conversations, where users can ask questions about the content of a video. It can describe a video in detail and answer specific questions about the number of people appearing in the video. The model uses a global compression setting, which can be toggled on or off, with specific configurations for compression type, layers, and image token ratios. The model is evaluated with a maximum of 512 frames and a generation configuration that includes no sampling, a temperature of 0.0, a maximum of 1024 new tokens, a top-p value of 0.1, and a single beam for decoding.

instruction: I have a collection of videos about cybersecurity. Could you help me find the ones most relevant to phishing prevention?
description: The mxbai-rerank-large-v2 is a large model in the family of powerful reranker models from Mixedbread. It is designed to support a range of tasks, including retrieval, with state-of-the-art performance and strong efficiency. The model provides multilingual support for over 100 languages, with particularly strong performance in English and Chinese. It also supports code and long-context processing. The model is trained using a three-step process that includes GRPO (Guided Reinforcement Prompt Optimization), contrastive learning, and preference learning. Performance benchmarks show an average BEIR score of 57.49, a multilingual score of 29.79, a Chinese score of 84.16, and a code search score of 32.05, with a latency of 0.89 seconds measured on an A100 GPU. The model is available for use through the mxbai-rerank package, which can be installed via pip. Users can rank documents based on a query and receive scores for the most relevant results. The technical report and additional details are expected to be released soon. If users find the model useful, they are encouraged to provide a star and citation as described in the provided arXiv and blog post references.

instruction: I’d like to see a video of a phoenix rising from ashes—can you make that happen?
description: This model is a dilated controlnet module designed for the Wan2.1 model. The dilated controlnet includes fewer basic blocks and incorporates a `stride` parameter. For the Wan14B model, the number of controlnet blocks is 6 and the stride is set to 4. The model supports inference tasks for video generation, including command-line interface and Gradio-based web demonstrations. It is intended to be used with the Wan-AI/Wan2.1-T2V-14B-Diffusers base model and the TheDenk/wan2.1-t2v-14b-controlnet-hed-v1 controlnet model. The model is associated with the HED controlnet type and is developed by Karachev Denis, with the code and model available on Github. Professional support can be requested via email.

instruction: Does the phrase 'The system is down' imply that 'Users cannot access the service'?
description: This model, named DeBERTa-v3-large-mnli-fever-anli-ling-wanli, was fine-tuned on the MNLI, Fever-NLI, Adversarial-NLI, and LingNLI datasets, which together contain 885,242 natural language inference (NLI) hypothesis-premise pairs. It is the best-performing NLI model on the Hugging Face Hub as of June 6, 2022, and is suitable for zero-shot classification. It significantly outperforms all other large models on the NLI task. The foundation model is DeBERTa-v3, which incorporates several recent innovations compared to classical masked language models like BERT and RoBERTa. The model was trained using the Hugging Face trainer with specific hyperparameters, including four training epochs, a learning rate of 5e-06, a per-device train batch size of 16, gradient accumulation steps of 2, a per-device evaluation batch size of 64, a warmup ratio of 0.06, a weight decay of 0.01, and mixed precision training. The training data explicitly excluded a dataset due to quality issues, as more data does not necessarily lead to better NLI model performance. The model was evaluated using the test sets for MultiNLI, ANLI, LingNLI, WANLI, and the dev set for Fever-NLI, with accuracy as the metric. It achieves state-of-the-art performance on each dataset, outperforming the previous ALBERT-XXL model by 8.3%. This is likely due to the use of a better pre-training objective (RTD), disentangled attention, and fine-tuning on higher quality NLI data. The model achieves an accuracy of 0.912 on MNLI test (matched), 0.908 on MNLI test (mismatched), 0.702 on ANLI test, 0.64 on ANLI test R3, 0.87 on LingNLI test, and 0.77 on WANLI test. The model's speed on an A100 GPU is 696.0 text per second for MNLI test (matched), 697.0 for MNLI test (mismatched), 488.0 for ANLI test, 425.0 for ANLI test R3, 828.0 for LingNLI test, and 980.0 for WANLI test. The model will reproduce statistical patterns present in the training data. For more information on the training data and potential biases, consult the original DeBERTa-v3 paper and literature on different NLI datasets. If you use this model, please cite the relevant preprint. Older versions of Hugging Face Transformers may have issues running the model, such as tokenizer-related problems. Using Transformers version 4.13 or higher may resolve some of these issues.

instruction: I need to isolate the main voice in a video clip where there are multiple people talking—can you do that?
description: TIGER is a lightweight model for speech separation that effectively extracts key acoustic features through frequency band-split, multi-scale, and full-frequency-frame modeling. The model, called the Time-Frequency Interleaved Gain Extraction and Reconstruction Network (TIGER), leverages prior knowledge to divide frequency bands and applies compression on frequency information. It employs a multi-scale selective attention (MSA) module to extract contextual features and introduces a full-frequency-frame attention (F^3A) module to capture both temporal and frequency contextual information. TIGER significantly outperformed the state-of-the-art (SOTA) model TF-GridNet on the EchoSet dataset in both inference speed and separation quality, while reducing the number of parameters by 94.3% and the MACs by 95.3%. These results indicate that by utilizing frequency band-split and interleaved modeling structures, TIGER achieves a substantial reduction in parameters and computational costs while maintaining high performance. Notably, TIGER is the first speech separation model with fewer than 1 million parameters that achieves performance close to the SOTA model. To more realistically evaluate the performance of speech separation models in complex acoustic environments, the researchers introduced a novel dataset called EchoSet. This dataset includes noise and more realistic reverberation, such as considering object occlusions and material properties, with speech from two speakers overlapping at random proportions.

instruction: I need to ensure this artwork is appropriate for a public exhibition. Can you check it for NSFW content?
description: This model is a vision transformer based on the EVA architecture, fine-tuned for NSFW content classification. It has been trained to detect four categories (neutral, low, medium, high) of visual content using 100,000 synthetically labeled images. The model can be used as a binary (true/false) classifier or to obtain full output probabilities. It outperforms other publicly available models in internal benchmarks, offering the ability to select the NSFW level that suits the use case. The model was trained for 3 epochs on 3 NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3090 GPUs using two sets: training and validation. No images with a cosine similarity higher than 0.92 were included to avoid duplicates and biases between training and validation. A custom loss was created to minimize predictions that are lower than the true class. For example, it is very rare for an image labeled as 'high' to be predicted as 'neutral' (this only happens 0.46% of the time). The model supports efficient batch processing for multiple images and is recommended to be used in bf16 for optimal performance. Using torch tensors is especially indicated in cases such as use with text to image models or similar because the output is already in tensor format. The model has been evaluated on a manually labeled dataset with careful attention to avoiding biases (gender, ethnicity, etc.). In global performance comparisons, the model outperforms AdamCodd and Falconsai in accuracy, particularly on high and neutral labels. It offers greater granularity, performing excellently at identifying low and medium NSFW content. Falconsai may classify some medium and low images as not NSFW or as safe for work (SFW), which could lead to unexpected results. AdamCodd classifies both low and medium categories as NSFW, which may not be desirable depending on the use case. Furthermore, 10% of images in low and medium are considered SFW. In AI-generated content detection, the model is the best option to detect NSFW content, outperforming other models. Falconsai should be avoided for AI-generated content to prevent prediction errors. The model is licensed under the MIT License and is based on the EVA architecture as described in the paper 'EVA-02: A Visual Representation for Neon Genesis'.

instruction: Is there an option to improve the matting quality of a segmented image by refining the edges?
description: BEN2 (Background Erase Network) introduces a novel approach to foreground segmentation through its innovative Confidence Guided Matting (CGM) pipeline. The architecture employs a refiner network that targets and processes pixels where the base model exhibits lower confidence levels, resulting in more precise and reliable matting results. This model is built on BEN. BEN2 was trained on the DIS5k and a 22K proprietary segmentation dataset. The enhanced model delivers superior performance in hair matting, 4K processing, object segmentation, and edge refinement. The Base model is open source. BEN2 supports tasks such as foreground segmentation, hair matting, 4K processing, object segmentation, and edge refinement. The model includes a postprocessing step called refine_foreground, which increases inference time but can improve matting edges. The default value for this step is False. For batch image processing, it is recommended that the batch size not exceed 3 for consumer GPUs as there are minimal inference gains due to the model's custom batch processing for the MVANet decoding steps. BEN2 also supports video segmentation, with options to control output format, frame rate, batch size, and background color when not using the webm format. RMBG 2.0 did not preserve the DIS 5k validation dataset.

instruction: Which document in this collection most accurately answers the question about the causes of the French Revolution?
description: jina-reranker-m0 is a multilingual multimodal reranker model designed to rank visual documents across multiple languages. It accepts a query and a collection of visually rich document images, including pages with text, figures, tables, infographics, and various layouts across multiple domains and over 29 languages. The model outputs a ranked list of documents based on their relevance to the input query. Compared to jina-reranker-v2-base-multilingual, jina-reranker-m0 improves text reranking for multilingual content, long documents, and code searching tasks. The model is built on a decoder-only vision language model architecture, using the base model Qwen2-VL-2B, with fine-tuning applied to the language model using LoRA techniques. An MLP head is post-trained to generate ranking scores that measure query-document relevance. The model is optimized with pairwise and listwise ranking losses to produce discriminative relevance scores. This represents a significant architectural shift from previous cross-encoder models, with a base model of Qwen2-VL-2B (2.4 B parameters) compared to Jina-XLM-RoBERTa (278 M parameters). The model supports a maximum context length of 10,240 tokens (query + document), dynamic image resolution processing (768 × 28 × 28 patches), and multilingual support for 29+ languages. It supports tasks including Text2Text, Text2Image, Image2Text, and Text2Mixed. The model is capable of zero-shot domain transfer, performing well on unseen domains and document types without specific fine-tuning. It also has enhanced capabilities for programming language search and technical document ranking. The model can be used via Jina AI's API or the transformers library, with support for reranking visual and textual documents, as well as querying textual or image documents with image queries. The model has been evaluated on visual retrieval benchmarks such as ViDoRe, MBEIR, and Winoground, as well as text-to-text reranking benchmarks, with performance metrics including NDCG@10 and recall@10. The model is licensed under CC BY-NC 4.0 for non-commercial use.

instruction: What is the most common reason cited for customer complaints in this dataset?
description: This model is a TAPAS base model fine-tuned on Sequential Question Answering (SQA). It has two versions: the default version corresponds to the `tapas_sqa_inter_masklm_base_reset` checkpoint, and the other version is `no_reset`, corresponding to `tapas_sqa_inter_masklm_base`. The model was pre-trained on masked language modeling (MLM) and an additional intermediate pre-training step, followed by fine-tuning on SQA. It uses relative position embeddings, resetting the position index at every cell of the table for the default version, while the non-default version uses absolute position embeddings. TAPAS is a BERT-like transformer model pre-trained on a large corpus of English data from Wikipedia in a self-supervised manner. It was pre-trained using two objectives: masked language modeling, where 15% of the words in a flattened table and associated context are randomly masked and the model must predict them, and intermediate pre-training, where the model is trained to classify whether a sentence is supported or refuted by the contents of a table using a balanced dataset of millions of syntactically created examples. This allows the model to learn a bidirectional representation of tables and associated texts, which can be used for downstream tasks such as answering questions about a table or determining if a sentence is entailed or refuted by the table. The model is fine-tuned by adding a cell selection head on top of the pre-trained model and jointly training this head with the base model on SQA. The model can be used for answering questions related to a table in a conversational setup. The model was fine-tuned on 32 Cloud TPU v3 cores for 200,000 steps with a maximum sequence length of 512 and a batch size of 128. The training took approximately 20 hours and used the Adam optimizer with a learning rate of 1.25e-5 and a warmup ratio of 0.2. An inductive bias was added to ensure the model only selects cells of the same column, as reflected by the `select_one_column` parameter of `TapasConfig`. The model's performance on the SQA development set shows a dev accuracy of 0.6874 for the base version with reset position embeddings and 0.6737 for the base version with no reset. The model card was written by the Hugging Face team and contributors, as the original TAPAS team did not provide one.
domain: Natural Language Processing Table Question Answering

instruction: Make a 5-second animation of a child blowing out birthday candles on a cake.
description: Wan2.1 is a comprehensive and open suite of video foundation models designed to push the boundaries of video generation. It offers state-of-the-art performance, consistently outperforming existing open-source models and state-of-the-art commercial solutions across multiple benchmarks. The T2V-1.3B model requires only 8.19 GB VRAM, making it compatible with most consumer-grade GPUs. It can generate a 5-second 480P video on an RTX 4090 in about 4 minutes, with performance comparable to some closed-source models. Wan2.1 supports multiple tasks, including Text-to-Video, Image-to-Video, Video Editing, Text-to-Image, and Video-to-Audio, advancing the field of video generation. It is the first video model capable of generating both Chinese and English text, enhancing its practical applications. The model features a powerful video VAE, Wan-VAE, which delivers exceptional efficiency and performance, encoding and decoding 1080P videos of any length while preserving temporal information. Wan2.1 is built on the mainstream diffusion transformer paradigm, incorporating innovations such as a novel spatio-temporal VAE, scalable training strategies, large-scale data construction, and automated evaluation metrics. The model uses a Flow Matching framework within the Diffusion Transformers paradigm, with a T5 Encoder to encode multilingual text input and cross-attention in each transformer block to embed the text into the model structure. An MLP with a Linear layer and a SiLU layer processes the input time embeddings and predicts six modulation parameters individually, shared across all transformer blocks. The model is trained on a curated and deduplicated dataset of image and video data, processed through a four-step data cleaning process focusing on fundamental dimensions, visual quality, and motion quality. Manual evaluations indicate that Wan2.1 outperforms both closed-source and open-source models. The model is available under the Apache 2.0 License, with usage restrictions to ensure compliance with applicable laws and ethical standards.

instruction: Can you identify the breed of this dog from the photo I just uploaded?
description: Dog-Breed-120 is an image classification vision-language encoder model fine-tuned from google/siglip2-base-patch16-224 for a single-label classification task. It is designed to classify dog images into specific breed categories using the SiglipForImageClassification architecture. The model achieves an accuracy of 86.81% and an evaluation loss of 0.497. It categorizes images into 121 dog breed classes, including affenpinscher, afghan_hound, african_hunting_dog, airedale, american_staffordshire_terrier, appenzeller, australian_terrier, basenji, basset, beagle, bedlington_terrier, bernese_mountain_dog, black-and-tan_coonhound, blenheim_spaniel, bloodhound, bluetick, border_collie, border_terrier, borzoi, boston_bull, bouvier_des_flandres, boxer, brabancon_griffon, briard, brittany_spaniel, bull_mastiff, cairn, cardigan, chesapeake_bay_retriever, chihuahua, chow, clumber, cocker_spaniel, collie, curly-coated_retriever, dandie_dinmont, dhole, dingo, doberman, english_foxhound, english_setter, english_springer, entlebucher, eskimo_dog, flat-coated_retriever, french_bulldog, german_shepherd, german_short-haired_pointer, giant_schnauzer, golden_retriever, gordon_setter, great_dane, great_pyrenees, greater_swiss_mountain_dog, groenendael, ibizan_hound, irish_setter, irish_terrier, irish_water_spaniel, irish_wolfhound, italian_greyhound, japanese_spaniel, keeshond, kelpie, kerry_blue_terrier, komondor, kuvasz, labrador_retriever, lakeland_terrier, leonberg, lhasa, malamute, malinois, maltese_dog, mexican_hairless, miniature_pinscher, miniature_poodle, miniature_schnauzer, newfoundland, norfolk_terrier, norwegian_elkhound, norwich_terrier, old_english_sheepdog, otterhound, papillon, pekinese, pembroke, pomeranian, pug, redbone, rhodesian_ridgeback, rottweiler, saint_bernard, saluki, samoyed, schipperke, scotch_terrier, scottish_deerhound, sealyham_terrier, shetland_sheepdog, shih-tzu, siberian_husky, silky_terrier, soft-coated_wheaten_terrier, staffordshire_bullterrier, standard_poodle, standard_schnauzer, sussex_spaniel, test, tibetan_mastiff, tibetan_terrier, toy_poodle, toy_terrier, vizsla, walker_hound, weimaraner, welsh_springer_spaniel, west_highland_white_terrier, whippet, wire-haired_fox_terrier, and yorkshire_terrier. The model is intended for use in dog breed image classification, with potential applications in pet identification, animal research, e-commerce, and educational purposes.

instruction: What is the relationship between the people in this video, and how can you tell?
description: The LLaVA-Video-7B-Qwen2 is a 7/72B parameter model trained on a mixture of 1.6 million single-image, multi-image, and video data, using the Qwen2 language model with a context window of 32K tokens. The model is based on the SO400M architecture and was initialized from the lmms-lab/llava-onevision-qwen2-7b-si model. It supports interaction with images, multi-image, and video content, with a specific focus on video. The model can process up to 64 frames and is trained for one epoch using bfloat16 precision. It is intended for tasks involving video understanding and description, including answering questions related to video content. The model was trained using 256 Nvidia Tesla A100 GPUs. The model supports English and Chinese languages. The model's training data and architecture are designed to enable detailed video analysis and interaction. The model is described as having the ability to describe videos in detail and answer related questions. The model is available for use and generation, with a provided example of a generation process using Python code. The model is associated with a research paper titled 'Video Instruction Tuning With Synthetic Data' by Yuanhan Zhang, Jinming Wu, Wei Li, Bo Li, Zejun Ma, Ziwei Liu, and Chunyuan Li, available on arXiv with the identifier 2410.02713.

instruction: How can I get a mask that accurately captures the shape of the text in this image?
description: SAM-HQ (Segment Anything in High Quality) is an enhanced version of the Segment Anything Model (SAM) that produces higher quality object masks from input prompts such as points or boxes. While SAM was trained on a dataset of 11 million images and 1.1 billion masks, its mask prediction quality falls short in many cases, particularly when dealing with objects that have intricate structures. SAM-HQ addresses these limitations with minimal additional parameters and computation cost. The model excels at generating high-quality segmentation masks, even for objects with complex boundaries and thin structures where the original SAM model often struggles. SAM-HQ maintains SAM's original promptable design, efficiency, and zero-shot generalizability while significantly improving mask quality. SAM-HQ builds upon the original SAM architecture with two key innovations while preserving SAM's pretrained weights: a High-Quality Output Token, a learnable token injected into SAM's mask decoder that is responsible for predicting high-quality masks, and Global-local Feature Fusion, which fuses mask-decoder features with early and final ViT features for improved mask details. This combines both the high-level semantic context and low-level boundary information for more accurate segmentation. SAM-HQ was trained on a carefully curated dataset of 44K fine-grained masks (HQSeg-44K) compiled from several sources with extremely accurate annotations. The training process takes only 4 hours on 8 GPUs, introducing less than 0.5% additional parameters compared to the original SAM model. The model has been evaluated on a suite of 10 diverse segmentation datasets across different downstream tasks, with 8 of them evaluated in a zero-shot transfer protocol. Results demonstrate that SAM-HQ can produce significantly better masks than the original SAM model while maintaining its zero-shot generalization capabilities. SAM-HQ addresses two key problems with the original SAM model: coarse mask boundaries, often neglecting thin object structures, and incorrect predictions, broken masks, or large errors in challenging cases. These improvements make SAM-HQ particularly valuable for applications requiring highly accurate image masks, such as automated annotation and image/video editing tasks.

instruction: Can you retrieve slides that show similar inflammatory responses as described in this report?
description: TITAN (Transformer-based pathology Image and Text Alignment Network) is a multimodal whole-slide foundation model pre-trained using visual self-supervised learning and vision-language alignment. It is developed by the Mahmood Lab AI for Pathology at Harvard/BWH and is based on pretrained vision-language encoders. TITAN leverages 335,645 whole-slide images (WSIs) from a diverse set of internally collected neoplastic, infectious, and inflammatory cases at Mass General Brigham, as well as over 182,000 pathology reports and more than 423,000 synthetic captions generated by a pathology co-pilot. The pretraining dataset, called Mass-340K, is sourced from private histology collections at BWH and MGH, in addition to slides from the public GTEx consortium. TITAN's slide embeddings achieve state-of-the-art performance on diverse downstream tasks, including linear probing, few-shot and zero-shot classification, rare cancer retrieval, cross-modal retrieval, and pathology report generation. TITAN is a vision-language model trained on CONCH v1.5 patch features with a patch size of 512x512 pixels at 20x magnification. It builds feature grids from CONCH v1.5 patch features using the coordinates and the distance between the patches. Patch coordinates are saved at the slides' level 0 magnification, and TITAN takes patch_size_lv0, which represents the distance between two adjacent patches at level 0 magnification. Slide-level feature extraction can be performed using the model's encode_slide_from_patch_features function. These pre-extracted features can be used for slide-level classification (via linear probing), retrieval (via l2 distance), and other machine learning settings, without task-specific fine-tuning. The model and associated code are released under the CC-BY-NC-ND 4.0 license and may only be used for non-commercial, academic research purposes with proper attribution. Any commercial use, sale, or other monetization of the TITAN model and its derivatives is prohibited and requires prior approval. Downloading the model requires prior registration on Hugging Face and agreeing to the terms of use. By downloading this model, users agree not to distribute, publish, or reproduce a copy of the model. If another user within an organization wishes to use the TITAN model, they must register as an individual user and agree to comply with the terms of use. Users may not attempt to re-identify the deidentified data used to develop the underlying model. If a user is a commercial entity, they must contact the corresponding author. Access to the model requires agreement to the outlined terms of use, with the primary email for the HuggingFace account matching the user's institutional email. Requests with a personal email (e.g., @gmail, @hotmail, @qq) will be denied. To resolve this, users must add their official institutional email to their HuggingFace account, confirm it, and set it as their primary email. Other reasons for access denial include mistakes in the submitted form, such as a full name with abbreviations, an affiliation not spelled out, an insufficiently described research use, or an unrecognized email domain. The model is available for use with the following requirements: torch==2.0.1, timm==1.0.3, einops==0.6.1, einops-exts==0.0.4, and transformers==4.46.0. The model can be loaded using the huggingface_hub library and the transformers library. TITAN is designed to support a range of tasks, including slide-level feature extraction, classification, retrieval, and report generation. The model is described in the article 'A multimodal whole-slide foundation model for pathology' published in Nature Medicine in 2025. The project was built on top of existing repositories, including ViT model implementations. The authors acknowledge the contributions of the developers of these repositories.

instruction: Is there a way to enhance the resolution of a video while preserving the original colors and motion?
description: The LTX Video Spatial Upscaler 0.9.7 is a component model designed to work in conjunction with the LTX-Video generation models. It is a diffusion-based model that enhances the spatial resolution of videos by being specifically trained to upscale the latent representations of videos generated by LTX Video models. LTX-Video is the first DiT-based video generation model capable of generating high-quality videos in real-time, producing 30 FPS videos at a 1216×704 resolution faster than they can be watched. It is trained on a large-scale dataset of diverse videos and can generate high-resolution videos with realistic and varied content for both text-to-video and image+text-to-video use cases. The LTX Video Spatial Upscaler is compatible with and can be used to improve the output quality of videos generated by both Lightricks/LTX-Video-0.9.7-dev and Lightricks/LTX-Video-0.9.7-distilled. The model is developed by Lightricks and is a Latent Diffusion Video Spatial Upscaler. It takes latent video frames from an LTX Video model as input and outputs higher-resolution latent video frames. The model works best on resolutions under 720 x 1280 and number of frames below 257. It is intended to be part of a multi-stage generation process and is used via the LTXLatentUpsamplePipeline in the diffusers library. The model is not intended or able to provide factual information. As a statistical model, this checkpoint might amplify existing societal biases. The model may fail to generate videos that match the prompts perfectly, and prompt following is heavily influenced by the prompting-style.

instruction: How would you turn a 10-second voice message into text?
description: Moonshine is a sequence-to-sequence automatic speech recognition (ASR) and speech translation model developed by Useful Sensors. The model is designed to transcribe English speech audio into English text and is intended for use in real-time speech transcription products based on low-cost hardware. Two model sizes are available: 'tiny' with 27 million parameters and 'base' with 61 million parameters. Both models are English-only and were trained on 200,000 hours of audio and corresponding transcripts collected from the internet and open datasets available on HuggingFace. The models are trained and evaluated primarily on English ASR tasks and may exhibit additional capabilities if fine-tuned for tasks like voice activity detection, speaker classification, or speaker diarization, though these have not been robustly evaluated. The models are intended for use by AI developers deploying English speech recognition systems in platforms with severe memory and computational constraints. However, the models may generate texts that are not actually spoken in the audio input (hallucinations), likely due to the model's general knowledge of language and its dual task of predicting the next word and transcribing the audio. The sequence-to-sequence architecture also makes the model prone to generating repetitive texts, which can be partially mitigated by beam search and temperature scheduling. These issues may be more pronounced for short audio segments or segments where parts of words are cut off. The models are not appropriate for use in high-risk domains or for subjective classification, particularly for inferring human attributes. The release of the models raises dual-use concerns, as the technology could be used to build surveillance systems or scale existing surveillance efforts. The models may also have some capabilities to recognize specific individuals, which presents safety concerns related to dual use and disparate performance. The models are available for use in Hugging Face Transformers and can be run with the Transformers library, Datasets, and Accelerate. The models were released in October 2024 and are described in a paper available on arXiv.

instruction: Find the image that aligns with the description 'a kitchen with a wooden table and a ceramic mug'.
description: SigLIP 2 Base extends the pretraining objective with prior, independently developed techniques into a unified recipe, for improved semantic understanding, localization, and dense features. The model can be used for tasks like zero-shot image classification and image-text retrieval, or as a vision encoder for VLMs and other vision tasks. SigLIP 2 adds training objectives such as decoder loss, global-local and masked prediction loss, and aspect ratio and resolution adaptibility. The model is pre-trained on the WebLI dataset and was trained on up to 2048 TPU-v5e chips. Evaluation results are presented in the associated paper. The model is described in the research titled 'SigLIP 2: Multilingual Vision-Language Encoders with Improved Semantic Understanding, Localization, and Dense Features' by Michael Tschannen, Alexey Gritsenko, Xiao Wang, Muhammad Ferjad Naeem, Ibrahim Alabdulmohsin, Nikhil Parthasarathy, Talfan Evans, Lucas Beyer, Ye Xia, Basil Mustafa, Olivier Hénaff, Jeremiah Harmsen, Andreas Steiner, and Xiaohua Zhai, available on arXiv with the identifier 2502.14786.

instruction: Where in the table can I find the net income for the last fiscal year?
description: FinTabQA is a model designed for financial table question-answering. It is based on the LayoutLM architecture and is intended to extract information from table images by answering questions about their content. The model is trained on an extractive question-answering task using the SynFinTabs dataset, a large-scale, labelled dataset of synthetic financial tables. FinTabQA supports tasks involving the interpretation of financial tables, including the extraction of specific values or information. It processes input by combining question text with OCR results from table images, using normalized bounding box coordinates to inform the model of word positions. The model is evaluated on real-world financial tables and compared to a state-of-the-art generative model. The model is available for use through the Hugging Face Transformers library and is intended to be used with a fast tokenizer. The model's performance is demonstrated using the synfintabs dataset, where it successfully predicts answers to questions about table content. The model is described as being trained using a method that generates synthetic tables, which the authors hope is transferable to other domains. The model and dataset generation code are publicly available.

instruction: What is the account number associated with this document?
description: This is a fine-tuned document question-answering model based on layoutlmv3-base. It is trained to understand documents using OCR data via PaddleOCR and accurately answer questions related to structured information in the document layout. The model is named 'document-qa-model' and was fine-tuned by Lakshya Singh as a solo contributor. It supports English, Spanish, French, German, and Italian languages and is licensed under Apache-2.0, inherited from the base model. The intended use is to extract answers to structured queries from scanned documents. The model is not suitable for conversational QA or images with no OCR-processed text. It can be used for question answering on document images such as PDFs, invoices, and utility bills, as well as for information extraction tasks using OCR and layout-aware understanding. The model was trained on a dataset consisting of images of utility bills and documents, OCR data with bounding boxes from PaddleOCR, queries in English, Spanish, and Chinese, and answer spans with match scores and positions. The training procedure involved preprocessing with PaddleOCR to extract tokens, positions, and structure, using the LayoutLMv3-base model for four epochs with a learning rate schedule. The model performs well on document-style QA tasks, especially with clearly structured OCR results and document types similar to utility bills, invoices, and forms.

instruction: Can you find any images in the document that show the structure of a protein molecule?
description: ColQwen2.5-7b-multilingual-v1.0 is a multilingual visual retriever based on Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct, utilizing the ColBERT strategy. It is designed to efficiently index documents using visual features and generates multi-vector representations of text and images. The model takes dynamic image resolutions as input without resizing, with a maximum resolution that allows up to 768 image patches. It was trained on 4xA100 80GB GPUs with a per_device_batch_size of 64 and gradient_accumulation_steps of 2 for 5 epochs. The training involved low-rank adapters with alpha=128 and r=128 on the transformer layers of the language model, as well as the final projection layer, using the paged_adamw_8bit optimizer. The training setup included a learning rate of 2e-4 with linear decay and 1% warmup steps, a batch size per device of 128, and gradient accumulation steps of 2 in bfloat16 format. The model was trained on data from the openbmb/VisRAG-Ret-Train-Synthetic-data, openbmb/VisRAG-Ret-Train-In-domain-data, Metric-AI/rag_docmatix_100k, vidore/colpali_train_set, and llamaindex/vdr-multilingual-train datasets. It is ranked #1 on the Vidore benchmark as of February 11, 2025. The model primarily focuses on PDF-type documents and high-resource languages, which may limit its generalization to other document types or less represented languages. It relies on multi-vector retrieving derived from the ColBERT late interaction mechanism, which may require engineering efforts to adapt to widely used vector retrieval frameworks that lack native multi-vector support. The vision language backbone model (Qwen2.5-VL) is licensed under the Qwen RESEARCH LICENSE AGREEMENT, while the adapters are under the MIT license.

instruction: I need the spoken words from this Brazilian Portuguese podcast episode converted into a script.
description: This model is a fine-tuned version of distil-whisper-large-v3 for automatic speech recognition (ASR) in Brazilian Portuguese. It was trained using the Common Voice 16 dataset in conjunction with a private dataset transcribed using Whisper Large v3. The model aims to perform automatic speech transcription in Brazilian Portuguese with high accuracy. By combining data from Common Voice 16 with an automatically transcribed private dataset, the model achieved a Word Error Rate (WER) of 8.221% on the validation set of Common Voice 16. The model is a speech recognition model based on distil-whisper-large-v3. It supports the language Brazilian Portuguese (pt-BR) and is distributed under the MIT license. It was fine-tuned from the model distil-whisper/distil-large-v3.

instruction: How many people are visible in the background of the second frame?
description: The Video-R1-7B model is a video understanding model designed to process and analyze video content. It supports a range of tasks, including answering questions about video content in various formats such as free-form, multiple choice, numerical, OCR, and regression. The model is built to handle multimodal inputs, including video and text, and is capable of generating detailed reasoning and final answers based on the input. It uses a processor and tokenizer for input preparation and relies on an LLM for inference, with configurable parameters such as temperature, top_p, and max_tokens. The model is trained to process video inputs with a maximum of 32 frames and 200704 pixels per frame. It is implemented using PyTorch and the vLLM library for efficient inference. The model is intended for use in scenarios where video content analysis and question answering are required, and it is provided with example code for inference on a single video example. However, the model's specific training details, evaluation results, and performance metrics are not described in the provided text.

instruction: Determine if the provided text on nutrition science supports the question about the benefits of a plant-based diet.
description: rank1 is a reasoning reranker model that generates explicit reasoning chains before making relevance judgments. This 7B parameter model is trained from the Qwen2.5-7B base model and uses test-time compute to produce a reasoning chain before deciding if a document is relevant to a query. The model receives a query and document pair, generates a reasoning chain within a `...` section, makes a binary relevance judgment (`true` or `false`), and returns a confidence score based on the logits of the true/false tokens. This approach helps the model break down complex relevance decisions into logical steps, improving performance across diverse retrieval tasks. rank1-7b demonstrates strong performance on retrieval benchmarks, particularly on tasks requiring complex reasoning. The model's ability to 'think through' relevance decisions makes it especially effective for nuanced topics. The model is part of a family that includes variants with different parameter sizes, including 0.5B, 1.5B, 3B, 7B, 14B, 32B, and models trained from Mistral and Llama 3.1 bases. Quantized versions of the model are also available. The model is compatible with the MTEB evaluation framework and can be used for tasks such as NevIR. For specific benchmark results and comparisons with other models, please refer to the paper and the official GitHub repository.

instruction: What directory structure do I need to set up for the system to work with ComfyUI?
description: This is a gguf quantized version of the wan 1.3b models, which can be run directly using the gguf-connector. Users can select a gguf file in the current directory to interact with by entering a command such as 'ggc w2'. Available GGUF files include 'wan2.1-t2v-1.3b-q4_0.gguf', 'wan2.1-t2v-1.3b-q8_0.gguf', 'wan2.1-vace-1.3b-q4_0.gguf', and 'wan2.1-vace-1.3b-q8_0.gguf'. The model can also be used with gguf-node via ComfyUI by placing the 'wan' model in the 'ComfyUI/models/diffusion_models' directory, the 'umt5' model in the 'ComfyUI/models/text_encoders' directory, and the 'pig' model in the 'ComfyUI/models/vae' directory. The wan architecture is compatible with both ComfyUI-gguf and gguf nodes. A full set of gguf files, including the model, encoder, and vae, is recommended for use with the gguf node. The vace model is preferred because it does not require a vision clip to function for i2v and v2v tasks and runs faster than other models based on initial test results. Users are advised to upgrade their node for umt5 gguf encoder support. When using the umt5 gguf, users may encounter out-of-memory issues after the first prompt due to the tokenizer being rebuilt, but this should resolve after the tokenizer is built for the session. The base model is derived from an unspecified source, and the implementation uses ComfyUI, ComfyUI-gguf, the pig architecture, and the gguf-node and gguf-connector tools.

instruction: How would you describe the appearance of this skin lesion to a patient?
description: MedGemma is a collection of multimodal and text-only variants designed for performance on medical text and image comprehension. Developers can use MedGemma to accelerate the building of healthcare-based AI applications. MedGemma currently comes in three variants: a 4B multimodal version and 27B text-only and multimodal versions. Both MedGemma multimodal versions utilize an image encoder specifically pre-trained on a variety of de-identified medical data, including chest X-rays, dermatology images, ophthalmology images, and histopathology slides. Their LLM components are trained on a diverse set of medical data, including medical text, medical question-answer pairs, FHIR-based electronic health record data (27B multimodal only), radiology images, histopathology patches, ophthalmology images, and dermatology images. MedGemma 4B is available in both pre-trained (suffix: `-pt`) and instruction-tuned (suffix `-it`) versions. The instruction-tuned version is a better starting point for most applications. The pre-trained version is available for those who want to experiment more deeply with the models. MedGemma 27B multimodal has pre-training on medical image, medical record, and medical record comprehension tasks. MedGemma 27B text-only has been trained exclusively on medical text. Both models have been optimized for inference-time computation on medical reasoning. This means it has slightly higher performance on some text benchmarks than MedGemma 27B multimodal. Users who want to work with a single model for both medical text, medical record, and medical image tasks are better suited for MedGemma 27B multimodal. Those that only need text use-cases may be better served with the text-only variant. Both MedGemma 27B variants are only available in instruction-tuned versions. MedGemma variants have been evaluated on a range of clinically relevant benchmarks to illustrate their baseline performance. These evaluations are based on both open benchmark datasets and curated datasets. Developers can fine-tune MedGemma variants for improved performance. MedGemma is optimized for medical applications that involve a text generation component. For medical image-based applications that do not involve text generation, such as data-efficient classification, zero-shot classification, or content-based or semantic image retrieval, the MedSigLIP is recommended. MedSigLIP is based on the same image encoder that powers MedGemma. MedGemma is built based on Gemma 3 and uses the same decoder-only transformer architecture as Gemma 3. The model supports text and vision as input modalities and generates text as output. It uses grouped-query attention (GQA) and supports a context length of at least 128K tokens. MedGemma was evaluated across a range of different multimodal classification, report generation, visual question answering, and text-based tasks. The multimodal performance of MedGemma 4B and 27B multimodal was evaluated across a range of benchmarks, focusing on radiology, dermatology, histopathology, ophthalmology, and multimodal clinical reasoning. MedGemma 4B outperforms the base Gemma 3 4B model across all tested multimodal health benchmarks. MedGemma chest X-ray (CXR) report generation performance was evaluated using the MIMIC CXR dataset. The instruction-tuned versions of MedGemma 4B and MedGemma 27B achieve lower scores due to the differences in reporting style compared to the MIMIC ground truth reports. Further fine-tuning on MIMIC reports enables users to achieve improved performance. MedGemma 4B and text-only MedGemma 27B were evaluated across a range of text-only benchmarks for medical knowledge and reasoning. The MedGemma models outperform their respective base Gemma models across all tested text-only health benchmarks. All models were evaluated on a question answer dataset from synthetic FHIR data to answer questions about patient records. MedGemma 27B multimodal's FHIR-specific training gives it significant improvement over other MedGemma and Gemma models. MedGemma was evaluated across categories relevant to ethics and safety, including child safety, content safety, representational harms, and general medical harms. For all areas of safety testing, we saw safe levels of performance across the categories of child safety, content safety, and representational harms. All testing was conducted without safety filters to evaluate the model capabilities and behaviors. For text-to-text, image-to-text, and audio-to-text, and across both MedGemma model sizes, the model produced minimal policy violations. A limitation of our evaluations was that they included primarily English language prompts. MedGemma is an open multimodal generative AI model intended to be used as a starting point that enables more efficient development of downstream healthcare applications involving medical text and images. MedGemma is intended for developers in the life sciences and healthcare space. Developers are responsible for training, adapting, and making meaningful changes to MedGemma to accomplish their specific intended use. MedGemma models can be fine-tuned by developers using their own proprietary data for their specific tasks or solutions. MedGemma is based on Gemma 3 and has been further trained on medical images and text. MedGemma enables further development in any medical context (image and textual), however the model was pre-trained using chest X-ray, pathology, dermatology, and fundus images. Examples of tasks within MedGemma's training include visual question answering pertaining to medical images, such as radiographs, or providing answers to textual medical questions. MedGemma is not intended to be used without appropriate validation, adaptation, and/or making meaningful modification by developers for their specific use case. The outputs generated by MedGemma are not intended to directly inform clinical diagnosis, patient management decisions, treatment recommendations, or any other direct clinical practice applications. Performance benchmarks highlight baseline capabilities on relevant benchmarks, but even for image and text domains that constitute a substantial portion of training data, inaccurate model output is possible. All outputs from MedGemma should be considered preliminary and require independent verification, clinical correlation, and further investigation through established research and development methodologies. MedGemma's multimodal capabilities have been primarily evaluated on single-image tasks. MedGemma has not been evaluated in use cases that involve comprehension of multiple images. MedGemma has not been evaluated or optimized for multi-turn applications. MedGemma's training may make it more sensitive to the specific prompt used than Gemma 3. When adapting MedGemma, developers should consider the following: bias in validation data and data contamination concerns. Developers should ensure that any downstream application is validated to understand performance using data that is appropriately representative of the intended use setting for the specific application. There is a risk of data contamination, where the model might have inadvertently seen related medical information during its pre-training, potentially overestimating its true ability to generalize to novel medical concepts. Developers should validate MedGemma on datasets not publicly available or otherwise made available to non-institutional researchers to mitigate this risk.

instruction: Find the most relevant information in a document to answer a specific question from a user.
description: This is a Sentence Transformer model trained on approximately 300k triple-samples. It maps sentences and paragraphs to a 1024-dimensional dense vector space and can be used for semantic textual similarity, semantic search, paraphrase mining, text classification, clustering, and more. The model was merged with a base model to maintain performance on other languages. The model has a maximum sequence length of 512 tokens and uses cosine similarity as the similarity function. The full model architecture includes a Transformer with a BERT model, a Pooling layer with a word embedding dimension of 1024 and pooling mode set to the CLS token, and a Normalize layer. The model was evaluated on several MTEB tasks, including classification, pair classification, retrieval, and semantic textual similarity. On the AmazonCounterfactualClassification task, the model achieved a score of 0.5401, which is a -2.48% decrease compared to the merged model. On the AmazonReviewsClassification task, the model achieved a score of 0.2782, which is a 0.44% increase compared to the merged model. On the FalseFriendsGermanEnglish task, the model achieved a score of 0.4703, which is a -1.05% decrease compared to the merged model. On the GermanQuAD-Retrieval task, the model achieved a score of 0.8628, which is an 8.18% increase compared to the merged model. On the GermanSTSBenchmark task, the model achieved a score of 0.6754, which is a 3.33% increase compared to the merged model. On the MassiveIntentClassification task, the model achieved a score of 0.4714, which is a -4.25% decrease compared to the merged model. On the MassiveScenarioClassification task, the model achieved a score of 0.6111, which is a 0.49% increase compared to the merged model. On the GermanDPR task, the model achieved a score of 0.7507, which is a 7.57% increase compared to the merged model. On the MTOPDomainClassification task, the model achieved a score of 0.7686, which is a 0.61% increase compared to the merged model. On the MTOPIntentClassification task, the model achieved a score of 0.4413, which is a -5.80% decrease compared to the merged model. On the PawsXPairClassification task, the model achieved a score of 0.5162, which is a -2.90% decrease compared to the merged model. The model was also evaluated on the German-RAG-Embedding-Benchmark, where it achieved an accuracy of 0.8866. The model was trained using Python 3.10.12, Sentence Transformers 3.2.1, Transformers 4.44.2, PyTorch 2.5.0+cu121, Accelerate 0.34.2, Datasets 2.19.0, and Tokenizers 0.19.1. The model is cited in the paper 'AnglE-optimized Text Embeddings' by Li, Xianming and Li, Jing, published in 2023 as an arXiv preprint.

instruction: Please summarize the key points from this recorded lecture.
description: InternVideo2.5 is a video multimodal large language model (MLLM) built upon InternVL2.5, enhanced with long and rich context (LRC) modeling. It improves the ability to perceive fine-grained details and capture long-form temporal structures. This is achieved through dense vision task annotations using direct preference optimization (TPO) and compact spatiotemporal representations via adaptive hierarchical token compression (HiCo). On the VideoBenchmark, InternVideo2.5 achieves 75.7 on MVBench, 60.6 on LongVideoBench, and 65.1 on VideoMME (without subtitles). Inference speed is measured in tokens per second for generating 1024 new tokens and 5198 (8192-2998) tokens with a video context of 2998 tokens under BF16 precision. With BF16 quantization, the speed is 33.40 tokens/s for 3022 tokens, 31.91 tokens/s for 8192 tokens without the video encoder, and 21.33 tokens/s with the encoder. With INT4 quantization, the speed is 31.95 tokens/s for 8192 tokens without the encoder and 26.37 tokens/s with the encoder. These measurements are conducted on a single A800-SXM4-80G GPU with PyTorch 2.4.0 and CUDA 12.1. The model supports video analysis tasks, including detailed video description and multi-turn conversational interactions. It is designed to handle up to 512 frames and can be configured to process video segments based on duration. The model uses a generation configuration with no sampling, a temperature of 0.0, a maximum of 1024 new tokens, a top-p of 0.1, and a single beam. The model is available for use with specific installation requirements, including the installation of several Python packages and the use of a pre-trained model path. The model processes video input by loading and transforming video frames, applying dynamic preprocessing, and generating outputs through a chat interface that supports both single-turn and multi-turn conversations.

instruction: Could you classify a document that reviews the latest trends in interior design and home décor?
description: The TopicClassifier-NoURL is a model that organizes web content into 17 categories based on the text contents of web pages, without using URL information. The model has 140M parameters and was fine-tuned on two stages of training data: 1 million documents annotated by Llama-3.1-8B for first-stage training, and 100,000 documents annotated by Llama-3.1-405B-FP8 for second-stage training. It is designed to classify web content into one of 24 categories, including Adult, Art & Design, Software Dev., Crime & Law, Education & Jobs, Hardware, Entertainment, Social Life, Fashion & Beauty, Finance & Business, Food & Dining, Games, Health, History, Home & Hobbies, Industrial, Literature, Politics, Religion, Science & Tech., Software, Sports & Fitness, Transportation, and Travel. The model expects input in the form of raw text and outputs logits that can be converted into a probability distribution over the 24 categories using a softmax function. For efficient inference, it is recommended to use the gte-base-en-v1.5 implementation with unpadding and memory efficient attention, which requires installing the `xformers` library and loading the model with specific parameters. The model is part of a broader system for organizing the web, and its development is described in the paper titled 'Organize the Web: Constructing Domains Enhances Pre-Training Data Curation' by Alexander Wettig, Kyle Lo, Sewon Min, Hannaneh Hajishirzi, Danqi Chen, and Luca Soldaini, available as an arXiv preprint.

instruction: Which of these images corresponds most to the phrase 'a child playing with a dog in a park'?
description: ViT-B-16-SigLIP2-256 is a SigLIP 2 Vision-Language model trained on the WebLI dataset. It is designed for contrastive image-text tasks and zero-shot image classification. The model is based on a Vision Transformer (ViT) architecture with a 16-pixel patch size and is optimized using the Sigmoid loss for language image pre-training. It has been converted for use in OpenCLIP from the original JAX checkpoints. The model supports encoding both images and text into feature representations and computing similarity scores between them using a sigmoid function. It is intended for applications requiring multilingual vision-language understanding, improved semantic comprehension, and dense feature extraction. The model's performance is detailed in the research paper titled 'SigLIP 2: Multilingual Vision-Language Encoders with Improved Semantic Understanding, Localization, and Dense Features.'

instruction: Create a short animation of a volcano erupting and lava flowing into the ocean.
description: This is a GGUF quantized and fp8 scaled version of the LTX-Video model.
