It is designed to support video generation tasks, including text-to-video (t2v) and image-text-to-video (i2v) generation. The model can be run using the gguf-connector or through the gguf-node via ComfyUI. It supports various GGUF file options, including ltxv-2b-0.9.8-distilled-iq4_nl.gguf, ltxv-2b-0.9.8-distilled-q6_k.gguf, and ltxv-2b-0.9.8-distilled-q8_0.gguf. The model can be executed by running commands such as 'ggc x2' or 'ggc vg' for the t2v panel, and 'ggc v1' for the i2v panel. The model includes a distilled version that allows for fewer inference steps, such as 15 instead of 30, to reduce loading time. It supports the use of different quantized versions of the T5XXL text encoder and various VAE configurations, including enhanced fp8 to fp32 versions. A low RAM version of the GGUF VAE is also available. The model can be used with the diffusers library, including the LTXPipeline, T5EncoderModel, and LTXVideoTransformer3DModel. It is possible to mix and match different model components, such as using different VAEs with different model files, and to create or convert custom fp8_e4m3fn scaled safetensors to GGUF format. The q2_k GGUF file is noted to be very fast but not suitable for production use and is recommended for testing only. The 0.9_fp8_e4m3fn and 0.9-vae_fp8_e4m3fn versions are reported to work well. The model has been upgraded from version 0.9 to 0.9.6 distilled for improved results. The model can be run entirely offline after the first-time launch, which automatically pulls the model files to a local cache.

instruction: Which of these documents best answers the question, 'How does the human brain process language?'
description: This is a model that maps sentences and paragraphs to a 384-dimensional dense vector space and is designed for semantic search. It has been trained on 215 million (question, answer) pairs from diverse sources. The model uses mean pooling to generate embeddings and produces normalized embeddings with a length of 1. It supports the use of dot-product, cosine-similarity, or euclidean distance as score functions. When loaded with sentence-transformers, dot-product is preferred as it is faster, and it is equivalent to cosine-similarity. The model is intended to encode queries and text paragraphs in a dense vector space to find relevant documents. It has a limit of 512 word pieces, and text longer than that will be truncated. The model was trained on input text up to 250 word pieces and may not work well for longer text. The training procedure involved fine-tuning the model using a contrastive learning objective on a large dataset of (question, answer) pairs. The training data includes duplicate question pairs from WikiAnswers, automatically generated (question, paragraph) pairs from Wikipedia, (title, body) and (title, answer) pairs from StackExchanges, triplets from Bing search engine, (query, answer) pairs from Google queries and featured snippets, (question, answer) pairs from Amazon product pages, Yahoo Answers, and other sources. The total number of training tuples is 214,988,242. The model was trained using mean-pooling, cosine-similarity as the similarity function, and a scale of 20.

instruction: Can you help me translate this paragraph into another language using the open models?
description: Gemma is a family of lightweight, state-of-the-art open models from Google, built from the same research and technology used to create the Gemini models. Gemma 3 models are multimodal, handling text and image input and generating text output, with open weights for both pre-trained variants and instruction-tuned variants. Gemma 3 has a large, 128K context window, multilingual support in over 140 languages, and is available in more sizes than previous versions. Gemma 3 models are well-suited for a variety of text generation and image understanding tasks, including question answering, summarization, and reasoning. Their relatively small size makes it possible to deploy them in environments with limited resources such as laptops, desktops or your own cloud infrastructure, democratizing access to state of the art AI models and helping foster innovation for everyone. The input includes text strings such as questions, prompts, or documents to be summarized, and images normalized to 896 x 896 resolution and encoded to 256 tokens each. The total input context is 128K tokens for the 4B, 12B, and 27B sizes, and 32K tokens for the 1B size. The output includes generated text in response to the input, such as an answer to a question, analysis of image content, or a summary of a document, with a total output context of 8192 tokens. These models were trained on a dataset of text data that includes a wide variety of sources. The 27B model was trained with 14 trillion tokens, the 12B model with 12 trillion tokens, the 4B model with 4 trillion tokens, and the 1B model with 2 trillion tokens. The training dataset includes web documents, code, mathematics, and images. Web documents ensure the model is exposed to a broad range of linguistic styles, topics, and vocabulary, including content in over 140 languages. Code helps the model learn the syntax and patterns of programming languages, improving its ability to generate code and understand code-related questions. Mathematics helps the model learn logical reasoning, symbolic representation, and to address mathematical queries. Images enable the model to perform image analysis and visual data extraction tasks. Rigorous CSAM filtering was applied at multiple stages in the data preparation process to ensure the exclusion of harmful and illegal content. Automated techniques were used to filter out certain personal information and other sensitive data from training sets. Additional methods were applied for content quality and safety in line with safety policies. Gemma was trained using Tensor Processing Unit (TPU) hardware (TPUv4p, TPUv5p, and TPUv5e). TPUs are specifically designed to handle the massive computations involved in training vision-language models (VLMs), offering performance, memory, scalability, and cost-effectiveness advantages. Training was done using JAX and ML Pathways. JAX allows researchers to take advantage of the latest generation of hardware, including TPUs, for faster and more efficient training of large models. ML Pathways is Google's effort to build artificially intelligent systems capable of generalizing across multiple tasks, suitable for foundation models like these. These models were evaluated against a large collection of different datasets and metrics to cover different aspects of text generation. In reasoning and factuality, the models showed performance across various benchmarks such as HellaSwag, BoolQ, PIQA, SocialIQA, TriviaQA, Natural Questions, ARC-c, ARC-e, WinoGrande, BIG-Bench Hard, and DROP. In STEM and code, the models were evaluated on benchmarks such as MMLU, AGIEval, MATH, GSM8K, GPQA, MBPP, and HumanEval. In multilingual tasks, the models were evaluated on benchmarks such as MGSM, Global-MMLU-Lite, WMT24++, FloRes, XQuAD, ECLeKTic, and IndicGenBench. In multimodal tasks, the models were evaluated on benchmarks such as COCOcap, DocVQA, InfoVQA, MMMU, TextVQA, RealWorldQA, ReMI, AI2D, ChartQA, VQAv2, BLINK, OKVQA, TallyQA, SpatialSense VQA, and CountBenchQA. The evaluation methods included structured evaluations and internal red-teaming testing of relevant content policies. Red-teaming was conducted by different teams with different goals and human evaluation metrics. The models were evaluated against categories such as child safety, content safety, and representational harms. For all areas of safety testing, the models showed major improvements in the categories of child safety, content safety, and representational harms relative to previous Gemma models. All testing was conducted without safety filters to evaluate the model capabilities and behaviors. For both text-to-text and image-to-text, and across all model sizes, the model produced minimal policy violations and showed significant improvements over previous Gemma models' performance with respect to ungrounded inferences. A limitation of the evaluations was that they included only English language prompts. These models have certain limitations that users should be aware of. The quality and diversity of the training data significantly influence the model's capabilities. Biases or gaps in the training data can lead to limitations in the model's responses. The scope of the training dataset determines the subject areas the model can handle effectively. Models are better at tasks that can be framed with clear prompts and instructions. Open-ended or highly complex tasks might be challenging. A model's performance can be influenced by the amount of context provided (longer context generally leads to better outputs, up to a certain point). Natural language is inherently complex. Models might struggle to grasp subtle nuances, sarcasm, or figurative language. Models generate responses based on information they learned from their training datasets, but they are not knowledge bases. They may generate incorrect or outdated factual statements. Models rely on statistical patterns in language. They might lack the ability to apply common sense reasoning in certain situations. The development of vision-language models (VLMs) raises several ethical concerns. VLMs trained on large-scale, real-world text and image data can reflect socio-cultural biases embedded in the training material. These models underwent careful scrutiny, input data pre-processing, and posterior evaluations. VLMs can be misused to generate text that is false, misleading, or harmful. Guidelines are provided for responsible use with the model. Transparency and accountability are emphasized, with details on the models' architecture, capabilities, limitations, and evaluation processes summarized in this model card. A responsibly developed open model offers the opportunity to share innovation by making VLM technology accessible to developers and researchers across the AI ecosystem. Risks identified include the perpetuation of biases, generation of harmful content, misuse for malicious purposes, and privacy violations. It is encouraged to perform continuous monitoring and the exploration of de-biasing techniques during model training, fine-tuning, and other use cases. Mechanisms and guidelines for content safety are essential. Developers are encouraged to exercise caution and implement appropriate content safety safeguards based on their specific product policies and application use cases. Technical limitations and developer and end-user education can help mitigate against malicious applications of VLMs. Educational resources and reporting mechanisms for users to flag misuse are provided. Prohibited uses of Gemma models are outlined in the Gemma Prohibited Use Policy. Models were trained on data filtered for removal of certain personal information and other sensitive data. Developers are encouraged to adhere to privacy regulations with privacy-preserving techniques. At the time of release, this family of models provides high-performance open vision-language model implementations designed from the ground up for responsible AI development compared to similarly sized models. Using the benchmark evaluation metrics described in this document, these models have shown to provide superior performance to other, comparably-sized open model alternatives.

instruction: Highlight the signatures in this scanned image of a legal agreement.
description: YOLOS is a Vision Transformer (ViT) trained using the DETR loss. A base-sized YOLOS model achieves 42 AP on COCO validation 2017, comparable to DETR and more complex frameworks like Faster R-CNN. This model has been finetuned to detect handwritten signatures in document images. It is designed for detecting handwritten signatures in scanned documents, contracts, or forms. The model is based on the YOLOS architecture introduced by Fang et al. in 2021. The training data was built from two public datasets, unified and processed into a dataset containing 1,980 training images, 420 validation images, and 419 test images, all in COCO JSON format with a resolution of 640x640 pixels. The model is licensed under the Apache 2.0 license, which allows for permissive use, including commercial applications, without requiring modifications to be open-sourced. The model may exhibit bias if the training data lacks diversity in signature styles or cultural context. False positives and negatives can occur, which may impact document validation. Performance may degrade on poor-quality images or in challenging visual conditions such as noise or lighting. The model is not suitable for detecting non-signature elements in documents, nor should it be solely relied on for high-stakes decisions without human validation. It must not be used for fraudulent or illegal activities. The model supports PyTorch for both the image processor and model. Inference times on a laptop with an Intel Core i7-9750H CPU and NVIDIA GeForce GTX 1650 GPU are 0.262 seconds on CPU and 0.014 seconds on GPU. The model has 6.47M parameters and achieves an mAP50 of 0.856 and an mAP50-95 of 0.395 on the test set. The model was developed and trained by Mario DEFRANCE, who is responsible for model development, performance evaluation, and technical documentation. The model is a finetuned version of the original YOLOS model, and users are encouraged to cite the original paper if they use this model.

instruction: Create a surreal image of a desert with floating islands and ancient ruins in the distance.
description: FLUX.1 [dev] is a 12 billion parameter rectified flow transformer designed to generate images from text descriptions. It offers cutting-edge output quality, second only to the state-of-the-art model FLUX.1 [pro]. The model demonstrates competitive prompt following, matching the performance of closed-source alternatives. It is trained using guidance distillation, which makes it more efficient. The model's weights are open to drive new scientific research and empower artists to develop innovative workflows. Generated outputs can be used for personal, scientific, and commercial purposes as described in the FLUX.1 [dev] Non-Commercial License. A reference implementation of FLUX.1 [dev], along with sampling code, is provided in a dedicated repository, which developers and creatives can use as a starting point for building on top of the model. The FLUX.1 models are available via API from multiple sources. FLUX.1 [dev] is also available in a node-based workflow for local inference. To use FLUX.1 [dev] with the diffusers Python library, users must first install or upgrade diffusers and then use FluxPipeline to run the model. The model is not intended or able to provide factual information and, as a statistical model, may amplify existing societal biases. It may fail to generate output that matches the prompts, and prompt following is heavily influenced by the prompting-style. The model and its derivatives may not be used in any way that violates applicable laws or regulations, for the purpose of exploiting or harming minors, to generate or disseminate verifiably false information with the intent to harm, to generate or disseminate personal identifiable information that can be used to harm an individual, to harass, abuse, threaten, stalk, or bully individuals or groups, to create non-consensual nudity or illegal pornographic content, for fully automated decision making that adversely impacts legal rights or creates binding obligations, or to generate or facilitate large-scale disinformation campaigns. This model is licensed under the FLUX.1 [dev] Non-Commercial License.

instruction: Can you extract the skeletal structure from this image to help with animation modeling?
description: SpinePose is a family of 2D human pose estimation models trained to estimate a 37-keypoint skeleton, extending standard human body models to include the spine, pelvis, and feet regions in detail. Four SpinePose variants (small, medium, large, and x-large) are available, with 0.72, 1.98, 4.22, and 17.37 GFLOPS respectively at inference time. The model is a top-down 2D keypoint estimator developed by the Technical University of Kaiserslautern and DFKI GmbH. It is compatible with PyTorch and ONNX Runtime, and accepts input resolutions of 256×192 or 384×288, depending on the variant. SpinePose is intended for human body and spine joint localization from RGB images or videos, real-time motion analysis for research, animation, or sports applications, and for augmenting general-purpose pose estimators for anatomically rich tasks. It can be integrated with clinical posture tracking systems, used for 3D pose lifting or musculoskeletal modeling via the SpineTrack synthetic subset, and fine-tuned on domain-specific datasets such as industrial, rehabilitation, or yoga. It is not intended for medical diagnosis or treatment without human oversight, full-body 3D reconstruction (which requires a separate lifting model), or unverified use in safety-critical systems. The model is trained primarily on controlled and synthetic datasets and may underperform in occluded or extreme poses. It has limited diversity in body types and cultural attire representation and inherits bias from the COCO/Body8 datasets used for pretraining the teachers. It is recommended to evaluate the model on specific domains and retrain or augment using domain-specific samples to mitigate dataset bias. The model is evaluated using Average Precision (AP) and Average Recall (AR) under varying Object Keypoint Similarity (OKS) thresholds, consistent with COCO conventions but extended to the 37-keypoint SpineTrack format. The SpineTrack dataset comprises both real and synthetic data, including SpineTrack-Real with annotated natural images and SpineTrack-Unreal with synthetic data rendered in Unreal Engine. The synthetic subset was primarily used in an active learning pipeline to bootstrap and refine annotations for real-world images. All released SpinePose models were trained exclusively on the real portion of the dataset. A small number of annotations in the synthetic subset are corrupted, and their use is not recommended until updated labels are released in the next dataset version.

instruction: Can you predict the species of a plant based on its physical characteristics from this dataset?
description: TabPFNMix classifier is a tabular foundation model pre-trained on purely synthetic datasets sampled from a mix of random classifiers. It is based on a 12-layer encoder-decoder Transformer with 37 million parameters. The pre-training strategy incorporates in-context learning, similar to that used by TabPFN and TabForestPFN. The model is designed for tasks involving tabular data classification and supports fine-tuning and inference. It is implemented within the AutoGluon framework, and users are instructed to install AutoGluon to utilize the model. Performance details are not explicitly provided in the text. The model is intended for use with structured data and is evaluated in the context of solving small tabular classification problems. Users are advised to cite the associated papers if they find TabPFNMix useful for their research. The project is licensed under the Apache-2.0 License.

instruction: Could you make a spoken version of this email to send to a client?
description: Kokoro is an open-weight text-to-speech (TTS) model with 82 million parameters. Despite its lightweight architecture, it delivers comparable quality to larger models while being significantly faster and more cost-efficient. With Apache-licensed weights, Kokoro can be deployed anywhere from production environments to personal projects. As of April 2025, the market rate of Kokoro served over API is under $1 per million characters of text input, or under $0.06 per hour of audio output. On average, 1000 characters of input is about 1 minute of output. Kokoro has been deployed in numerous projects and commercial APIs. Fake websites like kokorottsai_com and kokorotts_net are likely scams masquerading under the banner of a popular model. Any website containing 'kokoro' in its root domain is not owned by and not affiliated with this model page or its author, and attempts to imply otherwise are red flags. Kokoro v1.0 was published on 2025 Jan 27 and is based on a few hundred hours of training data. The model uses a StyleTTS 2 and ISTFTNet architecture, and it is decoder only, with no diffusion or encoder release. Kokoro was architected by Li et al and trained by @rzvzn on Discord. It supports multiple languages. Kokoro was trained exclusively on permissive/non-copyrighted audio data and IPA phoneme labels. Examples of permissive/non-copyrighted audio include public domain audio, audio licensed under Apache, MIT, etc., and synthetic audio generated by closed TTS models from large providers. No synthetic audio from open TTS models or 'custom voice clones' was used. The total dataset size is a few hundred hours of audio, and the total training cost was about $1000 for 1000 hours of A100 80GB vRAM. The following Creative Commons Attribution (CC BY) audio was part of the dataset used to train Kokoro v1.0.

instruction: What age group would you assign to the person in this picture?
description: Age-Classification-SigLIP2 is an image classification vision-language encoder model fine-tuned from google/siglip2-base-patch16-224 for a single-label classification task. It is designed to predict the age group of a person from an image using the SiglipForImageClassification architecture. The model categorizes images into five age groups: Class 0: 'Child 0-12', Class 1: 'Teenager 13-20', Class 2: 'Adult 21-44', Class 3: 'Middle Age 45-64', and Class 4: 'Aged 65+'. The model achieves a precision of 0.9744 for the 'Child 0-12' class, 0.8675 for the 'Teenager 13-20' class, 0.9053 for the 'Adult 21-44' class, 0.9059 for the 'Middle Age 45-64' class, and 0.9144 for the 'Aged 65+' class. The corresponding recall values are 0.9562, 0.7032, 0.9769, 0.8317, and 0.8397, respectively. The F1-scores are 0.9652, 0.7768, 0.9397, 0.8672, and 0.8755, with support counts of 2193, 1779, 9999, 3785, and 1260. The overall accuracy of the model is 0.9109. The model is intended for use in demographic analysis, health and fitness applications, security and access control, retail and marketing, and forensics and surveillance. It is designed to assist in age-based health recommendations, age verification in digital systems, and age estimation for security purposes, as well as to help businesses and researchers analyze age distribution and enhance personalized customer experiences.

instruction: Can you determine if this image was created by a deep learning system?
description: This is a Vision Transformer (ViT-Small) model trained on the largest dataset to date for detecting AI-generated images in forensic applications. It was developed by Jeongsoo Park and Andrew Owens from the University of Michigan and is based on the timm/vit_small_patch16_384.augreg_in21k_ft_in1k model. The model was trained using PyTorch 2.0 with bf16 mixed precision, the AdamW optimizer with a learning rate of 5e-5, for 10 epochs and a batch size of 32. The training dataset consists of 2.7 million images from 15+ generators and 4600+ models, totaling over 1.15TB of data. The model is licensed under the MIT license, which is compatible with the CreativeML OpenRAIL-M referenced in the associated paper. It has been adapted for Hugging Face inference compatibility by AI Without Borders. Unverified testing results indicate an accuracy of 97.2%, an F1 score of 0.968, an AUC-ROC of 0.992, and a false positive rate of 2.1%. These results are unverified due to a lack of resources to evaluate a dataset over 1.4TB in size. The model is presented as part of OpenSight, an upcoming open-source framework for adaptive deepfake detection, and is uploaded for community validation. A Hugging Face Space is planned to showcase ultra-fast inference methods and will eventually include a leaderboard. A re-sampled and refined dataset is also planned for future release. The model is associated with the paper titled 'Community Forensics: Using Thousands of Generators to Train Fake Image Detectors' by Jeongsoo Park and Andrew Owens, available on arXiv with the identifier 2411.04125.

instruction: Could you recognize the Arabic dialect in this short clip from a radio broadcast?
description: Tamyïz is an accurate and robust Transformer-based model for Arabic Dialect Identification (ADI) in speech. It is adapted from a pre-trained massively multilingual speech model and fine-tuned on diverse Arabic TV broadcast speech to identify five Arabic language varieties: Modern Standard Arabic (MSA), Egyptian Arabic (Masri and Sudani), Gulf Arabic (Khleeji, Iraqi, and Yemeni), Levantine Arabic (Shami), and Maghrebi Arabic (Dialects of al-Maghreb al-Arabi in North Africa). The model can be used as a component in a large-scale speech data collection pipeline to create resources for different Arabic dialects. It can also be used to filter speech data for Modern Standard Arabic (MSA) for text-to-speech (TTS) systems. The model is based on the wav2vec 2.0 architecture and is fine-tuned from the MMS-300m model. It is trained on the MGB-3 ADI-5 dataset, which consists of TV Broadcast speech from Al Jazeera TV, including news, interviews, discussions, and TV shows. The model has been evaluated on a challenging multi-domain benchmark and performed very well, with the expectation that it will be robust to real-world speech samples. The model should not be used to assess fluency or nativeness of speech, determine whether the speaker uses a formal or informal register, make judgments about a speaker's origin, education level, or socioeconomic status, or filter or discriminate against speakers based on dialect. Some Arabic varieties are not well-represented in the training data, and the model may not work well for some dialects such as Yemeni Arabic, Iraqi Arabic, and Saharan Arabic. Additional limitations include the possibility that very short audio samples (< 2 seconds) may not provide enough information for accurate classification, code-switching between dialects (especially mixing with MSA) may result in less reliable classifications, speakers who have lived in multiple dialect regions may exhibit mixed features, and speech from non-typical speakers such as children and people with speech disorders might be challenging for the model. For optimal results, it is recommended to use audio segments of at least 5-10 seconds. Confidence scores may not always be informative, as the model could make a wrong decision but still be very confident. For critical applications, human verification of model predictions is recommended. The model is licensed under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 (CC BY 4.0). If you use this model in your research, please cite the paper titled 'Voice Conversion Improves Cross-Domain Robustness for Spoken Arabic Dialect Identification' by Badr M. Abdullah, Matthew Baas, Bernd Möbius, and Dietrich Klakow, published in Interspeech 2025. For any questions, contact badr dot nlp at gmail dot com.

instruction: How would you express this English phrase in Turkmen?
description: This model is a LoRA fine-tuned version of the NLLB-200 (600 M) model, specifically adapted for Turkmen to English and English to Turkmen translation. The fine-tuning was performed using Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) on only the `q_proj` and `v_proj` matrices, resulting in approximately 2.4 million trainable parameters, which is about 0.38% of the total model parameters. The model was developed as a final project for a Deep-Learning class and as a contribution to the Turkmen AI community, where open-source resources are limited. It is intended for use in research prototypes, student projects, and quick experiments on Turkmen text, but is not suitable for commercial machine translation systems, critical medical or legal translation, or production workloads without further validation. The model is licensed under CC-BY-NC 4.0, which restricts its use to non-commercial purposes. The training data consisted of 619 parallel sentences (495 for training, 62 for validation, and 62 for testing) drawn from news and official communiqués. The training was conducted on a single NVIDIA A100 40 GB GPU using the AdamW optimizer with a learning rate of 1 × 10⁻⁵, a cosine schedule with 10% warm-up, over 5 epochs. The batch size was 4 for training and 8 for evaluation, with FP16 enabled. The LoRA configuration used `r=16`, `alpha=32`, `dropout=0.05`, and targeted the `q_proj` and `v_proj` modules. Evaluation results show that the fine-tuned model achieved a BLEU score of 8.24 for English to Turkmen translation, compared to 8.12 for the original model, and a BLEU score of 25.88 for Turkmen to English, compared to 26.48 for the original. The model also showed improvements in chrF and TER metrics for English to Turkmen translation, but a slight decline in BLEU and chrF for Turkmen to English. A manual review of 50 test sentences indicated that 36 translations were judged as “Good” or better in terms of adequacy, and 38 were considered fluent by native speakers. Limitations include a small training dataset of around 500 sentences, which may limit vocabulary and domain coverage, potential hallucination of proper nouns or numbers in longer inputs, and the inability to guarantee gender or politeness nuances. The model is open for contributions, including data, code, evaluation, and documentation improvements, with all contributions required to be compatible with non-commercial use. Future work includes experimenting with larger datasets, hyperparameter tuning, and exploring additional evaluation metrics.

instruction: Could you help me understand the meaning behind this abstract artwork?
description: Gemma is a family of lightweight, state-of-the-art open models from Google, built from the same research and technology used to create the Gemini models. Gemma 3 models are multimodal, handling text and image input and generating text output, with open weights for both pre-trained variants and instruction-tuned variants. Gemma 3 has a large, 128K context window, multilingual support in over 140 languages, and is available in more sizes than previous versions. Gemma 3 models are well-suited for a variety of text generation and image understanding tasks, including question answering, summarization, and reasoning. Their relatively small size makes it possible to deploy them in environments with limited resources such as laptops, desktops or your own cloud infrastructure, democratizing access to state of the art AI models and helping foster innovation for everyone. The input includes text strings such as questions, prompts, or documents to be summarized, and images normalized to 896 x 896 resolution and encoded to 256 tokens each. The total input context is 128K tokens for the 4B, 12B, and 27B sizes, and 32K tokens for the 1B size. The output is generated text in response to the input, such as an answer to a question, analysis of image content, or a summary of a document, with a total output context of 8192 tokens. The models were trained on a dataset of text data that includes a wide variety of sources. The 27B model was trained with 14 trillion tokens, the 12B model with 12 trillion tokens, the 4B model with 4 trillion tokens, and the 1B model with 2 trillion tokens. The training data includes web documents, code, mathematics, and images. Rigorous CSAM filtering was applied at multiple stages in the data preparation process to ensure the exclusion of harmful and illegal content. Automated techniques were used to filter out certain personal information and other sensitive data from training sets. Additional methods were applied for content quality and safety in line with safety policies. Gemma was trained using Tensor Processing Unit (TPU) hardware (TPUv4p, TPUv5p, and TPUv5e). TPUs are specifically designed to handle the massive computations involved in training vision-language models (VLMs), offering performance, memory, scalability, and cost-effectiveness advantages. Training was done using JAX and ML Pathways. JAX allows researchers to take advantage of the latest generation of hardware, including TPUs, for faster and more efficient training of large models. ML Pathways is Google's latest effort to build artificially intelligent systems capable of generalizing across multiple tasks, which is especially suitable for foundation models. The 'single controller' programming model of JAX and Pathways allows a single Python process to orchestrate the entire training run, dramatically simplifying the development workflow. The models were evaluated against a large collection of different datasets and metrics to cover different aspects of text generation. In reasoning and factuality, the models showed performance across various benchmarks such as HellaSwag, BoolQ, PIQA, SocialIQA, TriviaQA, Natural Questions, ARC-c, ARC-e, WinoGrande, BIG-Bench Hard, and DROP. In STEM and code, the models were evaluated on benchmarks such as MMLU, AGIEval, MATH, GSM8K, GPQA, MBPP, and HumanEval. In multilingual tasks, the models were evaluated on benchmarks such as MGSM, Global-MMLU-Lite, WMT24++, FloRes, XQuAD, ECLeKTic, and IndicGenBench. In multimodal tasks, the models were evaluated on benchmarks such as COCOcap, DocVQA, InfoVQA, MMMU, TextVQA, RealWorldQA, ReMI, AI2D, ChartQA, VQAv2, BLINK, OKVQA, TallyQA, SpatialSense VQA, and CountBenchQA. The evaluation methods included structured evaluations and internal red-teaming testing of relevant content policies. Red-teaming was conducted by different teams with different goals and human evaluation metrics. The models were evaluated against categories such as child safety, content safety, and representational harms. In addition to development level evaluations, assurance evaluations were conducted separately from the model development team to inform decision making about release. For all areas of safety testing, the models showed major improvements in the categories of child safety, content safety, and representational harms relative to previous Gemma models. All testing was conducted without safety filters to evaluate the model capabilities and behaviors. The models produced minimal policy violations and showed significant improvements over previous Gemma models' performance with respect to ungrounded inferences. A limitation of the evaluations was that they included only English language prompts. The models have certain limitations that users should be aware of. The quality and diversity of the training data significantly influence the model's capabilities. Biases or gaps in the training data can lead to limitations in the model's responses. The scope of the training dataset determines the subject areas the model can handle effectively. Models are better at tasks that can be framed with clear prompts and instructions. Open-ended or highly complex tasks might be challenging. A model's performance can be influenced by the amount of context provided (longer context generally leads to better outputs, up to a certain point). Natural language is inherently complex. Models might struggle to grasp subtle nuances, sarcasm, or figurative language. Models generate responses based on information they learned from their training datasets, but they are not knowledge bases. They may generate incorrect or outdated factual statements. Models rely on statistical patterns in language. They might lack the ability to apply common sense reasoning in certain situations. The development of vision-language models (VLMs) raises several ethical concerns. VLMs trained on large-scale, real-world text and image data can reflect socio-cultural biases embedded in the training material. These models underwent careful scrutiny, input data pre-processing, and posterior evaluations. VLMs can be misused to generate text that is false, misleading, or harmful. Guidelines are provided for responsible use with the model. Transparency and accountability are emphasized by summarizing details on the models' architecture, capabilities, limitations, and evaluation processes. A responsibly developed open model offers the opportunity to share innovation by making VLM technology accessible to developers and researchers across the AI ecosystem. Risks identified include the perpetuation of biases, generation of harmful content, misuse for malicious purposes, and privacy violations. It is encouraged to perform continuous monitoring and the exploration of de-biasing techniques during model training, fine-tuning, and other use cases. Mechanisms and guidelines for content safety are essential. Developers are encouraged to exercise caution and implement appropriate content safety safeguards based on their specific product policies and application use cases. Technical limitations and developer and end-user education can help mitigate against malicious applications of VLMs. Educational resources and reporting mechanisms for users to flag misuse are provided. Prohibited uses of Gemma models are outlined in the Gemma Prohibited Use Policy. Models were trained on data filtered for removal of certain personal information and other sensitive data. Developers are encouraged to adhere to privacy regulations with privacy-preserving techniques. At the time of release, this family of models provides high-performance open vision-language model implementations designed from the ground up for responsible AI development compared to similarly sized models. Using the benchmark evaluation metrics described in this document, these models have shown to provide superior performance to other, comparably-sized open model alternatives.

instruction: Given this table of sensor readings, can you estimate the temperature for the next hour?
description: TabPFN is a transformer-based foundation model for tabular data that leverages prior-data based learning to achieve strong performance on small tabular regression tasks without requiring task-specific training. It is developed by Prior Labs and is designed to provide accurate predictions on small datasets. The model is not intended for use with very large datasets or non-tabular data formats. It requires Python version 3.9 or higher, PyTorch version 2.1 or higher, and scikit-learn version 1.0 or higher. A minimum of 16GB of RAM is recommended, with CPU support and optional GPU support. The model was published in a paper titled 'Accurate predictions on small data with a tabular foundation model' in the journal Nature in January 2025. The research team includes Noah Hollmann, Samuel Müller, Lennart Purucker, Arjun Krishnakumar, Max Körfer, Shi Bin Hoo, Robin Tibor Schirrmeister, Frank Hutter, Eddie Bergman, and Léo Grinsztajn.

instruction: How can I ensure the audio file I submit is compatible with the system?
description: XCodec2 is a speech tokenizer designed for speech synthesis, offering single vector quantization, a processing speed of 50 tokens per second, multilingual speech semantic support, and high-quality speech reconstruction. It is based on the LLaMA architecture and is intended for use in audio language models. The model supports encoding and decoding of speech waveforms, with the ability to reconstruct audio from quantized codes. It is compatible with 16kHz speech and currently supports single input processing. For batch inference or large-scale code extraction, additional resources are available. The model can be installed and used via the Hugging Face platform. Performance and stability may vary between versions, with version 0.1.5 optimized for codec inference and llasa fine-tuning, while version 0.1.3 is recommended for more stable codec training. The model has been developed with a focus on addressing the semantic shortcomings of codecs in audio language models, as explored in the paper 'Codec Does Matter: Exploring the Semantic Shortcoming of Codec for Audio Language Model' (AAAI 2025).

instruction: How do I find a specific table in a scientific paper that compares different machine learning models?
description: ColQwen2.5 is a model based on a novel architecture and training strategy using Vision Language Models (VLMs) to efficiently index documents from their visual features. It generates multi-vector representations of text and images. The model takes dynamic image resolutions as input without resizing, which may change their aspect ratio, similar to ColPali. The maximum resolution is set to create at most 768 image patches, with experiments showing improvements with more patches at the cost of increased memory requirements. This version is trained with colpali-engine version 0.3.7 and uses the same data as the ColPali dataset described in the paper. The training dataset consists of 127,460 query-page pairs, including 63% from openly available academic datasets and 37% from a synthetic dataset made of web-crawled PDF pages augmented with VLM-generated pseudo-questions. The training set is entirely in English, allowing for the study of zero-shot generalization to non-English languages. No multi-page PDF document is used in both the training and evaluation sets to prevent contamination. A validation set of 2% of the samples is used for hyperparameter tuning. Multilingual data may be present in the pretraining corpus of the language model and in the multimodal training. All models are trained for one epoch on the training set using bfloat16 format, low-rank adapters with alpha=32 and r=32 on the transformer layers from the language model and the final projection layer, and the paged_adamw_8bit optimizer. Training is conducted on an 8 GPU setup with data parallelism, a learning rate of 5e-5 with linear decay and 2.5% warmup steps, and a batch size of 32. The model is intended for use with colpali-engine version 0.3.1 or higher and transformers version greater than 4.45.0. The model primarily focuses on PDF-type documents and high-resource languages, which may limit its generalization to other document types or less represented languages. It relies on multi-vector retrieval derived from the ColBERT late interaction mechanism, which may require engineering efforts to adapt to widely used vector retrieval frameworks that lack native multi-vector support. The vision language backbone model (Qwen2.5-VL) is licensed under the Qwen RESEARCH LICENSE AGREEMENT, while the adapters are under the MIT license.

instruction: Based on the video, what might happen next in the scenario?
description: The Video-R1/Qwen2.5-VL-7B-COT-SFT is a supervised fine-tuning (SFT) cold start model trained using the Video-R1-COT-165k dataset. This intermediate checkpoint is intended to serve as the base model for further reinforcement learning (RL) training on the Video-R1-260k dataset to produce the final Video-R1 models. Video-R1 is the first systematic exploration of the R1 paradigm for incentivizing video reasoning within multimodal large language models (MLLMs), inspired by the success of DeepSeek-R1. It addresses key challenges in video reasoning, particularly the lack of temporal modeling and the scarcity of high-quality video-reasoning data. To tackle these issues, Video-R1 proposes the T-GRPO algorithm, an extension of GRPO that explicitly encourages models to leverage temporal information in videos for reasoning. It also strategically incorporates high-quality image-reasoning data into the training process. The model was trained on two newly constructed datasets: Video-R1-CoT-165k for SFT cold start and Video-R1-260k for RL training, both comprising image and video data. Experimental results demonstrate that Video-R1 achieves significant improvements on various video reasoning benchmarks, including VideoMMMU, VSI-Bench, MVBench, and TempCompass. Notably, Video-R1-7B has shown competitive performance, even surpassing proprietary models like GPT-4o on certain video spatial reasoning tasks.

instruction: I want to know if the same person is speaking throughout this file. Can you check?
description: The `community-1` speaker diarization pipeline processes mono audio sampled at 16kHz and outputs speaker diarization. It automatically downmixes stereo or multi-channel audio to mono by averaging the channels and resamples audio files to 16kHz if they are at a different rate. The model supports speaker assignment and counting, simpler reconciliation with transcription timestamps using speaker diarization, and local processing without an internet connection. It also optionally supports processing on the pyannoteAI cloud. The pipeline is designed for use with the `pyannote.audio` library and can be run locally on a computer. It is reported to perform better than the `speaker-diarization-3.1` model, with performance metrics in terms of error rates (in percent) on a large collection of academic benchmarks, including 11.7% for the general case, 20.3% for channel 1, 17.0% for IHM, 19.9% for SDM, 44.6% for another benchmark, 26.7% for a specific benchmark, 20.2% for another, 46.8% for a development set, 22.8% for a different benchmark, 20.8% for another, 8.9% for phase2, and 11.2% for v0.3. The model can be processed on GPU by moving the pipeline to a CUDA device. Audio files can be pre-loaded in memory for faster processing. Progress monitoring is supported through hooks. The number of speakers can be controlled using the `num_speakers`, `min_speakers`, and `max_speakers` options. The pipeline also provides an exclusive speaker diarization, which helps in reconciling fine-grained speaker diarization timestamps with transcription timestamps. For offline use, the pipeline can be cloned to disk and loaded without an internet connection. The model is based on a speaker segmentation model, a speaker embedding model, and a speaker clustering method. Training and tuning were supported by resources on a supercomputer.

instruction: Identify the objects in this picture that match the phrase 'a person wearing a blue hat and holding a camera'.
description: OWL-ViT, short for Vision Transformer for Open-World Localization, is a zero-shot text-conditioned object detection model that allows querying an image with one or multiple text queries. It was proposed by Matthias Minderer, Alexey Gritsenko, Austin Stone, Maxim Neumann, Dirk Weissenborn, Alexey Dosovitskiy, Aravindh Mahendran, Anurag Arnab, Mostafa Dehghani, Zhuoran Shen, Xiao Wang, Xiaohua Zhai, Thomas Kipf, and Neil Houlsby. OWL-ViT uses CLIP as its multi-modal backbone, combining a ViT-like Transformer for visual features and a causal language model for text features. To enable detection, the final token pooling layer of the vision model is removed, and a lightweight classification and box head is attached to each transformer output token. Open-vocabulary classification is achieved by replacing the fixed classification layer weights with class-name embeddings from the text model. The CLIP backbone is trained from scratch and fine-tuned end-to-end with the classification and box heads on standard detection datasets using a bipartite matching loss. The model uses a CLIP backbone with a ViT-B/32 Transformer architecture as an image encoder and a masked self-attention Transformer as a text encoder. These encoders are trained to maximize the similarity of (image, text) pairs via a contrastive loss. The CLIP backbone is trained from scratch and fine-tuned together with the box and class prediction heads with an object detection objective. The CLIP backbone of the model was trained on publicly available image-caption data, including data crawled from the internet and pre-existing image datasets. The prediction heads of OWL-ViT, along with the CLIP backbone, are fine-tuned on publicly available object detection datasets. The model is intended as a research output for the research community, enabling researchers to better understand and explore zero-shot, text-conditioned object detection. The primary intended users are AI researchers, who are expected to use the model to study robustness, generalization, and other capabilities, biases, and constraints of computer vision models.

instruction: Can you determine if this image shows a natural or man-made structure?
description: This is a CLIP ViT-bigG/14 model trained using the LAION-2B English subset of the LAION-5B dataset, with further training conducted by Mitchell Wortsman. The model is licensed under the MIT license. It is intended for research purposes, particularly for exploring zero-shot, arbitrary image classification and enabling interdisciplinary studies on the potential impact of such models. The model supports tasks such as zero-shot image classification, image and text retrieval, as well as downstream uses like image classification fine-tuning, linear probe image classification, and image generation guiding and conditioning. However, any deployed use case—whether commercial or not—is considered out of scope due to the high need for task-specific testing, especially given CLIP’s variable performance with different class taxonomies. Non-deployed use cases are also not recommended unless there is thorough in-domain testing with a specific, fixed class taxonomy. Use cases related to surveillance and facial recognition are always out of scope due to the lack of testing norms and checks for fair use. Additionally, the model has not been trained or evaluated on any languages other than English, so its use should be limited to English language contexts. The training data consists of a 2 billion sample English subset of LAION-5B, with partial fine-tuning on LAION-A, a 900 million subset of LAION-2B filtered for aesthetic quality and deduplicated. The dataset is uncurated and may contain discomforting or disturbing content, so caution is advised when using it. A “safe” subset can be extracted using a trained NSFW classifier, though harmful content may still be present. The model was evaluated using a combination of VTAB+ datasets for classification and COCO and Flickr for retrieval. It achieves an 80.1 zero-shot top-1 accuracy on ImageNet-1k. Initial benchmarks on a wider range of datasets are planned for release. The model was trained using compute resources provided by the cited contributors. The model is based on the OpenCLIP framework and builds upon the methodology described in the OpenAI CLIP paper. The training procedure will be detailed in an upcoming blog post on laion.ai.

instruction: What's the expected value when I apply these variables?
description: The model is a RandomForestRegressor, designed for regression tasks. It is trained using a procedure that includes hyperparameters such as bootstrap set to True, ccp_alpha set to 0.0, criterion set to squared_error, max_features set to 1.0, min_samples_leaf set to 1, min_samples_split set to 2, n_estimators set to 100, oob_score set to False, and verbose set to 0. The model is evaluated using the test split method, with the root mean squared error (rmse) as the metric. The evaluation results show an rmse value of 0.314915. The model is unfitted, as indicated by its visual representation and associated styling. No specific intended uses or limitations are provided. No information is given on how to get started with the model, the authors of the model card, contact channels, or citation details.

instruction: I have two images of a park from different angles; can you find the overlapping visual features?
description: The LightGlue model was proposed by Philipp Lindenberger, Paul-Edouard Sarlin, and Marc Pollefeys. It is a deep neural network designed to match local features across images. The model matches two sets of interest points detected in an image and, when paired with a keypoint detector, can be used to match two images and estimate the pose between them. It is useful for tasks such as image matching, homography estimation, and other multiple-view geometry problems. LightGlue builds on the success of SuperGlue and introduces improvements that make it more efficient in terms of memory and computation, more accurate, and easier to train. One key property is that LightGlue is adaptive to the difficulty of the problem: inference is faster on image pairs that are easier to match, such as those with a larger visual overlap or limited appearance change. The model can stop inference at an early layer if all predictions are ready, and it discards points that are deemed not matchable early from further steps. This adaptability makes LightGlue faster, more accurate, and easier to train than SuperGlue. LightGlue is developed by ETH Zurich - Computer Vision and Geometry Lab and is categorized as an image matching model. It is licensed for academic or non-profit, non-commercial research use only, implied by the use of SuperPoint as its keypoint detector. The model is designed for feature matching and pose estimation tasks in computer vision and can handle challenging real-world indoor and outdoor environments. However, it may not perform well on tasks that require different types of visual understanding, such as object detection or image classification. LightGlue is trained on large annotated datasets for pose estimation, enabling it to learn priors for pose estimation and reason about the 3D scene. The training data consists of image pairs with ground truth correspondences and unmatched keypoints derived from ground truth poses and depth maps. LightGlue follows the supervised training setup of SuperGlue, first pre-trained with synthetic homographies sampled from 1 million images and then fine-tuned with the MegaDepth dataset, which includes 1 million crowd-sourced images of 196 tourism landmarks with camera calibration and poses recovered by SfM and dense depth by multi-view stereo. LightGlue is trained using the fp32 training regime. It is designed to be efficient and runs in real-time on a modern GPU, with a forward pass taking approximately 44 milliseconds (22 FPS) for an image pair. The model has 13.7 million parameters, making it relatively compact compared to some other deep learning models. Its inference speed is suitable for real-time applications and can be readily integrated into modern Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM) or Structure-from-Motion (SfM) systems.

instruction: Make an image of a bustling medieval marketplace with merchants and knights.
description: HiDream-I1 is a new open-source image generative foundation model with 17B parameters that achieves state-of-the-art image generation quality within seconds. It produces exceptional results across multiple styles including photorealistic, cartoon, and artistic. The model achieves a state-of-the-art HPS v2.1 score, which aligns with human preferences. It also achieves industry-leading scores on the GenEval and DPG benchmarks, outperforming all other open-source models. The model is released under the MIT license to foster scientific advancement and enable creative innovation. Generated images can be freely used for personal projects, scientific research, and commercial applications. The model includes a VAE component from FLUX.1 [schnell], licensed under Apache 2.0, and text encoders from google/t5-v1_1-xxl (licensed under Apache 2.0) and meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct (licensed under the Llama 3.1 Community License Agreement). The Transformer models in the repository are licensed under the MIT License. Users own all content they create with this model and can use their generated content freely, but must comply with the license agreement. Users are responsible for how they use the models and must not create illegal content, harmful material, personal information that could harm others, false information, or content targeting vulnerable groups.

instruction: Write a dialogue between a customer and a shop assistant discussing product features.
description: BLOOMZ and mT0 are families of models designed to follow human instructions in dozens of languages in a zero-shot manner. These models are finetuned from BLOOM and mT5, which are pretrained multilingual language models, on a crosslingual task mixture called xP3. The resulting models demonstrate the ability to generalize across unseen tasks and languages. The BLOOMZ and mT0 models are available in various parameter sizes, including 300M, 580M, 1.2B, 3.7B, 13B, 560M, 1.1B, 1.7B, 3B, 7.1B, and 176B parameters. Some models are multitask finetuned on xP3mt for non-English prompting, while others are multitask finetuned on P3 for research purposes, though these are strictly inferior to the main models. The original pretrained checkpoints are not recommended for use. The models are intended for performing tasks expressed in natural language, such as translation, generating related search terms, writing stories, and explaining technical concepts in different languages. The performance of the models may depend on the clarity of the prompt, and it is recommended to clearly indicate when the input ends to avoid the model continuing the input. For BLOOMZ models, it is advised to provide as much context as possible, especially when specifying the language of the output. The models were finetuned for 1750 steps on 3.67 billion tokens using a layout of 1x pipeline parallel, 1x tensor parallel, and 1x data parallel, with training conducted in float16 precision. The training was carried out on hardware including AMD CPUs with 512GB memory per node and 64 A100 80GB GPUs with 8 GPUs per node, using NVLink 4 inter-gpu connections and 4 OmniPath links. Communication was handled via an NCCL-communications network with a fully dedicated subnet. Evaluation results are detailed in Table 7 of the associated paper, with zero-shot performance reported for the best prompt per dataset configuration.

instruction: Detect and label the people and objects in this crowded market scene.
description: The D-FINE model was proposed by Yansong Peng, Hebei Li, Peixi Wu, Yueyi Zhang, Xiaoyan Sun, and Feng Wu. This is the Hugging Face Transformers implementation for D-FINE. D-FINE is a real-time object detector that achieves outstanding localization precision by redefining the bounding box regression task in DETR models. The model comprises two key components: Fine-grained Distribution Refinement (FDR) and Global Optimal Localization Self-Distillation (GO-LSD). D-FINE is trained on COCO (Lin et al. [2014]) train2017 and validated on COCO val2017 dataset. The standard AP metrics are reported, averaged over uniformly sampled IoU thresholds ranging from 0.50 to 0.95 with a step size of 0.05, and APval5000 is also reported, which is commonly used in real scenarios. D-FINE is ideal for real-time object detection in diverse applications such as autonomous driving, surveillance systems, robotics, and retail analytics. Its enhanced flexibility and deployment-friendly design make it suitable for both edge devices and large-scale systems, ensuring high accuracy and speed in dynamic, real-world environments. Variants of the model include 'coco' trained on COCO, 'obj365' trained on Object365, and 'obj2coco' trained on Object365 and then fine-tuned on COCO.

instruction: I want to create a painting-style image from the edges in my sketch—can you help?
description: ControlNet v1.1 is a neural network structure designed to control diffusion models by adding extra conditions. This version is specifically conditioned on Canny edges, which are represented as a monochrome image with white edges on a black background. ControlNet was proposed by Lvmin Zhang and Maneesh Agrawala. The model is intended to be used in combination with Stable Diffusion and is a diffusion-based text-to-image generation model. It was developed to support additional input conditions in a task-specific manner, with the learning process being robust even when the training dataset is small. The Canny version of ControlNet v1.1 was trained to address several issues present in the previous version, including duplicated grayscale human images, low-quality or blurry images with JPEG artifacts, and incorrect paired prompts. The training dataset was improved, and the model was trained on a machine with 8 Nvidia A100 80G GPUs for 3 days with a batch size of 256, costing 2160 USD. The model was resumed from Canny 1.0 and includes data augmentations such as random left-right flipping. Although evaluating ControlNet is challenging, Canny 1.1 is reported to be slightly more robust and to produce slightly higher visual quality than Canny 1.0.

instruction: Could you analyze the emotional tone of this French customer complaint?
description: This model is a fine-tuned version of `distilbert/distilbert-base-multilingual-cased` for multilingual sentiment analysis. It is designed to classify text into one of five sentiment classes: Very Negative, Negative, Neutral, Positive, and Very Positive. The model supports 23 languages, including English, Chinese, Spanish, Hindi, Arabic, Bengali, Portuguese, Russian, Japanese, German, Malay, Telugu, Vietnamese, Korean, French, Turkish, Italian, Polish, Ukrainian, Tagalog, Dutch, Swiss German, and Swahili. It is intended for use in multilingual social media monitoring, international customer feedback analysis, global product review sentiment classification, and worldwide brand sentiment tracking. The model was trained exclusively on synthetic multilingual data generated by advanced LLMs, ensuring wide coverage of sentiment expressions from various languages. The training procedure involved fine-tuning for 3.5 epochs, achieving a train_acc_off_by_one of approximately 0.93 on the validation dataset. The model leverages synthetic data from multiple sources to achieve robust performance across different languages and cultural contexts. While synthetic data reduces bias, validation in real-world scenarios is advised. The model can be used for commercial purposes with a license obtained by contacting info@tabularis.ai.

instruction: What are the most important details in this legal document?
description: Gemma is a family of lightweight, state-of-the-art open models from Google, built from the same research and technology used to create the Gemini models. Gemma 3 models are multimodal, handling text and image input and generating text output, with open weights for both pre-trained variants and instruction-tuned variants. Gemma 3 has a large, 128K context window, multilingual support in over 140 languages, and is available in more sizes than previous versions. Gemma 3 models are well-suited for a variety of text generation and image understanding tasks, including question answering, summarization, and reasoning. Their relatively small size makes it possible to deploy them in environments with limited resources such as laptops, desktops or your own cloud infrastructure, democratizing access to state of the art AI models and helping foster innovation for everyone. The input includes text strings, such as a question, a prompt, or a document to be summarized, and images normalized to 896 x 896 resolution and encoded to 256 tokens each. The total input context is 128K tokens for the 4B, 12B, and 27B sizes, and 32K tokens for the 1B size. The output is generated text in response to the input, such as an answer to a question, analysis of image content, or a summary of a document, with a total output context of 8192 tokens. The models were trained on a dataset of text data that includes a wide variety of sources. The 27B model was trained with 14 trillion tokens, the 12B model with 12 trillion tokens, the 4B model with 4 trillion tokens, and the 1B model with 2 trillion tokens. The training data includes web documents, code, mathematics, and images. Web documents ensure the model is exposed to a broad range of linguistic styles, topics, and vocabulary, with content in over 140 languages. Code helps the model learn the syntax and patterns of programming languages, improving its ability to generate code and understand code-related questions. Mathematics helps the model learn logical reasoning, symbolic representation, and to address mathematical queries. Images enable the model to perform image analysis and visual data extraction tasks. Rigorous CSAM filtering was applied at multiple stages in the data preparation process to ensure the exclusion of harmful and illegal content. Automated techniques were used to filter out certain personal information and other sensitive data from training sets. Additional methods were applied for content quality and safety in line with Google's policies. Gemma was trained using Tensor Processing Unit (TPU) hardware (TPUv4p, TPUv5p, and TPUv5e). TPUs are specifically designed to handle the massive computations involved in training vision-language models (VLMs), offering performance, memory, scalability, and cost-effectiveness advantages. Training was done using JAX and ML Pathways. JAX allows researchers to take advantage of the latest generation of hardware, including TPUs, for faster and more efficient training of large models. ML Pathways is Google's effort to build artificially intelligent systems capable of generalizing across multiple tasks, suitable for foundation models like these. The 'single controller' programming model of JAX and Pathways allows a single Python process to orchestrate the entire training run, simplifying the development workflow. These models were evaluated against a large collection of different datasets and metrics to cover different aspects of text generation. In reasoning and factuality, the models achieved high scores on benchmarks such as HellaSwag, BoolQ, PIQA, SocialIQA, TriviaQA, Natural Questions, ARC-c, ARC-e, WinoGrande, BIG-Bench Hard, and DROP. In STEM and code, the models performed well on MMLU, AGIEval, MATH, GSM8K, GPQA, MBPP, and HumanEval. In multilingual tasks, the models showed strong performance on MGSM, Global-MMLU-Lite, WMT24++, FloRes, XQuAD, ECLeKTic, and IndicGenBench. In multimodal tasks, the models achieved high scores on COCOcap, DocVQA, InfoVQA, MMMU, TextVQA, RealWorldQA, ReMI, AI2D, ChartQA, VQAv2, BLINK, OKVQA, TallyQA, SpatialSense VQA, and CountBenchQA. The evaluation methods included structured evaluations and internal red-teaming testing of relevant content policies. Red-teaming was conducted by different teams with different goals and human evaluation metrics. The models were evaluated against categories such as child safety, content safety, and representational harms. For all areas of safety testing, the models showed major improvements in the categories of child safety, content safety, and representational harms relative to previous Gemma models. All testing was conducted without safety filters to evaluate the model capabilities and behaviors. For both text-to-text and image-to-text, and across all model sizes, the model produced minimal policy violations and showed significant improvements over previous Gemma models' performance with respect to ungrounded inferences. A limitation of the evaluations was that they included only English language prompts. These models have certain limitations that users should be aware of. The quality and diversity of the training data significantly influence the model's capabilities. Biases or gaps in the training data can lead to limitations in the model's responses. The scope of the training dataset determines the subject areas the model can handle effectively. Models are better at tasks that can be framed with clear prompts and instructions. Open-ended or highly complex tasks might be challenging. A model's performance can be influenced by the amount of context provided (longer context generally leads to better outputs, up to a certain point). Natural language is inherently complex. Models might struggle to grasp subtle nuances, sarcasm, or figurative language. Models generate responses based on information they learned from their training datasets, but they are not knowledge bases. They may generate incorrect or outdated factual statements. Models rely on statistical patterns in language. They might lack the ability to apply common sense reasoning in certain situations. The development of vision-language models (VLMs) raises several ethical concerns. VLMs trained on large-scale, real-world text and image data can reflect socio-cultural biases embedded in the training material. These models underwent careful scrutiny, input data pre-processing, and posterior evaluations. VLMs can be misused to generate text that is false, misleading, or harmful. Guidelines are provided for responsible use with the model. This model card summarizes details on the models' architecture, capabilities, limitations, and evaluation processes. A responsibly developed open model offers the opportunity to share innovation by making VLM technology accessible to developers and researchers across the AI ecosystem. Risks identified include the perpetuation of biases, generation of harmful content, misuse for malicious purposes, and privacy violations. It is encouraged to perform continuous monitoring and the exploration of de-biasing techniques during model training, fine-tuning, and other use cases. Mechanisms and guidelines for content safety are essential. Developers are encouraged to exercise caution and implement appropriate content safety safeguards based on their specific product policies and application use cases. Technical limitations and developer and end-user education can help mitigate against malicious applications of VLMs. Educational resources and reporting mechanisms for users to flag misuse are provided. Prohibited uses of Gemma models are outlined in the Gemma Prohibited Use Policy. Models were trained on data filtered for removal of certain personal information and other sensitive data. Developers are encouraged to adhere to privacy regulations with privacy-preserving techniques. At the time of release, this family of models provides high-performance open vision-language model implementations designed from the ground up for responsible AI development compared to similarly sized models. Using the benchmark evaluation metrics described in this document, these models have shown to provide superior performance to other, comparably-sized open model alternatives.

description: DSE-QWen2-2b-MRL-V1 is a bi-encoder model designed to encode document screenshots into dense vectors for document retrieval. The Document Screenshot Embedding approach captures documents in their original visual format, preserving all information such as text, images, and layout, thus avoiding tedious parsing and potential information loss. DSE aims to provide a generalizable embedding model for Text, PDF documents, Webpage, and Slides retrieval. For example, DSE-QWen2-2b-MRL-V1 achieves 85.8 nDCG@5 on a leaderboard. The QWen vision encoder may require high GPU memory if the input image is large. Users should adjust the 'resized_height' and 'resized_width' parameters to fit VRAM based on GPU resources. This checkpoint is trained to support flexible representation dimension and flexible input image size. The model can encode both document screenshots and document text inputs, with the latter being warmed up using the Tevatron/msmarco-passage-aug dataset. The model is intended to be used for encoding text queries and document screenshots into embeddings, after which similarity scores can be computed using cosine similarity. The model is based on the Qwen2VLForConditionalGeneration architecture and uses the AutoProcessor for input processing. The model supports adjusting the embedding dimensionality for an effectiveness-efficiency trade-off. The model is available for use with the Hugging Face Transformers library and requires specific parameters for loading and processing inputs. The model is evaluated on a retrieval task and achieves a performance score of 85.8 nDCG@5. Users are advised to consider GPU memory constraints when using large input images and to adjust the input size accordingly. The model can be cited in academic or research contexts by referencing QWen2, Docmatix, ViDoRe, and the DSE work.

description: TableLLM is a large language model designed to handle tabular data manipulation tasks efficiently in real office usage scenarios. It is capable of generating either a code solution or a direct text answer, depending on the context of the task. Code generation is used for spreadsheet-embedded tabular data, involving operations such as insert, delete, update, query, merge, and plot. Text generation is used for document-embedded tabular data, typically involving query operations on short tables. The model is available in two distinct scales, which are fine-tuned based on different base models. Evaluation results show that TableLLM-13B achieves 62.4% on WikiTQ, 68.2% on TAT-QA, 74.5% on FeTaQA, 62.5% on OTTQA, 90.7% on WikiSQL, 83.4% on Spider, and 80.8% on a self-created benchmark, with an overall average of 74.7%. These results outperform several other models, including Llama2-Chat (13B) and CodeLlama (13B), and are competitive with GPT-4. The model is quantized using llama.cpp for efficient deployment. The prompt templates for code solution generation and text answer generation are provided, with examples of how the input data is formatted. For more details about how to use TableLLM, users are directed to the model's GitHub page.
domain: Natural Language Processing Table Question Answering

description: ModernBERT-Ja-70M is a variant of the BERT model that combines local and global attention mechanisms, enabling it to process long sequences while maintaining computational efficiency. It incorporates modern architectural improvements. The model is trained on a high-quality corpus of Japanese and English text containing 4.39T tokens, with a vocabulary size of 102,400 and a sequence length of 8,192 tokens. It is designed for masked language modeling and is intended to be fine-tuned for downstream tasks. It is not suitable for text generation, and users are advised to use a dedicated text generation model for such purposes. The model uses a unigram language model tokenizer with byte fallback, and does not apply pre-tokenization for Japanese, allowing raw sentences to be input directly. However, the token boundaries often do not align with morpheme boundaries, which may lead to poor performance in token classification tasks such as named entity recognition and span extraction. The model was constructed through a three-stage training process: pre-training with 3.51T tokens using a sequence length of 1,024 and a masking rate of 30%, followed by two phases of context length extension. In the first context extension phase, it was trained with 430B tokens at a sequence length of 8,192 and a masking rate of 30%. In the second phase, it was trained with 450B tokens, including 150B tokens of high-quality Japanese data, at a sequence length of 8,192 and a reduced masking rate of 15%. The model has 70M total parameters, with 31M parameters excluding the embedding layer. It has a hidden dimension of 384, an intermediate dimension of 1,536, and 13 layers. The attention configuration includes one global attention layer and two local attention layers. The sliding window attention window context size is 128, with global_rope_theta set to 160,000 and local_rope_theta set to 10,000. The model was evaluated on 12 datasets, including JGLUE, across various tasks such as knowledge-based tasks, Japanese linguistic acceptability classification, natural language inference, semantic textual similarity, and various classification tasks. These tasks are short-sequence evaluations with a maximum sequence length not exceeding 512. The model achieved an average performance of 86.77 across these tasks. The model is available for use with the transformers library version 4.48.0 or higher, and it is recommended to use Flash Attention 2 if supported by the GPU. The model may produce representations that reflect biases, and it may generate biased or harmful expressions when used for masked language modeling.

instruction: Identify any conserved motifs in the given RNA sequence.
description: RNAErnie is a pre-trained model on non-coding RNA (ncRNA) using a multi-stage masked language modeling (MLM) objective. It is designed to support various RNA-related tasks, including masked language modeling, sequence classification or regression, token classification or regression, and contact classification or regression. The model is intended to be used as a backbone for fine-tuning on downstream tasks. RNAErnie is a -style model pre-trained on a large corpus of non-coding RNA sequences in a self-supervised fashion, meaning it was trained on the raw nucleotides of RNA sequences only, with an automatic process to generate inputs and labels from those texts. The model has 12 layers, a hidden size of 768, 12 attention heads, an intermediate size of 3072, 86.06 million parameters, 22.37 gigaflops, 11.17 gigamacs, and a maximum of 512 tokens. It was trained using a 3-stage training pipeline with different masking strategies: base-level masking (each nucleotide in the sequence), subsequence-level masking (subsequences of 4-8bp in the sequence), and motif-level masking (motif datasets). The training data was a subset of RNAcentral containing 23 million sequences. During preprocessing, all tokens were preprocessed by replacing 'T's with 'S's, and the tokenizer may convert 'T's to 'U's, which can be disabled. The model was trained on 4 NVIDIA V100 GPUs with 32GiB memories, using a batch size of 50, 2,580,000 steps, the AdamW optimizer, a learning rate of 1e-4, a learning rate warm-up of 129,000 steps, a learning rate cool-down of 129,000 steps, a minimum learning rate of 5e-5, and a weight decay of 0.01. It is important to note that this is an unofficial implementation of RNAErnie, and the MultiMolecule team is unable to confirm that the provided model and checkpoints are producing the same intermediate representations as the original implementation. The model is licensed under the AGPL-3.0-or-later license.

instruction: Create a realistic face of someone with glasses and a neutral expression.
description: This model is a denoising diffusion probabilistic model (DDPM) trained on the CelebA HQ dataset at a resolution of 256x256. It is based on the original `google/ddpm-celebahq-256` implementation and has been updated to support the safetensors format for model storage. The model type is `UNet2DModel`, and it uses the DDPM diffusion process. The model is pretrained on the CelebA HQ dataset and is designed for high-quality image generation. The model weights are available in the safetensors format, which is a safer and more efficient format. The model can be loaded using the `diffusers` library from Hugging Face in safetensors format. The model supports discrete noise schedulers such as DDPM, DDIM, and PNDM for inference. The DDPM scheduler yields the highest quality but takes the longest to run, while the DDIM and PNDM schedulers offer a good trade-off between quality and inference speed. The model can be used to generate images by initializing the model and scheduler, creating an image with Gaussian noise, and performing a reverse diffusion process. The model weights are stored in the safetensors format, and the model configuration, scheduler configuration, and environment file are available for download. The model is provided under a license, and if used in research or projects, the original `google/ddpm-celebahq-256` repository should be cited.
