description: This is an xlm-roberta-large model that has been fine-tuned on multilingual (English, Danish, Hungarian) training data labeled with CAP codes. The model is designed for text classification tasks and supports the processing of English, Danish, and Hungarian text. It uses the sentencepiece tokenizer and is intended to be used with the Transformers library. The model was evaluated on a test set consisting of 165,443 English and Hungarian examples, achieving an accuracy of 0.87 and a weighted average F1-score of 0.86. Due to gated access, a token must be provided when loading the model, using either the `token` or `use_auth_token` parameter depending on the Transformers package version. The model is used by an open-source and free natural language processing tool designed to simplify and speed up projects for comparative research. Model performance can be improved by extending the training sets with CAP-coded corpora in any domain and language, which can be submitted via email or through a specified platform. If a RuntimeError occurs when loading the model using the from_pretrained() method, adding `ignore_mismatched_sizes=True` should resolve the issue. Additionally, for versions of Transformers prior to 4.27, the sentencepiece tokenizer must be installed manually.

description: This model is a fine-tuned version of an unspecified base model on an unknown dataset. It is designed to perform document question-answering tasks. The model was trained using the following hyperparameters: a learning rate of 5e-05, a training batch size of 4, an evaluation batch size of 8, a random seed of 42, the Adam optimizer with betas of (0.9, 0.999) and an epsilon of 1e-08, a linear learning rate scheduler, and a total of 20 training epochs. instruction: How would you score this reply in terms of helpfulness and correctness for a coding task?
description: The Skywork Reward Model Series includes two advanced reward models built on the Llama-3.1 and Gemma-2 architectures. These models were trained using the Skywork Reward Preference dataset, version v0.2, which contains 80K high-quality preference pairs sourced from publicly available data. The dataset was curated to include high-quality preference pairs and to target specific capability and knowledge domains, such as mathematics, coding, and safety. The models were developed to demonstrate that high-performance reward models can be achieved with a relatively small dataset and straightforward data curation techniques, without further algorithmic or architectural modifications. The resulting reward models excel at handling preferences in complex scenarios, including challenging preference pairs. During dataset curation, top samples from math, code, and other categories in the combined Magpie dataset were selected based on the average ArmoRM score. Adjustments were made to prioritize certain subsets, and a reward model was used to score and filter samples from the WildGuard dataset to improve performance in the Safety domain. The models were evaluated on the RewardBench leaderboard, where Skywork-Reward-Llama-3.1-8B-v0.2 ranks first among 8B models as of October 2024. The models are designed to provide reward scores for conversations, with the 27B model requiring the `flash_attention_2` or `eager` implementation for optimal performance. Users are advised to use the v0.2 version of both the dataset and models to avoid contamination issues. The models are available for both research and commercial use, subject to the terms and conditions outlined in the license agreement. The Skywork model should not be used for activities that pose a threat to national or societal security or engage in unlawful actions. Users are requested not to deploy the model for internet services without appropriate security reviews and records. The model developers disclaim any responsibility for issues arising from the use of the model, including data security issues, public opinion risks, or any risks from the model being misled, abused, or improperly utilized.

description: This model is based on the RoBERTa transformer architecture and is designed for metagenomic analysis. It uses a custom k-mer tokenizer with a k-mer length of 6 and overlapping tokens. The model was trained for one epoch on V100 GPUs using a diverse dataset of 220 million 400bp fragments from 18,000 bacterial and archaeal genomes. The model supports tasks involving the analysis of gene sequences, including tokenization, embedding generation, and computation of mean sequence embeddings. It is intended to be used with a custom K-mer Tokenizer and the Hugging Face Transformers library. The model is described in the paper 'Leveraging Large Language Models for Metagenomic Analysis' by Refahi, M.S., Sokhansanj, B.A., & Rosen, G.L. (2023), published in IEEE SPMB.

description: This model is based on the DeBERTa-v3-base-mnli model from the MoritzLaurer/DeBERTa-v3-base-mnli repository, with an added handler.py file to facilitate its use in inference endpoints for zero-shot classification tasks. It is designed to compare a premise and a hypothesis and classify their relationship as entailment, neutral, or contradiction. The model was trained on the MultiNLI dataset, which contains 392,702 natural language inference (NLI) hypothesis-premise pairs. The v3 variant of DeBERTa improves upon previous versions by incorporating a different pre-training objective, as detailed in the original DeBERTa paper. The model was trained using the Hugging Face trainer with the following hyperparameters: 5 training epochs, a learning rate of 2e-05, a per-device batch size of 32 for both training and evaluation, a warmup ratio of 0.1, a weight decay of 0.06, and mixed precision training (fp16). It was evaluated on the matched test set and achieved an accuracy of 0.90. The model is intended for use in natural language inference tasks and can be used via the Hugging Face Transformers library. However, it is noted that older versions of the Transformers library may have compatibility issues with this model, and using Transformers==4.13 may resolve some problems. The model has been tested on various datasets and achieved an average score of 80.01, outperforming the microsoft/deberta-v3-base model, which scored 79.04. As of 09/01/2023, it is ranked 1st among all tested models for the microsoft/deberta-v3-base architecture. Users are advised to consult the original DeBERTa paper and literature on NLI datasets for potential biases and limitations.

description: This model is a fine-tuned XLM-RoBERTa transformer model with a classification head on top, designed for language detection tasks. It supports sequence classification for 20 languages: arabic (ar), bulgarian (bg), german (de), modern greek (el), english (en), spanish (es), french (fr), hindi (hi), italian (it), japanese (ja), dutch (nl), polish (pl), portuguese (pt), russian (ru), swahili (sw), thai (th), turkish (tr), urdu (ur), vietnamese (vi), and chinese (zh). The model was fine-tuned on a dataset containing text sequences in these 20 languages, with 70k samples in the training set and 10k each in the validation and test sets. The average accuracy on the test set is 99.6%, which matches the average macro/weighted F1-score due to the test set being perfectly balanced. For each of the 20 languages, the model achieves the following test set performance: arabic (precision 0.998, recall 0.996, F1-score 0.997, support 500), bulgarian (precision 0.998, recall 0.964, F1-score 0.981, support 500), german (precision 0.998, recall 0.996, F1-score 0.997, support 500), modern greek (precision 0.996, recall 1.000, F1-score 0.998, support 500), english (precision 1.000, recall 1.000, F1-score 1.000, support 500), spanish (precision 0.967, recall 1.000, F1-score 0.983, support 500), french (precision 1.000, recall 1.000, F1-score 1.000, support 500), hindi (precision 0.994, recall 0.992, F1-score 0.993, support 500), italian (precision 1.000, recall 0.992, F1-score 0.996, support 500), japanese (precision 0.996, recall 0.996, F1-score 0.996, support 500), dutch (precision 1.000, recall 1.000, F1-score 1.000, support 500), polish (precision 1.000, recall 1.000, F1-score 1.000, support 500), portuguese (precision 0.988, recall 1.000, F1-score 0.994, support 500), russian (precision 1.000, recall 0.994, F1-score 0.997, support 500), swahili (precision 1.000, recall 1.000, F1-score 1.000, support 500), thai (precision 1.000, recall 0.998, F1-score 0.999, support 500), turkish (precision 0.994, recall 0.992, F1-score 0.993, support 500), urdu (precision 1.000, recall 1.000, F1-score 1.000, support 500), vietnamese (precision 0.992, recall 1.000, F1-score 0.996, support 500), and chinese (precision 1.000, recall 1.000, F1-score 1.000, support 500). When compared to the Python library langid, which was constrained to the same 20 languages, the model achieves an average accuracy of 98.5% on the test set. The model was fine-tuned using the Trainer API with the following hyperparameters: learning rate 2e-05, train batch size 64, eval batch size 128, seed 42, optimizer Adam with betas (0.9, 0.999) and epsilon 1e-08, lr scheduler type linear, number of epochs 2, and mixed precision training using Native AMP. The validation results on the valid split of the Language Identification dataset are as follows: after the first epoch, the training loss was 0.2492, validation loss 0.0149, accuracy 0.9969, and F1 0.9969; after the second epoch, the training loss was 0.0101, validation loss 0.0103, accuracy 0.9977, and F1 0.9977. The model was developed using the following framework versions: Transformers 4.12.5, PyTorch 1.10.0+cu111, Datasets 1.15.1, and Tokenizers 0.10.3.

description: BLIP-2 is a multimodal model consisting of three components: a CLIP-like image encoder, a Querying Transformer (Q-Former), and a large language model. The image encoder and large language model are initialized from pre-trained checkpoints and remain frozen during training, while the Q-Former, a BERT-like Transformer encoder, is trained to map a set of 'query tokens' to query embeddings that bridge the embedding spaces of the image encoder and the language model. The model is designed to predict the next text token given the query embeddings and the previous text. It supports tasks such as image captioning, visual question answering (VQA), and chat-like conversations by providing the image and previous conversation as input. BLIP-2 uses the Flan-T5-xxl model as its pre-trained language model, which is not fine-tuned during training. The model inherits the risks and limitations of the Flan-T5 model, including potential for harmful language generation. It is fine-tuned on image-text datasets collected from the internet, which may introduce biases or inappropriate content. BLIP-2 has not been tested in real-world applications and should not be directly deployed without a careful assessment of safety and fairness in the specific deployment context. This release is intended for research purposes only and is not evaluated for all downstream applications. Users are advised to evaluate and address potential concerns related to accuracy, safety, and fairness before deployment. The model can be used for conditional text generation given an image and optional text input.

description: The DETR (DEtection TRansformer) model is an encoder-decoder transformer with a convolutional backbone, specifically a ResNet-101 architecture. It is designed for end-to-end object detection and was trained on the COCO 2017 dataset, which includes 118,000 annotated images for training and 5,000 for validation. The model incorporates two heads on top of the decoder outputs: a linear layer for class label prediction and a multi-layer perceptron (MLP) for bounding box prediction. It uses 100 object queries to detect objects in an image, with each query searching for a specific object. The model is trained using a bipartite matching loss, which compares predicted classes and bounding boxes to ground truth annotations. The Hungarian matching algorithm is used to create a one-to-one mapping between queries and annotations, and the model is optimized using cross-entropy for class prediction and a combination of L1 and generalized IoU loss for bounding box regression. The model achieves an average precision (AP) of 43.5 on the COCO 2017 validation set. It is intended for object detection tasks and supports PyTorch. The model was trained for 300 epochs on 16 V100 GPUs, with a batch size of 64 and 4 images per GPU. Images were preprocessed by resizing and normalizing with the ImageNet mean and standard deviation. The model was introduced in the paper 'End-to-End Object Detection with Transformers' by Carion et al. and first released by the Hugging Face team, as the original model card was not written by the model's creators.

description: This is a model that maps sentences and paragraphs to a 768-dimensional dense vector space, suitable for tasks such as clustering or semantic search. The model is designed to be used as a sentence and short paragraph encoder, generating vectors that capture semantic information for applications like information retrieval, clustering, or sentence similarity. Input text longer than 128 word pieces is truncated by default. The model was developed using a self-supervised contrastive learning objective, where the model is trained to predict which sentence from a set of randomly sampled sentences was actually paired with a given sentence in the training data. The model was fine-tuned on a dataset containing over 1 billion sentence pairs, sourced from multiple datasets and weighted according to a configuration file. The training was conducted on a TPU v3-8 for 920,000 steps with a batch size of 512 (64 per TPU core), using a learning rate of 2e-5 and the AdamW optimizer. The sequence length was limited to 128 tokens, and a learning rate warm-up of 500 steps was applied. The model was developed as part of a project supported by efficient hardware infrastructure and guidance from Google's Flax, JAX, and Cloud teams. The full training script is available in the repository as `train_script.py`. The training data includes a wide range of sources such as citation pairs (abstracts and titles), duplicate question pairs, (question, answer) pairs, (title, body) pairs, (title, answer) pairs, triplets, image captions, and more, with a total of 1,124,818,467 training tuples. The model can be used with the Sentence-Transformers library or directly with HuggingFace Transformers, requiring tokenization, model inference, and pooling operations to generate normalized sentence embeddings.

description: EraX-VL-7B-V1.5 is a robust multimodal model designed for OCR (optical character recognition) and VQA (visual question-answering), with a focus on Vietnamese and multilingual capabilities. It is particularly effective in recognizing and extracting information from various types of documents, including medical forms, invoices, bills of sale, quotes, and medical records. The model is intended to support applications in hospitals, clinics, insurance companies, and similar domains. EraX-VL-7B-V1.5 is built upon a high-quality, Vietnamese-fluent base model and has been fine-tuned to enhance its performance. It is a multimodal Transformer model with over 7B parameters and is open-source under the Apache 2.0 license. The model is not an OCR-only tool like Tesseract but a multimodal LLM-based model that requires careful prompting for optimal use. It is not yet fine-tuned on medical (X-ray) datasets or car accident data, but updates are planned for early 2025. EraX-VL-7B-V1.5 supports multi-turn Q&A with strong reasoning capabilities and is part of the EraX's LànhGPT collection of LLM models. It was developed by Nguyễn Anh Nguyên, Nguyễn Hồ Nam, Phạm Huỳnh Nhật, and Phạm Đình Thục, and is funded by EraX. The model achieved a VI-MTVQA score of 47.2, outperforming several models of similar or larger size. It supports tasks such as OCR for multi-images, image captioning, and multi-turn visual question answering and reasoning. Examples of supported OCR tasks include extracting information from citizen identification cards, driver's licenses, vehicle registration certificates, receipts, and medical records. The model can also generate image captions and perform multi-turn visual question answering, including extracting patient information, diagnoses, medical history, test results, and medication costs from medical documents. The model is designed to be used with Python, requiring specific packages and a base64-encoded image input for inference. The model's performance is evaluated using the EraX-MTVQA-Benchmark, and the test code is available in the EraX-JS-Company/EraX-MTVQA-Benchmark repository. API access inquiries should be directed to nguyen@erax.ai.

instruction: Find the document in the database that best matches the query 'What are the challenges of adopting AI in healthcare?'
description: The gte-v1.5 series is an upgraded version of the gte embeddings, supporting a context length of up to 8192 while further enhancing model performance. These models are built upon the transformer++ encoder, which combines BERT, RoPE, and GLU components. The gte-v1.5 series achieves state-of-the-art scores on the MTEB benchmark within the same model size category and provides competitive results on the LoCo long-context retrieval tests. The models are developed by the Institute for Intelligent Computing, Alibaba Group, and are designed for text embeddings. The gte-v1.5 series includes multiple models with varying language support, model sizes, maximum sequence lengths, and embedding dimensions. For example, one model supports multiple languages with a model size of 7720 million parameters, a maximum sequence length of 32768, and an embedding dimension of 4096, achieving an MTEB-en score of 67.34 and a LoCo score of 87.57. Another model is specifically for English with a model size of 434 million parameters, a maximum sequence length of 8192, and an embedding dimension of 1024, achieving an MTEB-en score of 65.39 and a LoCo score of 86.71. A third English model has a model size of 137 million parameters, a maximum sequence length of 8192, and an embedding dimension of 768, achieving an MTEB-en score of 64.11 and a LoCo score of 87.44. The training process involves a multi-stage strategy to support the long context length of 8192. The model first undergoes masked language modeling (MLM) pre-training on the c4-en dataset, followed by weak-supervised contrastive pre-training (CPT) and supervised contrastive fine-tuning. The MLM pre-training includes stages with different learning rates, batch sizes, and sequence lengths. The model is evaluated on the MTEB and LoCo benchmarks, achieving high scores across various tasks. The gte-v1.5 model is available for use with the transformers and sentence-transformers libraries, and can be accessed via the model path 'Alibaba-NLP/gte-base-en-v1.5'. It is recommended to install xformers and enable unpadding for acceleration. The model can be used for tasks such as text embedding generation, similarity computation, and retrieval. The model is described as a SOTA instruction-tuned multi-lingual embedding model that ranked 2nd in MTEB and 1st in C-MTEB.

instruction: I need a way to automatically tag this collection of music files with relevant metadata.
description: The m-a-p model family is a series of pre-trained models for music audio understanding, developed with various pre-training paradigms and datasets. The models are designed to support a range of music-related tasks and are optimized for generalization across different functions. The MERT-v1 model, an updated version of MERT-v0, introduces several improvements including the use of 8 codebooks for pseudo labels, which enhances the model's ability to support music generation. It also incorporates in-batch noise mixture during MLM prediction, is trained with higher audio frequency (24K Hz), and uses a larger dataset of up to 160,000 hours of audio. The model is available in multiple sizes, including 95M and 330M parameters, to accommodate different hardware capabilities. The architecture is based on transformer layers, with the number of layers and feature dimensions varying across models. The feature rate, or the number of features output per second of audio, and the sample rate, or the frequency of audio the model is trained with, are also key configuration parameters. The models are evaluated based on their performance on downstream tasks, with larger models generally outperforming smaller ones. The MERT-v1 model was released on 17/03/2023 and is trained using the MLM paradigm. Earlier versions, such as the model released on 29/10/2022, were trained using the BYOL paradigm. The models are intended for use in applications requiring music understanding, and users are advised to select the appropriate model size and configuration based on their specific needs and hardware constraints.

instruction: How do I find the location of the nose bridge in this face photo?
description: Facial-Landmark-Detection is a real-time 3D facial landmark detection model optimized for mobile and edge deployment. It detects facial landmarks such as the nose, mouth, and other key features. The model's architecture was developed by Qualcomm and was trained on a proprietary dataset of faces, though it can be used on any image. The model has an input resolution of 128x128, 5.42 million parameters, and a model size of 20.7 MB in float precision and 5.27 MB in w8a8 precision. It is designed to run on a variety of Qualcomm® devices, including QCS8275, QCS8450, QCS8550, QCS9075, SA7255P, SA8255, SA8295P, SA8650, SA8775P, and Snapdragon®-powered devices such as the Samsung Galaxy S24 and S25. Performance metrics include inference times ranging from 0.118 ms to 3.655 ms and peak memory usage ranging from 0 MB to 146 MB, depending on the device and precision. The model is primarily executed on the NPU, with some configurations using the GPU or CPU. The model can be compiled for on-device deployment using the Qualcomm® AI Hub, and it supports TensorFlow Lite, QNN, and ONNX runtimes. It can be profiled and tested for performance and accuracy on cloud-hosted Qualcomm® devices. The model can also be deployed on Android using TensorFlow Lite or QNN shared libraries. The model is available for use via the Qualcomm® AI Hub, and users can access additional resources, including installation instructions, demos, and deployment guides. The license for the original implementation of the model is available separately.

instruction: Could you help me find educational resources or training materials on Korean finance regulations?
description: KURE-v1 is a Korean University Retrieval Embedding model that has demonstrated strong performance in Korean text retrieval, outperforming most multilingual embedding models. It is considered one of the best publicly available Korean retrieval models. The model is based on the 🤗 transformers framework and supports both Korean and English languages. It is licensed under the MIT license. KURE-v1 has a dimension of 1024 and a sequence length of 8192, and it was fine-tuned using Korean data. The model is designed to support tasks such as document retrieval and semantic similarity computation. It was trained on 2,000,000 examples of Korean query-document-hard_negative data. The training procedure used a loss function from the sentence-transformers library, with a batch size of 4096, a learning rate of 2e-05, and one epoch. Evaluation metrics include Recall, Precision, NDCG, and F1. KURE-v1 was tested on various Korean document retrieval benchmark datasets, including those related to finance, public administration, healthcare, law, commerce, and general knowledge. On average, KURE-v1 achieved a Recall_top1 of 0.52640, Precision_top1 of 0.60551, NDCG_top1 of 0.60551, and F1_top1 of 0.55784. It also showed strong performance in top-k retrieval settings, with the highest average Recall_top3 of 0.68678, Recall_top5 of 0.73851, and Recall_top10 of 0.79682. The model is available for use via the Sentence Transformers library and can be loaded using the identifier 'nlpai-lab/KURE-v1'.

instruction: How can I turn this simple sketch of a robot into a fully textured 3D model?
description: Hunyuan3D 2.0 is an advanced large-scale 3D synthesis system for generating high-resolution textured 3D assets. The system includes two foundation components: a large-scale shape generation model called Hunyuan3D-DiT and a large-scale texture synthesis model called Hunyuan3D-Paint. The shape generative model is built on a scalable flow-based diffusion transformer and is designed to create geometry that aligns with a given condition image, providing a foundation for downstream applications. The texture synthesis model uses strong geometric and diffusion priors to produce high-resolution and vibrant texture maps for either generated or hand-crafted meshes. Additionally, the system includes Hunyuan3D-Studio, a versatile and user-friendly production platform that simplifies the re-creation process of 3D assets, allowing both professional and amateur users to manipulate or animate their meshes efficiently. The system has been evaluated and shown to outperform previous state-of-the-art models, including open-source and closed-source models, in terms of geometry details, condition alignment, and texture quality. Evaluation results indicate that Hunyuan3D 2.0 achieves a CMMD score of 3.193, a FID_CLIP score of 49.165, a FID score of 282.429, and a CLIP-score of 0.809, surpassing all baseline models. The system supports a two-stage generation pipeline, starting with the creation of a bare mesh followed by the synthesis of a texture map for that mesh. This approach decouples the challenges of shape and texture generation and allows for flexible texturing of both generated and handcrafted meshes. The system is accessible via code and a Gradio App, with APIs designed for use with the Hunyuan3D-DiT and Hunyuan3D-Paint models. Pretrained versions of the models are available for use. The system is part of an open-source plan that includes inference code, model checkpoints, and a technical report, with future plans to include a ComfyUI and a TensorRT version. The system is based on prior research and contributions from various open-source repositories.

instruction: Could you help me understand the content of this blurry image with text?
description: Perception Encoder (PE) is a state-of-the-art encoder for image and video understanding trained via simple vision-language learning. It is a family of large-scale vision encoder models with state-of-the-art performance on a large variety of vision tasks. By using a robust contrastive pretraining recipe and finetuning on synthetically aligned videos, PE not only outperforms all existing models on classification and retrieval, but it also internally produces strong, general features that scale for downstream tasks. PE unlocks the ability for large-scale contrastive pretraining to transfer to downstream tasks with alignment tuning to capitalize on those general features. PE lang takes the strong language performance from the intermediate layers of PE core and further aligns for language modeling. It is specifically tuned to be versatile for any multimodal language modeling use case, including using different language model decoders (e.g., Llama / Qwen) and using different eval settings (e.g., native res / tiling). PE lang performs particularly well on OCR and document tasks. Two PE Lang checkpoints, L14-448 and G14-448, were released. In a benchmark setting with a frozen encoder using 2.6M SFT datamix and 448px only (with no tiling), and Llama 3.1 8B as the decoder, the L14-448 checkpoint achieved 81.9 on Doc VQA (val), 46.4 on InfoQA (val), 73.0 on TextVQA, 52.3 on MVBench, 54.7 on PerceptionTest (val), and 59.8 on EgoSchema (val). The G14-448 checkpoint achieved 84.4 on Doc VQA (val), 48.3 on InfoQA (val), 75.2 on TextVQA, 52.4 on MVBench, 56.0 on PerceptionTest (val), and 62.0 on EgoSchema (val). Using PE Core G aligned further with tiling using 36+1 image tiles / 32 video frames and Llama 3.1 8B as the decoder, the PLM-8B model achieved 94.6 on Doc VQA (test), 78.8 on InfoQA (test), 86.5 on TextVQA, 77.1 on MVBench, 82.7 on PerceptionTest (test), and 68.8 on EgoSchema (test). The PE-Core-G14-448 checkpoint was further trained using tiling. The tiling aligned checkpoint will be released soon. See the paper for full performance evaluations and fair comparisons to other models.

instruction: Based on the image of a kitchen, generate a realistic 3D model with accurate proportions and materials.
description: Tencent Hunyuan3D-1.0 is a two-stage framework for text-to-3D and image-to-3D generation. It includes a lite version and a standard version, both supporting text- and image-conditioned generation. In the first stage, a multi-view diffusion model generates multi-view RGB images in approximately 4 seconds, capturing rich details of the 3D asset from different viewpoints. In the second stage, a feed-forward reconstruction model rapidly and faithfully reconstructs the 3D asset from the generated multi-view images in approximately 7 seconds. The reconstruction network is designed to handle noises and inconsistencies introduced by the multi-view diffusion and to leverage information from the condition image to recover the 3D structure. The framework incorporates the text-to-image model Hunyuan-DiT, enabling a unified approach for both text- and image-conditioned 3D generation. The standard version has three times more parameters than the lite version and other existing models. Hunyuan3D-1.0 achieves a balance between speed and quality, significantly reducing generation time while maintaining the quality and diversity of the produced assets. The lite model produces a 3D mesh from a single image in about 10 seconds on an NVIDIA A100 GPU, while the standard model takes about 25 seconds. Evaluations show that Hunyuan3D-1.0 received the highest user preference across five metrics. The model supports both text-to-3D and image-to-3D generation, with bilingual Chinese and English support for text prompts. The model is available in three versions: Hunyuan3D-1/lite for multi-view generation, Hunyuan3D-1/std for multi-view generation, and Hunyuan3D-1/svrm for sparse-view reconstruction. The model requires specific hardware and software configurations, including a compatible GPU and environment setup. Inference can be performed using provided scripts, with different VRAM requirements for the standard and lite pipelines. The model supports various configuration options, including texture mapping, rendering, and memory-saving settings. Camera parameters for output views include fixed azimuth angles relative to the input view: +0, +60, +120, +180, +240, and +300 degrees.

instruction: How would you animate this still picture with motion and visual effects?
description: CogVideoX-5B-I2V is an open-source video generation model designed for image-to-video tasks. It is part of the CogVideoX series, which includes multiple versions with varying model sizes and capabilities. CogVideoX-5B-I2V is a larger model that provides higher video generation quality and better visual effects compared to the entry-level CogVideoX-2B model. The model is trained in BF16 precision and is recommended to use the same precision for inference. It supports video generation with a maximum length of 6 seconds, a frame rate of 8 frames per second, and a resolution of 720 x 480. The model uses a 3D RoPE position embedding with a learnable component. It accepts English prompts with a maximum length of 226 tokens. The model supports deployment using the Hugging Face diffusers library and can be fine-tuned using LORA or SFT methods. Fine-tuning requires significant GPU memory, with requirements ranging from 47 GB to 80 GB depending on the batch size and GPU configuration. The model can be optimized for lower memory usage through quantization techniques such as INT8 weight-only quantization using TorchAO, which allows it to run on devices with limited VRAM, such as T4 Colabs. However, quantization may reduce inference speed. The model supports various inference precision modes, including FP16, BF16, FP32, FP8, and INT8, with BF16 being the recommended option. Memory usage and inference speed vary depending on the GPU architecture, with H100 GPUs generally providing faster performance than A100. The model includes optimizations such as sequential CPU offloading, VAE slicing, and tiling to reduce memory consumption, though these optimizations may slow down inference. The model is not compatible with INT4 precision. It is important to note that the model only supports English input, and other languages must be translated into English for use. The model is available for commercial use through the Qingying and API Platform. The model is released under a specific license and can be cited using the provided academic citation.

instruction: What is the interest expense for the most recent quarter listed?
description: FinTabQA is a model designed for financial table question-answering. It is based on the LayoutLM architecture and is intended to extract information from table images by answering questions about their content. The model is trained on an extractive question-answering task using the SynFinTabs dataset, a large-scale, labelled dataset of synthetic financial tables. FinTabQA supports tasks involving the interpretation of financial tables, including the extraction of specific values or information. It processes input by combining question text with OCR results from table images, using normalized bounding box coordinates to inform the model of word positions. The model is evaluated on real-world financial tables and compared to a state-of-the-art generative model. The model is available for use through the Hugging Face Transformers library and is intended to be used with a fast tokenizer. The model's performance is demonstrated using the synfintabs dataset, where it successfully predicts answers to questions about table content. The model is described as being trained using a method that generates synthetic tables, which the authors hope is transferable to other domains. The model and dataset generation code are publicly available.

instruction: Find all the organizations mentioned in this research paper.
description: GLiNER-Multitask is a multi-task prompt-tunable model designed to extract various pieces of information from plain text based on a user-provided custom prompt. It leverages a bidirectional transformer encoder, similar to BERT, which ensures both high generalization and compute efficiency despite its compact size. The model supports a range of information extraction tasks, including named entity recognition, relation extraction, summarization, sentiment extraction, key-phrase extraction, question-answering, open information extraction, and text classification. The gliner-multitask-v1.0 variant achieves state-of-the-art performance on NER zero-shot benchmarks, demonstrating robustness and flexibility. On the CrossNER datasets, it achieves an average F1 score of 0.6325, compared to 0.6276 for the v0.5 variant. For relation extraction, it achieves an average F1 score of 0.749442 on the CrossRe dataset and 0.76598 on the DocRed dataset. For open information extraction, it achieves an F1 score of 0.4168 on the WiRe57_343-manual-oie dataset. For question-answering, it achieves an F1 score of 0.712745 on the SQuAD 2.0 dataset. For text classification, it achieves a micro F1 score of 0.7907 on the IMDb dataset and 0.7436 on the AG News dataset. The model also demonstrates performance on various NER benchmarks, achieving an F1 score of 0.6838 on CoNLL 2003, 0.7387 on bc5cdr, and 0.7845 on WikiNeural. The model is designed to be used with a custom prompt to extract specific information, and it can be fine-tuned for specific tasks. It is available for use through the GLiNER library, which can be installed via pip. The model is lightweight and efficient, making it suitable for a wide range of applications. However, it may have limitations in certain domains or with specific types of text, and its performance may vary depending on the task and dataset.

instruction: Can you animate the scene of a bird flying from one tree to another in a forest?
description: This is a gguf quantized version of the wan video model, designed for video generation tasks. It includes a 1.3b parameter model variant that is suitable for older or low-end machines. The model supports text-to-video (t2v) generation and video auto-encoding (vace) functions. It is compatible with the ComfyUI framework and requires specific model files to be placed in designated directories for proper operation. The model architecture is based on a lazy design referred to as 'pig,' which applies to all model, encoder, and vae gguf files. For compatibility with the comfyui-gguf node, the 'pig' architecture may need to be manually added to the IMG_ARCH_LIST in loader.py. The model can be run using either the diffusers library with a custom pipeline or through the gguf-connector. Performance considerations include the use of CPU offloading for encoder operations, which does not affect speed. The model has been updated to version wan2.1-v5-vace-1.3b, where all components except block weights are in f32 status to avoid triggering time or text embedding key errors during inference. The model is based on a reference architecture and integrates with external tools such as ComfyUI, gguf-connector, and the pig architecture framework.

instruction: Please create a segmentation map to identify the different parts of this object in the image.
description: U-2-Net is a deep learning model designed for image segmentation tasks, particularly for generating detailed masks. It leverages a unique architecture with nested U-blocks that allow the model to capture both high-level semantic features and fine-grained details. U-2-Net has demonstrated high performance in a variety of segmentation tasks, making it a versatile choice for applications such as background removal, object detection, and medical image analysis. The U-2-Net model is built upon a nested U-structure, where each U-block consists of multiple convolutional layers, pooling, and up-sampling operations. The architecture features a combination of down-sampling and up-sampling paths, enabling the model to learn features at different scales. This design allows the U-2-Net to produce accurate and high-resolution segmentation maps. The key components of the architecture include Residual U-blocks (RSU) that enhance feature representation and ensure efficient information flow through the network. The model is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 to match the original U-2-Net model.

instruction: I want to explore the career of a specific inventor across multiple patents—how can I track their work?
description: This is a sentence transformer model fine-tuned on the model 'sentence-transformers/all-mpnet-base-v2' and pretrained for the English language. It is designed for patent-inventor linking tasks, specifically for clustering unique patent-inventor records based on attributes such as name, occupation, year, address, firm, and patent title. The model can be used with the SentenceTransformer class or the LinkTransformer package, which implements the SLINK clustering algorithm with cosine similarity as the metric. The model architecture consists of a Transformer with a maximum sequence length of 384 and an MPNetModel, followed by a Pooling layer that averages token embeddings and a Normalize layer. Training was conducted using a DataLoader with a batch size of 64 and a custom loss function called 'linktransformer.modified_sbert.losses.SupConLoss_wandb'. The training parameters included 100 epochs, a learning rate of 2e-05, a weight decay of 0.01, and a warmup of 3100 steps. Evaluation was performed using standard information retrieval metrics, though specific results are not provided in the text. The model is associated with the research paper '300 Years of British Patents' by Enrico Berkes, Matthew Lee Chen, and Matteo Tranchero, and the LinkTransformer package by Abhishek Arora and Melissa Dell.

instruction: Generate a list of 10 creative gift ideas for a tech-savvy teenager.
description: Qwen3 is the latest generation of large language models in the Qwen series, offering a comprehensive suite of dense and mixture-of-experts (MoE) models. Built upon extensive training, Qwen3 delivers groundbreaking advancements in reasoning, instruction-following, agent capabilities, and multilingual support. It uniquely supports seamless switching between thinking mode (for complex logical reasoning, math, and coding) and non-thinking mode (for efficient, general-purpose dialogue) within a single model, ensuring optimal performance across various scenarios. It significantly enhances its reasoning capabilities, surpassing previous QwQ (in thinking mode) and Qwen2.5 instruct models (in non-thinking mode) on mathematics, code generation, and commonsense logical reasoning. It demonstrates superior human preference alignment, excelling in creative writing, role-playing, multi-turn dialogues, and instruction following, to deliver a more natural, engaging, and immersive conversational experience. It has expertise in agent capabilities, enabling precise integration with external tools in both thinking and unthinking modes and achieving leading performance among open-source models in complex agent-based tasks. It supports 100+ languages and dialects with strong capabilities for multilingual instruction following and translation. The Qwen3-4B model is a causal language model with 4.0B parameters, 3.6B non-embedding parameters, 36 layers, and 32 attention heads for Q and 8 for KV. It has a context length of 32,768 tokens natively. The model is designed to support both thinking and non-thinking modes, with the ability to switch between them via user input or API parameters. In thinking mode, the model generates reasoning content wrapped in a block, followed by the final response. In non-thinking mode, the model does not generate any thinking content. The model is compatible with various frameworks and tools, including Hugging Face transformers, vLLM, SGLang, Ollama, LMStudio, MLX-LM, and llama.cpp. It supports agentic use through the Qwen-Agent framework, which encapsulates tool-calling templates and parsers. The model natively supports context lengths of up to 32,768 tokens and can be extended to 131,072 tokens using RoPE scaling techniques. For optimal performance, it is recommended to use specific sampling parameters for thinking and non-thinking modes, adequate output length, and standardized output formats for benchmarking. The model's historical output in multi-turn conversations should only include the final response part, not the thinking content. The model is available for deployment via APIs and supports dynamic YaRN by default through the endpoint provided by Alibaba Model Studio.

instruction: What is the predicted secondary structure of this RNA sequence based on its nucleotide composition?
description: ERNIE-RNA is a pre-trained model on non-coding RNA (ncRNA) using a masked language modeling (MLM) objective. It is a -style model pre-trained on a large corpus of non-coding RNA sequences in a self-supervised fashion, meaning it was trained on the raw nucleotides of RNA sequences only, with an automatic process to generate inputs and labels from those texts. The model has 12 layers, a hidden size of 768, 12 attention heads, an intermediate size of 3072, 85.67 million parameters, 22.37 gigaflops, 11.18 gigamacs, and a maximum of 1024 tokens. It was developed by Weijie Yin, Zhaoyu Zhang, Liang He, Rui Jiang, Shuo Zhang, Gan Liu, Xuegong Zhang, Tao Qin, and Zhen Xie. The model is designed to support tasks such as masked language modeling, sequence classification or regression, token classification or regression, and contact classification or regression. For these tasks, the model must be fine-tuned on a downstream task. The model file depends on the multimolecule library, which can be installed using pip. The model uses the RnaTokenizer for preprocessing RNA sequences and replaces 'T's with 'S's during preprocessing, though this behavior can be disabled. The training data was sourced from RNAcentral, a public resource of non-coding RNA sequences, with redundancy removed using a 100% sequence identity cutoff, resulting in 25 million unique sequences. Sequences longer than 1024 nucleotides were excluded, leaving a final dataset of 20.4 million non-redundant RNA sequences. The model was trained using masked language modeling, where 15% of the tokens are masked, with 80% replaced by [MASK], 10% by a random token, and 10% left unchanged. The training was conducted on 24 NVIDIA V100 GPUs with 32GiB memories, using a learning rate of 1e-4, a warm-up of 20,000 steps, and a weight decay of 0.01. The model is licensed under the AGPL-3.0-or-later license. The model card was written by the MultiMolecule team, not the original authors, and the official repository of ERNIE-RNA is available elsewhere. The MultiMolecule team has confirmed that the provided model and checkpoints produce the same intermediate representations as the original implementation. The model is not fine-tuned for any specific task and must be adapted for use in downstream applications. The model is described in a paper titled 'ERNIE-RNA: An RNA Language Model with Structure-enhanced Representations' published in bioRxiv. The model is intended to provide comprehensive RNA structural representations and has achieved state-of-the-art performance after fine-tuning for various downstream tasks, including RNA structural and functional predictions. The model incorporates base-pairing restriction in its architecture to enhance RNA structural modeling without the use of multiple sequence alignment information.

instruction: Can you create a live English audio stream from a French speaker with synchronized text output?
description: Hibiki is a model for streaming speech translation, also known as simultaneous translation. It is designed to adapt its flow to accumulate just enough context to produce a correct translation in real-time, chunk by chunk. As the user speaks, Hibiki generates natural speech in the target language, optionally with voice transfer, along with a text translation. Hibiki currently only supports French-to-English translation. The model, referred to as Hibiki-M (for Mobile), is a 1.7B parameter hierarchical Transformer that produces speech and text tokens at a framerate of 12.5Hz, with audio being generated at a 1.1kbps bitrate. Hibiki is a decoder-only model for simultaneous speech translation. It leverages the multistream architecture to model source and target speech jointly, allowing it to continuously process the input stream while generating the target speech. Hibiki produces text and audio tokens at a constant framerate of 12.5Hz, enabling a continuous output audio stream along with timestamped text translation. It relies on simple temperature sampling, making it compatible with batching, unlike models that rely on complex inference policies. The fidelity of Hibiki's voice transfer can be controlled by changing the coefficient of the Classifier-Free Guidance: a larger coefficient will increase voice similarity, but excessive coefficients can lead to worse translations. The model is developed by Kyutai and is a simultaneous speech-to-speech and speech-to-text translation model. It supports French-to-English translation and is licensed under CC-BY. Hibiki can be used for streaming translation from French to English in real-time settings or for batched simultaneous translation of many input sequences. It is robust to noisy conditions and is trained on sequences up to 120 seconds. Some components of the model can be used independently or repurposed relatively easily. For example, the Mimi codec is a state-of-the-art audio neural codec that combines semantic and acoustic information into audio tokens running at 12Hz and a bitrate of 1.1kbps, making it particularly adapted to train speech language models or text-to-speech systems. Supporting other pairs of languages would require finetuning. The model is not intended to be used to impersonate other people or for any malicious use of any kind. The underlying model is trained on a mix of data including Wikipedia, Stack Exchange, open-access scientific articles (from peS2o), and Common Crawl. The unsupervised audio dataset used for pre-training is a collection of 7M hours of readily available audio content in English and 450k hours in French, following the preprocessing and recipe of a referenced source. The synthetic translation dataset consists of around 40k hours of parallel French-English data synthesized with contextual alignment, with various levels of speaker similarity. Translation finetuning is based on a 900-hour mixture of a resynthesized version of a referenced dataset and synthetic long-form utterances. The training procedure and hyper-parameters are detailed in the paper. The final model was trained on 48 H100 Nvidia GPUs.

instruction: How do solar panels work, and what are their main components?
description: The Llama 3.2 collection is a set of multilingual large language models (LLMs) available in 1B and 3B parameter sizes, designed for text-in and text-out tasks. These models are pretrained and instruction-tuned for multilingual dialogue use cases, including agentic retrieval and summarization tasks. They outperform many open source and closed chat models on common industry benchmarks. The models are developed by Meta and use an optimized transformer architecture. The instruction-tuned versions are aligned with human preferences for helpfulness and safety through supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and reinforcement learning with human feedback (RLHF). Llama 3.2 supports English, German, French, Italian, Portuguese, Hindi, Spanish, and Thai, with the potential for developers to fine-tune for additional languages, provided they comply with the Llama 3.2 Community License and the Acceptable Use Policy. The models are trained on a new mix of publicly available online data, with a knowledge cutoff in December 2023. They support up to 128k context length for the full models and 8k for the quantized versions. The models use Grouped-Query Attention (GQA) for improved inference scalability and have up to 9 trillion tokens in their pretraining data. Llama 3.2 is intended for commercial and research use in multiple languages, with instruction-tuned models suitable for assistant-like chat, agentic applications, knowledge retrieval, summarization, and mobile AI-powered writing assistants. Pretrained models can be adapted for additional natural language generation tasks, and quantized models are suitable for on-device use with limited compute resources. The models are not intended for use in any manner that violates applicable laws or regulations, or that is prohibited by the Acceptable Use Policy and Llama 3.2 Community License. Llama 3.2 is a static model trained on an offline dataset, with future versions potentially improving capabilities and safety. The model is governed by a custom, commercial license agreement. Training involved custom libraries, Meta's GPU cluster, and production infrastructure, with a total of 916k GPU hours of computation on H100-80GB hardware. Training resulted in an estimated 240 tons of location-based CO2eq emissions, though market-based emissions were 0 due to Meta's renewable energy use. The models incorporate logits from Llama 3.1 models during pretraining and use knowledge distillation after pruning. Post-training alignment includes SFT, Rejection Sampling (RS), and Direct Preference Optimization (DPO). Quantization methods include SpinQuant and QLoRA, with SpinQuant involving rotation matrix fine-tuning and GPTQ. The models were evaluated on various benchmarks, showing performance across general knowledge, reading comprehension, long context, summarization, instruction following, math, reasoning, tool use, and multilingual tasks. Inference metrics show improvements in decode speed, time-to-first-token, prefill speed, model size, and memory usage for quantized versions compared to BF16 baselines. The models are part of a responsible release approach, with safety strategies including enabling safe deployment, protecting against adversarial users, and providing community protections. Safety fine-tuning includes human-generated and synthetic data, with emphasis on model refusals and tone. Llama 3.2 should be deployed as part of an AI system with additional safety guardrails, such as Llama Guard, Prompt Guard, and Code Shield. Evaluations include adversarial datasets and red teaming exercises to identify and mitigate risks in areas like CBRNE, child safety, and cyber attacks. The models are part of industry partnerships and community initiatives, with grants and reporting mechanisms to support responsible use. Ethical considerations include openness, inclusivity, and helpfulness, with a note that the model may produce inaccurate, biased, or objectionable responses, requiring developers to perform safety testing and tuning for their specific applications.

instruction: I’m preparing this image for a social media post—can you remove the background for me?
description: RMBG v2.0 is a state-of-the-art background removal model that significantly improves upon RMBG v1.4. The model is designed to effectively separate foreground from background in a range of categories and image types. It has been trained on a carefully selected dataset that includes general stock images, e-commerce, gaming, and advertising content, making it suitable for commercial use cases and enterprise content creation at scale. The accuracy, efficiency, and versatility of the model currently rival leading source-available models. It is ideal for applications where content safety, legally licensed datasets, and bias mitigation are paramount. Developed by BRIA AI, RMBG v2.0 is available as a source-available model for non-commercial use. The model is a dichotomous image segmentation model trained exclusively on a professional-grade dataset. The training data consists of over 15,000 high-quality, high-resolution, manually labeled (pixel-wise accuracy), fully licensed images. The dataset includes balanced gender, balanced ethnicity, and people with different types of disabilities. The distribution of images is as follows: 45.11% objects only, 25.24% people with objects or animals, 17.35% people only, 8.52% people/objects/animals with text, 2.52% text only, and 1.89% animals only. The distribution of image types is 87.70% photorealistic and 12.30% non-photorealistic. The distribution of background types is 52.05% non-solid background and 47.95% solid background. The distribution of foreground content is 51.42% single main foreground object and 48.58% multiple objects in the foreground. RMBG-2.0 is developed on an enhanced architecture with proprietary dataset and training scheme, which significantly improves the model’s accuracy and effectiveness for the background-removal task. The model is released under a CC BY-NC 4.0 license for non-commercial use, and commercial use is subject to a commercial agreement with BRIA. If you use this model in your research, please cite the article titled 'Bilateral Reference for High-Resolution Dichotomous Image Segmentation' by Zheng, Peng and Gao, Dehong and Fan, Deng-Ping and Liu, Li and Laaksonen, Jorma and Ouyang, Wanli and Sebe, Nicu, published in CAAI Artificial Intelligence Research in 2024.

instruction: I need to know the number of cars in this parking lot image for analytics. Can you help?
description: The D-FINE model was proposed by Yansong Peng, Hebei Li, Peixi Wu, Yueyi Zhang, Xiaoyan Sun, and Feng Wu. This is the Hugging Face Transformers implementation for D-FINE. D-FINE is a powerful real-time object detector that achieves outstanding localization precision by redefining the bounding box regression task in DETR models. The model comprises two key components: Fine-grained Distribution Refinement (FDR) and Global Optimal Localization Self-Distillation (GO-LSD). D-FINE is trained on COCO (Lin et al. [2014]) train2017 and validated on COCO val2017 dataset. The standard AP metrics are reported, averaged over uniformly sampled IoU thresholds ranging from 0.50 to 0.95 with a step size of 0.05, and APval5000 is also reported, which is commonly used in real scenarios. D-FINE is ideal for real-time object detection in diverse applications such as autonomous driving, surveillance systems, robotics, and retail analytics. Its enhanced flexibility and deployment-friendly design make it suitable for both edge devices and large-scale systems, ensuring high accuracy and speed in dynamic, real-world environments.

instruction: Please provide the total number of colleges in the state of Kerala from the given data.
description: The model is a M2M100ForConditionalGeneration model fine-tuned for the task of table question answering (TableQA) in low-resource Indic languages, specifically Bengali and Hindi. It is designed to answer questions over structured tabular data, returning either individual cells or entire tables as output. The model supports tasks involving the processing of tabular data in these languages, where annotated data and neural models are scarce. The model's architecture is based on the M2M100 framework, which is a multilingual machine translation model. The model was trained on large-scale datasets generated through a fully automatic data generation process, which was developed to address the lack of annotated data for low-resource languages. Evaluation results show that models trained on these datasets outperform state-of-the-art large language models. The model is intended for use in environments where structured data needs to be queried in low-resource language settings. However, the model's performance may be limited by the quality and coverage of the automatically generated training data, and it is not guaranteed to perform well on tasks outside the scope of the training data. The model is part of a broader research effort focused on scalable data generation and evaluation procedures for low-resource TableQA, and it is released alongside datasets and code for further research and application.
domain: Natural Language Processing Table Question Answering

instruction: Are the statements 'She is allergic to nuts' and 'She can't eat almonds' likely to be similar?
description: This is a model finetuned from BERT-tiny using the Sentence Transformers library. It computes scores for pairs of texts, which can be used for semantic textual similarity, semantic search, paraphrase mining, text classification, clustering, and more. The model is a Cross Encoder with a maximum sequence length of 512 tokens and produces one output label. It is designed for the English language and is licensed under the Apache 2.0 license. The model was trained on a dataset containing 578,402 training samples with columns for question, answer, and label. The training dataset includes statistics based on the first 1000 samples, with question lengths ranging from 21 to 96 characters and answer lengths from 51 to 405 characters. The label distribution is approximately 82.90% for label 0 and 17.10% for label 1. The model was trained using a loss function with activation function torch.nn.modules.linear.Identity and pos_weight of 5. Training hyperparameters include an evaluation strategy of steps, a per-device train and evaluation batch size of 2048, a learning rate of 0.0005, one training epoch, a warmup ratio of 0.1, and a seed of 12. Training was conducted using bf16 precision on an NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3090 GPU and a 13th Gen Intel(R) Core(TM) i7-13700K CPU with 31.78 GB of RAM. The model was evaluated on several datasets including gooaq-dev, NanoMSMARCO, NanoNFCorpus, NanoNQ, and NanoBEIR_R100_mean. Evaluation metrics include map, mrr@10, and ndcg@10. On the gooaq-dev dataset, the model achieved an ndcg@10 score of 0.6157 with a change of +0.0245. On NanoMSMARCO, the ndcg@10 score was 0.4772 with a change of -0.0632. On NanoNFCorpus, the ndcg@10 score was 0.3308 with a change of +0.0058. On NanoNQ, the ndcg@10 score was 0.4859 with a change of -0.0147. On the NanoBEIR_R100_mean dataset, the ndcg@10 score was 0.4313 with a change of -0.0241. The model's training logs show the training loss and evaluation metrics at different steps and epochs. The environmental impact of training the model was measured, with energy consumption of 0.019 kWh, carbon emissions of 0.007 kg of CO2, and training duration of 0.099 hours.

instruction: Can you help analyze this gene sequence to identify potential functional regions?
description: This model is based on the RoBERTa transformer architecture and is designed for metagenomic analysis. It uses a custom k-mer tokenizer with a k-mer length of 6 and overlapping tokens. The model was trained for one epoch on V100 GPUs using a diverse dataset of 220 million 400bp fragments from 18,000 bacterial and archaeal genomes. The model supports tasks involving the analysis of gene sequences, including tokenization, embedding generation, and computation of mean sequence embeddings. It is intended to be used with a custom K-mer Tokenizer and the Hugging Face Transformers library. The model is described in the paper 'Leveraging Large Language Models for Metagenomic Analysis' by Refahi, M.S., Sokhansanj, B.A., & Rosen, G.L. (2023), published in IEEE SPMB.

instruction: Could you help me locate the exact coordinates of the person's limbs in this picture?
description: DWPose is a state-of-the-art whole-body pose estimation model designed to estimate body keypoints. It is intended for use in a pipeline and is provided in ONNX format for ease of integration. The model was developed by researchers Zhendong Yang, Ailing Zeng, Chun Yuan, and Yu Li. The methodology is described in the paper titled 'Effective Whole-body Pose Estimation with Two-stages Distillation,' published at ICCV 2023 (CV4Metaverse Workshop). DWPose is hosted by FASHN AI, which does not claim ownership of the model and provides the weights solely for integration with its virtual try-on pipeline. The model files include 'yolox_l.onnx,' a YOLOX-L detector for person detection, and 'dw-ll_ucoco_384.onnx,' the DWPose-l model for whole-body keypoint estimation. DWPose builds upon the work of other open-source projects, including an OpenMMLab pose estimation toolbox, a Real-Time Multi-Person Pose Estimation system, and a High-performance object detector. The model is released under a license that permits commercial use with proper attribution. Users are encouraged to visit the original repository for full documentation, training code, additional model variants, and discussions. If DWPose is used in research, the original authors should be cited as specified in the provided BibTeX citation.

instruction: I need to convert a voice message into text for a legal document. Can you handle that?
description: Ultravox is a multimodal Speech LLM that can consume both speech and text as input, such as a text system prompt and a voice user message. The input to the model is given as a text prompt with a special pseudo-token, and the model processor replaces this token with embeddings derived from the input audio. Using the merged embeddings as input, the model generates output text as usual. In a future revision of Ultravox, the token vocabulary will be expanded to support the generation of semantic and acoustic audio tokens, which can then be fed to a vocoder to produce voice output. No preference tuning has been applied to this revision of the model. The model uses a pre-trained backbone as well as the encoder part of another model. The multi-modal adapter is trained, the Whisper encoder is fine-tuned, while the Llama model is kept frozen. A knowledge-distillation loss is used where Ultravox tries to match the logits of the text-based Llama backbone. The training dataset is a mix of ASR datasets, extended with continuations generated by Llama 3.1 8B, and speech translation datasets, which yield a modest improvement in translation evaluations. The model is trained using supervised speech instruction finetuning via knowledge-distillation. The model was developed by Fixie.ai and is licensed under the MIT license. The model can be used as a voice agent, for speech-to-speech translation, and for the analysis of spoken audio. The model is trained using BF16 mixed precision training on 8x H100 GPUs. Evaluation results show the following performance metrics: for covost2 en_ar, 1.55 for Ultravox 0.5 1b, 12.99 for Ultravox 0.5 8B, and 20.21 for Ultravox 0.5 70B; for covost2 en_ca, 8.06 for Ultravox 0.5 1b, 31.54 for Ultravox 0.5 8B, and 40.01 for Ultravox 0.5 70B; for covost2 en_de, 14.21 for Ultravox 0.5 1b, 28.70 for Ultravox 0.5 8B, and 34.53 for Ultravox 0.5 70B; for covost2 es_en, 24.97 for Ultravox 0.5 1b, 40.19 for Ultravox 0.5 8B, and 43.29 for Ultravox 0.5 70B; for covost2 ru_en, 24.12 for Ultravox 0.5 1b, 42.13 for Ultravox 0.5 8B, and 48.99 for Ultravox 0.5 70B; for covost2 zh_en, 4.76 for Ultravox 0.5 1b, 17.22 for Ultravox 0.5 8B, and 21.37 for Ultravox 0.5 70B; and for big bench audio, 39.14 for Ultravox 0.5 1b, 66.54 for Ultravox 0.5 8B, and 82.70 for Ultravox 0.5 70B.

instruction: Can you generate a detailed image of a romantic couple in a passionate embrace, based on my written description?
description: The FLUX Uncensored LoRA v2 is an enhanced version of the original FLUX Uncensored LoRA, designed for the base model black-forest-labs/FLUX.1-dev. This version addresses issues present in the previous release and offers improved performance and stability. The LoRA weights have been fine-tuned to remove the base model's content restrictions, allowing for the generation of NSFW (Not Safe For Work) and other uncensored content. This LoRA extension can be loaded into the FLUX.1-dev pipeline using the diffusers library. It is optimized for high-quality, explicit image generation based on user-provided prompts. The model is intended for research and personal use, and adheres to the non-commercial license terms. The model has been optimized for better performance and faster generation times. Issues present in the previous version have been addressed, ensuring a more stable and reliable experience. The LoRA weights have been streamlined to reduce the model size without compromising quality. The base model is black-forest-labs/FLUX.1-dev. The LoRA weights are provided by enhanceaiteam/Flux-uncensored-v2. The LoRA weight file is lora.safetensors. The recommended torch data type is torch.bfloat16. A CUDA-enabled GPU is recommended for optimal performance. The model follows the FLUX-1-dev Non-Commercial License. Users should exercise caution and adhere to legal and ethical guidelines. This model allows the generation of explicit content. The creators do not endorse illegal or unethical use of the model. Content generated using this model should comply with platform guidelines and local regulations regarding NSFW material. Use the following trigger words to guide the model toward generating NSFW content: nsfw, naked, pron, kissing, erotic, nude, sensual, adult content, explicit. These keywords, along with descriptive prompts, help the model generate explicit imagery.

instruction: Could you help me write out what the speaker is saying in this clip?
description: Whisper is a pre-trained model for automatic speech recognition (ASR) and speech translation. Trained on 680,000 hours of labelled speech data using large-scale weak supervision, Whisper models demonstrate strong generalization across many datasets and domains without the need for fine-tuning. The model is based on a Transformer architecture and functions as a sequence-to-sequence model. It was developed by Alec Radford et al from OpenAI and is available in ten pre-trained checkpoints with five different configurations, varying in model size from 39 million to 1550 million parameters. The smallest four configurations are trained on either English-only or multilingual data, while the largest checkpoints are multilingual only. English-only models are trained for speech recognition, while multilingual models are trained for both speech recognition and speech translation. For speech recognition, the model predicts transcriptions in the same language as the audio, and for speech translation, it predicts transcriptions in a different language. Whisper requires a WhisperProcessor to pre-process audio inputs into log-Mel spectrograms and to post-process model outputs into text. The model is informed of the task (transcription or translation) through context tokens provided to the decoder. These tokens include a transcription token, a language token, a task token, and optionally a timestamp prediction token. Context tokens can be forced or un-forced, allowing control over the output language and task or letting the model predict them automatically. Whisper is capable of transcribing audio samples up to 30 seconds in duration, and with a chunking algorithm, it can handle audio of arbitrary length. The model has been evaluated on tasks like English speech recognition, showing a word error rate of 3.43% on the LibriSpeech test-clean dataset. Whisper models may exhibit hallucinations, where the model generates text not present in the audio, due to its tendency to predict the next word in a sequence. Performance varies across languages, with lower accuracy observed in low-resource or low-discoverability languages and across different accents and dialects. The model is also prone to generating repetitive text, which can be partially mitigated with beam search and temperature scheduling. Whisper is intended for use in ASR and speech translation, and while it may be useful for developers, especially for English speech recognition, it is not recommended for high-risk domains or subjective classification tasks. The model may be used to improve accessibility tools, but its release also raises concerns about potential dual use in surveillance technologies. The training data consists of 680,000 hours of audio and transcripts collected from the internet, with 65% in English, 18% in non-English audio with English transcripts, and 17% in non-English audio with non-English transcripts across 98 languages. Performance in a given language is correlated with the amount of training data available for that language. The model is not robustly evaluated for tasks like voice activity detection, speaker classification, or speaker diarization, and users are advised to perform their own evaluations before deployment. The model is not intended for use in decision-making contexts where accuracy flaws could lead to significant negative outcomes. The model may also have some capability to recognize specific individuals, which could present safety concerns.

instruction: Please evaluate this audio snippet for content quality and technical production.
description: This model provides unified automatic quality assessment for speech, music, and sound. It is designed to evaluate four axes of aesthetic scores: Content Enjoyment (CE), Content Usefulness (CU), Production Complexity (PC), and Production Quality (PQ). The model supports prediction tasks through both command-line interface (CLI) and Python scripts. Users can run predictions by providing a JSONL file containing audio file paths, with optional start and end times for specific segments. The model outputs a JSON-formatted dictionary with scores for each of the four axes. It can be used to infer from file paths or directly from PyTorch tensors. The model is based on a PyTorch framework and requires Python 3.9 and PyTorch 2.2 or greater. An evaluation dataset has been released, containing 4 axes of aesthetic annotation scores for various datasets, including LibriTTS, cv-corpus-13.0-2023-03-09, EARS, MUSDB18, musiccaps, audioset unbalanced_train_segments, and PAM. Each axis contains 10 scores annotated by 10 different human annotators. The model is primarily licensed under CC-BY 4.0, with some portions available under the MIT license. The model is described in a paper titled 'Meta Audiobox Aesthetics: Unified Automatic Quality Assessment for Speech, Music, and Sound,' authored by Andros Tjandra, Yi-Chiao Wu, Baishan Guo, John Hoffman, Brian Ellis, Apoorv Vyas, Bowen Shi, Sanyuan Chen, Matt Le, Nick Zacharov, Carleigh Wood, Ann Lee, and Wei-Ning Hsu, to be published in 2025.

instruction: Estimate the height of the tree relative to the surrounding structures in the image.
description: SpaceThinker-Qwen2.5VL-3B is a multimodal vision-language model (VLM) designed to enhance spatial reasoning through test-time compute. It is a fine-tuned version of UCSC-VLAA/VLAA-Thinker-Qwen2.5VL-3B, using synthetic reasoning traces generated by a 3D scene reconstruction pipeline involving Molmo, VGGT, and SAM2. The model has 3.75B parameters in FP16 format and is based on the Qwen2.5-VL-3B architecture. It was fine-tuned using LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) with a rank of 128 and an alpha of 256, targeting modules q_proj and v_proj. The training used AdamW optimizer with a learning rate of 2e-5, a batch size of 1, and 3 epochs. The model is trained on a dataset of over 12K synthetic spatial reasoning traces, covering question types such as distances (with units), spatial relations (e.g., left-of, above, inside), and object properties. It is designed to support tasks involving spatial reasoning, including distance estimation, object relations, and scene interpretation. The model demonstrates strong performance on benchmarks such as SpatialScore, SpaCE-10, and QSpatial++, particularly in the SpatialScore-Hard split, where it achieves an overall score of 19.47. It also performs well in the SpaCE-10 benchmark, achieving an overall score of 32.72. On the QSpatial++ benchmark, it achieves a success rate of 55% and a sample completion rate of 99 out of 100. The model is intended for applications such as robotics and drones, where planning and navigation in 3D space are required. However, it has limitations, including potential performance degradation in cluttered environments or under certain camera perspectives, and it was trained on synthetic data over an internet image dataset. Multimodal biases from the base model may persist, and the model is not intended for safety-critical or legal decision-making. Users are advised to evaluate outputs critically and consider domain-specific fine-tuning for improved safety and performance. The model is licensed under Apache-2.0.

instruction: What mood is the person in during this brief clip from a podcast interview?
description: This model implements categorical emotion classification as described in the paper 'Vox-Profile: A Speech Foundation Model Benchmark for Characterizing Diverse Speaker and Speech Traits'. The training pipeline used is the top-performing solution (SAILER) in the INTERSPEECH 2025—Speech Emotion Challenge. However, compared to the official challenge submission system, this model does not use all the augmentations and does not use the transcript, instead creating a speech-only system to make the model simple but still effective. The model is trained on the MSP-Podcast data and is designed to classify speech into the following emotions: Anger, Contempt, Disgust, Fear, Happiness, Neutral, Sadness, Surprise, and Other. The model may be sensitive to content information when making emotion predictions, which could be a beneficial feature for classifying emotions from online content. The model is based on the Whisper-Large V3 architecture and is available through Huggingface under the identifier 'tiantiaf/whisper-large-v3-msp-podcast-emotion'. It is intended for use with audio files that are a maximum of 15 seconds in length, sampled at 16kHz and in mono channel format. The model is released under the Open RAIL license, and users are required to respect the privacy and consent of data subjects and adhere to relevant laws and regulations in their jurisdictions. The model is not intended for clinical or diagnostic applications, surveillance, privacy-invasive applications, or commercial use.

instruction: Can you extract key features from a video clip showing a cat chasing a laser pointer?
description: The VideoMAEv2-Huge model is a video masked autoencoder pre-trained for 1200 epochs in a self-supervised manner on the UnlabeldHybrid-1M dataset. It was introduced in the paper 'VideoMAE V2: Scaling Video Masked Autoencoders with Dual Masking' by Wang et al. and first released in 2023. The model is designed for video feature extraction and can be used to extract features from video inputs. It supports tasks involving video processing and analysis. The model is based on an architecture that incorporates dual masking strategies to enhance learning from unlabeled video data. The model can be loaded using the Transformers library, with a video input processed through a VideoMAEImageProcessor and passed to the model for feature extraction. The model is available under the identifier 'OpenGVLab/VideoMAEv2-Huge'.

instruction: Can you extract the intermediate features from this image of a bicycle?
description: This is a MobileNet-V4 image classification model, trained on the ImageNet-1k dataset by Ross Wightman. The model was trained using `timm` scripts with hyper-parameters inspired by the MobileNet-V4 paper and enhanced by `timm` features. It is noted that these are the only known MobileNet-V4 (MNV4) weights available at the time, as official weights for Tensorflow models have not been released. The model is designed for image classification and can also serve as a feature backbone. It has 2.2 million parameters, 0.1 GMACs, and 1.2 million activations. The training image size is 224 x 224, and the test image size is 256 x 256. The model supports tasks such as image classification, feature map extraction, and image embeddings. For image classification, the model can be used to generate top-5 probabilities and class indices. For feature map extraction, the model can output feature maps with varying shapes, such as [1, 32, 112, 112] and [1, 480, 7, 7]. For image embeddings, the model can produce a tensor of image features by removing the classifier or using forward features and forward head methods. The model is based on the MobileNetV4 architecture described in the paper titled 'MobileNetV4 -- Universal Models for the Mobile Ecosystem' and is implemented using the PyTorch Image Models library. The model is evaluated on the ImageNet-1k dataset, and its performance is compared with other models in terms of top-1 accuracy, top-5 accuracy, parameter count, and image size. The model has a top-1 accuracy of 64.762% and a top-5 accuracy of 85.514% on 224 x 224 images. It is important to note that these are the only known MNV4 weights and that official weights for Tensorflow models are not yet available.

instruction: What are the key elements visible in this architectural blueprint?
description: Qwen2.5-VL is a vision-language model designed to understand and interact with visual and textual content. It is part of the Qwen family and builds upon the Qwen2-VL model with several key enhancements. The model is capable of recognizing common objects and analyzing texts, charts, icons, graphics, and layouts within images.
