It functions as a visual agent that can reason and dynamically direct tools, enabling it to perform computer and phone use. Qwen2.5-VL can understand long videos, up to one hour in length, and can pinpoint relevant video segments to capture events. It supports visual localization by generating bounding boxes or points and provides structured outputs for data such as scanned invoices, forms, and tables, which is beneficial for applications in finance and commerce. The model architecture includes dynamic resolution and frame rate training for video understanding, which allows the model to comprehend videos at various sampling rates. The model also features a streamlined and efficient vision encoder with window attention in the ViT architecture, optimized with SwiGLU and RMSNorm to align with the Qwen2.5 LLM structure. The model is available in three versions with 3, 7, and 72 billion parameters, with the 7B version being instruction-tuned and available in this repository. Qwen2.5-VL has been evaluated on various benchmarks, including image, video, and agent benchmarks, where it demonstrates strong performance across multiple metrics. The model supports a wide range of input formats for images and videos, including local files, URLs, and base64-encoded data. It also provides options for adjusting image resolution and handling long texts through techniques like YaRN for length extrapolation. However, it is noted that YaRN can impact the performance of temporal and spatial localization tasks and is not recommended for use in such scenarios.

instruction: Find all the books in this image and mark their locations with boxes.
description: LLMDet (base variant) was proposed by Shenghao Fu, Qize Yang, Qijie Mo, Junkai Yan, Xihan Wei, Jingke Meng, Xiaohua Xie, and Wei-Shi Zheng. LLMDet improves upon previous models by co-training the model with a large language model. The model can be used for zero-shot object detection. The raw model supports inference tasks, and checkpoints are available for use, though they do not include the large language model used during training. Inference with LLMDet is identical to that of other similar models. The model was trained on a combination of datasets, including O365, GoldG, GRIT, V3Det, O365V2, OpenImageV6, and GroundingCap-1M. Evaluation results on LVIS show that the model achieves a MiniVal APr of 44.7, MiniVal APc of 37.3, MiniVal APf of 39.5, and MiniVal AP of 50.7 when trained on (O365, GoldG, GRIT, V3Det) + GroundingCap-1M. When trained on (O365, GoldG, V3Det) + GroundingCap-1M, the model achieves a MiniVal APr of 48.3, MiniVal APc of 40.8, MiniVal APf of 43.1, and MiniVal AP of 54.3. When trained on (O365V2, OpenImageV6, GoldG) + GroundingCap-1M, the model achieves a MiniVal APr of 51.1, MiniVal APc of 45.1, MiniVal APf of 46.1, and MiniVal AP of 56.6. On Val1.0, the model achieves APr of 34.9, APc of 26.0, APf of 30.1, and AP of 44.3 when trained on (O365, GoldG, GRIT, V3Det) + GroundingCap-1M. When trained on (O365, GoldG, V3Det) + GroundingCap-1M, the model achieves Val1.0 APr of 38.5, APc of 28.2, APf of 34.3, and AP of 47.8. When trained on (O365V2, OpenImageV6, GoldG) + GroundingCap-1M, the model achieves Val1.0 APr of 42.0, APc of 31.6, APf of 38.8, and AP of 50.2. The model is designed to detect objects in images using textual descriptions as input, and it supports postprocessing of outputs to extract bounding boxes, confidence scores, and detected labels. The model is intended for use in scenarios where object detection is required without prior training on specific object categories.

instruction: I’m trying to build a recommendation system—can you help by finding similar items?
description: This is a Sentence Transformer model trained on the train_set dataset. It maps sentences and paragraphs to a 1024-dimensional dense vector space and can be used for semantic textual similarity, semantic search, paraphrase mining, text classification, clustering, and more. The model is trained using a Transformer Encoder and is designed to handle a maximum sequence length of 8192 tokens. It uses cosine similarity as the similarity function. The model architecture includes a Transformer with XLMRobertaModel, a Pooling layer with specific configuration parameters, and a Normalize layer. The model is trained on a Korean dataset in addition to Chinese and English, but learning other languages beyond these is insufficient, so additional learning is needed to optimize use of other languages. Evaluation results show that the model performs well on the Korean Embedding Benchmark and the Miracl benchmark, with high cosine_ndcg@10 scores. However, the model's performance may vary depending on the domain and the length of the corpus. The model was trained with specific hyperparameters, including a batch size of 32768, a learning rate of 3e-05, and a warmup ratio of 0.03333333333333333.

instruction: Describe the content of the first three minutes of this video.
description: Qwen2.5-VL is a vision-language model designed to understand and interact with visual and textual information. It is part of the Qwen family and builds upon the Qwen2-VL model, incorporating improvements based on developer feedback. The model is capable of recognizing common objects, analyzing texts, charts, icons, graphics, and layouts within images. It functions as a visual agent that can reason and dynamically direct tools, enabling it to perform computer and phone use. Qwen2.5-VL can understand long videos, up to one hour in length, and pinpoint relevant video segments. It supports visual localization by generating bounding boxes or points and provides structured outputs for data such as scanned invoices, forms, and tables, which is beneficial for applications in finance and commerce. The model architecture includes dynamic resolution and frame rate training for video understanding, using dynamic FPS sampling and updated mRoPE for temporal sequence and speed learning. The vision encoder is streamlined and efficient, incorporating window attention, SwiGLU, and RMSNorm to align with the Qwen2.5 LLM structure. The model is available in three versions with 3, 7, and 72 billion parameters, with this repository containing the instruction-tuned 7B model with AWQ. Evaluation results show performance metrics on benchmarks such as MMMU_VAL, DocVQA_VAL, MMBench_DEV_EN, and MathVista_MINI, with accuracy values reported for different model sizes and quantization methods. The model supports various input formats for images and videos, including local files, base64, and URLs. Users can control image resolution and processing parameters to balance performance and resource usage. For long text and video inputs, the model can be configured with YaRN to enhance length extrapolation, though this may impact performance on temporal and spatial localization tasks. The model is available through Hugging Face Transformers and ModelScope, with specific installation and usage instructions provided.

instruction: Tôi cần tìm các văn bản pháp lý có nội dung gần giống với 'phạt tiền khi vi phạm giao thông'.
description: Vietnamese_Embedding is an embedding model fine-tuned from the BGE-M3 model to enhance retrieval capabilities for Vietnamese. The model was trained on approximately 300,000 triplets of queries, positive documents, and negative documents for Vietnamese. The model was trained with a maximum sequence length of 2048. The model type is a Sentence Transformer. The base model is unspecified. The maximum sequence length is 2048 tokens. The output dimensionality is 1024 dimensions. The similarity function used is dot product similarity. The model is designed for the Vietnamese language. The model is licensed under Apache 2.0. The model is intended for tasks involving sentence embeddings and document retrieval. Evaluation results on the Legal Zalo 2021 dataset, which was not part of the training data, show the following performance metrics: Accuracy@1 is 0.7274, Accuracy@3 is 0.8992, Accuracy@5 is 0.9305, Accuracy@10 is 0.9568, and MRR@10 is 0.8181. These results are compared to other models such as Vietnamese_Reranker with Accuracy@1 of 0.7944 and MRR@10 of 0.8672, Vietnamese_Embedding_v2 with Accuracy@1 of 0.7262 and MRR@10 of 0.8149, Vietnamese-bi-encoder (BKAI) with Accuracy@1 of 0.7109 and MRR@10 of 0.7951, and BGE-M3 with Accuracy@1 of 0.5682 and MRR@10 of 0.6822. Vietnamese_Reranker and Vietnamese_Embedding_v2 were trained on 1,100,000 triplets. Although the score on the legal domain drops slightly for Vietnamese_Embedding_v2, it performs well on other domains due to the larger dataset used in training. The model can be accessed via a link. Evaluation results can be reproduced by running the code python evaluation_model.py with data downloaded from Kaggle.

instruction: What is the overall theme of this visual essay on modern art?
description: mPLUG-Owl3 is a state-of-the-art multi-modal large language model designed to address the challenges of long image sequence understanding. It introduces Hyper Attention, a mechanism that enhances the speed of long visual sequence understanding in multi-modal large language models by sixfold, enabling the processing of visual sequences that are eight times longer. The model maintains strong performance on single-image, multi-image, and video tasks. It supports chat with images and videos, and is compatible with specific attention implementations such as 'sdpa' and 'flash_attention_2'. The model is available for use with a processor that handles both image and video inputs, and it includes a tokenizer for text processing. Performance is maintained across a range of tasks, including those involving extended visual sequences. The model is described in a paper titled 'mPLUG-Owl3: Towards Long Image-Sequence Understanding in Multi-Modal Large Language Models' by Jiabo Ye, Haiyang Xu, Haowei Liu, Anwen Hu, Ming Yan, Qi Qian, Ji Zhang, Fei Huang, and Jingren Zhou, available on arXiv with the identifier 2408.04840.

instruction: What is the likely time of day shown in this photograph?
description: Perception Encoder (PE) is a state-of-the-art encoder for image and video understanding, trained using a simple vision-language learning approach. It is a family of large-scale vision encoder models with state-of-the-art performance on a wide variety of vision tasks. PE is developed by Meta and uses a robust contrastive pretraining recipe, followed by finetuning on synthetically aligned videos. This approach allows PE to outperform existing models on classification and retrieval tasks, while also producing strong, general features that scale for downstream tasks. PE enables large-scale contrastive pretraining to transfer to downstream tasks through alignment tuning, leveraging those general features. The PE core is the base model, trained with a robust image pretraining schedule and finetuned on data generated by a synthetic video data engine. PE core currently comes in three sizes: B, L, and G, with G being the main checkpoint and B and L models distilled from it. The B/16 model has 0.09B vision parameters and 0.31B text parameters, with a resolution of 224px and 32 tokens. The L/14 model has 0.32B vision parameters and 0.31B text parameters, with a resolution of 336px and 32 tokens. The G/14 model has 1.88B vision parameters and 0.47B text parameters, with a resolution of 448px and 72 tokens. All PE core models use an attention pooling block with 8 heads on top of the vision tower, and the L and B models additionally have a class token for global aggregation. PE core achieves strong results on zero-shot image classification and retrieval, as well as zero-shot video classification and retrieval. For example, the B/16 model achieves 78.4% on IN-1k, 71.7% on IN-v2, 62.4% on IN-A, 71.9% on ObjectNet, 50.9% on COCO-T2I, 65.6% on Kinetics-400, and 47.6% on VTT-T2I. The L/14 model achieves 83.5% on IN-1k, 77.9% on IN-v2, 89.0% on IN-A, 84.7% on ObjectNet, 57.1% on COCO-T2I, 73.4% on Kinetics-400, and 50.3% on VTT-T2I. The G/14 model achieves 85.4% on IN-1k, 80.2% on IN-v2, 92.6% on IN-A, 88.2% on ObjectNet, 58.1% on COCO-T2I, 76.9% on Kinetics-400, and 51.2% on VTT-T2I. PE core performs particularly well on challenging benchmarks such as ObjectNet and ImageNet-A. The model can be used for image and text feature extraction, and it supports multiple configurations. It is available for use through a GitHub repository, and users can load the model using provided code. The model is based on a PyTorch implementation and requires specific dependencies for installation and use. The model is described in a technical report and is associated with two arXiv citations.

instruction: Create a video showing a person painting a landscape on a canvas, with the brushstrokes visible.
description: Wan2.1 is a comprehensive and open suite of video foundation models designed to push the boundaries of video generation. It offers state-of-the-art performance, consistently outperforming existing open-source models and state-of-the-art commercial solutions across multiple benchmarks. The T2V-1.3B model requires only 8.19 GB VRAM, making it compatible with nearly all consumer-grade GPUs. It can generate a 5-second 480P video on an RTX 4090 in about 4 minutes, with performance comparable to some closed-source models. Wan2.1 supports multiple tasks, including Text-to-Video, Image-to-Video, Video Editing, Text-to-Image, and Video-to-Audio, advancing the field of video generation. It is the first video model capable of generating both Chinese and English text, enhancing its practical applications. The suite includes a powerful video VAE called Wan-VAE, which delivers exceptional efficiency and performance, encoding and decoding 1080P videos of any length while preserving temporal information. Wan2.1 is built on the mainstream diffusion transformer paradigm, incorporating innovations such as a novel spatio-temporal VAE, scalable training strategies, large-scale data construction, and automated evaluation metrics. The model uses a Flow Matching framework within the Diffusion Transformer paradigm, with a T5 Encoder to encode multilingual text input and cross-attention in each transformer block to embed the text into the model structure. An MLP with a Linear layer and a SiLU layer processes the input time embeddings and predicts six modulation parameters individually, shared across all transformer blocks. The T2V-1.3B model has 1.3 billion parameters, with a dimension of 1536, input and output dimensions of 16, a feedforward dimension of 8960, a frequency dimension of 256, 12 heads, and 30 layers. The T2V-14B model has 14 billion parameters, with a dimension of 5120, input and output dimensions of 16, a feedforward dimension of 13824, a frequency dimension of 256, 40 heads, and 40 layers. The model is trained on a large-scale dataset of images and videos, curated through a four-step data cleaning process focusing on fundamental dimensions, visual quality, and motion quality. Evaluation results using the Wan-Bench framework show that the T2V-1.3B model surpasses the overall metrics of larger open-source models, demonstrating the effectiveness of Wan2.1's architecture and data construction pipeline. Computational efficiency tests on different GPUs show that the model can be run on a single 4090 GPU with offloading techniques. The model is licensed under the Apache 2.0 License, and users are responsible for ensuring their use complies with the license terms, including not generating harmful or illegal content. The model is intended to serve as an easy-to-use tool for creative teams in video creation and a high-quality foundational model for academic teams with limited computing resources, facilitating the rapid development of the video creation community and the swift advancement of video technology.

instruction: Please locate all the cars and trucks in this fisheye image taken from a self-driving car's camera.
description: The `fisheye8k_facebook_deformable-detr-detic` model is a component of the **Mcity Data Engine**, an open-source system designed for iterative model improvement through open-vocabulary data selection. This engine provides modules for the complete data-based development cycle, from data acquisition to model deployment, specifically addressing the challenge of detecting long-tail and novel classes in large amounts of unlabeled data, particularly in Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITS). The model is a fine-tuned version of the `deformable-detr-detic` base model, specializing in object detection within the context of the Mcity Data Engine's workflows. It enables the identification of objects even for classes not seen during its initial fine-tuning, through its open-vocabulary capabilities. The model is primarily intended for **zero-shot object detection** within Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITS) and related domains. It is designed to assist researchers and practitioners in identifying rare and novel classes of interest from raw visual data, facilitating the continuous improvement of AI models. Potential use cases include detecting various types of road users and vehicles in complex traffic scenarios, identifying long-tail or previously unseen objects in automotive perception datasets, and serving as a component within larger data curation and model training pipelines. Limitations include potential variability in performance on highly out-of-distribution scenarios, the requirement for a powerful GPU for optimal utilization of the Mcity Data Engine workflows, and possible differences in performance on standard perspective images, as the model was fine-tuned on fisheye camera data. The model was fine-tuned on the Fisheye8K dataset, with the Mcity Data Engine leveraging data selection processes to focus on detecting long-tail and novel classes, which is crucial for ITS applications. The model's `config.json` indicates it was fine-tuned for classes such as 'Bus', 'Bike', 'Car', 'Pedestrian', and 'Truck'. During training, the following hyperparameters were used: learning_rate of 5e-05, train_batch_size of 1, eval_batch_size of 8, seed of 0, optimizer of ADAMW_TORCH with betas of (0.9, 0.999) and epsilon of 1e-08, lr_scheduler_type of cosine, num_epochs of 36, and mixed_precision_training using Native AMP. Training loss values decreased over the course of training, with a final training loss of 1.5963 at epoch 16. The validation loss reached a minimum of 1.8862 at epoch 11, and the final validation loss was 2.1348. The model was developed using Transformers 4.48.3, Pytorch 2.5.1+cu124, Datasets 3.2.0, and Tokenizers 0.21.0. Mcity acknowledges Amazon Web Services (AWS) for their pivotal role in providing the cloud infrastructure on which the Data Engine depends. Special thanks are given to contributors to the Mcity Data Engine.

instruction: I need a detailed pose map of the person in this image for a scientific study. Can you provide that?
description: The SynthPose model was proposed by Yoni Gozlan, Antoine Falisse, Scott Uhlrich, Anthony Gatti, Michael Black, and Akshay Chaudhari. This model uses a VitPose Base backbone and is a new approach that enables fine-tuning of pre-trained 2D human pose models to predict an arbitrarily denser set of keypoints for accurate kinematic analysis through the use of synthetic data. This particular variant was fine-tuned on a set of keypoints usually found on motion capture setups, and includes COCO keypoints as well. The model predicts 52 markers, where the first 17 are the COCO keypoints and the next 35 are anatomical markers. The model is designed to support tasks related to human pose estimation, including the detection of human figures in images and the prediction of detailed anatomical keypoints for each detected person. The model is intended for use in applications requiring precise kinematic analysis, such as biomechanics or motion capture. The model's architecture is based on the VitPose Base variant, and it is fine-tuned to enhance its ability to predict a larger and more detailed set of keypoints than standard models. The model is evaluated for its ability to accurately predict these additional keypoints, which are typically not included in standard pose estimation models. The model includes explicit caveats regarding its intended use and the necessity of using appropriate detection and processing pipelines to achieve accurate results. It is recommended to use a detector to identify human figures in images before applying the model for pose estimation. The model is not intended for use in scenarios where the input data does not conform to the expected format or where the model's assumptions about the input are not met.

instruction: Which of these financial reports includes a breakdown of quarterly revenue by region?
description: jina-reranker-m0 is a multilingual multimodal reranker model designed to rank visual documents across multiple languages. It accepts a query and a collection of visually rich document images, including pages with text, figures, tables, infographics, and various layouts across multiple domains and over 29 languages. The model outputs a ranked list of documents based on their relevance to the input query. Compared to jina-reranker-v2-base-multilingual, jina-reranker-m0 improves text reranking for multilingual content, long documents, and code searching tasks. The model is built on a decoder-only vision language model architecture, using the base model Qwen2-VL-2B, with fine-tuning applied to the language model using LoRA techniques. An MLP head is post-trained to generate ranking scores that measure query-document relevance. The model is optimized with pairwise and listwise ranking losses to produce discriminative relevance scores. This represents a significant architectural shift from previous cross-encoder models, with a base model of Qwen2-VL-2B (2.4 B parameters) compared to Jina-XLM-RoBERTa (278 M parameters). The model supports a maximum context length of 10,240 tokens (query + document), dynamic image resolution processing (768 × 28 × 28 patches), and multilingual support for 29+ languages. It supports tasks including Text2Text, Text2Image, Image2Text, and Text2Mixed. The model is capable of zero-shot domain transfer, performing well on unseen domains and document types without specific fine-tuning. It also has enhanced capabilities for programming language search and technical document ranking. The model can be used via Jina AI's API or the transformers library, with support for reranking visual and textual documents, as well as querying textual or image documents with image queries. The model has been evaluated on visual retrieval benchmarks such as ViDoRe, MBEIR, and Winoground, as well as text-to-text reranking benchmarks, with performance metrics including NDCG@10 and recall@10. The model is licensed under CC BY-NC 4.0 for non-commercial use.

description: This model is a fine-tuned XLM-RoBERTa transformer model with a classification head on top, designed for language detection tasks. It supports sequence classification for 20 languages: arabic (ar), bulgarian (bg), german (de), modern greek (el), english (en), spanish (es), french (fr), hindi (hi), italian (it), japanese (ja), dutch (nl), polish (pl), portuguese (pt), russian (ru), swahili (sw), thai (th), turkish (tr), urdu (ur), vietnamese (vi), and chinese (zh). The model was fine-tuned on a dataset containing text sequences in these 20 languages, with 70k samples in the training set and 10k each in the validation and test sets. The average accuracy on the test set is 99.6%, which matches the average macro/weighted F1-score due to the test set being perfectly balanced. For each of the 20 languages, the model achieves the following test set performance: arabic (precision 0.998, recall 0.996, F1-score 0.997, support 500), bulgarian (precision 0.998, recall 0.964, F1-score 0.981, support 500), german (precision 0.998, recall 0.996, F1-score 0.997, support 500), modern greek (precision 0.996, recall 1.000, F1-score 0.998, support 500), english (precision 1.000, recall 1.000, F1-score 1.000, support 500), spanish (precision 0.967, recall 1.000, F1-score 0.983, support 500), french (precision 1.000, recall 1.000, F1-score 1.000, support 500), hindi (precision 0.994, recall 0.992, F1-score 0.993, support 500), italian (precision 1.000, recall 0.992, F1-score 0.996, support 500), japanese (precision 0.996, recall 0.996, F1-score 0.996, support 500), dutch (precision 1.000, recall 1.000, F1-score 1.000, support 500), polish (precision 1.000, recall 1.000, F1-score 1.000, support 500), portuguese (precision 0.988, recall 1.000, F1-score 0.994, support 500), russian (precision 1.000, recall 0.994, F1-score 0.997, support 500), swahili (precision 1.000, recall 1.000, F1-score 1.000, support 500), thai (precision 1.000, recall 0.998, F1-score 0.999, support 500), turkish (precision 0.994, recall 0.992, F1-score 0.993, support 500), urdu (precision 1.000, recall 1.000, F1-score 1.000, support 500), vietnamese (precision 0.992, recall 1.000, F1-score 0.996, support 500), and chinese (precision 1.000, recall 1.000, F1-score 1.000, support 500). When compared to the Python library langid, which was constrained to the same 20 languages, the model achieves an average accuracy of 98.5% on the test set. The model was fine-tuned using the Trainer API with the following hyperparameters: learning rate 2e-05, train batch size 64, eval batch size 128, seed 42, optimizer Adam with betas (0.9, 0.999) and epsilon 1e-08, lr scheduler type linear, number of epochs 2, and mixed precision training using Native AMP. The validation results on the valid split of the Language Identification dataset are as follows: after the first epoch, the training loss was 0.2492, validation loss 0.0149, accuracy 0.9969, and F1 0.9969; after the second epoch, the training loss was 0.0101, validation loss 0.0103, accuracy 0.9977, and F1 0.9977. The model was developed using the following framework versions: Transformers 4.12.5, PyTorch 1.10.0+cu111, Datasets 1.15.1, and Tokenizers 0.10.3.

instruction: Could you summarize the key points from this voice memo?
description: Voila is a new family of large voice-language foundation models designed to enhance human-AI interaction experiences. It breaks away from the limitations of traditional voice AI systems, such as high latency, loss of vocal nuances, and mechanical responses, by employing an innovative end-to-end model design and a novel hierarchical Transformer architecture. This approach enables real-time, autonomous, and rich voice interactions with latency as low as 195 ms, surpassing average human response times. Voila combines advanced voice and language modeling to offer customizable, persona-driven engagements and excels in a range of audio tasks, including automatic speech recognition (ASR), text-to-speech (TTS), and speech translation across six languages. The model supports millions of pre-built and custom voices, with fast voice switching during conversation, and functions as a unified model for various audio tasks. Evaluation results show that Voila achieves a score of 30.56 on the Voila Benchmark, outperforming SpeechGPT (13.29) and Moshi (11.45). In ASR performance on the LibriSpeech test-clean dataset, Voila attains a word error rate (WER) of 4.8%, outperforming Moshi's 5.7%. When both models use LibriSpeech training data, Voila achieves an impressive WER of 2.7%. In TTS evaluation, Voila achieves a WER of 3.2% (and 2.8% with LibriSpeech training data), outperforming Moshi (4.7%), Vall-E (5.9%), and YourTTS (7.7%). The model is available in several variants, including Voila-base, Voila-Chat, Voila-Autonomous (preview), Voila-Audio-alpha, and Voila-Tokenizer. The inference code and model weights have been released as of April 28, 2025. The model is accompanied by two datasets: Voila Benchmark, a novel speech evaluation benchmark, and Voila Voice Library, which provides millions of pre-built and customizable voices.

instruction: Can you produce a set of images that represent a dragon from different perspectives based on a written description?
description: MV-Adapter is a creative productivity tool that seamlessly transfers text-to-image models to multi-view generators. It is designed to create high-fidelity multi-view images using various base text-to-image models and various conditions. The model supports generating 768x768 multi-view images and works well with personalized models such as DreamShaper and Animagine, as well as LCM and ControlNet. It supports text or image to multi-view generation, enabling 3D reconstruction, and also supports generation with geometry guidance for 3D texture generation. Additionally, it allows for arbitrary view generation. The model is based on different base models, including SD2.1 and SDXL, and supports various configurations such as text-to-multiview, image-to-multiview, text-geometry-to-multiview, image-geometry-to-multiview, and image-to-arbitrary-views. The model is described in the paper titled 'MV-Adapter: Multi-view Consistent Image Generation Made Easy' by Huang, Zehuan and Guo, Yuanchen and Wang, Haoran and Yi, Ran and Ma, Lizhuang and Cao, Yan-Pei and Sheng, Lu, published as an arXiv preprint in 2024.

instruction: Please translate the following English dialogue into Turkish for a movie subtitle.
description: The opus-mt-tc-big-en-tr is a neural machine translation model designed to translate from English (en) to Turkish (tr). It is part of an initiative to make neural machine translation models widely available and accessible for many languages. The model was originally trained using the efficient NMT implementation written in pure C++ and has been converted to pyTorch using the transformers library by Hugging Face. The training data is sourced from the OPUSTCv20210807+bt dataset, and the training pipelines follow established procedures. The model uses the transformer-big architecture and applies SentencePiece tokenization with spm32k for both source and target languages. The model was released on 2022-02-25. It supports translation tasks and can be used with the Hugging Face Transformers library, either directly through model and tokenizer classes or via translation pipelines. The model has been evaluated on several test sets, achieving chr-F scores of 0.68726 on the tatoeba-test-v2021-08-07 dataset, 0.62829 on the flores101-devtest dataset, 0.58947 on the newsdev2016 dataset, 0.57624 on the newstest2016 dataset, 0.58858 on the newstest2017 dataset, and 0.57848 on the newstest2018 dataset. Corresponding BLEU scores are 42.3, 31.4, 21.9, 23.4, 25.4, and 22.6, respectively. The model conversion was performed using transformers version 4.16.2, with the OPUS-MT git hash 3405783, on April 13, 2022. The work is supported by multiple grants, including one funded by the European Research Council (ERC) under the European Union’s Horizon 2020 research and innovation programme (grant agreement No 771113) and another under grant agreement No 780069. Computational resources and IT infrastructure were provided by an institution in Finland.

instruction: Can you analyze this audio and tell me if it was made by an AI or a real artist?
description: SONICS is a model designed to distinguish between human-composed and synthetic songs, addressing the challenges posed by AI-generated music. It introduces a novel dataset for end-to-end Synthetic Song Detection (SSD), comprising over 97,000 songs (4,751 hours) with over 49,000 synthetic songs from platforms like Suno and Udio. The model emphasizes the importance of modeling long-range temporal dependencies in songs for effective authenticity detection, a feature absent in existing methods. To achieve this, the model introduces SpecTTTra, a novel architecture that improves time and memory efficiency over conventional CNN and Transformer-based models. For long songs, the top-performing variant of the model outperforms ViT by 8% in F1 score, is 38% faster, and uses 26% less memory, while also surpassing ConvNeXt with a 1% F1 score gain, 20% speed boost, and 67% memory reduction. The model's architecture includes an embedding dimension of 384, 6 heads, 12 layers, and an MLP ratio of 2.67. Audio processing involves a sample rate of 16kHz, an FFT size of 2048, a hop length of 512, 128 Mel bands, a frequency range of 20Hz to 8kHz, and mean-std normalization. The model is intended for use in identifying counterfeit songs and safeguarding artistic integrity in the context of AI-generated music.

instruction: Design a stable and creative brick layout for a table.
description: BrickGPT is the first approach for generating physically stable toy brick models from text prompts. It is an autoregressive model that operates in English and is licensed under the MIT license. The model was fine-tuned using LoRA applied to the `q_proj` and `v_proj` matrices, with AdamW optimization and a learning rate that followed a cosine decay with warmup. Training was conducted using bf16 mixed precision for 3 epochs with a global batch size of 64. The maximum learning rate was 0.002, with 100 warmup steps. The LoRA rank was 32, with a LoRA alpha of 16 and a LoRA dropout of 0.05. The model was trained on a dataset of 47k toy brick structures and was evaluated on 21 object categories: basket, bed, bench, birdhouse, bookshelf, bottle, bowl, bus, camera, car, chair, guitar, jar, mug, piano, pot, sofa, table, tower, train, and vessel. The model is limited to creating structures made of 1-unit-tall cuboid bricks on a 20x20x20 grid, and its performance may be limited for prompts outside these categories. The model was developed with support from the Packard Foundation, Cisco Research Grant, Amazon Faculty Award, the Manufacturing Futures Institute at Carnegie Mellon University through a grant from the Richard King Mellon Foundation, and the Microsoft Research PhD Fellowship. Training was conducted using 8x NVIDIA RTX A6000 (48 GB) GPUs for 0.5 hours. The model is associated with the PEFT 0.15.0 framework.

instruction: Can you provide an overview of how the immune system fights off infections?
description: ApolloMoE is a medical language model designed to support a wide range of languages, including 12 major languages such as English, Chinese, French, Hindi, Spanish, Arabic, Russian, Japanese, Korean, German, Italian, and Portuguese, as well as 38 minor languages. The model is intended to democratize access to medical large language models by providing multilingual support. It is based on a mixture-of-experts (MoE) architecture, which allows for efficient and scalable model design. The model includes both dense and post-MoE variants, with sizes ranging from 0.5B to 9B parameters. The model is trained and evaluated on the ApolloMoEDataset and tested using the ApolloMoEBench benchmark. Evaluation results include performance across multiple domains such as clinical knowledge, medical genetics, anatomy, professional medicine, college biology, and college medicine. The model is available for download through Hugging Face, and inference and training procedures are provided in the model card. The model supports different input formats depending on the model size, and users are advised to follow the specified formatting for each variant. The model's training and evaluation processes involve data preparation, tokenization, and generation of scores for benchmarking. The model is described as covering a broad range of languages and is intended for use in medical and educational contexts. However, some results were excluded from the paper due to high variability, and certain datasets were not used in the final evaluation.

instruction: Can you determine if this statement about defense spending relates to a major policy topic?
description: The xlm-roberta-large-czech-media-cap-v3 is an xlm-roberta-large model fine-tuned on multilingual training data containing texts from the media domain labeled according to the master codebook of the Comparative Agendas Project. The model is designed for text classification, specifically for identifying major policy topics in text. It supports the classification of text into 21 CAP major policy topic codes, with an additional label '999' to indicate the absence of relevant policy content. The model uses a sentencepiece tokenizer and was fine-tuned with the following key hyperparameters: 5 training epochs, a batch size of 40, and a learning rate of 5e-06. The model was evaluated on a test set of 34,410 examples, achieving an overall accuracy of 0.72. Performance metrics include precision, recall, and f1-score for each label, with the weighted average f1-score being 0.72. The model is used by an open-source and free natural language processing tool designed to simplify and speed up projects for comparative research. Model performance can be improved by extending the training sets with CAP-coded corpora of any domain and language. Due to gated access, a token must be provided when loading the model. Earlier versions of the Transformers package may require the use of the use_auth_token parameter instead. If a RuntimeError occurs when loading the model using the from_pretrained() method, adding ignore_mismatched_sizes=True should resolve the issue.

instruction: Suggest five books that are great for improving critical thinking skills.
description: Qwen2.5 is the latest series of Qwen large language models. The Qwen2.5 series includes a range of base language models and instruction-tuned language models with parameter sizes ranging from 0.5 to 72 billion. This model introduces several improvements over Qwen2, including significantly more knowledge and enhanced capabilities in coding and mathematics, achieved through specialized expert models in these domains. It also shows significant improvements in instruction following, generating long texts (over 8K tokens), understanding structured data such as tables, and generating structured outputs, especially JSON. The model is more resilient to the diversity of system prompts, which enhances role-play implementation and condition-setting for chatbots. It supports long-context processing up to 128K tokens and can generate up to 8K tokens. The model provides multilingual support for over 29 languages, including Chinese, English, French, Spanish, Portuguese, German, Italian, Russian, Japanese, Korean, Vietnamese, Thai, and Arabic. This specific model is the instruction-tuned 0.5B Qwen2.5 model. It is a causal language model that has undergone pretraining and post-training. The architecture includes transformers with RoPE, SwiGLU, RMSNorm, attention QKV bias, and tied word embeddings. It has 0.49B total parameters and 0.36B non-embedding parameters. The model consists of 24 layers and uses 14 attention heads for queries and 2 for keys and values. It supports a full context length of 32,768 tokens and can generate up to 8,192 tokens. The code for Qwen2.5 is available in the latest Hugging Face `transformers` library, and it is recommended to use the latest version to avoid errors. Earlier versions of `transformers` (before 4.37.0) may result in a KeyError: 'qwen2'. Detailed evaluation results are available in a separate document, and information on GPU memory requirements and throughput is also provided. If you find this work helpful, you may cite the associated publications.

instruction: Create a voice recording of this motivational quote in a calm and encouraging tone.
description: OuteTTS Version 1.0 is a compact, high-performance text-to-speech (TTS) model designed for speech synthesis and voice cloning. It supports a wide range of languages and features automatic word alignment, native multilingual text processing, and enhanced metadata integration for improved synthesis quality. The model uses a DAC audio encoder with two codebooks for high-fidelity audio reconstruction and includes special tokens for audio codebooks. It supports one-shot voice cloning with approximately 10 seconds of reference audio and can align raw text at the word level, even for languages without clear word boundaries. The model is trained on approximately 60,000 hours of audio and supports a maximum context window of 8,192 tokens. It is optimized for use with a speaker reference to maintain vocal characteristics, emotion, and accent. For optimal performance, a temperature of 0.4 is recommended, along with a repetition penalty of 1.1 applied to a 64-token recent window. The model is built on a base Llama3.2 architecture and has undergone continued pre-training and fine-tuning. It is intended for ethical use in applications that enhance accessibility, creativity, and communication, with prohibitions on impersonation, misleading content, harassment, and unauthorized voice cloning. The model is available through the outetts library, which automatically sets up necessary samplers and patches for all backends.

instruction: What is the overall mood or theme of this artwork based on the visual elements?
description: Qwen2.5-VL is a vision-language model designed to understand and interact with visual and textual information. It is part of the Qwen family and builds upon the Qwen2-VL model, incorporating improvements based on developer feedback. The model is capable of recognizing common objects, analyzing texts, charts, icons, graphics, and layouts within images. It functions as a visual agent that can reason and dynamically direct tools, enabling it to perform computer and phone use. Qwen2.5-VL can understand long videos, up to one hour in length, and pinpoint relevant video segments. It supports visual localization by generating bounding boxes or points and provides structured outputs for data such as scanned invoices, forms, and tables, which is beneficial for applications in finance and commerce. The model architecture includes dynamic resolution and frame rate training for video understanding, using dynamic FPS sampling and updated mRoPE for temporal sequence and speed learning. The vision encoder is streamlined and efficient, incorporating window attention, SwiGLU, and RMSNorm to align with the Qwen2.5 LLM structure. The model is available in three versions with 3, 7, and 72 billion parameters, with this repository containing the instruction-tuned 7B model with AWQ. Evaluation results show performance metrics on benchmarks such as MMMU_VAL, DocVQA_VAL, MMBench_DEV_EN, and MathVista_MINI, with accuracy values reported for different model sizes and quantization methods. The model supports various input formats for images and videos, including local files, base64, and URLs. Users can control image resolution and processing parameters to balance performance and resource usage. For long text and video inputs, the model can be configured with YaRN to enhance length extrapolation, though this may impact performance on temporal and spatial localization tasks. The model is available through Hugging Face Transformers and ModelScope, with specific installation and usage instructions provided.

instruction: Can you highlight the different components in this image and their positions?
description: The DETR (DEtection TRansformer) model is an encoder-decoder transformer with a convolutional backbone, specifically a ResNet-50 architecture. It is designed for end-to-end object detection and can be extended to perform panoptic segmentation by adding a mask head on top of the decoder outputs. The model uses object queries to detect objects in an image, with 100 object queries for the COCO dataset. It is trained using a bipartite matching loss, which compares predicted classes and bounding boxes of each object query to ground truth annotations. The Hungarian matching algorithm is used to create an optimal one-to-one mapping between queries and annotations. The loss function includes cross-entropy for class labels and a combination of L1 and generalized IoU loss for bounding boxes. The model was trained on the COCO 2017 panoptic dataset, which includes 118,000 annotated images for training and 5,000 for validation. During training, images were resized such that the shortest side was at least 800 pixels and the longest side at most 1333 pixels, and normalized using the ImageNet mean and standard deviation. The model was trained for 300 epochs on 16 V100 GPUs with a total batch size of 64. On the COCO 2017 validation set, the model achieves a box average precision (AP) of 38.8, a segmentation AP of 31.1, and a panoptic quality (PQ) of 43.4. The model is intended for use in panoptic segmentation tasks and is available for use with PyTorch. The model was introduced in a paper by Carion et al. and first released by the Hugging Face team, as the original team did not provide a model card.

instruction: Could you help identify the Arabic dialect used in this YouTube video clip?
description: Tamyïz is an accurate and robust Transformer-based model for Arabic Dialect Identification (ADI) in speech. It is adapted from a pre-trained massively multilingual speech model and fine-tuned on diverse Arabic TV broadcast speech to identify five Arabic language varieties: Modern Standard Arabic (MSA), Egyptian Arabic (Masri and Sudani), Gulf Arabic (Khleeji, Iraqi, and Yemeni), Levantine Arabic (Shami), and Maghrebi Arabic (Dialects of al-Maghreb al-Arabi in North Africa). The model can be used as a component in a large-scale speech data collection pipeline to create resources for different Arabic dialects. It can also be used to filter speech data for Modern Standard Arabic (MSA) for text-to-speech (TTS) systems. The model is based on the wav2vec 2.0 architecture and is fine-tuned from the MMS-300m model. It is trained on the MGB-3 ADI-5 dataset, which consists of TV Broadcast speech from Al Jazeera TV, including news, interviews, discussions, and TV shows. The model has been evaluated on a challenging multi-domain benchmark and performed very well, with the expectation that it will be robust to real-world speech samples. The model should not be used to assess fluency or nativeness of speech, determine whether the speaker uses a formal or informal register, make judgments about a speaker's origin, education level, or socioeconomic status, or filter or discriminate against speakers based on dialect. Some Arabic varieties are not well-represented in the training data, and the model may not work well for some dialects such as Yemeni Arabic, Iraqi Arabic, and Saharan Arabic. Additional limitations include the possibility that very short audio samples (< 2 seconds) may not provide enough information for accurate classification, code-switching between dialects (especially mixing with MSA) may result in less reliable classifications, speakers who have lived in multiple dialect regions may exhibit mixed features, and speech from non-typical speakers such as children and people with speech disorders might be challenging for the model. For optimal results, it is recommended to use audio segments of at least 5-10 seconds. Confidence scores may not always be informative, as the model could make a wrong decision but still be very confident. For critical applications, human verification of model predictions is recommended. The model is licensed under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 (CC BY 4.0). If you use this model in your research, please cite the paper titled 'Voice Conversion Improves Cross-Domain Robustness for Spoken Arabic Dialect Identification' by Badr M. Abdullah, Matthew Baas, Bernd Möbius, and Dietrich Klakow, published in Interspeech 2025. For any questions, contact badr dot nlp at gmail dot com.

instruction: How can I improve the structure of my mathematical reasoning for better results?
description: Qwen2.5-Math-RM-72B is a reward model specifically designed to guide the Qwen2.5-Math model throughout the training process by offering more granular feedback on the quality of reasoning and intermediate steps, ultimately facilitating more robust model improvements. It provides preference signals across two languages (Chinese and English) and in dual modes (Chain-of-Thought and Tool-integrated Reasoning), enhancing versatility. The model employs a data selection process via reward model scoring combined with Rejection Sampling to incrementally enhance the quality of responses. It integrates seamlessly into the reinforcement learning training and provides effective reward signals, further improving model performance. By leveraging a combination of response sampling and Best-of-N strategies, the model selects the response with the top score judged by the reward model, yielding better results with increased inference time. For example, Qwen2.5-Math-1.5B-Instruct obtains 83.9 on MATH in RM@8 setting and even surpasses the performance of Qwen2.5-Math-7B-Instruct 83.6 with greedy decoding. RM@N scores are substantially better than Maj@N scores across almost all benchmarks and models. The model is typically used for offering feedback on the quality of reasoning and intermediate steps, serving in Rejection Sampling, reinforcement learning training, and RM@N. It requires the `transformers` library version 4.40.0 or higher, as the latest version is recommended. This is a must because `transformers` integrated Qwen2.5 codes since version 4.37.0. For requirements on GPU memory and the respective throughput, see similar results of Qwen2.

instruction: Can you create a transparent background for this product image by identifying the main object?
description: U-2-Net is a deep learning model designed for image segmentation tasks, particularly for generating detailed masks. It leverages a unique architecture with nested U-blocks that allow the model to capture both high-level semantic features and fine-grained details. U-2-Net has demonstrated high performance in a variety of segmentation tasks, making it a versatile choice for applications such as background removal, object detection, and medical image analysis. The U-2-Net model is built upon a nested U-structure, where each U-block consists of multiple convolutional layers, pooling, and up-sampling operations. The architecture features a combination of down-sampling and up-sampling paths, enabling the model to learn features at different scales. This design allows the U-2-Net to produce accurate and high-resolution segmentation maps. The key components of the architecture include Residual U-blocks (RSU) that enhance feature representation and ensure efficient information flow through the network. The model is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 to match the original U-2-Net model.

instruction: What is the main idea or subject matter of this visual?
description: This is a CLIP ViT-B/16 model trained using the LAION-2B English subset of the LAION-5B dataset. The model was developed by Mehdi Cherti on a supercomputer. It is intended for research purposes, particularly for exploring zero-shot, arbitrary image classification and enabling interdisciplinary studies on the potential impact of such models. The model supports tasks such as zero-shot image classification, image and text retrieval, image classification and other image task fine-tuning, linear probe image classification, and image generation guiding and conditioning. However, any deployed use case—whether commercial or not—is currently out of scope. Non-deployed use cases, such as image search in a constrained environment, are also not recommended unless there is thorough in-domain testing with a specific, fixed class taxonomy. This is due to the high need for task-specific testing, especially given the variability of CLIP’s performance with different class taxonomies, which can make untested and unconstrained deployment potentially harmful. Use cases related to surveillance and facial recognition are always out of scope, regardless of model performance, due to the lack of testing norms and checks to ensure fair use. Additionally, since the model has not been trained or evaluated on any languages other than English, its use should be limited to English language use cases. The LAION-5B dataset used for training is uncurated and may contain discomforting or disturbing content. While it is possible to extract a safer subset using a customized NSFW classifier, harmful content may still be present. The dataset is provided to enable transparent research into the benefits and risks of large-scale models, and it is not recommended for creating ready-to-go industrial products. The model was evaluated using a combination of VTAB (with additional robustness datasets) for classification and COCO and Flickr for retrieval. It achieved a 70.2 zero-shot top-1 accuracy on ImageNet-1k. The training procedure is not detailed in the provided text. The model development was supported by the Gauss Centre for Supercomputing e.V. through the John von Neumann Institute for Computing on the GCS Supercomputer JUWELS Booster at Jülich Supercomputing Centre.

instruction: How can I interpret the output if the results seem inconsistent or unclear?
description: This model is a fine-tuned version of an unspecified base model on an unknown dataset. It is designed to perform a specific task, though the intended use is not explicitly described. The model achieves a loss of 5.7326 on the evaluation set. The training process used the following hyperparameters: learning rate of 5e-05, training batch size of 4, evaluation batch size of 8, random seed of 42, AdamW optimizer with betas of (0.9, 0.999) and epsilon of 1e-08, linear learning rate scheduler, and 20 training epochs. During training, the model's training loss and validation loss were recorded at various steps and epochs. The training loss decreased over time, reaching a minimum of 0.0006 at step 4150, epoch 18.3628, while the validation loss fluctuated and reached a final value of 5.7326 at step 4500, epoch 19.9115. The model was trained using the Transformers 4.46.2, PyTorch 2.5.1+cu121, Datasets 3.1.0, and Tokenizers 0.20.3 frameworks. More information is needed regarding the model's intended uses, limitations, and the training and evaluation data.

instruction: Can you detect any product names and organizations in this user-generated content?
description: The `boltuix/NeuroBERT-NER` model is a fine-tuned transformer for Named Entity Recognition (NER), built on the `boltuix/NeuroBERT-Mini` base model. It is designed to identify 36 entity types, such as people, places, organizations, dates, and money, in English text. The model is suitable for applications like information extraction, chatbots, and knowledge graph construction. It was trained on a dataset containing 143,709 entries, with approximately 115,812 training examples, 15,680 validation examples, and 12,217 test examples. The dataset includes text from news, user-generated content, and research corpora. The model supports both sentence-level and document-level NER and uses the BIO tagging scheme, which includes B- (beginning of an entity), I- (inside of an entity), and O (outside of any entity) tags. The model has approximately 11 million parameters and is licensed under Apache-2.0. It was trained before May 28, 2025, and is optimized for real-time inference. The model achieves a precision of 0.85, recall of 0.87, F1 score of 0.86, and accuracy of 0.92 on the test split of the `boltuix/conll2025-ner` dataset. It is limited to English text and may underperform on informal, social media, or code-mixed text due to domain bias in the training data. The model can be used for direct applications such as information extraction, chatbots, search enhancement, and knowledge graph construction. It can also be adapted for downstream tasks like domain-specific NER, multilingual extensions, and custom entity recognition. The model supports 36 NER tags, including B-CARDINAL, B-DATE, B-EVENT, B-FAC, B-GPE, B-LANGUAGE, B-LAW, B-LOC, B-MONEY, B-NORP, B-ORDINAL, B-ORG, B-PERCENT, B-PERSON, B-PRODUCT, B-QUANTITY, B-TIME, and B-WORK_OF_ART, along with their corresponding I- tags and the O tag. The model can be fine-tuned for domain adaptation or extended to other languages. It is lightweight, with a model size of approximately 50 MB, and can be run on CPU or GPU. The model and dataset repository URLs are placeholders and should be updated with correct Hugging Face URLs once available.

instruction: Can you extract the names of the medications and their dosages from this prescription?
description: EraX-VL-2B-V1.5 is a multimodal model designed for Optical Character Recognition (OCR) and Visual Question Answering (VQA) across multiple languages, with a particular emphasis on Vietnamese. It is capable of accurately recognizing and extracting information from a wide range of documents, including medical forms, invoices, bills of sale, quotes, and medical records. This functionality is intended to support applications in hospitals, clinics, and insurance companies. The model is based on a high-quality, Vietnamese-fluent foundation and has been fine-tuned to improve performance. EraX-VL-2B-V1.5 is a small model with over 2 billion parameters and is capable of multi-turn Q&A with reasonable reasoning. It is not an OCR-only tool like Tesseract but a Multimodal LLM-based model, requiring careful prompt engineering for effective use. It has not been fine-tuned on medical X-ray or car accident datasets, and future versions are planned. EraX-VL-2B-V1.5 is part of the EraX's LànhGPT collection of LLM models. It is developed by Nguyễn Anh Nguyên, Nguyễn Hồ Nam, Phạm Huỳnh Nhật, and Phạm Đình Thục, and funded by EraX. The model is licensed under Apache 2.0 and is primarily focused on Vietnamese with multilingual capabilities. In benchmark tests, EraX-VL-2B-V1.5 achieved a score of 38.2 on the VI-MTVQA leaderboard, outperforming several other open-source and commercial models. The model is intended for use in a variety of document processing and information extraction tasks, and API access can be requested by contacting nguyen@erax.ai.

instruction: I want to find images that visually represent a specific concept from a dataset of scanned documents. Can you help?
description: The embedding model trained by Jina AI. Jina Embeddings v4 is a universal embedding model for multimodal and multilingual retrieval. The model is specially designed for complex document retrieval, including visually rich documents with charts, tables, and illustrations. Built on the Qwen2.5-VL-3B-Instruct base model, Jina Embeddings v4 features unified embeddings for text, images, and visual documents, supporting both dense (single-vector) and late-interaction (multi-vector) retrieval. It provides multilingual support for 30+ languages and is compatible with a wide range of domains, including technical and visually complex documents. The model includes task-specific adapters for retrieval, text matching, and code-related tasks, which can be selected at inference time. It offers flexible embedding sizes, with dense embeddings defaulting to 2048 dimensions but capable of being truncated to as low as 128 with minimal performance loss. The model uses BFloat 16 as its data type, supports a maximum sequence length of 32768, and employs mean pooling as its pooling strategy. It also uses the FlashAttention2 attention mechanism. The model is available for use via the Jina AI Embeddings API, the Hugging Face Transformers library, the sentence-transformers interface, and vLLM. For vLLM, separate model versions are provided for each task, with specific adapters merged into the base model weights. The model is governed by the Qwen Research License. Alongside the model, Jina AI has released Jina-VDR, a multilingual, multi-domain benchmark for visual document retrieval. If used in research, the model should be cited with the provided citation information.

instruction: Please determine if this text contains any relevant policy information.
description: The xlm-roberta-large-slovak-media-cap-v3 is an xlm-roberta-large model fine-tuned on multilingual training data containing texts from the media domain labeled according to the master codebook of the Comparative Agendas Project. The model supports text classification tasks, assigning texts to one of 21 major policy topic codes or a 999 label, which indicates that the text contains no relevant policy content. The model was fine-tuned using 5 training epochs, a batch size of 40, and a learning rate of 5e-06. It was evaluated on a test set of 34,410 examples, achieving an overall accuracy of 0.72. Performance metrics per label include precision, recall, f1-score, and support, with values ranging from 0.53 to 0.92 for precision, 0.54 to 0.94 for recall, and 0.54 to 0.93 for f1-score. The model is used by an open-source and free natural language processing tool designed to simplify and speed up projects for comparative research. Model performance can be improved by extending training sets with CAP-coded corpora. The model requires a token for access due to gated access restrictions. It uses the sentencepiece tokenizer, and users may need to install it manually for versions of the Transformers library prior to 4.27. If a RuntimeError occurs when loading the model, adding the parameter ignore_mismatched_sizes=True may resolve the issue.

instruction: Based on a student's study habits and past performance, can you predict their upcoming exam score?
description: The model is a RandomForestRegressor, designed for regression tasks. It is trained using a procedure that includes hyperparameters such as bootstrap set to True, ccp_alpha set to 0.0, criterion set to squared_error, max_features set to 1.0, min_samples_leaf set to 1, min_samples_split set to 2, n_estimators set to 100, oob_score set to False, and verbose set to 0. The model is evaluated using the test split method, with the root mean squared error (rmse) as the metric. The evaluation results show an rmse value of 0.231662. The model is unfitted, as indicated by its visual representation and associated styling. No specific intended uses or limitations are described. No information is provided on how to get started with the model, the authors of the model card, contact channels, or citation details.

instruction: Can you extract visual features from this image tile for downstream analysis in a cancer research study?
description: UNI is a pretrained vision backbone model (ViT-H/14 via DINOv2) designed for multi-purpose evaluation on histopathology images. It was developed by the Mahmood Lab AI for Pathology at Harvard/BWH. The model was pretrained on over 200 million image tiles sampled from over 350,000 diverse H&E and IHC slides sourced from Mass General Brigham. The architecture is a custom ViT-H with 681 million parameters, featuring a patch size of 14, an embedding dimension of 1536, 24 heads, and SwiGLU feed-forward networks. The training regime used bf16 precision with PyTorch-FSDP mixed-precision and the DINOv2 self-supervised learning (SSL) recipe, which includes a DINO self-distillation loss with multi-crop, an iBOT masked-image modeling loss, and KoLeo regularization on [CLS] tokens. The model supports various downstream tasks, including ROI classification (via logistic regression, k-nearest neighbors, and nearest centroid classifiers), ROI retrieval, slide classification (via multiple instance learning), and segmentation tasks (with frameworks like ViTDet or ViT-Adapter). The model can be used without fine-tuning for competitive results on these tasks, or fine-tuned for improved performance on segmentation. Pre-extracted embeddings for the UNI 2 model are available for TCGA, CPTAC, and PANDA datasets. The model is released under the CC-BY-NC-ND 4.0 license for non-commercial, academic research purposes only, with proper attribution. Commercial use is prohibited without prior approval. Access to the model requires registration on Hugging Face, with the primary email for the account matching an institutional email. Users must agree to the terms of use, which include not distributing or publishing the model and not attempting to re-identify de-identified data used in its development.

instruction: Is there a paragraph in the document that describes the life cycle of a butterfly?
description: ColQwen2.5-3b-multilingual-v1.0 is a multilingual visual retriever based on Qwen2.5-VL-3B-Instruct, utilizing the ColBERT strategy. It is designed to efficiently index documents using visual features and generates multi-vector representations of text and images. The model was trained on 8xH100 80GB GPUs with a per_device_batch_size of 128 for 8 epochs. It supports dynamic image resolutions in input without resizing, with a maximum resolution that creates up to 768 image patches. Training was conducted using the `colpali-engine==0.3.9` version. The model uses low-rank adapters with `alpha=128` and `r=128` on the transformer layers from the language model, as well as the final randomly initialized projection layer. It was trained with a `paged_adamw_8bit` optimizer, a learning rate of 2e-4 with linear decay and 1% warmup steps, and a batch size per device of 128 in `bfloat16` format. The training data includes datasets for German and English from `tsystems/vqa_de_en_batch1`, a multilingual dataset from `llamaindex/vdr-multilingual-train`, synthetic data from `openbmb/VisRAG-Ret-Train-Synthetic-data`, in-domain VQA data from `openbmb/VisRAG-Ret-Train-In-domain-data`, and the Colpali dataset from `vidore/colpali_train_set`. The model primarily focuses on PDF-type documents and high-resource languages, which may limit its generalization to other document types or less represented languages. It relies on multi-vector retrieving derived from the ColBERT late interaction mechanism, which may require engineering efforts to adapt to widely used vector retrieval frameworks that lack native multi-vector support. The vision language backbone model (Qwen2.5-VL) is licensed under the Apache 2.0 license, while the adapters are under the MIT license.

instruction: Can you craft a letter from a medieval queen to a rival kingdom, full of political subtext?
description: Tifa-DeepSexV2-7b-MGRPO is a model based on Qwen2.5 7b, optimized for deep role-playing experiences. It features a 1024k (1 million) word context window and is trained using a dataset generated by Tifa_220B and an innovative MGRPO algorithm. The model is designed to support role-playing dialogues, creative writing requiring divergent thinking, complex logical reasoning with chain-of-thought (CoT), and deep contextual role interactions. It is not specifically optimized for generating non-compliant content, but efforts have been made to reduce rejection responses. The model has undergone four-stage architectural evolution: incremental pre-training with 0.1T tokens of novels, Tifa-COT-SFT cold start for improved logic and context association, MGRPO algorithm for enhanced role-play data training, and DPO to prevent repetition and improve political safety. The MGRPO algorithm was modified from GRPO to support literary content training, incorporating logical algorithms and multiple reward passes. The model also features layer propagation changes inspired by Universal Transformers and latent space concepts, with gradient clipping to prevent gradient explosion. Reward functions include logic, literary style, formatting, coherence, and anatomical accuracy. The model is trained on 0.1T novels, 100,000 SFT samples, and MGRPO reinforcement learning, using a 2x8×H100 GPU cluster. It is expected to take 3000 H100 hours to complete training. The model supports GGUF quantization and is licensed under Apache-2.0. It is recommended for role-playing and creative writing but not for mathematical computation, code generation, or fact-based tasks. Users are advised to follow official prompt templates and adjust frontend code to remove thinking tags from outputs. The model is currently in a verification phase, with 7% of training completed, and is expected to be followed by Chat and Crazy versions. The model has shown improved logical reasoning, spontaneous thinking, increased vocabulary for deep role-play, reduced rejection, and enhanced literary output. However, it may exhibit potential safety concerns, emotional consistency, ideological shifts, and a tendency toward dramatic outputs. Users should ensure generated content complies with local laws and regulations.

instruction: Produce a video showing a time-lapse of a city skyline changing from morning to evening.
description: Wan2.1 is a comprehensive and open suite of video foundation models designed to push the boundaries of video generation. It offers state-of-the-art performance, consistently outperforming existing open-source models and state-of-the-art commercial solutions across multiple benchmarks. The T2V-1.3B model requires only 8.19 GB VRAM, making it compatible with most consumer-grade GPUs. It can generate a 5-second 480P video on an RTX 4090 in about 4 minutes, with performance comparable to some closed-source models. Wan2.1 supports multiple tasks, including Text-to-Video, Image-to-Video, Video Editing, Text-to-Image, and Video-to-Audio, advancing the field of video generation. It is the first video model capable of generating both Chinese and English text, enhancing its practical applications. The model features a powerful video VAE, Wan-VAE, which delivers exceptional efficiency and performance, encoding and decoding 1080P videos of any length while preserving temporal information. The T2V-14B model establishes a new state-of-the-art performance benchmark among both open-source and closed-source models, demonstrating exceptional capabilities in generating high-quality visuals with significant motion dynamics. It is the only video model capable of producing both Chinese and English text and supports video generation at both 480P and 720P resolutions. Wan2.1 is designed on the mainstream diffusion transformer paradigm, with innovations including a novel spatio-temporal variational autoencoder (VAE), scalable training strategies, large-scale data construction, and automated evaluation metrics. The model uses a Flow Matching framework within the Diffusion Transformers paradigm, with a T5 Encoder to encode multilingual text input and cross-attention in each transformer block embedding the text into the model structure. An MLP with a Linear layer and a SiLU layer processes the input time embeddings and predicts six modulation parameters individually, shared across all transformer blocks. The model was trained on a curated and deduplicated dataset of image and video data, processed through a four-step data cleaning pipeline focusing on fundamental dimensions, visual quality, and motion quality. Manual evaluation shows that the results generated after prompt extension are superior to those from both closed-source and open-source models. The model is licensed under the Apache 2.0 License, and users are responsible for ensuring their usage complies with the provisions of this license, including not generating content that violates applicable laws, causes harm, or spreads misinformation.

instruction: What accent is most dominant in this 12-second English recording?
description: This model implements narrow accent classification based on the Whisper-Large v3 architecture, as described in the paper 'Vox-Profile: A Speech Foundation Model Benchmark for Characterizing Diverse Speaker and Speech Traits'. It is designed to classify English accents into 16 categories: 'East Asia', 'English', 'Germanic', 'Irish', 'North America', 'Northern Irish', 'Oceania', 'Other', 'Romance', 'Scottish', 'Semitic', 'Slavic', 'South African', 'Southeast Asia', 'South Asia', and 'Welsh'. The model is intended for use with audio inputs that are up to 15 seconds in length, sampled at 16kHz, and in mono channel format. Audio shorter than 3 seconds is considered unreliable for prediction, and audio longer than 15 seconds is subject to computational limitations. The model is implemented using the WhisperWrapper class and can be loaded from Huggingface. It is trained to process speech data and output probabilities for each accent category using a softmax function. One observed tendency is that text-to-speech (TTS) samples are more likely to be classified as Germanic. The model is released under the Open RAIL license, and users are required to respect the privacy and consent of data subjects and comply with relevant laws and regulations. The model is not intended for clinical or diagnostic applications, surveillance, privacy-invasive applications, or commercial use.

instruction: Can you clarify the underlying logic of this economic theory?
description: We introduce our first-generation reasoning models, DeepSeek-R1-Zero and DeepSeek-R1. DeepSeek-R1-Zero is a model trained via large-scale reinforcement learning (RL) without supervised fine-tuning (SFT) as a preliminary step. It demonstrated remarkable performance on reasoning tasks and naturally developed powerful and interesting reasoning behaviors. However, it encountered challenges such as endless repetition, poor readability, and language mixing. To address these issues and further enhance reasoning performance, we introduced DeepSeek-R1, which incorporates cold-start data before RL. DeepSeek-R1 achieves performance comparable to OpenAI-o1 across math, code, and reasoning tasks. We have open-sourced DeepSeek-R1-Zero, DeepSeek-R1, and six dense models distilled from DeepSeek-R1 based on Llama and Qwen. DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-32B outperforms OpenAI-o1-mini across various benchmarks, achieving new state-of-the-art results for dense models. The post-training process for DeepSeek-R1-Zero involves directly applying reinforcement learning to the base model without SFT, allowing the model to explore chain-of-thought (CoT) for solving complex problems. This approach resulted in the development of DeepSeek-R1-Zero, which demonstrates capabilities such as self-verification, reflection, and generating long CoTs. It is the first open research to validate that reasoning capabilities of LLMs can be incentivized purely through RL, without the need for SFT. The pipeline for developing DeepSeek-R1 includes two RL stages aimed at discovering improved reasoning patterns and aligning with human preferences, as well as two SFT stages that serve as the seed for the model's reasoning and non-reasoning capabilities. We demonstrate that the reasoning patterns of larger models can be distilled into smaller models, resulting in better performance compared to the reasoning patterns discovered through RL on small models. The open source DeepSeek-R1, as well as its API, will benefit the research community to distill better smaller models in the future. Using the reasoning data generated by DeepSeek-R1, we fine-tuned several dense models that are widely used in the research community. The evaluation results demonstrate that the distilled smaller dense models perform exceptionally well on benchmarks. We open-source distilled 1.5B, 7B, 8B, 14B, 32B, and 70B checkpoints based on Qwen2.5 and Llama3 series to the community. DeepSeek-R1-Zero and DeepSeek-R1 are trained based on DeepSeek-V3-Base. DeepSeek-R1-Distill models are fine-tuned based on open-source models, using samples generated by DeepSeek-R1. We slightly change their configurations and tokenizers. For all our models, the maximum generation length is set to 32,768 tokens. For benchmarks requiring sampling, we use a temperature of 0.6, a top-p value of 0.95, and generate 64 responses per query to estimate pass@1. DeepSeek-R1 achieves 90.8 on MMLU (Pass@1), 92.9 on MMLU-Redux (EM), 84.0 on MMLU-Pro (EM), 92.2 on DROP (3-shot F1), 83.3 on IF-Eval (Prompt Strict), 71.5 on GPQA-Diamond (Pass@1), 30.1 on SimpleQA (Correct), 82.5 on FRAMES (Acc.), 87.6 on AlpacaEval2.0 (LC-winrate), and 92.3 on ArenaHard (GPT-4-1106). In code tasks, DeepSeek-R1 achieves 65.9 on LiveCodeBench (Pass@1-COT), 96.3 on Codeforces (Percentile), 2029 on Codeforces (Rating), 49.2 on SWE Verified (Resolved), and 53.3 on Aider-Polyglot (Acc.). In math tasks, DeepSeek-R1 achieves 79.8 on AIME 2024 (Pass@1), 97.3 on MATH-500 (Pass@1), and 78.8 on CNMO 2024 (Pass@1). In Chinese tasks, DeepSeek-R1 achieves 92.8 on CLUEWSC (EM), 91.8 on C-Eval (EM), and 63.7 on C-SimpleQA (Correct). DeepSeek-R1-Distill models achieve 72.6 on AIME 2024 pass@1, 86.7 on AIME 2024 cons@64, 94.5 on MATH-500 pass@1, 65.2 on GPQA Diamond pass@1, 57.5 on LiveCodeBench pass@1, and 1633 on CodeForces rating. You can chat with DeepSeek-R1 on DeepSeek's official website and switch on the button 'DeepThink'. We also provide an OpenAI-Compatible API at DeepSeek Platform. To run DeepSeek-R1 models locally, please visit the repository for more information. Hugging Face's Transformers has not been directly supported yet. DeepSeek-R1-Distill models can be utilized in the same manner as Qwen or Llama models. For instance, you can easily start a service using vllm or sglang. We recommend adhering to the following configurations when utilizing the DeepSeek-R1 series models, including benchmarking, to achieve the expected performance: set the temperature within the range of 0.5-0.7 (0.6 is recommended) to prevent endless repetitions or incoherent outputs, avoid adding a system prompt; all instructions should be contained within the user prompt, for mathematical problems, include a directive in your prompt such as 'Please reason step by step, and put your final answer within \boxed{}', and when evaluating model performance, conduct multiple tests and average the results. Additionally, we have observed that the DeepSeek-R1 series models tend to bypass thinking pattern (i.e., outputting '\\n\\n\') when responding to certain queries, which can adversely affect the model's performance. To ensure that the model engages in thorough reasoning, we recommend enforcing the model to initiate its response with '\\n' at the beginning of every output. This code repository and the model weights are licensed under a permissive license. DeepSeek-R1 series support commercial use, allow for any modifications and derivative works, including, but not limited to, distillation for training other LLMs. Please note that DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-1.5B, DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-7B, DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-14B, and DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-32B are derived from models originally licensed under their respective licenses and are now fine-tuned with 800k samples curated with DeepSeek-R1. DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-8B is derived from Llama3.1-8B-Base and is originally licensed under its respective license. DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-70B is derived from Llama3.3-70B-Instruct and is originally licensed under its respective license. If you have any questions, please raise an issue or contact us.

instruction: What can you tell about the setting from the video footage provided?
description: The Video-R1/Qwen2.5-VL-7B-COT-SFT is a supervised fine-tuning (SFT) cold start model trained using the Video-R1-COT-165k dataset. This intermediate checkpoint is intended to serve as the base model for further reinforcement learning (RL) training on the Video-R1-260k dataset to produce the final Video-R1 models. Video-R1 is the first systematic exploration of the R1 paradigm for incentivizing video reasoning within multimodal large language models (MLLMs), inspired by the success of DeepSeek-R1. It addresses key challenges in video reasoning, particularly the lack of temporal modeling and the scarcity of high-quality video-reasoning data. To tackle these issues, Video-R1 proposes the T-GRPO algorithm, an extension of GRPO that explicitly encourages models to leverage temporal information in videos for reasoning. It also strategically incorporates high-quality image-reasoning data into the training process.
