The model was trained on two newly constructed datasets: Video-R1-CoT-165k for SFT cold start and Video-R1-260k for RL training, both comprising image and video data. Experimental results demonstrate that Video-R1 achieves significant improvements on various video reasoning benchmarks, including VideoMMMU, VSI-Bench, MVBench, and TempCompass. Notably, Video-R1-7B has shown competitive performance, even surpassing proprietary models like GPT-4o on certain video spatial reasoning tasks.

instruction: Could you summarize the main arguments of this political analysis article?
description: The Mistral 7B Text Summarizer is a model designed for text summarization tasks. It is based on the Mistral 7B architecture and uses Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) techniques to improve fine-tuning efficiency and performance. The model is built using the Hugging Face Transformers framework and is fine-tuned using Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) through the Hugging Face TRL library. It is a general-purpose model capable of summarizing content across diverse domains. The model implements Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) with LoRA adapters to enhance performance while reducing computational costs. It is optimized for fast and efficient inference using gradient checkpointing and optimized data management. The model supports 4-bit quantization, which significantly reduces memory usage and computation time while maintaining accuracy. It is fine-tuned on the SURESHBEEKHANI text-summarizer dataset. The model supports a maximum sequence length of 2048 tokens and is configurable to use either float16 or float32 precision for hardware optimization. It is optimized for a reduced memory footprint, allowing for larger batch sizes and effective handling of longer sequences. The model is suitable for tasks requiring concise summaries of lengthy texts, documents, or articles, and is ideal for use in content generation, report summarization, and information distillation. It is efficient for deployment in production systems requiring scalable and fast text summarization. However, it has limitations, including the need for additional memory and computational power for extremely long documents, and potential inconsistencies in performance for niche domains underrepresented in the training dataset. Steps have been taken to mitigate bias in the training data and ensure fairness in generated summaries. The model is designed to respect user privacy by adhering to best practices in handling input text data. Comprehensive documentation and model cards are provided to promote transparency and trust in AI-driven summarization. The model was fine-tuned by Suresh Beekhani and is based on the SURESHBEEKHANI text-summarizer dataset. It is open-source under the Hugging Face and unsloth licenses, allowing for free use and modification.

instruction: Can you recognize the type of sports activity shown in this short video clip?
description: ResNet (2+1)D Convolutions is a network that explicitly factorizes 3D convolution into two separate and successive operations: a 2D spatial convolution and a 1D temporal convolution. It is used for video understanding applications. This model is an implementation of ResNet-2Plus1D. The model is optimized for mobile deployment and is designed for sports and human action recognition in videos. It is trained on the Kinetics-400 dataset. The model has an input resolution of 112x112 and contains 31.5 million parameters. The model size is 120 MB in float precision and 30.8 MB in w8a8 precision. The model is designed to run on Qualcomm® devices and supports various target runtimes including TFLITE, QNN_DLC, and ONNX. Performance metrics include inference time and peak memory usage across a range of devices such as QCS8275, QCS8450, QCS8550, QCS9075, SA7255P, SA8255, SA8295P, SA8650, SA8775P, Samsung Galaxy S24, Samsung Galaxy S25, Snapdragon 8 Elite Gen 5 QRD, and Snapdragon X Elite CRD. The model can be compiled for on-device deployment using the Qualcomm® AI Hub, and it supports performance profiling, accuracy verification, and deployment to Android using TensorFlow Lite or QNN. The model is available for installation via pip and includes a demo for end-to-end execution. Deployment instructions and additional information are available on the Qualcomm® AI Hub. The license for the original implementation of ResNet-2Plus1D is available separately.

instruction: Can you explain the relationship between the image and the text in simple terms?
description: ViT-L-16-SigLIP2-512 is a SigLIP 2 Vision-Language model trained on the WebLI dataset. It is a contrastive image-text model capable of performing zero-shot image classification. The model has been converted for use in OpenCLIP from the original JAX checkpoints. It is based on the architecture described in the paper 'SigLIP 2: Multilingual Vision-Language Encoders with Improved Semantic Understanding, Localization, and Dense Features' and utilizes the sigmoid loss method for language image pre-training as described in the paper 'Sigmoid loss for language image pre-training'. The model supports encoding both images and text, and computing similarity scores between them for classification tasks. It is designed to work with multilingual data and provides improved semantic understanding, localization, and dense feature extraction capabilities.

instruction: Could you match a customer's question in Arabic to the most relevant support article?
description: Granite-Embedding-107M-Multilingual is a 107 million parameter dense biencoder embedding model from the Granite Embeddings suite, designed to generate high-quality text embeddings. It produces embedding vectors of size 384 and is trained using a combination of open source relevance-pair datasets with permissive, enterprise-friendly licenses and IBM-collected and generated datasets. The model is developed using contrastive finetuning, knowledge distillation, and model merging for improved performance. It supports 12 languages: English, German, Spanish, French, Japanese, Portuguese, Arabic, Czech, Italian, Korean, Dutch, and Chinese, and users may finetune it for additional languages. The model is intended to produce fixed-length vector representations for a given text, which can be used for text similarity, retrieval, and search applications. It is compatible with the SentenceTransformer library and the Huggingface Transformers library, and uses CLS pooling for embedding generation. The model is based on an encoder-only XLM-RoBERTa-like transformer architecture, trained internally at IBM Research. It has an embedding size of 384, 6 layers, 12 attention heads, an intermediate size of 1536, a GeLU activation function, a vocabulary size of 250002, a maximum sequence length of 512, and 107 million parameters. The training data includes unsupervised title-body paired data from the web, publicly available paired data with permissive licenses, IBM-internal paired data targeting specific technical domains, and IBM-generated synthetic data. The training data consists of 52,823,484 pairs from Multilingual MC4, 12,369,322 pairs from Multilingual Webhose, 20,745,403 pairs from English Wikipedia, 2,911,090 pairs from Multilingual Wikimedia, 10,120,398 pairs from Miracl Corpus, 304,525 pairs from Stack Exchange duplicate questions (titles), 250,519 pairs from Stack Exchange duplicate questions (bodies), 187,195 pairs from machine translations of Stack Exchange duplicate questions (titles), 4,067,139 pairs from Stack Exchange (Title, Answer) pairs, 23,978,013 pairs from Stack Exchange (Title, Body) pairs, 1,827,15 pairs from machine translations of Stack Exchange (Title+Body, Answer) pairs, 582,261 pairs from SearchQA, 41,769,185 pairs from S2ORC, 77,427,422 pairs from WikiAnswers duplicate question pairs, 614,664 pairs from CCNews, 226,711 pairs from XSum, 102,225 pairs from SimpleWiki, 28,376,115 pairs from machine translated cross-lingual parallel corpora, 684,100 pairs from SPECTER citation triplets, 4,104,600 pairs from machine translations of SPECTER citation triplets, 100,231 pairs from Natural Questions, 87,599 pairs from SQuAD2.0, 85,000 pairs from HotpotQA, 109,810 pairs from Fever, 20,000,000 pairs from PubMed, 81,409 pairs from Multilingual Miracl Triples, 48,715 pairs from Multilingual MrTydi Triples, 4,037 pairs from Sadeeem Question Answering, 4,635,922 pairs from DBPedia Title-Body Pairs, 1,879,093 pairs from synthetic English Query-Wikipedia Passage, 9,888 pairs from synthetic English Fact Verification, 300,266 pairs from synthetic Multilingual Query-Wikipedia Passage, 37,489 pairs from synthetic Multilingual News Summaries, 40,290 pairs from IBM Internal Triples, and 1,524,586 pairs from IBM Internal Title-Body Pairs. The model is trained using IBM's Cognitive Compute Cluster, which is equipped with NVIDIA A100 80GB GPUs. The data used to train the base language model was filtered to remove text containing hate, abuse, and profanity. The model is finetuned on 12 languages and has a context length of 512 tokens, with longer texts being truncated to this size. The average performance of the model on Multilingual Miracl (across 18 languages), Mintaka Retrieval (across 8 languages), and MTEB Retrieval for English (across 15 tasks), German (across 4 tasks), Spanish (across 2 tasks), French (across 5 tasks), Japanese (across 2 tasks), Arabic (1 task), Korean (1 task), and Chinese (across 8 tasks) is reported as follows: 55.9 on Miracl, 22.6 on Mintaka Retrieval, 45.3 on MTEB English, 70.3 on MTEB German, 48.7 on MTEB Spanish, 51.1 on MTEB French, 59.0 on MTEB Japanese, 63.2 on MTEB Arabic, 70.5 on MTEB Korean, and 40.8 on MTEB Chinese. The model is twice as fast as other models with similar embedding dimensions. The model is not trained on the MS-MARCO retrieval dataset due to its non-commercial license.

instruction: I need a voice clip in Spanish for a YouTube video. Can you make it sound friendly and engaging?
description: Chatterbox and Chatterbox Multilingual are production-grade open source text-to-speech (TTS) models. Chatterbox Multilingual supports 23 languages, including Arabic, Danish, German, Greek, English, Spanish, Finnish, French, Hebrew, Hindi, Italian, Japanese, Korean, Malay, Dutch, Norwegian, Polish, Portuguese, Russian, Swedish, Swahili, Turkish, and Chinese. The model is licensed under MIT and has been benchmarked against leading closed-source systems like ElevenLabs, with consistent preference in side-by-side evaluations. It is suitable for applications such as memes, videos, games, and AI agents. A unique feature is emotion exaggeration control, which allows for more expressive and dramatic speech. The model is based on a 0.5B Llama backbone and is trained on 0.5 million hours of cleaned data. It includes alignment-informed inference for ultra-stability and supports zero-shot TTS, including state-of-the-art zero-shot English TTS. The model also includes built-in PerTh watermarked outputs, which are imperceptible and survive MP3 compression, audio editing, and common manipulations while maintaining nearly 100% detection accuracy. For voice conversion, a script is provided. Users are advised to ensure that the reference clip matches the specified language tag to avoid accent transfer. If not, setting the CFG weight to 0 may help mitigate this issue. The model can be installed via pip and used in Python with the ChatterboxTTS and ChatterboxMultilingualTTS classes. Default settings for general use include exaggeration=0.5 and cfg=0.5. For expressive or dramatic speech, lower cfg values (e.g., ~0.3) and higher exaggeration values (e.g., 0.7 or higher) are recommended. The model is available for use in production environments with ultra-low latency of sub 200ms. A competitively priced TTS service is also available for scaling or tuning the model for higher accuracy.

instruction: Can you forecast the number of customers for a store next week based on past trends and local events?
description: TabPFNMix regressor is a tabular foundation model pre-trained on purely synthetic datasets sampled from a mix of random regressors. It is based on a 12-layer encoder-decoder Transformer with 37 million parameters. The pre-training strategy incorporates in-context learning, similar to that used by TabPFN and TabForestPFN. The model is designed for regression tasks and supports fine-tuning and inference for tabular data. It is implemented within the AutoGluon framework, and users can install AutoGluon via pip. The model can be used by importing pandas and the TabularPredictor class from autogluon.tabular, loading training and test data, specifying hyperparameters, and fitting the predictor to the training data. The model's performance can be evaluated using a leaderboard on test data. The model is associated with the Apache-2.0 License. Users are encouraged to cite the associated papers if they find TabPFNMix useful for their research.

instruction: How can I generate a clean segmentation of the main object in this high-resolution image?
description: BiRefNet is a model designed for high-resolution dichotomous image segmentation. It was trained with images in 2048x2048 resolution to support higher resolution inference. The model is intended for tasks such as standard dichotomous image segmentation (DIS), high-resolution salient object detection (HRSOD), and contrastive object detection (COD). It has achieved state-of-the-art performance on these tasks. The model was trained on the DIS-TR dataset and validated on DIS-TEs and DIS-VD. Performance metrics include maxFm, wFmeasure, MAE, Smeasure, meanEm, HCE, maxEm, meanFm, adpEm, adpFm, mBA, maxBIoU, and meanBIoU. Evaluation results show that at 2048x2048 resolution, the model achieves a maxFm of 0.925, wFmeasure of 0.894, MAE of 0.026, Smeasure of 0.927, meanEm of 0.952, HCE of 811, maxEm of 0.960, meanFm of 0.909, adpEm of 0.944, adpFm of 0.888, mBA of 0.828, maxBIoU of 0.837, and meanBIoU of 0.817. At 1024x1024 resolution, the model achieves a maxFm of 0.876, wFmeasure of 0.840, MAE of 0.041, Smeasure of 0.893, meanEm of 0.913, HCE of 1348, maxEm of 0.926, meanFm of 0.860, adpEm of 0.930, adpFm of 0.857, mBA of 0.765, maxBIoU of 0.769, and meanBIoU of 0.742. The model is implemented using PyTorch and can be loaded using the HuggingFace Transformers library or from local GitHub repositories. It supports inference with different resolutions and includes functions for visualization and deployment. The model is described in the paper 'Bilateral Reference for High-Resolution Dichotomous Image Segmentation' by Peng Zheng, Dehong Gao, Deng-Ping Fan, Li Liu, Jorma Laaksonen, Wanli Ouyang, and Nicu Sebe, published in CAAI Artificial Intelligence Research in 2024. The model weights and codes are available on GitHub, and online demos for inference are provided. The model was trained using GPU resources generously supported by @freepik.

instruction: What are the possible emotions associated with the phrase 'I can't believe how lucky I am'?
description: The model is a multi-label classification model trained on a dataset based on Reddit data with 28 labels. It is designed to classify input text into one or more of the 28 emotion-related categories. The model produces 28 probability outputs for each input text, with a typical threshold of 0.5 applied to determine the final prediction for each label. It was trained using the `AutoModelForSequenceClassification.from_pretrained` method with the problem type set to 'multi_label_classification', for 3 epochs with a learning rate of 2e-5 and a weight decay of 0.01. An ONNX version of the model, including an INT8 quantized variant, is available. These versions are faster for inference, especially for smaller batch sizes, reduce the size of dependencies required for inference, and are more multi-platform. The quantized version reduces the model file size by 75% while retaining almost all accuracy for inference purposes. Evaluation of the model on the test split of the dataset shows an overall accuracy of 0.474, precision of 0.575, recall of 0.396, and F1 score of 0.450. However, due to the multi-label nature of the task and the varying representation of labels in the dataset, per-label metrics are more meaningful. With a threshold of 0.5, the model achieves varying levels of performance across labels, with some labels like 'gratitude' achieving an F1 score of 0.919 and others like 'relief' achieving an F1 score of 0.000. Optimizing the threshold per label to maximize F1 score results in improved overall metrics, with a precision of 0.542, recall of 0.577, and F1 of 0.541. When weighted by the relative size of the support of each label, the model achieves a precision of 0.572, recall of 0.677, and F1 of 0.611. The dataset is noted to be challenging, with some labels having very few examples in the training and test splits, and potential issues with ambiguity and labeling errors in the training data. These factors are believed to constrain the model's performance, and data cleaning to address these issues could lead to a higher performing model.

instruction: I need to analyze the structural details of a molecule—can you assist with generating a high-resolution 3D model?
description: Uni-3DAR is an autoregressive model designed to unify various 3D tasks. It is capable of handling multiple 3D data types, including microscopic structures such as molecules, proteins, and crystals, as well as macroscopic 3D structures. The model supports a wide range of tasks, particularly those involving both generation and understanding of 3D data. Uni-3DAR achieves high efficiency by using octree compression in combination with a proposed 2-level subtree compression, which allows the full 3D space to be represented using only hundreds of tokens, as opposed to tens of thousands in a full-size grid. This efficiency is reflected in its faster inference speed compared to diffusion-based models. Additionally, the model maintains structural details by tokenizing fine-grained 3D patches, which contributes to its high accuracy and substantially better generation quality than previous diffusion-based models. The core model and a QM9 training and inference pipeline have been released. To reproduce results on the QM9 dataset, users can download a pretrained checkpoint and dataset archive, and follow provided instructions for inference or training from scratch. The model is associated with a Docker image for convenience. Users are encouraged to cite the corresponding paper if they use the data, code, or model.

instruction: How can I get a timeline of who was speaking in this audio?
description: This is an open-source speaker diarization model based on pyannote.audio version 2.1.1. It is designed to identify and separate individual speakers in audio recordings, assigning each segment of speech to a specific speaker. The model supports fully automatic processing, including no manual voice activity detection, no manual specification of the number of speakers (though it can be provided), and no fine-tuning of internal models or pipeline hyper-parameters for each dataset. Users can specify the number of speakers in advance using the `num_speakers` option, or provide lower and upper bounds using the `min_speakers` and `max_speakers` options. The model's performance is benchmarked on a growing collection of datasets, with evaluations conducted under the least forgiving diarization error rate (DER) setup, which includes no forgiveness collar and evaluation of overlapped speech. The real-time factor is approximately 2.5%, using one Nvidia Tesla V100 SXM2 GPU for neural inference and one Intel Cascade Lake 6248 CPU for clustering, meaning it takes about 1.5 minutes to process one hour of audio. The technical report associated with this model describes the principles behind version 2.1 of the pyannote.audio speaker diarization pipeline and provides recipes for adapting the pipeline to custom annotated data, which can lead to significant performance improvements over the out-of-the-box results. The model is available for use after accepting user conditions on the Hugging Face platform and requires an access token for instantiation. It outputs diarization results in RTTM format.

instruction: I want to know if the woman in the hat is in the second photo. Can you describe how you'd locate her?
description: Rex-Thinker is a Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning model designed for object referring tasks. It addresses two key challenges: lack of interpretability and the inability to reject unmatched expressions. Instead of directly predicting bounding boxes, Rex-Thinker reasons step-by-step over candidate objects to determine which, if any, match a given expression. The model follows a structured three-stage reasoning paradigm: Planning, where the referring expression is decomposed into interpretable subgoals; Action, where each candidate object is evaluated against these subgoals using step-by-step reasoning; and Summarization, where intermediate results are aggregated to output the final prediction or abstain when no object matches. Each reasoning step is grounded in a specific candidate object region through Box Hints, making the process transparent and verifiable. Rex-Thinker is implemented on top of QwenVL-2.5 and trained in two stages: first, through Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) using GPT-4o-generated CoT traces as supervision, and second, through reinforcement learning with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) to enhance accuracy, faithfulness, and generalization. This CoT-based framework enables Rex-Thinker to make faithful, interpretable predictions while generalizing well to out-of-domain referring scenarios.

instruction: Can you animate a scene where a cat is chasing a laser dot across a room?
description: This model is a dilated controlnet module designed for the Wan2.1 model. The dilated controlnet includes fewer basic blocks and incorporates a `stride` parameter. For the Wan1.3B model, the controlnet consists of 8 blocks with a stride of 3. The model supports inference tasks for video generation based on text prompts and controlnet inputs. It is intended to be used with the Wan2.1-T2V-1.3B-Diffusers base model and the specified controlnet model path. The model can be run via command-line interface or through a Gradio-based web interface. Detailed inference allows for customization of parameters such as controlnet weight, guidance start and end, number of inference steps, guidance scale, video resolution, number of frames, negative prompts, seed, and output frame rate. The model is based on original code and models, and users are encouraged to raise issues directly in the repository. Professional support can be requested via email.

instruction: Could you turn this image of a beach into a 5-second video with waves gently crashing in the background?
description: Wan2.1 is a comprehensive and open suite of video foundation models designed to push the boundaries of video generation. It offers state-of-the-art performance, consistently outperforming existing open-source models and state-of-the-art commercial solutions across multiple benchmarks. The T2V-1.3B model requires only 8.19 GB VRAM, making it compatible with most consumer-grade GPUs. It can generate a 5-second 480P video on an RTX 4090 in about 4 minutes, with performance comparable to some closed-source models. Wan2.1 supports multiple tasks, including Text-to-Video, Image-to-Video, Video Editing, Text-to-Image, and Video-to-Audio, advancing the field of video generation. It is the first video model capable of generating both Chinese and English text, enhancing its practical applications. The suite includes a powerful video VAE called Wan-VAE, which delivers exceptional efficiency and performance, encoding and decoding 1080P videos of any length while preserving temporal information. The T2V-14B model establishes a new state-of-the-art performance benchmark among both open-source and closed-source models, demonstrating exceptional capabilities in generating high-quality visuals with significant motion dynamics. It is the only video model capable of producing both Chinese and English text and supports video generation at both 480P and 720P resolutions. Wan2.1 is built on the mainstream diffusion transformer paradigm, with innovations such as a novel spatio-temporal variational autoencoder (VAE), scalable training strategies, large-scale data construction, and automated evaluation metrics. The model uses a 3D causal VAE architecture specifically designed for video generation, improving spatio-temporal compression, reducing memory usage, and ensuring temporal causality. The model's architecture employs the Flow Matching framework within the Diffusion Transformers paradigm, using a T5 Encoder to encode multilingual text input and cross-attention in each transformer block to embed the text into the model structure. An MLP with a Linear layer and a SiLU layer processes the input time embeddings and predicts six modulation parameters individually, shared across all transformer blocks. The model was trained on a curated and deduplicated dataset of image and video data, processed through a four-step data cleaning pipeline focusing on fundamental dimensions, visual quality, and motion quality. The model was evaluated using a set of 1,035 internal prompts across 14 major dimensions and 26 sub-dimensions, with total scores calculated using weights derived from human preferences. The model is licensed under the Apache 2.0 License, and users are responsible for ensuring their usage complies with the license provisions, including not generating or sharing content that violates applicable laws, causes harm, or spreads misinformation.

instruction: Could you calculate the distance between the two buildings in this image?
description: Depth Pro is a foundation model for zero-shot metric monocular depth estimation. It synthesizes high-resolution depth maps with sharpness and high-frequency details. The model produces metric depth predictions with absolute scale, without requiring metadata such as camera intrinsics. It is capable of generating a 2.25-megapixel depth map in 0.3 seconds on a standard GPU. This performance is enabled by an efficient multi-scale vision transformer for dense prediction, a training protocol that combines real and synthetic datasets to achieve high metric accuracy and fine boundary tracing, dedicated evaluation metrics for boundary accuracy in depth maps, and state-of-the-art focal length estimation from a single image. The model was introduced by Aleksei Bochkovskii, Amaël Delaunoy, Hugo Germain, Marcel Santos, Yichao Zhou, Stephan R. Richter, and Vladlen Koltun. The checkpoint in this repository is a reference implementation that has been re-trained. Its performance is close to the model reported in the paper but does not match it exactly.

instruction: I want to create a video of a storm approaching the coast—can you generate that?
description: This is a gguf quantized version of the wan video model, designed for video generation tasks. It includes a 1.3b parameter model variant that is suitable for older or low-end machines. The model supports text-to-video (t2v) generation and video auto-encoding (vace) functions. It is compatible with the ComfyUI framework and requires specific model files to be placed in designated directories for proper operation. The model architecture is based on a lazy design referred to as 'pig,' which applies to all model, encoder, and vae gguf files. For compatibility with the comfyui-gguf node, the 'pig' architecture may need to be manually added to the IMG_ARCH_LIST in loader.py. The model can be run using either the diffusers library with a custom pipeline or through the gguf-connector. Performance considerations include the use of CPU offloading for encoder operations, which does not affect speed. The model has been updated to version wan2.1-v5-vace-1.3b, where all components except block weights are in f32 status to avoid triggering time or text embedding key errors during inference. The model is based on a reference architecture and integrates with external tools such as ComfyUI, gguf-connector, and the pig architecture framework.

instruction: What are the legal steps for registering a trademark in Turkmenistan?
description: This is a Turkmen language large language model (LLM) that has been fine-tuned using Turkmen law data. It was developed by Annamyrat Saparow and funded on 30.04.2025. The model was shared on 07.05.2025 and is categorized as an instruction model. It supports the Turkmen and English languages and is licensed under the MIT license. The model was fine-tuned from the llama 3.1 8b instruct model. The training data used is the maximilianshwarzmullers/hukukchy dataset, which is the developer's own dataset for law. The training procedure involved QLora. The model uses a preprocessing method where each example is formatted with an instruction, input, and response, followed by an end-of-sequence token. The dataset was loaded from the maximilianshwarzmullers/hukukchy repository and processed in batches. The training was conducted using the SFTTrainer from the trl library with specific hyperparameters including a per-device train batch size of 2, gradient accumulation steps of 4, warmup steps of 5, a maximum of 750 training steps, a learning rate of 3e-5, and support for either fp16 or bf16 precision depending on hardware support. Additional training settings include logging every step, the use of the adamw_8bit optimizer, a weight decay of 0.01, a linear learning rate scheduler, a seed of 3407, and an output directory named 'outputs'.
domain: Natural Language Processing Table Question Answering

instruction: Find an image that visually matches the sentence 'a child laughing while holding a balloon'.
description: ViT-B-16-SigLIP2-512 is a SigLIP 2 Vision-Language model trained on the WebLI dataset. It is designed for contrastive image-text tasks and zero-shot image classification. The model is based on a Vision Transformer (ViT) architecture with a 16-pixel patch size and 512-dimensional embeddings. It has been converted for use in OpenCLIP from the original JAX checkpoints. The model supports encoding both images and text into feature representations and computing similarity scores using a sigmoid loss function. It is intended for applications requiring multilingual vision-language understanding, improved semantic comprehension, and dense feature extraction. The model's performance is based on the training methodology described in the SigLIP 2 and Sigmoid loss for language image pre-training research papers. Users are advised to cite the relevant publications when using this model.

instruction: Could you assist me in finding the page that contains the methodology section of a scientific study?
description: ColQwen2 is a model based on a novel architecture and training strategy using Vision Language Models (VLMs) to efficiently index documents from their visual features. It generates multi-vector representations of text and images, extending the Qwen2-VL-2B-Instruct model with a ColBERT strategy. The model was introduced in a paper and first released as a HuggingFace `transformers` 🤗 implementation contributed by Tony Wu and Yoni Gozlan. It is designed for document retrieval tasks, particularly for PDF-type documents, and supports the generation of embeddings for both images and text queries. The model was trained on a dataset of 127,460 query-page pairs, consisting of 63% from openly available academic datasets and 37% from a synthetic dataset of web-crawled PDF pages augmented with VLM-generated pseudo-questions. The training set is fully English, allowing for the study of zero-shot generalization to non-English languages. A validation set of 2% of the samples was used to tune hyperparameters. The model relies on multi-vector retrieval derived from the ColBERT late interaction mechanism, which may require engineering efforts to adapt to vector retrieval frameworks that lack native multi-vector support. ColQwen2's vision language backbone model (Qwen2-VL) is licensed under the Apache-2.0 license, and ColQwen2 inherits this license. The model primarily focuses on PDF-type documents and high-resource languages, which may limit its generalization to other document types or less represented languages.

instruction: Can you provide a translated English version of a French speech with voice preservation on the fly?
description: Hibiki is a model for streaming speech translation, also known as simultaneous translation. It is designed to adapt its flow to accumulate just enough context to produce a correct translation in real-time, chunk by chunk. As the user speaks, Hibiki generates natural speech in the target language, optionally with voice transfer, along with a text translation. Hibiki currently only supports French-to-English translation. The model, referred to as Hibiki-M (for Mobile), is a 1.7B parameter hierarchical Transformer that produces speech and text tokens at a framerate of 12.5Hz, with audio being generated at a 1.1kbps bitrate. Hibiki is a decoder-only model for simultaneous speech translation. It leverages the multistream architecture to model source and target speech jointly, allowing it to continuously process the input stream while generating the target speech. Hibiki produces text and audio tokens at a constant framerate of 12.5Hz, enabling a continuous output audio stream along with timestamped text translation. It relies on simple temperature sampling, making it compatible with batching, unlike models that rely on complex inference policies. The fidelity of Hibiki's voice transfer can be controlled by changing the coefficient of the Classifier-Free Guidance: a larger coefficient will increase voice similarity, but excessive coefficients can lead to worse translations. The model is developed by Kyutai and is a simultaneous speech-to-speech and speech-to-text translation model. It supports French-to-English translation and is licensed under CC-BY. Hibiki can be used for streaming translation from French to English in real-time settings or for batched simultaneous translation of many input sequences. It is robust to noisy conditions and is trained on sequences up to 120 seconds. Some components of the model can be used independently or repurposed relatively easily. For example, the Mimi codec is a state-of-the-art audio neural codec that combines semantic and acoustic information into audio tokens running at 12Hz and a bitrate of 1.1kbps, making it particularly adapted to train speech language models or text-to-speech systems. Supporting other pairs of languages would require finetuning. The model is not intended to be used to impersonate other people or for any malicious use of any kind. The underlying model is trained on a mix of data including Wikipedia, Stack Exchange, open-access scientific articles (from peS2o), and Common Crawl. The unsupervised audio dataset used for pre-training is a collection of 7M hours of readily available audio content in English and 450k hours in French, following the preprocessing and recipe of a referenced source. The synthetic translation dataset consists of around 40k hours of parallel French-English data synthesized with contextual alignment, with various levels of speaker similarity. Translation finetuning is based on a 900-hour mixture of a resynthesized version of a referenced dataset and synthetic long-form utterances. The training procedure and hyper-parameters are detailed in the paper. The final model was trained on 48 H100 Nvidia GPUs.

instruction: What does this video clip from a science experiment demonstrate?
description: mPLUG-Owl3 is a state-of-the-art multi-modal large language model designed to address the challenges of long image sequence understanding. It introduces Hyper Attention, a mechanism that enhances the speed of long visual sequence understanding in multi-modal large language models by sixfold, enabling the processing of visual sequences that are eight times longer. The model maintains strong performance on single-image, multi-image, and video tasks. It supports chat with images and videos, and is compatible with specific attention implementations such as 'sdpa' and 'flash_attention_2'. The model is available for use with a processor that handles both image and video inputs, and it includes a tokenizer for text processing. Performance is maintained across a range of tasks, including those involving extended visual sequences. The model is described in a paper titled 'mPLUG-Owl3: Towards Long Image-Sequence Understanding in Multi-Modal Large Language Models' by Jiabo Ye, Haiyang Xu, Haowei Liu, Anwen Hu, Ming Yan, Qi Qian, Ji Zhang, Fei Huang, and Jingren Zhou, available on arXiv with the identifier 2408.04840.

instruction: Please describe the underlying structure of this image in simple terms.
description: The Vision Transformer (ViT) is a transformer encoder model, similar in design to BERT, designed for supervised image classification on ImageNet. It has been adapted for self-supervised image feature extraction, allowing the model to learn meaningful features (embeddings) from images without requiring labeled data. The DINOv2 version of the ViT introduces a modification to address artifacts in attention maps, which are caused by the use of image patches as 'registers'. The authors propose adding new tokens, called 'register' tokens, during pre-training, which are discarded afterward. This modification results in no artifacts, interpretable attention maps, and improved performance. The model does not include any fine-tuned heads and is intended for pre-training to learn an internal representation of images. These representations can be used for downstream tasks, such as training a standard classifier by placing a linear layer on top of the pre-trained encoder, typically using the [CLS] token's last hidden state as a representation of the entire image. The model can be used for feature extraction, and users are encouraged to look for fine-tuned versions for specific tasks.

instruction: What category would you place an article describing a government press conference in?
description: This is an xlm-roberta-large model that has been fine-tuned on multilingual (English, Danish) training data labeled with from the . Additionally, the model incorporates seven media codes, as follows: State and Local Government Administration (24), Weather and Natural Disaster (26), Fires (27), Sports and Recreation (29), Death Notices (30), Churches and Religion (31), and Other, Miscellaneous and Human Interest (99). The model was evaluated on a test set of 91,331 examples, achieving a weighted average F1-score of 0.68. The model requires a token for access due to gated access restrictions, and users must provide the token parameter when loading the model. Earlier versions of the Transformers package may require the use of the use_auth_token parameter instead. The model uses the sentencepiece tokenizer, and users running the model before transformers version 4.27 may need to install it manually. If a RuntimeError occurs when loading the model using the from_pretrained() method, adding ignore_mismatched_sizes=True should resolve the issue. Model performance can be significantly improved by extending the training sets, and contributions of CAP-coded corpora (of any domain and language) are welcomed at poltextlab@poltextlab.com or by using the .

instruction: Identify and label the different types of birds in this nature scene.
description: MM Grounding DINO (large variant) was proposed by Xiangyu Zhao, Yicheng Chen, Shilin Xu, Xiangtai Li, Xinjiang Wang, Yining Li, and Haian Huang. It improves upon previous models by enhancing the contrastive class head and removing parameter sharing in the decoder, which leads to improved zero-shot detection performance. Specifically, it achieves 50.6 AP (an increase of 2.2) on COCO and 31.9 val AP (an increase of 11.8) and 41.4 minival AP (an increase of 12.6) on LVIS. The model is intended for zero-shot object detection and can be used with the provided code example, which demonstrates how to load the model, process inputs, run inference, and postprocess the results. The model was trained on a variety of datasets, though the specific datasets are not listed. Evaluation results show that the model performs well across different backbones and pre-training data combinations. For example, with a Swin-T backbone and pre-training data including O365, GoldG, and V3Det, the model achieves 50.6 COCO mAP. On LVIS, the model achieves 41.4 minival AP and 31.9 val AP when trained on O365, GoldG, GRIT, and V3Det. The model is available in the specified collection, and users are encouraged to cite the provided BibTeX entry for any use of the model.

instruction: Find and list all the symptoms the patient is currently experiencing.
description: BioClinical ModernBERT is a domain-adapted encoder that builds on ModernBERT, incorporating long-context processing and substantial improvements in speed and performance for biomedical and clinical natural language processing (NLP). The model is available in two sizes: one with 150 million parameters and another with 396 million parameters. It is trained on the largest biomedical and clinical corpus to date, consisting of over 53.5 billion tokens. This includes 50.7 billion tokens of biomedical text from PubMed and PMC, and 2.8 billion tokens of clinical text from 20 datasets spanning diverse institutions, domains, and geographic regions. These datasets include clinical notes, radiology reports, pathology reports, and more, with examples from sources such as MIMIC-III, MIMIC-IV, CheXpert Plus, and others. BioClinical ModernBERT is trained in two phases: the first on a large biomedical and clinical corpus with a 30% masking probability, and the second on clinical data only with a 15% masking probability and a 1-sqrt learning rate decay. The model is designed for tasks such as masked language modeling, classification, retrieval, and question answering. It can be used with the Hugging Face Transformers library, and for optimal performance, it is recommended to use it with Flash Attention 2 if supported by the GPU. Unlike some earlier BERT models, BioClinical ModernBERT does not use token type IDs. Evaluation results show that BioClinical ModernBERT outperforms several existing models on tasks such as ChemProt, Phenotype, COS, Social History, and DEID, with performance metrics including 89.9 for ChemProt, 58.1 for Phenotype, 95.1 for COS, 58.5 for Social History, and 82.7 for DEID in the base version, and 90.8 for ChemProt, 60.8 for Phenotype, 95.1 for COS, 57.1 for Social History, and 83.8 for DEID in the large version. The model weights and training checkpoints are released under the MIT license. If used in research, it should be cited with the provided citation information. This project was a collaboration between members of the Dana-Farber Cancer Institute, LightOn, MIT, OpenEvidence, and Microsoft.

instruction: Can you detect and label the visible objects in this picture for me?
description: YoloX is a machine learning model that predicts bounding boxes and classes of objects in an image. It is optimized for mobile and edge deployment and is designed for real-time object detection. The model is an implementation of Yolo-X Small, with an input resolution of 640x640. It has 8.98 million parameters and a model size of 34.3 MB in float precision, 9.53 MB in w8a16 precision, and 8.96 MB in w8a8 precision. The model is intended to run on Qualcomm® devices and supports various target runtimes, including TFLITE, QNN_DLC, and ONNX. Performance metrics include inference time and peak memory usage across different devices and chipsets. For example, on the Samsung Galaxy S25 with Snapdragon® 8 Elite For Galaxy Mobile, the model achieves an inference time of 1.099 ms in w8a8 precision and 2.313 ms in w8a16 precision. The model can be compiled for on-device deployment using the Qualcomm® AI Hub Workbench, and it supports performance profiling, accuracy verification, and deployment to Android using TensorFlow Lite or QNN. The model can be run on cloud-hosted Qualcomm® devices for evaluation and testing. The model is available for installation via pip and includes a demo for end-to-end execution. The original implementation of Yolo-X is licensed separately.

instruction: I need to isolate the main subject in this photo so I can apply a new background for a social media post. Can you help?
description: RMBG v2.0 is a state-of-the-art background removal model developed by BRIA AI that significantly improves upon RMBG v1.4. It is designed to effectively separate foreground from background in a range of categories and image types. The model has been trained on a carefully selected dataset that includes general stock images, e-commerce, gaming, and advertising content, making it suitable for commercial use cases and enterprise content creation at scale. The model's accuracy, efficiency, and versatility currently rival leading source-available models. It is ideal for applications where content safety, legally licensed datasets, and bias mitigation are paramount. RMBG v2.0 is available as a source-available model for non-commercial use under a CC BY-NC 4.0 license, while commercial use is subject to a commercial agreement with BRIA. The model is a dichotomous image segmentation model trained exclusively on a professional-grade dataset. It outputs a single-channel 8-bit grayscale alpha matte, where each pixel value indicates the opacity level of the corresponding pixel in the original image. This non-binary output approach offers developers the flexibility to define custom thresholds for foreground-background separation, catering to varied use case requirements and enhancing integration into complex pipelines. The model was trained with over 15,000 high-quality, high-resolution, manually labeled (pixel-wise accuracy), fully licensed images. The training data includes balanced gender, balanced ethnicity, and people with different types of disabilities. The data distribution includes 45.11% objects only, 25.24% people with objects/animals, 17.35% people only, 8.52% people/objects/animals with text, 2.52% text only, and 1.89% animals only. The distribution of image types includes 87.70% photorealistic and 12.30% non-photorealistic. The background distribution includes 52.05% non-solid background and 47.95% solid background. The foreground distribution includes 51.42% single main foreground object and 48.58% multiple objects in the foreground. RMBG-2.0 is developed on an enhanced architecture with a proprietary dataset and training scheme, which significantly improves the model’s accuracy and effectiveness for the background-removal task. If used in research, the model should be cited as described in the provided citation. The model requires torch, torchvision, pillow, kornia, and transformers for usage. The model can be used by loading it with the AutoModelForImageSegmentation class, applying data transformations, and performing inference to generate a mask that is then applied to the original image to remove the background.

instruction: How can I achieve faster results in image diffusion without sacrificing clarity?
description: The Deep Compression Autoencoder (DC-AE) is a new family of autoencoder models designed to accelerate high-resolution diffusion models. Existing autoencoder models perform well at moderate spatial compression ratios (e.g., 8x) but struggle to maintain satisfactory reconstruction accuracy at higher ratios (e.g., 64x). DC-AE addresses this challenge through two key techniques: Residual Autoencoding, which involves learning residuals based on space-to-channel transformed features to reduce the optimization difficulty of high spatial-compression autoencoders, and Decoupled High-Resolution Adaptation, a three-phase training strategy that mitigates the generalization penalty of high spatial-compression autoencoders. These techniques allow DC-AE to achieve a spatial compression ratio of up to 128 while maintaining reconstruction quality. When applied to latent diffusion models, DC-AE provides significant speedup without a drop in accuracy. For example, on ImageNet 512x512, DC-AE provides 19.1x inference speedup and 17.9x training speedup on H100 GPU for UViT-H while achieving a better FID compared with the widely used SD-VAE-f8 autoencoder.

instruction: Is the sentence 'The plant needs sunlight' entailed by 'It will not grow without light'?
description: This model, named DeBERTa-v3-large-mnli-fever-anli-ling-wanli, was fine-tuned on the MNLI, Fever-NLI, Adversarial-NLI, and LingNLI datasets, which together contain 885,242 natural language inference (NLI) hypothesis-premise pairs. It is the best-performing NLI model on the Hugging Face Hub as of June 6, 2022, and is suitable for zero-shot classification. It significantly outperforms all other large models on the NLI task. The foundation model is DeBERTa-v3, which incorporates several recent innovations compared to classical masked language models like BERT and RoBERTa. The model was trained using the Hugging Face trainer with specific hyperparameters, including four training epochs, a learning rate of 5e-06, a per-device train batch size of 16, gradient accumulation steps of 2, a per-device evaluation batch size of 64, a warmup ratio of 0.06, a weight decay of 0.01, and mixed precision training. The training data explicitly excluded a dataset due to quality issues, as more data does not necessarily lead to better NLI model performance. The model was evaluated using the test sets for MultiNLI, ANLI, LingNLI, WANLI, and the dev set for Fever-NLI, with accuracy as the metric. It achieves state-of-the-art performance on each dataset, outperforming the previous ALBERT-XXL model by 8.3%. This is likely due to the use of a better pre-training objective (RTD), disentangled attention, and fine-tuning on higher quality NLI data. The model achieves an accuracy of 0.912 on MNLI test (matched), 0.908 on MNLI test (mismatched), 0.702 on ANLI test, 0.64 on ANLI test R3, 0.87 on LingNLI test, and 0.77 on WANLI test. The model's speed on an A100 GPU is 696.0 text per second for MNLI test (matched), 697.0 for MNLI test (mismatched), 488.0 for ANLI test, 425.0 for ANLI test R3, 828.0 for LingNLI test, and 980.0 for WANLI test. The model will reproduce statistical patterns present in the training data. For more information on the training data and potential biases, consult the original DeBERTa-v3 paper and literature on different NLI datasets. If you use this model, please cite the relevant preprint. Older versions of Hugging Face Transformers may have issues running the model, such as tokenizer-related problems. Using Transformers version 4.13 or higher may resolve some of these issues.

instruction: Summarize this article on the impact of inflation on global markets.
description: This model is a version of PEGASUS fine-tuned for financial summarization. It was trained on a novel financial news dataset containing 2,000 articles covering topics such as stocks, markets, currencies, interest rates, and cryptocurrencies. The model is based on the PEGASUS architecture, which was originally proposed by Jingqing Zhang, Yao Zhao, Mohammad Saleh, and Peter J. Liu. PEGASUS was previously fine-tuned on the Extreme Summarization (XSum) dataset. The model is designed to generate concise summaries of financial news articles. Evaluation results show that after fine-tuning, the model achieved ROUGE-1 of 23.55, ROUGE-2 of 6.99, ROUGE-L of 18.14, and ROUGE-S of 21.36, compared to 13.8, 2.4, 10.63, and 12.03, respectively, before fine-tuning. A more advanced version of the model is available via Rapid API, offering more than a 16% increase in ROUGE scores compared to the base model. This advanced version also provides multiple plans tailored to different use cases and workloads. The model can be used with the Hugging Face Transformers library by loading the appropriate tokenizer and model. The model is described in the workshop paper 'Towards Human-Centered Summarization: A Case Study on Financial News,' published in the Proceedings of the First Workshop on Bridging Human-Computer Interaction and Natural Language Processing in 2021. Contact information is provided for those interested in a more sophisticated version of the model trained on a larger dataset and customized to specific needs.

instruction: Show me a rotating image of a tree with leaves blowing in the wind.
description: This LoRA is trained on the Wan2.1 14B I2V 480p model and allows you to rotate any object in an image. The effect works on a wide variety of objects, from animals to vehicles to people. It transforms any image into a video of it being rotated and provides consistent results across different object types. The model is designed to be used with a simple prompt structure that is easy to adapt. The key trigger phrase is 'r0t4tion 360 degrees rotation'. The model is available in Safetensors format and is intended to be used with a modified version of Kijai's Wan Video Wrapper workflow, which includes a Wan LoRA node connected to the base model. The model was trained on 30 seconds of video comprised of 12 short clips (each clip captioned separately) of things being rotated, over the course of 20 epochs. The base model used for training is Wan2.1 14B I2V 480p. Training was conducted using Diffusion Pipe for Training. Special thanks are given to Kijai for the ComfyUI Wan Video Wrapper and to tdrussell for the training scripts. Recommended settings for use include a LoRA Strength of 1.0, an Embedded Guidance Scale of 6.0, and a Flow Shift of 5.0.

instruction: Could you create a visual sequence of a dancer performing in a moonlit garden?
description: This model is a dilated controlnet module designed for the Wan2.1 model. The dilated controlnet includes fewer basic blocks and incorporates a `stride` parameter. For the Wan14B model, the number of controlnet blocks is 6 and the stride is set to 4. The model supports inference tasks for video generation, including command-line interface and Gradio-based web demonstrations. It is intended to be used with the Wan-AI/Wan2.1-T2V-14B-Diffusers base model and the TheDenk/wan2.1-t2v-14b-controlnet-hed-v1 controlnet model. The model is associated with the HED controlnet type and is developed by Karachev Denis, with the code and model available on Github. Professional support can be requested via email.

instruction: What objects are visible in this photo, and how are they segmented?
description: The DETR (DEtection TRansformer) model is an encoder-decoder transformer with a convolutional backbone, specifically a ResNet-50 architecture. It is designed for end-to-end object detection and can be extended to perform panoptic segmentation by adding a mask head on top of the decoder outputs. The model uses object queries to detect objects in an image, with 100 object queries for the COCO dataset. It is trained using a bipartite matching loss, which compares predicted classes and bounding boxes of each object query to ground truth annotations. The Hungarian matching algorithm is used to create an optimal one-to-one mapping between queries and annotations. The loss function includes cross-entropy for class labels and a combination of L1 and generalized IoU loss for bounding boxes. The model was trained on the COCO 2017 panoptic dataset, which includes 118,000 annotated images for training and 5,000 for validation. During training, images were resized such that the shortest side was at least 800 pixels and the longest side at most 1333 pixels, and normalized using the ImageNet mean and standard deviation. The model was trained for 300 epochs on 16 V100 GPUs with a total batch size of 64. On the COCO 2017 validation set, the model achieves a box average precision (AP) of 38.8, a segmentation AP of 31.1, and a panoptic quality (PQ) of 43.4. The model is intended for use in panoptic segmentation tasks and is available for use with PyTorch. The model was introduced in a paper by Carion et al. and first released by the Hugging Face team, as the original team did not provide a model card.

instruction: Can you create a simple budget plan for someone starting their first job?
description: Qwen3 is the latest generation of large language models in the Qwen series, offering a comprehensive suite of dense and mixture-of-experts (MoE) models. Built upon extensive training, Qwen3 delivers groundbreaking advancements in reasoning, instruction-following, agent capabilities, and multilingual support. It uniquely supports seamless switching between thinking mode (for complex logical reasoning, math, and coding) and non-thinking mode (for efficient, general-purpose dialogue) within a single model, ensuring optimal performance across various scenarios. It significantly enhances its reasoning capabilities, surpassing previous QwQ (in thinking mode) and Qwen2.5 instruct models (in non-thinking mode) on mathematics, code generation, and commonsense logical reasoning. It excels in human preference alignment, performing well in creative writing, role-playing, multi-turn dialogues, and instruction following, delivering a more natural, engaging, and immersive conversational experience. It is expert in agent capabilities, enabling precise integration with external tools in both thinking and unthinking modes and achieving leading performance among open-source models in complex agent-based tasks. It supports 100+ languages and dialects with strong capabilities for multilingual instruction following and translation. The FP8 version of Qwen3-32B is a causal language model with 32.8B parameters, 31.2B non-embedding parameters, 64 layers, and 64 attention heads for Q and 8 for KV. It has a context length of 32,768 tokens natively. The model can be used with inference frameworks such as transformers, sglang, and vllm. It supports switching between thinking and non-thinking modes via the enable_thinking parameter or user input tags like /think and /no_think. For thinking mode, it is recommended to use Temperature=0.6, TopP=0.95, TopK=20, and MinP=0. For non-thinking mode, Temperature=0.7, TopP=0.8, TopK=20, and MinP=0 are suggested. The model supports long text processing up to 131,072 tokens using RoPE scaling techniques. It is advised to use YaRN with a scaling factor of 4.0 for context lengths beyond 32,768 tokens. The model can be deployed using SGLang or vLLM to create an OpenAI-compatible API endpoint. It is recommended to use an output length of 32,768 tokens for most queries and 38,912 tokens for complex problems. For math problems, the prompt should include 'Please reason step by step, and put your final answer within \boxed{}.' For multiple-choice questions, the prompt should include a JSON structure to standardize responses. The model has known issues with the 'fine-grained fp8' method in transformers for distributed inference, and users may need to set the environment variable CUDA_LAUNCH_BLOCKING=1 if multiple devices are used. The model is available for local use with applications such as Ollama, LMStudio, MLX-LM, llama.cpp, and KTransformers. The model can be used with Qwen-Agent for agentic use, which encapsulates tool-calling templates and parsers to reduce coding complexity. The model's performance on long contexts may be impacted by static YaRN scaling, and it is recommended to modify the scaling factor as needed. The default max_position_embeddings in config.json is set to 40,960, which includes 32,768 tokens for outputs and 8,192 tokens for typical prompts. The endpoint provided by Alibaba Model Studio supports dynamic YaRN by default.

instruction: What are the top three slides most similar to this one in the training dataset?
description: TITAN (Transformer-based pathology Image and Text Alignment Network) is a multimodal whole-slide foundation model pre-trained using visual self-supervised learning and vision-language alignment. It is developed by the Mahmood Lab AI for Pathology at Harvard/BWH and is based on pretrained vision-language encoders. TITAN leverages 335,645 whole-slide images (WSIs) from a diverse set of internally collected neoplastic, infectious, and inflammatory cases at Mass General Brigham, as well as over 182,000 pathology reports and more than 423,000 synthetic captions generated by a pathology co-pilot. The pretraining dataset, called Mass-340K, is sourced from private histology collections at BWH and MGH, in addition to slides from the public GTEx consortium. TITAN's slide embeddings achieve state-of-the-art performance on diverse downstream tasks, including linear probing, few-shot and zero-shot classification, rare cancer retrieval, cross-modal retrieval, and pathology report generation. TITAN is a vision-language model trained on CONCH v1.5 patch features with a patch size of 512x512 pixels at 20x magnification. It builds feature grids from CONCH v1.5 patch features using the coordinates and the distance between the patches. Patch coordinates are saved at the slides' level 0 magnification, and TITAN takes patch_size_lv0, which represents the distance between two adjacent patches at level 0 magnification. Slide-level feature extraction can be performed using the model's encode_slide_from_patch_features function. These pre-extracted features can be used for slide-level classification (via linear probing), retrieval (via l2 distance), and other machine learning settings, without task-specific fine-tuning. The model and associated code are released under the CC-BY-NC-ND 4.0 license and may only be used for non-commercial, academic research purposes with proper attribution. Any commercial use, sale, or other monetization of the TITAN model and its derivatives is prohibited and requires prior approval. Downloading the model requires prior registration on Hugging Face and agreeing to the terms of use. By downloading this model, users agree not to distribute, publish, or reproduce a copy of the model. If another user within an organization wishes to use the TITAN model, they must register as an individual user and agree to comply with the terms of use. Users may not attempt to re-identify the deidentified data used to develop the underlying model. If a user is a commercial entity, they must contact the corresponding author. Access to the model requires agreement to the outlined terms of use, with the primary email for the HuggingFace account matching the user's institutional email. Requests with a personal email (e.g., @gmail, @hotmail, @qq) will be denied. To resolve this, users must add their official institutional email to their HuggingFace account, confirm it, and set it as their primary email. Other reasons for access denial include mistakes in the submitted form, such as a full name with abbreviations, an affiliation not spelled out, an insufficiently described research use, or an unrecognized email domain. The model is available for use with the following requirements: torch==2.0.1, timm==1.0.3, einops==0.6.1, einops-exts==0.0.4, and transformers==4.46.0. The model can be loaded using the huggingface_hub library and the transformers library. TITAN is designed to support a range of tasks, including slide-level feature extraction, classification, retrieval, and report generation. The model is described in the article 'A multimodal whole-slide foundation model for pathology' published in Nature Medicine in 2025. The project was built on top of existing repositories, including ViT model implementations. The authors acknowledge the contributions of the developers of these repositories.
