instruction: Can you estimate the home price for a house in a neighborhood with 3.5 rooms per dwelling and a population of 1,200 people?
description: This is a RandomForestQuantileRegressor trained on the California Housing dataset. The model is not ready to be used in production. It was trained using default parameters on a 5-fold cross-validation pipeline. The hyperparameters include bootstrap set to True, ccp_alpha set to 0.0, criterion set to 'squared_error', default_quantiles set to 0.5, max_features set to 1.0, max_samples_leaf set to 1, min_impurity_decrease set to 0.0, min_samples_leaf set to 1, min_samples_split set to 2, min_weight_fraction_leaf set to 0.0, n_estimators set to 100, oob_score set to False, random_state set to RandomState(MT19937), verbose set to 0, and warm_start set to False. The model's evaluation results include a Mean Absolute Percentage Error of 0.164007, a Median Absolute Error of 0.171, a Mean Squared Error of 0.25832, and an R-Squared value of 0.806.

instruction: Can you describe the color scheme or visual style of this video?
description: The LLaVA-Video-7B-Qwen2 is a 7/72B parameter model trained on a mixture of 1.6 million single-image, multi-image, and video data, using the Qwen2 language model with a context window of 32K tokens. The model is based on the SO400M architecture and was initialized from the lmms-lab/llava-onevision-qwen2-7b-si model. It supports interaction with images, multi-image, and video content, with a specific focus on video. The model can process up to 64 frames and is trained for one epoch using bfloat16 precision. It is intended for tasks involving video understanding and description, including answering questions related to video content. The model was trained using 256 Nvidia Tesla A100 GPUs. The model supports English and Chinese languages. The model's training data and architecture are designed to enable detailed video analysis and interaction. The model is described as having the ability to describe videos in detail and answer related questions. The model is available for use and generation, with a provided example of a generation process using Python code. The model is associated with a research paper titled 'Video Instruction Tuning With Synthetic Data' by Yuanhan Zhang, Jinming Wu, Wei Li, Bo Li, Zejun Ma, Ziwei Liu, and Chunyuan Li, available on arXiv with the identifier 2410.02713.

instruction: What image would best capture the idea of 'a futuristic city skyline at dusk'?
description: The CLIP model was developed by researchers at OpenAI to study what contributes to robustness in computer vision tasks and to test the ability of models to generalize to arbitrary image classification tasks in a zero-shot manner. It was not developed for general model deployment, and researchers are advised to carefully study its capabilities in relation to the specific context of deployment. The model was developed in January 2021 and uses a ViT-B/16 Transformer architecture as an image encoder and a masked self-attention Transformer as a text encoder. These encoders are trained to maximize the similarity of (image, text) pairs via a contrastive loss. The original implementation had two variants: one using a ResNet image encoder and the other using a Vision Transformer. This repository contains the variant with the Vision Transformer. The model is intended for research purposes and is primarily aimed at AI researchers to better understand robustness, generalization, and other capabilities, biases, and constraints of computer vision models. Any deployed use case of the model, whether commercial or not, is currently out of scope. Non-deployed use cases such as image search in a constrained environment are also not recommended unless there is thorough in-domain testing of the model with a specific, fixed class taxonomy. This is because the model's performance varies significantly with different class taxonomies, and untested deployment could be harmful. Use cases related to surveillance and facial recognition are always out of scope, regardless of the model's performance, due to the lack of testing norms and checks to ensure fair use. The model has not been trained or evaluated on any languages other than English, so its use should be limited to English language use cases. The model was trained on publicly available image-caption data, including a combination of crawling a handful of websites and using commonly-used pre-existing image datasets. A large portion of the data comes from crawling the internet, which may skew towards more developed nations and younger, male users. The goal of the dataset was to test robustness and generalizability in computer vision tasks, and the data was gathered in a mostly non-interventionist manner. However, only websites with policies against excessively violent and adult images were crawled, and such content was filtered out. The dataset is not intended for use as the basis for any commercial or deployed model and will not be released. The model has been evaluated on a wide range of benchmarks across various computer vision datasets, including Food101, CIFAR10, CIFAR100, Birdsnap, SUN397, Stanford Cars, FGVC Aircraft, VOC2007, DTD, Oxford-IIIT Pet dataset, Caltech101, Flowers102, MNIST, SVHN, IIIT5K, Hateful Memes, SST-2, UCF101, Kinetics700, Country211, CLEVR Counting, KITTI Distance, STL-10, RareAct, Flickr30, MSCOCO, ImageNet, ImageNet-A, ImageNet-R, ImageNet Sketch, ObjectNet (ImageNet Overlap), Youtube-BB, and ImageNet-Vid. The model has several limitations, including struggles with fine-grained classification and counting objects. It also exhibits issues with fairness and bias, which are discussed in the paper. The performance of CLIP can depend significantly on class design and the choices made for categories to include and exclude. Testing revealed significant disparities with respect to race and gender when classifying images of people into crime-related and non-human animal categories, and these disparities could shift based on how the classes were constructed. Evaluations on gender, race, and age classification using the Fairface dataset showed accuracy greater than 96% across all races for gender classification, with 'Middle Eastern' having the highest accuracy (98.4%) and 'White' having the lowest (96.5%). CLIP averaged approximately 93% for racial classification and approximately 63% for age classification. These evaluations are used to assess the model's performance across different demographics and to surface potential risks, not to endorse or promote such tasks. The approach to testing CLIP also has a limitation: in many cases, linear probes were used to evaluate performance, and there is evidence suggesting that linear probes can underestimate model performance.

instruction: I want to create a transparent version of this image for a design project—can you help me remove the background?
description: RMBG v2.0 is a state-of-the-art background removal model developed by BRIA AI that significantly improves upon RMBG v1.4. It is designed to effectively separate foreground from background in a range of categories and image types. The model has been trained on a carefully selected dataset that includes general stock images, e-commerce, gaming, and advertising content, making it suitable for commercial use cases and enterprise content creation at scale. The model's accuracy, efficiency, and versatility currently rival leading source-available models. It is ideal for applications where content safety, legally licensed datasets, and bias mitigation are paramount. RMBG v2.0 is available as a source-available model for non-commercial use under a CC BY-NC 4.0 license, while commercial use is subject to a commercial agreement with BRIA. The model is a dichotomous image segmentation model trained exclusively on a professional-grade dataset. It outputs a single-channel 8-bit grayscale alpha matte, where each pixel value indicates the opacity level of the corresponding pixel in the original image. This non-binary output approach offers developers the flexibility to define custom thresholds for foreground-background separation, catering to varied use case requirements and enhancing integration into complex pipelines. The model was trained with over 15,000 high-quality, high-resolution, manually labeled (pixel-wise accuracy), fully licensed images. The training data includes balanced gender, balanced ethnicity, and people with different types of disabilities. The data distribution includes 45.11% objects only, 25.24% people with objects/animals, 17.35% people only, 8.52% people/objects/animals with text, 2.52% text only, and 1.89% animals only. The distribution of image types includes 87.70% photorealistic and 12.30% non-photorealistic. The background distribution includes 52.05% non-solid background and 47.95% solid background. The foreground distribution includes 51.42% single main foreground object and 48.58% multiple objects in the foreground. RMBG-2.0 is developed on an enhanced architecture with a proprietary dataset and training scheme, which significantly improves the model’s accuracy and effectiveness for the background-removal task. If used in research, the model should be cited as described in the provided citation. The model requires torch, torchvision, pillow, kornia, and transformers for usage. The model can be used by loading it with the AutoModelForImageSegmentation class, applying data transformations, and performing inference to generate a mask that is then applied to the original image to remove the background.

instruction: I want to isolate the pedestrians in this video frame—can you do that?
description: BiSeNet (Bilateral Segmentation Network) is a novel architecture designed for real-time semantic segmentation. It addresses the challenge of balancing spatial resolution and receptive field by employing a Spatial Path to preserve high-resolution features and a context path to capture sufficient receptive field. The model is optimized for mobile deployment and is capable of segmenting images or video by class in real-time on device. It supports semantic segmentation tasks and is implemented with an input resolution of 720x960 and 12.0 million parameters. The model size in float precision is 45.7 MB. Performance metrics include inference times ranging from 3.775 ms to 232.951 ms and peak memory usage ranging from 0 MB to 239 MB, depending on the device and precision (float or w8a8). The model is compatible with various Qualcomm® devices and chipsets, including QCS8275, QCS8450, QCS8550, SA7255P, SA8255, SA8295P, SA8650, SA8775P, and Snapdragon series. It can be run using different runtimes such as TFLITE, QNN_DLC, and ONNX. The model is available for deployment on Android using TensorFlow Lite and QNN shared libraries. The model can be installed via pip and run using the Qualcomm® AI Hub Workbench for cloud-hosted device execution. It includes a demo for end-to-end execution and scripts for performance profiling, compilation, and on-device inference. The model is designed to be used with the Qualcomm® AI Hub for optimization, validation, and deployment on-device. The model is available for evaluation and deployment on various devices, and more details on performance can be found on the Qualcomm® AI Hub. The license for the original implementation of BiseNet is available.

instruction: Could you locate the paragraph in the white paper that introduces the new framework?
description: Nomic Embed Multimodal 7B is a dense state-of-the-art multimodal embedding model designed for visual document retrieval tasks. It achieves 58.8 NDCG@5 on the Vidore-v2 benchmark, outperforming all other dense multimodal embedding models. The model directly encodes interleaved text and images without requiring complex preprocessing. It is a 7B parameter multimodal embedding model with a vision-language architecture that supports unified text and image input processing. The model was fine-tuned from Qwen2.5-VL 7B Instruct and incorporates key innovations such as same-source sampling to create harder in-batch negatives and hard negative mining with positive-aware techniques. It is fully open-source, with model weights, training data, and code available. The model integrates seamlessly with Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) workflows by enabling direct document embedding, faster processing, and capturing both textual and visual cues in a single embedding. It is particularly effective for real-world document retrieval scenarios, including research papers, technical documentation, product catalogs, financial reports, visually rich content, and multilingual documents. Performance may vary when processing documents with unconventional layouts, unusual visual elements, handwriting, or heavily stylized fonts. While it supports multiple languages, performance is strongest on English content. Processing very large or complex documents may require dividing them into smaller chunks.

instruction: Translate this Burmese text into English for better understanding.
description: GemmaX2-28-2B-v0.1 is an LLM-based translation model. It has been fine-tuned on GemmaX2-28-2B-Pretrain, which is a language model developed through continual pretraining of Gemma2-2B using a mix of 56 billion tokens from both monolingual and parallel data across 28 different languages. GemmaX2-28-2B-Pretrain is obtained by continually pretraining Gemma2-2B on a large amount of monolingual and parallel data. Subsequently, GemmaX2-28-2B-v0.1 is derived through supervised fine-tuning on a small set of high-quality translation instruction data. The model supports translation in the following languages: Arabic, Bengali, Czech, German, English, Spanish, Persian, French, Hebrew, Hindi, Indonesian, Italian, Japanese, Khmer, Korean, Lao, Malay, Burmese, Dutch, Polish, Portuguese, Russian, Thai, Tagalog, Turkish, Urdu, Vietnamese, and Chinese. GemmaX2-28-2B-v0.1 only supports the 28 languages listed above and does not guarantee strong translation performance for other languages. We will continue to enhance the translation performance of GemmaX2-28-2B, and future models will be released in due course.

instruction: I want to simulate a depth map for a video frame—can you process this image?
description: DPT-Hybrid, also known as MiDaS 3.0, is a Dense Prediction Transformer (DPT) model trained on 1.4 million images for monocular depth estimation. It was introduced in the paper by Ranftl et al. (2021) and first released in an unspecified year. DPT uses the Vision Transformer (ViT) as a backbone and adds a neck and head on top for monocular depth estimation. This repository hosts the 'hybrid' version of the model as stated in the paper. DPT-Hybrid diverges from DPT by using a different backbone and taking some activations from the backbone. The model card was written in combination by the Hugging Face team and Intel. The model is intended for zero-shot monocular depth estimation and is suitable for anyone working in this domain. However, it is generally expected that the model will need to be fine-tuned for specific tasks and should not be used to intentionally create hostile or alienating environments for people. The model was trained on a dataset called MIX 6, which contains around 1.4 million images, and was initialized with ImageNet-pretrained weights. During training, images were resized such that the longer side was 384 pixels, and random square crops of size 384 were used. Random horizontal flips were applied for data augmentation. Inference was completed on an Intel Xeon Platinum 8280 CPU @ 2.70GHz with 8 physical cores and an NVIDIA RTX 2080 GPU. Performance on monocular depth estimation benchmarks includes a DIW WHDR of 11.06 (-11.2%), an ETH3D AbsRel of 0.093 (-27.6%), a Sintel AbsRel of 0.274 (-16.2%), a KITTI δ>1.25 of 11.56 (-51.6%), a NYU δ>1.25 of 8.69 (-9.0%), and a TUM δ>1.25 of 10.89 (-23.2%). The model is not intended to inform decisions central to human life or flourishing and is an aggregated set of monocular depth image datasets. No additional risk mitigation strategies were considered during model development, and the extent of the risks involved by using the model remains unknown. Users should be made aware of the risks, biases, and limitations of the model.

instruction: Could you generate an accurate depth map for this indoor scene to improve robotic navigation?
description: Prompt Depth Anything is a high-resolution and accurate metric depth estimation method. It leverages prompting to harness the power of depth foundation models, drawing inspiration from the success of prompting in vision-language and large language model foundation models. The method uses widely available iPhone LiDAR as a prompt to guide the model in producing up to 4K resolution accurate metric depth. A scalable data pipeline is introduced to train the method. Prompt Depth Anything is designed to benefit downstream applications, including 3D reconstruction and generalized robotic grasping. The model is compatible with Hugging Face Transformers and can be used with an image processor and depth estimation model from the transformers library. The model takes an input image and a prompt depth image, processes them, and outputs a predicted depth estimation. The predicted depth is post-processed to match the target image size. If you find this project useful, you are encouraged to cite the associated research paper titled 'Prompting Depth Anything for 4K Resolution Accurate Metric Depth Estimation' by Lin, Haotong and Peng, Sida and Chen, Jingxiao and Peng, Songyou and Sun, Jiaming and Liu, Minghuan and Bao, Hujun and Feng, Jiashi and Zhou, Xiaowei and Kang, Bingyi, published in arXiv in 2024.

instruction: Please create a voice recording of this message using the 'bm_lewis' voice for a professional tone.
description: Kokoro is a text-to-speech (TTS) model with 82 million parameters, designed to convert text input into audio output. It is intended for use in applications requiring high-quality speech synthesis and is noted for its resilience to quantization, allowing efficient performance at a reduced model size. The model supports multiple quantization formats, including fp32, fp16, q8, q4, and mixed precision, which vary in file size and computational efficiency. Kokoro provides a range of available voices, including both male and female voices from American and British nationalities, such as 'af_bella', 'am_liam', 'bf_lily', and 'bm_lewis'. The model can be used via JavaScript or Python, with specific libraries and code examples provided for implementation. The model is described as being capable of generating speech from text, as demonstrated with the example sentence 'Life is like a box of chocolates. You never know what you're gonna get.'

instruction: Can you tell if this video shows a running motion or a jumping motion?
description: ResNet Mixed Convolutions is a network that combines 2D and 3D convolutions for video understanding. It is designed for sports and human action recognition in videos and is optimized for mobile deployment. The model is based on the ResNet-Mixed-Convolution architecture and is trained on the Kinectics-400 dataset. It accepts an input resolution of 112x112 and has 11.7 million parameters. The model size is 44.6 MB in float precision and 11.5 MB in w8a16 precision. The model is intended to run on Qualcomm® devices and supports various target runtimes including TFLITE, QNN_DLC, and ONNX. Performance metrics include inference times and memory usage across multiple Qualcomm® chipsets and devices such as the QCS8275, QCS8450, QCS8550, QCS9075, SA7255P, SA8255, SA8295P, SA8650, SA8775P, and Snapdragon® 8 Gen 3 Mobile, 8 Elite Gen 5 Mobile, and X Elite. The model is primarily executed on the NPU, with some CPU-based execution for w8a16 precision on certain devices. The model can be installed via pip and includes a demo for end-to-end execution, as well as scripts for compiling, profiling, and verifying model accuracy on cloud-hosted Qualcomm® devices. Deployment options include TensorFlow Lite and QNN for Android applications. The model is available on the Qualcomm® AI Hub for further exploration and performance analysis. The original implementation of ResNet-Mixed-Convolution is licensed separately.

instruction: Can you help identify which documents are duplicates or very similar?
description: This is a model finetuned from a base model. It maps sentences and paragraphs to a 768-dimensional dense vector space and can be used for semantic textual similarity, semantic search, paraphrase mining, text classification, clustering, and more. The model is a Sentence Transformer with a maximum sequence length of 8192 tokens and uses cosine similarity as the similarity function. It is designed for the English language and is licensed under the Apache 2.0 license. The model architecture consists of a Transformer with a ModernBertModel, a Pooling layer with mean token pooling, and a Normalize layer. The model was trained on 2,828 triplet samples with anchor, positive, and negative text examples. Training used a batch size of 16, a learning rate of 2e-05, and one training epoch with a warmup ratio of 0.1. The model was evaluated on 489 samples, achieving a cosine accuracy of 0.9959 on the dev dataset. The model is intended for use with the Sentence Transformers library and supports inference for generating embeddings and computing similarity scores between text inputs.

instruction: Create an image of a futuristic cityscape using a continuous-valued generation process.
description: This model, referred to as MAR, presents a new method for autoregressive image generation that does not require vector quantization. Rather than using discrete tokens, the model functions within a continuous-valued space, employing a diffusion process to represent the per-token probability distribution. The model utilizes a Diffusion Loss function to enable efficient and high-quality image generation while leveraging the speed benefits of autoregressive sequence modeling. This method streamlines the generation process, making it suitable for a wider range of continuous-valued domains beyond image synthesis. The model can be loaded using the Hugging Face DiffusionPipeline, with options to customize parameters such as the model type, number of steps, and class labels. Three pre-trained MAR models are available in safetensors format: mar-base.safetensors, mar-large.safetensors, and mar-huge.safetensors. The model is based on the paper titled 'Autoregressive Image Generation without Vector Quantization' by Li, Tianhong and Tian, Yonglong and Li, He and Deng, Mingyang and He, Kaiming, published as an arXiv preprint arXiv:2406.11838 in 2024. The implementation is a Hugging Face Diffusers/GPU version of the paper, with the official PyTorch implementation also available. The developers acknowledge Congyue Deng and Xinlei Chen for helpful discussions, and Google TPU Research Cloud (TRC) for providing access to TPUs, and Google Cloud Platform for supporting GPU resources. A significant portion of the code in this repository is based on other sources.

instruction: Can you help me identify if there are any duplicate questions in this list of user inquiries?
description: This is a Sentence Transformer model trained on the train_set dataset. It maps sentences and paragraphs to a 1024-dimensional dense vector space and can be used for semantic textual similarity, semantic search, paraphrase mining, text classification, clustering, and more. The model is trained using a Transformer Encoder and is designed to handle a maximum sequence length of 8192 tokens. It uses cosine similarity as the similarity function. The model architecture includes a Transformer with XLMRobertaModel, a Pooling layer with specific configuration parameters, and a Normalize layer. The model is trained on a Korean dataset in addition to Chinese and English, but learning other languages beyond these is insufficient, so additional learning is needed to optimize use of other languages. Evaluation results show that the model performs well on the Korean Embedding Benchmark and the Miracl benchmark, with high cosine_ndcg@10 scores. However, the model's performance may vary depending on the domain and the length of the corpus. The model was trained with specific hyperparameters, including a batch size of 32768, a learning rate of 3e-05, and a warmup ratio of 0.03333333333333333.

instruction: Could you locate the contact number provided by the service provider?
description: This is a fine-tuned document question-answering model based on layoutlmv3-base. It is trained to understand documents using OCR data via PaddleOCR and accurately answer questions related to structured information in the document layout. The model is named 'document-qa-model' and was fine-tuned by Lakshya Singh as a solo contributor. It supports English, Spanish, French, German, and Italian languages and is licensed under Apache-2.0, inherited from the base model. The intended use is to extract answers to structured queries from scanned documents. The model is not suitable for conversational QA or images with no OCR-processed text. It can be used for question answering on document images such as PDFs, invoices, and utility bills, as well as for information extraction tasks using OCR and layout-aware understanding. The model was trained on a dataset consisting of images of utility bills and documents, OCR data with bounding boxes from PaddleOCR, queries in English, Spanish, and Chinese, and answer spans with match scores and positions. The training procedure involved preprocessing with PaddleOCR to extract tokens, positions, and structure, using the LayoutLMv3-base model for four epochs with a learning rate schedule. The model performs well on document-style QA tasks, especially with clearly structured OCR results and document types similar to utility bills, invoices, and forms.

instruction: Create a short video showing this image as the starting point of an evolving scene.
description: CogVideoX-5B-I2V is an open-source video generation model designed for image-to-video tasks. It is part of the CogVideoX series, which includes multiple versions with varying model sizes and capabilities. CogVideoX-5B-I2V is a larger model that provides higher video generation quality and better visual effects compared to the entry-level CogVideoX-2B model. The model is trained in BF16 precision and is recommended to use the same precision for inference. It supports video generation with a maximum length of 6 seconds, a frame rate of 8 frames per second, and a resolution of 720 x 480. The model uses a 3D RoPE position embedding with a learnable component. It accepts English prompts with a maximum length of 226 tokens. The model supports deployment using the Hugging Face diffusers library and can be fine-tuned using LORA or SFT methods. Fine-tuning requires significant GPU memory, with requirements ranging from 47 GB to 80 GB depending on the batch size and GPU configuration. The model can be optimized for lower memory usage through quantization techniques such as INT8 weight-only quantization using TorchAO, which allows it to run on devices with limited VRAM, such as T4 Colabs. However, quantization may reduce inference speed. The model supports various inference precision modes, including FP16, BF16, FP32, FP8, and INT8, with BF16 being the recommended option. Memory usage and inference speed vary depending on the GPU architecture, with H100 GPUs generally providing faster performance than A100. The model includes optimizations such as sequential CPU offloading, VAE slicing, and tiling to reduce memory consumption, though these optimizations may slow down inference. The model is not compatible with INT4 precision. It is important to note that the model only supports English input, and other languages must be translated into English for use. The model is available for commercial use through the Qingying and API Platform. The model is released under a specific license and can be cited using the provided academic citation.

instruction: What does this graph show about the changes in temperature over time?
description: Gemma is a family of lightweight, state-of-the-art open models from Google, built from the same research and technology used to create the Gemini models. Gemma 3 models are multimodal, handling text and image input and generating text output, with open weights for both pre-trained variants and instruction-tuned variants. Gemma 3 has a large, 128K context window, multilingual support in over 140 languages, and is available in more sizes than previous versions. Gemma 3 models are well-suited for a variety of text generation and image understanding tasks, including question answering, summarization, and reasoning. Their relatively small size makes it possible to deploy them in environments with limited resources such as laptops, desktops or your own cloud infrastructure, democratizing access to state of the art AI models and helping foster innovation for everyone. The input includes text strings, such as a question, a prompt, or a document to be summarized, and images normalized to 896 x 896 resolution and encoded to 256 tokens each. The total input context is 128K tokens for the 4B, 12B, and 27B sizes, and 32K tokens for the 1B size. The output is generated text in response to the input, such as an answer to a question, analysis of image content, or a summary of a document, with a total output context of 8192 tokens. The models were trained on a dataset of text data that includes a wide variety of sources. The 27B model was trained with 14 trillion tokens, the 12B model with 12 trillion tokens, the 4B model with 4 trillion tokens, and the 1B model with 2 trillion tokens. The training data includes web documents, code, mathematics, and images. Web documents ensure the model is exposed to a broad range of linguistic styles, topics, and vocabulary, with content in over 140 languages. Code helps the model learn the syntax and patterns of programming languages, improving its ability to generate code and understand code-related questions. Mathematics helps the model learn logical reasoning, symbolic representation, and to address mathematical queries. Images enable the model to perform image analysis and visual data extraction tasks. Rigorous CSAM filtering was applied at multiple stages in the data preparation process to ensure the exclusion of harmful and illegal content. Automated techniques were used to filter out certain personal information and other sensitive data from training sets. Additional methods were applied for content quality and safety in line with Google's policies. Gemma was trained using Tensor Processing Unit (TPU) hardware (TPUv4p, TPUv5p, and TPUv5e). TPUs are specifically designed to handle the massive computations involved in training vision-language models (VLMs), offering performance, memory, scalability, and cost-effectiveness advantages. Training was done using JAX and ML Pathways. JAX allows researchers to take advantage of the latest generation of hardware, including TPUs, for faster and more efficient training of large models. ML Pathways is Google's effort to build artificially intelligent systems capable of generalizing across multiple tasks, suitable for foundation models like these. The 'single controller' programming model of JAX and Pathways allows a single Python process to orchestrate the entire training run, simplifying the development workflow. These models were evaluated against a large collection of different datasets and metrics to cover different aspects of text generation. In reasoning and factuality, the models achieved high scores on benchmarks such as HellaSwag, BoolQ, PIQA, SocialIQA, TriviaQA, Natural Questions, ARC-c, ARC-e, WinoGrande, BIG-Bench Hard, and DROP. In STEM and code, the models performed well on MMLU, AGIEval, MATH, GSM8K, GPQA, MBPP, and HumanEval. In multilingual tasks, the models showed strong performance on MGSM, Global-MMLU-Lite, WMT24++, FloRes, XQuAD, ECLeKTic, and IndicGenBench. In multimodal tasks, the models achieved high scores on COCOcap, DocVQA, InfoVQA, MMMU, TextVQA, RealWorldQA, ReMI, AI2D, ChartQA, VQAv2, BLINK, OKVQA, TallyQA, SpatialSense VQA, and CountBenchQA. The evaluation methods included structured evaluations and internal red-teaming testing of relevant content policies. Red-teaming was conducted by different teams with different goals and human evaluation metrics. The models were evaluated against categories such as child safety, content safety, and representational harms. For all areas of safety testing, the models showed major improvements in the categories of child safety, content safety, and representational harms relative to previous Gemma models. All testing was conducted without safety filters to evaluate the model capabilities and behaviors. For both text-to-text and image-to-text, and across all model sizes, the model produced minimal policy violations and showed significant improvements over previous Gemma models' performance with respect to ungrounded inferences. A limitation of the evaluations was that they included only English language prompts. These models have certain limitations that users should be aware of. The quality and diversity of the training data significantly influence the model's capabilities. Biases or gaps in the training data can lead to limitations in the model's responses. The scope of the training dataset determines the subject areas the model can handle effectively. Models are better at tasks that can be framed with clear prompts and instructions. Open-ended or highly complex tasks might be challenging. A model's performance can be influenced by the amount of context provided (longer context generally leads to better outputs, up to a certain point). Natural language is inherently complex. Models might struggle to grasp subtle nuances, sarcasm, or figurative language. Models generate responses based on information they learned from their training datasets, but they are not knowledge bases. They may generate incorrect or outdated factual statements. Models rely on statistical patterns in language. They might lack the ability to apply common sense reasoning in certain situations. The development of vision-language models (VLMs) raises several ethical concerns. VLMs trained on large-scale, real-world text and image data can reflect socio-cultural biases embedded in the training material. These models underwent careful scrutiny, input data pre-processing, and posterior evaluations. VLMs can be misused to generate text that is false, misleading, or harmful. Guidelines are provided for responsible use with the model. This model card summarizes details on the models' architecture, capabilities, limitations, and evaluation processes. A responsibly developed open model offers the opportunity to share innovation by making VLM technology accessible to developers and researchers across the AI ecosystem. Risks identified include the perpetuation of biases, generation of harmful content, misuse for malicious purposes, and privacy violations. It is encouraged to perform continuous monitoring and the exploration of de-biasing techniques during model training, fine-tuning, and other use cases. Mechanisms and guidelines for content safety are essential. Developers are encouraged to exercise caution and implement appropriate content safety safeguards based on their specific product policies and application use cases. Technical limitations and developer and end-user education can help mitigate against malicious applications of VLMs. Educational resources and reporting mechanisms for users to flag misuse are provided. Prohibited uses of Gemma models are outlined in the Gemma Prohibited Use Policy. Models were trained on data filtered for removal of certain personal information and other sensitive data. Developers are encouraged to adhere to privacy regulations with privacy-preserving techniques. At the time of release, this family of models provides high-performance open vision-language model implementations designed from the ground up for responsible AI development compared to similarly sized models. Using the benchmark evaluation metrics described in this document, these models have shown to provide superior performance to other, comparably-sized open model alternatives.

instruction: What specific pattern is on the tile in the background of the image?
description: NoctOWL (Not only coarse-text OWL) is an adaptation of OWL-ViT (referred to as NoctOWL) and OWLv2 (referred to as NoctOWLv2), designed for Fine-Grained Open-Vocabulary Detection (FG-OVD). Unlike standard open-vocabulary object detectors, which focus primarily on class-level recognition, NoctOWL enhances the ability to detect and distinguish fine-grained object attributes such as color, material, transparency, and pattern. It maintains a balanced trade-off between fine- and coarse-grained detection, making it particularly effective in scenarios requiring detailed object descriptions. The model includes the following variants: NoctOWL Base (lorebianchi98/NoctOWL-base-patch16), NoctOWLv2 Base (lorebianchi98/NoctOWLv2-base-patch16), NoctOWL Large (lorebianchi98/NoctOWL-large-patch14), and NoctOWLv2 Large (lorebianchi98/NoctOWLv2-large-patch14). Performance is reported in terms of mean Average Precision (mAP) on the Fine-Grained Open-Vocabulary Detection benchmarks across different difficulty levels, as well as performance on rare classes from the LVIS dataset. For OWL (B/16), the mAP scores are 20.6 on LVIS (Rare), 53.9 on Trivial, 38.4 on Easy, 39.8 on Medium, 26.2 on Hard, 45.3 for Color, 37.3 for Material, 26.6 for Pattern, and 34.1 for Transparency. For OWL (L/14), the scores are 31.2 on LVIS (Rare), 65.1 on Trivial, 44.0 on Easy, 39.3 on Medium, 26.5 on Hard, 43.8 for Color, 44.9 for Material, 36.0 for Pattern, and 29.2 for Transparency. For OWLv2 (B/16), the scores are 29.6 on LVIS (Rare), 52.9 on Trivial, 40.0 on Easy, 38.5 on Medium, 25.3 on Hard, 45.1 for Color, 33.5 for Material, 19.2 for Pattern, and 28.5 for Transparency. For OWLv2 (L/14), the scores are 34.9 on LVIS (Rare), 63.2 on Trivial, 42.8 on Easy, 41.2 on Medium, 25.4 on Hard, 53.3 for Color, 36.9 for Material, 23.3 for Pattern, and 12.2 for Transparency. For NoctOWL (B/16), the scores are 11.6 on LVIS (Rare), 46.6 on Trivial, 44.4 on Easy, 45.6 on Medium, 40.0 on Hard, 44.7 for Color, 46.0 for Material, 46.1 for Pattern, and 53.6 for Transparency. For NoctOWL (L/14), the scores are 26.0 on LVIS (Rare), 57.4 on Trivial, 54.2 on Easy, 54.8 on Medium, 48.6 on Hard, 53.1 for Color, 56.9 for Material, 49.8 for Pattern, and 57.2 for Transparency. For NoctOWLv2 (B/16), the scores are 17.5 on LVIS (Rare), 48.3 on Trivial, 49.1 on Easy, 47.1 on Medium, 42.1 on Hard, 46.8 for Color, 48.2 for Material, 42.2 for Pattern, and 50.2 for Transparency. For NoctOWLv2 (L/14), the scores are 27.2 on LVIS (Rare), 57.5 on Trivial, 55.5 on Easy, 57.2 on Medium, 50.2 on Hard, 55.6 for Color, 57.0 for Material, 49.2 for Pattern, and 55.9 for Transparency.

instruction: How would you explain the concept of gravity to a child?
description: The Llama 3.2 collection is a set of multilingual large language models (LLMs) available in 1B and 3B parameter sizes, designed for text-in and text-out tasks. These models are pretrained and instruction-tuned for multilingual dialogue use cases, including agentic retrieval and summarization tasks. They outperform many open source and closed chat models on common industry benchmarks. The models are developed by Meta and use an optimized transformer architecture. The instruction-tuned versions are aligned with human preferences for helpfulness and safety through supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and reinforcement learning with human feedback (RLHF). Llama 3.2 supports English, German, French, Italian, Portuguese, Hindi, Spanish, and Thai, with the potential for developers to fine-tune for additional languages, provided they comply with the Llama 3.2 Community License and the Acceptable Use Policy. The models are trained on a new mix of publicly available online data, with a knowledge cutoff in December 2023. They support up to 128k context length for the full models and 8k for the quantized versions. The models use Grouped-Query Attention (GQA) for improved inference scalability and have up to 9 trillion tokens in their pretraining data. Llama 3.2 is intended for commercial and research use in multiple languages, with instruction-tuned models suitable for assistant-like chat, agentic applications, knowledge retrieval, summarization, and mobile AI-powered writing assistants. Pretrained models can be adapted for additional natural language generation tasks, and quantized models are suitable for on-device use with limited compute resources. The models are not intended for use in any manner that violates applicable laws or regulations, or that is prohibited by the Acceptable Use Policy and Llama 3.2 Community License. Llama 3.2 is a static model trained on an offline dataset, with future versions potentially improving capabilities and safety. The model is governed by a custom, commercial license agreement. Training involved custom libraries, Meta's GPU cluster, and production infrastructure, with a total of 916k GPU hours of computation on H100-80GB hardware. Training resulted in an estimated 240 tons of location-based CO2eq emissions, though market-based emissions were 0 due to Meta's renewable energy use. The models incorporate logits from Llama 3.1 models during pretraining and use knowledge distillation after pruning. Post-training alignment includes SFT, Rejection Sampling (RS), and Direct Preference Optimization (DPO). Quantization methods include SpinQuant and QLoRA, with SpinQuant involving rotation matrix fine-tuning and GPTQ. The models were evaluated on various benchmarks, showing performance across general knowledge, reading comprehension, long context, summarization, instruction following, math, reasoning, tool use, and multilingual tasks. Inference metrics show improvements in decode speed, time-to-first-token, prefill speed, model size, and memory usage for quantized versions compared to BF16 baselines. The models are part of a responsible release approach, with safety strategies including enabling safe deployment, protecting against adversarial users, and providing community protections. Safety fine-tuning includes human-generated and synthetic data, with emphasis on model refusals and tone. Llama 3.2 should be deployed as part of an AI system with additional safety guardrails, such as Llama Guard, Prompt Guard, and Code Shield. Evaluations include adversarial datasets and red teaming exercises to identify and mitigate risks in areas like CBRNE, child safety, and cyber attacks. The models are part of industry partnerships and community initiatives, with grants and reporting mechanisms to support responsible use. Ethical considerations include openness, inclusivity, and helpfulness, with a note that the model may produce inaccurate, biased, or objectionable responses, requiring developers to perform safety testing and tuning for their specific applications.

instruction: Can you generate a synthetic patient record with similar characteristics to the UCI Diabetes dataset?
description: Tabby is a post-training architecture modification for Transformer-based large language models, enabling their use for tabular dataset synthesis. This specific demo checkpoint is based on and fine-tuned on the UCI Diabetes dataset, using a novel Plain training method as an example of Tabby’s tabular synthesis capabilities. Tabby enhances transformer-based LLMs by incorporating Mixture of Experts (MoE) layers, allowing for better modeling of structured data. The model is developed by the University of Wisconsin-Madison and shared by Sonia Cromp et al. It is an MoE-enhanced GPT-2-based causal language model for tabular data, licensed under the MIT license. The model can be used for high-fidelity synthesis of diabetes patients based on the UCI Diabetes dataset, data augmentation for training machine learning models on the same dataset, and comparison with other tabular synthesis approaches. It can also be used for further fine-tuning on other structured datasets, such as financial records, medical records, or survey data, and for generating synthetic tabular data for privacy-preserving machine learning. This Tabby checkpoint inherits biases from the GPT-2 architecture and the UCI Diabetes dataset used for fine-tuning. Considerations include bias in synthetic data feature distributions, particularly those that may reflect real-world disparities in the dataset, and potential hallucinations that do not perfectly match real-world distributions.

instruction: I want to generate a voice line with a whisper at the beginning and a shout at the end. Is that possible?
description: Dia is a 1.6B parameter text to speech model created by Nari Labs. It directly generates highly realistic dialogue from a transcript. The output can be conditioned on audio, enabling control over emotion and tone. The model can also produce nonverbal communications such as laughter, coughing, and clearing the throat. To accelerate research, access to pretrained model checkpoints and inference code is provided. The model weights are hosted on a platform, and the model currently supports only English generation. A comparison of the model to other models is also provided. The model was not fine-tuned on a specific voice, so different voices will be generated with each run. Speaker consistency can be maintained by adding an audio prompt or by fixing the seed. Features include generating dialogue using [S1] and [S2] tags, generating non-verbal sounds like (laughs), (coughs), and others, and voice cloning by uploading an audio file and placing its transcript before the script. The model can be used as a Python library by importing and running the model from the Dia package. Hardware requirements include GPU support (pytorch 2.0+, CUDA 12.6), with CPU support to be added. On enterprise GPUs, audio can be generated in real-time, while older GPUs will have slower inference times. On an A4000 GPU, the model generates approximately 40 tokens per second, with 86 tokens equaling one second of audio. The full version of Dia requires around 10GB of VRAM, with a quantized version to be added in the future. The project is licensed under the Apache License 2.0. The model is intended for research and educational use, and the following uses are strictly forbidden: identity misuse, deceptive content, and illegal or malicious use. The creators are not responsible for any misuse and oppose unethical usage. Future work includes Docker support, optimizing inference speed, and adding quantization for memory efficiency. Contributions are welcome, and the team is small, consisting of one full-time and one part-time research-engineer. The project acknowledges computation resources provided by an unnamed entity, inspiration from other works, and support from HuggingFace for the ZeroGPU Grant. The name 'Nari' is a Korean word for lily, and Jason Y. is thanked for help with data filtering.

instruction: Please identify and outline all the individual people in this photo.
description: EoMT (Encoder-only Mask Transformer) is a Vision Transformer (ViT) architecture designed for high-quality and efficient image segmentation. It was introduced in the CVPR 2025 highlight paper by Tommie Kerssies, Niccolò Cavagnero, Alexander Hermans, Narges Norouzi, Giuseppe Averta, Bastian Leibe, Gijs Dubbelman, and Daan de Geus. The key insight is that, given sufficient scale and pretraining, a plain ViT along with additional few parameters can perform segmentation without the need for task-specific decoders or pixel fusion modules. The same model backbone supports semantic, instance, and panoptic segmentation with different post-processing. The original implementation can be found in the associated repository, and the HuggingFace model page is available at the provided link.

instruction: Could you tell me the name of the person with the highest score from this data?
description: The model is a conditional generation model based on the MBart architecture, designed for table question answering (TableQA) in low-resource Indic languages, specifically Hindi and Bengali. It is intended to answer questions over structured tabular data, returning either individual cells or tables as output. The model supports tasks involving structured information retrieval and generation in these languages, where annotated data and neural models are scarce. The model is trained on large-scale datasets generated through a fully automatic data generation process, which is scalable and applicable to any low-resource language with a web presence. Evaluation results show that models trained on these datasets outperform state-of-the-art large language models. The model is evaluated on aspects such as mathematical reasoning capabilities and zero-shot cross-lingual transfer. The model uses a linearized representation of tables, where headers and rows are processed and converted into a string format for input. The model is trained using a source sequence composed of a query and the linearized input table, with the target being the linearized output table. The model is implemented using the Hugging Face Transformers library and is fine-tuned for the Hindi and Bengali languages. The model includes a preprocessing pipeline that converts numerical values and handles language-specific characters, such as converting English digits to Hindi digits. The model is evaluated on the HindiTabQA dataset, which includes questions, input tables, and answers in Hindi. The model is trained using beam search with a beam size of 5 and a maximum sequence length of 1024 tokens. The model is available for use with the Hugging Face Transformers library and requires a tokenizer configured for the source and target language, with the source and target language set to Hindi (hi_IN). The model is trained on a GPU and uses the Hugging Face Datasets library for data loading. The model is part of a research effort published in the Proceedings of the 2024 Conference on Empirical Methods in Natural Language Processing, where the authors introduce the first scalable data generation and evaluation procedures for low-resource TableQA. The model is released along with the datasets and code for reproducibility and further research.

instruction: Could you help me find the paragraph that outlines the model's focus on high-resource languages?
description: ColPali is a model based on a novel architecture and training strategy using Vision Language Models (VLMs) to efficiently index documents from their visual features. It generates multi-vector representations of text and images, extending the PaliGemma-3B model with a ColBERT strategy. ColPali was introduced in a research paper and first released as a HuggingFace `transformers` 🤗 implementation contributed by Tony Wu and Yoni Gozlan. The model is trained on a dataset of 127,460 query-page pairs, consisting of 63% from openly available academic datasets and 37% from a synthetic dataset made up of web-crawled PDF documents augmented with VLM-generated pseudo-questions. The training set is fully English, allowing for the study of zero-shot generalization to non-English languages. A validation set of 2% of the samples is used to tune hyperparameters. The model is trained for one epoch in `bfloat16` format, using low-rank adapters with `alpha=32` and `r=32` on the transformer layers from the language model and the final projection layer. Training is conducted on an 8 GPU setup with data parallelism, a learning rate of 5e-5 with linear decay and 2.5% warmup steps, and a batch size of 32. ColPali is designed for document retrieval tasks, particularly for PDF-type documents. It relies on multi-vector retrieval derived from the ColBERT late interaction mechanism, which may require engineering efforts to adapt to vector retrieval frameworks that lack native multi-vector support. The model's vision language backbone (PaliGemma) is licensed under the `gemma` license, and ColPali inherits this license. The model is primarily focused on high-resource languages and may have limited generalization to other document types or less represented languages.

description: MuQ is a large music foundation model pre-trained via Self-Supervised Learning (SSL), achieving state-of-the-art performance in various MIR (Music Information Retrieval) tasks. MuQ-MuLan is a music-text joint embedding model trained via contrastive learning, supporting both English and Chinese texts. MuQ is based on a self-supervised learning approach using Mel Residual Vector Quantization and is designed to extract music audio features. MuQ-MuLan is designed to extract both music and text embeddings and calculate their similarity. MuQ has approximately 300 million parameters and was trained on the Million Song Dataset. MuQ-MuLan has approximately 700 million parameters and was trained on music-text pairs. It is important to note that the open-sourced MuQ model may not achieve the same level of performance as reported in the paper due to differences in dataset size.

instruction: Based on the table, which region experienced the highest rainfall this year?
description: TAPAS is a BERT-like transformer model pretrained on a large corpus of English data from Wikipedia in a self-supervised fashion. It was pretrained on raw tables and associated texts without human labeling, using an automatic process to generate inputs and labels. The model was pretrained with two objectives: masked language modeling (MLM), where 15% of the words in a flattened table and associated context are randomly masked and the model must predict them, and intermediate pre-training, where the model is trained to classify whether a sentence is supported or refuted by the contents of a table using a balanced dataset of millions of syntactically created training examples. This allows the model to learn a bidirectional representation of tables and associated text, useful for downstream tasks such as answering questions about a table or determining whether a sentence is entailed or refuted by the contents of a table. The model is available in two versions: the default version uses relative position embeddings (resetting the position index at every cell of the table) and corresponds to the `tapas_wtq_wikisql_sqa_inter_masklm_base_reset` checkpoint, while the non-default version uses absolute position embeddings and corresponds to the `tapas_wtq_wikisql_sqa_inter_masklm_base` checkpoint. The model was pre-trained on MLM and an additional intermediate pre-training step, and then fine-tuned in a chain on SQA, WikiSQL, and finally WTQ. Fine-tuning was performed on 32 Cloud TPU v3 cores for 50,000 steps with a maximum sequence length of 512 and a batch size of 512. The optimizer used was Adam with a learning rate of 1.93581e-5 and a warmup ratio of 0.128960. An inductive bias was added to ensure the model only selects cells of the same column, as reflected by the `select_one_column` parameter of `TapasConfig`. The model can be used for answering questions related to a table. The model's performance varies by size and position embedding type. For the base model, the dev accuracy is 0.4525 for the noreset version and 0.4638 for the reset version. The team releasing TAPAS did not write a model card for this model, so this model card was written by the Hugging Face team and contributors.
domain: Natural Language Processing Table Question Answering

instruction: How can I turn this unlabeled image into a useful input for a downstream model?
description: The Vision Transformer (ViT) is a transformer encoder model, similar in structure to BERT, designed for supervised image classification on ImageNet. It has been adapted for self-supervised image feature extraction, allowing the model to learn meaningful features (embeddings) from images without requiring labeled data. The DINOv2 version of the ViT introduces a modification to address artifacts in attention maps, which are caused by the use of image patches as 'registers'. The authors propose adding new tokens, called 'register' tokens, during pre-training, which are discarded afterward. This modification results in no artifacts, interpretable attention maps, and improved performance. The model does not include any fine-tuned heads and is intended for use as a pre-trained encoder to extract features for downstream tasks. A linear layer can be added on top of the [CLS] token to create a standard classifier for labeled image datasets. The model can be used for feature extraction, and users are encouraged to look for fine-tuned versions for specific tasks. The model was introduced in a paper by Darcet et al. and is available through the Hugging Face model repository. The model card was written by the Hugging Face team, as the original authors did not provide one.

instruction: How can I ensure that the retrieval system works well for non-English content?
description: Snowflake's arctic-embed-m-v2.0 is an embedding model designed for retrieval performance and inference efficiency, with a focus on multilingual capabilities. It is part of a suite of embedding models developed by Snowflake and is released under the Apache 2.0 license, making it suitable for enterprise-grade multilingual search and retrieval at scale. The model excels in both English and non-English retrieval, outperforming leading open-source and proprietary models on benchmarks such as MTEB Retrieval, CLEF, and MIRACL. It has 305 million total parameters, with 113 million non-embedding parameters, and operates with 768 dimensions. The model supports a context window of up to 8192 tokens using RoPE and is optimized for inference efficiency. It also supports compression-friendly embeddings, achieving high-quality retrieval with as small as 128 bytes per vector using Matryoshka Representation Learning (MRL) and 4-bit quantization. The MRL for this model is 256 dimensions, and high-quality 128-byte compression is achieved via 4-bit quantization. The model can be used with Sentence Transformers, Huggingface Transformers, or Huggingface Transformers.js, and it supports query and document encoding with specific prefixes for optimal retrieval quality. The model's performance is measured using NDCG@10 scores, with an average of 55.4 on the BEIR (15) benchmark, 55.2 on MIRACL (4), 51.7 on CLEF (Focused), and 53.9 on CLEF (Full). When using MRL to reduce vector size by 3x, there is approximately 3% degradation in quality. The model is available for commercial use free of charge.

instruction: Can you identify the gender of the speaker in this audio and transcribe their words?
description: MERaLiON-2 is a family of speech-text large language models designed for Singapore’s multilingual and multicultural context, as well as the wider Southeast Asian region. The flagship model, MERaLiON-2-10B, integrates a localized Whisper-large-v3 speech encoder and a Gemma-2-9b text decoder. It is fine-tuned on 120,000 hours of curated speech and audio data across six diverse tasks: Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR), Spoken Question Answering (SQA), Spoken Dialogue Summarization (SDS), Audio Captioning (AC), Audio-Scene Question Answering (ASQA), and Paralinguistic Question Answering (PQA). The model supports audio inputs up to 300 seconds (5 minutes) and is adapted to handle linguistic nuances, accents, and dialects common in Singapore and neighboring countries. MERaLiON-2-10B-ASR is an ASR-optimized variant that delivers a 5–30% performance improvement over OpenAI’s whisper-large-v3 on speech recognition tasks for Singapore’s four official languages—English, Mandarin, Malay, and Tamil—as well as Indonesian, Thai, and Vietnamese. The model also demonstrates robust handling of code-switching scenarios and local colloquialisms. MERaLiON-2-3B is a lightweight variant that balances performance with reduced computational requirements. The model supports a range of tasks, including transcription, translation, summarization, audio captioning, emotion recognition, gender recognition, and speech translation. It is trained to follow complex instructions and provides detailed explanations regarding speech content and speaker emotions. MERaLiON-2-10B exhibits competitive performance in benchmark evaluations, with results comparable to other state-of-the-art open-source AudioLLMs. The model is not intended for use in tool calling, math, and coding tasks. It is trained on a global batch size of 768 for around 200k steps using 16 nodes and 128 H100 GPUs. The model has not been specifically aligned for safety and may generate inappropriate, offensive, or harmful content. Developers and users are responsible for performing their own safety fine-tuning and implementing necessary security measures.

instruction: Find the paragraph that most directly addresses the question about the properties of water.
description: This is a model that maps sentences and paragraphs to a 768-dimensional dense vector space and was designed for semantic search. It has been trained on 215 million (question, answer) pairs from diverse sources. The model uses CLS pooling and is suitable for dot-product similarity functions. It does not produce normalized embeddings. The model is intended to encode queries and text paragraphs in a dense vector space to find relevant documents. It has a limit of 512 word pieces, and text longer than that will be truncated. The model was trained on input text up to 250 word pieces and may not work well for longer text. The training data includes duplicate question pairs from WikiAnswers, automatically generated (question, paragraph) pairs from Wikipedia, (title, body) and (title, answer) pairs from StackExchanges, triplets from Bing search engine, (query, answer) pairs from Google queries and featured snippets, (question, answer) pairs from Amazon product pages, Yahoo Answers, and other sources. The total number of training tuples is 214,988,242. The model was trained using CLS-pooling, dot-product as the similarity function, and a scale of 1. The full training script is accessible in the repository as `train_script.py`. The model was developed during a project organized by Hugging Face and benefited from hardware infrastructure including 7 TPUs v3-8 and support from Google's Flax, JAX, and Cloud teams.

instruction: Please turn this audio of a language lesson into a written summary in the target language.
description: Whisper is a state-of-the-art model for automatic speech recognition (ASR) and speech translation, proposed in the paper by Alec Radford et al. from OpenAI. Trained on over 5 million hours of labeled data, Whisper demonstrates a strong ability to generalize to many datasets and domains in a zero-shot setting. Whisper large-v3-turbo is a fine-tuned version of a pruned model. In other words, it's the exact same model, except that the number of decoding layers has been reduced from 32 to 4. As a result, the model is significantly faster, at the expense of a minor quality degradation. Whisper is a Transformer-based encoder-decoder model, also referred to as a sequence-to-sequence model. There are two flavors of Whisper model: English-only and multilingual. The English-only models were trained on the task of English speech recognition. The multilingual models were trained simultaneously on multilingual speech recognition and speech translation. For speech recognition, the model predicts transcriptions in the same language as the audio. For speech translation, the model predicts transcriptions to a different language to the audio. Whisper checkpoints come in five configurations of varying model sizes. The smallest four are available as English-only and multilingual. The largest checkpoints are multilingual only. All ten of the pre-trained checkpoints are available on the Hugging Face Hub. The pre-trained Whisper model demonstrates a strong ability to generalize to different datasets and domains. However, its predictive capabilities can be improved further for certain languages and tasks through fine-tuning. The primary intended users of these models are AI researchers studying robustness, generalization, capabilities, biases, and constraints of the current model. However, Whisper is also potentially quite useful as an ASR solution for developers, especially for English speech recognition. We recognize that once models are released, it is impossible to restrict access to only 'intended' uses or to draw reasonable guidelines around what is or is not research. The models are primarily trained and evaluated on ASR and speech translation to English tasks. They show strong ASR results in approximately 10 languages. They may exhibit additional capabilities, particularly if fine-tuned on certain tasks like voice activity detection, speaker classification, or speaker diarization but have not been robustly evaluated in these areas. We strongly recommend that users perform robust evaluations of the models in a particular context and domain before deploying them. In particular, we caution against using Whisper models to transcribe recordings of individuals taken without their consent or purporting to use these models for any kind of subjective classification. We recommend against use in high-risk domains like decision-making contexts, where flaws in accuracy can lead to pronounced flaws in outcomes. The models are intended to transcribe and translate speech, use of the model for classification is not only not evaluated but also not appropriate, particularly to infer human attributes. Our studies show that, over many existing ASR systems, the models exhibit improved robustness to accents, background noise, technical language, as well as zero shot translation from multiple languages into English; and that accuracy on speech recognition and translation is near the state-of-the-art level. However, because the models are trained in a weakly supervised manner using large-scale noisy data, the predictions may include texts that are not actually spoken in the audio input (i.e. hallucination). We hypothesize that this happens because, given their general knowledge of language, the models combine trying to predict the next word in audio with trying to transcribe the audio itself. Our models perform unevenly across languages, and we observe lower accuracy on low-resource and/or low-discoverability languages or languages where we have less training data. The models also exhibit disparate performance on different accents and dialects of particular languages, which may include higher word error rate across speakers of different genders, races, ages, or other demographic criteria. The sequence-to-sequence architecture of the model makes it prone to generating repetitive texts, which can be mitigated to some degree by beam search and temperature scheduling but not perfectly. Further analysis on these limitations are provided in the paper. It is likely that this behavior and hallucinations may be worse on lower-resource and/or lower-discoverability languages. We anticipate that Whisper models’ transcription capabilities may be used for improving accessibility tools. While Whisper models cannot be used for real-time transcription out of the box – their speed and size suggest that others may be able to build applications on top of them that allow for near-real-time speech recognition and translation. The real value of beneficial applications built on top of Whisper models suggests that the disparate performance of these models may have real economic implications. There are also potential dual use concerns that come with releasing Whisper. While we hope the technology will be used primarily for beneficial purposes, making ASR technology more accessible could enable more actors to build capable surveillance technologies or scale up existing surveillance efforts, as the speed and accuracy allow for affordable automatic transcription and translation of large volumes of audio communication. Moreover, these models may have some capabilities to recognize specific individuals out of the box, which in turn presents safety concerns related both to dual use and disparate performance. In practice, we expect that the cost of transcription is not the limiting factor of scaling up surveillance projects.

instruction: What is the difference in scores between the highest and lowest marks in the history exam?
description: The model is a multilingual conditional generation model based on the MBart architecture, specifically fine-tuned for the task of Table Question Answering (TableQA) in low-resource Indic languages, such as Bengali and Hindi. It is designed to answer questions over structured tabular data, returning either individual cells or entire tables as output. The model supports tasks involving the processing of tabular data in these languages, including the linearization of tables and the generation of answers based on queries. It is trained on large-scale datasets generated through an automatic data generation process tailored for low-resource languages. The model demonstrates strong performance, outperforming state-of-the-art large language models on TableQA tasks in these languages. The model is evaluated on mathematical reasoning capabilities and zero-shot cross-lingual transfer. The data generation method used is scalable and can be applied to any low-resource language with a web presence. The model is part of a research effort published in the 2024 Conference on Empirical Methods in Natural Language Processing. The model is available for use with the Hugging Face Transformers library and requires a tokenizer configured for the Bengali language (bn_IN). The model is intended for use with tabular data that has been linearized into a specific text format, combining the question and the processed table as input. The model generates answers in the same format. The model is trained and evaluated on a custom dataset called BanglaTabQA, which includes questions, input tables, and answers in Bengali. The model is designed for deployment on GPU hardware and uses beam search with a beam size of 5 for inference. The model is part of a broader research initiative focused on addressing the lack of annotated data and neural models for low-resource languages in the domain of TableQA.
domain: Natural Language Processing Table Question Answering

instruction: Can you check if this image contains any content that might be inappropriate for a professional setting?
description: Marqo/nsfw-image-detection-384 is a lightweight image classification model designed to identify NSFW (Not Safe For Work) images. The model is approximately 18–20x smaller than other open-source models and achieves a superior accuracy of 98.56% on its dataset. It uses 384x384 pixel images for input with 16x16 pixel patches. The model was trained on a proprietary dataset of 220,000 images, including 100,000 NSFW examples and 100,000 SFW examples, with a test set containing 10,000 NSFW examples and 10,000 SFW examples. The dataset includes a diverse range of content such as real photos, drawings, Rule 34 material, memes, and AI-generated images. The definition of NSFW can be subjective and contextual, and the dataset was constructed to include challenging examples, though it may not be 100% aligned with every use case. The model outperforms existing NSFW detectors on its dataset. Adjusting the threshold for the NSFW probability can allow for trade-offs between precision, recall, and accuracy, which may be useful in different applications where varying degrees of confidence are required. This model is a fine-tune of another model. Training was conducted with the following parameters: batch size of 256, color jitter of 0.2 with a 0.05 probability, cutmix of 0.1, drop rate of 0.1, drop path rate of 0.05, 20 epochs, Gaussian blur probability of 0.005, horizontal flip probability of 0.5, learning rate of 5.0e-05, mixup of 0.1 with a batch mode and 1.0 probability, momentum of 0.9, 2 classes, AdamW optimizer, pixel remode, 0.5 reprob, cosine schedule, label smoothing of 0.1, 2 warmup epochs, warmup learning rate of 1.0e-05, and warmup prefix disabled. Like all models, this one can make mistakes. NSFW content can be subjective and contextual, and this model is intended to help identify such content. It is recommended to experiment and try different thresholds to determine if the model is suitable for specific needs.

instruction: Translate this message into Somali for a humanitarian aid communication.
description: NLLB-200 is a machine translation model primarily intended for research in machine translation, especially for low-resource languages. It allows for single sentence translation among 200 languages. The model is a distilled 600M variant. The exact training algorithm, data, and strategies to handle data imbalances for high and low resource languages are described in the paper titled 'No Language Left Behind: Scaling Human-Centered Machine Translation' by the NLLB Team, published on Arxiv in 2022. The model is licensed under CC-BY-NC. The primary intended users are researchers and the machine translation research community. NLLB-200 is a research model and is not released for production deployment. It is trained on general domain text data and is not intended to be used with domain-specific texts, such as medical or legal texts. The model is not intended for document translation. It was trained with input lengths not exceeding 512 tokens, and translating longer sequences may result in quality degradation. NLLB-200 translations cannot be used as certified translations. The model was evaluated using BLEU, spBLEU, and chrF++ metrics, and human evaluation was performed using the XSTS protocol. The Flores-200 dataset was used for evaluation, as it provides full coverage of the languages in NLLB-200. The dataset was preprocessed using SentencePiece, and the SentencePiece model is released along with NLLB-200. The model was trained using parallel multilingual data from various sources, with detailed reports on data selection and construction provided in the paper. Monolingual data from Common Crawl was also used. Ethical considerations were taken into account, including the potential for misuse of the model by bad actors, the vulnerability of low-digital-literacy groups to misinformation, and the possibility of residual personally identifiable information in the training data. Mistranslations could have adverse impacts, particularly in health and safety contexts. The model has been tested on the Wikimedia domain with limited investigation on other domains supported in NLLB-MD. Supported languages may have variations that the model does not capture, and users should make appropriate assessments. The carbon dioxide (CO2e) estimate for the model is reported in Section 8.8 of the paper.

instruction: Could you determine the emotional sentiment of this Italian customer testimonial?
description: This model is a fine-tuned version of `distilbert/distilbert-base-multilingual-cased` for multilingual sentiment analysis. It is designed to classify text into one of five sentiment classes: Very Negative, Negative, Neutral, Positive, and Very Positive. The model supports 23 languages, including English, Chinese, Spanish, Hindi, Arabic, Bengali, Portuguese, Russian, Japanese, German, Malay, Telugu, Vietnamese, Korean, French, Turkish, Italian, Polish, Ukrainian, Tagalog, Dutch, Swiss German, and Swahili. It is intended for use in multilingual social media monitoring, international customer feedback analysis, global product review sentiment classification, and worldwide brand sentiment tracking. The model was trained exclusively on synthetic multilingual data generated by advanced LLMs, ensuring wide coverage of sentiment expressions from various languages. The training procedure involved fine-tuning for 3.5 epochs, achieving a train_acc_off_by_one of approximately 0.93 on the validation dataset. The model leverages synthetic data from multiple sources to achieve robust performance across different languages and cultural contexts. While synthetic data reduces bias, validation in real-world scenarios is advised. The model can be used for commercial purposes with a license obtained by contacting info@tabularis.ai.

instruction: Can you locate a part of the text that explains the role of DNA in heredity?
description: ColQwen2.5-3b-multilingual-v1.0 is a multilingual visual retriever based on Qwen2.5-VL-3B-Instruct, utilizing the ColBERT strategy. It is designed to efficiently index documents using visual features and generates multi-vector representations of text and images. The model was trained on 8xH100 80GB GPUs with a per_device_batch_size of 128 for 8 epochs. It supports dynamic image resolutions in input without resizing, with a maximum resolution that creates up to 768 image patches. Training was conducted using the `colpali-engine==0.3.9` version. The model uses low-rank adapters with `alpha=128` and `r=128` on the transformer layers from the language model, as well as the final randomly initialized projection layer. It was trained with a `paged_adamw_8bit` optimizer, a learning rate of 2e-4 with linear decay and 1% warmup steps, and a batch size per device of 128 in `bfloat16` format. The training data includes datasets for German and English from `tsystems/vqa_de_en_batch1`, a multilingual dataset from `llamaindex/vdr-multilingual-train`, synthetic data from `openbmb/VisRAG-Ret-Train-Synthetic-data`, in-domain VQA data from `openbmb/VisRAG-Ret-Train-In-domain-data`, and the Colpali dataset from `vidore/colpali_train_set`. The model primarily focuses on PDF-type documents and high-resource languages, which may limit its generalization to other document types or less represented languages. It relies on multi-vector retrieving derived from the ColBERT late interaction mechanism, which may require engineering efforts to adapt to widely used vector retrieval frameworks that lack native multi-vector support. The vision language backbone model (Qwen2.5-VL) is licensed under the Apache 2.0 license, while the adapters are under the MIT license.

instruction: Could you process this audio and return the text so I can review it later?
description: This model was merged using the TIES merge method. The TIES merge method was applied with a ties_density parameter of 0.9. The encoder weights were set to 0.8 and 0.2, and the decoder weights were set to 0.2 and 0.8. The model was created by merging two models: model_a located at '/mnt/cloud/llm/whisper/whisper-large-v3-russian' and model_b located at '/mnt/cloud/llm/whisper/whisper-large-v3-ru-podlodka'. The output directory for the merged model is '/mnt/cloud/llm/whisper/whisper-large-v3-russian-ties-podlodka'. The model is intended for use in automatic speech recognition (ASR) tasks, particularly for processing phone calls. It is recommended to preprocess audio recordings by normalizing volume and adjusting the sample rate before performing ASR. An example preprocessing command is provided using the sox tool. The model can be loaded using the Transformers library, with support for different device types such as CPU, CUDA, or MPS. The model is currently in a work in progress (WIP) state, with the goal of further fine-tuning it for speech recognition of phone calls. Contributions, especially in the form of relevant datasets, are welcomed and appreciated.

instruction: How do I interpret the output of a segmentation model trained on the Cityscapes dataset?
