description: This is a Keras model implementing the MixTransformer architecture, intended to be used as a backbone for the SegFormer architecture. The model is supported in both KerasCV and KerasHub, though KerasCV will no longer be actively developed, and users are encouraged to use KerasHub instead. The model includes several preset checkpoints provided by the Keras team, with weights ported from external sources. These checkpoints include mit_b0_ade20k_512 with 3.32M parameters, mit_b1_ade20k_512 with 13.16M parameters, mit_b2_ade20k_512 with 24.20M parameters, mit_b3_ade20k_512 with 44.08M parameters, mit_b4_ade20k_512 with 60.85M parameters, mit_b5_ade20k_640 with 81.45M parameters, mit_b0_cityscapes_1024 with 3.32M parameters, mit_b1_cityscapes_1024 with 13.16M parameters, mit_b2_cityscapes_1024 with 24.20M parameters, mit_b3_cityscapes_1024 with 44.08M parameters, mit_b4_cityscapes_1024 with 60.85M parameters, and mit_b5_cityscapes_1024 with 81.45M parameters. Each model is trained on either the ADE20K or Cityscapes dataset, with input resolutions of 512x512 or 1024x1024 pixels, respectively. The model supports tasks such as image segmentation and can be used with a backbone configuration. Example usage includes importing the model, preparing input data, and performing evaluation or training with a specified optimizer, loss function, and metrics.

instruction: Can you classify this Uzbek text as positive or negative: 'Bu ish juda yomon bajarilgan'?
description: The LLaMA-3.1-8B-Instruct-Uz model has been instruction-tuned using a mix of publicly available and synthetically constructed Uzbek and English data to preserve its original knowledge while enhancing its capabilities. This model is designed to support various natural language processing tasks in Uzbek, such as machine translation, summarization, and dialogue systems, ensuring robust performance across these applications. The model can be used with transformers and with the original `llama` codebase. For evaluation, the model was tested on translation tasks using FLORES+ Uz-En / En-Uz datasets, where the dev and test sets were merged to create a larger evaluation dataset for each subset. A specific prompt was used for one-shot Uz-En evaluation, with the positions of the words 'English' and 'Uzbek' changed for En-Uz evaluation. For Uzbek sentiment analysis, the model was evaluated using the **risqaliyevds/uzbek-sentiment-analysis** dataset, with binary labels (0: Negative, 1: Positive) created using the GPT-4o API. A specific prompt was used to determine the sentiment as either 'Positive' or 'Negative'. For Uzbek News Classification, the model was evaluated using the **risqaliyevds/uzbek-zero-shot-classification** dataset, with a prompt asking the model to predict the category of the news article from a list of ten categories. On MMLU, the model was evaluated using a 5-shot template, with the first token generated by the model used to measure accuracy. The model is a quantized version created using llama.cpp.

instruction: In what format is the training data stored?
description: This model is a fine-tuned version of sberbank-ai/ruBert-large designed for the task of document question answering. It has been adapted specifically for extracting answers from a provided document context and fine-tuned on a custom JSON dataset containing context, question, and answer triples. The model was fine-tuned using the Transformers library with a custom training pipeline. A loader reads a JSON file containing context, question, and answer triples. The script tokenizes the document and question with a sliding window approach to handle long texts. The training process leverages mixed precision training and the AdamW optimizer to improve optimization. The training script evaluates model performance on a validation set, saves checkpoints, and employs early stopping based on validation loss. The model was trained for 20 epochs with a batch size of 4 per device, using 0.1 of total steps for warmup. FP16 training was enabled if CUDA was available. Training was performed on an 1xRTX 3070. The input is formed by concatenating the question and the document context, guiding the model to focus on the relevant segments. This model is ideal for interactive document question answering tasks, making it a powerful tool for applications such as customer support, document search, and automated Q&A systems. While primarily focused on Russian texts, the model also supports English language inputs. The model also supports English language, but its support was not tested.

instruction: Make an image of a beach at sunset using a fast and memory-efficient process.
description: ARPG is a novel autoregressive image generation framework that combines BERT-style masked modeling with a GPT-style causal architecture. It is designed to generate images through an autoregressive process and supports tasks such as image generation with customizable parameters. The model can be loaded using the Hugging Face DiffusionPipeline, allowing users to specify the model type, number of steps, and class labels. ARPG is available in different sizes, including ARPG-L, ARPG-XL, and ARPG-XXL. It achieves a FID score of 1.94, indicating high-quality image generation. The model is noted for its fast speed, low memory usage, and ability to perform zero-shot inference. It also supports randomized order generation. Users can set a seed for reproducibility, adjust the classifier-free guidance scale, and choose between different guidance and sampling schedules. The model is evaluated on ImageNet class labels and can generate images based on provided class labels. The framework is based on an open-source codebase and was inspired by prior works.

instruction: Make a high-quality image of a mountain range using 50 steps for the generation process.
description: ARPG is a novel autoregressive image generation framework that combines BERT-style masked modeling with a GPT-style causal architecture. It is designed to generate images through an autoregressive process and supports tasks such as image generation with customizable parameters. The model can be loaded using the Hugging Face DiffusionPipeline, allowing users to specify the model type, number of steps, and class labels. ARPG is available in different sizes, including ARPG-L, ARPG-XL, and ARPG-XXL. It achieves a FID score of 1.94, indicating high-quality image generation. The model is noted for its fast speed, low memory usage, and ability to perform zero-shot inference. It also supports randomized order generation. Users can set a seed for reproducibility, adjust the classifier-free guidance scale, and choose between different guidance and sampling schedules. The model is evaluated on ImageNet class labels and can generate images based on provided class labels. The framework is based on an open-source codebase and was inspired by prior works.

instruction: I want to retrieve a part of a document that talks about economic growth in 2023.
description: ColSmolVLM-Instruct-500M is a visual retriever model based on SmolVLM-Instruct-500M, incorporating the ColBERT strategy for document retrieval using visual features. It is designed to efficiently index documents by generating multi-vector representations of text and images. The model was trained with a batch size of 32 for 3 epochs using the commit b983e40 of the Colpali repository. The training dataset consists of 127,460 query-page pairs, with 63% from openly available academic datasets and 37% from a synthetic dataset of web-crawled PDF documents augmented with VLM-generated pseudo-questions. The dataset is fully English, allowing for the study of zero-shot generalization to non-English languages. A validation set of 2% of the samples is used for hyperparameter tuning. Training is conducted in bfloat16 format, using low-rank adapters with alpha=32 and r=32 on the transformer layers of the language model and the final projection layer. The paged_adamw_8bit optimizer is used with a learning rate of 5e-4, linear decay, 2.5% warmup steps, and a batch size of 8 across a 4 GPU setup with data parallelism. The model supports document retrieval tasks and relies on multi-vector retrieval derived from the ColBERT late interaction mechanism. It primarily focuses on PDF-type documents and high-resource languages, which may limit its generalization to other document types or less represented languages. The model requires specific versions of the `colpali-engine` and `transformers` libraries for use. The vision language backbone model is licensed under Apache 2.0, while the adapters are under the MIT license.

instruction: I want a voice in Nepali for a motivational speech—can it be passionate and inspiring?
description: Indic Parler-TTS is a multilingual Indic extension of a text-to-speech model. It is a fine-tuned version trained on a 1,806-hour multilingual Indic and English dataset. Indic Parler-TTS Mini can officially speak in 20 Indic languages and in English. The 21 languages supported are Assamese, Bengali, Bodo, Dogri, English, Gujarati, Hindi, Kannada, Konkani, Maithili, Malayalam, Manipuri, Marathi, Nepali, Odia, Sanskrit, Santali, Sindhi, Tamil, Telugu, and Urdu. Thanks to its better prompt tokenizer, it can easily be extended to other languages. This tokenizer has a larger vocabulary and handles byte fallback, which simplifies multilingual training. The model is the result of a collaboration between the HuggingFace audio team and another team. The model accepts two primary inputs: a transcript, which is the text to be converted to speech, and a caption, which is a detailed description of how the speech should sound. The model officially supports 21 languages, with unofficial support for Chhattisgarhi, Kashmiri, and Punjabi. It provides 69 unique voices across the supported languages, with each language having a set of recommended voices optimized for naturalness and intelligibility. Ten languages officially support emotion-specific prompts: Assamese, Bengali, Bodo, Dogri, Kannada, Malayalam, Marathi, Sanskrit, Nepali, and Tamil. Emotion support for other languages exists but has not been extensively tested. Available emotions include Command, Anger, Narration, Conversation, Disgust, Fear, Happy, Neutral, Proper Noun, News, Sad, and Surprise. The model officially supports Indian English accents through its English voices, providing clear and natural speech. For other accents, the model allows customization by specifying accent details, such as 'A male British speaker' or 'A female American speaker,' using style transfer for more dynamic and personalized outputs. Indic Parler-TTS offers precise control over various speech characteristics using the caption input, including background noise, reverberation, expressivity, pitch, speaking rate, and voice quality. The model automatically adapts to the language it detects in the prompt. To ensure speaker consistency across generations, the model was trained on pre-determined speakers, characterized by name. The model includes 69 speakers across 18 officially supported languages, with each language having a set of recommended voices for optimal performance. Indic Parler-TTS has been evaluated using a MOS-like framework by native and non-native speakers. The results highlight its exceptional performance in generating natural and intelligible speech, especially for native speakers of Indian languages. Key strengths include exceptional performance for native speakers, with top scores for Maithili (95.36), Sanskrit (99.79), and Bodo (94.47), competitive results for lesser-resourced and unofficially supported languages like Kashmiri and Sindhi, and adaptability to non-native and anonymous speaker scenarios with consistently high clarity. The model was fine-tuned on a subset of the dataset used to train the pre-trained version: the Indic-Parler Dataset, a large-scale multilingual speech corpus designed to train the Indic Parler-TTS model. The dataset supports 16 official languages of India, along with English and Chhattisgarhi, making it comprehensive for regional language technologies. These languages include Assamese, Bengali, Bodo, Chhattisgarhi, Dogri, English, Gujarati, Hindi, Kannada, Malayalam, Manipuri, Marathi, Nepali, Odia, Punjabi, Sanskrit, Tamil, and Telugu. The model is permissively licensed under the Apache 2.0 license.

instruction: Can you match the description of this scene to the correct visual content?
description: ViT-L-16-SigLIP2-512 is a SigLIP 2 Vision-Language model trained on the WebLI dataset. It is a contrastive image-text model capable of performing zero-shot image classification. The model has been converted for use in OpenCLIP from the original JAX checkpoints. It is based on the architecture described in the paper 'SigLIP 2: Multilingual Vision-Language Encoders with Improved Semantic Understanding, Localization, and Dense Features' and utilizes the sigmoid loss method for language image pre-training as described in the paper 'Sigmoid loss for language image pre-training'. The model supports encoding both images and text, and computing similarity scores between them for classification tasks. It is designed to work with multilingual data and provides improved semantic understanding, localization, and dense feature extraction capabilities.

instruction: From the table, which state had the highest literacy rate in 2021?
description: TableQA is the task of answering questions over tables of structured information, returning individual cells or tables as output. TableQA research has focused primarily on high-resource languages, leaving medium- and low-resource languages with little progress due to scarcity of annotated data and neural models. This work addresses this gap by introducing a fully automatic large-scale tableQA data generation process for low-resource languages with limited budget. The data generation method is applied to two Indic languages, Bengali and Hindi, which have no tableQA datasets or models. TableQA models trained on the large-scale datasets outperform state-of-the-art LLMs. The trained models are further studied on different aspects, including mathematical reasoning capabilities and zero-shot cross-lingual transfer. This work is the first on low-resource tableQA focusing on scalable data generation and evaluation procedures. The proposed data generation method can be applied to any low-resource language with a web presence. Datasets, models, and code are released.
domain: Natural Language Processing Table Question Answering

instruction: What is the percentage of urban population in the state of Rajasthan according to the table?
description: The model is a M2M100ForConditionalGeneration model fine-tuned for the task of table question answering (TableQA) in low-resource Indic languages, specifically Bengali and Hindi. It is designed to answer questions over structured tabular data, returning either individual cells or entire tables as output. The model supports tasks involving the processing of tabular data in these languages, where annotated data and neural models are scarce. The model's architecture is based on the M2M100 framework, which is a multilingual machine translation model. The model was trained on large-scale datasets generated through a fully automatic data generation process, which was developed to address the lack of annotated data for low-resource languages. Evaluation results show that models trained on these datasets outperform state-of-the-art large language models. The model is intended for use in environments where structured data needs to be queried in low-resource language settings. However, the model's performance may be limited by the quality and coverage of the automatically generated training data, and it is not guaranteed to perform well on tasks outside the scope of the training data. The model is part of a broader research effort focused on scalable data generation and evaluation procedures for low-resource TableQA, and it is released alongside datasets and code for further research and application.
domain: Natural Language Processing Table Question Answering

instruction: How do I compare the meaning of these two short articles about technology?
description: The all-roberta-large-v1 model is a sentence and short paragraph encoder that maps input text to a 1024-dimensional dense vector space. It is designed to capture semantic information and can be used for tasks such as information retrieval, clustering, and sentence similarity. The model is based on a pretrained Roberta architecture and was fine-tuned using a contrastive learning objective on a dataset containing over 1 billion sentence pairs. During training, the model was optimized to predict which sentences were paired in the dataset by computing cosine similarity between sentence pairs and applying cross-entropy loss. The training was conducted over 400,000 steps with a batch size of 256, a learning rate of 2e-5, and a sequence length limit of 128 tokens. The model was developed using efficient hardware infrastructure, including 7 TPUs v3-8, and benefited from support from Google's Flax, JAX, and Cloud teams. Input text longer than 128 word pieces is truncated by default. The model can be used with the Sentence-Transformers library or directly with HuggingFace Transformers, where token embeddings are pooled and normalized to produce final sentence embeddings.

instruction: Which of these product comparisons is the most helpful for someone deciding between two smartphones?
description: This is a model finetuned from BERT-tiny using the Sentence Transformers library. It computes scores for pairs of texts, which can be used for semantic textual similarity, semantic search, paraphrase mining, text classification, clustering, and more. The model is a Cross Encoder with a maximum sequence length of 512 tokens and produces one output label. It is designed for the English language and is licensed under the Apache 2.0 license. The model was trained on a dataset of 578,402 samples containing questions, answers, and labels. Training was conducted using a batch size of 2048, a learning rate of 0.0005, and one training epoch with a warmup ratio of 0.1. The model was trained on an NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3090 GPU and a 13th Gen Intel Core i7-13700K CPU with 31.78 GB of RAM. The training consumed 0.019 kWh of energy and emitted 0.007 kg of CO2. The model was evaluated on several datasets, including gooaq-dev, NanoMSMARCO, NanoNFCorpus, NanoNQ, and NanoBEIR_R100_mean, achieving an ndcg@10 score of 0.6157 on gooaq-dev, 0.4772 on NanoMSMARCO, 0.3308 on NanoNFCorpus, 0.4859 on NanoNQ, and 0.4313 on NanoBEIR_R100_mean. The model can be used with the Sentence Transformers library by loading it as a CrossEncoder and running inference on pairs of texts or ranking different texts based on similarity to a single text.

instruction: Could you generate a feature embedding for this image suitable for robotic vision tasks?
description: This model is a PyTorch port of the original JAX implementation of ResNet-10, which is a 4-stage ResNet with basic blocks. The conversion maintains the original model's architecture and weights while making it accessible to PyTorch users. The original model is from the HIL-SERL paper and is useful for robotics tasks due to its small size. The model has approximately 4.9 million parameters, with hidden sizes of [64, 128, 256, 512]. It accepts 3-channel RGB images with a resolution of 128x128 as input and produces an embedding size of 64. The conversion was performed using an automated JAX to PyTorch pipeline, ensuring weight preservation, architecture matching, and numerical stability.

instruction: What emotional tone is conveyed in this part of the CEO's speech during the conference call?
description: FinVoc2Vec is a vocal tone classifier designed for real-world corporate disclosures. The model is trained in two stages: first, a self-supervised pre-training procedure that adapts the base model to the acoustic characteristics of disclosure environments using 500,000 unlabeled sentences of conference call speech. Second, a supervised fine-tuning procedure that enables the model to learn representations of human-labeled vocal tone. The training data consists of 5,000 audio recordings of linguistically neutral sentences from conference calls, each manually labeled with perceived vocal tone — positive, negative, or neutral. The labeled audio data cannot be made publicly available due to third-party property rights, but interested researchers with an active license may contact the authors directly to explore potential access options. The model is based on a derivative of Wav2Vec2 and is licensed under the Creative Commons Attribution Non Commercial 4.0 licence (CC-BY-NC-4.0).

instruction: Generate a regular expression that matches valid email addresses according to standard formats.
description: Qwen2.5-Coder is a code-specific large language model, part of the Qwen series, previously known as CodeQwen. It is available in six different model sizes, ranging from 0.5 to 32 billion parameters, to accommodate the needs of various developers. Qwen2.5-Coder introduces significant improvements in code generation, code reasoning, and code fixing. It was trained on 5.5 trillion tokens, including source code, text-code grounding, and synthetic data. The Qwen2.5-Coder-32B model is currently the state-of-the-art open-source code language model, with coding abilities comparable to GPT-4o. The model supports real-world applications such as Code Agents, maintaining strong performance in mathematics and general competencies. It provides long-context support up to 128K tokens. The instruction-tuned 7B Qwen2.5-Coder model is a causal language model with 7.61B total parameters and 6.53B non-embedding parameters. It has 28 layers and uses a transformer architecture with RoPE, SwiGLU, RMSNorm, and Attention QKV bias. The model has 28 attention heads for queries and 4 for key-value pairs. It supports a full context length of 131,072 tokens. For long texts beyond 32,768 tokens, the model uses YaRN, a technique for enhancing model length extrapolation. YaRN can be enabled by adding a specific configuration to the model's config.json file. However, YaRN is currently only supported with static scaling in vLLM, which may affect performance on shorter texts. The model is compatible with the latest Hugging Face transformers library, and using versions below 4.37.0 may result in errors. The model can be loaded and used with the AutoModelForCausalLM and AutoTokenizer classes from the transformers library. Evaluation results and performance details are available in the provided references. The model is described in the Qwen2.5-Coder Technical Report and the Qwen2 Technical Report, both available on arXiv.

instruction: What items are visible in this photo that I can identify?
description: LLMDet (tiny variant) was proposed by Shenghao Fu, Qize Yang, Qijie Mo, Junkai Yan, Xihan Wei, Jingke Meng, Xiaohua Xie, and Wei-Shi Zheng. LLMDet improves upon previous models by co-training with a large language model. The model can be used for zero-shot object detection. The checkpoints provided are for inference only and do not include the large language model used during training. Inference is performed in the same manner as other similar models. The model was trained on a combination of datasets including O365, GoldG, GRIT, V3Det, O365V2, OpenImageV6, and GroundingCap-1M. Evaluation results on LVIS show the following performance metrics: for one variant, MiniVal APr is 44.7, MiniVal APc is 37.3, MiniVal APf is 39.5, MiniVal AP is 50.7, Val1.0 APr is 34.9, Val1.0 APc is 26.0, Val1.0 APf is 30.1, and Val1.0 AP is 44.3. For another variant, MiniVal APr is 48.3, MiniVal APc is 40.8, MiniVal APf is 43.1, MiniVal AP is 54.3, Val1.0 APr is 38.5, Val1.0 APc is 28.2, Val1.0 APf is 34.3, and Val1.0 AP is 47.8. For a third variant, MiniVal APr is 51.1, MiniVal APc is 45.1, MiniVal APf is 46.1, MiniVal AP is 56.6, Val1.0 APr is 42.0, Val1.0 APc is 31.6, Val1.0 APf is 38.8, and Val1.0 AP is 50.2.

instruction: Does this recording sound more guttural or enunciated?
description: This model implements voice (sounding) quality classification based on the Vox-Profile framework, which is designed to characterize diverse speaker and speech traits. It is specifically trained to classify speech samples into a set of predefined voice quality attributes. The model evaluates voice characteristics across five categories: pitch, texture, volume, clarity, and rhythm. The classification labels include 'shrill', 'nasal', 'deep', 'silky', 'husky', 'raspy', 'guttural', 'vocal-fry', 'booming', 'authoritative', 'loud', 'hushed', 'soft', 'crisp', 'slurred', 'lisp', 'stammering', 'singsong', 'pitchy', 'flowing', 'monotone', 'staccato', 'punctuated', 'enunciated', and 'hesitant'. The model is based on the Whisper Large v3 architecture and is fine-tuned for voice quality classification. Performance is evaluated using speaker-level Macro-F1 scores, calculated by averaging the Macro-F1 scores across speakers. The speaker-level score is derived from five randomly sampled utterances per speaker, with the stratification process repeated 20 times. The final performance metric is the unweighted average of speaker-level Macro-F1 scores between the VoxCeleb and Expresso datasets. EARS is excluded from ParaSpeechCaps due to the limited number of samples in the holdout set. The model is intended for use with audio inputs that are no shorter than 3 seconds and no longer than 15 seconds, sampled at 16kHz and in mono channel format. Predictions are made using a sigmoid function on the model's output logits, with a threshold of 0.7 used to determine the presence of each voice quality label. The model is not intended for clinical or diagnostic applications, surveillance, privacy-invasive applications, or commercial use.

instruction: Please convert the following text from English to Japanese accurately.
description: PLaMo Translation Model is a specialized large-scale language model developed by Preferred Networks for translation tasks. The model includes a post-trained model for translation, a base model for translation, and a pair-wise evaluation model. It is released under the PLaMo community license, and users are required to check and agree to the license before downloading. This model has not been instruction-tuned for chat dialog or other downstream tasks. Commercial users must check the PLaMo community license and contact the organization via the provided form to use the model for commercial purposes. The model supports translation between English and Japanese, and can be used with a specific prompt format to generate translations or evaluate translation outputs. The model may produce inaccurate, biased, or objectionable responses in some cases, and developers are advised to perform safety testing and tuning tailored to their specific applications. The model was developed under the project 'Research and Development Project of the Enhanced Infrastructures for Post 5G Information and Communication System' (JPNP 20017), subsidized by the New Energy and Industrial Technology Development Organization (NEDO).

instruction: What is the maximum sequence length the model can process during inference?
description: This model is a TAPAS mini model fine-tuned on Sequential Question Answering (SQA). It has two versions: the default version corresponds to the `tapas_sqa_inter_masklm_mini_reset` checkpoint, and the other version is `no_reset`, corresponding to `tapas_sqa_inter_masklm_mini`. The model was pre-trained on masked language modeling (MLM) and an additional step called intermediate pre-training, followed by fine-tuning. It uses relative position embeddings, resetting the position index at every cell of the table. The non-default version uses absolute position embeddings. TAPAS is a BERT-like transformer model pre-trained on a large corpus of English data from Wikipedia in a self-supervised manner. It was pre-trained with two objectives: masked language modeling, where 15% of the words in a flattened table and associated context are randomly masked and the model must predict them, and intermediate pre-training, where the model is trained to classify whether a sentence is supported or refuted by the contents of a table using a balanced dataset of millions of syntactically created examples. This allows the model to learn a bidirectional representation of tables and associated texts, which can be used for downstream tasks such as answering questions about a table or determining if a sentence is entailed or refuted by the table. Fine-tuning involves adding a cell selection head on top of the pre-trained model and jointly training this head with the base model on SQA. The model can be used for answering questions related to a table in a conversational setup. The texts are lowercased and tokenized using WordPiece with a vocabulary size of 30,000. The model inputs are of the form: [CLS] Question [SEP] Flattened table [SEP]. The model was fine-tuned on 32 Cloud TPU v3 cores for 200,000 steps with a maximum sequence length of 512 and a batch size of 128. Fine-tuning takes approximately 20 hours. The optimizer used is Adam with a learning rate of 1.25e-5 and a warmup ratio of 0.2. An inductive bias is added so that the model only selects cells of the same column, as reflected by the `select_one_column` parameter of `TapasConfig`. The model's performance on the SQA development set for the mini size is 0.4574 for the noreset version and 0.5148 for the reset version. The team releasing TAPAS did not write a model card for this model; this model card was written by the Hugging Face team and contributors.
domain: Natural Language Processing Table Question Answering

instruction: Would you generate an audio version of this lesson in a clear and professional tone?
description: Kokoro is an open-weight text-to-speech (TTS) model with 82 million parameters. Despite its lightweight architecture, it delivers comparable quality to larger models while being significantly faster and more cost-efficient. With Apache-licensed weights, Kokoro can be deployed anywhere from production environments to personal projects. As of April 2025, the market rate of Kokoro served over API is under $1 per million characters of text input, or under $0.06 per hour of audio output. On average, 1000 characters of input is about 1 minute of output. Kokoro has been deployed in numerous projects and commercial APIs. Fake websites like kokorottsai_com and kokorotts_net are likely scams masquerading under the banner of a popular model. Any website containing 'kokoro' in its root domain is not owned by and not affiliated with this model page or its author, and attempts to imply otherwise are red flags. Kokoro v1.0 was published on 2025 Jan 27 and is based on a few hundred hours of training data. The model uses a StyleTTS 2 and ISTFTNet architecture, and it is decoder only, with no diffusion or encoder release. Kokoro was architected by Li et al and trained by @rzvzn on Discord. It supports multiple languages. Kokoro was trained exclusively on permissive/non-copyrighted audio data and IPA phoneme labels. Examples of permissive/non-copyrighted audio include public domain audio, audio licensed under Apache, MIT, etc., and synthetic audio generated by closed TTS models from large providers. No synthetic audio from open TTS models or 'custom voice clones' was used. The total dataset size is a few hundred hours of audio, and the total training cost was about $1000 for 1000 hours of A100 80GB vRAM. The following Creative Commons Attribution (CC BY) audio was part of the dataset used to train Kokoro v1.0.

instruction: I'm doing a project on AI ethics—can you test if this image is a deepfake?
description: The Deep-Fake-Detector-v2-Model is a deep learning model designed to detect deepfake images. It is based on the Vision Transformer (ViT) architecture, specifically the `google/vit-base-patch16-224-in21k` model, which has been fine-tuned on a dataset of real and deepfake images. The model classifies images into two categories: 'Realism' or 'Deepfake'. It achieves a precision of 0.9683 for 'Realism' and 0.8826 for 'Deepfake', with a recall of 0.8708 for 'Realism' and 0.9715 for 'Deepfake'. The F1 scores are 0.9170 for 'Realism' and 0.9249 for 'Deepfake', with an overall accuracy of 0.9212 on a test set of 56,001 images. The model processes RGB images resized to 224x224 pixels and uses a binary classification head for output. The Vision Transformer architecture divides the input image into 16x16 pixel patches, which are embedded and processed by a transformer encoder using multi-head self-attention mechanisms. The model was trained using the AdamW optimizer with a learning rate of 1e-6, a batch size of 32 for training and 8 for evaluation, and for two epochs. Data augmentation techniques included random rotation (±90 degrees), random sharpness adjustment, and random resizing and cropping. The model was fine-tuned using Hugging Face's `Trainer` API and evaluated using accuracy, F1 score, and a confusion matrix. The training dataset consists of real images of human faces and deepfake images generated using advanced AI techniques. The model may not generalize well to other deepfake datasets or domains, and its performance may degrade on low-resolution or heavily compressed images. It is designed for image classification and does not detect deepfake videos directly. The model should not be used for malicious purposes, such as creating or spreading deepfakes. It may inherit biases from the training dataset, and users should be informed when deepfake detection tools are used to analyze their content. Future work includes extending the model to detect deepfake videos, improving generalization by training on larger and more diverse datasets, and incorporating explainability techniques to provide insights into model predictions.

instruction: How strong is the interaction between the E protein and a specific antibody in this dataset?
description: The BindPred model is a Gradient Boosted Trees (GBT) regressor trained on ESM2 embeddings from Meta’s ESM2 protein language model. It is designed for binding affinity predictive tasks. The model uses ESM2 as its base model and employs a CatBoostRegressor architecture within the CatBoost framework for regression tasks. The model is trained using CatBoost Gradient Boosting on ESM2 embeddings, which are extracted from a 33-layer transformer with 650 million parameters. Evaluation of the model is performed using RMSE and R^2 metrics. The model is limited by the quality of the ESM2 embeddings and its performance is dependent on the training dataset used. It is not a deep-learning model but instead uses GBTs to provide fast and interpretable predictions. Two pretrained models are available: one for predicting binding affinity between ACE2 (human and animals) and RBD proteins, and a general-purpose model trained on ESM2 embeddings.

instruction: What is the status distribution of all change requests submitted this month?
description: This model is a custom fine-tuned version of the TAPAS architecture, designed for question answering over tabular data, specifically for ServiceNow ITSM tables such as incident, change, and problem. The model is intended to answer natural language queries about these tables, such as identifying the most common issues or counting records in specific categories. The training data was based on synthetic datasets generated from a subset of fields in ServiceNow ITSM tables, including problem ID, priority, status, root cause, and resolved at. The data was structured in a synthetic question-answering (SQA) format, with each entry containing an ID, question, table file, answer coordinates, the actual answer, and a float answer for numeric values. The training process used full fine-tuning with specific hyperparameters, including one training epoch, a batch size of 32 for training and 64 for evaluation, a learning rate of 0.00001, and a random seed of 42. The model was evaluated on three benchmarks: WikiTableQuestions (WTQ), TabFact, and SQA. On the synthetic test set, the model's accuracy improved from 0.2933 to 0.4667 after fine-tuning. On the WTQ validation set and TabFact, the model maintained pre-training accuracy of 0.3405 and 0.5005, respectively. On the SQA dataset, the model showed a slight improvement. The model requires both a natural language question and a structured table in dataframe format to function. It outputs an aggregation operation, the predicted answer, and the cells used to derive the answer. Limitations include suboptimal accuracy, potential issues with larger table sizes, and the need for preprocessing to convert input data into the required format. The model is not guaranteed to achieve 100% accuracy, and increasing the number of training samples or using a larger model may improve performance.
domain: Natural Language Processing Table Question Answering

instruction: Can you detect and label all the visible animals in this image?
description: MM Grounding DINO (tiny variant) is a model proposed by Xiangyu Zhao, Yicheng Chen, Shilin Xu, Xiangtai Li, Xinjiang Wang, Yining Li, and Haian Huang. It improves upon previous models by enhancing the contrastive class head and removing parameter sharing in the decoder, which leads to improved zero-shot detection performance. On the COCO dataset, it achieves 50.6 AP, an improvement of 2.2 points. On the LVIS dataset, it achieves 31.9 val AP and 41.4 minival AP, improvements of 11.8 and 12.6 points, respectively. The model is designed for zero-shot object detection and can be used with the Transformers library for inference. It was trained on a combination of datasets including O365, GoldG, GRIT, and V3Det. Evaluation results show that the model performs well across different backbones and pre-training data combinations. The model is available in the Hugging Face model repository under the collection rziga/mm_grounding_dino_tiny_o365v1_goldg_grit.

instruction: Can you help me understand the scene in this image of a child playing with a dog?
description: BLIP-2 is a multimodal model consisting of three components: a CLIP-like image encoder, a Querying Transformer (Q-Former), and a large language model. The image encoder and large language model are initialized from pre-trained checkpoints and remain frozen during training, while the Q-Former, a BERT-like Transformer encoder, is trained to map a set of 'query tokens' to query embeddings that bridge the embedding spaces of the image encoder and the language model. The model is designed to predict the next text token given the query embeddings and the previous text. It supports tasks such as image captioning, visual question answering (VQA), and chat-like conversations by providing the image and previous conversation as input. BLIP-2 uses the Flan-T5-xxl model as its pre-trained language model, which is not fine-tuned during training. The model inherits the risks and limitations of the Flan-T5 model, including potential for harmful language generation. It is fine-tuned on image-text datasets collected from the internet, which may introduce biases or inappropriate content. BLIP-2 has not been tested in real-world applications and should not be directly deployed without a careful assessment of safety and fairness in the specific deployment context. This release is intended for research purposes only and is not evaluated for all downstream applications. Users are advised to evaluate and address potential concerns related to accuracy, safety, and fairness before deployment. The model can be used for conditional text generation given an image and optional text input.

instruction: Generate an image of a fantasy warrior with silver hair, wearing a dragon-themed armor, standing on a cliff overlooking a stormy sea.
description: Animagine XL 4.0, also stylized as Anim4gine, is an anime-themed fine-tuned SDXL model and the latest version in the series. It was retrained from scratch using a dataset of 8.4 million diverse anime-style images, with a knowledge cutoff of January 7th, 2025, and fine-tuned for approximately 2650 GPU hours. The model was trained using the tag ordering method for identity and style training. The optimized version, Animagine XL 4.0 Opt, was further refined with an additional dataset, improving stability, anatomy accuracy, noise reduction, color saturation, and overall color accuracy. These enhancements make Animagine XL 4.0 Opt more consistent and visually appealing while maintaining the signature quality of the series. The model is a diffusion-based text-to-image generative model designed to generate and modify anime-themed images based on text prompts. It is fine-tuned from an unspecified base model. The model supports a variety of tasks, including generating anime-style images with specific character details, styles, and ratings. It is intended for use with the diffusers library and can be integrated into applications using the StableDiffusionXLPipeline. The model was trained using state-of-the-art hardware, including 7 x H100 80GB SXM5 GPUs, with a batch size of 32 and gradient accumulation steps of 2. Training was conducted at a resolution of 1024x1024 using the Adafactor optimizer and mixed precision (fp16). The model supports special tags for quality, score, temporal, and rating control, which influence the artistic style, content safety, and overall image quality. The model has limitations, including a reliance on tag-based prompts, potential difficulties with complex anatomy, lack of text rendering support, and challenges with new or multiple characters. Higher resolutions may show degradation due to training on the original SDXL resolution. The model is licensed under the original SDXL license from Stability AI, which permits commercial use, modifications, and distributions, but prohibits illegal activities, harmful content generation, discrimination, and exploitation. The license requires including a copy of the license, stating any changes, and preserving notices. The model is provided 'AS IS' without warranties.

instruction: Could you estimate the depth of each object in this picture of a city street?
description: This is a model card for the `marigold-depth-v1-1` model for monocular depth estimation from a single image. The model is fine-tuned from the `stable-diffusion-2` as described in the papers titled 'Repurposing Diffusion-Based Image Generators for Monocular Depth Estimation' and 'Marigold: Affordable Adaptation of Diffusion-Based Image Generators for Image Analysis'. The model can be used to generate an estimated depth map of an input image. Even though any resolution can be processed, the model inherits the base diffusion model's effective resolution of roughly 768 pixels. This means that for optimal predictions, any larger input image should be resized to make the longer side 768 pixels before feeding it into the model. This model was designed for usage with the DDIM scheduler and between 1 and 50 denoising steps. The predicted values are between 0 and 1, interpolating between the near and far planes of the model's choice. An uncertainty map is produced only when multiple predictions are ensembled with ensemble size larger than 2.

instruction: Could you analyze this audio file and tell me if the style and content suggest AI involvement?
description: SONICS is a model designed to distinguish between human-composed and synthetic songs, addressing the challenges posed by AI-generated music. It introduces a novel dataset for end-to-end Synthetic Song Detection (SSD), comprising over 97,000 songs (4,751 hours) with over 49,000 synthetic songs from platforms like Suno and Udio. The model emphasizes the importance of modeling long-range temporal dependencies in songs for effective authenticity detection, a feature not addressed in existing methods. To achieve this, the model introduces SpecTTTra, a novel architecture that improves time and memory efficiency over conventional CNN and Transformer-based models. For long songs, the top-performing variant of the model outperforms ViT by 8% in F1 score, is 38% faster, and uses 26% less memory, while also surpassing ConvNeXt with a 1% F1 score gain, 20% speed boost, and 67% memory reduction. The model's architecture includes an embedding dimension of 384, 6 heads, 12 layers, and an MLP ratio of 2.67. Audio processing involves a sample rate of 16kHz, an FFT size of 2048, a hop length of 512, 128 Mel bands, a frequency range of 20Hz to 8kHz, and mean-std normalization. The model is intended for use in identifying counterfeit songs where all components (vocals, music, lyrics, and style) could be AI-generated. It is important to note that existing datasets lack music-lyrics diversity, long-duration songs, and open-access fake songs, which the model aims to address.

instruction: Is it possible to get a text version of this Japanese audio file?
description: This is a fine-tuned XLSR-53 large model for speech recognition in Japanese. It was fine-tuned using the train and validation splits of unspecified datasets. When using this model, the speech input must be sampled at 16kHz. The model was fine-tuned with the support of GPU credits provided by an unspecified source. The model is designed to transcribe speech into text and can be used directly without a language model. It is based on the Wav2Vec2 architecture and is intended for use with Japanese speech data. Evaluation results on the Japanese test data of Common Voice show a Word Error Rate (WER) of 81.80% and a Character Error Rate (CER) of 20.16%. These results were obtained using a specific evaluation script. The model can be used with the Huggingsound library or with a custom inference script that includes preprocessing and decoding steps. The model's performance may be affected by the specific evaluation setup used. Users are advised to ensure that their speech input is properly sampled and preprocessed before using the model.

instruction: How do I get an accurate mask for the foreground in this photo?
description: BiRefNet is a model designed for high-resolution dichotomous image segmentation, specifically for general matting tasks. It is based on the concept of bilateral reference, which aims to improve segmentation accuracy by leveraging reference information from both foreground and background regions. The model is trained on a variety of datasets, including P3M-10k (excluding TE-P3M-500-NP), TR-humans, AM-2k, AIM-500, Human-2k (synthesized with BG-20k), Distinctions-646 (synthesized with BG-20k), HIM2K, and PPM-100. It is validated on the TE-P3M-500-NP dataset. Performance evaluations show that BiRefNet achieves an Smeasure of 0.978, maxFm of 0.996, meanEm of 0.990, MSE of 0.003, maxEm of 0.996, meanFm of 0.987, wFmeasure of 0.989, adpEm of 0.962, adpFm of 0.980, HCE of 0.000, mBA of 0.825, maxBIoU of 0.934, and meanBIoU of 0.888 on the TE-P3M-500-NP dataset. On the TE-AM-2k dataset, it achieves an Smeasure of 0.970, maxFm of 0.995, meanEm of 0.989, MSE of 0.003, maxEm of 0.996, meanFm of 0.986, wFmeasure of 0.987, adpEm of 0.985, adpFm of 0.990, HCE of 0.000, mBA of 0.830, maxBIoU of 0.937, and meanBIoU of 0.876. The model is developed by Peng Zheng, Dehong Gao, Deng-Ping Fan, Li Liu, Jorma Laaksonen, Wanli Ouyang, and Nicu Sebe, affiliated with Nankai University, Northwestern Polytechnical University, National University of Defense Technology, Aalto University, Shanghai AI Laboratory, and University of Trento, respectively.

instruction: Show me pictures that best represent the phrase 'a futuristic city with flying vehicles and glowing skyscrapers'.
description: LLM2CLIP is a novel approach that leverages the power of large language models (LLMs) to enhance the capabilities of CLIP. By fine-tuning the LLM in the caption space with contrastive learning, the method extracts textual capabilities into the output embeddings, significantly improving the output layer's textual discriminability. The fine-tuned LLM then acts as a powerful teacher for CLIP's visual encoder in an efficient training process. This allows the incorporation of longer and more complex captions, overcoming the limitations of the vanilla CLIP text encoder's context window and ability constraints. Experiments show that this approach brings substantial improvements in cross-modal tasks. Specifically, it directly boosted the performance of the previously state-of-the-art EVA02 model by 16.5% on both long-text and short-text retrieval tasks, transforming a CLIP model trained solely on English data into a state-of-the-art cross-lingual model. When integrated into multimodal training with models like Llava 1.5, it consistently outperformed CLIP across nearly all benchmarks, demonstrating comprehensive performance improvements. LLM2CLIP is a vision foundation model and feature backbone, pretrained on datasets including CC3M, CC12M, YFCC15M, and a 30M subset of Recap-DataComp-1B. It is important to note that all results presented in the paper are evaluated using PyTorch weights, and there may be differences in performance when using Hugging Face (hf) models.

instruction: Can you show me images that correspond to the phrase 'a red sports car speeding on a highway'?
description: The CLIP model was developed by researchers at OpenAI to study what contributes to robustness in computer vision tasks and to test the ability of models to generalize to arbitrary image classification tasks in a zero-shot manner. It was not developed for general model deployment, and researchers are advised to carefully study its capabilities in relation to the specific context of deployment. The model was developed in January 2021 and uses a ViT-L/14 Transformer architecture as an image encoder and a masked self-attention Transformer as a text encoder. These encoders are trained to maximize the similarity of (image, text) pairs via a contrastive loss. The original implementation had two variants: one using a ResNet image encoder and the other using a Vision Transformer. This repository contains the variant with the Vision Transformer. The model is intended for research use by AI researchers to better understand robustness, generalization, and other capabilities, biases, and constraints of computer vision models. Any deployed use case, whether commercial or not, is currently out of scope. Non-deployed use cases such as image search in a constrained environment are also not recommended unless there is thorough in-domain testing of the model with a specific, fixed class taxonomy. This is because the model's performance varies significantly with different class taxonomies, and untested deployment may be harmful. Use cases involving surveillance and facial recognition are always out of scope due to the lack of testing norms and checks to ensure fair use. The model has not been trained or evaluated on languages other than English, so its use should be limited to English language use cases. The model was trained on publicly available image-caption data, including data crawled from the internet and pre-existing image datasets. The data is more representative of people and societies most connected to the internet, which tend to skew towards more developed nations and younger, male users. The goal of the dataset was to test robustness and generalizability in computer vision tasks, and the data was gathered in a mostly non-interventionist manner, with crawling limited to websites that had policies against excessively violent and adult images. The dataset is not intended for use as the basis for any commercial or deployed model and will not be released. The model has been evaluated on a wide range of benchmarks across various computer vision datasets, including Food101, CIFAR10, CIFAR100, Birdsnap, SUN397, Stanford Cars, FGVC Aircraft, VOC2007, DTD, Oxford-IIIT Pet dataset, Caltech101, Flowers102, MNIST, SVHN, IIIT5K, Hateful Memes, SST-2, UCF101, Kinetics700, Country211, CLEVR Counting, KITTI Distance, STL-10, RareAct, Flickr30, MSCOCO, ImageNet, ImageNet-A, ImageNet-R, ImageNet Sketch, ObjectNet (ImageNet Overlap), Youtube-BB, and ImageNet-Vid. The model has several limitations, including struggles with fine-grained classification and counting objects. It also exhibits issues with fairness and bias, which depend significantly on class design and the choices made for categories to include and exclude. Testing revealed significant disparities with respect to race and gender when classifying images of people into crime-related and non-human animal categories, and these disparities could shift based on how the classes were constructed. Evaluations on the Fairface dataset showed accuracy greater than 96% across all races for gender classification, with 'Middle Eastern' having the highest accuracy (98.4%) and 'White' having the lowest (96.5%). CLIP averaged approximately 93% for racial classification and approximately 63% for age classification. These evaluations are used to assess performance across different demographics and to surface potential risks, not to endorse or promote such tasks.

instruction: Generate a painting-style image of a woman in a flowing dress dancing under the stars.
description: This model is an 8-step distilled LoRA trained based on the FLUX.1-dev model by the AlimamaCreative Team. It uses a multi-head discriminator to enhance the distillation quality. The model supports text-to-image generation, inpainting with controlnet, and other FLUX-related tasks. It is recommended to use a guidance_scale of 3.5 and a lora_scale of 1. The model can be used with the diffusers library and is compatible with the FluxPipeline. Training was conducted on 1 million open source and internal images with an aesthetic score of 6.3 or higher and a resolution greater than 800. Adversarial training was employed, with the original FLUX.1-dev transformer fixed as the discriminator backbone and multi-heads added to every transformer layer. The guidance scale was fixed at 3.5 during training, and a time shift of 3 was used. Training was performed using mixed precision (bf16), a learning rate of 2e-5, a batch size of 64, and an image size of 1024x1024.

instruction: How would you label this Danish news article for categorization purposes?
description: xlm-roberta-large-twitter-cap-minor is an xlm-roberta-large model finetuned on multilingual (english, danish, hungarian) training data labelled with from the . The model is designed for text classification tasks, particularly for processing text in multiple languages. It supports the classification of text inputs, with a focus on english, danish, and hungarian. The model was evaluated on a test set of 15 349 english examples, which constituted 20% of the english data. On this test set, the model achieved an accuracy of 0.67 and a weighted average F1-score of 0.61. The model uses the sentencepiece tokenizer. Users must provide a token when loading the model due to gated access, using the token parameter in the Transformers package or the use_auth_token parameter in earlier versions. If a RuntimeError occurs when loading the model using the from_pretrained() method, adding ignore_mismatched_sizes=True should resolve the issue. The model's performance can be improved by expanding the training data, and the developers welcome submissions of CAP-coded corpora in any domain and language. Submissions can be sent to poltextlab{at}poltextlab{dot}com or through the specified method.

instruction: How can I represent a collection of news articles in a way that preserves their meaning and context?
description: Ettin is an open suite of paired encoder-only and decoder-only models trained with identical data, architecture, and training recipes. The models are designed to enable fair comparisons between encoder and decoder architectures across multiple scales, providing state-of-the-art performance for open-data models in their respective size categories. Ettin models use identical training data, matched architectures (differing only in attention patterns and training objectives), and a consistent training recipe across all models. The training data is publicly available and includes a high-quality mixture of diverse sources totaling 2T+ tokens, with phases including pre-training, mid-training, and decay. The model family includes encoder and decoder models with parameter sizes ranging from 17M to 1B, as well as cross-objective models that demonstrate the results of continuing training encoders as decoders and vice versa. Ettin supports a variety of tasks, including classification, embeddings, text generation, and retrieval. Performance highlights show that Ettin encoders outperform ModernBERT on GLUE and MTEB tasks, while Ettin decoders outperform SmolLM2 and Llama 3.2 on generative tasks. The models are based on a Transformer architecture with RoPE, GLU activations, and prenorm layers, and support context lengths up to 8K tokens. Training checkpoints are available for research and analysis, enabling detailed study of training dynamics and capability emergence. Ettin is intended for research applications such as architecture studies, training dynamics analysis, scaling law investigations, and replication studies. All training artifacts, including data, model weights, and hyperparameters, are publicly available. Caveats include the need to load cross-objective models using the architecture they were converted to, not their original architecture.

instruction: Which part of the research paper discusses the impact of deforestation on biodiversity?
description: This is a model that maps sentences and paragraphs to a 768-dimensional dense vector space and was designed for semantic search. It has been trained on 215 million (question, answer) pairs from diverse sources. The model uses CLS pooling and is suitable for dot-product similarity functions. It does not produce normalized embeddings. The model is intended to encode queries and text paragraphs in a dense vector space to find relevant documents. It has a limit of 512 word pieces, and text longer than that will be truncated. The model was trained on input text up to 250 word pieces and may not work well for longer text. The training data includes duplicate question pairs from WikiAnswers, automatically generated (question, paragraph) pairs from Wikipedia, (title, body) and (title, answer) pairs from StackExchanges, triplets from Bing search engine, (query, answer) pairs from Google queries and featured snippets, (question, answer) pairs from Amazon product pages, Yahoo Answers, and other sources. The total number of training tuples is 214,988,242. The model was trained using CLS-pooling, dot-product as the similarity function, and a scale of 1. The full training script is accessible in the repository as `train_script.py`. The model was developed during a project organized by Hugging Face and benefited from hardware infrastructure including 7 TPUs v3-8 and support from Google's Flax, JAX, and Cloud teams.

instruction: Quelle est la signification de ce terme juridique dans ce passage de texte français ?
description: JuriBERT is a set of BERT models (tiny, mini, small, and base) pre-trained from scratch on French legal-domain specific corpora. The models are trained on 6.3GB of legal French raw text from two sources: one dataset is crawled from Légifrance, and the other consists of anonymized court decisions and pleadings from the Court of Cassation (mémoires ampliatifs), which contains more than 100,000 long documents from different court cases. JuriBERT is available on Hugging Face in four versions with varying numbers of parameters. The four models are dascim/juribert-tiny with 6 million parameters and a Tiny architecture (L=2, H=128, A=2), dascim/juribert-mini with 15 million parameters and a Mini architecture (L=4, H=256, A=4), dascim/juribert-small with 42 million parameters and a Small architecture (L=6, H=512, A=8), and dascim/juribert-base with 110 million parameters and a Base architecture (L=12, H=768, A=12). JuriBERT is designed to support tasks such as filling masks in legal French text and extracting contextual embedding features from its output. The model can be loaded along with its sub-word tokenizer using the Transformers library. JuriBERT was trained and evaluated at École Polytechnique in collaboration with HEC Paris by Stella Douka, Hadi Abdine, Mihalis Vazirgiannis, Rajaa El Hamdani, and David Restrepo Amariles.

instruction: Could you help me create a 3D model of a statue using several photos taken from different positions?
description: LucidFusion is a flexible end-to-end feed-forward framework designed to generate high-resolution 3D Gaussians from unposed, sparse, and arbitrary numbers of multiview images. The model introduces the Relative Coordinate Map (RCM) to align geometric features coherently across different views, eliminating the need for pose information that traditional methods rely on to link images to 3D world coordinates. This makes LucidFusion highly adaptable for 3D generation from arbitrary, unposed images. The framework seamlessly integrates with the original single-image-to-3D pipeline, producing detailed 3D Gaussians at a resolution of 512 by 512. It is suitable for a wide range of applications and operates at approximately 13 frames per second on an A800 GPU. Recent large reconstruction models have struggled with controllability due to incomplete or inconsistent 3D reconstructions from single images, but LucidFusion addresses this limitation by leveraging multiple views to improve reconstruction quality.

instruction: Could you explain the meaning behind this visual pattern?
description: InternVL3 is an advanced multimodal large language model (MLLM) series that demonstrates superior overall performance. Compared to InternVL 2.5, InternVL3 exhibits superior multimodal perception and reasoning capabilities, while further extending its multimodal capabilities to encompass tool usage, GUI agents, industrial image analysis, 3D vision perception, and more. InternVL3 integrates newly incrementally pre-trained InternViT with various pre-trained LLMs, including InternLM 3 and Qwen 2.5, using a randomly initialized MLP projector. The model retains the same architecture as its predecessors, following the 'ViT-MLP-LLM' paradigm. A pixel unshuffle operation is applied to reduce the number of visual tokens to one-quarter of the original, and a dynamic resolution strategy divides images into tiles of 448×448 pixels. InternVL3 introduces support for multi-image and video data, and utilizes V2PE, which employs smaller, more flexible position increments for visual tokens, enhancing long context understanding. The model is trained using a Native Multimodal Pre-Training approach that interleaves multimodal data with large-scale textual corpora, allowing the model to learn both linguistic and multimodal representations simultaneously. Supervised Fine-Tuning in InternVL3 uses higher-quality and more diverse training data, including samples for tool use, 3D scene understanding, GUI operations, long context tasks, video understanding, scientific diagrams, creative writing, and multimodal reasoning. Mixed Preference Optimization (MPO) is employed to align the model response distribution with the ground-truth distribution, improving reasoning performance. The training objective of MPO combines preference loss, quality loss, and generation loss. Test-Time Scaling is used to enhance reasoning abilities, employing a Best-of-N evaluation strategy with a critic model to select the best response. InternVL3 achieves better overall text performance than the Qwen2.5 series due to Native Multimodal Pre-Training. Evaluation results show that models fine-tuned with MPO demonstrate superior reasoning performance across seven multimodal reasoning benchmarks. The introduction of V2PE leads to significant performance gains across most evaluation metrics. InternVL3 supports a wide range of tasks, including multimodal reasoning and mathematics, OCR, chart and document understanding, multi-image and real-world comprehension, visual grounding, multilingual understanding, video understanding, GUI grounding, and spatial reasoning. The model can be loaded and used with the Transformers library, supporting various configurations including 16-bit and 8-bit quantization, and multi-GPU setups. It can handle pure-text conversations, single-image and multi-image conversations, batch inference, and video multi-round conversations. InternVL3 is compatible with LMDeploy for deployment, supporting multi-image inference, batch prompts, multi-turn conversations, and RESTful API services. The project is released under the MIT License and uses the pre-trained Qwen2.5, which is licensed under the Qwen License.

instruction: What is the main idea of the text shown in the image of the article I shared?
description: Qwen2.5-VL-32B-Instruct is a vision-language model designed to understand and process visual and textual information. It has been enhanced through reinforcement learning to improve mathematical and problem-solving abilities, as well as subjective user experience, with response styles adjusted to better align with human preferences. The model is capable of recognizing common objects and analyzing texts, charts, icons, graphics, and layouts within images. It functions as a visual agent that can reason and dynamically direct tools, enabling computer and phone use. It can understand long videos of over one hour and capture events by pinpointing relevant video segments. It supports visual localization in different formats by generating bounding boxes or points and provides stable JSON outputs for coordinates and attributes. It can generate structured outputs for data like scans of invoices, forms, and tables, which is beneficial for applications in finance and commerce. The model architecture includes dynamic resolution and frame rate training for video understanding, which extends dynamic resolution to the temporal dimension by adopting dynamic FPS sampling. This allows the model to comprehend videos at various sampling rates and learn temporal sequence and speed, enabling the ability to pinpoint specific moments. The vision encoder is streamlined and efficient, with training and inference speeds enhanced by implementing window attention into the ViT. The ViT architecture is further optimized with SwiGLU and RMSNorm, aligning it with the structure of the Qwen2.5 LLM. The model has 32 billion parameters and is instruction-tuned. Evaluation results show strong performance across various vision and text benchmarks, including MMMU, MathVista, OCRBenchV2, DocVQA, and MMLU. The model supports input formats such as local files, base64, and URLs for images, and local files for videos. It can handle batch inference and long texts with a context length up to 32,768 tokens, with an option to use YaRN for enhancing model length extrapolation. However, this method is not recommended for tasks involving temporal and spatial localization. For long video inputs, the max_position_embeddings can be modified to a larger value, such as 64k.

description: This model is the speech encoder component of the SeamlessM4Tv2-Large model, which performs strongly on cross- and multilingual sequence-level audio classification tasks. It is designed to extract features from audio inputs for classification purposes. The model supports audio classification tasks, particularly demonstrating performance on the SIB-Fleurs dataset. It is based on the architecture of the original SeamlessM4Tv2-Large model. The model can be used with the `AutoModel` or `AutoModelForAudioClassification` classes from the Transformers library. It is recommended to use the model with a GPU for optimal performance. The model expects a 16 kHz audio waveform as input and returns attention masks for batching. It has been evaluated on the SIB-Fleurs dataset, which contains 7 labels. Users are advised to cite the original SeamlessM4Tv2 paper when using this model.

description: VideoLLaMA 3 is a state-of-the-art series of multimodal foundation models designed to excel in both image and video understanding tasks. These models leverage advanced architectures to process and interpret visual content across various contexts. They are specifically designed to address complex multimodal challenges, such as integrating textual and visual information, extracting insights from sequential video data, and performing high-level reasoning over both dynamic and static visual scenes. The VideoLLaMA 3 series includes multiple model variants, such as VideoLLaMA3-7B, VideoLLaMA3-2B, VideoLLaMA3-7B-Image, and VideoLLaMA3-2B-Image, each based on different base models like Qwen2.5-7B and Qwen2.5-1.5B. Additionally, a tuned vision encoder for VideoLLaMA3-7B is available for wider application, based on the siglip-so400m-patch14-384 model. The models support video conversation tasks, where they can process video inputs along with textual questions to generate detailed responses. They are designed for use in applications requiring both image and video understanding, with the ability to handle sequential video data and perform high-level reasoning. The models are available for use through model repositories and include inference code for deployment. The VideoLLaMA 3 series is the result of ongoing research and development, with previous iterations including VideoLLaMA 2 and Video-LLaMA, each advancing spatial-temporal modeling and audio understanding in video language models. The models are intended for use in research and applications related to video understanding, and users are encouraged to cite the relevant publications when using them.

instruction: What images are available for the phrase 'a vibrant coral reef teeming with marine life and colorful fish'?
description: LLM2CLIP is a novel approach that leverages the power of large language models (LLMs) to enhance the capabilities of CLIP. By fine-tuning the LLM in the caption space using contrastive learning, the method extracts textual capabilities into the output embeddings, significantly improving the textual discriminability of the output layer. The fine-tuned LLM is then used as a teacher for CLIP’s visual encoder in an efficient training process. This allows the incorporation of longer and more complex captions, overcoming the limitations of the vanilla CLIP text encoder's context window and processing ability. Experiments show that this approach leads to substantial improvements in cross-modal tasks. Specifically, the method improved the performance of the previously state-of-the-art EVA02 model by 16.5% on both long-text and short-text retrieval tasks, enabling a CLIP model trained on English data to become a state-of-the-art cross-lingual model. When integrated into multimodal training with models like Llava 1.5, it consistently outperformed CLIP across nearly all benchmarks, demonstrating comprehensive performance improvements. The model is a vision foundation model and feature backbone, pretrained on datasets including CC3M, CC12M, YFCC15M, and a 30M subset of Recap-DataComp-1B. It is important to note that all results presented in the paper are evaluated using PyTorch weights, and there may be differences in performance when using Hugging Face (hf) models.

instruction: How would you translate this dialogue from a game script, making sure the character's personality comes through in English?
description: This is a qlora fine-tune created using a new version of the VNTL dataset. The purpose of this fine-tune is to improve the performance of LLMs at translating Japanese visual novels to English. Unlike the previous version, this one does not include the 'chat mode'. For this new version of VNTL 8B, the dataset was rebuilt and expanded from the ground up, and it performs well, outperforming the previous version in terms of accuracy and stability, making far fewer mistakes even when running at high temperatures, though temperature 0 is still recommended for the best accuracy. Major changes in this version include switching to the default LLaMA3 prompt format due to issues with the custom one, adding proper support for multi-line translations (the old version only handled single lines), and overall better translation accuracy. One thing to note is that while the translations are more accurate, they tend to be more literal compared to the previous version. For optimal results, it is highly recommended to use neutral sampling parameters (temperature 0 with no repetition penalty) when using this model. This fine-tune was done using similar hyperparameters, with the only difference being the dataset, which is a brand-new one. The rank is 128, the alpha is 32, the effective batch size is 45, the warmup ratio is 0.02, the learning rate is 6e-5, the embedding learning rate is 1e-5, the optimizer is grokadamw, the learning rate schedule is cosine, and the weight decay is 0.01. The train loss is 0.42. This fine-tune uses the LLaMA 3 prompt format. An example prompt for translation includes metadata with character information and Japanese text to be translated. The generated translation for that prompt, with temperature 0, is provided. The Metadata section is not limited to character information; it can also include trivia and teach the model the correct way to pronounce words it struggles with. An example includes metadata with character and element information, followed by Japanese text and the generated English translation.

instruction: What's the best way to summarize a lengthy research paper in simple terms?
description: The Llama 4 collection of models are natively multimodal AI models that enable text and multimodal experiences. These models leverage a mixture-of-experts architecture to offer industry-leading performance in text and image understanding. These Llama 4 models mark the beginning of a new era for the Llama ecosystem. We are launching two efficient models in the Llama 4 series, Llama 4 Scout, a 17 billion parameter model with 16 experts, and Llama 4 Maverick, a 17 billion parameter model with 128 experts. The Llama 4 models are auto-regressive language models that use a mixture-of-experts (MoE) architecture and incorporate early fusion for native multimodality. Llama 4 Scout was pretrained on ~40 trillion tokens and Llama 4 Maverick was pretrained on ~22 trillion tokens of multimodal data from a mix of publicly available, licensed data and information from Meta’s products and services. This includes publicly shared posts from Instagram and Facebook and people’s interactions with Meta AI. The pretraining data has a cutoff of August 2024. Llama 4 Scout has a context length of 10 million tokens and Llama 4 Maverick has a context length of 1 million tokens. Llama 4 is intended for commercial and research use in multiple languages. Instruction tuned models are intended for assistant-like chat and visual reasoning tasks, whereas pretrained models can be adapted for natural language generation. For vision, Llama 4 models are also optimized for visual recognition, image reasoning, captioning, and answering general questions about an image. The Llama 4 model collection also supports the ability to leverage the outputs of its models to improve other models including synthetic data generation and distillation. The Llama 4 Community License allows for these use cases. Llama 4 has been trained on a broader collection of languages than the 12 supported languages (pre-training includes ). Developers may fine-tune Llama 4 models for languages beyond the 12 supported languages provided they comply with the Llama 4 Community License and the Acceptable Use Policy. Developers are responsible for ensuring that their use of Llama 4 in additional languages is done in a safe and responsible manner. Llama 4 has been tested for image understanding up to 5 input images. If leveraging additional image understanding capabilities beyond this, Developers are responsible for ensuring that their deployments are mitigated for risks and should perform additional testing and tuning tailored to their specific applications. Llama 4 was developed following the best practices outlined in our . The primary objective of conducting safety fine-tuning is to offer developers a readily available, safe, and powerful model for various applications, reducing the workload needed to deploy safe AI systems. Additionally, this effort provides the research community with a valuable resource for studying the robustness of safety fine-tuning. We employ a multi-faceted approach to data collection, combining human-generated data from our vendors with synthetic data to mitigate potential safety risks. We’ve developed many large language model (LLM)-based classifiers that enable us to thoughtfully select high-quality prompts and responses, enhancing data quality control. Building on the work we started with our Llama 3 models, we put a great emphasis on driving down model refusals to benign prompts for Llama 4. We included both borderline and adversarial prompts in our safety data strategy, and modified our safety data responses to follow tone guidelines. We expanded our work on the refusal tone from Llama 3 so that the model sounds more natural. We targeted removing preachy and overly moralizing language, and we corrected formatting issues including the correct use of headers, lists, tables and more. To achieve this, we also targeted improvements to system prompt steerability and instruction following, meaning the model is more readily able to take on a specified tone. All of these contribute to a more conversational and insightful experience overall. Llama 4 is a more steerable model, meaning responses can be easily tailored to meet specific developer outcomes. Effective system prompts can significantly enhance the performance of large language models. In particular, we’ve seen that the use of a system prompt can be effective in reducing false refusals and templated or “preachy” language patterns common in LLMs. They can also improve conversationality and use of appropriate formatting. Large language models, including Llama 4, are not designed to be deployed in isolation but instead should be deployed as part of an overall AI system with additional guardrails as required. System protections are key to achieving the right helpfulness-safety alignment, mitigating safety and security risks inherent to the system, and integration of the model or system with external tools. We provide the community with system level  - like Llama Guard, Prompt Guard and Code Shield - that developers should deploy with Llama models or other LLMs. All of our  demos contain these safeguards by default so developers can benefit from system-level safety out-of-the-box. We evaluated Llama models for common use cases as well as specific capabilities. Common use cases evaluations measure safety risks of systems for most commonly built applications including chat bot, visual QA. We built dedicated, adversarial evaluation datasets and evaluated systems composed of Llama models and Llama Guard 3 to filter input prompt and output response. It is important to evaluate applications in context, and we recommend building dedicated evaluation dataset for your use case. Prompt Guard and Code Shield are also available if relevant to the application. Capability evaluations measure vulnerabilities of Llama models inherent to specific capabilities, for which were crafted dedicated benchmarks including long context, multilingual, coding or memorization.
