We conduct recurring red teaming exercises with the goal of discovering risks via adversarial prompting and we use the learnings to improve our benchmarks and safety tuning datasets. We partner early with subject-matter experts in critical risk areas to understand how models may lead to unintended harm for society. Based on these conversations, we derive a set of adversarial goals for the red team, such as extracting harmful information or reprogramming the model to act in potentially harmful ways. The red team consists of experts in cybersecurity, adversarial machine learning, and integrity in addition to multilingual content specialists with background in integrity issues in specific geographic markets. We spend additional focus on the following critical risk areas: To assess risks related to proliferation of chemical and biological weapons for Llama 4, we applied expert-designed and other targeted evaluations designed to assess whether the use of Llama 4 could meaningfully increase the capabilities of malicious actors to plan or carry out attacks using these types of weapons. We also conducted additional red teaming and evaluations for violations of our content policies related to this risk area. We leverage pre-training methods like data filtering as a first step in mitigating Child Safety risk in our model. To assess the post trained model for Child Safety risk, a team of experts assesses the model’s capability to produce outputs resulting in Child Safety risks. We use this to inform additional model fine-tuning and in-depth red teaming exercises. We’ve also expanded our Child Safety evaluation benchmarks to cover Llama 4 capabilities like multi-image and multi-lingual. Our cyber evaluations investigated whether Llama 4 is sufficiently capable to enable catastrophic threat scenario outcomes. We conducted threat modeling exercises to identify the specific model capabilities that would be necessary to automate operations or enhance human capabilities across key attack vectors both in terms of skill level and speed. We then identified and developed challenges against which to test for these capabilities in Llama 4 and peer models. Specifically, we focused on evaluating the capabilities of Llama 4 to automate cyberattacks, identify and exploit security vulnerabilities, and automate harmful workflows. Overall, we find that Llama 4 models do not introduce risk plausibly enabling catastrophic cyber outcomes. Generative AI safety requires expertise and tooling, and we believe in the strength of the open community to accelerate its progress. We are active members of open consortiums, including the AI Alliance, Partnership on AI and MLCommons, actively contributing to safety standardization and transparency. We encourage the community to adopt taxonomies like the MLCommons Proof of Concept evaluation to facilitate collaboration and transparency on safety and content evaluations. Our Trust tools are open sourced for the community to use and widely distributed across ecosystem partners including cloud service providers. We encourage community contributions to our . We also set up the  program to identify and support the most compelling applications of Meta’s Llama model for societal benefit across three categories: education, climate and open innovation. The 20 finalists from the hundreds of applications can be found . Finally, we put in place a set of resources including an  and  to continuously improve the Llama technology with the help of the community. Our AI is anchored on the values of freedom of expression - helping people to explore, debate, and innovate using our technology. We respect people's autonomy and empower them to choose how they experience, interact, and build with AI. Our AI promotes an open exchange of ideas. It is meant to serve everyone, and to work for a wide range of use cases. It is thus designed to be accessible to people across many different backgrounds, experiences and perspectives. Llama 4 addresses users and their needs as they are, without inserting unnecessary judgment, while reflecting the understanding that even content that may appear problematic in some cases can serve valuable purposes in others. It respects the autonomy of all users, especially in terms of the values of free thought and expression that power innovation and progress. Llama 4 is a new technology, and like any new technology, there are risks associated with its use. Testing conducted to date has not covered, nor could it cover, all scenarios. For these reasons, as with all LLMs, Llama 4’s potential outputs cannot be predicted in advance, and the model may in some instances produce inaccurate or other objectionable responses to user prompts. Therefore, before deploying any applications of Llama 4 models, developers should perform safety testing and tuning tailored to their specific applications of the model. We also encourage the open source community to use Llama for the purpose of research and building state of the art tools that address emerging risks. Please refer to available resources including our Developer Use Guide: AI Protections,  solutions, and other  to learn more.

instruction: I need the English version of this Italian text for a project.
description: LlaMaestra is a small Llama model fine-tuned for text translation between English and Italian. It was trained using approximately 300,000 examples of English-to-Italian and Italian-to-English translations. The fine-tuning process was designed to enable the model to provide direct translations with minimal explanation. Training was completed in about 10 hours on an A10G Nvidia GPU. The model is optimized for performance on CPUs due to its compact size. The base model used is `meta-llama/Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct`, and the fine-tuning was conducted by Leonard Püttmann. The training data consists of sentence pairs sourced from tatoeba.com.

instruction: How can I classify a Japanese text into one of several predefined categories based on its content?
description: ModernBERT-Ja-310M is a variant of the BERT model that combines local and global attention mechanisms, enabling it to handle long sequences while maintaining high computational efficiency. It incorporates modern architectural improvements. The model is trained on a high-quality corpus of Japanese and English text comprising 4.09T tokens, with a vocabulary size of 102,400 and a sequence length of 8,192 tokens. It is designed for masked language modeling and is intended to be fine-tuned for downstream tasks. It is not suitable for text generation, and users are advised to use a dedicated text generation model for such purposes. The model uses a unigram language model tokenizer with byte fallback, and does not apply pre-tokenization for Japanese, allowing raw sentences to be input directly. However, the token boundaries often do not align with morpheme boundaries, which may lead to poor performance in token classification tasks such as named entity recognition and span extraction. The model was constructed through a three-stage training process: pre-training with 3.51T tokens using a sequence length of 1,024 and a masking rate of 30%, followed by two phases of context length extension. Phase 1 used 430B tokens with a sequence length of 8,192 and a masking rate of 30%, and Phase 2 used 150B tokens with a sequence length of 8,192 and a reduced masking rate of 15%. The model's configuration includes a vocabulary size of 102,400, a head dimension of 64, and an activation function of GELU. The attention mechanism consists of one global attention layer and two local attention layers, with a sliding window attention window context size of 128. The global_rope_theta is set to 160,000 and the local_rope_theta to 10,000. The model was evaluated on 12 datasets, including JGLUE, across various tasks such as knowledge-based tasks, Japanese linguistic acceptability classification, natural language inference, semantic textual similarity, and various classification tasks. The evaluation used a maximum sequence length not exceeding 512, with settings aligned to existing models. Hyperparameters such as learning rate and number of epochs were explored, and the model was fine-tuned with a classification head consisting of a linear layer, a GELU activation function, and another linear layer. The embedding of the special token at the beginning of the sentence was used for classification, as the model does not perform next sentence prediction during pretraining. Evaluations were conducted using 5-fold cross-validation, with optimal hyperparameters determined based on validation set performance and final scores reported on test sets. For datasets without predefined splits, 10% of the data was set aside as a test set, and for datasets with only train and validation sets, the validation set was treated as the test set. The model achieved state-of-the-art performance across evaluation tasks, even when compared with much larger models. Despite being a long-context model capable of processing sequences of up to 8,192 tokens, it also exhibited strong performance in short-sequence evaluations. The model may produce representations that reflect biases, and when used for masked language modeling, it may generate biased or harmful expressions.

instruction: Can you create a voice file from this article in a calm and professional tone?
description: Kokoro is a text-to-speech (TTS) model with 82 million parameters, designed to convert text input into audio output. It supports multiple voices with different nationalities and genders, including American and British accents for both male and female voices. The model is available in various quantized and precision formats, such as fp32, fp16, q8, q4, and mixed precision, with file sizes ranging from 86 MB to 326 MB. Kokoro can be used in JavaScript and Python environments, with libraries like kokoro-js providing methods to generate speech from text and save the output as audio files. The model requires tokenized input, which is processed through an inference session for audio generation. It is important to note that the model has a context length of 512 tokens, with padding required at the start and end of the input. The model is described as a frontier TTS model for its size.

instruction: Can you determine the percentage of females in the sample based on the table?
description: The model is a conditional generation model based on the MBart architecture, designed for table question answering (TableQA) in low-resource Indic languages, specifically Hindi and Bengali. It is intended to answer questions over structured tabular data, returning either individual cells or tables as output. The model supports tasks involving structured information retrieval and generation in these languages, where annotated data and neural models are scarce. The model is trained on large-scale datasets generated through a fully automatic data generation process, which is scalable and applicable to any low-resource language with a web presence. Evaluation results show that models trained on these datasets outperform state-of-the-art large language models. The model is evaluated on aspects such as mathematical reasoning capabilities and zero-shot cross-lingual transfer. The model uses a linearized representation of tables, where headers and rows are processed and converted into a string format for input. The model is trained using a source sequence composed of a query and the linearized input table, with the target being the linearized output table. The model is implemented using the Hugging Face Transformers library and is fine-tuned for the Hindi and Bengali languages. The model includes a preprocessing pipeline that converts numerical values and handles language-specific characters, such as converting English digits to Hindi digits. The model is evaluated on the HindiTabQA dataset, which includes questions, input tables, and answers in Hindi. The model is trained using beam search with a beam size of 5 and a maximum sequence length of 1024 tokens. The model is available for use with the Hugging Face Transformers library and requires a tokenizer configured for the source and target language, with the source and target language set to Hindi (hi_IN). The model is trained on a GPU and uses the Hugging Face Datasets library for data loading. The model is part of a research effort published in the Proceedings of the 2024 Conference on Empirical Methods in Natural Language Processing, where the authors introduce the first scalable data generation and evaluation procedures for low-resource TableQA. The model is released along with the datasets and code for reproducibility and further research.
domain: Natural Language Processing Table Question Answering

instruction: Show me a video of a flower blooming in slow motion from a bud to a full bloom.
description: Wan2.1 is a comprehensive and open suite of video foundation models designed to push the boundaries of video generation. It offers state-of-the-art performance, consistently outperforming existing open-source models and state-of-the-art commercial solutions across multiple benchmarks. The T2V-1.3B model requires only 8.19 GB VRAM, making it compatible with nearly all consumer-grade GPUs. It can generate a 5-second 480P video on an RTX 4090 in about 4 minutes, with performance comparable to some closed-source models. Wan2.1 supports multiple tasks, including Text-to-Video, Image-to-Video, Video Editing, Text-to-Image, and Video-to-Audio, advancing the field of video generation. It is the first video model capable of generating both Chinese and English text, enhancing its practical applications. The suite includes a powerful video VAE called Wan-VAE, which delivers exceptional efficiency and performance, encoding and decoding 1080P videos of any length while preserving temporal information. Wan2.1 is built on the mainstream diffusion transformer paradigm, incorporating innovations such as a novel spatio-temporal VAE, scalable training strategies, large-scale data construction, and automated evaluation metrics. The model uses a Flow Matching framework within the Diffusion Transformer paradigm, with a T5 Encoder to encode multilingual text input and cross-attention in each transformer block to embed the text into the model structure. An MLP with a Linear layer and a SiLU layer processes the input time embeddings and predicts six modulation parameters individually, shared across all transformer blocks. The T2V-1.3B model has 1.3 billion parameters, with a dimension of 1536, input and output dimensions of 16, a feedforward dimension of 8960, a frequency dimension of 256, 12 heads, and 30 layers. The T2V-14B model has 14 billion parameters, with a dimension of 5120, input and output dimensions of 16, a feedforward dimension of 13824, a frequency dimension of 256, 40 heads, and 40 layers. The model is trained on a large-scale dataset of images and videos, curated through a four-step data cleaning process focusing on fundamental dimensions, visual quality, and motion quality. Evaluation results using the Wan-Bench framework show that the T2V-1.3B model surpasses the overall metrics of larger open-source models, demonstrating the effectiveness of Wan2.1's architecture and data construction pipeline. Computational efficiency tests on different GPUs show that the model can be run on a single 4090 GPU with offloading techniques. The model is licensed under the Apache 2.0 License, and users are responsible for ensuring their use complies with the license terms, including not generating harmful or illegal content. The model is intended to serve as an easy-to-use tool for creative teams in video creation and a high-quality foundational model for academic teams with limited computing resources, facilitating the rapid development of the video creation community and the swift advancement of video technology.

instruction: Can you identify the main objects or elements present in the video?
description: VideoChat-Flash-2B is built upon UMT-L (300M) and Qwen2.5-1.5B, using only 16 tokens per frame. It leverages Yarn to extend the context window to 128k, allowing input sequences of up to approximately 10,000 frames. The model is designed for video modeling and supports tasks such as describing videos in detail and answering questions about video content in a single-turn or multi-turn conversation format. It is recommended to use English for interaction, as the model exhibits basic Chinese comprehension due to a predominantly English training corpus. The model achieves a performance of 70.0 on MVBench, 58.3 on LongVideoBench, and 57.0 on VideoMME (without subtitles) with a maximum input of 10,000 frames. With additional configurations, it can reach 73.2 on MVBench, 64.2 on LongVideoBench, and 64.0 on VideoMME (without subtitles) with 10,000 frames, and 73.4 on MVBench, 66.5 on LongVideoBench, and 63.5 on VideoMME (without subtitles) with 50,000 frames. The model can be configured with or without global compression, and it supports up to 512 frames in evaluation settings. It uses a generation configuration with no sampling, a temperature of 0.0, a maximum of 1024 new tokens, a top-p value of 0.1, and a single beam for generation.

instruction: How can I get a clear segmentation of this image into sky, trees, and ground?
description: EoMT (Encoder-only Mask Transformer) is a Vision Transformer (ViT) architecture designed for high-quality and efficient image segmentation. It was introduced in the CVPR 2025 highlight paper titled 'Your ViT is Secretly an Image Segmentation Model' by Tommie Kerssies, Niccolò Cavagnero, Alexander Hermans, Narges Norouzi, Giuseppe Averta, Bastian Leibe, Gijs Dubbelman, and Daan de Geus. The key insight is that, given sufficient scale and pretraining, a plain ViT along with additional few parameters can perform segmentation without the need for task-specific decoders or pixel fusion modules. The same model backbone supports semantic, instance, and panoptic segmentation with different post-processing. The original implementation is available in a repository, and the HuggingFace model page is also available.

instruction: Create a spoken reply that sounds like it was generated in response to the previous message.
description: CSM (Conversational Speech Model) is a speech generation model that generates RVQ audio codes from text and audio inputs. The model architecture employs a backbone and a smaller audio decoder that produces audio codes. A fine-tuned variant of CSM powers a system shown in a demonstration. A hosted version is also available for testing audio generation. CSM is designed to generate audio from conversational inputs, supporting both text and audio context. It can be used to generate speech in a conversational setting, where it can take into account previous messages and audio inputs to produce more contextually appropriate outputs. CSM supports batched inference, allowing multiple prompts to be processed simultaneously. It also supports full-graph compilation with CUDA graphs, which can improve performance by reducing overhead during repeated inferences. The model can be fine-tuned using a training process that involves preparing conversational data with text and audio inputs, and using a data collator to format the inputs for training. CSM is not a general-purpose multimodal LLM and cannot generate text. It is intended to be used in conjunction with a separate LLM for text generation. The model is capable of producing a variety of voices but has not been fine-tuned on any specific voice. It has some capacity for non-English languages due to data contamination in the training data, but performance in these languages is likely to be limited. The model is provided for research and educational purposes, and its use is subject to ethical and legal constraints. It is explicitly prohibited to use the model for impersonation, fraud, misinformation, deception, or any illegal or harmful activities. The developers are not responsible for any misuse of the model.

instruction: Please analyze this image and provide a visual breakdown of the body structure.
description: The model is designed for pose estimation and is compatible with Transformers.js through ONNX weights. It supports tasks involving image processing to predict heatmaps and extract keypoints for pose estimation. The model architecture is based on the ViTPose framework, specifically the base-simple variant. It can be used with the Transformers.js library, which allows for model loading, image processing, heatmap prediction, and post-processing to obtain pose estimation results. The model outputs include keypoints and scores, which can be visualized by drawing connections between keypoints and marking each keypoint on the image. The model is intended to be used with ONNX weights as a temporary solution until WebML becomes more widely adopted. Users are advised to convert their models to ONNX format and organize their repositories with ONNX weights in a subfolder named 'onnx' to make them web-ready.

instruction: How can I adapt a model trained on city scenes to work with different types of outdoor environments?
description: This is a Keras model implementing the MixTransformer architecture, intended to be used as a backbone for the SegFormer architecture. The model is supported in both KerasCV and KerasHub, though KerasCV will no longer be actively developed, and users are encouraged to use KerasHub instead. The model includes several preset checkpoints provided by the Keras team, with weights ported from external sources. These checkpoints include mit_b0_ade20k_512 with 3.32M parameters, mit_b1_ade20k_512 with 13.16M parameters, mit_b2_ade20k_512 with 24.20M parameters, mit_b3_ade20k_512 with 44.08M parameters, mit_b4_ade20k_512 with 60.85M parameters, mit_b5_ade20k_640 with 81.45M parameters, mit_b0_cityscapes_1024 with 3.32M parameters, mit_b1_cityscapes_1024 with 13.16M parameters, mit_b2_cityscapes_1024 with 24.20M parameters, mit_b3_cityscapes_1024 with 44.08M parameters, mit_b4_cityscapes_1024 with 60.85M parameters, and mit_b5_cityscapes_1024 with 81.45M parameters. Each model is trained on either the ADE20K or Cityscapes dataset, with input resolutions of 512x512 or 1024x1024 pixels, respectively. The model supports tasks such as image segmentation and can be used with a backbone configuration. Example usage includes importing the model, preparing input data, and performing evaluation or training with a specified optimizer, loss function, and metrics.

instruction: Can you show me the same scene from a low-angle perspective, as if looking up?
description: Puffin is a unified camera-centric multimodal model that extends spatial awareness along the camera dimension. It integrates language regression and diffusion-based generation to interpret and create scenes from arbitrary viewpoints. Puffin is designed to bridge the modality gap between cameras and vision-language by treating camera as language, enabling thinking with camera. This approach aligns spatially grounded visual cues with photographic terminology while reasoning across geometric context. The model is trained on Puffin-4M, a large-scale dataset of 4 million vision-language-camera triplets, which includes both global camera parameters and pixel-wise camera maps. Puffin supports a range of tasks, including camera-centric understanding and generation from a single image or a pair of text and camera, world exploration through cross-view generation, spatial imagination based on an initial view and target camera configuration, and 3D virtual object insertion in AR/VR by calibrating camera parameters. Puffin demonstrates superior performance over specialized models for camera-centric generation and understanding. With instruction tuning, it generalizes to diverse cross-view tasks. The model is developed by Kang Liao, Size Wu, Zhonghua Wu, Linyi Jin, Chao Wang, Yikai Wang, Fei Wang, Wei Li, and Chen Change Loy, affiliated with S-Lab, Nanyang Technological University. It was first released as an arXiv pre-print in 2025. Puffin operates within a unified multimodal framework and supports the following modalities: Image → Text+Camera; Text+Camera → Image; Image+Camera → Image; Image+Camera → Text.

instruction: Could you identify the main scene or activity happening in this video clip?
description: Perception Encoder (PE) is a state-of-the-art encoder for image and video understanding, trained using a simple vision-language learning approach. It is a family of large-scale vision encoder models with state-of-the-art performance on a wide variety of vision tasks. PE is developed by Meta and uses a robust contrastive pretraining recipe, followed by finetuning on synthetically aligned videos. This approach allows PE to outperform existing models on classification and retrieval tasks, while also producing strong, general features that scale for downstream tasks. PE enables large-scale contrastive pretraining to transfer to downstream tasks through alignment tuning, leveraging those general features. The PE core is the base model, trained with a robust image pretraining schedule and finetuned on data generated by a synthetic video data engine. PE core currently comes in three sizes: B, L, and G, with G being the main checkpoint and B and L models distilled from it. The B/16 model has 0.09B vision parameters and 0.31B text parameters, with a resolution of 224px and 32 tokens. The L/14 model has 0.32B vision parameters and 0.31B text parameters, with a resolution of 336px and 32 tokens. The G/14 model has 1.88B vision parameters and 0.47B text parameters, with a resolution of 448px and 72 tokens. All PE core models use an attention pooling block with 8 heads on top of the vision tower, and the L and B models additionally have a class token for global aggregation. PE core achieves strong results on zero-shot image classification and retrieval, as well as zero-shot video classification and retrieval. For example, the B/16 model achieves 78.4% on IN-1k, 71.7% on IN-v2, 62.4% on IN-A, 71.9% on ObjectNet, 50.9% on COCO-T2I, 65.6% on Kinetics-400, and 47.6% on VTT-T2I. The L/14 model achieves 83.5% on IN-1k, 77.9% on IN-v2, 89.0% on IN-A, 84.7% on ObjectNet, 57.1% on COCO-T2I, 73.4% on Kinetics-400, and 50.3% on VTT-T2I. The G/14 model achieves 85.4% on IN-1k, 80.2% on IN-v2, 92.6% on IN-A, 88.2% on ObjectNet, 58.1% on COCO-T2I, 76.9% on Kinetics-400, and 51.2% on VTT-T2I. PE core performs particularly well on challenging benchmarks such as ObjectNet and ImageNet-A. The model can be used for image and text feature extraction, and it supports multiple configurations. It is available for use through a GitHub repository, and users can load the model using provided code. The model is based on a PyTorch implementation and requires specific dependencies for installation and use. The model is described in a technical report and is associated with two arXiv citations.

instruction: I want to know what is in this image and where each item is located.
description: The DETR (DEtection TRansformer) model is an encoder-decoder transformer with a convolutional backbone, specifically a ResNet-101 architecture. It is designed for end-to-end object detection and was trained on the COCO 2017 dataset, which includes 118,000 annotated images for training and 5,000 for validation. The model incorporates two heads on top of the decoder outputs: a linear layer for class label prediction and a multi-layer perceptron (MLP) for bounding box prediction. It uses 100 object queries to detect objects in an image, with each query searching for a specific object. The model is trained using a bipartite matching loss, which compares predicted classes and bounding boxes to ground truth annotations. The Hungarian matching algorithm is used to create a one-to-one mapping between queries and annotations, and the model is optimized using cross-entropy for class prediction and a combination of L1 and generalized IoU loss for bounding box regression. The model achieves an average precision (AP) of 43.5 on the COCO 2017 validation set. It is intended for object detection tasks and supports PyTorch. The model was trained for 300 epochs on 16 V100 GPUs, with a batch size of 64 and 4 images per GPU. Images were preprocessed by resizing and normalizing with the ImageNet mean and standard deviation. The model was introduced in the paper 'End-to-End Object Detection with Transformers' by Carion et al. and first released by the Hugging Face team, as the original model card was not written by the model's creators.

instruction: I found a track online—could you check if it's likely to be AI-generated?
description: SONICS is a model designed to distinguish between human-composed and synthetic songs, addressing the challenges posed by AI-generated music. It introduces a novel dataset for end-to-end Synthetic Song Detection (SSD), comprising over 97,000 songs (4,751 hours) with over 49,000 synthetic songs from platforms like Suno and Udio. The model emphasizes the importance of modeling long-range temporal dependencies in songs for effective authenticity detection, a feature not addressed in existing methods. To achieve this, the model introduces SpecTTTra, a novel architecture that improves time and memory efficiency over conventional CNN and Transformer-based models. For long songs, the top-performing variant of the model outperforms ViT by 8% in F1 score, is 38% faster, and uses 26% less memory, while also surpassing ConvNeXt with a 1% F1 score gain, 20% speed boost, and 67% memory reduction. The model's architecture includes an embedding dimension of 384, 6 heads, 12 layers, and an MLP ratio of 2.67. Audio processing involves a sample rate of 16kHz, an FFT size of 2048, a hop length of 512, 128 Mel bands, a frequency range of 20Hz to 8kHz, and mean-std normalization. The model is intended for use in identifying counterfeit songs where all components (vocals, music, lyrics, and style) could be AI-generated. It is important to note that existing datasets lack music-lyrics diversity, long-duration songs, and open-access fake songs, which the model aims to address.

instruction: Do you have a way to determine if this picture is a deepfake?
description: This is a Vision Transformer (ViT-Small) model trained on the largest dataset to date for detecting AI-generated images in forensic applications. It was developed by Jeongsoo Park and Andrew Owens from the University of Michigan and is based on the timm/vit_small_patch16_384.augreg_in21k_ft_in1k model. The model was trained using PyTorch 2.0 with bf16 mixed precision, the AdamW optimizer with a learning rate of 5e-5, for 10 epochs and a batch size of 32. The training dataset consists of 2.7 million images from 15+ generators and 4600+ models, totaling over 1.15TB of data. The model is licensed under the MIT license, which is compatible with the CreativeML OpenRAIL-M referenced in the associated paper. It has been adapted for Hugging Face inference compatibility by AI Without Borders. Unverified testing results indicate an accuracy of 97.2%, an F1 score of 0.968, an AUC-ROC of 0.992, and a false positive rate of 2.1%. These results are unverified due to a lack of resources to evaluate a dataset over 1.4TB in size. The model is presented as part of OpenSight, an upcoming open-source framework for adaptive deepfake detection, and is uploaded for community validation. A Hugging Face Space is planned to showcase ultra-fast inference methods and will eventually include a leaderboard. A re-sampled and refined dataset is also planned for future release. The model is associated with the paper titled 'Community Forensics: Using Thousands of Generators to Train Fake Image Detectors' by Jeongsoo Park and Andrew Owens, available on arXiv with the identifier 2411.04125.

instruction: Can you help me identify all the objects in this image and separate them into distinct segments?
description: SAM 2 is a foundation model designed for promptable visual segmentation in images and videos, developed by FAIR. It supports a wide range of segmentation tasks, including automatic mask generation, image segmentation using point clicks, bounding box inputs, and multi-object segmentation. The model can process both single images and batches of images, allowing for efficient inference across multiple inputs. It also supports video segmentation and tracking, enabling the tracking of objects across video frames with the ability to refine segmentation through additional prompts. SAM 2 can use masks from previous predictions as input to improve segmentation accuracy. The model is built to handle complex scenarios, such as batched images with multiple objects and multiple points per object, as well as batched bounding boxes. It supports streaming video inference for real-time applications and can track multiple objects simultaneously in video by adding them all at once. The model is available through the Hugging Face Transformers library and can be used with the `mask-generation` pipeline for automatic mask generation. SAM 2 is based on the Hiera architecture and is optimized for performance using mixed-precision inference with bfloat16. The model has been evaluated on a variety of tasks and is capable of generating multiple mask predictions per input, ranked by quality score. It is designed to be flexible, supporting both single and multi-object segmentation, as well as refinement through additional input points or masks. The model is intended for use in applications requiring accurate and efficient segmentation of objects in both static images and video sequences.

instruction: Could you generate a realistic continuation of the road that ends abruptly in this image?
description: ControlNet v1.1 is a neural network structure designed to control diffusion models by adding extra conditions. This specific checkpoint is conditioned on inpaint images and is intended to be used in combination with Stable Diffusion models. The model was developed by Lvmin Zhang and Maneesh Agrawala and is based on a diffusion-based text-to-image generation approach. It supports English language inputs and is licensed under a framework adapted from responsible AI licensing practices. The model allows for task-specific conditions to be learned in an end-to-end manner, and it is robust even with small training datasets. The model can be used to generate images based on conditions such as inpainting, where a masked region of an image is filled in according to a given prompt. The authors have released 14 different checkpoints, each trained to support a different type of conditioning, including canny edge detection, pixel-to-pixel instruction, multi-level line segment detection, depth estimation, surface normal estimation, image segmentation, line art generation, anime line art generation, human pose estimation, scribble-based image generation, soft edge image generation, image shuffling, and image tiling. Each checkpoint is trained to interpret a specific type of control image, such as monochrome edge images, line segments, depth maps, or segmented regions, to guide the image generation process. The model is implemented using the diffusers format and can be integrated with the Stable Diffusion pipeline for image generation tasks. The model is described in the paper titled 'Adding Conditional Control to Text-to-Image Diffusion Models' by Lvmin Zhang and Maneesh Agrawala, available on arXiv with the identifier 2302.05543.

instruction: Please create a video from this image that includes a walking person and a moving background.
description: Wan2.1 is a comprehensive and open suite of video foundation models designed to push the boundaries of video generation. It offers state-of-the-art performance, consistently outperforming existing open-source models and state-of-the-art commercial solutions across multiple benchmarks. The T2V-1.3B model requires only 8.19 GB VRAM, making it compatible with most consumer-grade GPUs. It can generate a 5-second 480P video on an RTX 4090 in about 4 minutes, with performance comparable to some closed-source models. Wan2.1 supports multiple tasks, including Text-to-Video, Image-to-Video, Video Editing, Text-to-Image, and Video-to-Audio, advancing the field of video generation. It is the first video model capable of generating both Chinese and English text, enhancing its practical applications. The model features a powerful video VAE called Wan-VAE, which delivers exceptional efficiency and performance, encoding and decoding 1080P videos of any length while preserving temporal information. Wan2.1 is built on the mainstream diffusion transformer paradigm, incorporating innovations such as a novel spatio-temporal VAE, scalable training strategies, large-scale data construction, and automated evaluation metrics. The model uses a Flow Matching framework within the Diffusion Transformer paradigm, with a T5 Encoder to encode multilingual text input and cross-attention in each transformer block to embed the text into the model structure. An MLP with a Linear layer and a SiLU layer processes the input time embeddings and predicts six modulation parameters individually, shared across all transformer blocks. The model was trained on a large-scale, curated, and deduplicated dataset of image and video data, processed through a four-step data cleaning pipeline focusing on fundamental dimensions, visual quality, and motion quality. Manual evaluations indicate that Wan2.1 outperforms both closed-source and open-source models. The model is available in different versions, including T2V-1.3B, T2V-14B, I2V-14B-480P, and I2V-14B-720P, with varying resolutions and GPU requirements. The 1.3B model can generate videos at 720P resolution, but results are less stable compared to 480P. The models are licensed under the Apache 2.0 License, and users are responsible for ensuring their usage complies with the license provisions and applicable laws.

instruction: Can you retrieve some examples of Korean healthcare policy documents from the Ministry of Health?
description: KURE-v1 is a Korean University Retrieval Embedding model that has demonstrated strong performance in Korean text retrieval, outperforming most multilingual embedding models. It is considered one of the best publicly available Korean retrieval models. The model is based on the 🤗 transformers framework and supports both Korean and English languages. It is licensed under the MIT license. KURE-v1 has a dimension of 1024 and a sequence length of 8192, and it was fine-tuned using Korean data. The model is designed to support tasks such as document retrieval and semantic similarity computation. It was trained on 2,000,000 examples of Korean query-document-hard_negative data. The training procedure used a loss function from the sentence-transformers library, with a batch size of 4096, a learning rate of 2e-05, and one epoch. Evaluation metrics include Recall, Precision, NDCG, and F1. KURE-v1 was tested on various Korean document retrieval benchmark datasets, including those related to finance, public administration, healthcare, law, commerce, and general knowledge. On average, KURE-v1 achieved a Recall_top1 of 0.52640, Precision_top1 of 0.60551, NDCG_top1 of 0.60551, and F1_top1 of 0.55784. It also showed strong performance in top-k retrieval settings, with the highest average Recall_top3 of 0.68678, Recall_top5 of 0.73851, and Recall_top10 of 0.79682. The model is available for use via the Sentence Transformers library and can be loaded using the identifier 'nlpai-lab/KURE-v1'.

instruction: I want to remove the background from a product photo. Can you provide a mask with sharp edges?
description: SAM-HQ (Segment Anything in High Quality) is an enhanced version of the Segment Anything Model (SAM) that produces higher quality object masks from input prompts such as points or boxes. While SAM was trained on a dataset of 11 million images and 1.1 billion masks, its mask prediction quality falls short in many cases, particularly when dealing with objects that have intricate structures. SAM-HQ addresses these limitations with minimal additional parameters and computation cost. The model excels at generating high-quality segmentation masks, even for objects with complex boundaries and thin structures where the original SAM model often struggles. SAM-HQ maintains SAM's original promptable design, efficiency, and zero-shot generalizability while significantly improving mask quality. SAM-HQ builds upon the original SAM architecture with two key innovations while preserving SAM's pretrained weights: a High-Quality Output Token, a learnable token injected into SAM's mask decoder that is responsible for predicting high-quality masks, and Global-local Feature Fusion, which fuses mask-decoder features with early and final ViT features for improved mask details. This combines both the high-level semantic context and low-level boundary information for more accurate segmentation. SAM-HQ was trained on a carefully curated dataset of 44K fine-grained masks (HQSeg-44K) compiled from several sources with extremely accurate annotations. The training process takes only 4 hours on 8 GPUs, introducing less than 0.5% additional parameters compared to the original SAM model. The model has been evaluated on a suite of 10 diverse segmentation datasets across different downstream tasks, with 8 of them evaluated in a zero-shot transfer protocol. Results demonstrate that SAM-HQ can produce significantly better masks than the original SAM model while maintaining its zero-shot generalization capabilities. SAM-HQ addresses two key problems with the original SAM model: coarse mask boundaries, often neglecting thin object structures, and incorrect predictions, broken masks, or large errors in challenging cases. These improvements make SAM-HQ particularly valuable for applications requiring highly accurate image masks, such as automated annotation and image/video editing tasks.

instruction: Identify the different types of plants in this indoor garden photo.
description: MM Grounding DINO (tiny variant) is a model proposed by Xiangyu Zhao, Yicheng Chen, Shilin Xu, Xiangtai Li, Xinjiang Wang, Yining Li, and Haian Huang. It improves upon previous models by enhancing the contrastive class head and removing parameter sharing in the decoder, which leads to improved zero-shot detection performance. On the COCO dataset, it achieves 50.6 AP, an improvement of 2.2 points. On the LVIS dataset, it achieves 31.9 val AP and 41.4 minival AP, improvements of 11.8 and 12.6 points, respectively. The model is designed for zero-shot object detection and can be used with the provided code example. It was trained on a combination of datasets including O365, GoldG, GRIT, and V3Det. Evaluation results show that the model with a Swin-T backbone and pre-training data of O365, GoldG, V3Det achieves 50.6 COCO mAP. On LVIS, the model with pre-training data of O365, GoldG, GRIT, and V3Det achieves 41.4 minival AP and 31.9 val AP. The model is available in the specified collection, and users are provided with a BibTeX entry for citation.

instruction: Is there a way to tell if this X-ray was taken in a PA or AP view?
description: This model performs both segmentation and classification on chest radiographs (X-rays). The model uses a tf_efficientnetv2_s backbone with a U-Net decoder for segmentation and a linear layer for classification. For frontal radiographs, the model segments the right lung, left lung, and heart. The model also predicts the chest X-ray view (AP, PA, lateral), patient age, and patient sex. The (small version) and datasets were used to train the model. Segmentation masks were obtained from the CheXmask. The final dataset comprised 335,516 images from 96,385 patients and was split into 80% training and 20% validation. A holdout test set was not used since minimal tuning was performed. The view classifier was trained only on CheXpert images (NIH images excluded from loss function), given that lateral radiographs are only present in CheXpert. This is to avoid unwanted bias in the model, which can occur if one class originates only from a single dataset. Validation performance is as follows: Segmentation (Dice similarity coefficient): Right Lung: 0.957, Left Lung: 0.948, Heart: 0.943. Age Prediction: Mean Absolute Error: 5.25 years. Classification: View (AP, PA, lateral): 99.42% accuracy, Female: 0.999 AUC. The model is for demonstration and research purposes only and has not been approved by any regulatory agency for clinical use. The user assumes any and all responsibility regarding their own use of this model and its outputs.

instruction: What insights can you provide about the base-pairing interactions in this RNA fragment?
description: ERNIE-RNA is a pre-trained model on non-coding RNA (ncRNA) using a masked language modeling (MLM) objective. It is a -style model pre-trained on a large corpus of non-coding RNA sequences in a self-supervised fashion, meaning it was trained on the raw nucleotides of RNA sequences only, with an automatic process to generate inputs and labels from those texts. The model has 12 layers, a hidden size of 768, 12 attention heads, an intermediate size of 3072, 85.67 million parameters, 22.37 gigaflops, 11.18 gigamacs, and a maximum of 1024 tokens. It was developed by Weijie Yin, Zhaoyu Zhang, Liang He, Rui Jiang, Shuo Zhang, Gan Liu, Xuegong Zhang, Tao Qin, and Zhen Xie. The model is designed to support tasks such as masked language modeling, sequence classification or regression, token classification or regression, and contact classification or regression. For these tasks, the model must be fine-tuned on a downstream task. The model file depends on the multimolecule library, which can be installed using pip. The model uses the RnaTokenizer for preprocessing RNA sequences and replaces 'T's with 'S's during preprocessing, though this behavior can be disabled. The training data was sourced from RNAcentral, a public resource of non-coding RNA sequences, with redundancy removed using a 100% sequence identity cutoff, resulting in 25 million unique sequences. Sequences longer than 1024 nucleotides were excluded, leaving a final dataset of 20.4 million non-redundant RNA sequences. The model was trained using masked language modeling, where 15% of the tokens are masked, with 80% replaced by [MASK], 10% by a random token, and 10% left unchanged. The training was conducted on 24 NVIDIA V100 GPUs with 32GiB memories, using a learning rate of 1e-4, a warm-up of 20,000 steps, and a weight decay of 0.01. The model is licensed under the AGPL-3.0-or-later license. The model card was written by the MultiMolecule team, not the original authors, and the official repository of ERNIE-RNA is available elsewhere. The MultiMolecule team has confirmed that the provided model and checkpoints produce the same intermediate representations as the original implementation. The model is not fine-tuned for any specific task and must be adapted for use in downstream applications. The model is described in a paper titled 'ERNIE-RNA: An RNA Language Model with Structure-enhanced Representations' published in bioRxiv. The model is intended to provide comprehensive RNA structural representations and has achieved state-of-the-art performance after fine-tuning for various downstream tasks, including RNA structural and functional predictions. The model incorporates base-pairing restriction in its architecture to enhance RNA structural modeling without the use of multiple sequence alignment information.

instruction: Could you help me identify the artist of this song using just the audio?
description: The m-a-p model family is a series of pre-trained models for music audio understanding, developed with various pre-training paradigms and datasets. The models are designed to support a range of music-related tasks and are optimized for generalization across different functions. The MERT-v1 model, an updated version of MERT-v0, introduces several improvements including the use of 8 codebooks for pseudo labels, which enhances the model's ability to support music generation. It also incorporates in-batch noise mixture during MLM prediction, is trained with higher audio frequency (24K Hz), and uses a larger dataset of up to 160,000 hours of audio. The model is available in multiple sizes, including 95M and 330M parameters, to accommodate different hardware capabilities. The architecture is based on transformer layers, with the number of layers and feature dimensions varying across models. The feature rate, or the number of features output per second of audio, and the sample rate, or the frequency of audio the model is trained with, are also key configuration parameters. The models are evaluated based on their performance on downstream tasks, with larger models generally outperforming smaller ones. The MERT-v1 model was released on 17/03/2023 and is trained using the MLM paradigm. Earlier versions, such as the model released on 29/10/2022, were trained using the BYOL paradigm. The models are intended for use in applications requiring music understanding, and users are advised to select the appropriate model size and configuration based on their specific needs and hardware constraints.

instruction: Is there a way to see the logic behind how the system segments different objects in an image?
description: Seg-Zero-7B is a model that introduces a novel framework for reasoning segmentation, addressing the limitations of traditional supervised fine-tuning methods, which often struggle with out-of-domain generalization and lack explicit reasoning processes. The framework features a decoupled architecture consisting of a reasoning model and a segmentation model. The reasoning model interprets user intentions, generates explicit reasoning chains, and produces positional prompts, which are subsequently used by the segmentation model to generate precise pixel-level masks. Seg-Zero is trained exclusively via reinforcement learning with GRPO, without explicit reasoning data, achieving robust zero-shot generalization and emergent test-time reasoning capabilities. A sophisticated reward mechanism integrating both format and accuracy rewards guides the optimization. Experiments show that Seg-Zero-7B achieves a zero-shot performance of 57.5 on the ReasonSeg benchmark, surpassing the prior LISA-7B by 18%. Seg-Zero demonstrates the following key features: it generates a reasoning chain before producing the final segmentation mask; it is trained exclusively using reinforcement learning, without any explicit supervised reasoning data; and it achieves superior performance on both in-domain and out-of-domain data compared to supervised fine-tuning methods. The model is based on EasyR1 and veRL, which supports model split during sampling and is more GPU memory friendly. It supports both Qwen2-VL and Qwen2.5-VL series models. It already implements commonly used rewards in Object Detection and Object Segmentation, including IoU reward and L1 reward. Seg-Zero employs a decoupled architecture, including a reasoning model and segmentation model. A sophisticated reward mechanism is manually designed to integrate both the format and accuracy rewards. The model generates several samples, calculates the rewards, and then optimizes towards samples that achieve higher rewards. The recommended training requirement for the 7B model is a 4x80G GPUs server or an 8x46G GPUs server. The GRPO algorithm is used for training, and hyper-parameters can be adjusted based on GPU memory availability. The model is evaluated using the ReasonSeg benchmark, and the best results on different benchmarks are evaluated using different checkpoints. For evaluation, all available checkpoints are assessed, and their values are recorded. The model is based on the paper titled 'Seg-Zero: Reasoning-Chain Guided Segmentation via Cognitive Reinforcement' and 'VisionReasoner: Unified Visual Perception and Reasoning via Reinforcement Learning'.

instruction: Visualize a scene of a haunted house at midnight with fog, creaking doors, and flickering lights.
description: This repository contains the code for a dilated controlnet module designed for the Wan2.1 model. The dilated controlnet includes fewer basic blocks and introduces a `stride` parameter. For the Wan1.3B model, the controlnet consists of 8 blocks with a stride of 3. The model is intended to support tasks involving video generation with control over visual features, using a hed controlnet type. It can be used for inference through command-line interface or Gradio-based web interface. The model accepts video input and text prompts to generate video outputs with specified parameters such as controlnet weight, guidance start and end, number of inference steps, guidance scale, video resolution, number of frames, and negative prompts to avoid undesired visual elements. The model is based on the Wan-AI/Wan2.1-T2V-1.3B-Diffusers base model and uses TheDenk/wan2.1-t2v-1.3b-controlnet-hed-v1 as the controlnet model. The model includes detailed configuration options for inference, including video height, width, frame count, output frame rate, and output path. The model is developed by Karachev Denis and is available on Github. Issues should be reported directly in the repository, and professional support can be requested via email.

instruction: How can I interpret the depth of the river in this aerial view?
description: DPT-Large, also known as MiDaS 3.0, is a Dense Prediction Transformer (DPT) model trained on 1.4 million images for monocular depth estimation. It was introduced in the paper by Ranftl et al. (2021) and first released in March 2022. DPT uses the Vision Transformer (ViT) as a backbone and adds a neck and head on top for monocular depth estimation. The model is designed for zero-shot monocular depth estimation and can be used by anyone performing this task. It is typically intended to be fine-tuned for specific applications and should not be used to intentionally create hostile or alienating environments for people. The model was trained on a dataset called MIX 6, which contains around 1.4 million images, and was initialized with ImageNet-pretrained weights. During training, images were resized such that the longer side was 384 pixels, and random square crops of size 384 were used, with random horizontal flips for data augmentation. The model was evaluated on several benchmarks, including DIW WHDR, ETH3D AbsRel, Sintel AbsRel, KITTI δ>1.25, NYU δ>1.25, and TUM δ>1.25, achieving results of 10.82, 0.089, 0.270, 8.46, 8.32, and 9.97, respectively. The model is available under the Apache 2.0 license. The training and inference were conducted on an Intel Xeon Platinum 8280 CPU with 8 physical cores and an NVIDIA RTX 2080 GPU. Users should be made aware of the risks, biases, and limitations of the model. The model is not intended to inform decisions central to human life or flourishing and is an aggregated set of monocular depth image datasets. No additional risk mitigation strategies were considered during model development, and the extent of the risks involved by using the model remains unknown.

instruction: Please transcribe this audio and then translate it into Spanish.
description: Granite-speech-3.3-8b is a compact and efficient speech-language model specifically designed for automatic speech recognition (ASR) and automatic speech translation (AST). It uses a two-pass design, where the first pass transcribes audio files into text, and the second pass processes the transcribed text using the underlying Granite language model. Each step must be explicitly initiated by the user. The model was trained on a collection of public corpora comprising diverse datasets for ASR and AST, as well as synthetic datasets tailored to support the speech translation task. It was trained by modality aligning granite-3.3-8b-instruct to speech on publicly available open source corpora containing audio inputs and text targets. Compared to revision 3.3.1, revision 3.3.2 supports multilingual speech inputs in English, French, German, Spanish, and Portuguese and provides additional accuracy improvements for English ASR. Compared to the initial release, revision 3.3.2 is also trained on additional data and uses a deeper acoustic encoder for improved transcription accuracy. The model was evaluated alongside other speech-language models in the less than 8b parameter range as well as dedicated ASR and AST systems on standard benchmarks. The evaluation spanned multiple public benchmarks, with particular emphasis on English ASR tasks while also including multilingual ASR and AST for X-En and En-X translations. The model is intended to be used in enterprise applications that involve processing of speech inputs. It is well-suited for English, French, German, Spanish, and Portuguese speech-to-text and speech translations to and from English for the same languages plus English-to-Japanese and English-to-Mandarin. The model can also be used for tasks that involve text-only input since it calls the underlying granite-3.3-8b-instruct when the user specifies a prompt that does not contain audio. The architecture of granite-speech-3.3-8b revision 3.3.2 consists of a speech encoder with 16 conformer blocks trained with CTC on character-level targets, a speech projector and temporal downsampler using a 2-layer window query transformer, a large language model (granite-3.3-8b-instruct with 128k context length), and LoRA adapters with rank=64 applied to the query and value projection matrices. The model was trained on a combination of publicly available datasets and synthetic data created from publicly available datasets specifically targeting the speech translation task. The training data includes datasets such as CommonVoice-17, MLS, Librispeech, VoxPopuli, AMI, YODAS, Earnings-22, Switchboard, CallHome, Fisher, and Voicemail, among others. The model was trained using IBM's super computing cluster, Blue Vela, equipped with NVIDIA H100 GPUs, and the training was completed in 13 days on 32 H100 GPUs. The use of large speech and language models can trigger certain risks and ethical considerations. Although alignment processes include safety considerations, the model may in some cases produce inaccurate, biased, offensive, or unwanted responses to user prompts. Smaller models may exhibit increased susceptibility to hallucination in generation scenarios due to their reduced sizes, which could limit their ability to generate coherent and contextually accurate responses. This aspect is an active area of research. IBM recommends using this model for ASR and translation tasks. The model's modular design improves safety by limiting how audio inputs can influence the system. If an unfamiliar or malformed prompt is received, the model simply echoes it with its transcription, minimizing the risk of adversarial inputs. More general speech tasks may pose higher inherent risks of triggering unwanted outputs. To enhance safety, IBM recommends using granite-speech-3.3-8b alongside Granite Guardian, a fine-tuned instruct model designed to detect and flag risks in prompts and responses across key dimensions outlined in the IBM AI Risk Atlas.

instruction: Identify and count the number of people in this photo.
description: MM Grounding DINO (base variant) was proposed by Xiangyu Zhao, Yicheng Chen, Shilin Xu, Xiangtai Li, Xinjiang Wang, Yining Li, and Haian Huang. It improves upon previous models by enhancing the contrastive class head and removing parameter sharing in the decoder, which leads to improved zero-shot detection performance. Specifically, it achieves 50.6 AP (an increase of 2.2) on COCO and 31.9 val AP (an increase of 11.8) and 41.4 minival AP (an increase of 12.6) on LVIS. The model is designed for zero-shot object detection and can be used with raw inputs. It was trained on a combination of datasets including O365, GoldG, GRIT, and V3Det. Evaluation results show that the model performs well across different backbones and pre-training data combinations. For example, with a Swin-T backbone and pre-training data of O365, GoldG, and V3Det, it achieves 50.6 COCO mAP. On LVIS, the model achieves 41.4 minival AP and 31.9 val AP when trained on O365, GoldG, GRIT, and V3Det. The model is available in the collection under the identifier rziga/mm_grounding_dino_base_o365v1_goldg_v3det.

instruction: Can you help identify if this audio file was generated using AI music tools like Suno or Udio?
description: SONICS is a model designed to distinguish between human-composed and synthetic songs, addressing the challenges posed by AI-generated music. It introduces a novel dataset for end-to-end Synthetic Song Detection (SSD), comprising over 97,000 songs (4,751 hours) with over 49,000 synthetic songs from platforms like Suno and Udio. The model emphasizes the importance of modeling long-range temporal dependencies in songs for effective authenticity detection, a feature absent in existing methods. To achieve this, the model introduces SpecTTTra, a novel architecture that improves time and memory efficiency over conventional CNN and Transformer-based models. For long songs, the top-performing variant of the model outperforms ViT by 8% in F1 score, is 38% faster, and uses 26% less memory, while also surpassing ConvNeXt with a 1% F1 score gain, 20% speed boost, and 67% memory reduction. The model's architecture includes an embedding dimension of 384, 6 heads, 12 layers, and an MLP ratio of 2.67. Audio processing involves a sample rate of 16kHz, an FFT size of 2048, a hop length of 512, 128 Mel bands, a frequency range of 20Hz to 8kHz, and mean-std normalization. The model is intended for use in identifying counterfeit songs and safeguarding artistic integrity in the context of AI-generated music.

instruction: Can you create a detailed depth estimation for this image of a park?
description: Depth Pro is a foundation model for zero-shot metric monocular depth estimation. It synthesizes high-resolution depth maps with sharpness and high-frequency details. The model produces metric depth predictions with absolute scale, without requiring metadata such as camera intrinsics. It is capable of generating a 2.25-megapixel depth map in 0.3 seconds on a standard GPU. These capabilities are enabled by an efficient multi-scale vision transformer for dense prediction, a training protocol that combines real and synthetic datasets to achieve high metric accuracy and fine boundary tracing, dedicated evaluation metrics for boundary accuracy in depth maps, and state-of-the-art focal length estimation from a single image. The model was introduced by Aleksei Bochkovskii, Amaël Delaunoy, Hugo Germain, Marcel Santos, Yichao Zhou, Stephan R. Richter, and Vladlen Koltun. The checkpoint provided in this repository is a reference implementation that has been re-trained; its performance is close to the model reported in the paper but does not match it exactly.

instruction: I have an image of a person — can you extract just the person with a clean background?
description: BEN (Background Erase Network) is a model designed for foreground segmentation using a Confidence Guided Matting (CGM) pipeline. The architecture includes a refiner network that processes pixels where the base model has lower confidence, leading to more accurate matting results. BEN2, an enhanced version, is publicly available and was trained on the DIS5k dataset and a proprietary 22K segmentation dataset. It supports tasks such as hair matting, 4K processing, object segmentation, and edge refinement. The base model is available for free commercial use on HuggingFace. The model generates both a binary mask and a foreground image from input images and supports CUDA for GPU acceleration. Performance metrics for the BEN_Base model on the Disk 5k evaluation include a MAE of 0.0309, DICE of 0.8806, IOU of 0.8371, BER of 0.0516, and ACC of 0.9718. The BEN_Base combined with the commercial refiner model achieves a MAE of 0.0270, DICE of 0.8989, IOU of 0.8506, BER of 0.0496, and ACC of 0.9740. The model is implemented in Python and can be integrated using a simple API. Users are required to clone the repository and install the dependencies to use the model.

instruction: List the items shown in this product catalog page.
description: Qwen2.5-VL is a vision-language model designed to understand and interact with visual and textual content. It is part of the Qwen family and builds upon the Qwen2-VL model with several key enhancements. The model is capable of recognizing common objects and analyzing texts, charts, icons, graphics, and layouts within images. It functions as a visual agent that can reason and dynamically direct tools, enabling it to perform computer and phone use. Qwen2.5-VL can understand long videos, up to one hour in length, and can pinpoint relevant video segments to capture events. It supports visual localization by generating bounding boxes or points and provides structured outputs for data such as scanned invoices, forms, and tables, which is beneficial for applications in finance and commerce. The model architecture includes dynamic resolution and frame rate training for video understanding, which allows the model to comprehend videos at various sampling rates. The model also features a streamlined and efficient vision encoder with window attention in the ViT architecture, optimized with SwiGLU and RMSNorm to align with the Qwen2.5 LLM structure. The model is available in three versions with 3, 7, and 72 billion parameters, with the 7B version being instruction-tuned and available in this repository. Qwen2.5-VL has been evaluated on various benchmarks, including image, video, and agent benchmarks, where it demonstrates strong performance across multiple metrics. The model supports a wide range of input formats for images and videos, including local files, URLs, and base64-encoded data. It also provides options for adjusting image resolution and handling long texts through techniques like YaRN for length extrapolation. However, it is noted that YaRN can impact the performance of temporal and spatial localization tasks and is not recommended for use in such scenarios.

instruction: Can you clarify the main points of this confusing legal document?
description: Gemma is a family of lightweight, state-of-the-art open models from Google, built from the same research and technology used to create the Gemini models. Gemma 3 models are multimodal, handling text and image input and generating text output, with open weights for both pre-trained variants and instruction-tuned variants. Gemma 3 has a large, 128K context window, multilingual support in over 140 languages, and is available in more sizes than previous versions. Gemma 3 models are well-suited for a variety of text generation and image understanding tasks, including question answering, summarization, and reasoning. Their relatively small size makes it possible to deploy them in environments with limited resources such as laptops, desktops or your own cloud infrastructure, democratizing access to state of the art AI models and helping foster innovation for everyone. The input includes text strings such as questions, prompts, or documents to be summarized, and images normalized to 896 x 896 resolution and encoded to 256 tokens each. The total input context is 128K tokens for the 4B, 12B, and 27B sizes, and 32K tokens for the 1B size. The output is generated text in response to the input, such as an answer to a question, analysis of image content, or a summary of a document, with a total output context of 8192 tokens. The models were trained on a dataset of text data that includes a wide variety of sources. The 27B model was trained with 14 trillion tokens, the 12B model with 12 trillion tokens, the 4B model with 4 trillion tokens, and the 1B model with 2 trillion tokens. The training dataset includes web documents, code, mathematics, and images. Web documents ensure the model is exposed to a broad range of linguistic styles, topics, and vocabulary, with content in over 140 languages. Code helps the model learn the syntax and patterns of programming languages, improving its ability to generate and understand code. Mathematics helps the model learn logical reasoning, symbolic representation, and to address mathematical queries. Images enable the model to perform image analysis and visual data extraction tasks. Rigorous CSAM filtering was applied at multiple stages in the data preparation process to ensure the exclusion of harmful and illegal content. Automated techniques were used to filter out certain personal information and other sensitive data from training sets. Additional methods were applied for content quality and safety in line with safety policies. Gemma was trained using Tensor Processing Unit (TPU) hardware (TPUv4p, TPUv5p, and TPUv5e). TPUs are specifically designed to handle the massive computations involved in training vision-language models (VLMs), offering performance, memory, scalability, and cost-effectiveness advantages. Training was done using JAX and ML Pathways. JAX allows researchers to take advantage of the latest generation of hardware, including TPUs, for faster and more efficient training of large models. ML Pathways is Google's effort to build artificially intelligent systems capable of generalizing across multiple tasks, suitable for foundation models like these. The models were evaluated against a large collection of different datasets and metrics to cover different aspects of text generation. In reasoning and factuality, the models showed performance across various benchmarks such as HellaSwag, BoolQ, PIQA, SocialIQA, TriviaQA, Natural Questions, ARC, WinoGrande, BIG-Bench Hard, and DROP. In STEM and code, the models were evaluated on MMLU, AGIEval, MATH, GSM8K, GPQA, MBPP, and HumanEval. In multilingual tasks, the models were evaluated on MGSM, Global-MMLU-Lite, WMT24++, FloRes, XQuAD, ECLeKTic, and IndicGenBench. In multimodal tasks, the models were evaluated on COCOcap, DocVQA, InfoVQA, MMMU, TextVQA, RealWorldQA, ReMI, AI2D, ChartQA, VQAv2, BLINK, OKVQA, TallyQA, SpatialSense VQA, and CountBenchQA. The evaluation methods included structured evaluations and internal red-teaming testing of relevant content policies. Red-teaming was conducted by different teams with different goals and human evaluation metrics. The models were evaluated against categories such as child safety, content safety, and representational harms. Evaluation results showed major improvements in these categories relative to previous Gemma models. All testing was conducted without safety filters to evaluate the model capabilities and behaviors. The models produced minimal policy violations and showed significant improvements over previous models' performance with respect to ungrounded inferences. A limitation of the evaluations was that they included only English language prompts. The models have certain limitations that users should be aware of. The quality and diversity of the training data significantly influence the model's capabilities. Biases or gaps in the training data can lead to limitations in the model's responses. The scope of the training dataset determines the subject areas the model can handle effectively. Models are better at tasks that can be framed with clear prompts and instructions. Open-ended or highly complex tasks might be challenging. A model's performance can be influenced by the amount of context provided. Natural language is inherently complex, and models might struggle to grasp subtle nuances, sarcasm, or figurative language. Models generate responses based on information they learned from their training datasets, but they are not knowledge bases and may generate incorrect or outdated factual statements. Models rely on statistical patterns in language and might lack the ability to apply common sense reasoning in certain situations. The development of vision-language models raises several ethical concerns. VLMs trained on large-scale, real-world text and image data can reflect socio-cultural biases embedded in the training material. VLMs can be misused to generate text that is false, misleading, or harmful. Guidelines are provided for responsible use with the model. Transparency and accountability are emphasized, with details on the models' architecture, capabilities, limitations, and evaluation processes. A responsibly developed open model offers the opportunity to share innovation by making VLM technology accessible to developers and researchers across the AI ecosystem. Risks identified include the perpetuation of biases, generation of harmful content, misuse for malicious purposes, and privacy violations. Mitigations include continuous monitoring, content safety safeguards, developer and end-user education, and adherence to privacy regulations. At the time of release, this family of models provides high-performance open vision-language model implementations designed for responsible AI development compared to similarly sized models. Using the benchmark evaluation metrics described, these models have shown to provide superior performance to other, comparably-sized open model alternatives.

instruction: What would be the best choice to fill in the masked token in this technical explanation?
description: ModernBERT-Ja-70M is a variant of the BERT model that combines local and global attention mechanisms, enabling it to process long sequences while maintaining computational efficiency. It incorporates modern architectural improvements. The model is trained on a high-quality corpus of Japanese and English text containing 4.39T tokens, with a vocabulary size of 102,400 and a sequence length of 8,192 tokens. It is designed for masked language modeling and is intended to be fine-tuned for downstream tasks. It is not suitable for text generation, and users are advised to use a dedicated text generation model for such purposes. The model uses a unigram language model tokenizer with byte fallback, and does not apply pre-tokenization for Japanese, allowing raw sentences to be input directly. However, the token boundaries often do not align with morpheme boundaries, which may lead to poor performance in token classification tasks such as named entity recognition and span extraction. The model was constructed through a three-stage training process: pre-training with 3.51T tokens using a sequence length of 1,024 and a masking rate of 30%, followed by two phases of context length extension. In the first context extension phase, it was trained with 430B tokens at a sequence length of 8,192 and a masking rate of 30%. In the second phase, it was trained with 450B tokens, including 150B tokens of high-quality Japanese data, at a sequence length of 8,192 and a reduced masking rate of 15%. The model has 70M total parameters, with 31M parameters excluding the embedding layer. It has a hidden dimension of 384, an intermediate dimension of 1,536, and 13 layers. The attention configuration includes one global attention layer and two local attention layers. The sliding window attention window context size is 128, with global_rope_theta set to 160,000 and local_rope_theta set to 10,000. The model was evaluated on 12 datasets, including JGLUE, across various tasks such as knowledge-based tasks, Japanese linguistic acceptability classification, natural language inference, semantic textual similarity, and various classification tasks. These tasks are short-sequence evaluations with a maximum sequence length not exceeding 512. The model achieved an average performance of 86.77 across these tasks. The model is available for use with the transformers library version 4.48.0 or higher, and it is recommended to use Flash Attention 2 if supported by the GPU. The model may produce representations that reflect biases, and it may generate biased or harmful expressions when used for masked language modeling.

instruction: How can I create a multilingual chatbot that understands queries in different languages?
description: Granite-Embedding-278M-Multilingual is a 278 million parameter model from the Granite Embeddings suite designed to generate high-quality text embeddings. It produces embedding vectors of size 768 and is trained using a combination of open source relevance-pair datasets with permissive, enterprise-friendly licenses and IBM-collected and generated datasets. The model is developed using contrastive fine-tuning, knowledge distillation, and model merging for improved performance. It supports 12 languages: English, German, Spanish, French, Japanese, Portuguese, Arabic, Czech, Italian, Korean, Dutch, and Chinese. Users may fine-tune the model for additional languages. The model is intended to produce fixed-length vector representations for a given text, which can be used for text similarity, retrieval, and search applications. It is compatible with the SentenceTransformer library and the Huggingface Transformers library. The model is based on an encoder-only XLM-RoBERTa-like transformer architecture, trained internally at IBM Research. It has 12 layers, 12 attention heads, an intermediate size of 3072, a vocabulary size of 250002, a maximum sequence length of 512, and uses the GeLU activation function. The training data includes unsupervised title-body paired data from the web, publicly available paired data with permissive licenses, IBM-internal paired data targeting specific technical domains, and IBM-generated synthetic data. The training data consists of 52,823,484 pairs from Multilingual MC4, 12,369,322 pairs from Multilingual Webhose, 20,745,403 pairs from English Wikipedia, 2,911,090 pairs from Multilingual Wikimedia, 10,120,398 pairs from Miracl Corpus, 304,525 pairs from Stack Exchange duplicate questions (titles), 250,519 pairs from Stack Exchange duplicate questions (bodies), 187,195 pairs from machine-translated Stack Exchange duplicate questions (titles), 4,067,139 pairs from Stack Exchange (Title, Answer) pairs, 23,978,013 pairs from Stack Exchange (Title, Body) pairs, 1,827,15 pairs from machine-translated Stack Exchange (Title+Body, Answer) pairs, 582,261 pairs from SearchQA, 41,769,185 pairs from S2ORC, 77,427,422 pairs from WikiAnswers duplicate question pairs, 614,664 pairs from CCNews, 226,711 pairs from XSum, 102,225 pairs from SimpleWiki, 28,376,115 pairs from machine-translated cross-lingual parallel corpora, 684,100 pairs from SPECTER citation triplets, 4,104,600 pairs from machine-translated SPECTER citation triplets, 100,231 pairs from Natural Questions, 87,599 pairs from SQuAD2.0, 85,000 pairs from HotpotQA, 109,810 pairs from Fever, 20,000,000 pairs from PubMed, 81,409 pairs from Multilingual Miracl Triples, 48,715 pairs from Multilingual MrTydi Triples, 4,037 pairs from Sadeeem Question Answering, 4,635,922 pairs from DBPedia Title-Body Pairs, 1,879,093 pairs from synthetic English Query-Wikipedia Passage, 9,888 pairs from synthetic English Fact Verification, 300,266 pairs from synthetic Multilingual Query-Wikipedia Passage, 37,489 pairs from synthetic Multilingual News Summaries, 40,290 pairs from IBM Internal Triples, and 1,524,586 pairs from IBM Internal Title-Body Pairs. The model is trained using IBM's Cognitive Compute Cluster, which is equipped with NVIDIA A100 80GB GPUs. The data used to train the base language model was filtered to remove text containing hate, abuse, and profanity. The model is fine-tuned on 12 languages and has a context length of 512 tokens, with longer texts being truncated to this size.
