The average performance of the model on Multilingual Miracl (across 18 languages), Mintaka Retrieval (across 8 languages), and MTEB Retrieval for English (across 15 tasks), German (across 4 tasks), Spanish (across 2 tasks), French (across 5 tasks), Japanese (across 2 tasks), Arabic (1 task), Korean (1 task), and Chinese (across 8 tasks) is reported as follows: 58.3 on Miracl, 23.2 on Mintaka Retrieval, 48.2 on MTEB English, 71.2 on MTEB German, 52.6 on MTEB Spanish, 54.1 on MTEB French, 61.7 on MTEB Japanese, 64.2 on MTEB Arabic, 71.8 on MTEB Korean, and 45.2 on MTEB Chinese. The model does not use the MS-MARCO retrieval dataset in its training corpus due to its non-commercial license, while other open-source models train on this dataset due to its high quality.

instruction: I need a Turkish version of the English recipe instructions. Can you help?
description: The opus-mt-tc-big-en-tr is a neural machine translation model designed to translate from English (en) to Turkish (tr). It is part of an initiative to make neural machine translation models widely available and accessible for many languages. The model was originally trained using the efficient NMT implementation written in pure C++ and has been converted to pyTorch using the transformers library by Hugging Face. The training data is sourced from the OPUSTCv20210807+bt dataset, and the training pipelines follow established procedures. The model uses the transformer-big architecture and applies SentencePiece tokenization with spm32k for both source and target languages. The model was released on 2022-02-25. It supports translation tasks and can be used with the Hugging Face Transformers library, either directly through model and tokenizer classes or via translation pipelines. The model has been evaluated on several test sets, achieving chr-F scores of 0.68726 on the tatoeba-test-v2021-08-07 dataset, 0.62829 on the flores101-devtest dataset, 0.58947 on the newsdev2016 dataset, 0.57624 on the newstest2016 dataset, 0.58858 on the newstest2017 dataset, and 0.57848 on the newstest2018 dataset. Corresponding BLEU scores are 42.3, 31.4, 21.9, 23.4, 25.4, and 22.6, respectively. The model conversion was performed using transformers version 4.16.2, with the OPUS-MT git hash 3405783, on April 13, 2022. The work is supported by multiple grants, including one funded by the European Research Council (ERC) under the European Union’s Horizon 2020 research and innovation programme (grant agreement No 771113) and another under grant agreement No 780069. Computational resources and IT infrastructure were provided by an institution in Finland.

instruction: What are the most noticeable sounds in this recording, and what might they indicate?
description: This is the fine-tuned version of the EAT-base model, further trained on the AS-2M dataset. Compared to the pre-trained model, this version provides enhanced audio representations and typically yields better performance in downstream audio understanding tasks such as classification and captioning. The model supports both frame-level (~50Hz) and utterance-level (CLS token) representations. It is designed for feature extraction and is intended for use in audio understanding tasks. The model is based on the EAT framework, which is described in the associated paper titled 'EAT: Self-supervised pre-training with efficient audio transformer' by Chen, Wenxi and Liang, Yuzhe and Ma, Ziyang and Zheng, Zhisheng and Chen, Xie, published as an arXiv preprint in 2024.

instruction: Can you detect the position of the sun in this outdoor image?
description: MM Grounding DINO (large variant) was proposed by Xiangyu Zhao, Yicheng Chen, Shilin Xu, Xiangtai Li, Xinjiang Wang, Yining Li, and Haian Huang. It improves upon previous models by enhancing the contrastive class head and removing parameter sharing in the decoder, which leads to improved zero-shot detection performance. Specifically, it achieves 50.6 AP (an increase of 2.2) on COCO and 31.9 val AP (an increase of 11.8) and 41.4 minival AP (an increase of 12.6) on LVIS. The model is intended for zero-shot object detection and can be used with the provided code example. It was trained on a combination of O365V2, OpenImageV6, and GoldG datasets. Evaluation results show that the model achieves 53.0 COCO mAP with a Swin-L backbone and 41.4 minival AP with a Swin-T backbone when trained on O365, GoldG, GRIT, and V3Det. The model is available in the specified collection, and all original checkpoints can be found there.

instruction: Visualize a 3D model of a floating island with waterfalls and ruins.
description: This project presents an experimental pipeline that connects natural language prompts to 3D model generation using a modified version of a pre-trained multi-view diffusion model. It is part of a final year project for the Comprehensive Creative Technologies Project at UWE Bristol, with the goal of exploring the potential of AI-assisted 3D content creation through natural language input. The model is based on a pre-trained model developed for the ML for 3D Course by researchers at Google Research. This project does not claim authorship of the model architecture or training process but serves as a custom wrapper for experimentation with text-to-3D workflows. The model allows input of a natural language description, internally maps the input to a representative image or multi-view description, and generates a 3D model using the LGM pipeline. It is a prototype intended for academic use only, with limited ability to handle complex or abstract text. The performance and quality of the model depend entirely on the base pre-trained model. The project acknowledges Hugging Face and the authors of LGM for making their models publicly available. The author is Gordon CHIN HO AU, a final year BSc Digital Media student at the University of the West of England, Bristol.

instruction: Could you generate a depth map with fine boundary details for this indoor scene?
description: Depth Pro is a foundation model for zero-shot metric monocular depth estimation. It synthesizes high-resolution depth maps with sharpness and high-frequency details. The model produces metric depth predictions with absolute scale, without requiring metadata such as camera intrinsics. It is capable of generating a 2.25-megapixel depth map in 0.3 seconds on a standard GPU. These capabilities are enabled by an efficient multi-scale vision transformer for dense prediction, a training protocol that combines real and synthetic datasets to achieve high metric accuracy and fine boundary tracing, dedicated evaluation metrics for boundary accuracy in depth maps, and state-of-the-art focal length estimation from a single image. The checkpoint provided in this repository is a reference implementation that has been re-trained; its performance is close to the model reported in the paper but does not match it exactly.

instruction: What key visual characteristics can you identify in this portrait?
description: The Vision Transformer (ViT) is a transformer encoder model, similar in design to BERT, designed for supervised image classification on ImageNet. It has been adapted for self-supervised image feature extraction, allowing the model to learn meaningful features (embeddings) from images without requiring labeled data. The DINOv2 version of the ViT introduces a modification to address artifacts in attention maps, which are caused by the use of image patches as 'registers'. The authors propose adding new tokens, called 'register' tokens, during pre-training, which are discarded afterward. This modification results in no artifacts, interpretable attention maps, and improved performance. The model does not include any fine-tuned heads and is intended for pre-training to learn an internal representation of images. These representations can be used for downstream tasks, such as training a standard classifier by placing a linear layer on top of the pre-trained encoder, typically using the [CLS] token's last hidden state as a representation of the entire image. The model can be used for feature extraction, and users are encouraged to look for fine-tuned versions for specific tasks.

instruction: What is the Italian phrase for 'I miss you'?
description: LlaMaestra is a small Llama model fine-tuned for text translation between English and Italian. It was trained using approximately 300,000 examples of English-to-Italian and Italian-to-English translations. The fine-tuning process was designed to enable the model to provide direct translations with minimal explanation. Training was completed in about 10 hours on an A10G Nvidia GPU. The model is optimized for performance on CPUs due to its compact size. The base model used is `meta-llama/Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct`, and the fine-tuning was conducted by Leonard Püttmann. The training data consists of sentence pairs sourced from tatoeba.com.

instruction: Produce an image of a bustling street market in an ancient Middle Eastern city.
description: SDXL-Turbo is a fast generative text-to-image model that can synthesize photorealistic images from a text prompt in a single network evaluation. It is a distilled version of a larger model, trained for real-time synthesis. The model is based on a novel training method called Adversarial Diffusion Distillation (ADD), which allows sampling large-scale foundational image diffusion models in 1 to 4 steps at high image quality. This approach uses score distillation to leverage large-scale off-the-shelf image diffusion models as a teacher signal and combines this with an adversarial loss to ensure high image fidelity even in the low-step regime of one or two sampling steps. SDXL-Turbo is developed and funded by Stability AI. The model is intended for both non-commercial and commercial usage, including research on generative models, real-time applications of generative models, the impact of real-time generative models, safe deployment of models that can generate harmful content, probing and understanding the limitations and biases of generative models, generation of artworks and use in design and other artistic processes, and applications in educational or creative tools. The model is not intended for use in generating factual or true representations of people or events, and such use is considered out-of-scope. The model has several limitations, including the generation of images at a fixed resolution of 512x512 pixels, inability to render legible text, potential improper generation of faces and people, and a lossy autoencoding component. The model supports text-to-image and image-to-image generation tasks. For image-to-image generation, it is important that the product of num_inference_steps and strength is greater than or equal to 1. The model does not use guidance_scale or negative_prompt and is best used with guidance_scale set to 0.0. A single inference step is sufficient to generate high-quality images. The model is intended for use with the diffusers library and can be loaded using the AutoPipelineForText2Image or AutoPipelineForImage2Image classes. The model is not trained to be factual or true representations of people or events, and therefore using the model to generate such content is out-of-scope for the abilities of this model. The model should not be used in any way that violates Stability AI's policies.

instruction: How do I start learning about artificial intelligence and machine learning?
description: Qwen2.5 is the latest series of Qwen large language models. The Qwen2.5 series includes a range of base language models and instruction-tuned language models with parameter sizes ranging from 0.5 to 72 billion. Qwen2.5 introduces several improvements over Qwen2, including significantly more knowledge and enhanced capabilities in coding and mathematics, achieved through specialized expert models in these domains. It also shows significant improvements in instruction following, generating long texts (over 8K tokens), understanding structured data such as tables, and generating structured outputs, especially JSON. The model is more resilient to the diversity of system prompts, which enhances role-play implementation and condition-setting for chatbots. It supports long-context processing up to 128K tokens and can generate up to 8K tokens. The model provides multilingual support for over 29 languages, including Chinese, English, French, Spanish, Portuguese, German, Italian, Russian, Japanese, Korean, Vietnamese, Thai, and Arabic. This specific model is the instruction-tuned 3B Qwen2.5 model. It is a causal language model that has undergone pretraining and post-training. The architecture includes transformers with RoPE, SwiGLU, RMSNorm, attention QKV bias, and tied word embeddings. It has 3.09 billion parameters, with 2.77 billion non-embedding parameters. The model consists of 36 layers and uses 16 attention heads for Q and 2 for KV. It supports a context length of 32,768 tokens and can generate up to 8192 tokens. The code for Qwen2.5 is available in the latest Hugging Face `transformers` library, and it is recommended to use the latest version to avoid errors. Using versions of `transformers` earlier than 4.37.0 may result in a KeyError: 'qwen2'. Detailed evaluation results are available in a separate document, and information on GPU memory requirements and throughput is also provided. If you find this work helpful, you may cite the associated publications.

instruction: Can you explain the events shown in this video of a car race?
description: mPLUG-Owl3 is a state-of-the-art multi-modal large language model designed to address the challenges of long image sequence understanding. It introduces Hyper Attention, a mechanism that enhances the speed of long visual sequence understanding in multi-modal large language models by sixfold, enabling the processing of visual sequences that are eight times longer. The model maintains strong performance on single-image, multi-image, and video tasks. It supports chat with images and videos, and is compatible with specific attention implementations such as 'sdpa' and 'flash_attention_2'. The model is capable of generating text-based responses to visual inputs, including descriptions of images and videos. It is based on a 2B parameter architecture and is available for use with the Transformers library. The model has been developed by Jiabo Ye, Haiyang Xu, Haowei Liu, Anwen Hu, Ming Yan, Qi Qian, Ji Zhang, Fei Huang, and Jingren Zhou, and is described in a 2024 paper titled 'mPLUG-Owl3: Towards Long Image-Sequence Understanding in Multi-Modal Large Language Models' with the arXiv identifier 2408.04840.

instruction: What do the colors in this image represent?
description: Pix2Struct is an image encoder - text decoder model trained on image-text pairs for various tasks, including image captioning and visual question answering. It is designed for purely visual language understanding and can be fine-tuned on tasks involving visually-situated language. Pix2Struct is pretrained by learning to parse masked screenshots of web pages into simplified HTML. The web provides a large source of pretraining data well suited to the diversity of downstream tasks. This objective subsumes common pretraining signals such as OCR, language modeling, and image captioning. The model introduces a variable-resolution input representation and a more flexible integration of language and vision inputs, where language prompts such as questions are rendered directly on top of the input image. Pix2Struct has achieved state-of-the-art results in six out of nine tasks across four domains: documents, illustrations, user interfaces, and natural images. This model has been fine-tuned on visual question answering (VQA) tasks and supports question answering in a format that includes multiple choices. The model was originally contributed by Kenton Lee, Mandar Joshi et al. and added to the Hugging Face ecosystem. If you use this model, you should consider citing the original paper titled 'Pix2Struct: Screenshot Parsing as Pretraining for Visual Language Understanding' published on arXiv in 2022.

instruction: Could you provide an English version of this Japanese research summary?
description: PLaMo Translation Model is a specialized large-scale language model developed by Preferred Networks for translation tasks. The model includes a post-trained model for translation, a base model for translation, and a pair-wise evaluation model. It is released under the PLaMo community license, and users are required to check and agree to the license before downloading. This model has not been instruction-tuned for chat dialog or other downstream tasks. Commercial users must check the PLaMo community license and contact the organization via the provided form to use the model for commercial purposes. The model supports translation between English and Japanese, and can be used with a specific prompt format to generate translations or evaluate translation outputs. The model may produce inaccurate, biased, or objectionable responses in some cases, and developers are advised to perform safety testing and tuning tailored to their specific applications. The model was developed under the project 'Research and Development Project of the Enhanced Infrastructures for Post 5G Information and Communication System' (JPNP 20017), subsidized by the New Energy and Industrial Technology Development Organization (NEDO).

instruction: What would be the likely binding strength if I combine these two proteins in a lab experiment?
description: The BindPred model is a Gradient Boosted Trees (GBT) regressor trained on ESM2 embeddings from Meta’s ESM2 protein language model. It is designed for binding affinity predictive tasks. The model uses ESM2 as its base model and employs a CatBoostRegressor architecture within the CatBoost framework for regression tasks. The model is trained using CatBoost Gradient Boosting on ESM2 embeddings, which are extracted from a 33-layer transformer with 650 million parameters. Evaluation of the model is performed using RMSE and R^2 metrics. The model is limited by the quality of the ESM2 embeddings and its performance is dependent on the training dataset used. It is not a deep-learning model but instead uses GBTs to provide fast and interpretable predictions. Two pretrained models are available: one for predicting binding affinity between ACE2 (human and animals) and RBD proteins, and a general-purpose model trained on ESM2 embeddings.

instruction: I found a stray dog—can you help determine its breed from a picture?
description: Dog-Breed-120 is an image classification vision-language encoder model fine-tuned from google/siglip2-base-patch16-224 for a single-label classification task. It is designed to classify dog images into specific breed categories using the SiglipForImageClassification architecture. The model achieves an accuracy of 86.81% and an evaluation loss of 0.497. It categorizes images into 121 dog breed classes, including affenpinscher, afghan_hound, african_hunting_dog, airedale, american_staffordshire_terrier, appenzeller, australian_terrier, basenji, basset, beagle, bedlington_terrier, bernese_mountain_dog, black-and-tan_coonhound, blenheim_spaniel, bloodhound, bluetick, border_collie, border_terrier, borzoi, boston_bull, bouvier_des_flandres, boxer, brabancon_griffon, briard, brittany_spaniel, bull_mastiff, cairn, cardigan, chesapeake_bay_retriever, chihuahua, chow, clumber, cocker_spaniel, collie, curly-coated_retriever, dandie_dinmont, dhole, dingo, doberman, english_foxhound, english_setter, english_springer, entlebucher, eskimo_dog, flat-coated_retriever, french_bulldog, german_shepherd, german_short-haired_pointer, giant_schnauzer, golden_retriever, gordon_setter, great_dane, great_pyrenees, greater_swiss_mountain_dog, groenendael, ibizan_hound, irish_setter, irish_terrier, irish_water_spaniel, irish_wolfhound, italian_greyhound, japanese_spaniel, keeshond, kelpie, kerry_blue_terrier, komondor, kuvasz, labrador_retriever, lakeland_terrier, leonberg, lhasa, malamute, malinois, maltese_dog, mexican_hairless, miniature_pinscher, miniature_poodle, miniature_schnauzer, newfoundland, norfolk_terrier, norwegian_elkhound, norwich_terrier, old_english_sheepdog, otterhound, papillon, pekinese, pembroke, pomeranian, pug, redbone, rhodesian_ridgeback, rottweiler, saint_bernard, saluki, samoyed, schipperke, scotch_terrier, scottish_deerhound, sealyham_terrier, shetland_sheepdog, shih-tzu, siberian_husky, silky_terrier, soft-coated_wheaten_terrier, staffordshire_bullterrier, standard_poodle, standard_schnauzer, sussex_spaniel, test, tibetan_mastiff, tibetan_terrier, toy_poodle, toy_terrier, vizsla, walker_hound, weimaraner, welsh_springer_spaniel, west_highland_white_terrier, whippet, wire-haired_fox_terrier, and yorkshire_terrier. The model is intended for use in dog breed image classification, with potential applications in pet identification, animal research, e-commerce, and educational purposes.

instruction: What is the total population of the cities listed in this table?
description: This model is a TAPAS small model fine-tuned on Sequential Question Answering (SQA). It has two versions: the default version corresponds to the `tapas_sqa_inter_masklm_small_reset` checkpoint, and the other version is `no_reset`, corresponding to `tapas_sqa_inter_masklm_small`. The model was pre-trained on masked language modeling (MLM) and an additional step called intermediate pre-training, followed by fine-tuning. It uses relative position embeddings, resetting the position index at every cell of the table for the default version, while the non-default version uses absolute position embeddings. TAPAS is a BERT-like transformer model pre-trained on a large corpus of English data from Wikipedia in a self-supervised manner. It was pre-trained with two objectives: masked language modeling, where 15% of the words in a flattened table and associated context are randomly masked and the model must predict them, and intermediate pre-training, where the model is trained to classify whether a sentence is supported or refuted by a table using a balanced dataset of syntactically created examples. The model learns an inner representation of English language used in tables and associated texts, which can be used for downstream tasks such as answering questions about a table or determining if a sentence is entailed or refuted by a table. Fine-tuning involves adding a cell selection head on top of the pre-trained model and jointly training this classification head with the base model on SQA. The model can be used for answering questions related to a table in a conversational setup. The texts are lowercased and tokenized using WordPiece with a vocabulary size of 30,000. The model inputs are of the form [CLS] Question [SEP] Flattened table [SEP]. The model was fine-tuned on 32 Cloud TPU v3 cores for 200,000 steps with a maximum sequence length of 512 and a batch size of 128. Fine-tuning takes approximately 20 hours. The optimizer used is Adam with a learning rate of 1.25e-5 and a warmup ratio of 0.2. An inductive bias is added so that the model only selects cells of the same column, as reflected by the `select_one_column` parameter of `TapasConfig`. The model achieves a dev accuracy of 0.6155 for the reset version and 0.5876 for the noreset version in the small size category. The team releasing TAPAS did not write a model card for this model; this model card was written by the Hugging Face team and contributors.
domain: Natural Language Processing Table Question Answering

instruction: What is the most interesting part of this image?
description: BLIP-2 is a multimodal model consisting of three components: a CLIP-like image encoder, a Querying Transformer (Q-Former), and a large language model. The image encoder and large language model are initialized from pre-trained checkpoints and remain frozen during training, while the Q-Former, a BERT-like Transformer encoder, is trained to map a set of 'query tokens' to query embeddings that bridge the embedding spaces of the image encoder and the language model. The model is designed to predict the next text token given the query embeddings and the previous text. It supports tasks such as image captioning, visual question answering (VQA), and chat-like conversations by providing the image and previous conversation as input. BLIP-2 uses the Flan-T5-xl language model, which inherits the same risks and limitations, including potential misuse for harmful language generation. The model is fine-tuned on image-text datasets collected from the internet, which may result in the generation of inappropriate content or the replication of biases present in the training data. BLIP-2 has not been tested in real-world applications and should not be directly deployed without a careful assessment of safety and fairness concerns specific to the intended use. This release is for research purposes only and is not evaluated for all downstream applications. Users are strongly encouraged to evaluate and address potential concerns related to accuracy, safety, and fairness before deployment, particularly in high-risk scenarios where errors or misuse could significantly impact people's lives, rights, or safety.

instruction: Find me a list of scientific studies on the effectiveness of meditation.
description: This is a model finetuned from BAAI/bge-base-en-v1.5. It maps sentences and paragraphs to a 768-dimensional dense vector space and can be used for semantic textual similarity, semantic search, paraphrase mining, text classification, clustering, and more. The model is a Sentence Transformer with a maximum sequence length of 512 tokens and an output dimensionality of 768. The similarity function used is cosine similarity. The model architecture consists of a Transformer with a BERT model, a Pooling layer with specific configuration parameters, and a Normalize layer. The model was trained on a dataset of 45 samples with anchor and positive columns, and evaluated on a dataset of 5 samples with similar structure. The training process used a loss function with parameters scale set to 20.0 and similarity_fct set to 'cos_sim'. Training hyperparameters included an evaluation strategy of steps, a per-device train and evaluation batch size of 16, a learning rate of 3e-06, a maximum of 24 steps, and a warmup ratio of 0.1. The model was trained using Python version 3.12.8, Sentence Transformers version 3.1.1, Transformers version 4.45.2, PyTorch version 2.2.2, Accelerate version 1.2.1, Datasets version 3.2.0, and Tokenizers version 0.20.3.

instruction: Does this summary of historical events match the user's inquiry about the Industrial Revolution?
description: rank1 is a reasoning reranker model that generates explicit reasoning chains before making relevance judgments. This 7B parameter model is trained from the Qwen2.5-7B base model and uses test-time compute to produce a reasoning chain before deciding if a document is relevant to a query. The model receives a query and document pair, generates a reasoning chain within a `...` section, makes a binary relevance judgment (`true` or `false`), and returns a confidence score based on the logits of the true/false tokens. This approach helps the model break down complex relevance decisions into logical steps, improving performance across diverse retrieval tasks. rank1-7b demonstrates strong performance on retrieval benchmarks, particularly on tasks requiring complex reasoning. The model's ability to 'think through' relevance decisions makes it especially effective for nuanced topics. The model is part of a family that includes variants with different parameter sizes, including 0.5B, 1.5B, 3B, 7B, 14B, 32B, and models trained from Mistral and Llama 3.1 bases. Quantized versions of the model are also available. The model is compatible with the MTEB evaluation framework and can be used for tasks such as NevIR. For specific benchmark results and comparisons with other models, please refer to the paper and the official GitHub repository.

instruction: Could you create a depth visualization for this portrait to show how the background fades into the distance?
description: Distill-Any-Depth is a state-of-the-art monocular depth estimation model trained using a proposed knowledge distillation algorithm. It is designed to perform depth estimation from a single image. The model supports zero-shot depth estimation tasks and is compatible with the transformers library. It was introduced in the paper titled 'Distill Any Depth: Distillation Creates a Stronger Monocular Depth Estimator' by Xiankang He, Dongyan Guo, Hongji Li, Ruibo Li, Ying Cui, and Chi Zhang, available as an arXiv preprint arXiv:2502.19204. The model can be used with the pipeline API or directly with the model and processor classes from the transformers library. It processes input images, performs inference, and outputs depth maps that can be post-processed and visualized. The model is intended for use in applications requiring depth estimation from monocular images, and users are encouraged to cite the associated paper if they find the project useful.

instruction: Please provide a Spanish version of this technical manual, ensuring that the terminology is accurate and consistent.
description: SalamandraTA-7b-instruct is a translation large language model (LLM) that has been instruction-tuned from SalamandraTA-7b-base. The base model is the result of continual pre-training on parallel data and is reserved for internal use. SalamandraTA-7b-instruct is proficient in 35 European languages (plus 3 varieties) and supports translation-related tasks, including sentence-level-translation, paragraph-level-translation, document-level-translation, automatic post-editing, grammar checking, machine translation evaluation, alternative translations, named-entity-recognition, and context-aware translation. It is not designed for chat capabilities and has not been trained with any chat instructions. The base model, SalamandraTA-7b-base, was pre-trained using 424 billion tokens of parallel data, including all official European languages plus Catalan, Basque, Galician, Asturian, Aragonese, and Aranese, amounting to 6,574,251,526 parallel sentence pairs. The model has 7,768,117,248 total parameters, with an embedding size of 1,048,576,000, 32 layers, a hidden size of 4,096, 32 attention heads, a context length of 8,192, a vocabulary size of 256,000, and uses bfloat16 precision. It employs RoPE embedding, SwiGLU activation function, RMS Norm for layer normalization, and supports Flash attention and Grouped Query Attention with 8 query groups. The model is intended for both research and commercial use in any of the languages included in the training data for general machine translation tasks. It is not intended for malicious activities, such as harming others or violating human rights, and any downstream application must comply with current laws and regulations. Irresponsible usage in production environments without proper risk assessment and mitigation is discouraged. The model was trained using NVIDIA’s framework, leveraging PyTorch Lightning for efficient model training in highly distributed settings. It was trained on a pre-exascale EuroHPC supercomputer hosted and operated by Barcelona Supercomputing Center, with nodes featuring 4x Nvidia Hopper GPUs, 2x Intel Sapphire Rapids CPUs, 4x NDR200 interconnects, 512 GB DDR5 memory, and 460 GB NVMe storage. The instruction-following model uses the ChatML template for interaction. It has been fine-tuned on approximately 135,000 instructions, primarily targeting machine translation performance for Catalan, English, and Spanish, with additional data for other European and Iberian languages. Evaluation results show strong performance across multiple languages and tasks, with metrics such as BLEU, TER, ChrF, COMET, and others. The model has been evaluated on English, Spanish, Catalan, and other European languages, with results compared to state-of-the-art models. It also performs well on low-resource languages of Spain, such as Asturian, Aranese, and Aragonese. The model has been tested for gender-aware translation, showing competitive accuracy in translating gendered content. Ethical considerations include the potential for biases and the need for further analysis to address translation accuracy across different languages, dialects, and domains. The model has only undergone preliminary instruction tuning, and developers are urged to consider potential limitations and conduct safety testing tailored to their specific applications. The model is the result of collaboration between the Language Technologies Unit at Barcelona Supercomputing Center and other partners. It is funded by the Government of Catalonia and the Ministerio para la Transformación Digital y de la Función Pública, with support from EU funding. The model is available for use under a license, and users are encouraged to cite the associated research paper.

instruction: Show me a futuristic underwater city with transparent tunnels and marine life swimming around.
description: FLUX.1 [dev] is a 12 billion parameter rectified flow transformer designed to generate images from text descriptions. It offers cutting-edge output quality, second only to the state-of-the-art model FLUX.1 [pro]. The model demonstrates competitive prompt following, matching the performance of closed-source alternatives. It is trained using guidance distillation, which makes it more efficient. The model's weights are open to drive new scientific research and empower artists to develop innovative workflows. Generated outputs can be used for personal, scientific, and commercial purposes as described in the FLUX.1 [dev] Non-Commercial License. A reference implementation of FLUX.1 [dev], along with sampling code, is provided in a dedicated repository, which developers and creatives can use as a starting point for building on top of the model. The FLUX.1 models are available via API from multiple sources. FLUX.1 [dev] is also available in a node-based workflow for local inference. To use FLUX.1 [dev] with the diffusers Python library, users must first install or upgrade diffusers and then use FluxPipeline to run the model. The model is not intended or able to provide factual information and, as a statistical model, may amplify existing societal biases. It may fail to generate output that matches the prompts, and prompt following is heavily influenced by the prompting-style. The model and its derivatives may not be used in any way that violates applicable laws or regulations, for the purpose of exploiting or harming minors, to generate or disseminate verifiably false information with the intent to harm, to generate or disseminate personal identifiable information that can be used to harm an individual, to harass, abuse, threaten, stalk, or bully individuals or groups, to create non-consensual nudity or illegal pornographic content, for fully automated decision making that adversely impacts legal rights or creates binding obligations, or to generate or facilitate large-scale disinformation campaigns. This model is licensed under the FLUX.1 [dev] Non-Commercial License.

instruction: Can you help me find the section of this document that discusses recent advancements in neural networks?
description: ColQwen2 is a model based on a novel model architecture and training strategy using Vision Language Models (VLMs) to efficiently index documents from their visual features. It generates multi-vector representations of text and images. This version is trained with a batch size of 256 for 5 epochs and includes an updated pad token. The model takes dynamic image resolutions as input without resizing, which may change the aspect ratio, similar to ColPali. The maximum resolution is set to create at most 768 image patches, with experiments showing improvements with more patches at the cost of increased memory requirements. This version is trained with colpali-engine version 0.3.1 and uses the same data as the ColPali dataset described in the paper. The training dataset consists of 127,460 query-page pairs, including 63% from openly available academic datasets and 37% from a synthetic dataset made of web-crawled PDF pages augmented with VLM-generated pseudo-questions. The training set is entirely in English, allowing for the study of zero-shot generalization to non-English languages. No multi-page PDF document is used in both the training and evaluation sets to prevent contamination. A validation set of 2% of the samples is used for hyperparameter tuning. Multilingual data may be present in the pretraining corpus of the language model and likely in the multimodal training. All models are trained for 1 epoch on the train set in bfloat16 format, using low-rank adapters with alpha=32 and r=32 on the transformer layers from the language model and the final randomly initialized projection layer. The paged_adamw_8bit optimizer is used, with training on an 8 GPU setup using data parallelism, a learning rate of 5e-5 with linear decay and 2.5% warmup steps, and a batch size of 32. The model relies on multi-vector retrieving derived from the ColBERT late interaction mechanism, which may require engineering efforts to adapt to widely used vector retrieval frameworks that lack native multi-vector support. The model primarily focuses on PDF-type documents and high-resource languages, potentially limiting its generalization to other document types or less represented languages. The vision language backbone model (Qwen2-VL) is licensed under Apache 2.0, while the adapters are under the MIT license.

instruction: What percentage of words in the training data were masked during pre-training?
description: This model is a TAPAS medium model fine-tuned on Sequential Question Answering (SQA). It has two versions: the default version corresponds to the `tapas_sqa_inter_masklm_medium_reset` checkpoint, and the other version is `no_reset`, corresponding to `tapas_sqa_inter_masklm_medium`. The model was pre-trained on masked language modeling (MLM) and an additional intermediate pre-training step, followed by fine-tuning on SQA. It uses relative position embeddings, resetting the position index at every cell of the table. The non-default version uses absolute position embeddings. TAPAS is a BERT-like transformer model pre-trained on a large corpus of English data from Wikipedia in a self-supervised manner. It was pre-trained with two objectives: masked language modeling, where 15% of the words in a flattened table and associated context are randomly masked and the model must predict them, and intermediate pre-training, where the model is trained to classify whether a sentence is supported or refuted by the contents of a table using a balanced dataset of millions of syntactically created examples. This allows the model to learn a bidirectional representation of tables and associated texts, which can be used for downstream tasks such as answering questions about a table or determining if a sentence is entailed or refuted by the table. Fine-tuning involves adding a cell selection head on top of the pre-trained model and jointly training this head with the base model on SQA. The model can be used for answering questions related to a table in a conversational setup. The texts are lowercased and tokenized using WordPiece with a vocabulary size of 30,000. The model inputs are of the form [CLS] Question [SEP] Flattened table [SEP]. The model was fine-tuned on 32 Cloud TPU v3 cores for 200,000 steps with a maximum sequence length of 512 and a batch size of 128. Fine-tuning takes approximately 20 hours and uses the Adam optimizer with a learning rate of 1.25e-5 and a warmup ratio of 0.2. An inductive bias is added so that the model only selects cells of the same column, as reflected by the `select_one_column` parameter of `TapasConfig`. The model achieves a dev accuracy of 0.6561 with the reset version and 0.6464 with the noreset version for the medium size. The team releasing TAPAS did not write a model card for this model, and this model card was written by the Hugging Face team and contributors.
