instruction: Is it possible to create a 3D-like visualization of a house using just a photo and some text?
description: MV-Adapter is a creative productivity tool that seamlessly transfers text-to-image models to multi-view generators. It is designed to create high-fidelity multi-view images using various base text-to-image models and various conditions. The model supports generating 768x768 multi-view images and works well with personalized models such as DreamShaper and Animagine, as well as LCM and ControlNet. It supports text or image to multi-view generation, enabling 3D reconstruction, and also supports generation with geometry guidance for 3D texture generation. Additionally, it allows for arbitrary view generation. The model is based on different base models, including SD2.1 and SDXL, and supports various configurations such as text-to-multiview, image-to-multiview, text-geometry-to-multiview, image-geometry-to-multiview, and image-to-arbitrary-views. The model is described in the paper titled 'MV-Adapter: Multi-view Consistent Image Generation Made Easy' by Huang, Zehuan and Guo, Yuanchen and Wang, Haoran and Yi, Ran and Ma, Lizhuang and Cao, Yan-Pei and Sheng, Lu, published as an arXiv preprint in 2024.

instruction: How can I improve my coding skills in Python?
description: Qwen2.5 is the latest series of Qwen large language models. The Qwen2.5 series includes a range of base language models and instruction-tuned language models with parameter sizes ranging from 0.5 to 72 billion. Compared to Qwen2, Qwen2.5 offers significant improvements in knowledge, coding, and mathematics capabilities, supported by specialized expert models in these domains. It also shows enhanced instruction following, long text generation (over 8K tokens), structured data understanding (e.g., tables), and structured output generation, especially JSON. The model is more resilient to the diversity of system prompts, which improves role-play implementation and condition-setting for chatbots. It supports long-context processing up to 128K tokens and can generate up to 8K tokens. It provides multilingual support for over 29 languages, including Chinese, English, French, Spanish, Portuguese, German, Italian, Russian, Japanese, Korean, Vietnamese, Thai, and Arabic. The instruction-tuned 1.5B Qwen2.5 model is a causal language model that has undergone pretraining and post-training. It uses a transformer architecture with RoPE, SwiGLU, RMSNorm, attention QKV bias, and tied word embeddings. It has 1.54 billion parameters, 1.31 billion non-embedding parameters, 28 layers, and 12 attention heads for Q and 2 for KV. It supports a context length of 32,768 tokens and can generate up to 8192 tokens. The model is available on Hugging Face and requires the latest version of the `transformers` library to avoid errors. Detailed evaluation results and performance metrics are available in the provided documentation. If you find this work helpful, you may cite the associated publications.

instruction: Is this a rare or endangered species of butterfly in the image?
description: BioCLIP 2 is a foundation model for biology organismal images. It is trained on a ViT-L/14 Transformer pre-trained on LAION-2B. BioCLIP 2 demonstrates state-of-the-art performance in recognizing various species and exhibits emergent properties beyond species classification after extensive hierarchical contrastive training. The model is trained on the TreeOfLife-200M dataset, the largest and most diverse available dataset of biology images. Through training at scale, BioCLIP 2 improves species classification by 18.1% over BioCLIP. It generalizes to diverse biological questions beyond species classification using only species-level supervision. Further analysis reveals that BioCLIP 2 acquires two emergent properties through scaling up hierarchical contrastive learning: inter-species ecological alignment and intra-species variation separation. The model uses a ViT-L/14 Transformer as an image encoder and a masked self-attention Transformer as a text encoder. It is licensed under the MIT license and was fine-tuned from a CLIP pre-trained on LAION-2B. BioCLIP 2 can be used for zero-shot classification provided the species names, for few-shot classification with some images serving as the support set, and as a visual encoder for other biological visual tasks. The model was trained on nearly 214 million images covering 952,000 taxa. A subset of LAION-2B consisting of 26 million samples was used for experience replay. The training used standard CLIP image preprocessing, bf16 mixed precision, an Adam optimizer with a maximum learning rate of 1e-4, 1,875 warm-up steps, and cosine decay. The batch size of biological images was 2,816 per GPU, and that of replay data was 320 per GPU. The model was trained on 32 GPUs for 30 epochs with a weight decay of 0.2. Each input image was resized to 224 x 224 resolution. BioCLIP 2 was evaluated on species classification and other biological visual tasks. For species classification, it was tested on 10 tasks, including NABirds, Plankton, Insects, Insects2, PlantNet, Fungi, PlantVillage, Medicinal Leaf, camera trap images, and a dataset of 400 species labeled Near Threatened through Extinct in the Wild. For tasks beyond species classification, it was tested on predicting feeding path and habitat labels, binary classification tasks, attribute classification, semi-supervised clustering for new species discovery, and plant disease classification. BioCLIP 2 surpasses BioCLIP by 18.0% on zero-shot species classification benchmarks and achieves the best performance on tasks beyond species classification. It yields a 10.2% performance gap over DINOv2 on these tasks. The model was trained on 32 NVIDIA H100-80GB GPUs distributed over 4 nodes on the Bridges-2 Cluster, and the training took 10 days to complete 30 epochs. BioCLIP 2 is trained on an imbalanced dataset, with the TreeOfLife-200M dataset exhibiting a long-tailed distribution across taxa. Therefore, the predictions of BioCLIP 2 might be biased toward well-represented species. While BioCLIP 2 and TreeOfLife-200M have the potential to improve conservation efforts by facilitating the recognition of threatened species, there is also potential for misuse by bad actors. Improved classification capability could be exploited by poachers to identify protected species, though these tools can also be used to monitor illicit trade. The primary risk to endangered species comes from the disclosure of precise location information rather than improved classification capability. The data used does not include geo-tagged information, minimizing vulnerabilities related to poaching.

instruction: Can you match this image to a similar one from a database?
description: Perception Encoder (PE) is a state-of-the-art encoder for image and video understanding trained via simple vision-language learning. It is a family of large-scale vision encoder models with state-of-the-art performance on a large variety of vision tasks. By using a robust contrastive pretraining recipe and finetuning on synthetically aligned videos, PE not only outperforms all existing models on classification and retrieval, but it also internally produces strong, general features that scale for downstream tasks. PE unlocks the ability for large-scale contrastive pretraining to transfer to downstream tasks with alignment tuning to capitalize on those general features. PE core is the base model trained with a robust image pretraining schedule and finetuned on the data generated by a synthetic video data engine. PE core currently comes in three sizes: B/16, L/14, and G/14. PE core G is the main checkpoint, with L and B models distilled from it. All PE core models use an attention pooling block with 8 heads on top of the vision tower. The L and B models additionally have a class token for global aggregation. PE core obtains extremely strong results across the board on zero-shot image classification and retrieval as well as zero-shot video classification and retrieval. For example, on the IN-1k benchmark, the B/16 model achieves 78.4%, the L/14 model achieves 83.5%, and the G/14 model achieves 85.4%. On the IN-v2 benchmark, the B/16 model achieves 71.7%, the L/14 model achieves 77.9%, and the G/14 model achieves 80.2%. On the IN-A benchmark, the B/16 model achieves 62.4%, the L/14 model achieves 89.0%, and the G/14 model achieves 92.6%. On the ObjectNet benchmark, the B/16 model achieves 71.9%, the L/14 model achieves 84.7%, and the G/14 model achieves 88.2%. On the COCO-T2I benchmark, the B/16 model achieves 50.9%, the L/14 model achieves 57.1%, and the G/14 model achieves 58.1%. On the Kinetics-400 benchmark, the B/16 model achieves 65.6%, the L/14 model achieves 73.4%, and the G/14 model achieves 76.9%. On the VTT-T2I benchmark, the B/16 model achieves 47.6%, the L/14 model achieves 50.3%, and the G/14 model achieves 51.2%. PE core performs particularly well on the hard benchmarks such as ObjectNet and ImageNet-A.

instruction: Can you create a voice in Urdu for a greeting message that sounds friendly and warm?
description: Indic Parler-TTS is a multilingual Indic extension of a text-to-speech model. It is a fine-tuned version trained on a 1,806-hour multilingual Indic and English dataset. Indic Parler-TTS Mini can officially speak in 20 Indic languages and in English. The 21 languages supported are Assamese, Bengali, Bodo, Dogri, English, Gujarati, Hindi, Kannada, Konkani, Maithili, Malayalam, Manipuri, Marathi, Nepali, Odia, Sanskrit, Santali, Sindhi, Tamil, Telugu, and Urdu. Thanks to its better prompt tokenizer, it can easily be extended to other languages. This tokenizer has a larger vocabulary and handles byte fallback, which simplifies multilingual training. The model is the result of a collaboration between the HuggingFace audio team and another team. The model accepts two primary inputs: a transcript, which is the text to be converted to speech, and a caption, which is a detailed description of how the speech should sound. The model officially supports 21 languages, with unofficial support for Chhattisgarhi, Kashmiri, and Punjabi. It provides 69 unique voices across the supported languages, with each language having a set of recommended voices optimized for naturalness and intelligibility. Ten languages officially support emotion-specific prompts: Assamese, Bengali, Bodo, Dogri, Kannada, Malayalam, Marathi, Sanskrit, Nepali, and Tamil. Emotion support for other languages exists but has not been extensively tested. Available emotions include Command, Anger, Narration, Conversation, Disgust, Fear, Happy, Neutral, Proper Noun, News, Sad, and Surprise. The model officially supports Indian English accents through its English voices, providing clear and natural speech. For other accents, the model allows customization by specifying accent details, such as 'A male British speaker' or 'A female American speaker,' using style transfer for more dynamic and personalized outputs. Indic Parler-TTS offers precise control over various speech characteristics using the caption input, including background noise, reverberation, expressivity, pitch, speaking rate, and voice quality. The model automatically adapts to the language it detects in the prompt. To ensure speaker consistency across generations, the model was trained on pre-determined speakers, characterized by name. The model includes 69 speakers across 18 officially supported languages, with each language having a set of recommended voices for optimal performance. Indic Parler-TTS has been evaluated using a MOS-like framework by native and non-native speakers. The results highlight its exceptional performance in generating natural and intelligible speech, especially for native speakers of Indian languages. Key strengths include exceptional performance for native speakers, with top scores for Maithili (95.36), Sanskrit (99.79), and Bodo (94.47), competitive results for lesser-resourced and unofficially supported languages like Kashmiri and Sindhi, and adaptability to non-native and anonymous speaker scenarios with consistently high clarity. The model was fine-tuned on a subset of the dataset used to train the pre-trained version: the Indic-Parler Dataset, a large-scale multilingual speech corpus designed to train the Indic Parler-TTS model. The dataset supports 16 official languages of India, along with English and Chhattisgarhi, making it comprehensive for regional language technologies. These languages include Assamese, Bengali, Bodo, Chhattisgarhi, Dogri, English, Gujarati, Hindi, Kannada, Malayalam, Manipuri, Marathi, Nepali, Odia, Punjabi, Sanskrit, Tamil, and Telugu. The model is permissively licensed under the Apache 2.0 license.

instruction: Find all the cities and countries mentioned in this news article about recent events.
description: The bert-large-NER is a fine-tuned BERT model designed for Named Entity Recognition (NER) and achieves state-of-the-art performance for the NER task. It is trained to recognize four types of entities: location (LOC), organizations (ORG), person (PER), and Miscellaneous (MISC). This model is based on the bert-large-cased architecture and was fine-tuned on the English version of the standard dataset. A smaller BERT model fine-tuned on the same dataset is also available. The model can be used with the Transformers pipeline for NER. However, it is limited by its training dataset of entity-annotated news articles from a specific time period, which may not generalize well to all use cases in different domains. Additionally, the model occasionally tags subword tokens as entities, and post-processing of results may be necessary to handle these cases. The model was fine-tuned on the English version of the standard dataset, which distinguishes between the beginning and continuation of an entity to allow for the identification of back-to-back entities of the same type. Each token is classified as one of the following classes: O (Outside of a named entity), B-MIS (Beginning of a miscellaneous entity right after another miscellaneous entity), I-MIS (Miscellaneous entity), B-PER (Beginning of a person’s name right after another person’s name), I-PER (Person’s name), B-ORG (Beginning of an organization right after another organization), I-ORG (Organization), B-LOC (Beginning of a location right after another location), and I-LOC (Location). The training dataset was derived from the Reuters corpus and consists of news stories. The dataset includes 7140 training examples for LOC, 3438 for MISC, 6321 for ORG, and 6600 for PER. The development set includes 1837 LOC, 922 MISC, 1341 ORG, and 1842 PER examples. The test set includes 1668 LOC, 702 MISC, 1661 ORG, and 1617 PER examples. The training dataset contains 946 articles, 14,987 sentences, and 203,621 tokens. The development dataset contains 216 articles, 3,466 sentences, and 51,362 tokens. The test dataset contains 231 articles, 3,684 sentences, and 46,435 tokens. The model was trained on a single NVIDIA V100 GPU using recommended hyperparameters from the training and evaluation on the CoNLL-2003 NER task. The model achieved an f1 score of 95.7 on the development set and 91.7 on the test set. The precision was 95.3 on the development set and 91.2 on the test set. The recall was 96.1 on the development set and 92.3 on the test set. The test metrics are slightly lower than the official Google BERT results, which included document context and experimented with CRF.

instruction: Can you summarize the key findings from this X-ray report?
description: EraX-VL-7B-V1.5 is a robust multimodal model designed for OCR (optical character recognition) and VQA (visual question-answering), with a focus on Vietnamese and multilingual capabilities. It is particularly effective in recognizing and extracting information from various types of documents, including medical forms, invoices, bills of sale, quotes, and medical records. The model is intended to support applications in hospitals, clinics, insurance companies, and similar domains. EraX-VL-7B-V1.5 is built upon a high-quality, Vietnamese-fluent base model and has been fine-tuned to enhance its performance. It is a multimodal Transformer model with over 7B parameters and is open-source under the Apache 2.0 license. The model is not an OCR-only tool like Tesseract but a multimodal LLM-based model that requires careful prompting for optimal use. It is not yet fine-tuned on medical (X-ray) datasets or car accident data, but updates are planned for early 2025. EraX-VL-7B-V1.5 supports multi-turn Q&A with strong reasoning capabilities and is part of the EraX's LànhGPT collection of LLM models. It was developed by Nguyễn Anh Nguyên, Nguyễn Hồ Nam, Phạm Huỳnh Nhật, and Phạm Đình Thục, and is funded by EraX. The model achieved a VI-MTVQA score of 47.2, outperforming several models of similar or larger size. It supports tasks such as OCR for multi-images, image captioning, and multi-turn visual question answering and reasoning. Examples of supported OCR tasks include extracting information from citizen identification cards, driver's licenses, vehicle registration certificates, receipts, and medical records. The model can also generate image captions and perform multi-turn visual question answering, including extracting patient information, diagnoses, medical history, test results, and medication costs from medical documents. The model is designed to be used with Python, requiring specific packages and a base64-encoded image input for inference. The model's performance is evaluated using the EraX-MTVQA-Benchmark, and the test code is available in the EraX-JS-Company/EraX-MTVQA-Benchmark repository. API access inquiries should be directed to nguyen@erax.ai.

instruction: How would you classify the vocalizations in this baby's crying recording?
description: voc2vec is a foundation model specifically designed for non-verbal human data. The voc2vec-hubert-ls-pt model is built upon the HuBERT framework and follows its pre-training setup. The pre-training datasets include AudioSet (vocalization), FreeSound (babies), HumanVoiceDataset, NNIME, NonSpeech7K, ReCANVo, SingingDatabase, TUT (babies), VocalSketch, and VocalSound. This model continues pre-training from a HuBERT-like model that was initially trained on the LibriSpeech dataset. The model is evaluated on six datasets: ASVP-ESD, ASPV-ESD (babies), CNVVE, NonVerbal Vocalization Dataset, Donate a Cry, and VIVAE. The model achieves an average performance of Unweighted Average Recall (UAR) of .696±.189 and F1 Macro of .678±.200 across these six datasets. This is currently the best released model of the voc2vec collection.

instruction: Please analyze this text and tell me if it belongs to the sports or entertainment category.
description: The TopicClassifier-NoURL is a model that organizes web content into 17 categories based on the text contents of web pages, without using URL information. The model has 140M parameters and was fine-tuned on two stages of training data: 1 million documents annotated by Llama-3.1-8B for first-stage training, and 100,000 documents annotated by Llama-3.1-405B-FP8 for second-stage training. It is designed to classify web content into one of 24 categories, including Adult, Art & Design, Software Dev., Crime & Law, Education & Jobs, Hardware, Entertainment, Social Life, Fashion & Beauty, Finance & Business, Food & Dining, Games, Health, History, Home & Hobbies, Industrial, Literature, Politics, Religion, Science & Tech., Software, Sports & Fitness, Transportation, and Travel. The model expects input in the form of raw text and outputs logits that can be converted into a probability distribution over the 24 categories using a softmax function. For efficient inference, it is recommended to use the gte-base-en-v1.5 implementation with unpadding and memory efficient attention, which requires installing the `xformers` library and loading the model with specific parameters. The model is part of a broader system for organizing the web, and its development is described in the paper titled 'Organize the Web: Constructing Domains Enhances Pre-Training Data Curation' by Alexander Wettig, Kyle Lo, Sewon Min, Hannaneh Hajishirzi, Danqi Chen, and Luca Soldaini, available as an arXiv preprint.

instruction: Tôi cần tài liệu pháp luật liên quan đến quyền sở hữu trí tuệ, hãy sắp xếp theo mức độ liên quan.
description: Vietnamese_Reranker is a reranker model fine-tuned from the bge-reranker-v2-m3 model to enhance retrieval capabilities for Vietnamese. The model was trained on approximately 1,100,000 triplets of queries, positive documents, and negative documents for Vietnamese. The model was trained with a maximum sequence length of 2304 (256 for query and 2048 for passages). The model type is a Sentence Transformer. The base model is unspecified. The maximum sequence length is 2304 tokens (256 for query and 2048 for passages). The output dimensionality is 1024 dimensions. The similarity function used is dot product similarity. The model is designed for the Vietnamese language. The model is licensed under Apache 2.0. The model was evaluated on the entire training dataset of Legal Zalo 2021, a dataset on which it was not trained. On this dataset, the model achieved an Accuracy@1 of 0.7944, an Accuracy@3 of 0.9324, an Accuracy@5 of 0.9537, an Accuracy@10 of 0.9740, and an MRR@10 of 0.8672. In comparison, Vietnamese_Embedding_v2 achieved an Accuracy@1 of 0.7262, an Accuracy@3 of 0.8927, an Accuracy@5 of 0.9268, an Accuracy@10 of 0.9578, and an MRR@10 of 0.8149. Vietnamese_Embedding achieved an Accuracy@1 of 0.7274, an Accuracy@3 of 0.8992, an Accuracy@5 of 0.9305, an Accuracy@10 of 0.9568, and an MRR@10 of 0.8181. Vietnamese-bi-encoder (BKAI) achieved an Accuracy@1 of 0.7109, an Accuracy@3 of 0.8680, an Accuracy@5 of 0.9014, an Accuracy@10 of 0.9299, and an MRR@10 of 0.7951. BGE-M3 achieved an Accuracy@1 of 0.5682, an Accuracy@3 of 0.7728, an Accuracy@5 of 0.8382, an Accuracy@10 of 0.8921, and an MRR@10 of 0.6822. Vietnamese_Reranker and Vietnamese_Embedding_v2 were both trained on 1,100,000 triplets. Although the score on the legal domain drops a bit on Vietnamese_Embedding_v2 (Phase 2), since this phase data is much larger, it is good for other domains.

instruction: What is the main idea conveyed in this visual chart?
description: INFRL-Qwen2.5-VL-72B-Preview improves visual reasoning upon a previous model. As of March 25th, 2025, INFRL-Qwen2.5-VL-72B-Preview is the best-performing open-sourced visual language (VL) model on various visual reasoning benchmarks. On the MathVision (test) benchmark, it achieves a score of 41.9, outperforming models such as GPT4o (30.6), Gemini-2.0-Flash (41.3), Claude 3.5 Sonnet (33.5), QvQ-72B (35.9), InternVL2.5-78B (34.9), and Qwen-VL-2.5-72B (38.1). On the MathVista (testmini) benchmark, it achieves a score of 77.8, outperforming GPT4o (60), Gemini-2.0-Flash (70.1), Claude 3.5 Sonnet (67.7), QvQ-72B (71.4), InternVL2.5-78B (72.3), and Qwen-VL-2.5-72B (74.8). On the MathVerse (testmini) benchmark, it achieves a score of 58.84, outperforming GPT4o (41.2), Gemini-2.0-Flash (50.6), Claude 3.5 Sonnet (47.8), QvQ-72B (48.6), InternVL2.5-78B (51.7), and Qwen-VL-2.5-72B (57.18). A code repository for VLM evaluation will be released, supporting RL training with simple rule-based rewards and aligning with LLM-Judge results.

instruction: Can you extract the key ideas from this audio recording in English?
description: Ultravox is a multimodal Speech LLM that can consume both speech and text as input, such as a text system prompt and a voice user message. The input to the model is given as a text prompt with a special pseudo-token, and the model processor replaces this token with embeddings derived from the input audio. Using the merged embeddings as input, the model generates output text as usual. In a future revision of Ultravox, the token vocabulary will be expanded to support the generation of semantic and acoustic audio tokens, which can then be fed to a vocoder to produce voice output. No preference tuning has been applied to this revision of the model. The model uses a pre-trained backbone as well as the encoder part of another model. The multi-modal adapter is trained, the Whisper encoder is fine-tuned, while the Llama model is kept frozen. A knowledge-distillation loss is used where Ultravox tries to match the logits of the text-based Llama backbone. The training dataset is a mix of ASR datasets, extended with continuations generated by Llama 3.1 8B, and speech translation datasets, which yield a modest improvement in translation evaluations. The model is trained using supervised speech instruction finetuning via knowledge-distillation. The model was developed by Fixie.ai and is licensed under the MIT license. The model can be used as a voice agent, for speech-to-speech translation, and for the analysis of spoken audio. The model is trained using BF16 mixed precision training on 8x H100 GPUs. Evaluation results show the following performance metrics: for covost2 en_ar, 1.55 for Ultravox 0.5 1b, 12.99 for Ultravox 0.5 8B, and 20.21 for Ultravox 0.5 70B; for covost2 en_ca, 8.06 for Ultravox 0.5 1b, 31.54 for Ultravox 0.5 8B, and 40.01 for Ultravox 0.5 70B; for covost2 en_de, 14.21 for Ultravox 0.5 1b, 28.70 for Ultravox 0.5 8B, and 34.53 for Ultravox 0.5 70B; for covost2 es_en, 24.97 for Ultravox 0.5 1b, 40.19 for Ultravox 0.5 8B, and 43.29 for Ultravox 0.5 70B; for covost2 ru_en, 24.12 for Ultravox 0.5 1b, 42.13 for Ultravox 0.5 8B, and 48.99 for Ultravox 0.5 70B; for covost2 zh_en, 4.76 for Ultravox 0.5 1b, 17.22 for Ultravox 0.5 8B, and 21.37 for Ultravox 0.5 70B; and for big bench audio, 39.14 for Ultravox 0.5 1b, 66.54 for Ultravox 0.5 8B, and 82.70 for Ultravox 0.5 70B.

instruction: What is the person doing in this video? Can you name the action?
description: The VideoMAE Base model is fine-tuned on the UCF101 dataset. It is designed for video action recognition. The model is available under the MIT license and is developed for the English language. During training, clips were sampled from videos consisting of 64 frames, and a uniform sample of 16 frames was used as input. PyTorchVideo was used to apply mixup and cutmix during training. The model was trained and evaluated on one fold of the UCF101 annotations. Evaluation results include an accuracy of 0.758209764957428 and a top 5 accuracy of 0.8983050584793091. It is noted that the results may be slightly lower than those achieved in the VideoMAE paper, as inference was not performed over multiple crops or segments of validation videos. Users are advised to be aware of the model's risks, biases, and limitations, although more information is needed for further recommendations.

instruction: Could you highlight all the dates present in this contract?
description: This is an 18-class named entity recognition (NER) model for English, designed to identify and classify named entities in text. It achieves an F1-score of 89.27 on the Ontonotes dataset. The model predicts 18 distinct entity types, including cardinal values, dates, events, buildings, geo-political entities, languages, laws, locations, money, affiliations, ordinals, organizations, percentages, persons, products, quantities, times, and works of art. The model is based on a combination of GloVe word embeddings and contextual string embeddings from forward and backward Flair models, with a stacked embedding architecture and an LSTM-CRF sequence tagger. Training was conducted using the Ontonotes corpus, with a hidden layer size of 256 and a maximum of 150 training epochs. The model is intended for use in natural language processing tasks that require entity recognition and classification. Users are advised to cite the associated research paper when using this model.

instruction: Can you provide the total number of students who passed in the Bengali literature exam based on this table?
description: The model is a multilingual conditional generation model based on the MBart architecture, specifically fine-tuned for the task of Table Question Answering (TableQA) in low-resource Indic languages, such as Bengali and Hindi. It is designed to answer questions over structured tabular data, returning either individual cells or entire tables as output. The model supports tasks involving the processing of tabular data in these languages, including the linearization of tables and the generation of answers based on queries. It is trained on large-scale datasets generated through an automatic data generation process tailored for low-resource languages. The model demonstrates strong performance, outperforming state-of-the-art large language models on TableQA tasks in these languages. The model is evaluated on mathematical reasoning capabilities and zero-shot cross-lingual transfer. The data generation method used is scalable and can be applied to any low-resource language with a web presence. The model is part of a research effort published in the 2024 Conference on Empirical Methods in Natural Language Processing. The model is available for use with the Hugging Face Transformers library and requires a tokenizer configured for the Bengali language (bn_IN). The model is intended for use with tabular data that has been linearized into a specific text format, combining the question and the processed table as input. The model generates answers in the same format. The model is trained and evaluated on a custom dataset called BanglaTabQA, which includes questions, input tables, and answers in Bengali. The model is designed for deployment on GPU hardware and uses beam search with a beam size of 5 for inference. The model is part of a broader research initiative focused on addressing the lack of annotated data and neural models for low-resource languages in the domain of TableQA.
domain: Natural Language Processing Table Question Answering

instruction: I want to hear the phrase 'You are doing well' spoken in a soft and encouraging manner in English.
description: Zonos-v0.1 is an open-weight text-to-speech model trained on more than 200,000 hours of varied multilingual speech, delivering expressiveness and quality on par with or surpassing top TTS providers. The model enables highly natural speech generation from text prompts when given a speaker embedding or audio prefix and can accurately perform speech cloning with a reference clip as short as a few seconds. The conditioning setup allows for fine control over speaking rate, pitch variation, audio quality, and emotions such as happiness, fear, sadness, and anger. The model outputs speech natively at 44kHz. Zonos follows a straightforward architecture: text normalization and phonemization via eSpeak, followed by DAC token prediction through a transformer or hybrid backbone. Zonos supports zero-shot TTS with voice cloning, where users can input desired text and a 10-30 second speaker sample to generate high-quality TTS output. It also supports audio prefix inputs, allowing users to add text plus an audio prefix for richer speaker matching, which can be used to elicit behaviors such as whispering. The model provides multilingual support for English, Japanese, Chinese, French, and German. It offers fine-grained control over many aspects of the generated audio, including speaking rate, pitch, maximum frequency, audio quality, and various emotions. The model runs with a real-time factor of approximately 2x on an RTX 4090. Zonos comes with a Gradio WebUI for easy speech generation and can be installed and deployed using a provided Docker file. Currently, the repository supports Linux systems, preferably Ubuntu 22.04 or 24.04, with recent NVIDIA GPUs (3000-series or newer, 6GB+ VRAM). The model depends on the eSpeak library for phonemization, which can be installed on Ubuntu using the command 'apt install -y espeak-ng'. Installation can be performed using a new uv virtual environment, the system or active environment with uv or pip, or via Docker. A minimal example is provided to confirm that the installation works. For academic use, the model should be cited as @misc{zyphra2025zonos, title={Zonos-v0.1: An Expressive, Open-Source TTS Model}, author={Dario Sucic, Mohamed Osman, Gabriel Clark, Chris Warner, Beren Millidge}, year={2025},}.

instruction: Does the sentence 'He won the race by a mile' entail that 'He was the fastest runner'?
description: ModernBERT is a multi-task fine-tuned model based on the BERT architecture, trained on a collection of NLI (Natural Language Inference) tasks from Tasksource, including MNLI, ANLI, SICK, WANLI, doc-nli, LingNLI, FOLIO, FOL-NLI, LogicNLI, Label-NLI, and other datasets. This model is equivalent to an 'instruct' version and was trained for 200k steps on an Nvidia A30 GPU. It is particularly effective for reasoning tasks, long context reasoning, sentiment analysis, and zero-shot classification with new labels. The model demonstrates strong performance on a wide range of NLI and classification tasks, with test accuracy scores across multiple datasets. These scores reflect the performance of a single transformer with different classification heads on top. Further improvements can be achieved through single-task fine-tuning, but this checkpoint is optimized for zero-shot classification and natural language inference tasks involving contradiction, entailment, and neutral classification. The model supports zero-shot classification pipelines and natural language inference pipelines, and can also serve as a backbone for further fine-tuning. It has stronger reasoning and fine-grained classification abilities compared to the base version.

instruction: I want to compare the production quality of two audio files—can you analyze both?
description: This model provides unified automatic quality assessment for speech, music, and sound. It is designed to evaluate four axes of aesthetic scores: Content Enjoyment (CE), Content Usefulness (CU), Production Complexity (PC), and Production Quality (PQ). The model supports prediction tasks through both command-line interface (CLI) and Python scripts. Users can run predictions by providing a JSONL file containing audio file paths, with optional start and end times for specific segments. The model outputs a JSON-formatted dictionary with scores for each of the four axes. It can be used to infer from file paths or directly from PyTorch tensors. The model is based on a PyTorch framework and requires Python 3.9 and PyTorch 2.2 or greater. An evaluation dataset has been released, containing 4 axes of aesthetic annotation scores for various datasets, including LibriTTS, cv-corpus-13.0-2023-03-09, EARS, MUSDB18, musiccaps, audioset unbalanced_train_segments, and PAM. Each axis contains 10 scores annotated by 10 different human annotators. The model is primarily licensed under CC-BY 4.0, with some portions available under the MIT license. The model is described in a paper titled 'Meta Audiobox Aesthetics: Unified Automatic Quality Assessment for Speech, Music, and Sound,' authored by Andros Tjandra, Yi-Chiao Wu, Baishan Guo, John Hoffman, Brian Ellis, Apoorv Vyas, Bowen Shi, Sanyuan Chen, Matt Le, Nick Zacharov, Carleigh Wood, Ann Lee, and Wei-Ning Hsu, to be published in 2025.

instruction: How can I create a role-playing scenario where a detective solves a mystery in a futuristic city?
description: Qwen3 is the latest generation of large language models in the Qwen series, offering a comprehensive suite of dense and mixture-of-experts (MoE) models. Built upon extensive training, Qwen3 delivers groundbreaking advancements in reasoning, instruction-following, agent capabilities, and multilingual support. It uniquely supports seamless switching between thinking mode (for complex logical reasoning, math, and coding) and non-thinking mode (for efficient, general-purpose dialogue) within a single model, ensuring optimal performance across various scenarios. It significantly enhances its reasoning capabilities, surpassing previous QwQ (in thinking mode) and Qwen2.5 instruct models (in non-thinking mode) on mathematics, code generation, and commonsense logical reasoning. It excels in human preference alignment, delivering a more natural, engaging, and immersive conversational experience in creative writing, role-playing, multi-turn dialogues, and instruction following. It demonstrates expertise in agent capabilities, enabling precise integration with external tools in both thinking and non-thinking modes and achieving leading performance among open-source models in complex agent-based tasks. It supports 100+ languages and dialects with strong capabilities for multilingual instruction following and translation. The Qwen3-32B model is a causal language model with 32.8 billion parameters, 31.2 billion non-embedding parameters, 64 layers, and 64 attention heads for Q and 8 for KV. It natively supports a context length of 32,768 tokens and can be extended to 131,072 tokens using RoPE scaling techniques. For thinking mode, it is recommended to use Temperature=0.6, TopP=0.95, TopK=20, and MinP=0, avoiding greedy decoding. For non-thinking mode, Temperature=0.7, TopP=0.8, TopK=20, and MinP=0 are suggested. Adequate output length is recommended, with 32,768 tokens for most queries and 38,912 tokens for complex problems. Standardized output formats are recommended for benchmarking, including specific prompts for math problems and multiple-choice questions. In multi-turn conversations, the historical model output should only include the final output part, excluding thinking content. The model can be deployed using frameworks such as SGLang, vLLM, Ollama, LMStudio, MLX-LM, llama.cpp, and KTransformers. It supports switching between thinking and non-thinking modes via user input using /think and /no_think tags. For agentic use, it excels in tool calling capabilities and can be integrated with Qwen-Agent for reduced coding complexity. The model's performance on long contexts can be enhanced using YaRN, with static scaling factors that may impact performance on shorter texts. The default max_position_embeddings is set to 40,960, reserving 32,768 tokens for outputs and 8,192 tokens for typical prompts. The endpoint provided by Alibaba Model Studio supports dynamic YaRN by default. The model is available in the latest Hugging Face transformers and can be used with the latest version of transformers. For deployment, it can be used with SGLang or vLLM to create an OpenAI-compatible API endpoint. The model is recommended for use in scenarios requiring both high reasoning capabilities and efficient dialogue, with explicit caveats regarding the use of greedy decoding and the potential impact of YaRN on shorter texts.

instruction: Can you help locate an image for the description 'a person reading a book in a cozy room'?
description: ViT-B-16-SigLIP2-512 is a SigLIP 2 Vision-Language model trained on the WebLI dataset. It is designed for contrastive image-text tasks and zero-shot image classification. The model is based on a Vision Transformer (ViT) architecture with a 16-pixel patch size and 512-dimensional embeddings. It has been converted for use in OpenCLIP from the original JAX checkpoints. The model supports encoding both images and text into feature representations and computing similarity scores using a sigmoid loss function. It is intended for applications requiring multilingual vision-language understanding, improved semantic comprehension, and dense feature extraction. The model's performance is based on the training methodology described in the SigLIP 2 and Sigmoid loss for language image pre-training research papers. Users are advised to cite the relevant publications when using this model.

instruction: Draft a response to a customer complaint that is empathetic and solution-focused.
description: The Llama 3.2 collection is a set of multilingual large language models (LLMs) available in 1B and 3B parameter sizes, designed for text-in and text-out generation. These models are pretrained and instruction-tuned for multilingual dialogue use cases, including agentic retrieval and summarization tasks. They outperform many open source and closed chat models on common industry benchmarks. The models are developed by Meta and use an optimized transformer architecture with auto-regressive language modeling. Instruction-tuned versions are aligned with human preferences for helpfulness and safety using supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and reinforcement learning with human feedback (RLHF). The models support multilingual text and code as input and output, with a context length of 128k tokens for the standard version and 8k for the quantized version. The training data consists of a new mix of publicly available online data, with a knowledge cutoff in December 2023. The models are trained using Meta's custom GPU cluster and production infrastructure, with training energy use totaling 916k GPU hours and estimated greenhouse gas emissions of 240 tons CO2eq. The models are available under a custom commercial license and are intended for commercial and research use in multiple languages. Instruction-tuned models are suitable for assistant-like chat, agentic applications, knowledge retrieval, summarization, and mobile AI-powered writing assistants. Pretrained models can be adapted for additional natural language generation tasks, and quantized models are suitable for on-device use with limited compute resources. The models are not intended for use in any manner that violates applicable laws or regulations, or that is prohibited by the Acceptable Use Policy and Llama 3.2 Community License. The models are not designed to be deployed in isolation but as part of an overall AI system with additional safety guardrails. Developers are expected to deploy system safeguards when building agentic systems. The models have been evaluated on standard automatic benchmarks, showing varying performance across different tasks and quantization methods. The models are available in different quantization schemes, including SpinQuant and QLoRA, which aim to improve inference scalability and reduce model size and memory usage. The models are part of a responsible release approach that includes safety fine-tuning, adversarial evaluations, and red teaming exercises to identify and mitigate potential risks. The models are intended to be used in a variety of use cases, with developers expected to tailor safety for their specific applications. The models are part of an ongoing effort to promote generative AI safety through industry partnerships, open-source tools, and community contributions.

instruction: I have a folder of images; can you detect all license plates across them?
description: YOLOv8 is a cutting-edge, state-of-the-art model that builds upon the success of previous YOLO versions and introduces new features and improvements to further boost performance and flexibility. YOLOv8 is designed to be fast, accurate, and easy to use, making it an excellent choice for a wide range of object detection and tracking, instance segmentation, image classification, and pose estimation tasks. This model has been fine-tuned for detecting Iranian vehicle license plates. The model can be used directly in the Command Line Interface (CLI) with a yolo command, and it can also be used in a Python environment. It supports training, validation, prediction, and deployment. The model accepts arguments such as image size and device selection. It can evaluate model performance on a validation set, perform object detection on an image, and export the model to ONNX format. The model can be used with code to detect objects, extract bounding boxes, and display or save the detected objects. The model outputs bounding box coordinates, and the detected object can be cropped from the input image for further processing or visualization.

instruction: I want to write a dramatic scene set in a post-apocalyptic world — can you assist with the dialogue?
description: Tifa-DeepSexV2-7b-MGRPO is a model based on Qwen2.5 7b, optimized for deep role-playing experiences. It features a 1024k (1 million) word context window and is trained using a dataset generated by Tifa_220B and an innovative MGRPO algorithm. The model is designed to support role-playing dialogues, creative writing requiring divergent thinking, complex logical reasoning with chain-of-thought (CoT), and deep contextual role interactions. It is not specifically optimized for generating non-compliant content, but efforts have been made to reduce rejection responses. The model has undergone four-stage architectural evolution: incremental pre-training with 0.1T tokens of novels, Tifa-COT-SFT cold start for improved logic and context association, MGRPO algorithm for enhanced role-play data training, and DPO to prevent repetition and improve political safety. The MGRPO algorithm was modified from GRPO to support literary content training, incorporating logical algorithms and multiple reward passes. The model also features layer propagation changes inspired by Universal Transformers and latent space concepts, with gradient clipping to prevent gradient explosion. Reward functions include logic, literary style, formatting, coherence, and anatomical accuracy. The model is trained on 0.1T novels, 100,000 SFT samples, and MGRPO reinforcement learning, using a 2x8×H100 GPU cluster. It is expected to take 3000 H100 hours to complete training. The model supports GGUF quantization and is licensed under Apache-2.0. It is recommended for role-playing and creative writing but not for mathematical computation, code generation, or fact-based tasks. Users are advised to follow official prompt templates and adjust frontend code to remove thinking tags from outputs. The model is currently in a verification phase, with 7% of training completed, and is expected to be followed by Chat and Crazy versions. The model has shown improved logical reasoning, spontaneous thinking, increased vocabulary for deep role-play, reduced rejection, and enhanced literary output. However, it may exhibit potential safety concerns, emotional consistency, ideological shifts, and a tendency toward dramatic outputs. Users should ensure generated content complies with local laws and regulations.

instruction: Please translate the following news headline from Turkmen to English.
description: This model is a LoRA fine-tuned version of the NLLB-200 (600 M) model, specifically adapted for Turkmen to English and English to Turkmen translation. The fine-tuning was performed using Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) on only the `q_proj` and `v_proj` matrices, resulting in approximately 2.4 million trainable parameters, which is about 0.38% of the total model parameters. The model was developed as a final project for a Deep-Learning class and as a contribution to the Turkmen AI community, where open-source resources are limited. It is intended for use in research prototypes, student projects, and quick experiments on Turkmen text, but is not suitable for commercial machine translation systems, critical medical or legal translation, or production workloads without further validation. The model is licensed under CC-BY-NC 4.0, which restricts its use to non-commercial purposes. The training data consisted of 619 parallel sentences (495 for training, 62 for validation, and 62 for testing) drawn from news and official communiqués. The training was conducted on a single NVIDIA A100 40 GB GPU using the AdamW optimizer with a learning rate of 1 × 10⁻⁵, a cosine schedule with 10% warm-up, over 5 epochs. The batch size was 4 for training and 8 for evaluation, with FP16 enabled. The LoRA configuration used `r=16`, `alpha=32`, `dropout=0.05`, and targeted the `q_proj` and `v_proj` modules. Evaluation results show that the fine-tuned model achieved a BLEU score of 8.24 for English to Turkmen translation, compared to 8.12 for the original model, and a BLEU score of 25.88 for Turkmen to English, compared to 26.48 for the original. The model also showed improvements in chrF and TER metrics for English to Turkmen translation, but a slight decline in BLEU and chrF for Turkmen to English. A manual review of 50 test sentences indicated that 36 translations were judged as “Good” or better in terms of adequacy, and 38 were considered fluent by native speakers. Limitations include a small training dataset of around 500 sentences, which may limit vocabulary and domain coverage, potential hallucination of proper nouns or numbers in longer inputs, and the inability to guarantee gender or politeness nuances. The model is open for contributions, including data, code, evaluation, and documentation improvements, with all contributions required to be compatible with non-commercial use. Future work includes experimenting with larger datasets, hyperparameter tuning, and exploring additional evaluation metrics.

instruction: Generate a 10-second audio clip in English with a happy tone that says 'Welcome to our event!'
description: Zonos-v0.1 is an open-weight text-to-speech model trained on more than 200,000 hours of varied multilingual speech, delivering expressiveness and quality on par with or surpassing top TTS providers. The model enables highly natural speech generation from text prompts when given a speaker embedding or audio prefix and can accurately perform speech cloning with a reference clip as short as a few seconds. The conditioning setup allows for fine control over speaking rate, pitch variation, audio quality, and emotions such as happiness, fear, sadness, and anger. The model outputs speech natively at 44kHz. Zonos follows a straightforward architecture: text normalization and phonemization via eSpeak, followed by DAC token prediction through a transformer or hybrid backbone. Zonos supports zero-shot TTS with voice cloning, where users can input desired text and a 10-30 second speaker sample to generate high-quality TTS output. It also supports audio prefix inputs, allowing users to add text plus an audio prefix for richer speaker matching, which can be used to elicit behaviors such as whispering. The model provides multilingual support for English, Japanese, Chinese, French, and German. It offers fine-grained control over many aspects of the generated audio, including speaking rate, pitch, maximum frequency, audio quality, and various emotions. The model runs with a real-time factor of approximately 2x on an RTX 4090. Zonos comes with a Gradio WebUI for easy speech generation and can be installed and deployed using a provided Docker file. Currently, the repository supports Linux systems, preferably Ubuntu 22.04 or 24.04, with recent NVIDIA GPUs (3000-series or newer, 6GB+ VRAM). The model depends on the eSpeak library for phonemization, which can be installed on Ubuntu using the command 'apt install -y espeak-ng'. Installation can be performed using a new uv virtual environment, the system or active environment with uv or pip, or via Docker. A minimal example is provided to confirm that the installation works. For academic use, the model should be cited as @misc{zyphra2025zonos, title={Zonos-v0.1: An Expressive, Open-Source TTS Model}, author={Dario Sucic, Mohamed Osman, Gabriel Clark, Chris Warner, Beren Millidge}, year={2025},}.

instruction: What is the combined revenue from the first and third quarters?
description: This model is a TAPAS base model fine-tuned on Sequential Question Answering (SQA). It has four versions, with the latest and default version corresponding to the `tapas_sqa_inter_masklm_base_reset` checkpoint. The model was pre-trained on masked language modeling (MLM) and an additional intermediate pre-training step, followed by fine-tuning on SQA. It uses relative position embeddings by default, resetting the position index at every cell of the table. Other versions include `tapas_sqa_inter_masklm_base` with absolute position embeddings, `tapas_sqa_masklm_base_reset` with relative position embeddings and no intermediate pre-training, and `tapas_sqa_masklm_base` with absolute position embeddings and no intermediate pre-training. TAPAS is a BERT-like transformer model pre-trained on a large corpus of English data from Wikipedia in a self-supervised manner. It was pre-trained using two objectives: masked language modeling, where 15% of the words in a flattened table and associated context are randomly masked and the model must predict them, and intermediate pre-training, where the model is trained on a balanced dataset of millions of syntactically created examples to predict whether a sentence is supported or refuted by a table. The model learns an inner representation of English language used in tables and associated texts, which can be used for downstream tasks such as answering questions about a table or determining whether a sentence is entailed or refuted by the contents of a table. Fine-tuning involves adding a cell selection head on top of the pre-trained model and jointly training this classification head with the base model on SQA. The model can be used for answering questions related to a table in a conversational setup. The texts are lowercased and tokenized using WordPiece with a vocabulary size of 30,000. The model inputs are of the form [CLS] Question [SEP] Flattened table [SEP]. The model was fine-tuned on 32 Cloud TPU v3 cores for 200,000 steps with a maximum sequence length of 512 and a batch size of 128. Fine-tuning takes approximately 20 hours. The optimizer used is Adam with a learning rate of 1.25e-5 and a warmup ratio of 0.2. An inductive bias is added so that the model only selects cells of the same column, as reflected by the `select_one_column` parameter of `TapasConfig`. The model card was written by the Hugging Face team and contributors, as the original TAPAS team did not provide one.
domain: Natural Language Processing Table Question Answering

instruction: Show me the positions of all the books and notebooks in this desk image.
description: MM Grounding DINO (base variant) was proposed by Xiangyu Zhao, Yicheng Chen, Shilin Xu, Xiangtai Li, Xinjiang Wang, Yining Li, and Haian Huang. It improves upon previous models by enhancing the contrastive class head and removing parameter sharing in the decoder, which leads to improved zero-shot detection performance. Specifically, it achieves 50.6 AP (an increase of 2.2) on COCO and 31.9 val AP (an increase of 11.8) and 41.4 minival AP (an increase of 12.6) on LVIS. The model is designed for zero-shot object detection and can be used with raw inputs. It was trained on a combination of datasets including O365, GoldG, GRIT, and V3Det. Evaluation results show that the model achieves strong performance across different backbones and pre-training data combinations. For example, with a Swin-T backbone and pre-training data from O365, GoldG, and V3Det, the model achieves 50.6 COCO mAP. With a Swin-B backbone and pre-training data from O365, GoldG, and V3Det, the model achieves 52.5 COCO mAP. With a Swin-L backbone and pre-training data from O365V2, OpenImageV6, and GoldG, the model achieves 53.0 COCO mAP. On LVIS, the model achieves 41.4 minival AP and 31.9 val AP with pre-training data from O365, GoldG, GRIT, and V3Det. The model is available in the mm_grounding_dino_base_all collection, and users can find all original checkpoints there. The model can be used for zero-shot object detection by preparing a processor and model, loading an image, and running inference with text labels. Postprocessing is required to extract detection results, including boxes, scores, and labels. The model is described in the paper titled 'An Open and Comprehensive Pipeline for Unified Object Grounding and Detection,' which is available as an arXiv preprint.

instruction: How would you categorize the content of this video clip?
description: V-JEPA 2 is a frontier video understanding model developed by FAIR at Meta. It extends the pretraining objectives of previous models, resulting in state-of-the-art video understanding capabilities, leveraging data and model sizes at scale. The model is intended to represent any video (and image) to perform video classification, retrieval, or as a video encoder for vision-language models. V-JEPA 2 is designed to process video inputs by sampling a specific number of frames, with this model using 64 frames. For image inputs, the model repeats the image across the desired number of frames. The model can generate video or image embeddings using its vision features. The code for V-JEPA 2 is released, and the model can be loaded using the transformers library. V-JEPA 2 is described in a technical report titled 'V-JEPA~2: Self-Supervised Video Models Enable Understanding, Prediction and Planning' by Mahmoud Assran, Adrien Bardes, David Fan, Quentin Garrido, Russell Howes, Mojtaba Komeili, Matthew Muckley, Ammar Rizvi, Claire Roberts, Koustuv Sinha, Artem Zholus, Sergio Arnaud, Abha Gejji, Ada Martin, Francois Robert Hogan, Daniel Dugas, Piotr Bojanowski, Vasil Khalidov, Patrick Labatut, Francisco Massa, Marc Szafraniec, Kapil Krishnakumar, Yong Li, Xiaodong Ma, Sarath Chandar, Franziska Meier, and Yann LeCun, published by FAIR at Meta in 2025.

instruction: 가장 최근에 등록된 레코드를 하나 조회하는 SQL 문을 생성해줘.
description: Llama-3.1-8B-Spider-SQL-Ko는 한국어 질문을 SQL 쿼리로 변환하는 Text-to-SQL 모델입니다. 이 모델은 train 데이터셋을 한국어로 번역한 데이터셋을 활용하여 미세조정되었습니다. Spider 한국어 검증 데이터셋(1,034개) 평가 결과, 정확 일치율은 42.65% (441/1034), 실행 정확도는 65.47% (677/1034)입니다. 실행 정확도가 정확 일치율보다 높은 이유는 SQL 문법이 다르더라도 동일한 결과를 반환하는 경우가 많기 때문입니다. 이 모델은 Llama 3.1 8B Instruct (4bit 양자화)를 기반으로 하며, 1-epoch 학습 데이터를 사용하였습니다. 166개의 다양한 도메인 데이터베이스를 지원하며, LoRA (r=16, alpha=32)를 학습 방법으로 사용하였습니다. 영어 테이블/컬럼명을 사용하며, Spider 데이터셋 도메인에 최적화되어 있습니다. NoSQL, 그래프 DB는 미지원하며, 매우 복잡한 중첩 쿼리는 정확도가 하락할 수 있습니다. 학습은 NVIDIA Tesla T4 (16GB) GPU에서 약 4시간 동안 진행되었으며, 최대 7.6GB VRAM을 사용하였습니다. 학습 시 하이퍼파라미터로 per_device_train_batch_size 2, gradient_accumulation_steps 4, learning_rate 5e-4, num_train_epochs 1, optimizer adamw_8bit, lr_scheduler_type cosine, warmup_ratio 0.05를 사용하였습니다. LoRA 설정은 r=16, lora_alpha=32, lora_dropout=0, target_modules로 q_proj, k_proj, v_proj, o_proj, gate_proj, up_proj, down_proj를 포함합니다.
domain: Natural Language Processing Table Question Answering

instruction: Summarize the key findings of this scientific study for a general audience.
description: Gemma is a family of lightweight, state-of-the-art open models from Google, built from the same research and technology used to create the Gemini models. Gemma 3 models are multimodal, handling text and image input and generating text output, with open weights for both pre-trained variants and instruction-tuned variants. Gemma 3 has a large, 128K context window, multilingual support in over 140 languages, and is available in more sizes than previous versions. Gemma 3 models are well-suited for a variety of text generation and image understanding tasks, including question answering, summarization, and reasoning. Their relatively small size makes it possible to deploy them in environments with limited resources such as laptops, desktops or your own cloud infrastructure, democratizing access to state of the art AI models and helping foster innovation for everyone. The input includes text strings such as questions, prompts, or documents to be summarized, and images normalized to 896 x 896 resolution and encoded to 256 tokens each. The total input context is 128K tokens for the 4B, 12B, and 27B sizes, and 32K tokens for the 1B size. The output is generated text in response to the input, such as an answer to a question, analysis of image content, or a summary of a document, with a total output context of 8192 tokens. The models were trained on a dataset of text data that includes a wide variety of sources. The 27B model was trained with 14 trillion tokens, the 12B model with 12 trillion tokens, the 4B model with 4 trillion tokens, and the 1B model with 2 trillion tokens. The training data includes web documents, code, mathematics, and images. The combination of these diverse data sources is crucial for training a powerful multimodal model that can handle a wide variety of different tasks and data formats. Rigorous data cleaning and filtering methods were applied to the training data, including CSAM filtering, sensitive data filtering, and additional methods based on content quality and safety. Gemma was trained using Tensor Processing Unit (TPU) hardware (TPUv4p, TPUv5p, and TPUv5e). TPUs offer advantages in performance, memory, scalability, and cost-effectiveness for training vision-language models. Training was done using JAX and ML Pathways. JAX allows researchers to take advantage of the latest generation of hardware, including TPUs, for faster and more efficient training of large models. ML Pathways is Google's effort to build artificially intelligent systems capable of generalizing across multiple tasks. Together, JAX and ML Pathways allow a single Python process to orchestrate the entire training run, simplifying the development workflow. The models were evaluated against a large collection of different datasets and metrics to cover different aspects of text generation, including reasoning and factuality, STEM and code, multilingual, and multimodal tasks. Evaluation results showed major improvements in the categories of child safety, content safety, and representational harms relative to previous Gemma models. The models have certain limitations, including the influence of training data quality and diversity, context and task complexity, language ambiguity and nuance, factual accuracy, and common sense. Ethical considerations include bias and fairness, misinformation and misuse, transparency and accountability, and privacy violations. The models are intended for a wide range of applications, including content creation and communication, research and education, and knowledge exploration. Risks identified include the perpetuation of biases, generation of harmful content, misuse for malicious purposes, and privacy violations. Mitigations include continuous monitoring, content safety safeguards, developer and end-user education, and adherence to privacy regulations.

instruction: Can you help me find the most relevant section of a textbook on machine learning?
description: Granite-Embedding-125m-English is a 125 million parameter dense biencoder embedding model from the Granite Embeddings suite, designed to generate high-quality text embeddings. It produces embedding vectors of size 768. The model was trained using open-source relevance-pair datasets with permissive, enterprise-friendly licenses, as well as IBM-collected and generated datasets. It maintains competitive scores on academic benchmarks such as BEIR and performs well on enterprise use cases. The model is developed using retrieval-oriented pretraining, contrastive fine-tuning, and knowledge distillation. It is intended for use in text similarity, retrieval, and search applications. The model is compatible with the SentenceTransformer library and can be used to encode text and calculate cosine similarity between embeddings. It is also compatible with the Huggingface Transformers library and PyTorch, using CLS pooling for embedding generation. The model is based on an encoder-only RoBERTa-like transformer architecture, trained internally at IBM Research. It has 12 layers, 12 attention heads, an intermediate size of 3072, and uses the GeLU activation function. The vocabulary size is 50265, and the maximum sequence length is 512 tokens. The model's training data includes unsupervised title-body pairs from the web, publicly available paired data with permissive licenses, IBM-internal data targeting technical domains, and IBM-generated synthetic data. Notably, the popular MS-MARCO retrieval dataset was excluded due to its non-commercial license. The model was trained using IBM's Cognitive Compute Cluster, which uses NVIDIA A100 80GB GPUs. The model is trained only on English texts and has a context length of 512 tokens, with longer texts being truncated. The data used to train the base language model was filtered to remove text containing hate, abuse, and profanity. The model's performance on the MTEB Retrieval (BEIR) and CoIR benchmarks is reported as 52.3 and 50.3, respectively.

instruction: Locate the different types of vehicles in this traffic jam image.
description: MM Grounding DINO (tiny variant) is a model proposed by Xiangyu Zhao, Yicheng Chen, Shilin Xu, Xiangtai Li, Xinjiang Wang, Yining Li, and Haian Huang. It improves upon previous models by enhancing the contrastive class head and removing parameter sharing in the decoder, which leads to improved zero-shot detection performance. Specifically, it achieves 50.6 AP (an increase of 2.2) on COCO and 31.9 val AP (an increase of 11.8) and 41.4 minival AP (an increase of 12.6) on LVIS. The model is designed for zero-shot object detection and can be used with raw inputs. It was trained on a combination of datasets including O365, GoldG, GRIT, and V3Det. Evaluation results show that the model performs well across different backbones and pre-training data combinations. The model can be accessed through the Transformers library using the identifier 'rziga/mm_grounding_dino_tiny_o365v1_goldg_grit_v3det'.

instruction: What steps would you take to recover a misplaced window on your screen?
description: UI-TARS-1.5 is an open-source multimodal agent built upon a powerful vision-language model, designed to perform diverse tasks within virtual worlds, particularly excelling in playing games and performing GUI tasks. It integrates advanced reasoning enabled by reinforcement learning, allowing the model to reason through its thoughts before taking action, which enhances its performance and adaptability, especially in inference-time scaling. The model achieves state-of-the-art results across a variety of standard benchmarks, demonstrating strong reasoning capabilities and notable improvements over prior models. UI-TARS-1.5 outperforms previous models and competitors such as OpenAI CUA and Claude 3.7 in computer use, browser use, and phone use benchmarks. It also shows superior performance in grounding capability evaluations and in game-based scenarios such as Poki Game and Minecraft tasks. The model's performance is further enhanced when it includes thought processes during execution. The UI-TARS-1.5-7B variant focuses on general computer use and is not specifically optimized for game-based scenarios, where the full UI-TARS-1.5 model holds a significant advantage. The model is available for early research access to facilitate collaborative research.

instruction: Rate the sharpness and composition of this wildlife photo.
description: VisualQuality-R1-7B is a non-reference image quality assessment (NR-IQA) model enhanced by reinforcement learning to rank (RL2R). It is designed to perform both quality description and rating through reasoning. The model is trained on a diverse combination of synthetic and realistic datasets. It is capable of assessing the quality of images by generating a rating as a float between 1 and 5, with 1 representing very poor quality and 5 representing excellent quality. The model supports both single and batch image quality rating tasks, with options for non-thinking mode to reduce inference time or thinking mode to include a reasoning process. The model is based on the Qwen2.5-VL architecture and uses the flash_attention_2 implementation for efficient computation. The model's training involves preparing IQA images in a single folder, generating a JSON file from an original MOS file, and executing training scripts either within a single node or across multiple nodes. The model's development was supported by the VLM-R1 codebase and acknowledges contributions from the field of AR generation. The model's paper has been accepted as a spotlight at NeurIPS 2025.

description: mPLUG-Owl3 is a state-of-the-art multi-modal large language model designed to address the challenges of long image sequence understanding. It introduces Hyper Attention, a mechanism that enhances the speed of long visual sequence understanding in multi-modal large language models by sixfold, enabling the processing of visual sequences that are eight times longer. The model maintains strong performance on single-image, multi-image, and video tasks. It supports chat with images and videos, and is compatible with specific attention implementations such as 'sdpa' and 'flash_attention_2'. The model is capable of generating text-based responses to visual inputs, including descriptions of images and videos. It is based on a 2B parameter architecture and is available for use with the Transformers library. The model has been developed by Jiabo Ye, Haiyang Xu, Haowei Liu, Anwen Hu, Ming Yan, Qi Qian, Ji Zhang, Fei Huang, and Jingren Zhou, and is described in a 2024 paper titled 'mPLUG-Owl3: Towards Long Image-Sequence Understanding in Multi-Modal Large Language Models' with the arXiv identifier 2408.04840.

description: EraX-VL-7B-V2.0-Preview is a robust multimodal model designed for OCR (optical character recognition) and VQA (visual question-answering), with a particular focus on Vietnamese. It excels in recognizing and analyzing various documents, including medical forms, invoices, bills of sale, quotes, and medical records. This model is intended to support applications in hospitals, clinics, insurance companies, and similar fields. Built on a high-quality foundation, it has been fine-tuned to enhance performance. It is a preview version of the final V2.0, which is planned for release after Lunar New Year ( Ất Tỵ 2025). EraX-VL-7B-V2.0-Preview is a multimodal LLM-based model, not a typical OCR-only tool. It is designed to analyze medical images, such as routine chest X-rays and complex brain scans, identifying potential issues with clarity. It also scrutinizes accident photos, detailing damages and proposing repair options. The model is part of the EraX's LànhGPT collection of LLM models. It is developed by Nguyễn Anh Nguyên, Nguyễn Hồ Nam, Phạm Huỳnh Nhật, and Phạm Đình Thục, and funded by EraX. The model type is a Multimodal Transformer with over 7B parameters, primarily supporting Vietnamese with multilingual capabilities. It is licensed under Apache 2.0 and has been fine-tuned from an unspecified source. The model supports a range of tasks, including OCR for multi-images, image captioning, multi-turn visual question answering and reasoning, and document analysis. It has demonstrated high performance in benchmarks, particularly in the VI-MTVQA task, achieving a score of 47.2 in the EraX-VL-7B-V1.5 version. The model is intended for use in various applications, including radiology, dental imaging, car accident analytics, and document processing. It is designed to handle images with potential issues such as scratches, water damage, or rotations. The model is intended to be used with carefully crafted prompts to achieve optimal results. It is not a typical OCR tool but a multimodal LLM-based model. The model is intended to be used in a variety of applications, including medical imaging, automotive engineering, and document processing. instruction: I want to create a voice-based notification system. How can I compress the audio files for optimal performance?
description: The stable-codec-speech-16k is a Transformer-based codec model designed for high-quality, low-bitrate audio coding. It processes audio waveforms by encoding them into discrete tokens, which can later be decoded back into the original audio waveform. This released model is a speech codec designed to compress real-world speech data into a suitable format for generative modeling. It provides a foundational tool for developing downstream applications in speech understanding and generation, such as text-to-speech systems and conversational AI models. The model was trained on datasets derived from creative commons or public domain audiobook recordings. Intended uses include the efficient compression of speech signals for storage or streaming purposes, enhancing speech-based applications such as telecommunication systems and real-time communication platforms, research and development in audio coding and speech synthesis including understanding and improving codec performance, and the development of downstream applications including speech recognition and generation. This model is purely trained on non-overlapping clean English speech and exhibits optimal performance in these situations. It is not suitable for applications requiring high-fidelity music or environmental sound coding. For individuals or organizations generating annual revenue of US $1,000,000 (or local currency equivalent) or more, regardless of the source of that revenue, you must obtain an enterprise commercial license directly from Stability AI before commercially using Stable Codec, any derivative work of Stable Codec (such as a fine tune model), or their outputs.

instruction: What are the early indicators of skin cancer that should not be ignored?
description: ApolloMoE is a medical language model designed to support a wide range of languages, including 12 major languages such as English, Chinese, French, Hindi, Spanish, Arabic, Russian, Japanese, Korean, German, Italian, and Portuguese, as well as 38 minor languages. The model is intended to democratize access to medical large language models by providing multilingual support. It is based on a mixture-of-experts (MoE) architecture, which allows for efficient and scalable model design. The model includes both dense and post-MoE variants, with sizes ranging from 0.5B to 9B parameters. The model is trained and evaluated on the ApolloMoEDataset and tested using the ApolloMoEBench benchmark. Evaluation results include performance across multiple domains such as clinical knowledge, medical genetics, anatomy, professional medicine, college biology, and college medicine. The model is available for download through Hugging Face, and inference and training procedures are provided in the model card. The model supports different input formats depending on the model size, and users are advised to follow the specified formatting for each variant. The model's training and evaluation processes involve data preparation, tokenization, and generation of scores for benchmarking. The model is described as covering a broad range of languages and is intended for use in medical and educational contexts. However, some results were excluded from the paper due to high variability, and certain datasets were not used in the final evaluation.

instruction: What emotional cues are present in this audio that you can detect?
description: This model is designed for speech emotion recognition, leveraging the Whisper Large V3 model to classify audio recordings into emotional categories, including Angry, Disgust, Fearful, Happy, Neutral, Sad, and Surprised. The model is fine-tuned for audio classification tasks and maps emotion labels to numeric IDs for training and evaluation. The training dataset includes multiple sources and contains labeled recordings with the following distribution: 752 samples for Sad, 752 for Happy, 752 for Angry, 716 for Neutral, 652 for Disgust, 652 for Fearful, 652 for Surprised, and 192 for Calm. The Calm emotion was excluded from training due to its underrepresentation. Audio preprocessing involves loading files with Librosa and extracting features using the Whisper Feature Extractor, which standardizes and normalizes the audio for model input. The model was trained with a learning rate of 5e-05, a train batch size of 2, an eval batch size of 2, a random seed of 42, and gradient accumulation steps of 5, resulting in an effective batch size of 10. Training used the Adam optimizer with betas (0.9, 0.999) and epsilon 1e-08, a linear learning rate scheduler with a warmup ratio of 0.1, and 25 epochs with native AMP for mixed precision. Training was monitored using Wandb. Evaluation metrics after training include a loss of 0.5008, accuracy of 0.9199, precision of 0.9230, recall of 0.9199, and an F1 score of 0.9198. These metrics indicate strong performance in identifying emotional states from speech data. The model is implemented using the Transformers library and requires PyTorch, with specific versions including Transformers 4.44.2, PyTorch 2.4.1+cu121, Datasets 3.0.0, and Tokenizers 0.19.1.
